abstract: This review summarizes five decades of on-field concussion biomechanics research in American football, from early helmet instrumentation in the 1960s through NFL laboratory reconstructions, college and high school Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) system studies, and youth football investigations. Key findings include that head impact exposure and concussion tolerance both increase with player age, and that substantial differences exist between helmet models in their ability to reduce concussion risk.
tldr: On-field biomechanical research across all levels of American football has produced validated helmet rating systems that show significant differences between helmet models in concussion risk reduction, providing actionable data for equipment selection and policy decisions.
methods: Narrative review of on-field biomechanical data collection studies across NFL (laboratory reconstructions and video analysis), collegiate and high school (HIT system, CARE Consortium), and youth football levels. Covers sensor technologies, injury metrics (HIC, SI, HIP, STAR, DAMAGE, UBrIC), risk function development, and helmet evaluation methodologies.
findings:
• Average head kinematics for concussive impacts: NFL ~98±28g linear, 6432±1813 rad/s² rotational; college/high school ~104±30g, 4726±1931 rad/s²
• Head impact exposure and injury tolerance both increase with player age
• Youth players show lower concussion tolerance than adults
• Linemen sustain highest frequency of impacts; skill positions sustain higher magnitude impacts
• Helmet performance varies substantially between models; STAR rating system quantifies these differences
• Practice structure modifications (reducing tackling drill time) can reduce high-magnitude impacts by 38%
• Mouthguard sensors more tightly coupled to skull, offering more accurate head kinematics than helmet-mounted sensors
limitations: Concussions are vastly underreported in football; helmet-mounted sensors measure helmet, not head, acceleration; NFL reconstruction data overestimated risk due to sampling bias toward injurious impacts; youth data limited by small concussion sample sizes.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and sport science staff should use validated helmet rating data (STAR methodology) to guide helmet selection at all levels. Practice structure modifications that reduce high-magnitude head impacts are evidence-based interventions. Individual-level head impact monitoring should account for player position and personal exposure history.
athleteDev: Biomechanical data supports position-specific head impact profiling: linemen require highest-priority helmet performance, while skill position players face fewer but higher-magnitude impacts. Practice design should minimize unnecessary high-magnitude head impacts through drill modification and contact limitations during preseason.
rtp: Biomechanical threshold data informs understanding of impact severity associated with concussion diagnosis and can guide graduated RTP protocols. The finding that concussed players sustain greater head impact exposure on days of injury supports monitoring of cumulative exposure as part of pre-injury baseline and RTP decision frameworks.

citation: Tucker S, Milne I, Pitcher M, et al. A review of syndesmosis injuries and preferred treatment in football players. Curr Rev Musculoskelet Med. 2025. DOI: 10.1007/s12178-025-09954-x.
abstract: This narrative review examines syndesmotic ankle injuries in American football players at the professional and collegiate levels, covering anatomy, injury mechanisms, epidemiology, diagnosis, and treatment options. Syndesmotic injuries account for 24.6% of all ankle sprains in NCAA football players and carry a 9–16 week average recovery period in the NFL. The review concludes with a surgical algorithm and RTP protocol, finding flexible fixation (suture button) generally preferred over rigid fixation.
tldr: Syndesmotic ankle injuries are common and highly disabling in American football players, with MRI as the diagnostic gold standard and flexible fixation generally preferred over rigid fixation, though rigid fixation may be warranted for high-risk positions such as linemen.
methods: Narrative review of epidemiology, anatomy, biomechanics, diagnosis, and treatment of syndesmotic injuries in football athletes. West Point Ankle Grading System used as classification framework. Three institutional case examples presented with surgical technique details.
findings:
• Syndesmotic injuries account for 24.6% of NCAA ankle sprains and 16% of NFL Combine ankle injury history
• NFL recovery averages 9–16 weeks; syndesmosis with fracture median RTP 250 days in NFL
• Artificial turf significantly increases syndesmotic injury incidence (2.9 vs. 2.2 per 10,000 AE on grass)
• Linemen and running backs at greatest risk; tackling (31%) and blocking (22%) most common mechanisms
• Flexible fixation shows similar clinical outcomes to rigid fixation with less implant failure and hardware removal
• MRI is gold standard; overall RTP rate after adequately treated syndesmotic injury exceeds 90%
• Direct arthroscopic visualization of reduction is superior to fluoroscopy alone
limitations: Review article without systematic meta-analysis; case examples represent a single institution; indications for flexible versus rigid fixation remain subjective; long-term outcomes data in high-demand football athletes are limited.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers should recognize that syndesmotic injuries carry significantly longer RTP timelines than lateral ankle sprains (9–16 weeks vs. days–weeks). Physical exam findings combined with MRI are necessary for accurate diagnosis. Postoperative rehab protocol spans 14–16 weeks before full RTP.
athleteDev: NFL and collegiate programs should implement position-specific risk awareness for syndesmotic injuries, with linemen and running backs at highest risk. Turf surface management and technique coaching may reduce incidence. Pre-participation screening at the NFL Combine should include thorough ankle syndesmosis assessment.
rtp: Syndesmotic injuries require staged RTP: 2 weeks splint, 2–6 weeks boot, weeks 6–12 functional rehab and strengthening, weeks 14–16 jogging and conditioning with full RTP as strength and proprioception normalize. Syndesmosis with fracture or diastasis represents the longest RTP timeline (median 250 days in NFL).

citation: Oliver JM, Jones MT, Anzalone AJ, et al. A season of American football is not associated with changes in plasma tau. J Neurotrauma. 2017;34(23):3295-3300.
abstract: This longitudinal study examined plasma tau concentrations in 19 NCAA Division I football athletes over the 2014–2015 season across eight serial blood samples, comparing them to 19 non-contact swim athlete controls. Despite routine sub-concussive head impacts throughout the season, no significant changes in plasma tau were observed, and no group differences were detected between football athletes and swim athletes.
tldr: Despite routine sub-concussive head impact exposure over a full American football season, plasma tau concentrations did not change significantly in collegiate football athletes, suggesting plasma tau is not sensitive enough to detect axonal damage from repetitive sub-concussive impacts at this level of play.
methods: Longitudinal observational cohort study; 19 NCAA Division I football athletes underwent serial fasting plasma tau sampling at 8 time points over 27 weeks (pre-season through post-season). 19 NCAA swim athletes provided a single comparison sample. Athletes classified as starters vs. non-starters. Plasma tau measured via Simoa HD-1 analyzer.
findings:
• No significant change in plasma tau across the football season regardless of starter status
• No significant difference between football athletes and swim controls after non-contact or contact periods
• Football starters had higher tau than non-starters at baseline (T1; p=0.039) and late season but not compared to swim athletes
• Tau AUC was higher for starters vs. non-starters (p=0.052), approaching but not reaching significance
• No concussions occurred in the football cohort during the study
limitations: Small sample size (n=19 football, n=19 swim); absence of concurrent biomechanical head impact data; no concussion diagnoses during study; single plasma sample from swimmers precludes longitudinal comparison; blood sampling window may miss acute tau peaks.
practicalImplications: Plasma tau does not appear to be a reliable real-time biomarker for monitoring sub-concussive impact burden in collegiate American football athletes. Other markers such as serum neurofilament light (NF-L) may offer greater sensitivity for sub-concussive axonal injury detection.
athleteDev: Routine plasma tau monitoring over a season is unlikely to yield actionable data for athlete health management related to sub-concussive exposure. Biomarker-based monitoring programs should consider NF-L or other markers with demonstrated sensitivity.
rtp: Plasma tau does not serve as a useful RTP biomarker for concussion or sub-concussive impact assessment. Clinicians should not rely on tau normalization as an RTP criterion. Future biomarker-assisted RTP protocols should incorporate markers with demonstrated acute sensitivity such as NF-L, GFAP, or S100B.

citation: Kluczynski MA, Kelly WH, Lashomb WM, Bisson LJ. A systematic review of the orthopaedic literature involving National Football League players. Orthop J Sports Med. 2019;7(8):2325967119864356.
abstract: This systematic review summarized all data published between January 1980 and March 2018 on orthopaedic injuries experienced by NFL Combine candidates and professional NFL players. A total of 147 articles met inclusion criteria and were divided into 11 anatomic categories. The majority of studies (74%) were level 4 evidence, with knee (24%), spine (13%), shoulder (13%), and foot (12%) injuries most commonly examined. Data sources included the NFL Combine database (26%), internet searches (24%), and the NFL Injury Surveillance System (22%).
tldr: This is the first comprehensive systematic review of orthopaedic injuries in NFL players, covering 147 studies across 11 anatomic sites; knee, spine, and shoulder injuries dominate the literature, most evidence is level 4, and internet-based data sources likely undercount true injury rates compared to official surveillance systems.
methods: Systematic review of PubMed, Embase, and CINAHL databases (January 1980–March 2018); included orthopaedic injury studies in NFL Combine participants and professional NFL players; excluded concussions, TBI, facial injuries, vascular injuries, and case reports; articles categorized by anatomic site, level of evidence, and data source.
findings:
• 147 articles included across 11 anatomic categories
• Most studied sites: knee (24%), spine (13%), shoulder (13%), foot (12%)
• 74% of studies were level 4 evidence; no level 1 or 2 studies identified
• ACL RTP ranged 60–90% depending on position; performance diminished post-ACL in most positions except QB
• Shoulder instability decreased NFL Combine draft likelihood and career longevity
• Cervical disc herniation negatively affected games played; RTP ~70% after ACDF
• Achilles tendon rupture RTP 66–72%; performance metrics decreased 2–3 years post-repair
• Internet-based studies identified far fewer injury cases than NFLISS-based studies
limitations: Majority of studies are level 4 evidence; internet-based data sources are incomplete; 17% of studies collected data from a single team; survey data subject to recall bias; no level 1 or 2 studies exist for any orthopaedic injury category in NFL players.
practicalImplications: Team physicians and athletic trainers should reference this review as a single-source summary of injury frequency and RTP outcomes by anatomic site. Medical grading at the NFL Combine should incorporate shoulder instability, meniscal injury, ACL tear, and lower extremity injury history as these have well-documented effects on performance and availability.
athleteDev: NFL Combine medical evaluations should weigh history of shoulder injury, lumbar spine conditions, ACL reconstruction, and foot pathology as these are associated with reduced draft probability, diminished performance, and shorter careers. Physical performance at the Combine is not diminished by isolated ACL reconstruction history.
rtp: RTP benchmarks by injury type: ACL reconstruction 60–90% (position-dependent, ~6 months minimum); shoulder instability surgery 80–90% RTP but ~9 months; ACDF ~70% RTP; Achilles tendon rupture 66–72% RTP with 2–3 years of performance decrement; lateral meniscectomy 60% RTP at 8–9 months; lumbar discectomy 75–80% RTP. Corticosteroid injection for syndesmosis and muscle strains allows earlier RTP by approximately 10 days.

citation: Wang A, Healy J, Hyett N, Berthelot G, Kryger KO. A systematic review on methodological variation in acute:chronic workload research in elite male football players. Sci Med Football. 2020. DOI: 10.1080/24733938.2020.1765007.
abstract: This systematic review investigated methodological variation in acute:chronic workload ratio (ACWR) research and its relationship to injury in elite male soccer players. After searching four databases, 12 studies met inclusion criteria from 2,689 initial results. All studies were deemed poor quality by Newcastle-Ottawa Scale assessment. Substantial heterogeneity was found in workload definitions, GPS speed zone thresholds, ACWR time windows, and statistical methods, precluding meaningful cross-study comparison.
tldr: Note: This paper pertains to soccer/association football, not American football. Major methodological inconsistencies across 12 ACWR-injury studies in elite male soccer players make it impossible to draw firm conclusions about whether acute:chronic workload ratios reliably predict injury risk, underscoring the need for standardized protocols before ACWR is used operationally.
methods: PRISMA-guided systematic review of four databases (PubMed, Web of Science, ScienceDirect, Embase); included longitudinal cohort studies of elite male soccer players examining ACWR and injury rate/risk; quality assessed via Newcastle-Ottawa Scale; data extracted on population characteristics, workload protocol, injury classification, and statistical analysis.
findings:
• 12 studies included; all rated poor quality by Newcastle-Ottawa Scale
• Five studies used GPS-derived external workload; heterogeneity in speed zone definitions across all studies
• Nine studies incorporated sRPE as internal workload measure
• All studies used rolling average ACWR with 1:2, 1:3, or 1:4-week windows
• No consensus on exposure types (training only vs. training + matches + recovery sessions)
• Significant variation in injury classification, missing data handling, and statistical approaches
• None of the included studies received NOS points for comparability
limitations: All 12 included studies were of poor methodological quality; no standardized ACWR definition or workload measure exists; FIFA match GPS restrictions prevented data collection in two studies; heterogeneity precluded meta-analysis.
practicalImplications: Sport scientists using ACWR to monitor injury risk in football players should be cautious given the lack of methodological consensus. Practitioners should clearly define acute and chronic windows, standardize speed zone thresholds for GPS-derived external load, and specify which exposure types are included. ACWR may not be a reliable standalone injury prediction tool.
athleteDev: The methodological concerns raised — inconsistent workload definitions, variable ACWR windows, lack of standardization — are broadly applicable to any team sport load monitoring program including American football. Practitioners implementing ACWR-based athlete monitoring should develop consistent internal protocols rather than relying on published benchmarks from heterogeneous soccer research.
rtp: The lack of standardized injury definitions and RTP decision-making criteria across the 12 studies highlights that even injury classification varies substantially, complicating development of evidence-based RTP protocols in football contexts.

citation: Philipp NM, Crawford DA, Fry AC. A total score of athleticism to estimate the amount of variance explained in on-field performance within collegiate American football players. Int J Strength Cond. 2022. DOI: 10.47206/ijsc.v2i1.04.
abstract: This study aimed to create position-specific statistical models using physical performance tests to explain variance in on-field performance across four collegiate American football seasons at an NCAA Division 2 school. Four position groups were analyzed: Wide Receiver/Tight End, Running Back, Linebacker/Defensive Line, and Defensive Backs. Off-season tests included back squat, bench press, hang clean, 40-yard dash, pro-agility, L-drill, vertical jump, and broad jump. Linear mixed models with backward selection explained 22–49% of variance in on-field performance.
tldr: Position-specific models combining at least three physical performance tests (e.g., broad jump, hang clean, 40-yard dash, squat, L-drill) explain 22–49% of on-field performance variance in collegiate American football players, confirming that no single test captures overall athletic ability and that physical preparation priorities should differ by position.
methods: Retrospective analysis of preexisting off-season test data and individual player game statistics from four NCAA Division 2 football seasons (2016–2019); data standardized to z-scores by position group; linear mixed models with backward selection using AIC and marginal/conditional r²; Total Score of Performance calculated as mean of position-specific standardized game statistics.
findings:
• WR/TE model: broad jump, bench press, L-drill, hang clean, pro-agility explained 48.7% of variance (ICC=0.711)
• Running Back model: squat, 40-yard dash, broad jump explained 48% of variance (ICC=0.789)
• Defensive Back model: squat, 40-yard dash, vertical jump, L-drill explained 22% of variance
• LB/DL model: squat, hang clean, L-drill, pro-agility explained 47.8% of variance
• LB/DL group showed large gap between marginal (48%) and conditional (81%) r², suggesting interindividual random factors are important
limitations: Offensive linemen excluded due to lack of quantifiable on-field statistics; quarterbacks excluded due to small sample size; single NCAA Division 2 institution; does not account for coaching quality, scheme, or opponent strength.
practicalImplications: S&C staff should use position-specific test batteries rather than a single metric when profiling athletes and making training decisions. Hang clean and L-drill are particularly relevant for LB/DL; broad jump and 40-yard dash for skill positions. The large unexplained variance in most models suggests physical preparation alone is insufficient — coaching, tactical awareness, and motivation also drive performance.
athleteDev: Physical performance tests used at the NFL Combine (40-yard dash, broad jump, L-drill, pro-agility) are meaningful performance predictors when combined into position-specific models. Teams can use these findings to prioritize testing battery components, individualize off-season training programming, and support talent identification decisions.
rtp: The position-specific physical profiles identified could inform rehabilitation benchmarks and readiness-to-return criteria by establishing normative physical performance standards for each position group.

citation: Baker LB, Alfred S, Lee KA, et al. Accuracy of a smart bottle in measuring fluid intake by American football players during pre-season training. Sci Rep. 2023;13:11383.
abstract: This study determined the accuracy of a smart squeeze bottle (Gatorade Smart G² Bottle) equipped with optical sensor cap technology in measuring fluid intake in 30 male American football players during three outdoor pre-season practice sessions. Smart bottle measurements were compared to gravimetric reference measurements across 110 measurement time points. There was no significant mean difference in total fluid intake between methods, with Bland-Altman 95% limits of agreement of approximately ±15% of overall fluid intake, confirming the device as a valid real-world hydration monitoring tool.
tldr: A smart squeeze bottle with optical sensor cap technology accurately measured fluid intake in American football players during pre-season practice at the group level (no significant bias) with individual-level error of approximately ±15%, making it a practical real-time hydration monitoring tool for sport practitioners.
methods: 30 male American football players at IMG Academy participated in three outdoor pre-season practice sessions (~29°C, ~75% RH) during one week in August. Reference fluid intake measured by gravimetry; smart bottle used optical Time-of-Flight sensors and Bluetooth. Bland-Altman analysis, paired t-tests, Deming regression used; body mass measured pre/post practice.
findings:
• No significant mean difference in total fluid intake between smart bottle (1220±371 ml) and reference method (1236±389 ml; p=0.39)
• Mean difference expressed as percentage: −0.6±7.5%
• Bland-Altman 95% LOA: −212 to 180 ml (~±15% of total fluid intake)
• Strong correlation between methods (r=0.96, p<0.0001)
• Mean fluid balance: −0.64±0.73% body mass loss
• Sweating rates ranged 0.47–2.51 L/h; total sweat losses 0.77–3.72 L
limitations: Each player tested only once per day; smart bottle requires resting on a flat surface within 10 m of a paired smartphone; large temperature variations may affect sensor accuracy; study funded by Gatorade Sports Science Institute (PepsiCo R&D), a potential conflict of interest.
practicalImplications: Sport scientists and athletic trainers can use smart bottle technology to monitor real-time fluid intake in American football players during practice with acceptable accuracy. Combined with individualized sweat testing, smart bottles could help practitioners close the loop on real-time hydration during high-risk summer pre-season environments.
athleteDev: Pre-season American football practice involves substantial sweat losses (0.47–2.51 L/h), and wide inter-individual variation in fluid balance was observed. Smart bottle monitoring enables individualized hydration tracking at scale across a team roster during pre-season camp, supporting performance and safety.
rtp: Monitoring fluid balance and hydration status is relevant to RTP contexts — dehydration can impair neurocognitive function and physical performance, and ensuring adequate rehydration between sessions is a component of recovery and safe participation readiness for athletes returning from any time-loss event.

citation: Terry DP, Grashow R, Iverson GL, et al. Age of first exposure does not relate to post-career health in former professional American-style football players. Sports Med. 2024. DOI: 10.1007/s40279-024-02062-9.
abstract: This study examined whether age of first exposure (AFE) to American football before age 12 was associated with adverse later-life health outcomes in a large cohort of 4,189 former professional American-style football players from the Football Players Health Study (FPHS). Multivariable regressions adjusting for age, race, BMI, playing position, professional seasons, and prior concussion burden revealed no significant associations between AFE<12 and any of 17 outcomes including depression, anxiety, cognitive difficulties, dementia, hypertension, sleep apnea, chronic pain, memory loss, and ADHD.
tldr: In the largest study of its kind (n=4,189 former NFL players), starting tackle football before age 12 was not independently associated with worse post-career health outcomes after adjusting for relevant covariates, contradicting earlier small-sample studies.
methods: Cross-sectional survey study within the FPHS at Harvard University; 4,189 former professional American-style football players; AFE dichotomized at age 12 and also examined as a continuous variable; outcomes assessed via PHQ-2, GAD-2, Neuro-QoL scales, and self-reported medical diagnoses; multivariable linear and logistic regressions with penalized spline regression in generalized additive models to test non-linear AFE relationships.
findings:
• n=4,189 former players; mean age 52±14 years; 39% self-identified as Black
• Univariable: AFE<12 associated with slightly greater depression (p=0.03, η²=0.001) and anxiety — negligible effect sizes
• Multivariable: AFE<12 not significantly associated with any of 17 outcomes
• AFE<12 group reported lower rates of high blood pressure, sleep apnea, and low testosterone prescriptions
• No non-linear AFE relationships detected in spline models
• Concussion burden (cumulative signs and symptoms score) was a key covariate and meaningful determinant of adverse outcomes
limitations: Self-reported AFE and health outcomes subject to recall bias; medical diagnoses not validated against medical records; voluntary participation introduces ascertainment and healthy-worker bias; cross-sectional design; limited to former professional players.
practicalImplications: Clinicians and sport medicine staff evaluating former NFL players should focus on concussion burden, BMI, and playing position (lineman status) as more meaningful covariates of adverse health outcomes than the age at which a player began tackle football.
athleteDev: This study challenges prior research suggesting that youth tackle football participation before age 12 leads to irreversible long-term neurological harm in those who go on to play professionally. Player development staff can be reassured that early football participation was not independently linked to worse cognitive or psychiatric outcomes in this large sample.
rtp: Concussion burden — not age of first exposure — appears to be a more meaningful determinant of long-term health, underscoring the value of evidence-based concussion RTP protocols throughout an athlete's football career.

citation: Askling CM, Tengvar M, Thorstensson A. Acute hamstring injuries in Swedish elite football: a prospective randomised controlled clinical trial comparing two rehabilitation protocols. Br J Sports Med. 2013;00:1–8.
abstract: This prospective randomised controlled trial compared two rehabilitation protocols for acute hamstring injuries in 75 Swedish elite football players verified by MRI. Players were assigned to a lengthening-focused protocol (L-protocol, n=37) or a conventional exercise protocol (C-protocol, n=38), with the primary outcome of days to return to full team training and match availability. Time to return was significantly shorter in the L-protocol (mean 28 days) compared to the C-protocol (mean 51 days), across both sprinting-type and stretching-type injuries. Only one reinjury was recorded across the 12-month follow-up, occurring in the C-protocol group. MRI and palpation measures confirmed that injury proximity to the ischial tuberosity and lesion length were independently correlated with longer return times.
tldr: A hamstring rehabilitation protocol emphasising lengthening exercises at long muscle-tendon lengths returned elite football players to play ~23 days faster than a conventional protocol, supporting specificity of training as a key rehabilitation principle.
methods: Prospective RCT in Swedish elite football (top two divisions); 75 MRI-confirmed acute hamstring injuries; stratified randomisation by gender and injury type (sprinting vs. stretching); L-protocol: three lengthening exercises (The Extender, The Diver, The Glider); C-protocol: three conventional exercises (contract-relax stretching, cable-pendulum, pelvic lift); supervised weekly, pain-free progression; Askling H-test as return-to-play criterion; 12-month reinjury follow-up; Mann-Whitney U test and Spearman correlations; SPSS v.19.
findings:
• L-protocol mean return: 28 ± 15 days (range 8–58); C-protocol mean: 51 ± 21 days (range 12–94); p < 0.001.
• L-protocol faster for both sprinting-type (43 vs. 74 days) and stretching-type injuries (23 vs. 41 days).
• 54/75 injuries (72%) were sprinting-type; primary muscle was biceps femoris long head in 69% of cases.
• One reinjury recorded (C-protocol, sprinting-type, BFlh) vs. zero in L-protocol over 12 months.
• Shorter distance to ischial tuberosity and longer oedema length on MRI both correlated with longer return time (Spearman r = −0.736 to −0.832).
• MRI-negative players (n=11, all sprinting-type, all L-protocol) returned mean 6 days, significantly faster than MRI-positive sprinting injuries.
limitations: Single test leader (CMA) performed all clinical examinations and supervised protocols, increasing potential for bias; no independent blinding of clinical assessors; performance-motivated elite players may have returned faster than recreational populations; Askling H-test added ~7 days to rehabilitation period on average; no direct comparator data from prior RCTs in football.
practicalImplications: Hamstring rehabilitation should prioritise exercises that load the muscle at long lengths under eccentric conditions (e.g., Nordic-style, hip-dominant lengthening movements). Establishing injury type (sprinting vs. stretching) and MRI characteristics at presentation provides early prognostic information and can guide expected return timelines. A functional criterion test (equivalent to the Askling H-test) should gate return-to-play decisions.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football: sprinting-type hamstring injuries dominate in skill positions (WR, DB, RB) due to high-speed running demands. Incorporating lengthening-based hamstring exercises (RDLs, Nordic curls, single-leg hip hinges at full range) into off-season and in-season programming addresses both primary prevention and rehabilitation specificity. Paper is from soccer/association football context.
rtp: Return to full team training and match availability occurred ~23 days sooner with the lengthening protocol (mean 28 vs. 51 days). Proximal free tendon involvement, injury closer to the ischial tuberosity, and longer MRI oedema length each independently predicted longer return times. A validated dynamic stability test (Askling H-test) should be passed before clearance to avoid premature return and reinjury.

citation: Crema MD, Guermazi A, Tol JL, Niu J, Hamilton B, Roemer FW. Acute hamstring injury in football players: Association between anatomical location and extent of injury—A large single-center MRI report. J Sci Med Sport. 2015;(in press).
abstract: This retrospective observational study included 275 male professional football (soccer) players with MRI-confirmed acute hamstring injuries, generating 393 lesions assessed for location and cross-sectional area of edema and tears. Lesions were classified across seven anatomical zones per muscle (proximal tendon through distal myofascial junction) in four hamstring muscles. The long head of biceps femoris (LHBF) was the most commonly affected muscle (56.5%), with proximal myotendinous junction and proximal locations most prevalent overall. Proximal myotendinous junction location was associated with significantly greater edema extent in the LHBF and semitendinosus, while distal locations in the semitendinosus had significantly larger tears.
tldr: In 275 professional football players, MRI demonstrated that proximal myotendinous junction injuries produce the largest edema and proximal biceps femoris injuries are most frequent, while distal semitendinosus injuries carry the largest tears—findings with direct prognostic relevance since lesion extent predicts recovery time.
methods: Retrospective review of 275 consecutive male professional football players referred to ASPETAR (Qatar) 2009–2012 with acute hamstring injury and positive MRI within 5 days; 1.5T MRI (Magnetom Espree, Siemens) using PDw fat-suppressed and T2-weighted sequences; manual segmentation of edema and tear cross-sectional area (cm²) by a blinded radiologist with 7 years of experience; inter-reader reliability assessed on random 40-player subsample; one-sample t-test with significance at p < 0.05; SAS v.9.2.
findings:
• LHBF was most commonly affected muscle accounting for 222/393 lesions (56.5%).
• Overall, injuries most prevalent at proximal myotendinous junction (33%) and proximal locations (31.8%).
• Proximal MTJ of LHBF and ST had significantly greater mean edema area than muscle-wide mean (p = 0.0027 and p = 0.0083, respectively).
• Proximal LHBF locations had significantly larger edema than distal locations (4.09 vs. 3.23 cm²; p = 0.02).
• Distal ST locations had significantly larger tears than proximal locations (3.47 vs. 1.02 cm²; p < 0.001).
• 88.6% of lesions exhibited edema; 27.7% had measurable tears; 60.8% edema only with no tear.
• Kappa = 1.00 for edema detection; ICC = 0.98 for edema area; ICC = 1.00 for tear area (excellent reliability).
limitations: Only MRI-positive players included; no control group; potential initial-read bias mitigated by 2-month re-read interval; field-of-view did not cover distal tendon insertions so distal tendon lesions were excluded; retrospective design prevents causal inference.
practicalImplications: Clinicians should document the specific anatomical location of hamstring injuries at initial MRI assessment because proximal myotendinous junction injuries carry greater edema burden (associated with longer recovery and reinjury risk) while distal semitendinosus injuries tend to have larger tears. Knowing location at diagnosis provides early, indirect prognostic information about expected recovery timeline.
athleteDev: Applicable to American football skill positions prone to high-speed running injuries. Biceps femoris long head proximal myotendinous junction is the highest-risk site; programming should emphasise hip-dominant eccentric loading that targets this zone (RDLs, GHRs, hip extensions). Paper is from soccer/association football context.
rtp: Proximal myotendinous junction location is associated with greater edema extent, which prior studies link to longer recovery time and higher reinjury risk. Distal semitendinosus injuries with larger tear area also predict longer return timelines. MRI location and edema/tear cross-sectional area should inform individualised return-to-play timelines rather than relying on symptom resolution alone.

citation: Crossland BW, Rigby BR, Duplanty AA, King GA, Juma S, Levine NA, Clark CE, Ramirez KP, Varone NL. Acute Supplementation with Cannabidiol Does Not Attenuate Inflammation or Improve Measures of Performance following Strenuous Exercise. Healthcare. 2022;10(6):1133.
abstract: This double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover RCT investigated whether acute supplementation with 5 mg/kg CBD isolate attenuates inflammation, muscle damage, and performance decrements following strenuous eccentric exercise in 24 female NCAA collegiate athletes. Participants completed 100 repetitions of unilateral eccentric leg extension (muscle-damaging protocol) with CBD or placebo administered at −2.5 h, immediately post, and +10 h. Blood markers (IL-6, IL-10, IL-1β, myoglobin) and performance measures (vertical jump, isokinetic peak torque at 60°/180°/300°/s, isometric torque) were assessed at baseline, 4 h, 24 h, and 48 h post-damage. No significant differences were observed between CBD and placebo treatments for any inflammatory marker, muscle damage marker, or performance variable at any timepoint.
tldr: Acute CBD supplementation at 5 mg/kg did not reduce inflammation, limit muscle damage, or restore performance in well-trained female collegiate athletes following intense eccentric exercise, suggesting CBD is not an effective acute recovery intervention.
methods: Double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover RCT; 24 female NCAA DI/DII athletes (mean age 21.2 ± 1.8 yr); 5 mg/kg CBD isolate (0% THC verified) vs. matched placebo (microcrystalline cellulose); administered at −2.5 h, 0 h (post muscle damage), and +10 h; ~28-day washout to control for menstrual cycle; muscle damage: 10×10 eccentric leg extensions at 30°/s on Biodex; performance: countermovement vertical jump (Just Jump mat), concentric isokinetic torque at 60°/180°/300°/s, 5-s isometric torque; VAFS fatigue scale; blood draws at −2.5 h, 4 h, 24 h, 48 h; cytokines (IL-6, IL-10, IL-1β) via MILLIPLEX bead panel; myoglobin via Abnova ELISA; RM MANOVA and post hoc RM ANOVA with Šídák adjustment; IBM SPSS v.24.
findings:
• No significant treatment × trial interaction for IL-6, IL-10, or IL-1β (p = 0.573 for treatment effect across cytokines).
• No significant differences between CBD and placebo for myoglobin (p = 0.116).
• Myoglobin rose significantly from baseline at 4 h when treatments combined (p = 0.002), confirming successful muscle damage.
• No significant treatment differences for vertical jump (p = 0.806), isokinetic strength at 60° (p = 0.479), 180° (p = 0.426), or 300°/s (p = 0.927), or isometric torque (p = 0.671).
• 60°/s peak torque was significantly lower than baseline at 4 h and 24 h regardless of treatment (p = 0.003 and p = 0.001).
• VAFS fatigue scores increased significantly from baseline at 4 h, 24 h, and 48 h (p < 0.001) with no treatment difference (p = 0.126).
limitations: Female-only sample limits generalisability to male athletes; acute dosing design may not reflect chronic supplementation benefits; absolute dose range was 224–408 mg per timepoint (potentially below therapeutic threshold for humans); only one form of eccentric exercise used; no pharmacokinetic confirmation of CBD absorption; PCA composite score failed, requiring MANOVA.
practicalImplications: Practitioners and athletes should not expect acute CBD supplementation to meaningfully reduce inflammation, accelerate muscle damage recovery, or restore strength performance following high-intensity eccentric training. Current evidence does not support its use as an acute post-exercise recovery supplement. Chronic supplementation protocols and effects in male athletes require further investigation before any recommendations can be made.
athleteDev: Relevant to American football athletes considering CBD as a recovery supplement during pre-season two-a-days or following high-volume eccentric training phases. Given null findings, sports dietitians and strength staff should direct athletes toward evidence-based recovery strategies (nutrition timing, sleep, progressive loading) rather than CBD for acute recovery.
rtp: No evidence that acute CBD supplementation accelerates recovery of strength or reduces inflammatory markers following muscle-damaging exercise. Athletes returning from muscle strains or surgery who are using CBD for recovery should be counselled that current data do not support acute benefits on objective performance or inflammatory biomarkers.

citation: Silva H, Nakamura FY, Serpiello FR, Ribeiro J, Roriz P, Marcelino R. Adapting the percentage intensity method to assess accelerations and decelerations in football: moving beyond absolute and arbitrary thresholds. Sports Biomechanics. 2024;(online ahead of print).
abstract: This study adapted the Sonderegger et al. (2016) percentage intensity approach to individualise the assessment of accelerations and decelerations in professional football using GPS data from 42 male players across four training microcycles. Raw velocity-time data from 10-Hz Catapult GPS devices were used to calculate acceleration and deceleration magnitudes across seven starting speed bandwidth intervals (<5 to >30 km/h), and effort intensities were classified as very low (<25%), low (25–50%), moderate (50–75%), and high (>75%) of each player's maximal observed value per speed band. Acceleration capacity decreased strongly with increasing starting speed (r = −0.97), while deceleration capacity was less starting-speed dependent (r = −0.94). The adaptation allows practitioners to classify individual efforts during real-life training scenarios without requiring dedicated field tests.
tldr: A GPS-based individualised method for classifying acceleration and deceleration intensities in football showed that acceleration capacity is highly dependent on starting speed whereas deceleration is less so, enabling practitioners to monitor training loads without arbitrary absolute thresholds or field tests.
methods: 42 male professional footballers from two Portuguese first-division teams; 10-Hz Catapult GPS (Vector S7 and X7); one full mesocycle (4 weeks) per team across 2020/21 and 2021/22 seasons (812 training files total); maximal acceleration/deceleration per starting speed bandwidth interval (<5, 5–10, 10–15, 15–20, 20–25, 25–30, >30 km/h) extracted from proprietary OpenField software; percentage intensity = (action value / maximal value for that speed band) × 100; intensity zones: very low <25%, low 25–50%, moderate 50–75%, high >75%; Pearson r and linear regressions analysed maximal effort vs. starting speed; mean paired differences with Cohen's d (90% CI) compared adjacent speed bands; Linear Mixed Model with player individuality and position as random factors; jamovi with ESCI package.
findings:
• 86% of very low intensity accelerations and 79% of very low intensity decelerations started from <5 km/h.
• Correlation between maximal acceleration and starting speed: r = −0.97 (p < 0.001); deceleration: r = −0.94 (p < 0.01).
• Maximal acceleration decreased significantly at each successive starting speed band (all very large effect sizes, Cohen's d range −1.57 to −3.34).
• Deceleration differences between adjacent starting speed bands ranged from unclear to very large effect sizes, confirming weaker starting-speed dependence.
• Linear Mixed Model: acceleration F = 1085.81, R² = 0.96; deceleration F = 35.08, R² = 0.42—initial velocity explained nearly all variance in acceleration but not deceleration.
• Very low intensity efforts (<25%) dominated the training distribution and likely represent noise or negligible mechanical load.
limitations: Only training sessions analysed; competition data excluded; maximal values derived from training may underestimate true maximal capacity compared to isolated field tests; only two teams from one country; goalkeepers excluded due to positional specificity; >30 km/h starting speed band contained very few efforts, potentially inflating maximal values for that interval.
practicalImplications: Load monitoring systems using absolute acceleration thresholds (e.g., >3 m/s²) misclassify individual effort intensity because the same magnitude is harder to achieve from low speeds. Practitioners should collect maximal acceleration per starting speed band from each training session (using the week's highest value) to build a rolling individual reference without dedicated testing days. Very low intensity efforts (<25% of max) can be excluded from analysis as they represent minimal mechanical stimulus. For decelerations, starting speed matters less—a single threshold approach may be more acceptable.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football GPS/GNSS monitoring programs. Skill positions (WR, DB, RB) who accelerate frequently from standing starts are misrepresented by absolute m/s² thresholds. Adopting a relative, individualised classification system would allow strength and conditioning staff to accurately quantify high-intensity acceleration work across different positions and training phases. Paper is from soccer/association football context.
rtp: Although not a return-to-play study, the individualised threshold method can be applied during rehabilitation to verify that an athlete is producing high-intensity accelerations and decelerations relative to their own maximal capacity—not just crossing arbitrary absolute values—before being cleared for full competition loads.

citation: Mitton JL, Meyers MC, van Woerden I, Lindsay RP, Sterling JC, Robinson SK. Adductor-related groin injury: Prevalence and etiology during NCAA football games on artificial and natural grass surfaces. PM&R. 2025;1–10.
abstract: This 16-season (2006–2021) prospective cohort study quantified the incidence and etiology of adductor-related groin injuries during NCAA Football Bowl Subdivision (FBS) games across heavyweight artificial turf and natural grass surfaces at 41 universities. Of 9360 total game injuries across 2243 documented games, 111 (1.2%) were adductor-related groin injuries: 48 (43.2%) on artificial turf and 63 (56.8%) on natural grass, with no statistically significant surface effect on any outcome. The vast majority were minor muscle strains (87%), occurring most frequently during defensive play (41%) and noncontact sprinting/running (39%). MANOVA indicated no significant difference between surfaces in injury severity, classification, category, primary type, mechanism, or situation.
tldr: Across 16 seasons of NCAA FBS football, heavyweight artificial turf was as safe as natural grass for adductor-related groin injuries, with surface type explaining minimal variance in incidence or severity; focus should shift to early detection and adductor-specific prevention programming.
methods: Prospective cohort; 41 FBS universities; 2006–2021 competitive seasons; injury surveillance via two-sided single-page forms completed by certified ATCs within 7 business days; artificial turf restricted to heavyweight infill systems (≥9.0 lbs/ft²); 23 player positions condensed into offense, defense, special teams and six skill positions; outcomes: injury severity (minor 0–6 days, substantial 7–21 days, severe ≥22 days), classification (acute/recurrent), category (shoe-surface contact/noncontact), primary injury type, mechanism, situation, player position, cleat design, field conditions, temperature, imaging, surgery, and turf age; injury incidence rate = (injuries ÷ games) × 10; IBM SPSS 28.0; MANOVA with Wilks' λ; effect sizes as partial η².
findings:
• 111 adductor-related groin injuries over 16 seasons (48 artificial turf, 63 natural grass); IIR = 0.45 vs. 0.53 per 10 games; no significant surface effect (Wilks' λ p = 0.602; η² = 0.009).
• 87% minor injuries; 16.2% substantial; 3.6% severe—no significant surface difference in severity (p = 0.602).
• Primary injury type: muscle strain/spasm predominated on both surfaces (42 artificial, 55 natural grass).
• Most common mechanisms: noncontact sprinting/running (39%) and defensive play situations (41% on artificial turf).
• No significant surface effect for injury classification, category, mechanism, situation, player position, cleat design, field conditions, temperature, imaging, surgery, or turf age.
• Defensive players (especially secondary) experienced the highest counts on both surfaces.
• Molded/hybrid cleats were most common at injury, consistent with their prevalence in the game population.
limitations: Inability to control for inherent random variation in team-sport injury; athlete strength/conditioning and body composition status not controlled; previous injury history not fully controlled; opportunity to obtain long-term quality-of-life data was beyond scope; findings generalise only to FBS-level competition with heavyweight artificial turf; partially funded by FieldTurf (potential conflict of interest disclosed).
practicalImplications: Playing surface type (heavyweight artificial turf vs. natural grass) should not be a primary concern when managing adductor injury risk in college football. Prevention focus should be on early detection of adductor strains, adductor strength and flexibility assessment, and implementation of structured resistance programs that progress from general lower-body development to adductor-specific training. Defensive players, particularly secondary, warrant heightened monitoring given their higher observed injury counts.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football programs at all levels. Adductor strains comprise ~1.2% of game injuries and are concentrated in high-speed, change-of-direction movements common to defensive backs and linebackers. Pre-season adductor screening (squeeze tests, Copenhagen adduction strength) and progressive adductor loading programs (Copenhagen adduction exercise, resisted lateral band walks, adductor-specific machines) should be incorporated into S&C programming to reduce strain risk and improve return-to-sport timelines.
rtp: Following adductor-related groin trauma, 87% of cases were minor (0–6 day time loss), suggesting most athletes can return within the same week. A structured rehabilitation progression—general lower-body resistance training in the first days post-injury advancing to adductor-specific loading in subsequent weeks—is recommended. Early detection and avoiding premature return are prioritised over surface modification as the key return-to-play considerations.

citation: Brett BL, Huber DL, Wild A, Nelson LD, McCrea MA. Age of First Exposure to American Football and Behavioral, Cognitive, Psychological, and Physical Outcomes in High School and Collegiate Football Players. Sports Health. 2019;Vol XX(No X).
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined the relationship between age of first exposure (AFE) to American football and behavioral, cognitive, psychological, and physical outcomes in 1,802 active high school and collegiate football players. Players were dichotomized into AFE before age 12 vs. age 12 or older, with general linear models used to assess differences across numerous clinical outcome measures. After controlling for covariates including age, concussion history, race, and ADHD diagnosis, AFE before age 12 did not significantly predict poorer performance on any clinical outcome measures (Ps >0.05). A single statistically significant association was found: AFE <12 was associated with lower somatization scores, suggesting potentially less somatic distress.
tldr: Starting football before age 12 was not associated with worse behavioral, cognitive, psychological, or physical outcomes in high school and college players; earlier starters actually reported lower somatic symptom severity, suggesting youth football participation does not impair functioning during adolescence.
methods: Cross-sectional study; N=1,802 active high school (9 schools) and collegiate (4 colleges) football players enrolled in the Project Head to Head 1 and 2 studies (August 2012–September 2017). AFE was self-reported and dichotomized at age 12. Potential covariates identified via t-tests and chi-square tests were entered into a multivariable binary logistic regression. Single-predictor general linear models (GLMs) and covariate-adjusted GLMs were performed for each clinical outcome variable including neurocognitive (ImPACT, SAC, TMT, WAIS-IV), psychological (BSI-18, BSSS, DIS-11), and physical (BESS, King-Devick) measures.
findings:
- AFE <12 vs. ≥12 did not significantly differ on any clinical outcome after covariate adjustment (all Ps >0.05 except somatization).
- AFE <12 was significantly associated with lower BSI-18 somatization scores (F=11.39, p=0.01) after covariate adjustment.
- Without covariates, AFE <12 showed significantly higher ImPACT visual memory scores (F=6.98, p<0.01).
- Covariates of age, race, ADHD, and concussion history differed significantly between AFE groups and were more strongly associated with clinical outcomes than AFE itself.
- Effect sizes for AFE on clinical outcomes were uniformly near zero (partial η² ≤ 0.005).
limitations: Cross-sectional design limits causal inference. AFE relied on athlete self-report, introducing recall bias. Other sport participation throughout development was not controlled. High correlation between AFE and total football exposure (r=0.80) makes it difficult to disentangle the two. Results may not generalize to former or older athletes where long-term sequelae may be more apparent.
practicalImplications: Current evidence does not support restricting youth football participation before age 12 based on cognitive or psychological harm during adolescence. Screening and management of ADHD, concussion history, and demographic factors are more clinically relevant predictors of outcome than age of football start. Programs focused on athlete well-being should address these modifiable health factors rather than focusing solely on AFE.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football player development. Findings suggest that beginning tackle football before age 12 does not impair neurocognitive or psychological development through high school and college. This supports age-appropriate youth football participation without evidence of intermediate-term harm, though long-term effects in former players remain an open question.
rtp: No direct return-to-play implications studied. However, the null finding for AFE on concussion-related outcomes (SAC, SCAT3 symptom scores) suggests that a younger start to football does not inherently elevate concussion symptom burden at baseline, which is relevant context for baseline testing and RTP protocols in young athletes.

citation: Humphreys BR, Ruseski JE. Air quality and employee performance in teams: Evidence from the NFL. Economics and Human Biology. 2023;51:101310.
abstract: This economics study examined the impact of ambient PM2.5 particulate matter concentration on NFL team performance using play-level data from approximately 90,000 offensive plays across 900 NFL games from 2008 to 2016. Air quality data from EPA monitoring stations within 10 miles of 22 NFL stadiums were linked to game-day PM2.5 readings. A reduced-form linear regression model estimated yards gained or lost per offensive play as a function of local PM2.5 concentration, controlling for game-specific and situational factors. Results indicated that higher PM2.5 concentration was associated with significantly more yards gained per passing play, suggesting that defensive team production is relatively more sensitive to poor air quality than offensive production.
tldr: Higher game-day PM2.5 air pollution increased passing yards per play in NFL games, indicating that defensive players — who must react to unscripted offensive actions — are more impaired by poor air quality than offensive players who execute predetermined plays.
methods: Retrospective observational study using play-by-play data from ArmchairAnalysis.com for NFL games 2008–2016 (n=113,782 plays; 65,421 pass plays, 48,361 rush plays across ~900 games at 22 stadiums with nearby EPA AQS monitoring stations). PM2.5 concentration was averaged over 12pm–6pm on game day. Reduced-form linear models estimated yards gained per play as a function of PM2.5, controlling for offensive/defensive team-season fixed effects, week-of-season, quarter, score difference, yards to go, yards to goal line, play number on drive, and domed stadium indicator. Robustness checks included nonlinear PM2.5 bins, outdoor-stadium subsamples with weather controls, ozone substitution, and incomplete pass exclusions.
findings:
- A one standard deviation increase in PM2.5 (~5.63 µg/m³) was associated with approximately 4.3 additional passing yards per game per team.
- PM2.5 coefficient was 0.014 yards/play overall (p<0.05), driven entirely by passing plays (0.021, p<0.05); rushing plays showed no significant association.
- Defensive teams are affected more than offensive teams by poor air quality, based on the mechanism of reactive vs. scripted play execution.
- Home team PM2.5 air quality shocks reduced home rushing yards; visiting team PM2.5 shock increased visiting team rushing yards on defense, consistent with defense being the primary channel.
- Effect sizes are small but statistically significant and robust across multiple model specifications.
limitations: Cannot directly control for coaching decisions or play-calling adjustments in response to air quality. Sample limited to 22 stadiums with nearby EPA monitoring stations, excluding 9 NFL teams. Game-day PM2.5 is measured at the nearest monitoring station, not inside the stadium, introducing measurement error. Cannot fully account for within-game variation or exact game start times. Air quality on game days is generally in healthy or moderate ranges, limiting generalizability to high-pollution scenarios.
practicalImplications: Air quality is an environmental factor that affects team athletic performance even at moderate concentration levels. For NFL and other professional teams, scheduling, venue selection, and indoor versus outdoor stadium considerations may have measurable performance implications. Staff monitoring environmental conditions on game day could inform preparation strategies, particularly for defensive personnel whose reactive performance appears most vulnerable to PM2.5 exposure.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football. Findings suggest that environmental air quality on game day impairs reactive decision-making and execution for defensive players more than offensive players. This distinction between scripted (offense) and reactive (defense) roles has implications for how player preparation and performance monitoring accounts for environmental stressors during the competitive season.
rtp: Not directly studied. However, the finding that even moderate ambient PM2.5 levels impair cognitive-physical performance in healthy elite athletes suggests that air quality monitoring could be a relevant environmental health consideration in return-to-activity protocols, particularly for athletes recovering from respiratory or neurological injuries.

citation: Heintz EC, Scott DP, Simms KR, Foreman JJ. Air Quality Is Predictive of Mistakes in Professional Baseball and American Football. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health. 2023;20:542.
abstract: This study examined the impact of air quality (measured by the EPA's Air Quality Index, AQI) on performance errors in Major League Baseball (MLB) and NFL quarterback performance from 1999–2020 (MLB) and 2006–2021 (NFL). Linear regression analyses used median county-level annual AQI as the independent variable of interest, predicting team errors per game (MLB), interception percentage (NFL), and total quarterback rating (QBR, NFL). AQI was a significant predictor in all three models: higher AQI (worse air quality) was associated with more errors per game in MLB, higher interception percentage, and lower QBR in the NFL, even after controlling for relevant confounders including payroll, manager/experience variables, and prior-season performance.
tldr: Worse annual air quality in the county where a professional baseball or football team plays is associated with more team errors in MLB, more interceptions by NFL quarterbacks, and lower overall quarterback performance ratings, indicating that air pollution meaningfully degrades elite cognitive-physical sport performance.
methods: Two separate OLS regression models were used. MLB sample: 632 team-season observations (1999–2020) with errors per game as the dependent variable; predictors included median annual AQI, year, manager tenure, manager age, and natural log of team payroll. NFL sample: 705 quarterback-season observations (2007–2021) for QBs starting no earlier than 1999 and playing for one team per season; dependent variables were interception percentage and QBR; predictors included cumulative AQI, year, NFL experience, prior-season performance, and games started. Air quality data sourced from EPA (epa.gov); MLB performance data from baseball-reference.com; NFL data from pro-football-reference.com.
findings:
- A one-point increase in AQI increased MLB errors by 0.000993 per game (p=0.005); teams in high-AQI counties (e.g., Phoenix) could commit 10+ more errors over a 162-game season.
- Cumulative AQI was a marginally significant positive predictor of NFL interception percentage (p=0.059) and a significant negative predictor of QBR (p=0.035).
- A 67-point worse AQI (e.g., average county vs. Phoenix/Maricopa County) was associated with an expected decrease in QBR of ~15.4 points (~0.7 SD) and ~1.27% higher interception rate (~0.4 SD).
- Team payroll and year were significant confounders in the MLB model; NFL experience and games started were significant for QB performance.
- Results are consistent with air pollution impairing both cognitive and physical performance in elite professional athletes.
limitations: Annual median AQI is a coarse measure that does not capture game-day or acute exposure fluctuations. Sample does not account for individual athlete adaptation to local air quality or acclimatization. Environmental factors such as temperature, altitude, and atmospheric pressure were not controlled. MLB analysis cannot examine cumulative individual effects due to roster turnover. Generalizability is limited to professional male athletes performing in short, intense sport contexts.
practicalImplications: Teams located in or traveling to counties with poor annual air quality may face a compounding performance disadvantage. Performance staff and medical teams should consider monitoring AQI as part of environmental preparedness, especially when games or training camps are scheduled in high-pollution areas. The cognitive demand of quarterback decision-making and fielding accuracy appear particularly vulnerable to ambient air pollution exposure.
athleteDev: Relevant to American football, specifically quarterback development and performance. Cumulative exposure to poor air quality across a career appears to degrade the high-level cognitive performance required for accurate decision-making and pass execution. Teams drafting or developing quarterbacks in high-AQI markets may face performance headwinds unrelated to talent or training quality.
rtp: Not directly studied. However, findings that even chronic moderate AQI exposure impairs elite QB cognitive performance suggest that air quality monitoring is relevant when determining readiness for return to full competition, particularly after respiratory illness or concussion, when cognitive-physical integration is already compromised.

citation: Ballmann CG, Rogers RR. American Football Headgear Impairs Visuomotor Drill Performance in Division I NCAA Football Athletes. Journal of Functional Morphology and Kinesiology. 2024;9:169.
abstract: This study investigated whether American football facemask reinforcement level and visor/eye shield type impair dynamic visuomotor drill performance in Division I NCAA football athletes. Sixteen male athletes (age 20.4±1.3 years) completed two counterbalanced repeated-measures experiments using a FITLIGHT reactive agility drill. Experiment 1 (EXP1) compared baseline (no helmet) against three facemask reinforcement levels (light, medium, heavy). Experiment 2 (EXP2) compared baseline against three visor conditions (clear, smoke-tinted, mirrored). All helmeted and all visor conditions produced significantly slower response times than baseline, with the impairment consistent across central, mid, and outer sensor positions and upper and lower sensor levels. No significant differences in response time were found between facemask reinforcement levels or between visor tint conditions.
tldr: Any American football helmet with a facemask — regardless of how reinforced it is or what type of visor is attached — significantly slows reactive visuomotor performance in Division I NCAA athletes, suggesting the helmet-facemask combination itself is the primary source of impairment rather than the specific facemask or visor type.
methods: Randomized, counterbalanced repeated-measures design with two experiments in Division I NCAA football players (n=16; offense=8, defense=6, special teams=2). EXP1: four conditions — baseline/no helmet (BL), helmet + light facemask (HL), helmet + medium facemask (HM), helmet + heavy facemask (HH) using a Schutt Vengeance Pro helmet. EXP2: four conditions — BL, helmet + clear visor (HCV; ~90% VLT), helmet + smoke visor (HSV; ~48% VLT), helmet + mirrored visor (HMV; ~28% VLT). Dynamic visuomotor test: FITLIGHT system with 10 LED sensors on 5 poles in a semi-circle (1 m radius); participants stepped to deactivate each illuminated sensor as fast as possible; 20 total responses per attempt, 3 attempts per condition with 3-min rest. Primary outcome: average response time (ms) from the single fastest attempt. Statistical analysis: 1×4 repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni-Holm post-hoc; effect sizes as partial η².
findings:
- EXP1: All helmeted conditions (HL, HM, HH) produced significantly slower overall response times than BL (all p<0.001; η²=0.091); no significant differences between facemask reinforcement levels (p>0.05).
- EXP1 by sensor position: BL significantly faster than all helmeted conditions at outer, mid, and central positions; response time slower at central vs. outer and mid positions.
- EXP1 by sensor level: BL faster than all helmeted conditions at upper and lower levels (p<0.001); upper sensors responded to faster than lower sensors (p=0.041).
- EXP2: All visor conditions (HCV, HSV, HMV) significantly slower than BL (all p<0.001; η²=0.321); no significant differences between visor types (p>0.05).
- EXP2 by sensor position: BL faster than all visor conditions; interaction between condition and position was significant (p<0.001; η²=0.037).
- EXP2 by sensor level: BL faster than all visor conditions at both upper and lower levels (all p<0.001; η²=0.461).
limitations: Small sample size (n=16) limits statistical power to detect differences between helmet conditions. Experiment conducted indoors under standardized lighting, which does not replicate outdoor game-day conditions where tinted visors may provide visibility benefits. Participants were unaware of hypotheses, but performance validity testing was not employed. Study does not examine head or neck kinematics that may partially explain compensation mechanisms. All male Division I athletes — results may not generalize to other levels or female athletes.
practicalImplications: All American football headgear configurations impair reactive visuomotor performance relative to no helmet, regardless of facemask or visor type. This suggests that training drills requiring rapid visual-motor response should be conducted in full game-day headgear to habituate athletes to these impairments. Performance and safety staff should consider the visual field and reaction time constraints imposed by headgear when designing catching, route-running, and tackling drills. Coaches advocating for reduced facemask reinforcement may not achieve meaningful reaction time benefits under dynamic movement conditions.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football at all levels. Findings indicate that the helmet itself — not the specific facemask or visor — is the primary limiter of dynamic visuomotor performance. For athlete development, this means players should train reaction-based skills while wearing full game equipment to build appropriate visual-motor adaptations. Visor selection (tint level) does not appear to add meaningful impairment beyond the facemask combination under standardized indoor conditions.
rtp: Relevant to return-to-play protocols following concussion or ocular injury. Because all helmet configurations impair dynamic reactive visuomotor performance, progressive return-to-sport testing that includes full equipment conditions is important to accurately assess readiness. Athletes may appear capable in no-helmet baseline testing but still show functional performance deficits in game equipment, which should be accounted for in RTP decision-making.

citation: Walton SR, Kerr ZY, Powell JR, Giovanello KS, McCrea MA, Guskiewicz KM, Brett BL. An 18-Year Study of Changes in Neurocognitive Function and Associations with Repetitive Head Trauma among Former Collegiate American Football Players: A Case Series. Archives of Clinical Neuropsychology. 2025;00:1–10.
abstract: This preliminary longitudinal case series examined neurocognitive test performance changes over approximately 18 years in 31 former collegiate American football players (mean age at follow-up 38.4±1.3 years), assessed at baseline during college (T1; c.1999–2001) and at 18-year follow-up (T2; c.2016–2018). A battery of paper-and-pencil neurocognitive tests was administered at both time points: HVLT-R Immediate and Delayed Recall, FAS Verbal Fluency, Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT), and Trail Making Test-B (TMT-B). Neither sport-related concussion (SR-CHx) history nor the Head Impact Exposure Estimate (HIEE) was significantly associated with changes in neurocognitive test scores over the study period. Group-level cognitive performance did not significantly change over 18 years, though individual-level variability was extremely high.
tldr: Group-level neurocognitive test performance did not significantly decline over 18 years among former collegiate football players now in early midlife, and neither concussion history nor repetitive head impact exposure was related to cognitive change scores, though the high individual variability underscores the need for larger longitudinal studies.
methods: Longitudinal case series; n=31 former collegiate American football players who participated in the original NCAA Concussion Study (T1: August 1999–January 2002) and a 15-year follow-up study (T2: July 2016–August 2018). Neurocognitive battery: HVLT-R Immediate Recall, HVLT-R Delayed Recall, FAS Verbal Fluency, SDMT, and TMT-B. Raw change scores (T2–T1) were computed alongside Cohen's d effect sizes and coefficients of variation (CoV). Repeated-measures ANCOVA models controlled for estimated premorbid ability (WTAR). Multivariable linear regression models tested associations of SR-CHx (categorical and continuous) and HIEE with change scores. Exploratory Spearman correlations examined associations between cognitive change and BSI-18 symptom domains, PSQI sleep quality, AUDIT alcohol use, DAST drug abuse, and total years of football play.
findings:
- No significant group-level changes in any neurocognitive test over 18 years (all p-values ≥ 0.06); small-to-medium effect sizes (Cohen's d: 0.18–0.41).
- Individual-level variability was very high across all measures (CoV: 244%–560%), with both improvements and declines observed across participants.
- Neither SR-CHx nor HIEE was significantly associated with change in any neurocognitive test outcome (all ps > 0.05); SR-CHx as a continuous variable approached significance for HVLT-R Immediate Recall (p=0.054).
- Higher BSI-18 Somatic symptom scores at T2 were significantly correlated with greater declines in HVLT-R Immediate Recall (ρ=−0.395, p=0.028) and SDMT (ρ=−0.380, p=0.035).
- Higher DAST scores (drug abuse screening) were significantly correlated with greater declines in HVLT-R Immediate Recall (ρ=−0.370, p=0.040).
- Over 40% of participants had HVLT-R scores ≥1.5 SD below normative means at T1, and far fewer at T2, suggesting potential practice/learning effects or true improvement.
limitations: Very small sample (n=31) substantially limits statistical power. No non-athlete control group for comparison. Self-reported concussion history and HIEE reliance introduces recall and estimation error. HIEE does not capture pre-high school participation. Cross-sectional exploratory factors (BSI-18, DAST) measured only at T2, precluding examination of concurrent changes. Performance validity measures not employed. Limited racial/ethnic and socioeconomic diversity reduces generalizability.
practicalImplications: Former collegiate football players at approximately 15+ years post-sport do not show uniform neurocognitive decline as a group, which provides some reassurance for players and staff concerned about long-term brain health. However, the wide individual variability means that population-level reassurances do not preclude meaningful decline in specific individuals. Somatic symptom burden and substance use emerged as potentially meaningful correlates of cognitive change and should be monitored and addressed in former player health programs.
athleteDev: Relevant to long-term athlete health monitoring in American football. The lack of a clear group-level cognitive decline trajectory over 18 years among former collegiate players in early midlife provides longitudinal evidence that competitive collegiate football does not universally result in measurable neurocognitive impairment by middle age. High individual variability suggests that identifying who is most at risk requires larger prospective studies accounting for lifestyle, health behaviors, and biological factors.
rtp: Not a return-to-play study; participants were 15+ years removed from collegiate play. However, the finding that somatic symptoms and substance use — rather than head trauma history alone — correlate with cognitive change scores suggests that comprehensive health monitoring protocols for retiring players should extend beyond concussion history to include mental and physical health screening as part of long-term athlete care.

citation: Funk JR, Cormier JM, Lessley DJ, Bailey AM, Sánchez EJ, Sherwood CP, Crandall JR, Arbogast KB, Myers BS, Mayer T, Sills AK. An Analysis of Guardian Cap Use and Changes in the Concussion Rate in National Football League Preseason Practices From 2018 to 2023. Am J Sports Med. 2025;1-8.
abstract: This cohort study examined the effect of the Guardian Cap NXT requirement on concussion incidence in NFL preseason practices from 2018 to 2023 (excluding 2020). Data from the NFL Injury Surveillance System were used to compare concussion rates before (2018, 2019, 2021) and after (2022, 2023) the Guardian Cap NXT mandate for offensive linemen, defensive linemen, tight ends, and linebackers. A total of 139 concussions occurred during the study period. Two Poisson regression models were run to assess overall concussion rates and helmet-shell-specific concussion rates while controlling for preseason, position, practice intensity, and running back/fullback status.
tldr: Requiring the Guardian Cap NXT was associated with a 54–62% reduction in NFL practice concussion rates for mandated positions, but the effect was no longer significant when only helmet-shell-involved concussions were analyzed, suggesting mechanisms beyond padding compression contributed to the reduction.
methods: Cohort study using NFL Injury Surveillance System data; concussion rates normalized to player practice exposures; univariate chi-square comparisons; two Poisson regression models (all concussions and helmet-shell concussions only); qualitative video review for impact source and helmet shell involvement; multiple imputation for missing shell impact data.
findings: • Guardian Cap NXT requirement associated with 54% reduction in concussion rate (univariate, P=.001) and 62% reduction in regression model (P=.004) for mandated positions. • No statistically significant association between Guardian Cap use and concussion rate when only helmet-shell concussions were analyzed (P=.122). • Concussions in GC group decreased from 23.3/year pre-requirement to 11.0/year post-requirement. • Non-GC group showed a 42% increase in concussions after the requirement (not attributable to Guardian Cap). • Padded practices had 3.97× higher concussion odds than unpadded practices. • Reduction was shared across all four required positions.
limitations: Small sample sizes in some subgroups; incremental rollout of Guardian Cap requirement; no data on Guardian Cap use by non-required players; inability to account for all factors affecting concussion risk including changes in practice style; video review unavailable for 18% of concussion cases; assumptions about universal compliance in required positions.
practicalImplications: Requiring padded helmet covers such as the Guardian Cap NXT for linemen, tight ends, and linebackers during practices may significantly reduce concussion incidence. Staff should note the benefit appears partially independent of shell padding compression (i.e., mass/inertia effects may contribute), and risk compensation behavior remains a theoretical concern that warrants monitoring.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football programs considering Guardian Cap NXT policies in preseason and regular-season contact practices. The mandate covering offensive/defensive linemen, tight ends, and linebackers reflects the highest-contact positions; programs at all levels may benefit from adopting similar policies during padded contact sessions.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play protocols; however, reducing practice concussion incidence by 54–62% has downstream implications for athlete availability and recovery burden. Teams with lower practice concussion rates will face fewer RTP decisions and associated disruptions to development.

citation: Copeland A, Heinze KL. An examination of competitive rule evolution in the National Football League. Managing Sport and Leisure. 2024; DOI: 10.1080/23750472.2024.2369323.
abstract: This study examined how competitive rules evolve in professional sport using a qualitative document analysis of NFL rule books published between 1980 and 2020. The authors focused on player conduct rules—specifically unsportsmanlike conduct, unnecessary roughness, roughing the passer, chop blocking, crackback blocking, and clipping—to trace longitudinal trajectories of rule change. An inductive coding approach identified rule components (actions, actors, times, spaces, sanctions) and change types (expansion, elaboration, reduction, deletion). Findings were contextualized using NFL public communications to connect rule changes to institutional factors.
tldr: NFL player conduct rules generally expanded in scope and length from 1980 to 2020, driven by institutional pressures around player safety, officiating quality, and competitive fairness, offering a framework for understanding how sport rules evolve incrementally over time.
methods: Qualitative document analysis (QDA) of 35 NFL rule books (1980–2020); Microsoft Word 'compare documents' feature used to identify text changes at five-year intervals; inductive coding in NVivo to identify rule components and change types; analyst triangulation; trajectories constructed for six player conduct rules.
findings: • Rule changes generally expanded the set of prohibited actions and the number of actors protected or prohibited. • Rules applied to more game moments (times) and more field/corporeal spaces over time. • Sanctions for rule violations tended to increase in severity. • Rules followed an expansion-elaboration trajectory with a mid-period reduction phase before expanding again by 2020. • Rule word count increased substantially across the observation period. • Institutional pressures—player safety, officiating quality, league legitimacy—appeared to drive most changes.
limitations: Rule books from six seasons (1990, 1991, 2004, 2005, 2007, 2008) were missing and substituted with adjacent years; qualitative approach limits generalizability; only six rules were analyzed; focus on player conduct rules excludes scoring and other game mechanics rules; study does not measure outcomes of rule changes.
practicalImplications: Sport organizations and governing bodies can use this framework to track, anticipate, and justify rule changes by examining changes at the component level. Leaders should communicate rule changes in terms of their institutional rationale (safety, fairness, entertainment) to improve player, coach, and official buy-in.
athleteDev: While not directly a sport science study, understanding how NFL rules evolve—particularly safety-related rule changes (e.g., roughing the passer, crackback blocking)—helps coaching and sports science staff anticipate rule-driven changes to blocking, tackling, and contact techniques that affect player development and practice design in American football.
