Eleven of 16 studies used the same NFL Player Care Foundation/Living Heart Foundation Screening Program dataset, raising concern for overlap. Most studies are cross-sectional. BMI may be an inadequate measure for this population due to high muscle mass; percent body fat may be more appropriate. Selection bias may affect self-reported survey studies.
practicalImplications: Former linemen are a particularly high-risk group for metabolic syndrome and warrant proactive cardiovascular screening and lifestyle intervention programs post-retirement. Weight management strategies during playing career may reduce long-term cardiovascular risk. Obstructive sleep apnea screening should be standard for retired players, especially linemen. Despite general cardiovascular risk, former players show protective factors (less smoking, more physical activity history) that can be leveraged in preventive care.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL football. Progressive weight gain from high school to professional play is linked to cardiovascular disease, sleep apnea, and chronic pain in later life. Monitoring body composition trajectories from recruiting through career transition and emphasizing healthy weight management as part of long-term athlete development may reduce post-career cardiometabolic risk, particularly for linemen.
rtp: Not a return-to-play study. However, cardiovascular screening findings may be relevant during RTP evaluations after cardiac events in current players. The high prevalence of metabolic syndrome risk factors in linemen, even during their active careers, suggests cardiovascular assessment should be integrated into pre-participation and return-to-play protocols.

citation: Grashow R, Tan CO, Thomas JD, DiGregorio H, Lee H, Rotem RS, Marengi D, Terry DP, Eagle SR, Iverson GL, Whittington AJ, Zafonte RD, Weisskopf MG, Baggish AL. Career Duration and Later-Life Health Conditions Among Former Professional American-Style Football Players. Occupational and Environmental Medicine. 2024;0:1-9.
abstract: This survey-based cross-sectional cohort study of 4,189 former professional American-style football (ASF) players examined associations between self-reported career duration and four self-reported health conditions: chronic pain, arthritis, mental health problems, and subjective cognitive symptoms. Using flexible regression with penalized B-splines to model non-linear relationships, the study found inverted U-shaped associations between career duration and all four health outcomes (all p<0.001), indicating adverse health effects were most common among men with intermediate career durations. Similar non-linear patterns were observed for four play-related exposures including concussion signs and symptoms, performance-enhancing drug use, intracareer surgeries, and average snaps per game. Findings challenge the assumption that career duration is a reliable linear proxy for neurotrauma exposure.
tldr: Former professional football players with intermediate career lengths (not the shortest or longest) showed the highest risk of chronic pain, arthritis, mental health problems, and cognitive symptoms, revealing an inverted U-shaped relationship that challenges the assumption that longer careers mean worse health outcomes. Researchers and clinicians should avoid using career duration as a simple linear proxy for cumulative health risk in former football players.
methods: Survey-based cross-sectional cohort study from the Football Players Health Study (FPHS) at Harvard University. Former professional ASF players (n=4,189; mean age 52±14 years; 39% Black, 34% lineman) recruited via residential and electronic mail. Health outcomes (chronic pain, arthritis, mental health problems, subjective cognitive symptoms via Neuro-QoL) self-reported. Career exposures included concussion signs and symptoms (CSS) score, performance-enhancing drug use, intracareer surgeries, and average snaps per game. Flexible regression with penalized B-splines and piecewise linear regression used to characterize non-linear career duration-outcome relationships. Models adjusted for age, race, and lineman status.
findings:
• Inverted U-shaped relationships observed between career duration and chronic pain, arthritis, mental health problems, and subjective cognitive symptoms (all p<0.001).
• Players with 4–8 seasons had significantly higher odds of chronic pain (OR 1.21; 95% CI 1.02–1.42; p=0.02) and arthritis compared to those with 0–3 seasons.
• Players with 13+ seasons had similar or lower odds of adverse health outcomes compared to the 0–3 season reference group.
• Piecewise regression identified breakpoints at 6.9 seasons (chronic pain), 9.5 (arthritis), 4.5 (mental health), and 7.0 (cognitive symptoms) where slope reversed.
• Similar inverted-U patterns observed for all four football-specific exposures (CSS score, snaps per game, performance-enhancing drugs, intracareer surgeries).
• Findings were consistent across lineman and non-lineman subgroups.
limitations: All outcomes and exposures were self-reported, lacking clinical confirmation. Recall bias possible, though findings were consistent with intracareer surgery data (salient events less subject to recall bias). Healthy worker survivor bias (HWSB) may explain attenuation in longer-career players: those remaining active longest may be biologically or psychologically more resilient. Snap data available for only a subset (n=1,980). Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference.
practicalImplications: Career duration is a poor and potentially misleading proxy for cumulative health risk in former ASF players. Clinicians and researchers should account for non-linear associations and consider using more direct exposure metrics (e.g., cumulative head impact index, total snaps). Medical providers caring for former players should not assume those with the longest careers are necessarily at greatest health risk — mid-career range players may carry the highest burden. Screening and intervention programs should target all career-length groups.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL and professional American football. The finding that mid-range career players (roughly 5–12 seasons) show the highest health burden suggests that player development and athlete longevity programs should focus on reducing cumulative physical and neurological exposure during the middle portion of careers, not only at career endpoints. Monitoring concussion symptom burden and surgical load as ongoing exposure metrics may be more informative than career length alone.
rtp: Not a RTP study. However, the non-linear relationship between career duration and health outcomes — driven partly by concussion sign/symptom accumulation and intracareer surgeries — underscores that cumulative injury burden across a career significantly shapes long-term health. RTP decisions should consider cumulative exposure history, not just the presenting injury, and total career injury load should be a factor in ongoing RTP and load management frameworks.

citation: Khalil LS, Jildeh TR, Abbas MJ, McIntosh MJ, Sokoli A, Cominos N, Okoroha KR. Career Longevity and Performance Following Shoulder Instability in National Football League Athletes. Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic and Related Surgery. 2021. doi: 10.1016/j.arthro.2020.12.225.
abstract: This retrospective case-control study investigated career longevity, game utilization, and performance of NFL athletes following glenohumeral instability events treated operatively versus nonoperatively from 2000 to 2019. Ninety-seven NFL players sustained a first-time shoulder instability event; 91 (93.8%) returned to play. Final analysis included 58 operatively managed and 33 nonoperatively managed players. Operatively managed players played significantly more seasons following RTP (4.1±2.7 vs. 2.8±2.5; P=0.015) and experienced less recurrent instability (27% vs. 50%; P=0.035) with longer intervals before recurrence (105.7±100.1 vs. 24.7±40.6 weeks; P<0.001). No significant differences existed in games played, games started, snap counts, or performance (SAV) between cohorts.
tldr: NFL players who undergo surgical stabilization following shoulder instability return to play with similar workload and performance as nonoperatively managed players but experience greater career longevity, fewer recurrent instability events, and longer time between instability events. Nonoperative management offers faster initial RTP but is associated with higher recurrence risk and shorter careers.
methods: Retrospective case-control study. NFL players with first shoulder instability events from September 2000 to February 2019 identified via public internet sources (team websites, injury reports, sports news). Inclusion required prior NFL experience, RTP after injury, and no prior ipsilateral upper extremity surgery. Players categorized as operative or nonoperative based on final treatment at end of index season. Outcomes: seasons played post-RTP, games played/started, offensive/defensive snap count percentage (OSCP/DSCP), season approximate value (SAV), recurrent instability rate, and interval to recurrence. SAV collected from Pro Football Reference. Statistical analysis: t-tests, Wilcoxon rank sum, chi-square, Fisher's exact, repeated measures ANOVA; significance p<0.05.
findings:
• 97 NFL players with first shoulder instability identified; 91 (93.8%) returned to play.
• Quarterbacks significantly more likely to undergo immediate surgery vs. other positions (P=0.023).
• Operative group played significantly more seasons post-RTP (4.1±2.7 vs. 2.8±2.5; P=0.015).
• Time to RTP significantly longer for operative group (36.62±10.32 vs. 5.43±12.33 weeks; P<0.05).
• Recurrent instability significantly lower in operative group (27% vs. 50%; P=0.035).
• Interval to recurrent instability significantly longer in operative group (105.7±100.1 vs. 24.7±40.6 weeks; P<0.001).
• No significant differences in games played, games started, OSCP, DSCP, or SAV between operative and nonoperative cohorts.
• Performance metrics (SAV) did not significantly change before vs. after injury for either cohort.
limitations: Retrospective design with reliance on publicly available data may introduce reporting inaccuracies. Players who did not return to play were excluded, potentially introducing selection bias toward more successful outcomes. Treatment group classification relied on news reports and absence of in-game data rather than surgical records. Limited ability to account for rehabilitation quality, team medical staff variation, or player motivation. Level of evidence: III.
practicalImplications: For NFL athletes with glenohumeral instability, surgical stabilization provides meaningful career longevity benefits and significantly reduces recurrent instability without compromising performance upon return. Medical staff should counsel players that operative management involves a longer initial recovery (~9 months vs. ~1 week nonoperative) but substantially reduces the likelihood of reinjury (27% vs. 50%) and supports a longer career. Quarterbacks appear particularly likely to elect surgery, likely reflecting the high performance demands of the position.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL football. Shoulder instability prevalence in NFL players is reported at 10–14%. Defensive players represent the largest proportion requiring surgical management. Position-specific shoulder stability demands should inform strength and conditioning programming, particularly for defensive players and quarterbacks. Pre-participation screening for shoulder laxity and prior instability may help identify players at elevated risk requiring prophylactic programming.
rtp: Directly relevant to RTP decision-making. Nonoperative management allows faster initial RTP (~5 weeks) but carries 50% recurrent instability risk and shorter careers (2.8 vs. 4.1 post-injury seasons). Surgical stabilization requires ~37-week RTP timeline but reduces recurrence to 27% and extends career longevity. Staff should use shared decision-making weighing the team's season timeline against the player's career investment. Performance outcomes (snap count %, SAV) are equivalent regardless of management choice, supporting that full return to prior performance level is achievable via both pathways.

citation: Muench JR, Sinson GP, Glaeser B, Shah AS, Rowson S, Duma S, Mihalik J, Brooks A, Cameron K, Giza C, Goldman J, McGinty G, Riggen LD, Pasquina PF, Broglio SP, McAllister T, McCrea MA, Harezlak J, Stemper BD. Change in Head Impact Exposure Following Return-to-Sport in Concussed Football Athletes. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2025. doi: 10.1249/MSS.0000000000003833.
abstract: This study examined how head impact exposure (HIE) changed during return-to-sport (RTS) following sport-related concussion in 52 concussed NCAA Division I college football athletes from six programs (2015–2019 seasons) using the helmet-based Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System. Daily volume of head impacts after concussion was compared to pre-injury levels and to team- and position-matched non-concussed controls. Seventy-five percent of concussed athletes reduced HIE in their immediate RTS period compared to pre-injury levels, and over 40% never reached their pre-injury HIE at any point during the remainder of the concussion season. Concussed athletes had significantly lower HIE post-RTS compared to matched controls, and longer recovery duration was associated with larger reductions in HIE after RTS.
tldr: Most concussed college football athletes naturally reduce their head impact exposure upon returning to sport within the same season — 75% immediately reduced impacts and 40% never returned to pre-injury exposure levels — with longer recovery periods associated with larger post-RTS impact reductions. These findings suggest a natural conservative shift in playing behavior after concussion that may offer some neurological protection during the vulnerable post-injury window.
methods: Subset of NCAA-DoD CARE Consortium data from six NCAA Division I FBS programs (Air Force Academy, West Point, UCLA, UNC Chapel Hill, University of Wisconsin, Virginia Tech). Fifty-two concussed college football athletes with recorded contact days both before and after concussion within the same season were included; only first concussion analyzed. Each athlete equipped with helmet-based HIT System measuring head accelerations (>10 g threshold). Head impact data collected across all practice, scrimmage, and competition activities (2015–2019 seasons). Three time periods analyzed: Pre-Injury, First 7 Days Post-RTS, and >7 Days Post-RTS. Daily HIE compared to pre-injury baseline and to team- and position-matched non-concussed controls. Regression analysis used to examine association between recovery duration and change in HIE. Preseason and regular season segments analyzed separately.
findings:
• 75% of concussed athletes reduced HIE in their immediate RTS period compared to pre-injury levels.
• Over 40% of concussed athletes did not reach their pre-injury HIE level at any point during the remainder of the concussion season.
• Concussed athletes had significantly lower HIE post-RTS compared to team- and position-matched non-concussed controls over the same time period.
• Longer post-concussion recovery times were significantly associated with larger reductions in HIE after RTS.
• The reduction in HIE was most pronounced in the immediate days following RTS (First 7 Days Post-RTS window).
• Findings are consistent with the broader shift toward more conservative post-concussion management seen across multiple sports and playing levels.
limitations: Study limited to NCAA Division I FBS programs, reducing generalizability to non-elite or youth populations. Only athletes with recorded contact days both before and after concussion within the same season included, which may exclude more severely concussed athletes. HIE measured only via HIT System accelerometers (linear acceleration); rotational components not fully captured. Psychological factors (fear of reinjury, playing style changes) were not directly measured. Only first concussion per athlete included, limiting examination of repeat concussion effects.
practicalImplications: Concussed athletes naturally reduce head impact exposure upon RTS, which aligns with conservative post-concussion management guidelines and may contribute to neurological recovery. Medical staff should be aware that while most athletes self-reduce their impact exposure post-RTS, over 40% still do not return to pre-injury baseline — a finding that may warrant ongoing HIE monitoring even after clinical clearance. Longer recovery periods appear to reinforce more conservative playing behavior, suggesting that appropriate recovery time has biomechanical as well as clinical benefits.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college and professional football. The finding that athletes naturally reduce head impact exposure after RTS suggests a behavioral adaptation that may reduce re-concussion risk during the vulnerable post-injury window. Athlete development staff and sports scientists should track HIE in the immediate post-RTS period, particularly in the first 7 days, to identify athletes who are not reducing impact exposure as expected and may need additional guidance or load modification.
rtp: Directly relevant to RTP protocols. The study provides objective biomechanical data showing that most concussed football athletes reduce HIE upon RTS within the same season, supporting the neurobiological rationale for gradual RTS protocols. The association between longer recovery and greater HIE reduction post-RTS suggests that premature RTP may not only increase re-injury risk clinically but also biomechanically (less HIE reduction). Monitoring head impact exposure in the first 7 days post-RTS may serve as a useful objective metric to complement standard neurocognitive and symptom-based RTP criteria.

citation: Dairi I, Brown C, DiGregorio H, Wasfy M, Baggish A, Pitler L, Copen WA, Doyle M, Wu O, Zafonte R, Tenforde AS. A Case Report of Reversible Cognitive Decline in a Former Professional American-Style Football Player: Findings from the Football Players Health Study In-Person Assessments. Current Sports Medicine Reports. 2023;22(5):154-157.
abstract: This case report presents a 54-year-old former NFL linebacker enrolled in the Football Players Health Study (FPHS) at Harvard who had been diagnosed with early-onset frontotemporal dementia (FTD) and presented with cognitive impairment, urinary incontinence, and gait disturbance. Brain MRI obtained during the 3-day in-person research assessment revealed mild hydrocephalus rather than frontotemporal atrophy, prompting neurosurgical referral. Following ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement in February 2020, the patient experienced substantial improvement in mood and resolution of most cognitive complaints at 2-year follow-up.
tldr: Not all cognitive decline in former football players represents irreversible neurodegeneration. This case demonstrates that hydrocephalus — a treatable structural condition — can mimic frontotemporal dementia in a former professional player, and that thorough clinical evaluation including brain MRI can identify and redirect care toward curative treatment. NFL and college football medical staffs should ensure that post-career cognitive evaluations include brain imaging and systematic workup for treatable conditions (hydrocephalus, sleep apnea, depression, cardiometabolic disease) before attributing impairment to CTE or irreversible head-trauma sequelae.
methods: Single case report from the Football Players Health Study (FPHS) at Harvard University in-person assessment protocol. The participant was a 54-year-old former professional NFL linebacker (4 seasons) assessed in January 2020. The 3-day evaluation included telephone cognitive screening (T-MoCA-22), brain MRI, EEG, sleep study (polysomnography), cardiovascular testing (ECG, echocardiogram, peak VO2), laboratory panels (lipids, vitamins, hormones), and a structured exit interview with a study physician. Follow-up interview was conducted by study nurse in September 2022.
findings: T-MoCA score was 17, indicating mild cognitive impairment. Brain MRI showed mild enlargement of the third and lateral ventricles with balloon-like rounding of the frontal horns consistent with mild hydrocephalus; no frontotemporal atrophy was present. EEG showed nonspecific cortical slowing of indeterminate etiology. Sleep study indicated moderate sleep apnea. Peak VO2 was 23.4 mL/kg/min (73% predicted). Laboratory testing was notable for low HDL and low vitamin D. Following ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement, the patient reported substantial improvement in mood and near-resolution of cognitive complaints at 2-year follow-up.
limitations: Single case report limits generalizability. The prevalence of hydrocephalus among former NFL players has not been systematically studied. The 2-year gap between the research visit and the surgical intervention makes it difficult to attribute all improvements solely to the shunting procedure. The prior FTD diagnosis may have been influenced by the patient's football history and assumptions about head-trauma-related neurodegeneration rather than a thorough structural workup.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football medical staffs should not assume cognitive decline or behavioral changes in former players are attributable solely to CTE or irreversible neurodegeneration. Brain MRI should be obtained when evaluating players presenting with new or progressive cognitive complaints, gait disturbance, or urinary incontinence. Treatable conditions including hydrocephalus, sleep apnea, depression, and cardiometabolic disease should be systematically assessed before attributing impairment to football-related head trauma.
athleteDev: This case underscores the importance of comprehensive post-career health monitoring for former football players. Athletic development and player wellness programs should educate current players on the range of potentially treatable conditions that can affect cognitive function after playing careers, reducing fatalism about post-career brain health and encouraging proactive medical evaluation rather than automatic acceptance of a neurodegenerative diagnosis.
rtp: Not directly applicable as a return-to-play study. However, the case illustrates the importance of thorough differential diagnosis when a current player presents with cognitive symptoms, gait disturbance, or urinary incontinence — conditions that might be attributed to concussion history but could reflect a treatable structural etiology such as communicating hydrocephalus. MRI brain imaging should be considered in the workup of persistent post-concussion symptoms that do not resolve on expected timelines.

citation: Fitzgerald CF, Jensen RL. A Comparison of the National Football League's Annual National Football League Combine 1999-2000 to 2015-2016. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. 2020;34(3):771-781.
abstract: This cross-sectional study compared NFL Combine performance data from players across the 1999-2000 and 2015-2016 draft cycles, analyzing seven combine tests (40-yard dash, pro-agility shuttle, 225-lb bench press repetitions, vertical jump, broad jump, 3-cone drill) and anthropometric measurements across eight position groups (QB, RB, WR, TE, DL, OL, LB, DB). Two-way ANOVA revealed statistically significant improvements from 1999-2000 to 2015-2016 in 40-yard dash speed, vertical jump, broad jump, and 3-cone drill times, with no significant changes in height or overall body mass, suggesting elite prospects have become faster and more explosive without significant size increases.
tldr: NFL Combine prospects measurably improved in speed (40-yard dash), lower-body power (vertical and broad jump), and agility (3-cone drill) from 1999-2000 to 2015-2016, suggesting that elite football player physical qualities have trended upward over nearly two decades. For strength and conditioning coaches and scouts, the longitudinal normative benchmarks by position provide a historical baseline for combine preparation targets. The finding that broad jump improved at virtually every position suggests that lower-body explosive training should be a universal priority across all NFL positional development programs.
methods: Cross-sectional retrospective analysis comparing NFL Combine performance data from the 1999-2000 draft class (n=635) and the 2015-2016 draft class (n=628) using a two-way non-repeated ANOVA. Data were obtained from public NFL historical databases. Performance variables included the 40-yard dash, pro-agility shuttle, 225-lb bench press repetitions, vertical jump, broad jump, 3-cone drill, height, and body mass. Participants were separated by position group: QB, RB, WR, TE, DL, OL, LB, and DB. Punters, kickers, and long snappers were excluded. Statistical significance set at p<0.05.
findings: Significant improvements from 1999-2000 to 2015-2016 were found for: 40-yard dash (4.85 to 4.80 s, p=0.002), vertical jump (82.04 to 83.46 cm, p=0.028), broad jump (282.88 to 292.18 cm, p<0.001), and 3-cone drill (7.41 to 7.29 s, p<0.001). No significant differences were found for height or overall body mass. Position-specific significant improvements in 40-yard dash were found for RBs, WRs, OLs, and DBs. QB body mass and WR body mass increased significantly. Broad jump showed significant improvement at nearly every position group. Bench press repetitions did not differ significantly between time points.
limitations: Data obtained from public online databases, which may contain entry errors. Not all combine invitees complete every test, creating variable sample sizes across measures. Punters, kickers, and long snappers excluded, limiting generalizability to all roster positions. Body composition (body fat percentage) was not directly measured. The two time-point comparison cannot establish causal mechanisms for observed physical changes (e.g., training advances, nutrition science, population changes).
practicalImplications: NFL and college football strength and conditioning staffs can use these longitudinal normative benchmarks to set position-specific combine preparation targets and evaluate where current athletes stand relative to historically draftable prospects. The consistent improvement in broad jump across positions reinforces the value of prioritizing lower-body explosive training in all positional development programs. Coaches can identify position-specific physical qualities where gaps between current athletes and combine norms are largest.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football athlete development and NFL Draft preparation. Speed and lower-body power improvements across positions from 1999-2000 to 2015-2016 indicate that multi-year explosive training programs can produce measurable gains at the elite scouting level. Programs can use position-specific normative data to set precise development targets for the 40-yard dash, broad jump, vertical jump, and agility drills, and track longitudinal progress toward draftable benchmarks.
rtp: Not the primary focus of this study. However, position-specific combine performance benchmarks could inform return-to-play physical readiness standards following injury by providing normative values for speed, agility, and power metrics used during NFL pre-participation evaluations, helping clinicians and coaches determine when a rehabilitating athlete has returned to pre-injury or draft-standard physical capacity.

citation: Krill MK, Borchers JR, Hoffman JT, Krill ML, Hewett TE. Analysis of Football Injuries by Position Group in Division I College Football: A 5-Year Program Review. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine. 2020;30(3):216-223.
abstract: This prospective observational study evaluated injury characteristics by position group (line, skill, other) in a single Division I collegiate football program over 5 fall regular seasons (2012-2016), recording 200 injuries from 48,615 athlete exposures (AEs). Injury rates (IRs) per 1,000 AEs and injury rate ratios (IRRs) were calculated across all monitored injury variables including time in season, body part, mechanism, injury type, and exposure type. Skill players demonstrated significantly higher IRs in the preseason, for noncontact mechanisms, and for upper leg/thigh injuries compared to line and other position groups.
tldr: Skill position players (DBs, WRs, RBs) carry the highest injury risk in Division I college football, especially during preseason from noncontact mechanisms targeting the upper leg and thigh, while linemen and hybrid positions sustain most injuries in the first half of the regular season from contact. For football medical and strength staffs, the clearest actionable finding is that preseason hamstring and quadriceps strain prevention in speed-position players represents the greatest single opportunity to reduce overall team injury rates. Reducing live contact exposures will have the greatest impact on concussions and contact injuries for linemen and other position groups.
methods: Prospective observational study of a single major Division I collegiate football program during the 2012-2016 fall regular seasons, including all games and practices. Attendance and injury data were prospectively collected by team physicians and certified athletic trainers using the NCAA Injury Surveillance System (ISS) reportable injury definition. Players classified into three position groups: line (OL/DL), skill (DBs, WRs, RBs), and other (LBs, TEs, QBs, special teams). Exposure types (noncontact, full-pad, live contact) were tracked per NCAA definitions starting in 2014. IRs per 1,000 AEs with 95% CIs and IRRs using Mid-P exact tests were calculated. Statistical significance set at p<0.05.
findings: 200 injuries from 48,615 AEs. Overall 5-year IR: 4.11/1,000 AEs (95% CI 3.57-4.72). Skill players had the highest IR (5.18/1,000 AEs) vs. line (3.62) and other (3.27). Preseason IR: skill 7.56 vs. line 4.26 and other 4.10 (skill vs. line IRR 1.75, skill vs. other IRR 1.85, both p<0.05). Skill players had significantly higher IRR for noncontact MOI vs. line (IRR 2.07, p=0.004) and other (IRR 3.03, p<0.001). Upper leg/thigh injuries: skill vs. line IRR 6.91 (p=0.002), skill vs. other IRR 6.07 (p=0.005). Game IR (11.05/1,000 AEs) significantly higher than practice IR (3.14). Sprains, strains, and concussions were the three most common injury types; head and knee were the most common body parts injured (19.9% each). No significant differences in IRs between position groups by exposure type.
limitations: Single-institution study limits generalizability across all NCAA programs. Athlete exposures by position were estimated using weighted averages from roster data rather than individual daily position tracking. Weather, playing surface, and other environmental variables not recorded. Exposure-type analysis covers only 3 seasons (2014-2016). Broad position group categorization may mask individual position-level differences (e.g., within the skill group between WRs and RBs).
practicalImplications: Preseason soft-tissue strain prevention programs targeting skill position players should be prioritized as the single greatest opportunity to reduce overall team IR. Hamstring and quadriceps screening, load management, and evidence-based eccentric training during fall camp are critical intervention points. Reducing live contact exposures will have the greatest impact on concussions and contact injuries for linemen and other positions. Position-specific warm-up and neuromuscular training protocols should reflect the distinct injury profiles of each group.
athleteDev: Skill position players (speed/agility athletes — DBs, WRs, RBs) require targeted preseason conditioning and soft-tissue injury prevention programs. Gradual preseason volume progression, adequate recovery between high-intensity sessions, and progressive hamstring and quadriceps loading are essential during fall camp. Strength and conditioning staff should monitor preseason training loads for skill positions more closely than for linemen in the first weeks of fall practice, as 90.9% of all upper leg/thigh strains in the preseason were sustained by skill players.
rtp: The high incidence of upper leg/thigh strains in skill position players during preseason provides context for return-to-sport decision-making in college football. Athletes returning from lower extremity injury who re-enter full contact during preseason face elevated risk due to the already elevated preseason IR for skill positions. RTP progressions for hamstring and quadriceps strains should include sequential exposure-type progression (noncontact to full-pad to live contact) aligned with NCAA definitions before full clearance.

citation: Coburn SL, Crossley KM, Kemp JL, Gassert F, Luitjens J, Warden SJ, Culvenor AG, Scholes MJ, King MG, Lawrenson P, Link T, Heerey JJ. Association between hip muscle strength/function and hip cartilage defects in sub-elite football players with hip/groin pain. Osteoarthritis and Cartilage. 2024. doi:10.1016/j.joca.2024.03.121
abstract: This cross-sectional exploratory study used baseline data from the FORCe cohort to examine associations between isometric hip muscle strength, functional task performance, and MRI-assessed hip cartilage defects in 182 sub-elite soccer and Australian football players (37 women, mean age 26±7 years) with hip/groin pain lasting more than 6 months. Hip cartilage defects were assessed using the SHOMRI scoring tool across 10 femoral and acetabular subregions. Overall, few associations were found between hip strength or functional task performance and cartilage defects, with the notable exceptions that greater hip extension strength was associated with higher cartilage defect severity scores, higher one-leg rise performance was linked to lower odds of superomedial cartilage defects, and in females greater external rotation strength was associated with lateral cartilage defects.
tldr: In young sub-elite football players with chronic hip/groin pain, overall isometric hip strength is not strongly linked to hip cartilage health on MRI. The most actionable finding for football medical staff is that better one-leg rise performance was associated with fewer superomedial cartilage defects, suggesting this functional test may serve as a clinically useful indicator of hip cartilage status. Greater hip extension strength was paradoxically associated with worse cartilage scores — likely reflecting that stronger athletes load the joint more during high-intensity activity — not that strengthening causes harm. Hip muscle strengthening should remain a rehabilitation priority for symptom management, but clinicians should not use strength measures alone to infer cartilage health.
methods: Cross-sectional study using baseline data from the FORCe (Femoroacetabular Impingement and Hip OsteoaRthritis Cohort) study. Participants: 182 sub-elite soccer and Australian football players (146 men, 38 women; age 26±7 years) with hip/groin pain >6 months recruited August 2015-October 2018 in Melbourne and Brisbane, Australia. Isometric hip strength assessed bilaterally in 6 directions using a hand-held dynamometer (JTECH Commander). Functional tasks: one-leg rise (repetitions), side-bridge endurance (seconds), hop for distance (cm). Hip cartilage defects assessed on 3.0T MRI (Phillips Ingenia) using SHOMRI scoring (10 subregions) by two blinded musculoskeletal radiologists with consensus read. Logistic regression and negative binomial regression with GEE controlled for BMI, age, test site, cam morphology, and sex. Sex-specific interaction terms tested.
findings: 58% of symptomatic hips (n=288) had cartilage defects; most prevalent sites: superolateral (41%), lateral femoral (29%), superomedial (22%); 35% had defects in 2+ subregions. Greater hip extension strength (per 10 N) was associated with higher cartilage total score (aIRR 1.01, 95%CI 1.0-1.02, p=0.013) and superolateral defects (aOR 1.03, 95%CI 1.01-1.06, p<0.01). Greater one-leg rise repetitions were associated with lower odds of superomedial defects (aOR 0.96, 95%CI 0.94-0.99, p<0.01). In women only: greater external rotation strength was associated with lateral cartilage defects (aOR 1.61, 95%CI 1.05-2.48, p=0.03) and higher cartilage total score (aIRR 1.25, 95%CI 1.01-1.66, p=0.042). Intra- and inter-rater reliability for SHOMRI was almost perfect (kappas 0.86-0.94 intra-rater; 0.96 inter-rater).
limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference; the relationship between cartilage and strength could be interpreted in either direction. The proportion of women (38/182) limited power for sex-stratified analyses. Multiple regression models increase Type I error risk. Pain during strength testing may have influenced strength values. Acetabular morphology not fully characterized (acetabular retroversion excluded). Findings may not generalize to elite, asymptomatic, or non-football athletes.
practicalImplications: Football medical and performance staff should not rely on isolated hip strength testing to assess cartilage health in players with chronic hip/groin pain. The one-leg rise test may offer added clinical value as a functional indicator of superomedial cartilage status and should be incorporated into hip/groin injury assessments. Hip muscle strengthening remains an important component of rehabilitation for symptom management and functional recovery even though its direct relationship to structural cartilage protection is unclear.
athleteDev: Football players with persistent hip/groin pain have a high prevalence of underlying cartilage defects (58% of symptomatic hips), with half of these occurring in multiple subregions. Monitoring hip muscle strength and one-leg rise performance during pre-season screening may help identify athletes at higher risk for hip OA progression. Balancing hip extensor and external rotator strengthening with appropriate load management is important for players with cam morphology or persistent groin symptoms to avoid compounding joint loading.
rtp: Players returning from hip/groin injury should be assessed with functional tasks including the one-leg rise, as performance on this test is associated with reduced superomedial cartilage defect odds. Given the high prevalence of underlying cartilage changes in sub-elite football players with hip/groin pain, MRI evaluation should be considered for players with recurrent or persistent symptoms before full return to training. Return-to-play criteria should incorporate functional task benchmarks alongside pain resolution and strength symmetry.

citation: Geiss Santos RC, Van Hellemnondt F, Yamashiro E, Holtzhausen L, Serner A, Farooq A, Whiteley R, Tol JL. Association Between Injury Mechanisms and Magnetic Resonance Imaging Findings in Rectus Femoris Injuries in 105 Professional Football Players. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine. 2021;00:1-6.
abstract: This combined retrospective (January 2010 to October 2013) and prospective (October 2013 to January 2019) descriptive injury study examined 105 MRI-confirmed acute rectus femoris injuries in male professional football (soccer) players from the Qatar Stars League, assessed at Aspetar Hospital within 7 days of injury. Injury mechanisms were categorized as kicking (54.3%), sprinting (30.4%), or other (15.2%). MRI findings were classified by location (proximal free tendon, myotendinous junction, myofascial) and grade (1-3). A significant association was found between injury mechanism and MRI location (p<0.001): all 20 proximal free tendon injuries were exclusively caused by kicking, and 75% of those were complete tears, while sprinting injuries never involved the proximal free tendon.
tldr: In professional football players, kicking is the dominant mechanism for rectus femoris injuries (54%) and is the exclusive cause of all proximal free tendon ruptures — 75% of which are complete tears requiring a fundamentally different clinical approach than the MTJ strains caused by sprinting. For American football athletic trainers and sports medicine staff, the critical takeaway is that any anterior thigh injury occurring during a kicking motion (kickers, punters, or field players) warrants urgent MRI and high suspicion for a complete proximal free tendon rupture, not a routine quadriceps strain. This distinction changes surgical decision-making, rehabilitation protocol, and return-to-play timeline substantially.
methods: Combined retrospective cohort (January 2010-October 2013) and prospective cohort (October 2013-January 2019) descriptive injury study conducted at Aspetar Orthopedic and Sports Medicine Hospital, Doha, Qatar. Participants: male professional football players >18 years from the Qatar Stars League, referred within 7 days of acute indirect rectus femoris injury with positive MRI finding. MRI conducted using 1.5T (Siemens Espree) or 3.0T (GE Discovery 750w) with standard STIR and T2 fat-saturated sequences. Injury mechanism categorized by clinical history as kicking, sprinting, or other. MRI graded 1-3 at three anatomical locations: free tendon (direct, indirect, conjoined), myotendinous junction/intramuscular tendon, and myofascial. Free tendon injuries classified as complete or partial. Chi-square tests assessed categorical associations; significance p<0.05.
findings: 105 injuries: kicking 57 (54.3%), sprinting 32 (30.4%), other 16 (15.2%). MRI locations: free tendon 20 (19.0%), myotendinous junction 67 (63.8%), myofascial 18 (17.1%). Significant association between injury mechanism and MRI location (p<0.001). All 20 proximal free tendon injuries were exclusively from kicking; 15/20 (75%) were complete tears of at least one tendon. Sprinting was never associated with proximal free tendon injury. MTJ injuries: kicking 29 (50.9%), sprinting 26 (81.3%), other 12 (75.0%). No significant association between MRI grade and mechanism (p=0.155). Grade distribution at MTJ/myofascial: grade 1 32 (37.6%), grade 2 43 (50.6%), grade 3 10 (11.8%). Central intramuscular tendon disruption present in 25.4% of MTJ cases; degloving injuries in 8.9%. Dominant leg involved in 58% of injuries.
limitations: Cohort limited to professional players in one country (Qatar), limiting generalizability to other settings, youth players, and American football. Injury mechanism was recorded by clinical history only — no video analysis was performed, which may introduce recall bias. Negative MRI (grade 0) cases were excluded. Adolescent players at risk for apophyseal avulsion injuries were not included. Combining retrospective and prospective data is a design limitation. Return-to-play outcomes, clinical scores, and rehabilitation duration were not captured.
practicalImplications: American football kickers, punters, and any player sustaining an anterior thigh injury during a kicking action should be evaluated urgently with MRI, with high clinical suspicion for a complete proximal free tendon rupture. This is categorically different from a typical quadriceps or hamstring strain and requires a different surgical and rehabilitation pathway. Players sustaining quad injuries during sprinting will almost always have an MTJ injury, allowing more predictable conservative rehabilitation. Mechanism-specific clinical decision trees for rectus femoris injury should be implemented in NFL and college football sideline and clinic protocols.
athleteDev: Rectus femoris injury prevention for American football should account for mechanism-specific loading demands. Kicking mechanics place unique eccentric demands on the proximal free tendon that differ fundamentally from sprint-related loading of the myotendinous junction. Kicker and punter development programs should include progressive eccentric quadriceps and hip flexor loading, and kicking volume should be carefully managed during preseason when fatigue is high. Skill position players benefit most from programs addressing sprint-related MTJ resilience via eccentric hamstring and quad loading.
rtp: Return-to-play timelines for rectus femoris injuries must be stratified by injury mechanism and confirmed MRI location. Proximal free tendon complete ruptures (exclusive to kicking mechanism, 75% of all free tendon injuries) require substantially longer recovery — potentially surgical intervention — compared to myotendinous junction strains from sprinting. Applying a uniform RTP timeline for all quadriceps strains will systematically underestimate recovery time for kicking-mechanism injuries. MRI within 7 days of injury is essential for accurate diagnosis and RTP planning.

citation: Majano C, Garcia-Unanue J, Fernández-Cuevas I, Escamilla-Galindo V, Alonso-Callejo A, Sanchez-Sanchez J, Gallardo L, Felipe JL. Association between physical demands, skin temperature and wellbeing status in elite football players. Scientific Reports. 2023;13:13780. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-023-40396-y
abstract: This study examined the relationship between physical demands (GPS), skin temperature assessed via infrared thermography (IRT), and wellbeing status in 30 elite male football players over a 12-week competitive period (21/22 season). Thermography was performed three days post-match using a FLIR T435bx camera, and players were grouped into high (≥0.3°C) and low (<0.3°C) thermal asymmetry clusters across 44 bilateral lower-limb regions of interest. High-intensity actions were associated with greater thermal asymmetries in the thighs, while hamstring and adductor asymmetries correlated with reduced high-intensity outputs and worse wellness scores (stress, soreness, rest).
tldr: Infrared thermography can objectively reveal which muscles are under the most stress from high-intensity training and match loads in elite football players. High hamstring thermal asymmetry is linked to fewer high-intensity actions completed, adductor asymmetry correlates with worse stress and muscle soreness, and knee asymmetry is associated with more fatigue and worse rest quality. Coaches and sports scientists should integrate routine thermography alongside GPS and wellness questionnaires to proactively identify athletes at elevated injury or recovery risk—particularly targeting the hamstrings, adductors, and knees—and individualize training load reductions accordingly.
methods: Longitudinal observational study of 30 male professional football players from a Smartbank League (Spanish second division) club during a 12-week competitive period (14 February–11 May, 21/22 season). Age: 25.37±3.60 years; BMI: 23.06±1.04; VO2peak: 61.40±2.93 ml/min/kg. Thermography was performed with a FLIR T435bx camera in a controlled room (22°C±1.5°C, 40–60% relative humidity) using the Glamorgan protocol; images analyzed with ThermoHuman software v2.21 across 44 bilateral ROIs on the lower limbs. GPS movement data were collected with WIMU PRO devices (18 Hz), capturing 20+ variables including HSR (>21.1 km/h), accelerations/decelerations (≥3 m/s²), sprints (>24 km/h), HMLD, and ACWR ratios. A digital wellness questionnaire (RPE, stress, rest time, rest quality, muscle soreness on 1–10 scales) was completed weekly. Players trained 5×/week and participated in 11 official matches across 11 competitive microcycles. Players were grouped into high asymmetry (HA, ≥0.3°C) and low asymmetry (LA, <0.3°C) clusters for each ROI and each microcycle.
findings: High-intensity actions (distance at HSR, accelerations, sprints, Vmax) were significantly associated with higher thermal asymmetry in the thigh muscles. The high hamstring asymmetry group completed significantly fewer sprints, HSR, and distances between 18–21 km/h and 21–24 km/h compared to the low asymmetry group (ES: 0.22–0.42). Adductor high-asymmetry players showed higher stress, higher muscle soreness, and less rest (ES: 0.32–0.45). Knee high-asymmetry was associated with more fatigue and worse rest and rest quality (ES: 0.35–0.41). Positive correlations existed between thigh asymmetry and high-intensity GPS loads; negative correlations between hamstring asymmetry and HSR distance, 21–24 km/h distance, and sprints. Acute-chronic workload ratios showed negative correlations with thigh asymmetry and positive correlations with hamstring asymmetry. High-intensity acceleration and deceleration distance decreased across the 11 microcycles (~1,250 m to ~600 m), consistent with a tapering effect.
limitations: The asymmetry cut-off (0.3°C) was set by club policy rather than the published clinical standard (0.5°C), which may reduce specificity and limit comparisons with other studies. The sample was small (n=30), exclusively male, and from a single club, limiting external validity. Weather variability and individual thermoregulatory differences could not be fully controlled. Psychological factors (stress, anxiety) may independently influence skin temperature readings. Dietary and nutritional variations affecting metabolic rate were not accounted for. Causal directionality between asymmetry and workload cannot be established from an observational design.
practicalImplications: Routine IRT screening three days post-match provides an objective, non-invasive complement to GPS and wellness data. Players flagged with elevated thermal asymmetry in the hamstrings, adductors, or knees should have training intensity reviewed—particularly high-speed running and acceleration/deceleration volumes. Adductor asymmetry warrants particular attention given its association with worse stress and recovery scores. Performance staff can design individualized recovery modalities (e.g., cold water immersion, massage) based on specific thermal hotspots identified by IRT.
athleteDev: Thermography provides a cost-effective and non-invasive tool for tracking load-related muscle stress during high-intensity training phases in developing football players. Players performing large sprint and acceleration volumes—common in American football speed and conditioning programs—can be monitored bilaterally to detect asymmetries before subjective soreness is reported. Coaches can use this data to customize training loads and recovery interventions by individual muscle group.
rtp: Elevated thermal asymmetry in the hamstrings post-match or post-training may indicate elevated neuromuscular stress warranting modified loads before full return to high-intensity training. The finding that high hamstring asymmetry correlates with reduced sprint and high-speed running capacity suggests these athletes should receive thermographic clearance alongside functional testing before full RTP. Adductor and knee asymmetry data may similarly inform phased RTP decisions, with persistent asymmetry suggesting incomplete tissue recovery.

citation: Massidda M, Flore L, Cugia P, Piras F, Scorcu M, Kikuchi N, Cieszczyk P, Maciejewska-Skrendo A, Tocco F, Calo CM. Association Between Total Genotype Score and Muscle Injuries in Top-Level Football Players: A Pilot Study. Sports Medicine - Open. 2024;10:22. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40798-024-00682-z
abstract: This pilot study investigated the combined effect of four gene polymorphisms—ACE I/D (rs4341), ACTN3 c.1729C>T (rs1815739), COL5A1 C>T (rs2722), and MCT1 c.1470A>T (rs1049434)—on muscle injury incidence in 64 elite Italian male professional football players (Serie A) followed for 10 years (2009–2019). A total genotype score (TGS) on a 0–100 arbitrary unit scale was calculated using the Williams and Folland polygenic method. Mean TGS was 63.7±13.0 a.u. in non-injured vs. 42.5±12.5 a.u. in injured players (p<0.001), and a TGS cut-off of 56.2 a.u. was identified with OR=3.5 (95%CI 1.8–6.8) for sustaining an injury.
tldr: A combined polygenic score (TGS) from four SNPs—ACE I/D, ACTN3 c.1729C>T, COL5A1 C>T, and MCT1 c.1470A>T—strongly predicted muscle injury incidence in elite Italian Serie A football players over a 10-year period. Players with TGS below 56.2 a.u. had 3.5x higher odds of injury, and the ACE DD, ACTN3 CC, COL5A1 CC, and MCT1 TT genotypes each conferred individual protection. This framework supports the future use of genetic risk profiling to individualize training load management and injury prevention strategies, with the long-term goal of adapting training volume, intensity, specificity, recovery, and density based on an athlete's genetic muscle injury risk profile.
methods: Longitudinal cohort study of 64 male professional football players (age 23.1±5.5 years; height 179.3±7.3 cm; weight 73.0±7.9 kg) from one Italian Serie A club. Muscle injury data were prospectively collected over 10 years (2009–2019) per UEFA consensus guidelines. Buccal swabs provided genomic DNA; PCR genotyping for four SNPs. Genotype scores of 2 (protective), 1 (heterozygous), or 0 (worst) were assigned per polymorphism and summed to compute TGS (0–100 scale). Injury severity was classified as minor (4–7 days), moderate (8–28 days), or major (>28 days). ROC curve analysis assessed TGS discriminatory accuracy for injury incidence and severity. Linear regression evaluated TGS as a continuous predictor. Analyses were conducted in SPSS v21.0 and Genepop v4.0.3; Bonferroni corrections applied.
findings: ACE I/D genotype frequency differed significantly between injured and non-injured players (p=0.001); MCT1 c.1470A>T (p=0.005) and ACTN3 c.1729C>T (p=0.016) also differed significantly. COL5A1 trended toward significance (p=0.079). Protective genotypes (ACE DD, ACTN3 CC, MCT1 TT) were associated with significantly lower injury incidence. Mean TGS: 63.7±13.0 a.u. (non-injured) vs. 42.5±12.5 a.u. (injured; p<0.001). ROC AUC=0.873 (95%CI 0.788–0.958; p<0.001; sensitivity=0.857, specificity=0.241). TGS threshold of 56.2 a.u. yielded OR=3.5 (95%CI 1.8–6.8; p<0.001) for injury. TGS was a significant predictor of injury incidence (R²=0.29, p<0.001) but not injury severity (R²=0.02; AUC=0.636, p=0.189). Mean injury incidence per 1,000 h: 2.28±2.80 for TGS>56.2 a.u. vs. 0.12±0.28 for TGS<56.2 a.u.
limitations: Small sample (n=64) from a single Italian club limits statistical power and generalizability to other populations or ethnic groups. Results are restricted to male Italian Caucasian players. Only four polymorphisms were analyzed; additional relevant SNPs (AMPD1, CKM, MLCK) shown to influence injury in non-Italian populations were not included. Injury incidence may be influenced by training type, playing surface, player age, and competition level. Conclusions need replication in larger independent cohorts before clinical translation.
practicalImplications: Genetic profiling using a TGS derived from ACE, ACTN3, COL5A1, and MCT1 polymorphisms may help identify professional football players at elevated muscle injury risk. Coaches and medical staff could use this information to individualize training load parameters—volume, intensity, specificity, recovery, and density—for genetically at-risk athletes. Players with TGS below approximately 56 a.u. warrant enhanced monitoring and potentially modified training loads, particularly during high-volume or high-intensity phases.
athleteDev: Polygenic risk scoring for muscle injuries has direct applications in American football player development. Players with unfavorable ACE, ACTN3, COL5A1, and MCT1 genotype combinations may benefit from earlier integration of injury prevention programs targeting the hamstrings and connective tissues. Genetic profiling could guide position-specific training load prescriptions and long-term athlete development pathways for NFL and collegiate football players.
rtp: Although TGS did not predict injury severity, it significantly predicted injury incidence, suggesting that players with lower TGS returning from muscle injury face elevated re-injury risk. Incorporating genetic profiling into RTP frameworks could support more conservative return timelines and enhanced monitoring for athletes with unfavorable polygenic profiles. Players with ACE II, ACTN3 TT, or MCT1 AA genotypes may warrant extended rehabilitation and reduced loading during the return-to-play continuum.

citation: Varillas-Delgado D. Association of Genetic Profile with Muscle Mass Gain and Muscle Injury Prevention in Professional Football Players after Creatine Supplementation. Nutrients. 2024;16:2511. https://doi.org/10.3390/nu16152511
abstract: This longitudinal study examined the relationship between a total genotype score (TGS) derived from six muscle-performance SNPs—ACE I/D, ACTN3 c.1729C>T, AMPD1 c.34C>T, CKM c.*800A>G, MLCK c.49C>T, and MLCK c.37885C>A—and changes in BMI, fat mass, and muscle mass in 161 professional male football players following a standardized creatine supplementation protocol during the 2022/2023 LaLiga season. The study also assessed TGS as a predictor of non-contact muscle injury risk during the season. Players with TGS above 54.16 a.u. had ~3x greater odds of gaining muscle mass (OR=2.985; p=0.001) and those below 54.16 a.u. had ~9x greater odds of sustaining a non-contact muscle injury (OR=9.385; p<0.001).
tldr: A player's polygenic profile—summarized as a TGS from six muscle-performance SNPs—significantly predicted both muscle mass response to creatine supplementation and non-contact muscle injury risk in 161 professional Spanish LaLiga football players. The AMPD1 CC genotype and the C allele were particularly linked to favorable muscle mass response under creatine loading. Players with lower TGS had drastically higher injury rates during the season. Performance staff should consider that genetic profiling alongside creatine supplementation may allow personalized supplementation strategies and targeted injury prevention programs for professional football players.
methods: Longitudinal study of 161 male professional football players (age 26.44±4.54 years; weight 71.64±7.23 kg; height 180.71±6.12 cm) from LaLiga Santander and LaLiga Smartbank clubs, 2022/2023 season. Six SNPs (ACE, ACTN3, AMPD1, CKM, MLCK×2) were genotyped via Single-Nucleotide Primer Extension (SNPE) with SNaPshot Multiplex Kit on ABI3500 capillary electrophoresis. TGS calculated on 0–100 a.u. scale. Creatine protocol: 20 g/day (5-day loading) followed by 3–5 g/day (7-week maintenance) of creatine monohydrate (Myprotein) under medical supervision. Anthropometric data (BMI, fat mass, muscle mass) collected before and after supplementation per ISAK guidelines (SECA 220 rod; Inbody 770). Injury data (non-contact lower limb muscle tears) recorded from October 2022 to May/June 2023. Responders vs. non-responders identified via smallest worthwhile change (SWC). ROC curves and binary logistic regression evaluated TGS discriminatory power. Statistical analysis: SPSS v21.0.
findings: Of 161 players, 41 (25.5%) were BMI responders and 68 (42.2%) were muscle mass responders after creatine supplementation. TGS was significantly higher in muscle mass responders (58.69±11.38 a.u.) vs. non-responders (51.25±12.28 a.u.; p<0.001). AUC for muscle mass response: 0.668 (95%CI 0.584–0.751; p<0.001); TGS threshold 54.16 a.u. yielded OR=2.985 (95%CI 1.560–5.711; p=0.001). AMPD1 c.34C>T CC genotype was significantly more frequent in muscle mass responders (78.0% vs. 59.2%; trend p=0.082 before correction). ACTN3 CC and CKM GG genotypes were also more frequent in BMI responders. TGS did not significantly predict fat mass reduction (AUC=0.591; p=0.096). Players with TGS below 54.16 a.u. had OR=9.385 (95%CI 4.535–19.425; p<0.001) for non-contact muscle injury during the season. Allele frequencies for ACE and AMPD1 differed significantly between muscle mass responders and non-responders (all p<0.05).
limitations: Single-sex cohort of Spanish Caucasian professional players limits generalizability across ethnicities and genders. The creatine supplementation protocol was not randomized or placebo-controlled. Only non-contact lower-limb muscle injuries were analyzed; upper-limb and contact injuries were excluded. Players using other supplements were excluded, potentially limiting external validity. The same cohort was analyzed in a prior study by the same author (Maestro et al.), which limits independence of conclusions.
practicalImplications: Performance staff should consider that an athlete's genetic profile significantly influences both the muscle mass response to creatine supplementation and injury risk during the competitive season. Players with lower TGS (below ~54 a.u.) are far less likely to gain muscle mass from standard creatine protocols and are at markedly elevated non-contact injury risk—suggesting enhanced monitoring and injury prevention programming is warranted. The AMPD1 CC genotype appears to be a particularly useful marker of creatine responsiveness and should be included in future genetic screening panels for professional football players.
athleteDev: In American football—where creatine supplementation is widely used for strength and mass development—genetic profiling could help optimize supplementation strategies at the individual player level. High-TGS players may derive greater muscle mass benefit from creatine loading protocols, informing off-season and preseason supplementation planning. Low-TGS players might benefit from alternative muscle mass strategies or more aggressive injury prevention programming in parallel with supplementation.
rtp: Players with a lower TGS (fewer protective alleles across AMPD1, ACE, ACTN3, CKM, MLCK) face approximately 9x higher odds of non-contact muscle injury. In an RTP context, low-TGS players returning from muscle injuries should be treated as elevated re-injury risks, warranting more conservative load progression and extended rehabilitation timelines. Creatine supplementation during rehabilitation may also be less effective at restoring muscle mass in these players, which should be factored into body composition-based RTP readiness assessments.

citation: Schwarz E, Duffield R, Novak AR, Compton DA, Meyer T. Associations Between Match-Play Characteristics and Environmental Temperatures in 4 Professional Football Leagues. European Journal of Sport Science. 2025;00:e12256. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsc.12256
abstract: This observational study investigated the association between environmental temperature (dry-bulb temperature [T] and wet-bulb globe temperature [WBGT]) and 17 match-play performance indicators (PIs) across 1,585 matches from four professional football leagues: German Bundesliga 1 and 2, Spanish La Liga, and Australian A-League. Environmental data were obtained retrospectively from public sources. Using linear regression per league and linear mixed models across all four leagues, 8 of 17 PIs were significantly associated with both T and WBGT (p<0.002). In hotter conditions, passes (especially short passes), touches, take-ons, and turnovers were all reduced, while pass success rates were slightly higher.
tldr: Across four professional football leagues spanning Germany, Spain, and Australia, higher temperatures significantly reduced the volume of match actions—particularly passes, touches, and take-ons—while key passes, shots, goals, and pass success rates were maintained. These small but consistent effects suggest players adopt a pacing strategy in the heat, reducing overall match activity while preserving quality. Football performance and coaching staff should anticipate a more static, slower style of play in warm weather, and may consider heat acclimatization, hydration protocols, pre-cooling, and tactical adjustments that favor efficiency over volume of ball involvement.
methods: Observational multi-league study of 1,585 professional male football matches (natural grass): Bundesliga 1 (306 matches, 2021–22), Bundesliga 2 (306 matches, 2021–22), La Liga (520 matches, 2021–23), Australian A-League (453 matches, 2016–20). Match performance data came from FBREF (European leagues) and A-League organizations, all generated via Opta/STATS PERFORM optical tracking. Environmental T and WBGT were obtained retrospectively via Meteostat.net (European leagues) and UBIMET GmbH (A-League); WBGT was estimated with the validated Liljegren et al. (2008) model, with wind speed standardized to 1 m/s. A total of 17 PIs across shooting, passing, dribbling, and defending categories were analyzed. For each league, linear regression modeled PI vs. T and WBGT. A combined linear mixed model (league as random effect) was fit across all 1,585 matches. Bonferroni correction applied (significance threshold p<0.00294). Effect size categories: small β 0.10–0.29, medium 0.30–0.49, large >0.50.
findings: Environmental conditions ranged from −1.7°C to 37.1°C T and −2.0°C to 29.6°C WBGT across leagues. Combined model results per 1°C T increase: total passes −2.3 (95%CI −3.1 to −1.5; p<0.001; β=−0.17); short passes −1.8 (95%CI −2.4 to −1.8; p<0.001; β=−0.17); pass success rate +0.06% (p=0.001); passes into final third −0.18 (95%CI −0.25 to −0.05; p=0.001); total touches −3.25 (95%CI −4.05 to −2.44; p<0.001; β=−0.23); touches in offensive third −0.9 (95%CI −1.2 to −0.6; p<0.001); take-ons −0.11 (p<0.001; β=−0.10); turnovers −1.27 (95%CI −1.53 to −1.02; p<0.001; β=−0.28). No significant associations for goals, shots, long passes, key passes, tackles, fouls, or cards (all p>0.04). All significant effects were small (β: 0.10–0.29).
limitations: Environmental data were obtained retrospectively rather than measured directly at stadiums, with possible error especially for solar radiation and wind. Analysis was at the match level (both teams combined), not individual team or player level. PI definitions varied across leagues, partially addressed by including league as a random effect. Very few matches occurred under high or extreme WBGT conditions (3 extreme, 10 high, 142 moderate), limiting power to detect effects at the most dangerous heat levels. Female cohorts were not studied. Player acclimatization status, playing style differences, and competition structures were not controlled.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football staff should be aware that heat stress meaningfully reduces match actions in football, with direct implications for warm-weather game planning and preseason camp design. While association football and American football differ structurally, the underlying physiology (impaired cognitive performance, early fatigue, altered pacing) is shared. Staff should anticipate reduced technical tempo in the heat, ensure adequate hydration and pre-cooling protocols, and note that quality metrics are maintained even as volume decreases—suggesting self-regulation rather than performance degradation.
athleteDev: For football players training through summer conditioning or preseason in warm climates, heat acclimatization is important not only for safety but for maintaining technical output. The consistent reduction in touches and take-ons in hotter conditions suggests that technical training quality may decline in hot environments, which should factor into periodization decisions—scheduling high-skill technical work during cooler parts of the day or season.
rtp: Players returning from injury in warm conditions may face a reduced technical and physical demand environment that does not fully replicate the match stresses used to benchmark RTP readiness. Staff should account for environmental conditions when interpreting performance data collected during warm-weather training or competition, and may need to apply corrected benchmarks when heat is expected to suppress normal performance metrics.

citation: Binney Z, Smoliga JM. Bad Altitude: Categorizing Elevation Produces Spurious Association With Concussions in the National Football League. Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy. 2022. https://doi.org/10.2519/jospt.2022.11039
abstract: This cohort replication study assessed whether prior findings of an association between NFL stadium altitude and concussion rates would replicate using a larger dataset and altitude analyzed as a continuous variable. All NFL regular-season games from 2012–2019 were analyzed (1,159 concussions in 867 players). Stadium elevations were identified via USGS and Google Maps. Generalized additive models (GAMs), Poisson regression, and logistic mixed models found no association between continuous altitude and concussion rates (IRR=1.00, 95%CI 0.99–1.01 per 100 ft increase). The previously reported 644 ft categorical cutpoint was significant in 2012–13 but did not replicate in 2014–19.
tldr: When stadium altitude is treated as a continuous variable rather than categorized, there is no association between elevation and NFL concussion rates. The previously reported finding of lower concussion rates at altitudes above 644 ft was a statistical artifact of inappropriate variable categorization—the result was specific to the 2012–13 seasons, was sensitive to the exact cutpoint chosen, and disappeared in subsequent seasons and with continuous analysis. NFL staff, clinicians, and equipment managers should not alter concussion safety, rehabilitation, or equipment practices based on stadium elevation, and should be skeptical of claims that altitude-mimicking devices (e.g., jugular compression collars) reduce concussion risk.
methods: Cohort replication study using publicly available data from all NFL regular-season games (weeks 1–16) from 2012 to 2019. Concussion data sourced from PBS Concussion Watch (2012–15) and Football Outsiders injury database (2012–19), based on official NFL injury reports. A total of 1,159 concussions in 867 players (1,103 player-seasons) were identified. Stadium altitude was obtained via USGS elevation maps and Google Maps coordinates for each venue. Concussion rates were calculated per game per stadium. GAMs with restricted cubic splines examined non-linear continuous altitude-concussion relationships. Mixed-effects Poisson and logistic regression models (team-level random effects, adjusting for home/away) assessed continuous and categorical (644 ft cutpoint) associations across three time periods: 2012–13, 2014–19, and 2012–19. Sensitivity analyses tested alternative cutpoints (±100 ft) and NFL median altitude split (≥397 ft vs. <397 ft).
findings: No continuous association between altitude and concussion rates was found in any time period: Poisson model IRR=1.00 (95%CI 0.99–1.01) per 100 ft increase for 2012–19. GAM plots were flat across all time periods. The 644 ft categorical cutpoint produced a significant result for 2012–13 only (IRR=0.71, 95%CI 0.54–0.94), replicating Myer et al., but did not replicate in 2014–19 (IRR=0.99, 95%CI 0.84–1.14) or for the full 2012–19 period (IRR=0.91, 95%CI 0.80–1.04). Moving the cutpoint ±100 ft substantially attenuated the association (IRRs of 0.76 and 0.92 respectively, both non-significant). Using the true median altitude split (397 ft) eliminated the association entirely (IRR=1.00, 95%CI 0.79–1.25). Denver (5,195 ft, highest NFL stadium) showed approximately average concussion rates. Logistic model results were consistent with Poisson results.
limitations: Concussion data relied on publicly reported injury reports, estimated to be missing approximately 10% of total NFL concussions based on comparison to official NFL Health and Wellness data for 2015–19. Study could not differentiate between concussions occurring in practice versus games, though 95% of regular-season concussions are game-related. Multiple concussions in close succession could not be confirmed as separate incidents. No college or high school American football data were available for comparison. Findings may not generalize to other sports or settings with much greater altitude ranges.
practicalImplications: NFL medical, performance, and equipment staff should not adjust concussion safety or rehabilitation protocols based on stadium elevation, as no epidemiological evidence supports an altitude effect at NFL-relevant elevations. The previous finding of lower concussion rates above 644 ft was a statistical artifact and should not inform clinical or equipment decisions. Specifically, the evidence does not support the use of jugular compression collar devices based on their altitude-mimicking rationale. Continuous variables should not be arbitrarily categorized in injury epidemiology research, as this practice routinely generates spurious findings.
athleteDev: No altitude effect on concussion rates exists at the elevations of any NFL stadium, meaning training site elevation should not factor into concussion risk assessments for developing American football players. Athlete development programs should direct resources toward evidence-based concussion prevention strategies—blocking and tackling technique, helmet fit, and rule compliance—rather than elevation-based interventions.
rtp: Concussion RTP protocols in American football should not be modified based on the elevation of a team's home or away stadium. RTP decisions should be governed solely by the NFL's established concussion management protocol and player-specific symptom resolution. The previously reported spurious finding that higher-altitude games produce fewer concussions should not influence RTP timelines, clearance thresholds, or equipment decisions at any level of play.

citation: Sieljacks P, Knudsen L, Wernbom M, Vissing K. Body position influences arterial occlusion pressure: implications for the standardization of pressure during blood flow restricted exercise. European Journal of Applied Physiology. 2018;118(2):303–312. doi:10.1007/s00421-017-3770-2
abstract: This study investigated the effect of body position on arterial occlusion pressure (AOP) in 24 healthy subjects using Doppler ultrasound with both wide (13 cm) and narrow (6 cm) pneumatic cuffs on the thigh. AOP was measured in supine and seated positions to determine the impact of hydrostatic pressure changes and to identify predictors of AOP across positions. Results showed AOP increased significantly from supine to seated for both cuff widths, with narrow cuffs producing substantially higher and more variable AOP values exceeding 600 mmHg in some subjects. Thigh circumference was the strongest predictor of AOP in all conditions, with diastolic blood pressure explaining additional variance for the wide cuff.
methods: Cross-sectional study with 24 healthy subjects (14 men, 10 women; mean age 32 years). Thigh circumference, skinfold, and blood pressure were measured. AOP was determined via Doppler ultrasound (posterior tibial artery) in randomized supine and seated positions using a wide cuff (Hokanson SC12; 13 cm) and a narrow cuff (Hokanson SC5; 6 cm) placed on contralateral legs. Pressure was increased in 5 mmHg increments up to a system limit of 600 mmHg. Multiple linear regression identified predictors of AOP across body positions.
findings: Supine AOP was 148±19 mmHg (wide) and 348±85 mmHg (narrow); seated AOP increased to 177±20 mmHg (wide, p<0.0001, d=1.5) and 409±101 mmHg (narrow, p<0.01, d=0.7). Four subjects exceeded 600 mmHg with the narrow cuff seated. Supine-to-seated AOP correlation was strong for the wide cuff (R²=0.81) but weaker for the narrow cuff (R²=0.50). Thigh circumference was the primary predictor of AOP for both cuffs; diastolic blood pressure added predictive value with the wide cuff (combined adj. R²=0.655–0.659 for wide cuff models). Mean relative AOP change from supine to seated was approximately 20% for both cuff widths.
limitations: Results apply only to lower body BFR; upper body AOP is likely less influenced by body position due to proximity to the heart. AOP was measured at rest, not during exercise, and exercise-induced blood pressure increases would further elevate true AOP during exercise. Prediction equations are specific to the 12-cm and 5-cm cuffs tested. The standing position was not assessed. Clinical population applicability is unknown.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football staff implementing BFR training should measure AOP in the seated position for lower body exercises rather than relying on supine measurements. Using supine AOP overestimates the relative blood flow restriction applied during seated exercise, particularly with narrow cuffs. Wide cuffs are strongly preferred for athletes with large thigh circumferences — common in football linemen — due to unpredictably extreme pressures narrow cuffs can require. Individual AOP measurement by a trained clinician in the exercise position remains the gold standard for safe BFR prescription.
athleteDev: For football players undergoing BFR-augmented hypertrophy work (e.g., post-practice low-load leg training), cuff pressure should be individualized and measured in the exercise position. Athletes with large thigh circumferences — offensive and defensive linemen, running backs — require higher absolute pressures and face greater risk of inadequate or excessive restriction if standardized pressures are used. Incorporating thigh circumference and diastolic blood pressure into AOP estimation using the wide-cuff prediction equations can help standardize relative restriction when direct Doppler measurement is unavailable.
rtp: BFR training is increasingly used in early post-surgical rehabilitation to preserve muscle mass under low mechanical load. This paper underscores that AOP must be re-measured or corrected for body position as the athlete transitions from supine (early rehab) to seated or standing exercises. Using supine AOP throughout a progressive rehab program will cause relative restriction to diminish as the athlete moves to upright positions, potentially blunting the stimulus. Wide pneumatic cuffs should be standard in clinical RTP protocols to ensure safe, predictable blood flow restriction across all stages of return to play.
tldr: When performing BFR training with lower body exercises in a seated position, do not rely on AOP values measured while the athlete is supine — seated AOP averages roughly 20% higher due to hydrostatic pressure differences, meaning a cuff set to 60% of supine AOP may only represent about 50% of true seated AOP. Wide cuffs are far safer and more predictable than narrow cuffs, which can require dangerously high pressures in athletes with large thigh circumferences. For any lower body BFR protocol, measure AOP in the position the athlete will actually exercise in, and use wide cuffs whenever possible.

citation: Chalmers S, Siegler J, Lovell R, Lynch G, Gregson W, Marshall P, Jay O. Brief in-play cooling breaks reduce thermal strain during football in hot conditions. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport. 2019;22(8):912–917. doi:10.1016/j.jsams.2019.04.009
abstract: This laboratory study examined whether three in-play cooling interventions could attenuate the rise in core (rectal) temperature during simulated soccer/football matches in hot, humid conditions (35°C, 55% RH, 30°C WBGT). Twelve well-trained football players completed four counterbalanced trials: a regular match (REG), a match with two 3-min cooling breaks using chilled water at the 30-min mark of each half (COOLwater), the same with an ice towel added around the neck (COOLtowel), and a match with an extended 20-min half-time (HTextended). All three interventions significantly reduced core temperature rise, exercising heart rate, and session RPE compared to REG, with no significant differences between the three interventions in core temperature effect.
tldr: Brief 3-minute cooling breaks during each half of a football match played in hot, humid conditions (30°C WBGT) meaningfully reduce core body temperature rise (~0.25°C), heart rate (~10–13 bpm), and perceived exertion (~1.3–1.4 RPE units) compared to playing without breaks. Simply stopping play and having players drink chilled water for 3 minutes at the 30-minute mark of each half is as effective as adding an ice towel.
