Football program administrators and sports medicine staff should continue to follow concussion protocols rigorously and minimize unnecessary head impacts in practice, while communicating to families that CTE risk from amateur participation appears to be low based on current neuropathological evidence.
athleteDev: For youth through college football programs, this study supports a nuanced risk-communication approach: the neuropathological changes associated with CTE appear uncommon in men who played at the amateur level, and absolute prevalence of CTE-NC features was statistically similar regardless of contact sport history. Emphasis should remain on proper tackling technique, appropriate practice contact limits, and concussion identification and management, rather than wholesale avoidance of football participation. Sports scientists and team physicians should stay current with evolving consensus definitions of CTE-NC, as the diagnostic criteria changed substantially between 2016 and 2021.
rtp: CTE-NC is a postmortem neuropathological diagnosis and is not directly applicable to clinical return-to-play decisions. However, the broader CTE literature informs how repetitive head impacts are approached in RTP protocols. This study's finding that CTE-NC features were not significantly associated with TBI history at the amateur level suggests that individual TBI events alone may not reliably drive CTE pathology in this population. RTP after concussion should continue to follow established stepwise graduated protocols (e.g., NFL or CISG concussion protocols), prioritizing full neurological recovery and symptom resolution before contact resumption, regardless of cumulative exposure history.

citation: Bourgon V, Duval F-G, Forest G. Circadian athletic variations when traveling in the Canadian Football League. Chronobiology International. 2026. DOI: 10.1080/07420528.2026.2637795
abstract: This study investigated circadian disadvantage related to travel direction and longitudinal distance in the Canadian Football League (CFL) using 8 years (2014–2021) of regular season evening game data (n=387 away games). One-way ANOVAs and linear regression analyses were applied across three travel direction categories (westward, same time zone, eastward) for team, offensive, and defensive performance variables. Results showed that westward travel was associated with worse score differentials and time of possession, and that greater longitudinal distance traveled westward predicted poorer defensive performance, while eastward travel was associated with performance improvements.
tldr: CFL teams traveling westward for evening games perform significantly worse than those traveling eastward or within the same time zone. Defensive variables were most sensitive to circadian disruption from westward travel, while offensive variables were largely unaffected except total first downs. Performance decline scales with longitudinal distance traveled westward. Staff should prioritize circadian management strategies — light exposure, sleep scheduling, and travel planning — especially when teams face long westward trips to evening games.
methods: Retrospective analysis of 387 CFL regular season away evening games (after 18:00 local time) from 2014 to 2021 (pre-COVID). Data extracted from footballdb.com and cfl.com. Variables categorized as team (score differential, time of possession, penalties), offensive (first downs, yards per play, pass completion, interceptions, sacks, fumbles, kickoff/punt returns, field goal %), and defensive (same metrics allowed). Travel direction (westward, same time zone, eastward) and longitudinal distance traveled were the independent variables. One-way ANOVAs with LSD post hoc t-tests and linear regression analyses were conducted.
findings: Significant effects of travel direction on score differential (F[2,286]=3.86, p=0.022, η²=.06) and time of possession (F[2,286]=5.42, p=0.005, η²=.09). Post hoc tests confirmed westward travelers performed worse on both variables. Defensive variables significantly affected by travel direction included average yards allowed per play (p=0.008), average yards allowed per passing play (p=0.005), and average kickoff return yards allowed (p=0.035). Regression analyses confirmed that greater longitudinal distance traveled westward significantly predicted worse score differential (β=.13, p<.001), less time of possession (β=2.06, p<.001), fewer first downs made (β=.03, p=.004), and worse defensive metrics including yards allowed per play (β=-.01, p<.001) and first downs allowed (β=-.04, p=.003).
limitations: Data were observational and derived from publicly available sources, limiting causal inference. Circadian phase was inferred from travel direction and time zone crossings rather than direct biological measures (melatonin, core body temperature, actigraphy). Performance metrics were assessed at the team level, potentially masking individual variability. Travel duration, sleep quality, injuries, and lineup changes were not controlled. Data limited to 2014–2021.
practicalImplications: NFL and CFL teams should incorporate structured circadian management protocols for westward-traveling teams playing evening games. Interventions such as controlled light exposure, strategic sleep scheduling, phase-advancing strategies, and careful travel planning can help mitigate circadian disadvantages. The finding that eastward travel may provide a circadian advantage suggests scheduling considerations for game times and travel logistics could offer a meaningful competitive edge.
athleteDev: Player development programs should educate athletes on chronobiology and circadian rhythm management as a performance factor. The differential impact on defensive versus offensive play suggests reactive skill development (defensive reads, pass coverage, decision speed) may be especially vulnerable to circadian disruption, making circadian optimization strategies particularly important for defensive players during road trips.
rtp: Return-to-play protocols for CFL and NFL athletes should account for circadian disruption from travel, particularly westward travel for evening games. Clinicians assessing readiness should consider that reactive, cognitively demanding tasks (pass coverage, pursuit angles) may be more impaired by travel fatigue than pre-planned offensive sequences. Scheduling return-to-participation assessments at the same time of day as upcoming games helps ensure accurate functional readiness evaluation.

citation: Smith RS, Guilleminault C, Efron B. Circadian Rhythms and Enhanced Athletic Performance in the National Football League. Sleep. 1997;20(5):362-365.
abstract: This retrospective study examined 25 years (1970–1994) of Monday Night Football (MNF) games between West Coast (WC) and East Coast (EC) NFL teams to test the hypothesis that circadian timing confers an athletic advantage. WC teams playing at 9:00 p.m. EST are effectively competing at 6:00 p.m. their local time (closer to peak performance), while EC teams play near their biological nadir. Logistic regression controlling for point spreads and home-field advantage showed that WC teams won significantly more often and by more points than EC teams, outperforming Las Vegas point spread predictions by a statistically significant margin.
tldr: West Coast NFL teams hold a significant circadian performance advantage over East Coast teams during Monday Night Football, winning 63.5% of games and outperforming Las Vegas point spread predictions in 67.9% of cases. This advantage is comparable to or greater than home-field advantage in magnitude. EC teams playing at 9:00 p.m. EST are performing near their biological nadir, while WC teams are competing around their physiological peak (6:00 p.m. local time). Football staff should treat game time relative to home time zone as a meaningful performance variable, not just travel logistics.
methods: Retrospective data analysis of all 63 MNF games involving EC versus WC NFL teams from the 1970–1994 seasons (25 seasons) with 9:00 p.m. EST start times. EC teams defined as those in the Eastern Standard time zone; WC teams as those in the Pacific time zone. Win-loss records, point margins, and performance relative to Las Vegas point spreads were examined using logistic regression with home-field advantage as an indicator variable. Two logistic regressions were run: one with home games as the indicator and one also including point spread as a predictor. All-time home and away winning percentages since 1970 were calculated and compared to MNF performance.
findings: WC teams won 63.5% of games versus EC teams' 36.5%, winning by an average of 14.7 points per game compared to EC's 9.0 points. WC teams outperformed Las Vegas point spread predictions at p<0.01 in both logistic regressions. Simply picking the WC team predicted the winner against the spread 67.9% of the time. WC teams' MNF home winning % was 71% (vs. their 59.3% all-time home rate); EC teams' MNF home winning % was 43.8% (vs. their 56.5% all-time). Overall record difference between WC and EC was only 4.4 percentage points, far less than the 27-point MNF gap.
limitations: Retrospective study unable to determine exact circadian phase of each athlete at game time. Travel strategies of teams changed over the 25-year study window. Individual variation in circadian adjustment to time zone changes could not be assessed. Point spread data from 1970–1972 (7 games) could not be located and were excluded from the point-spread regression. Confounding variables, though largely nullified by the point spread analysis, could not be completely eliminated.
practicalImplications: NFL staff should treat game time relative to home time zone as a critical performance variable. EC teams playing MNF against WC opponents should consider phase-delaying circadian clocks by 3 hours in the days prior to the game. WC teams should maintain home-zone scheduling when traveling east. The circadian effect appears at least as impactful as home-field advantage, suggesting sleep and circadian management is a high-ROI intervention for performance staff.
athleteDev: Circadian rhythm education should be integrated into athlete development programs. Training times influence biological peak performance timing, and athletes who consistently train in the late afternoon may benefit from scheduling practices aligned with anticipated game times. For developing players, establishing consistent sleep-wake schedules tied to competition times can optimize performance readiness over the long season.
rtp: Circadian considerations are relevant when scheduling return-to-play progression for injured athletes. Rehabilitation sessions and functional testing should ideally be conducted at the same time of day as typical game times to ensure assessments reflect true game-ready performance. Athletes returning from injury during road trips should have travel-related circadian disruption factored into clearance decisions.

citation: Bourgon V, Duval FG, Forest G. Circadian variation in coaches' decision-making in the National Football League's evening games. Chronobiology International. 2024. DOI: 10.1080/07420528.2024.2393880
abstract: This study examined whether circadian misalignment from westward travel affects NFL coaches' fourth-down decision-making across 20 seasons (2000–2020, n=5360 games). Factorial ANCOVA and moderation analyses tested whether travel direction and longitudinal distance interacted with game time to predict offensive play-calling on fourth down. Coaches on westward-traveling teams called significantly more offensive fourth-down plays (14.90%) during evening games compared to same-time-zone (12.20%) and eastward-traveling (11.35%) teams, an effect absent in daytime games, suggesting circadian misalignment impairs coaching cognition.
tldr: NFL coaches traveling westward for evening games call significantly more aggressive (risky) fourth-down plays than coaches traveling eastward or within the same time zone — an effect that disappears entirely for daytime games. This is the first study to show that circadian misalignment extends beyond player physical performance to coaching decision-making. The effect scales with westward longitudinal distance and only emerges in games starting after approximately 16:24h. Sleep deprivation and circadian disruption from westward travel may compromise in-game cognitive judgment, and protocols to mitigate this (sleep interventions, phase-shifting) should extend to coaching staff.
methods: Retrospective analysis of 5360 regular season NFL visiting team games from 2000–2020 (pre-COVID), excluding international games and playoff games. Data extracted from profootballreference.com and nfl.com. Variables included travel direction (westward, same time zone, eastward), longitudinal degrees traveled (−51° to +51°), time of game (day <18:00h vs. evening ≥18:00h, and as continuous variable), year of season, and percentage of offensive plays on fourth down. A factorial ANCOVA (Time of game × Direction of travel) with year as covariate was conducted, followed by post hoc ANOVAs and Tukey t-tests for day (n=4410) and evening (n=950) games separately. A moderation analysis tested whether time of game moderated the relationship between longitudinal degrees traveled and fourth-down play calling.
findings: Significant interaction between Time of game and Direction of travel (F[2,5358]=5.39, p=0.005). In evening games, westward-traveling teams had 14.90% offensive fourth-down plays vs. 12.20% (same time zone, p=0.03) and 11.35% (eastward, p=0.007). No significant direction effect in daytime games (p=0.62). Moderation analysis: significant longitudinal degrees × time of game interaction (F[1,5355]=9.51, p=0.002). Westward longitudinal degrees significantly predicted greater fourth-down aggression only for games starting at 16:24h or later. The explained variance was modest (0.2%) but statistically significant and meaningful in the context of elite competition.
limitations: No data on coaches' individual sleep patterns or chronotypes. Game situational variables (score, time remaining, field position, opponent strength) were unavailable and could not be controlled. Fourth-down decisions represent only one of many coaching decisions per game and may be influenced by cumulative in-game context. The study was exploratory with a modest effect size. Causal inference is limited by the retrospective observational design.
practicalImplications: Sleep and circadian health protocols should extend beyond players to include all coaching and support staff. Teams traveling westward for evening games should implement pre-travel phase-shifting strategies (morning bright light, evening melatonin) for coaches as well as players. NFL schedulers should consider offering earlier kickoff times to teams traveling west across multiple time zones. Organizations should monitor in-game decision metrics in relation to travel schedule as part of performance management.
athleteDev: This research broadens the scope of circadian science in football to include coaching staff cognitive performance. For player development, it underscores the importance of preparing players for complex decision-making demands (quarterbacks, defensive captains) under conditions of circadian disruption, particularly when preparing for westward road trips with evening kickoffs.
rtp: From a return-to-play perspective, this study highlights that cognitive readiness — not just physical readiness — must be considered when clearing athletes after travel-heavy schedules. Medical and performance staff should monitor decision-making quality and cognitive load tolerance in players returning from injury during periods of circadian disruption, particularly in westward-travel evening game scenarios.

citation: Mack C, Matava M, Zeidler K, Sills A, Solomon G. Clinical Research in the National Football League: The Player Scientific and Medical Research Protocol. Current Sports Medicine Reports. 2020;19(5):168-174.
abstract: This invited commentary describes the NFL and NFLPA's jointly developed Medical Research Approval Process (MRAP), a structured, multilevel governance framework governing the use of NFL player workplace injury and illness data for clinical research. Established as a side letter to the 2011 CBA (effective January 26, 2015), MRAP outlines seven distinct research pathways based on the requester's affiliation, research intent, and data sensitivity. The protocol ensures research feasibility, protects player confidentiality, avoids duplication of effort, and requires sequential approvals from IQVIA, NFL legal, the NFL Chief Medical Officer, NFLPA, and IRBs where applicable.
tldr: The NFL and NFLPA jointly created the Medical Research Approval Process (MRAP) to govern how player injury and illness data can be used for research. Seven pathways cover everything from internal club use to third-party publications, each with a specific approval chain. All research using NFL EMR data must go through this system. Key points for staff: research using NFL player data requires multi-level institutional approval; publicly available injury data (news reports, injury reports) are unreliable as primary sources; and any research project, even internally focused, must follow MRAP to avoid sanctions including committee removal and club discipline.
methods: This is an invited commentary and descriptive article outlining the MRAP governance structure. The authors describe the seven sequential research pathways (I: Interventional; II: Internal Club EMR; III: Medical Committee De-identified; IV: Medical Committee Identifiable; V: EMR Research for Publication; VI: Case Study for Publication; VII: Third-Party Data Request), the approval parties required at each pathway, and the process for initiating, reviewing, and disseminating research under the MRAP framework. No experimental design or statistical methods are employed.
findings: MRAP consists of seven distinct pathways governing NFL medical research by type of requester, data sensitivity, and intended use. All research requires sequential approval; denial at any stage terminates the request. The protocol explicitly states the NFL and NFLPA may offer nonbinding comments but have no editorial control over publications. Publicly sourced injury data are identified as unreliable, particularly for lower-grade injuries not resulting in missed playing time. MRAP-governed EMR data linked with game statistics and player demographics provides the most reliable injury incidence and outcome data. Since 2019, individual clubs have access to injury dashboards comparing their data to league averages including return-to-play metrics.
limitations: As a descriptive commentary, no empirical limitations apply. MRAP is specific to the NFL-NFLPA collectively bargained environment, which may limit direct generalizability to other sports leagues or non-unionized athletic contexts. The article does not provide data on the volume or outcomes of research proposals reviewed through MRAP.
practicalImplications: NFL team physicians, athletic trainers, and performance staff who wish to conduct or publish research on player data must route all requests through the MRAP system via the NFL MRAP web portal. Research proposals require feasibility review by IQVIA and approval from NFL management council legal, the NFL Chief Medical Officer, and NFL EVP of Health and Safety. Drafts must be submitted 60 days before journal submission and 30 days before final publication. Teams should avoid using publicly available injury data as primary sources due to systematic underreporting of lower-grade injuries.
athleteDev: The MRAP framework enables systematic, high-quality research on NFL player health across the career arc, including injury prevention, performance outcomes post-surgery, and long-term health. Access to comprehensive, curated EMR-linked game statistics allows researchers to study position-specific injury patterns, recovery trajectories, and performance-based return-to-sport outcomes at a scale not possible with public data.
rtp: MRAP is directly relevant to return-to-play research quality. Studies using MRAP-approved NFL EMR data capture the full spectrum of injuries — including lower-grade injuries not reported publicly — providing more accurate incidence rates and RTP timelines. Practitioners should prioritize research published through MRAP-governed data channels over studies relying on publicly sourced injury reports when setting evidence-based return-to-play benchmarks for NFL players.

citation: Assef N, Endsley C, Skoumal C, Schlafke J, Dewald M. Closed Kinetic Chain Upper Extremity Stability Test Reference Values for NCAA American Football. International Journal of Sports Physical Therapy. 2025;20(9):1338-1344. doi:10.26603/001c.143374
abstract: This cross-sectional study established CKCUEST reference values for 80 male NCAA football players (Divisions 1, 2, and 3) from three institutions, accounting for position, injury history, year in school, and division. Players averaged 27.3 touches with a minimal detectable change of 1.2 touches (clinically, 2 touches). Linemen (offensive and defensive combined) averaged significantly fewer touches (25.7) than all other positions combined (28.1, p<0.01), and linebackers averaged more touches (29.7) than offensive linemen (24.8, p=0.03). No significant differences were found based on NCAA division, year in school, or history of upper extremity injury.
tldr: NCAA American football players averaged 27 touches on the CKCUEST, with a position-based distinction: linemen score significantly lower (~25.7) than all other positions (~28.1). A change of at least 2 touches represents a clinically meaningful difference. Prior upper extremity injury did not reduce scores, supporting use of this test as an objective RTP benchmark regardless of injury history. Staff should apply position-specific reference values rather than a single universal cutoff when using the CKCUEST for return-to-sport clearance in football.
methods: Cross-sectional study approved by three university IRBs with data collected spring/summer 2024. Eighty male NCAA football players (Division 1 n=21, Division 2 n=37, Division 3 n=22) completed three 15-second CKCUEST trials (push-up position, hands 36 inches apart, alternating hand touches for maximum repetitions) with 45-second rest between trials; trials were averaged for the score. Independent variables: position, height, mass, BMI, year in school, upper extremity injury history. Comparisons used one-way ANOVA with Bonferroni post hoc corrections, independent t-tests for linemen vs. all other positions, and Pearson correlation coefficients for continuous anthropometric variables. MDC95 calculated from SEM.
findings: Mean touches: 27.3 (95% CI 26.4–28.1). MDC: 1.2 touches (2 touches needed for clinically meaningful change). Linebackers (29.7) vs. offensive linemen (24.8): p=0.03. Linemen as a group (25.7) vs. all other positions (28.1): p<0.01. No significant correlations between touches and height (r=−0.02), mass (r=−0.18), BMI (r=−0.20), year in school (r=0.16), or division (r=0.12). No significant difference between players with (26.6) and without (27.6) upper extremity injury history (p=0.30). No significant differences by NCAA division (Division 1: 26.0, Division 2: 28.0, Division 3: 27.2).
limitations: Data collected at three sites with different researchers, introducing potential inter-rater variability (mitigated by standardized video instructions and phone follow-up). Different floor surfaces across facilities may have influenced touch counts. Some positions had small sample sizes, limiting statistical power for pairwise position comparisons. Injury history was self-reported and not differentiated by injury type or severity.
practicalImplications: A reference value of 27 touches should be used as a benchmark for NCAA football players on the CKCUEST. A change of at least 2 touches represents a clinically meaningful difference. Position-specific norms should be applied: linemen (OL and DL) typically score around 25–26 touches, while non-lineman positions average 28+ touches. Staff should not use a single universal benchmark for return-to-sport clearance without accounting for positional demands. Pre-season CKCUEST scores below 21 touches have been previously associated with elevated in-season shoulder injury risk.
athleteDev: The CKCUEST is a practical, low-equipment upper extremity functional assessment suitable for pre-season screening of all football position groups. Establishing position-specific baseline scores at the start of each season allows staff to track changes in upper extremity stability and identify at-risk athletes. Linemen's lower normative scores (25.7) reflect their unique positional demands and higher body mass, and development programs for linemen should incorporate closed-chain upper extremity strength and stability training to optimize performance on this metric.
rtp: The CKCUEST with a football-specific reference value of 27 touches (or position-adjusted: ~25.7 for linemen, ~28.1 for non-linemen) provides a standardized, validated benchmark for upper extremity return-to-sport clearance following shoulder, elbow, or wrist injuries in NCAA and NFL-level football players. A minimum of 2 touches improvement from post-injury baseline is needed to confirm measurable recovery. Prior upper extremity injury history did not reduce CKCUEST performance, supporting its use as an objective clearance criterion regardless of injury background.

citation: Eichner ER. College Football: Conditioning Them to Death; Splenic Infarction: Keeping it in the Family. Curr Sports Med Rep. 2018;17(9):278-279.
abstract: This editorial commentary addresses two related topics in college football sports medicine. The first section examines conditioning-related deaths in college football since 2000, cataloguing 30 fatalities comprising 12 from exertional sickling (ECAST), 10 from cardiac disease, 7 from exertional heat stroke (EHS), and 1 from asthma, arguing that reckless coaching practices bear significant responsibility. The second section presents two case reports of familial splenic infarction in individuals with sickle cell trait (SCT) at altitude, illustrating the hereditary pattern and noting the condition responds well to conservative management. The author emphasizes that NCAA screening programs for SCT have sharply reduced ECAST deaths in Division I football, and that EHS deaths remain preventable through commonsense conditioning practices.
tldr: Conditioning-related deaths in college football are preventable — coaches who enforce excessive drills bear accountability — and athletes with sickle cell trait face altitude-triggered splenic infarction risk that responds to conservative treatment including descent.
methods: Narrative review and editorial commentary drawing on surveillance data from the University of Oklahoma head athletic trainer Scott Anderson tracking college non-traumatic football deaths since 2000; case reports of two familial splenic infarction pairs (father-son) with SCT at altitude.
findings:
- 30 college football conditioning deaths since 2000: 12 ECAST, 10 cardiac, 7 EHS, 1 asthma
- NCAA SCT screening program (begun 2010) sharply cut ECAST deaths in Division I: only 1 ECAST death in 8 years of the program vs. 10 in the prior 10 years
- A June 2018 lineman death followed a session of 10x110-yard sprints; player subsequently required emergency liver transplant and was attributed to EHS
- Liver transplants for EHS have been performed but are not a substitute for prevention
- Familial SCT splenic infarction occurs at moderate altitude (7,200–12,756 ft) with acute left upper quadrant pain; managed conservatively without splenectomy in some cases; father healed in 10 days, son in 5 days
limitations: Editorial/opinion piece without systematic methodology; relies on anecdotal case reports for the splenic infarction section; some conditioning death classifications may be misattributed (e.g., HCM misclassified as EHS); external reviews have not reliably prevented recurrence at some programs.
practicalImplications: Conditioning sessions must be scientifically based, sport-specific, reasonably progressed, and appropriately monitored. Coaches who enforce inappropriate or excessive drills should be held accountable. SCT screening and commonsense training precautions dramatically reduce ECAST risk. Athletes with SCT traveling to altitude should be counseled about splenic infarction risk and instructed to descend and seek care promptly if left upper quadrant pain develops.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college football: underscores the obligation of strength and conditioning staff to individualize and progress training loads appropriately, avoid punitive or excessive conditioning sessions, and ensure emergency action plans for EHS and ECAST are in place and practiced.
rtp: An athlete who experiences EHS or ECAST requires complete medical clearance before return to play; liver transplant recipients face a protracted and uncertain RTP pathway. Athletes with SCT who suffer splenic infarction can typically return after conservative management (descent, rest, monitoring), but must be educated about recurrence risk at altitude.

citation: Adlou B, Grace J, Wilburn C, Weimar W. College-to-NFL Stadium Turf Transitions as a Risk Factor for Lower Extremity Non-Contact Injuries in Rookie Players: A 13-Year Cohort Analysis. Healthcare. 2025;13(14):1415.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined whether the change in home stadium turf type from a player's final college season to their rookie NFL season influences lower extremity non-contact injury (LE-NCI) risk in 826 first- and second-round draft picks from 2012 to 2024. Six transition categories were defined (Grass-to-Grass, Grass-to-Artificial, Artificial-to-Grass, Artificial-to-Artificial, Grass-to-Hybrid, Artificial-to-Hybrid). Competing risk analysis and multivariable logistic regression were used to estimate cumulative LE-NCI incidence and assess associations with turf transition, position group, draft cohort, and college training surface access. Overall, 21.2% of players sustained a LE-NCI during their rookie season, with cumulative incidence reaching 33.1% by season end and injury risk clustering between weeks 4 and 10.
tldr: Surface type transitions from college to the NFL did not significantly increase lower extremity non-contact injury risk in rookie players; instead, player position — particularly skill positions — was the dominant risk factor, with skill players having 88% higher adjusted odds of injury compared to hybrid position players.
methods: Retrospective cohort study of 826 NFL first- and second-round draft picks (2012–2024) from Auburn University Sport Biomechanics Lab. Data on college/NFL home surfaces (six transition types), position group (linemen, skill, hybrid, quarterback), college training surface access, and rookie season LE-NCIs collected from public sources (Pro-Football-Reference, NFL.com, ESPN.com, CSB Sports). Competing risk analysis (Aalen-Johansen estimator) estimated cumulative LE-NCI incidence. Multivariable logistic regression with two models assessed associations; sensitivity analysis compared LE-NCI players to uninjured players only.
findings:
- 21.2% (175/826) of players sustained a LE-NCI during their rookie season
- Cumulative LE-NCI incidence reached 33.1% (95% CI: 28.7–37.5%) by season end
- Injury risk accelerated between weeks 4 and 10 of the rookie season
- No specific turf transition category showed statistically significant association with LE-NCI risk vs. Grass-to-Grass reference (p = 0.175)
- Skill position players had significantly higher LE-NCI odds vs. hybrid players (AOR = 1.88; 95% CI: 1.20–2.97; p = 0.006)
- College training access to multiple surface types was not protective (AOR = 0.97; 95% CI: 0.65–1.45; p = 0.874)
- Skill players had the highest raw LE-NCI rate (30.2%; 88/291); quarterbacks lowest (13.2%; 7/53)
- Grass-to-Grass skill players had the highest absolute LE-NCI count (27/70; 38.6%)
limitations: Retrospective design relying on publicly available injury reports with possible under-reporting or misclassification; home stadium surface as proxy for actual playing surface exposure may not reflect all practice and away-game surfaces; interaction terms between transition type and position group were not included due to model instability from small cell sizes in certain strata; only first- and second-round picks studied, limiting generalizability to later-round picks.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and performance staff should prioritize position-specific injury prevention strategies — particularly for skill position players — rather than focusing primarily on surface transition protocols. Enhanced neuromuscular training (cutting mechanics, landing strategies, proprioceptive conditioning) for skill players is warranted. Heightened monitoring and load management between weeks 4 and 10 of the rookie season represents a high-yield intervention window.
athleteDev: For American football rookie development programs, this study suggests that surface type transition from college to the NFL is less important than position-specific biomechanical demands in determining early-career injury risk. Skill position players (wide receivers, running backs, defensive backs, cornerbacks, safeties) should receive priority attention in pre-season injury screening and conditioning programs targeting cutting, deceleration, and landing mechanics.
rtp: The temporal clustering of LE-NCIs between weeks 4 and 10 suggests that rookies who sustain a lower extremity non-contact injury during this window should be managed with particular care regarding return timelines, given the high cumulative incidence (33.1%) and the ongoing physical adaptation demands of the professional transition period.

citation: Miller KC, Adams WM. Common body temperature sites provide invalid measures of body core temperature in hyperthermic humans wearing American football uniforms. Temperature. 2021;8(2):166-175.
abstract: This experimental study investigated the criterion validity of four common body temperature measurement sites — axillary (T_AXL), oral (T_ORL), aural (T_EAR), and forehead liquid crystal monitor (T_FHD) — as surrogates for rectal temperature (T_REC) in men wearing full American football uniform pads (PADS) during simulated exertional heat stroke (EHS) scenarios. Thirteen men completed four sequential periods: rest, exercise until T_REC was 39.7–39.8°C, cold water immersion (CWI) until all sites read ≤38°C, and 15-minute post-immersion recovery. Body temperatures were compared across 34 measurement timepoints using repeated-measures ANOVA and Bland-Altman analyses. All four peripheral sites demonstrated unacceptable bias (>±0.27°C) from T_REC across conditions, with T_AXL and T_ORL exceeding the bias threshold at all 34 measurement times.
tldr: Oral, axillary, aural, and forehead temperatures are all invalid surrogates for rectal temperature in football players wearing full pads during exercise, cold water immersion, and recovery — only rectal temperature should be used to diagnose and manage exertional heat stroke in this population.
methods: Controlled laboratory experiment. Thirteen recreationally active, unacclimated men (age 22±2 y; mass 77.5±8.8 kg; height 181.3±5.7 cm) wore full American football PADS (helmet, shoulder, knee, thigh, hip, tailbone pads, jersey, undergarments, half-length pants) in an environmental chamber (38.7±0.8°C, 38.9±2.4% RH). T_REC measured via self-inserted rectal thermistor; T_AXL via taped skin thermistor; T_ORL via digital thermometer; T_EAR via digital ear thermometer; T_FHD via liquid crystal forehead monitor. Repeated-measures ANOVA assessed temperature site × time interactions; Bland-Altman plots assessed mean bias and limits of agreement vs. T_REC. Bias threshold for invalidity: >±0.27°C.
findings:
- T_AXL and T_ORL statistically differed from T_REC and exceeded ±0.27°C bias threshold at all 34 measurement times (100%)
- T_FHD differed from T_REC 8 times during rest, 8 times during exercise, 0 times during CWI, and twice during recovery (18/34, 53%); exceeded bias threshold 28 times total (82%)
- T_EAR differed from T_REC at 5 times during rest, 0 during exercise, 5 during CWI, once during recovery (11/34, 32%); exceeded threshold 15 times (44%)
- During CWI, T_AXL showed the largest bias (mean 11.8±5.5°C), severely underestimating T_REC as axilla cooled rapidly in water
- Overall mean bias: T_AXL = 4.7±1.9°C, T_ORL = 1.0±0.2°C, T_FHD = -0.3±0.4°C, T_EAR = 0.5±0.2°C
- All sites exhibited large limits of agreement, ranging from -0.3°C to 1.4°C (aural) to -6.8°C to 15.9°C (axillary)
limitations: Only one type of PADS configuration tested (not 'shells'); T_AXL recorded via taped thermistor rather than standard clinical digital thermometer (may have created axillary microclimate); non-circulating water bath used for CWI; small, all-male convenience sample of non-athletes; some thermometers (T_ORL, T_EAR) required longer lead times limiting continuous recording during CWI; only one type of American football uniform tested.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and medical staff must use rectal temperature as the sole valid method to assess body core temperature when EHS is suspected in American football players wearing pads. Use of oral, axillary, aural, or forehead temperature in this context risks misdiagnosis (e.g., falsely consistent with heat exhaustion) and could lead to inappropriate clinical decisions — including premature termination of cold water immersion — with potentially fatal consequences.
athleteDev: American football players at all levels face an 11 times higher risk of exertional heat illness than all other sports combined at the secondary school level. Preseason training in full pads during hot weather is a particularly high-risk period. Programs must have rectal thermometers available at all practices and games, and athletic trainers must be empowered to use them. Education of athletes on heat acclimatization, hydration, and recognition of early EHS symptoms is essential.
rtp: EHS is a medical emergency requiring confirmed T_REC ≥40°C for diagnosis. Cold water immersion should be maintained until T_REC ≤38°C — terminating CWI based on peripheral temperature sites risks premature removal and recurrent hyperthermia. Following EHS, athletes require complete medical clearance including a graduated heat acclimatization protocol before return to full activity; return timelines are individualized based on severity of organ involvement.

citation: Romero-Morales C, Berzosa-Rojo A, Di Luca-Calabrese D, Vázquez-González S, Abuín-Porras V, Jaén-Crespo G, García-Sanz F, Pareja-Galeano H. Comparative analysis of patellar tendon, achilles tendon and plantar fascia structure in indoor and outdoor football players: a novel cross-sectional pilot study. Sci Rep. 2024;14:3930.
abstract: This cross-sectional pilot study compared patellar tendon (PT), Achilles tendon (AT), plantar fascia (PF), and Hoffa's fat pad thickness via ultrasound imaging (USI) between 15 outdoor soccer players and 15 indoor soccer (futsal) players aged 18–30 years. All players had been active for at least 8 years and trained 3 sessions plus 1 match per week. Measurements were taken bilaterally for PT longitudinal and transverse thickness, AT longitudinal and transverse thickness, PF thickness, and Hoffa's fat pad thickness. The only statistically significant between-group difference found was a decrease in left-side Hoffa's fat pad thickness in outdoor soccer players compared to futsal players (p = 0.026), while PT, AT, and PF showed no significant differences between groups.
tldr: Ultrasound assessment of tendon and plantar fascia thickness revealed no meaningful structural differences between outdoor and indoor soccer players, suggesting the load differences between these football modalities are insufficient to drive adaptive changes in patellar tendon, Achilles tendon, or plantar fascia thickness — though Hoffa's fat pad showed a modest reduction in outdoor players.
methods: Cross-sectional pilot study conducted March–May 2023 at Universidad Europea de Madrid, following STROBE guidelines. Sample: 30 registered male football players (outdoor soccer n=15; indoor/futsal n=15), ages 18–30, playing ≥8 years, 3 training sessions + 1 match/week (90-min sessions). USI performed by one trained therapist using LogiQ P7, GE Healthcare (4–14 MHz linear transducer, L612 RS). Measured: PT longitudinal (PT-L) and transverse (PT-T) thickness, Hoffa's fat pad (FP) thickness, AT longitudinal (AT-L) and transverse (AT-T) thickness, PF thickness — all bilaterally. Statistical analysis: Shapiro-Wilk normality, Student t-test and Mann-Whitney U, Levene's test for variance equality, multivariate linear regression (stepwise) for left Hoffa's fat pad. SPSS v22.0.
findings:
- No significant differences between outdoor and indoor football players in PT-L, PT-T, AT-L, AT-T, or PF thickness on either side (all p > 0.05)
- Hoffa's fat pad showed significant left-side difference between groups (p = 0.026): outdoor 1.28±0.13 cm vs. indoor 1.38±0.10 cm
- Right-side Hoffa's fat pad: outdoor 1.34±0.11 cm vs. indoor 1.36±0.06 cm (p = 0.545, not significant)
- Multivariate regression showed left Hoffa's fat pad difference was significant (p = 0.048) with R² = 0.137; dominant leg side did not predict the difference
- Groups differed significantly in age (outdoor 22.53±1.24 vs. indoor 24.26±2.43 years; p = 0.021) but not height, weight, or BMI
limitations: Small pilot sample (n=30 total, n=15 per group); only healthy participants; dynamic USI not performed; dominant vs. non-dominant limb comparison rather than direct comparison of limbs performing specific tasks; exact years of sports practice not recorded; competition calendar differences between groups not fully controlled; USI measures thickness only (not stiffness via elastography or vascularity via Doppler); results should be interpreted with caution given limited statistical power.
practicalImplications: The load differences between outdoor soccer (natural/artificial grass) and indoor soccer (hard court) do not appear sufficient to produce measurable structural adaptations in the PT, AT, or PF in active, healthy players. Coaching teams should not assume tendon structure differs substantially by football modality when designing load management or injury prevention programs. Hoffa's fat pad monitoring via USI may be a useful adjunct clinical assessment in football players given its sensitivity to load changes.
athleteDev: NOTE: This paper studies soccer (outdoor) and futsal (indoor) players — not American football. The findings are applicable to sports science contexts involving tendon health monitoring in field vs. court sport athletes. For American football contexts, the principle that surface type alone may not drive tendon structural adaptation is relevant; position-specific load characteristics (volume, intensity of cutting, jumping, acceleration) are likely more important determinants of tendon adaptation than surface type.
rtp: No RTP data presented. The finding that structural tendon differences were absent between groups suggests that surface type transition alone is unlikely to be a primary driver of tendinopathy risk in active players. Clinically, USI-based assessment of PT, AT, PF, and Hoffa's fat pad may assist in monitoring tendon health and identifying subclinical pathology prior to symptom onset.

citation: Bailey AM, McMurry TL, Cormier JM, Funk JR, Crandall JR, Mack CD, Myers BS, Arbogast KB. Comparison of laboratory and on-field performance of American football helmets. Ann Biomed Eng. 2020;(published online 6 October 2020).
abstract: This study assessed the relationship between laboratory and on-field performance of 31 American football helmet models worn by NFL players during the 2015–2019 seasons. Laboratory testing used linear impactor impacts at six locations and three velocities on an instrumented Hybrid III head-neck assembly; a Helmet Performance Score (HPS) was calculated from a linear combination of the Head Injury Criterion (HIC) and Diffuse Axonal Multi-Axis General Evaluation (DAMAGE). On-field performance was quantified as model-specific concussion rates per player-play, derived from NFL game statistics, helmet use tracking, and the NFL Game Day Concussion Diagnosis Protocol. A positive correlation (r² = 0.61, p < 0.001) was found between laboratory HPS and on-field concussion rates, and NFL-prohibited helmet models had significantly higher adjusted odds of concussion (OR 1.24; 95% CI 1.04–1.47; p = 0.017) compared to non-prohibited models.
tldr: Laboratory helmet performance scores are meaningfully correlated with actual concussion rates in the NFL, and helmets prohibited by the NFL based on poor lab performance had 24% higher adjusted concussion odds on the field — supporting the use of laboratory testing as a basis for helmet prohibition policies.
methods: Laboratory: Two samples of each of 31 helmet models placed on instrumented 50th-percentile Hybrid III head-neck assembly on a sliding table; pneumatic ram delivered impacts at 6 locations (Side Upper, Oblique Front, Rear Oblique, Side, Facemask Side, Facemask Central Oblique) × 3 speeds (5.5, 7.4, 9.3 m/s) = 18 conditions. HARM (linear combination of HIC and DAMAGE) calculated per condition; weighted HPS computed from weighted sum of 18 HARM values reflecting NFL concussion incidence by speed and location. On-field: NFL Game Statistics & Information System (GSIS) provided player-play participation data for 2015–2019 (5,905,882 total player-plays; 1,040 concussions). Helmet use tracked via NFL Game Management System (GMS) and RFID (2017–2019). Video review confirmed concussion plays. Logistic regression model controlled for position, play type, season year, season type, and prior concussion count. Prohibited vs. non-prohibited classification based on NFLPA 2020 Helmet Laboratory Testing Performance Results.
findings:
- Laboratory HPS ranged from 0.874 to 1.163 (mean 1.03, SD 0.07) across 31 models
- On-field concussion rates for helmet models with >50,000 player-plays ranged from 1.23 to 3.11 per 10,000 player-plays
- Weighted linear regression: r² = 0.61 (p < 0.001) between HPS and on-field concussion rate
- NFL-prohibited helmet models had 1.62× higher raw concussion rate than non-prohibited models
- After covariate adjustment (position, play type, season year, season type, prior concussions): prohibited helmets OR = 1.24 (95% CI 1.04–1.47; p = 0.017) vs. non-prohibited
- Returned kickoffs had highest concussion probability; non-returned punt plays lowest
- Preseason had highest concussion odds vs. regular and postseason
- Prior concussion count significantly predicted concussion (p < 0.001)
- Tight ends and wide receivers had highest concussion probability by position; defensive linemen lowest
limitations: Laboratory testing designed primarily for helmet-to-helmet impacts, accounting for only ~36% of NFL concussions; helmet-to-body and helmet-to-ground impacts not fully captured; only one helmet size tested per model; position-specific impact weighting not used (general NFL concussion incidence weights applied); play type used as proxy for exposure/impact severity rather than direct impact measurement; results specific to professional NFL play and should not be generalized to youth, high school, or collegiate football.
practicalImplications: Equipment managers and medical staff should prioritize helmets with better laboratory performance scores (lower HPS) and avoid models on the NFL/NFLPA prohibited list. The correlation between lab performance and on-field concussion rates provides evidence-based justification for annual helmet technology reviews and player education about helmet model selection. Players with prior concussion history represent a high-risk group warranting enhanced monitoring regardless of helmet model.
athleteDev: For American football programs at all levels, this study supports using laboratory-tested helmet performance rankings as a guide for helmet procurement decisions. Ensuring athletes — particularly skill positions with higher concussion rates — wear top-performing helmet models is a modifiable injury prevention strategy. Programs should revisit helmet inventories annually as new ratings are published and prohibit use of poorly rated models.
rtp: Prior concussion count was a significant independent predictor of concussion risk (p < 0.001), reinforcing the importance of accurate concussion history documentation in RTP decision-making. Players with multiple prior concussions should be considered at elevated risk upon return and may benefit from extended monitoring periods and enhanced protective equipment selection.

citation: Bailey AM, McMurry TL, Cormier JM, Funk JR, Crandall JR, Mack CD, Myers BS, Arbogast KB. Comparison of Laboratory and On-Field Performance of American Football Helmets. Ann Biomed Eng. 2020. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10439-020-02627-5
abstract: This study assessed the relationship between laboratory performance scores and on-field concussion rates for 31 football helmet models worn by NFL players during the 2015-2019 seasons. Linear impactor tests at six impact locations and three velocities were used to compute a Helmet Performance Score (HPS) based on head injury criterion (HIC) and diffuse axonal multi-axis evaluation (DAMAGE) metrics. On-field concussion data were collected across over 5.4 million player-plays, and logistic regression related HPS to helmet-specific concussion rates while controlling for position, play type, season year, season type, and prior concussion history. Helmets prohibited by the NFL/NFLPA based on poor laboratory performance had significantly higher on-field concussion risk.
tldr: Laboratory helmet performance scores positively correlate with on-field concussion rates in the NFL (r² = 0.61, p < 0.001), meaning helmets that perform worse in lab testing are associated with higher concussion rates on the field. Helmets prohibited by the NFL/NFLPA based on poor lab performance had 24% higher odds of concussion (OR 1.24; 95% CI 1.04-1.47; p = 0.017) compared to non-prohibited models. Football equipment staff should use annual NFL/NFLPA helmet ratings as the primary guide for equipment procurement and ensure all players are in approved, high-performing helmets. Kickoff play types carry the highest concussion probability, and prior concussion history is a strong independent predictor of future concussion risk.
methods: Cross-sectional observational study. Two samples each of 31 helmet models worn by NFL players (2015-2019) were tested using a pneumatic ram and sliding table at 18 test conditions (3 speeds x 6 locations). Six-degree-of-freedom head kinematics computed HARM (0.0148 x HIC + 15.6 x DAMAGE) per condition; a weighted composite HPS was calculated per helmet. NFL Game Statistics and Information System and Helmet Tracker RFID data identified helmet use across 5,460,388 player-plays. Concussion diagnoses came from the NFL electronic medical record. Logistic mixed regression with random player effects estimated concussion probability, controlling for position, play type, season year, season type, and prior concussion count. Helmets were also grouped as prohibited or non-prohibited per the 2020 NFL/NFLPA helmet poster.
findings: HPS for the 31 helmets ranged from 0.874 to 1.163 (mean 1.03, SD 0.07); 24 of 31 helmets were within one SD of the mean. A positive correlation between HPS and on-field concussion rate was found for helmets with >50,000 player-plays (r² = 0.61, p < 0.001). On-field concussion rates ranged from 1.23 to 3.11 per 10,000 player-plays. Prohibited helmets had a raw concussion rate 1.62x higher than non-prohibited helmets and significantly higher adjusted odds (OR 1.24; 95% CI 1.04-1.47; p = 0.017). Returned kickoffs had the highest concussion probability; tight ends and wide receivers had the highest positional risk. Concussion probability increased significantly with each additional prior concussion (p < 0.001). Concussion probability was significantly lower in 2018 and 2019 relative to 2015.
limitations: Laboratory testing used only helmet-to-helmet impact conditions accounting for approximately 36% of NFL concussions, not helmet-to-ground or helmet-to-shoulder impacts. Only one helmet size per model was tested; padding thickness varies with size within a model. Player position served as a surrogate for impact frequency and severity rather than direct head impact sensor data. Findings are specific to professional-level football and may not apply to youth, high school, or collegiate play.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football equipment staff should consult annual NFL/NFLPA helmet laboratory testing results and ensure all players use models not on the prohibited list. Equipment upgrade programs targeting high-risk positions (tight ends, wide receivers, cornerbacks) offer the greatest potential for reducing concussion incidence. Kickoff rule changes already implemented in 2016 and 2018 appear to have contributed to declining concussion rates. Prior concussion history must be carefully tracked as an independent risk factor when making return-to-play and medical clearance decisions.
athleteDev: Equipment selection is a foundational element of player development programs. Integrating up-to-date helmet performance ratings into equipment purchasing decisions protects athlete longevity and developmental continuity. Educating players from high school through the NFL about helmet selection may reduce cumulative head impact burden across a career and support sustained physical development.
rtp: Prior concussion count was the strongest modifiable predictor of future concussion risk, underscoring the importance of thorough concussion documentation and conservative return-to-play decision-making. Players returning from concussion who wear lower-performing helmet models should be considered for equipment upgrades. Preseason play had the highest odds ratio for concussion relative to the regular season, warranting heightened RTP protocol vigilance during preseason camp.

citation: Merrigan JJ, Rentz LE, Hornsby WG, Wagle JP, Stone JD, Smith HT, Galster SM, Joseph M, Hagen JA. Comparisons of Countermovement Jump Force-Time Characteristics Among NCAA Division I American Football Athletes: Use of Principal Component Analysis. J Strength Cond Res. 2021;XX(X):000-000.
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined countermovement jump (CMJ) force-time characteristics across four positional groups (skills, hybrid, linemen, specialists) in 82 NCAA Division I Power 5 football athletes using dual force plates. One-way ANOVA evaluated positional differences in absolute and relative metrics, and principal component analysis (PCA) was used to reduce the dimensionality of 27 force-time variables. Four principal components explained 89.5% of the variance in CMJ metrics, representing explosive transferability to concentric power, powerful eccentric loading, countermovement strategy, and jump height and power output. Linemen produced the greatest absolute forces but had the lowest jump heights, while skill players achieved the highest jump heights and best relative power outputs.
tldr: Force plate CMJ testing reveals meaningful positional differences in jump strategy that go well beyond jump height alone. Skill players jump highest (46.7 cm) with superior relative power, while linemen produce the greatest absolute forces but struggle to transfer eccentric energy into concentric power efficiently, reflected in longer concentric phase durations and lower RSImod. Four principal components explained 89.5% of CMJ variance organized around: (1) explosive eccentric-to-concentric transferability (RSImod, concentric mean power), (2) eccentric loading quality, (3) countermovement strategy depth and duration, and (4) jump height and power output. Practitioners should monitor 1-2 metrics from each PC dimension rather than relying on a single outcome measure, and should design position-specific programs emphasizing elastic properties and stretch-shortening cycle development for linemen, and maximal strength development for skills and hybrids.
methods: Cross-sectional design using off-season (January) CMJ testing of 82 NCAA Division I football athletes (ages 18-22) grouped as linemen (n=21), hybrid (n=18), skill (n=30), and specialists (n=13). Athletes performed 2 maximal-effort, no arm-swing CMJs on ForceDecks FD4000 dual force platforms sampled at 1,000 Hz after a standardized warm-up. Metrics were averaged across two trials. One-way ANOVA with post hoc Tukey tests evaluated positional differences (p < 0.05). PCA using the correlational method via the FactoMineR package in R 4.0.3 reduced 27 force-time metrics. Bartlett's sphericity test and Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin sampling adequacy (KMO = 0.80, meritorious) confirmed factor analysis suitability. Scree plot and eigenvalue >1.0 criterion identified four principal components. Squared cosine (cos²) values and v-test statistics quantified variable importance and positional group loadings.
findings: Linemen (132.0 ± 14.5 kg) had significantly greater body mass than all groups (p < 0.001) and produced greater absolute eccentric and concentric forces, but lower relative forces and power. Skill players achieved the highest jump height (46.7 ± 4.6 cm) vs hybrid (42.8 ± 5.5), specialists (38.7 ± 4.0), and linemen (34.1 ± 5.3 cm). Skills and hybrids had the highest RSImod values (0.61 and 0.57 m/s respectively) vs linemen (0.41 m/s). Four PCs explained 89.5% of CMJ variance (PC1 = 52.3%, PC2 = 22.2%, PC3 = 9.2%, PC4 = 5.8%). PC1 was driven by RSImod (cos² = 0.85), concentric mean power (0.84), concentric RPD 50ms (0.83), concentric RPD 100ms (0.83), and eccentric-to-concentric mean force ratio (0.82). Linemen had a significant negative loading on PC1 (v-test = -5.6); skill players had significant positive loadings on PC1 (v-test = 4.4) and PC2 (v-test = 2.4). Concentric phase duration was significantly longer in linemen than skill and hybrid athletes.
limitations: Single time point (off-season January) limits generalizability across training phases and longitudinal metric tracking. Body composition and muscle physiology data were not collected, which would provide important context. PCA metric combinations may change with different scenarios, timepoints, competition levels, or sports. Sample was from one Division I Power 5 program, limiting external validity. Reliability of rate of force development metrics must be considered when applying these findings.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning staff at NFL and college programs should use force plate CMJ testing by position rather than relying solely on jump height. Linemen programs should emphasize exercises that improve stretch-shortening cycle efficiency and elastic energy storage at the hip, knee, and ankle to improve eccentric-to-concentric power transfer. Skill and hybrid programs should focus on maximizing absolute strength as the primary ceiling on total relative power output. For efficient monitoring, select 1-2 metrics per PCA dimension: RSImod or concentric mean power (PC1), eccentric peak velocity or peak power (PC2), countermovement depth or braking duration (PC3), and jump height (PC4).
athleteDev: PCA-guided metric selection enables efficient, position-specific neuromuscular monitoring throughout training blocks. Off-season CMJ profiling can identify athletes with poor eccentric-to-concentric transfer—especially linemen—who may benefit from targeted plyometric and reactive strength training blocks. Jump strategy profiles can inform appropriate positional assignment and individualized programming decisions for developing athletes at the collegiate and professional levels.
rtp: CMJ force-time monitoring provides a sensitive, non-fatiguing neuromuscular assessment tool for tracking recovery and readiness following injury. Tracking RSImod and concentric mean power longitudinally can help identify when an athlete has returned to baseline neuromuscular output. Position-specific normative reference values derived from this type of data can improve RTP decision-making by establishing meaningful benchmarks for determining readiness to return to full-contact practice.

citation: Roach MA, Owens MF. Competitive Position, Expectations, and Managerial Approaches to Risk in National Football League Football. J Sport Manag. 2025;39(5):342-353. https://doi.org/10.1123/jsm.2024-0254
abstract: This study analyzed how NFL coaches incorporate competitive position and win expectations into fourth-down decision-making using play-by-play data from 2008-2018 NFL regular seasons. Probit regression models estimated the likelihood of attempting a fourth-down conversion as a function of current win probability, pregame betting line, and maximum in-game win probability, while controlling for game situation variables, coach experience, and season fixed effects. The study tested predictions from prospect theory and rank-order tournament theory, finding that teams in weaker competitive positions and those with higher prior in-game success were systematically more aggressive on fourth down.
tldr: NFL coaches take more risk on fourth down when their win probability is low and when they have experienced high in-game win probabilities earlier in the game, consistent with prospect theory loss-frame behavior. Despite this increased aggressiveness, teams still systematically attempt too few fourth-down plays relative to the win-probability-maximizing threshold, representing a persistent coaching inefficiency. Being a pregame betting favorite and having a high prior in-game max win probability both independently increase fourth-down aggressiveness. Football operations and analytics staff should use expected-value frameworks to guide fourth-down decisions, as increased aggressiveness in these situations—even in non-optimal subsets—improves expected win outcomes by approximately 1.56 percentage points per attempt.
methods: Play-by-play data from 2008-2018 NFL regular seasons obtained from sports-reference.com (37,692 total regular season fourth downs; primary analytical subset of 18,366 plays where attempting a conversion offered a greater expected change in win probability than punting or a field goal). Three expected-change-in-win-probability models estimated the optimal fourth-down decision for each play. Probit regression models estimated probability of a fourth-down attempt as a function of win probability, betting line, max win probability, and their interactions, controlling for field position, yards to go, game quarter, lead, home team, division rival, neutral site, game week, and coach experience. OLS regression estimated actual change in win probability from fourth-down attempts.
findings: Teams attempted fourth-down conversions 12.2% of the time in the primary subset, converting 64.6% of attempts. A 10-percentage-point increase in win probability reduced the likelihood of attempting a fourth down by 2.67 percentage points in the optimal-play subset (Specification 4). Being favored by one additional betting-line point increased fourth-down attempt likelihood by approximately 0.26 percentage points. A 10-percentage-point increase in maximum in-game win probability increased the likelihood of attempting a fourth-down play by 0.54 percentage points in the optimal subset. Teams in the 0-25% win probability bin attempted plays 18.2% of the time vs 8.2% for teams in the 75-100% bin. Fourth-down attempts generated an average expected change in win probability of +1.56 percentage points relative to punting or a field goal across all situations, confirming persistent under-aggressiveness. Interactions between win probability and betting line, and win probability and max win probability, confirmed that reference point effects are concentrated in low win probability situations.
limitations: Win probability data from sports-reference.com were unavailable after 2018, limiting the sample to a period before the wider adoption of analytics-driven fourth-down culture. The study cannot separate rational quality-differential explanations from behavioral loss-aversion explanations for the betting-line effects. Coach-level motivations and individual decision processes are unobservable. Reference points are theoretical constructs not directly measured. Results may reflect changing league norms during the study period.
practicalImplications: Football operations and coaching staffs should implement data-driven fourth-down decision frameworks that use expected win probability as the primary decision criterion. The persistent tendency to punt or kick when analytics favor going for it represents a systematic and exploitable inefficiency. Understanding that teams in desperate positions are more aggressive on fourth down can inform defensive preparation and opponent game-planning. Organizations should track their own fourth-down decision accuracy over time to identify systematic biases in their coaching staff.
athleteDev: While primarily a sport management and economics paper, findings have indirect implications for game planning and situational football preparation. Understanding that fourth-down conversion attempts succeed 64.6% of the time in optimal situations informs offensive and defensive coordinators about practice priorities in short-yardage, goal-line, and fourth-down scenarios. Investing developmental resources in these situational packages may yield compounding win-probability benefits.
rtp: This paper does not address return-to-play topics. Its focus is on in-game managerial risk decisions and coaching behavior, not player health or injury recovery.

citation: Blake AL, McVicar CL, Retino M, Hall EE, Ketcham CJ. Concussion history influences sleep disturbances, symptoms, and quality of life in collegiate student-athletes. Sleep Health. 2019;5(1):72-77. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sleh.2018.10.011
abstract: This cross-sectional study determined whether self-reported concussion history was associated with persistent sleep disturbances, concussion symptoms, and quality of life (QOL) dysfunctions in 82 collegiate club and varsity student-athletes not currently recovering from a concussion. Participants completed an online survey including concussion history categorized as 0, 1-2, or 3+ concussions, the Rivermead Post-Concussion Symptom Questionnaire (RPQ), and an adapted Rivermead Head Injury Follow-Up Questionnaire (RHFUQ). Athletes with prior concussions reported significantly more sleep disturbances, concussion symptoms, and QOL dysfunctions than those with no concussion history, even after returning to sport and academics.
tldr: Collegiate athletes with a history of concussion continue to experience significantly more sleep disturbances, ongoing concussion symptoms, and reduced quality of life even after returning to play and academics. The number of sleep disturbances strongly correlated with both symptom severity (r = 0.48) and QOL dysfunction (r = 0.35). For football staff and athletic trainers, these findings emphasize that post-concussion monitoring must extend well beyond initial symptom clearance and RTP milestones—athletes with multiple concussions may require ongoing sleep screening and multidisciplinary support to protect academic performance, athletic output, and mental health.
methods: Cross-sectional online survey of 82 collegiate student-athletes (club and varsity; ages 18-25, mean 20.3 ± 1.5 years) from a small private liberal arts university in the southeastern United States, not currently recovering from a concussion. Concussion history was self-reported as 0, 1-2, or 3+ concussions. Sleep disturbances were measured using a checklist of 6 common disturbances (difficulty falling asleep, restlessness, sleeping too much, sleeping too little, difficulty staying asleep, fatigue). Concussion symptoms were assessed with the 16-item RPQ (5-point Likert scale, max score 64). QOL was assessed with an adapted 10-item RHFUQ (5-point Likert scale, max score 40). One-way ANOVAs with LSD post hoc tests compared concussion history groups (alpha < 0.05). Pearson correlations assessed relationships between sleep disturbances, symptoms, and QOL.
findings: The most common sleep disturbances were fatigue (32%), sleeping too little (27%), restlessness (24%), and difficulty falling asleep (24%). Significant group differences were found in total sleep disturbances (F = 3.73, P = .028), with the 0-concussion group having significantly fewer than the 3+ group (P = .012). Significant differences existed in total symptoms (F = 3.72, P = .029) and symptom severity (F = 4.51, P = .014), with the 0-concussion group reporting fewer symptoms than both 1-2 (P = .020) and 3+ (P = .046) concussion groups. Significant differences were found in QOL dysfunctions (F = 6.20, P = .003) and QOL dysfunction severity (F = 4.34, P = .016), with the 0-concussion group reporting fewer than both concussion groups (P = .005 and P = .006). Sleep disturbances positively correlated with symptoms (r = .54, P < .001), symptom severity (r = .48, P < .001), QOL dysfunctions (r = .35, P = .001), and QOL severity (r = .34, P = .002). Sport level (varsity vs. club) did not influence outcomes.
limitations: Modest sample size (N = 82) from a single institution limits generalizability. Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference and time since last concussion varied across participants. Self-reported concussion history is subject to recall bias. Sport-specific differences could not be fully evaluated due to small within-sport sample sizes. No objective sleep measures (e.g., actigraphy) were used. The RHFUQ was adapted from its original format, which may affect validity.
practicalImplications: Football medical and performance staff should implement routine sleep and symptom screening for athletes with any concussion history, not only during the acute recovery window. Athletes with 3 or more concussions are particularly vulnerable to persistent sleep disturbances that impair recovery, cognitive function, and academic performance. Integrating sleep quality assessments into annual pre-participation physicals and ongoing wellness monitoring programs can help identify athletes who may benefit from referral for sleep interventions, neurological follow-up, or performance support services.
athleteDev: Persistent sleep disturbances after concussion impair muscle recovery, immune function, cognitive processing, attention, and motivation—all of which are critical for athlete development. Football programs should address sleep hygiene as a performance variable for any athlete with a concussion history, potentially integrating wearable sleep monitoring and structured sleep education as components of the development program, particularly for athletes with 3 or more prior concussions.
rtp: This study demonstrates that concussion symptoms and QOL dysfunctions persist even after formal return-to-play, indicating that current RTP protocols may not fully capture lingering recovery deficits. Clinicians should consider extending post-clearance follow-up to include validated sleep assessments, especially in athletes with multiple concussion histories. Athletes reporting ongoing sleep disturbances after neurological clearance may warrant re-evaluation before resuming full-contact practice or high-demand training.

citation: Kerr ZY, Register-Mihalik JK, Kay MC, DeFreese JD, Marshall SW, Guskiewicz KM. Concussion Nondisclosure During Professional Career Among a Cohort of Former National Football League Athletes. Am J Sports Med. 2017. DOI: 10.1177/0363546517728264
abstract: This retrospective cross-sectional study examined the prevalence of and factors associated with concussion nondisclosure to team medical staff among 829 former NFL players who played from pre-WWII through 2001, using data from the Retired NFL Players General Health Survey administered in 2001 and 2010. Among those who recalled sustaining concussions, 50.3% reported not disclosing at least one concussion to medical staff during their professional career. Multivariable binomial regression identified race/ethnicity, position, playing era, and career concussion history as significant factors associated with nondisclosure, with nonwhite players and those with more career concussions showing the highest rates.
tldr: Half of former NFL players who recalled sustaining concussions during their professional career (50.3%) reported not disclosing at least one concussion to team medical staff. Nondisclosure was more prevalent among nonwhite players (adjusted PR = 1.19), linebackers and linemen, those who played after the 1976 spearing rule change, and those with higher career concussion totals—ranging from 35.5% among players with 1-2 career concussions to 75.0% among those with 10 or more. For current NFL and college football programs, these findings reinforce that concussion detection cannot rely on athlete self-disclosure alone; proactive sideline surveillance, independent neurotrauma consultants, racially and culturally concordant medical staff, and a team culture that removes perceived penalties for disclosure are essential for meaningful concussion management.
methods: Retrospective cross-sectional survey using the Retired NFL Players General Health Survey (GHS). The baseline 2001 GHS was sent to all living NFL Retired Players Association members (n = 3,729; 68.0% response rate). A 2010 follow-up GHS was sent to 2,102 eligible participants (62.6% response). The final analytical sample was 829 players who reported at least one career concussion with complete disclosure data. Concussion was defined as a blow to the head followed by associated symptoms. Multivariable binomial regression with Poisson residuals and robust variance estimation estimated adjusted prevalence ratios (PR) with 95% confidence intervals, controlling for race/ethnicity, number of years played, primary position played, professional career concussion history, and playing era (pre vs. post-1976 spearing rule change). Interaction terms between concussion history and playing era were included given P < .10 threshold.
findings: Overall, 417 (50.3%) of 829 former players reported not disclosing at least one concussion during their professional career. Nonwhite players had a significantly higher adjusted prevalence of nondisclosure than white/non-Hispanic players (PR = 1.19; 95% CI 1.02-1.38). In multivariable models, linebackers (PR = 1.53; 95% CI 1.11-2.11), offensive linemen (PR = 1.46; 95% CI 1.06-2.00), and defensive linemen (PR = 1.48; 95% CI 1.04-2.08) had significantly higher nondisclosure than running backs. A significant interaction between playing era and concussion history was found (P = .08): among players with 1-2 career concussions, post-spearing-rule-change players had 41% higher nondisclosure prevalence (PR = 1.41; 95% CI 1.05-1.89); among those with 3-4 concussions, 37% higher (PR = 1.37; 95% CI 1.07-1.74). Nondisclosure prevalence ranged from 35.5% (1-2 career concussions) to 75.0% (10+ career concussions).
limitations: Retrospective self-report design is subject to recall bias, social desirability bias, and measurement error inherent to reports of nonreports. The cohort does not include players who retired after 2001, limiting generalizability to modern NFL athletes. Reasons for nondisclosure were not captured. Findings may not generalize to other competition levels or sports. Concussion diagnoses relied on participant recall and may not meet clinical definitions. The study cannot establish causation.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs must recognize that concussion nondisclosure is systemic and cannot be solved by athlete education alone. Proactive concussion identification strategies—including independent sideline neurotrauma consultants, video review protocols, and structured symptom check-ins—are essential. Programs should evaluate racial and cultural concordance between athletes and medical staff, as discordance may reduce disclosure comfort. Team culture must explicitly remove perceived career penalties for disclosure. Athletes with high career concussion histories require enhanced active surveillance beyond standard self-report mechanisms.
athleteDev: Nondisclosure of concussions during developmental years compounds long-term brain health risk, as unmanaged concussions are associated with depression, cognitive impairment, and chronic traumatic encephalopathy. Programs must build a culture of psychological safety around concussion reporting from the earliest developmental stages—youth through collegiate and professional levels—emphasizing that disclosure protects career longevity rather than threatening it. Trust between athletes and medical staff is a prerequisite for effective concussion management.
rtp: Because nondisclosure is highest among players with the most career concussions, current RTP protocols that rely heavily on athlete self-report of symptoms are structurally vulnerable. Active surveillance tools including validated sideline assessment instruments, independent neurotrauma consultants, and video review—as currently employed in the NFL—are essential complements to self-report. RTP protocols should be designed with the explicit assumption that some proportion of concussed players will not voluntarily disclose, particularly among linemen and linebackers who show the highest nondisclosure rates.

citation: Rim AJ, Liu C, Jackson M, Miller JT, Chukwumerije N, El Chami R, Ibrahim R, Kauser T, Miller A, Simpson E, Vaughn WL, Galante A, Clark C, Putukian M, Grashow R, Baggish AL, Kim JH. Concussions Are Associated With Increases in Blood Pressure and Cardiovascular Risk in American-Style Football Athletes. JACC Advances. 2025;4(5):101717. DOI: 10.1016/j.jacadv.2025.101717
abstract: This longitudinal case-controlled study examined whether clinically diagnosed concussions during collegiate American-style football (ASF) participation are associated with increases in systolic blood pressure (SBP) and maladaptive cardiovascular phenotypes. Thirty-nine ASF athletes who sustained concussions were followed for up to 2 seasons and compared to 39 weight- and position-matched controls at two NCAA Division-I programs (Georgia Tech, Furman). Cardiovascular assessments included blood pressure, echocardiography, and vascular applanation tonometry. Concussions were independently associated with increased pulse wave velocity (PWV), elevated SBP and DBP, increased LV mass index, and decreased diastolic function (E' velocity), findings that persisted and worsened through 2 seasons of follow-up.
tldr: Collegiate football players who sustain concussions develop measurable and progressive cardiovascular damage — including arterial stiffening and rising blood pressure — that extends well beyond the brain. This is the first prospective longitudinal study demonstrating that concussions are an independent cardiovascular risk factor among active ASF athletes, with hypertension prevalence rising from 36% to 86% in concussed athletes over two seasons. Sports medicine and performance staff should incorporate blood pressure surveillance into standard post-concussion management protocols, as hypertension may develop silently and compound long-term health risks for football athletes.
