Overall, 89.7% of players returned to play with an average return time of 80.5 ± 132.9 days; defensive players returned faster (68.1 days) than offensive players (90.1 days), and higher age at injury significantly increased failure-to-RTP risk (OR=1.24, p=0.0088).
tldr: High ankle sprains are rising in frequency in the NFL, now occurring at nearly 2.5 per 10,000 player exposures — up from 1.75 a decade ago — and represent a significant source of missed playing time at approximately 11 weeks per injury. Nine in 10 players successfully return to play, but offensive linemen and wide receivers carry the greatest positional burden. Older players at the time of injury are meaningfully more likely to fail RTP (OR=1.24 per year of age), while more experienced players are more likely to return. Defensive players return faster than offensive players. Performance staff should plan for ~11-week absences when a high ankle sprain occurs and recognize that younger/more experienced players have a clear RTP advantage.
methods: Retrospective review of publicly available NFL injury data from the 2009-10 through 2019-20 regular seasons using Pro-Football-Reference.com and ESPN. Injuries identified as 'high ankle sprain,' 'syndesmosis,' or 'syndesmotic injury.' Player characteristics recorded: age (mean 25.8 ± 3.1 yr), BMI (mean 31.8 ± 4.6), years of experience, and position. Injury setting (preseason/regular season/postseason) and timing documented. RTP defined as first date activated and played a preseason/training camp or regular season game. Incidence calculated per 10,000 athlete exposures (53 players/team, 105 practices/season, 20 games/season, 32 teams). Linear regression assessed incidence trends; multivariate logistic regression identified RTP failure risk factors (controlling for year of injury, position, BMI, age, experience). Kaplan-Meier survivorship plots computed for RTP.
findings: 533 high ankle sprains identified in 498 players. Incidence increased linearly from 1.75 to 2.49 per 10,000 player exposures (R²=0.3357). Offensive players: 304/533 injuries (57.0%); offensive linemen most affected (111/304, 36.5%), followed by wide receivers (73/304, 24%). Defensive secondary accounted for 86/228 (37.7%) of defensive injuries. 89.7% (478/533) of players returned to play; average RTP 80.5 ± 132.9 days. Defensive players returned faster (68.1 ± 114.6 days) than offensive players (90.1 ± 144.8 days, p=0.084). Higher age at injury increased failure-to-RTP risk (OR=1.24, 95% CI 1.05-1.46, p=0.0088). More years of experience decreased failure-to-RTP risk (OR=0.71, 95% CI 0.59-0.86, p=0.0004). Injuries occurred predominantly in the regular season (422/533, 79.2%).
limitations: Data sourced from public databases, which may result in over- or under-reporting; high-profile players may be overrepresented. Minor high ankle injuries with rapid recovery may be underreported, potentially inflating the average RTP time. Injury severity, imaging findings, and treatment type (operative vs. nonoperative) were not available, preventing severity-stratified analysis. Player injury history was unavailable, precluding analysis of re-injury risk. Variables such as playing surface and contact mechanism could not be assessed. Team rehabilitation protocols and personnel decisions influencing RTP could not be controlled.
practicalImplications: With an average RTP of ~11 weeks and a linearly increasing incidence rate, high ankle sprains represent one of the most significant sources of offensive player unavailability in the NFL. Programs should have standardized high ankle sprain management protocols that account for the full expected timeline rather than the 2-3 week timelines reported in older NCAA literature. Age at injury is an independent predictor of RTP failure — players 28+ years old warrant closer monitoring and more conservative RTP criteria. Offensive linemen and wide receivers are the highest-risk positional groups and should receive targeted preventive attention including ankle stability training and appropriate taping/bracing protocols.
athleteDev: High ankle sprains have a documented rising incidence in the NFL, likely related to increasing athlete size, speed, and strength — all of which are also developmental targets. This creates a tension where optimizing physical development may increase syndesmotic injury risk, particularly for offensive linemen who bear the highest absolute injury burden. Developmental programs should incorporate ankle dorsiflexion mobility, syndesmotic stability exercises, and progressive contact load management to prepare linemen and wide receivers for the high-force environments that cause these injuries. The injury peak in the regular season (79.2%) rather than preseason suggests that load and collision intensity — not conditioning — are primary drivers.
rtp: High ankle sprains require an average of 80.5 days for RTP in the NFL, approximately 3-4x longer than historically reported in NCAA studies, likely reflecting more severe injury profiles or higher return-to-performance standards at the professional level. The 90% RTP rate is encouraging, but the 10% failure rate — concentrated in older, less-experienced players — should inform realistic counseling at time of injury. Defensive players return approximately 22 days faster than offensive players (68.1 vs. 90.1 days), which may reflect positional demand differences or different operative management rates. Age at injury is the strongest modifiable risk predictor: each additional year of age increases failure-to-RTP odds by 24%. Conservative criteria and extended timelines should be applied for veteran players sustaining these injuries.

citation: Hill KP, Wu A, Solomon G, Mack C, Ling G, Mayer T, Sills A. Design Considerations in the National Football League and National Football League Players Association Prescription Drug Monitoring Program: An Introduction and Overview. Current Sports Medicine Reports. 2021;20(11):614-616.
abstract: This special communication describes the design and implementation of the NFL/NFLPA Prescription Drug Monitoring Program (PDMP), established in May 2019 as part of the collectively bargained Pain Management Committee (PMC). The PDMP monitors all prescriptions entered in the NFL Electronic Medical Record (EMR) for all 32 clubs, tracking opioids, muscle relaxants, NSAIDs, nonnarcotic analgesics, and other pain medications from training camp through the week following each club's last game. Biannual reports are issued to NFL and NFLPA medical leadership, with deidentified trend data shared with team medical staffs to inform evidence-based prescribing practices.
tldr: The NFL and NFLPA created the first league-wide prescription drug monitoring program in professional sports in 2019, mandating that all 32 teams document every pain medication prescription in a shared EMR system. The PDMP's goals are to understand prescribing patterns, identify potential opioid misuse, and guide evidence-based pain management — recognizing that NFL players face continuous cycles of performance, pain, and recovery that create unique opioid risk. For team medical and performance staff, this program means prescription practices are now tracked and benchmarked league-wide. Staff should be familiar with the PDMP's scope, use it to guide prescribing toward evidence-based alternatives to opioids, and treat it as a tool for player health rather than surveillance.
methods: Special communication describing the design and implementation process of the NFL/NFLPA PDMP. Origin: May 2019 NFL/NFLPA collective bargaining agreement mandated documentation of all player medications. IQVIA (independent health IT company) contracted to design data collection protocols and monitor real-time reporting. Four PDMP components: (1) prescription data collection via NFL EMR, (2) required club reporting from training camp through season end, (3) required player reporting of all prescription medications to club physicians, (4) biannual deidentified reports to league medical leadership and individual clubs. Drug classification uses Generic Product Identifier (GPI) groups mapped from FDA National Drug Codes: opioids, muscle relaxants, NSAIDs, nonnarcotic analgesics, corticosteroids, narcotic antitussives, nerve pain medications, antimigraine medications.
findings: The PDMP was the first of its kind in professional or amateur sports. It captures all prescriptions written for NFL players by club and unaffiliated physicians entered into the NFL EMR. Clubs can access their own aggregated prescribing patterns benchmarked against the rest of the league via an interactive dashboard. The PMC used PDMP data to develop educational resources on evidence-based alternatives to opioids, including cannabinoids, for both players and medical staffs. The program identified that completeness and accuracy depend on diligent manual entry by club medical staff, creating potential for incomplete capture. No outcome data on prescribing changes or opioid misuse rates are reported in this introductory overview.
limitations: The EMR is not the only route through which prescriptions can be ordered; some prescriptions must be entered manually by busy club medical staff who may not always be the primary prescriber. The EMR records prescriptions written but does not record whether the prescription was filled or taken. Injury diagnosis linking prescriptions to specific conditions may be missing in some records. No consensus definition exists for aberrant prescribing behavior, reducing the PDMP's ability to detect problematic prescribing patterns. The program was in early stages at publication, with data collection processes still evolving. Prescriptions from unaffiliated physicians must be self-reported by players to club staff for entry, creating a dependency on player compliance.
practicalImplications: NFL team medical and performance staff should understand that prescription practices for all pain medications — including NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, and opioids — are now monitored, benchmarked league-wide, and reported biannually to team and league medical leadership. This creates accountability and an opportunity to shift toward evidence-based alternatives to opioids for pain management. The PMC's educational resources on cannabinoids and non-opioid analgesics should be integrated into team pain management protocols. Staff should actively support complete and accurate EMR entry of all prescriptions to ensure the PDMP fulfills its patient safety mission. The program sets a precedent that could be adopted by college programs seeking to monitor prescription patterns in student-athletes.
athleteDev: While the PDMP is focused on professional players, its underlying principle — systematic monitoring of prescription pain medication use in the context of performance, pain, and recovery cycles — is equally applicable to college football programs. Young players entering high-load training environments are at risk for inappropriate opioid use patterns that can persist post-career. Development programs should implement education on opioid risks, establish clear non-opioid-first pain management protocols, and consider tracking prescription patterns as a component of player health documentation. The PMC's emphasis on educating players about evidence-based alternatives to opioids is a model that college programs should adopt proactively.
rtp: The PDMP's recognition that NFL players are engaged in 'continuous cycles of performance, pain, and recovery' is directly relevant to RTP contexts. Pain medication prescribing during injury rehabilitation is a critical period where opioid overuse patterns can develop. Team medical staff making RTP decisions should audit prescription patterns during rehabilitation — enabled by PDMP data — to ensure that pain management is not masking incomplete recovery or driving premature return. The shift toward evidence-based non-opioid alternatives (NSAIDs, nerve pain medications, cannabinoids) for managing post-injury pain during rehabilitation aligns with both the PDMP's goals and best-practice RTP medicine.

citation: Svirsky SE, Wood CC, Raymond O, McIntyre P, Appleton H, Wagner C, Gill J, Puccio AM. Detection of Injury Biomarkers in Sweat of Collegiate Athletes Pre- and Post-Football Season: A Pilot Study. Neurotrauma Reports. 2025;6(1):700-705. DOI: 10.1177/08977151251367345
abstract: This pilot study investigated whether sweat could serve as a non-invasive biofluid to detect traumatic brain injury (TBI) biomarkers in collegiate football players across a season. Thirty-four NCAA Division III football players wore non-occlusive sweat patches (~24 h) at pre-season and 18 returned for post-season collection. Four hallmark TBI proteins—total-tau, neurofilament light (Nf-L), glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), and ubiquitin carboxyl-terminal hydrolase L1 (UCH-L1)—were quantified via Simoa immunoarray. GFAP and UCH-L1 were significantly elevated pre-season in athletes with a prior TBI history compared to those without, and total-tau and UCH-L1 increased significantly from pre- to post-season despite no reported concussions during the season.
tldr: Sweat may provide a practical, non-invasive window into cumulative brain stress in football athletes. This study is the first to detect TBI-related proteins (tau, GFAP, UCH-L1, Nf-L) in sweat and found that pre-season levels of GFAP and UCH-L1 were elevated in athletes with prior TBI history—suggesting sensitivity to historical injury—while tau and UCH-L1 rose across a full season even without reported concussions. For sports medicine and performance staff, this points toward potential wearable biomarker monitoring tools that could flag subconcussive accumulation before symptoms appear, supporting smarter return-to-play and long-term athlete health decisions.
methods: Prospective observational pilot study; N=34 pre-season, N=18 post-season; NCAA Division III football players (2023 season); non-occlusive PharmChem sweat patch worn ~24 h on the upper arm at pre-season (August) and post-season (December); Simoa Neurology 4-Plex A Advantage kit (Quanterix) used to measure total-tau, Nf-L, GFAP, and UCH-L1 at 4x dilution in duplicate; OSU-TBI questionnaire and Rivermead Post Concussion Symptoms Questionnaire used to assess TBI history and symptoms; Mann-Whitney and Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed-rank tests used for unpaired and paired comparisons.
findings: Athletes with a prior TBI history showed significantly higher pre-season GFAP (p=0.0036) and UCH-L1 (p=0.0169) compared to those without TBI history. Paired pre-to-post-season analysis (N=17) showed significant increases in total-tau (p=0.0046; mean 0.89 to 3.18 pg/mL) and UCH-L1 (p=0.0032; mean 40.74 to 125.30 pg/mL). Nf-L and GFAP did not differ significantly between pre- and post-season. No concussions were reported during the season, suggesting these changes reflect subconcussive exposure.
limitations: No non-contact sport comparison group and no female athletes included, limiting specificity to head impacts. No standardized method to normalize sweat volume or rate across participants. Small sample size with high pre-season attrition (6 patches fell off, 2 not returned). Self-reported concussion history and symptom data are subject to recall and reporting bias. Blood was not collected concurrently, preventing direct comparison of sweat vs. serum biomarker profiles.
practicalImplications: Sweat-based TBI biomarker monitoring could offer a scalable, non-invasive tool for NFL and college football medical staffs to track cumulative neurological stress across a season without venipuncture. Elevated GFAP and UCH-L1 at pre-season in players with prior TBI history suggest these markers may serve as baseline risk indicators. Rising tau and UCH-L1 levels across the season—even absent reported concussions—support the concept of sub-threshold cumulative load monitoring, which could inform individualized load management and return-to-play thresholds.
athleteDev: For athlete development programs, this research supports the idea that repetitive sub-concussive exposure across a season produces measurable neurological stress even in asymptomatic athletes. Monitoring sweat biomarkers longitudinally could help development staff understand individual neurological resilience and adapt training and contact loads accordingly, particularly for younger players with prior TBI histories.
rtp: This study is directly relevant to return-to-play decision-making. Athletes with prior TBI history showed elevated GFAP and UCH-L1 at the start of the season—biomarkers that are already used in clinical TBI settings. Elevated post-season tau and UCH-L1 without reported concussions suggests that current symptom-based RTP protocols may miss biologically meaningful injury accumulation. Sweat biomarker panels could eventually supplement existing RTP protocols as an objective, non-invasive physiological check.

citation: Heintz EC, Breaux L, Lewis A, Foreman JJ. Determinants of Missed Games Following Concussions in the National Football League. Frontiers in Sports and Active Living. 2020;2:595445. DOI: 10.3389/fspor.2020.595445
abstract: This study examined which factors beyond medical severity predict the number of NFL games missed following a concussion. Using PBS Frontline Concussion Watch data from 2012–2015 (643 reported concussions), researchers applied OLS regression with player salary, position (quarterback vs. other), average plays per game, prior concussions, season, and week of injury as predictors. Results showed that quarterbacks missed approximately one more game than non-quarterbacks, while players involved in more plays per game missed fewer games. Prior concussions and later seasons also predicted more missed games, while salary and week of injury did not reach significance.
tldr: Not all NFL players receive equal time off after a concussion—player value and role appear to influence recovery time beyond medical factors alone. Quarterbacks (the most cognitively demanding position) were given significantly more recovery time, while heavily relied-upon players were returned faster despite their high play volume. Each successive concussion added approximately 0.25 games missed, and time-off has increased each year from 2012 to 2015, likely reflecting evolving protocols and media pressure. For football performance and medical staffs, this highlights potential disparities in concussion management and the importance of applying consistent, evidence-based return-to-play protocols regardless of position or role.
methods: Longitudinal observational design; secondary analysis of publicly available data; concussion data from PBS Frontline Concussion Watch (2012–2015 NFL seasons); N=643 reported concussions; independent variables: player salary (millions), quarterback status (dummy variable), average plays per game pre-injury, prior concussions, season count (2012=1 through 2015=4), and week of season; dependent variable: games missed; OLS regression with multicollinearity checks (VIF ≤1.15); stepwise regression used for model validation.
findings: Quarterbacks missed approximately 0.91 more games than other positions (p=0.003). Players averaging more plays per game missed fewer games (coefficient=-0.007, p=0.033); a player averaging 27 plays/game was expected to miss ~0.28 more games than one averaging 67 plays/game. Each prior concussion added ~0.25 games missed (p=0.059). Each subsequent season from 2012–2015 added ~0.16 games missed (p=0.050). Salary (p=0.897) and week of injury (p=0.470) were not significant predictors. Mean games missed was 0.99 (range 0–13).
limitations: Study did not account for concussion severity, which is the primary medical determinant of recovery time. Data are limited to three seasons (2012–2015) and do not include more recent seasons with updated protocols. Only reported concussions were included; an estimated 56% of NFL players admit to hiding concussions, meaning a substantial proportion of events are absent from the dataset. Individual differences in concussion response and symptoms were not captured.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and performance staffs should be aware that non-medical factors—particularly player value and position—may be influencing time-off decisions after concussions. Players who are heavily relied upon (high average plays per game) appear to be returned to play sooner, potentially before full recovery, which increases long-term CTE and repeat-injury risk. Consistent application of evidence-based concussion protocols regardless of a player's roster value is essential for long-term player health and liability management.
athleteDev: From an athlete development standpoint, the finding that high-usage players receive less recovery time post-concussion is a concern at all levels of football, not just the NFL. Programs should build concussion monitoring systems that are independent of playing time or positional value, ensuring that players whose roles demand high snap counts are not pressured back prematurely.
rtp: This study provides direct evidence that return-to-play decisions in the NFL are influenced by factors other than medical recovery, specifically player position and role importance. Quarterbacks are protected longer, suggesting cognitive position demands are recognized informally even if not formalized in protocols. Medical staffs should advocate for position-blind, symptom- and biomarker-based RTP standards and monitor whether high-utilization skill players are being cleared using the same rigor applied to quarterbacks.

citation: Wannop JW, Stefanyshyn DJ, Anderson RB, Coughlin MJ, Kent R. Development of a Footwear Sizing System in the National Football League. Sports Health. 2018. DOI: 10.1177/1941738118789402
abstract: This descriptive review details the development and deployment of an objective 3D footwear fitting system in the NFL, motivated by evidence that improperly fitted footwear contributes to foot injuries including fifth metatarsal stress fractures, turf toe, blisters, and foot deformities. The authors synthesized literature from 1980–2017 (65 sources) and documented three development phases: scanning 1,451 NFL players to characterize foot shape, measuring the internal dimensions of all relevant shoe models via radiographic contrast imaging, and building a proprietary shoe-foot matching algorithm refined with player comfort feedback. Measurement of 50+ nominal size-12 shoes revealed a 15 mm range in internal length (equivalent to >1.5 US shoe sizes) and 12 mm range in width, confirming the inadequacy of nominal sizing alone.
tldr: Proper shoe fit in football is a meaningful injury prevention issue—this paper documents the NFL's development of the first sports league-specific 3D footwear fitting system. Using 3D foot scans of 1,451 players and radiographic internal measurements of hundreds of shoe models, the system matches individual foot shapes to available footwear. Key finding: nominal shoe sizes are highly variable (a size-12 shoe can differ by more than 1.5 US sizes across brands), meaning players who select footwear by label alone may be chronically wearing ill-fitting shoes. For football equipment and sports medicine staffs, implementing 3D-scan-based fitting can reduce foot and lower extremity injury risk.
methods: Descriptive review synthesizing literature from 1980–2017 (65 sources); three-phase NFL system development: (1) foot scanning of 1,451 NFL players in locker rooms using commercially available 3D scanners; (2) internal length and width measurement of 50+ shoe models via PlusMed12 device and radiographic contrast imaging; (3) development of a proprietary shoe-foot matching algorithm using existing foot scan databases, subjective comfort assessments from 43 scanned players (15 completed full survey), and expert fitting input from NFL equipment managers; findings compared to general population anthropometric data.
findings: Internal length of nominal size-12 shoes ranged from 287–302 mm in 2016 (15 mm range, >1.5 US size difference) and 291–302 mm in 2017 (11 mm range after manufacturer feedback). Internal width ranged 93.5–105.5 mm in 2016 and 92–107 mm in 2017. More than 50% of NFL players had at least a half nominal size difference between their right and left feet. Brands labeled as 'wide' were not consistently wider than 'regular' versions before manufacturer feedback. NFL player foot length and width exceeded general population norms.
limitations: The fitting algorithm and shoe measurement methods are proprietary, limiting independent validation. No published outcome data yet demonstrate whether the system reduces lower extremity injury rates. Player comfort assessments were completed by only 15 of 43 scanned players. Shoe libraries require constant updating as new models are introduced yearly, and dimensional scaling across sizes could not be validated. Individual fit preferences introduce variability that a purely geometric algorithm cannot fully capture.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football equipment staffs should consider implementing 3D foot scanning as part of pre-season or draft evaluations. The demonstrated variability in shoe internal dimensions—even within the same nominal size—means that relying on labeled sizes alone is inadequate for injury prevention. Properly sized footwear can reduce the incidence of fifth metatarsal stress fractures, turf toe, and blisters, all of which affect availability and performance. This system could also be used to standardize footwear procurement and better match position-specific footwear needs.
athleteDev: For developmental football programs, this research supports proactive foot scanning as part of the equipment intake process. Athletes entering programs with improperly sized footwear from youth or high school levels may already have developing foot pathology. Identifying fit mismatches early—particularly in linemen who bear extreme ground reaction forces—can protect long-term foot health and reduce chronic injury burden over an athlete's career.
rtp: While not a clinical RTP study, this work has indirect RTP relevance: foot injuries from poor footwear fit (fifth metatarsal fractures, turf toe) are among the most common causes of missed games in football. An objective fitting system that reduces the incidence of these injuries reduces the RTP burden on medical staffs. Additionally, for athletes returning from foot or ankle injuries, a 3D-based fitting system could ensure that return-to-play footwear properly accommodates swelling, post-surgical anatomy, or orthotic use.

citation: Rosbrook P, Looney DP, Margolis LM, Perez Yudin S, Hostler D, Pryor RR, Pryor JL. Dietary Carbohydrates Influence the Performance Outcomes of Short-Term Heat Acclimation. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2025 (Published ahead of print). DOI: 10.1249/MSS.0000000000003726
abstract: This RCT examined whether a high-carbohydrate (HC) diet enhances 3.22 km run time trial (TT) performance in the heat following a 6-day, exercise-intensive short-term heat acclimation (STHA) protocol. Fourteen healthy military-age males were pair-matched by fitness and randomized to either a HC diet (70% kcal from CHO, ~6 g/kg/day, n=7) or a lower-CHO comparison diet (COMP; 35% kcal from CHO, ~3 g/kg/day, n=7) during and for one week after HA. Both groups achieved equivalent physiological heat adaptations (reduced HR, rectal and skin temperature, increased sweat rate), but only the HC group improved TT performance at one day (p=0.01, g=0.59) and five days post-HA (p=0.04, g=0.59), with HC running 7.0±4.9% faster than COMP at one day post-HA (p=0.03, g=1.23).
tldr: Carbohydrate intake during and after an intense, exercise-based heat acclimation protocol is a critical determinant of whether performance actually improves after HA. Despite both groups achieving equivalent thermoregulatory adaptations, only athletes consuming ~6 g/kg/day of carbohydrate (70% kcal) showed meaningful improvements in a 3.22 km heat-based run time trial—improving ~7-8% versus essentially no change in the lower-CHO group. For football strength and conditioning coaches running summer camp or two-a-day training blocks in heat, this means that failing to meet carbohydrate targets during high-exertion heat exposure can nullify expected performance gains from heat adaptation, even when physiological markers of acclimation look normal.
methods: Randomized controlled trial; N=14 healthy males of military age (18-40 years), paired by VO2peak and training volume; 6-day STHA protocol (6 sessions in a hot room: 39.3°C, 38.3% RH) with high-intensity intervals until Tre reached 38.5°C, then maintained Tre 38.5-39.0°C for ≥60 min; HC diet: 70% kcal CHO (~6 g/kg/day); COMP diet: 35% kcal CHO (~3 g/kg/day); isonitrogenous (1.4 g/kg/day protein); 3.22 km self-paced run TT in heat (37.2°C, 41.7% RH) assessed pre-HA (HST1), 1 day post-HA (HST2), and 5 days post-HA (HST3); VO2peak assessed in hot room; mixed-model ANCOVA with HST1 as covariate; Bonferroni post-hoc corrections.
findings: Both groups acclimated equally, showing significant reductions in end-walk HR, rectal temperature (Tre), skin temperature (Tsk), and increased sweat rate at HST2 and HST3 (all p<0.01). HC group ran significantly faster at HST2 vs. COMP (959±103 s vs. 1067±172 s, p=0.02, g=0.71). HC improved TT from baseline at HST2 (p=0.01, g=0.59) and HST3 (p=0.04, g=0.59); COMP did not improve at either time point. HC showed 7.0±4.9% TT reduction at HST2 vs. 0.7±4.7% for COMP (p=0.03, g=1.23) and 8.4±4.9% vs. 2.8±3.9% improvement at HST3 (p=0.01, g=1.18). No between-group differences in any physiological strain marker during TTs.
limitations: Small sample size (n=7 per group) limits statistical power. Only male participants; findings may not generalize to female athletes. Participants could not be blinded to diet assignment. The HA exercise protocol was not pair-matched or randomized across sessions due to individual capacity differences. The 35% kcal CHO comparison diet, while representing the lowest recommended CHO floor for active individuals, is below the habitual intake of young active males (~48% kcal), potentially exaggerating the contrast. No muscle glycogen measurements were taken to directly confirm glycogen status differences between groups.
practicalImplications: For NFL and college football coaching and performance staffs, this study directly challenges the assumption that completing a heat acclimation block guarantees performance improvements. If athletes are not meeting carbohydrate targets (~6 g/kg/day) during pre-season two-a-days or training camp in the heat, they may undergo the physiological stress of HA without realizing the performance benefits. Nutrition staffs should actively ensure CHO targets are met during any heat-intensive training block, particularly when athletes face high energy demands, limited meal access, or selective eating patterns.
athleteDev: During pre-season training camp and summer conditioning—periods of heavy heat exposure in football—athletes' carbohydrate availability may be the limiting factor determining whether the training stimulus translates to performance adaptation. Programs should prioritize nutrition education and intake monitoring during heat-intensive developmental periods, particularly for younger athletes who may under-eat due to fatigue, unfamiliar environments, or poor nutrition habits. Meeting ~6 g/kg/day CHO during HA blocks appears to be the threshold for converting heat stress into performance gains.
rtp: Athletes returning to practice in hot conditions after injury-related absence may face an unacclimatized state combined with potential nutritional deficits from their recovery period. This study suggests that ensuring adequate carbohydrate intake during the re-acclimation phase is critical for safely restoring performance capacity in the heat. RTP protocols for summer and pre-season returns should include a nutritional assessment component to confirm CHO availability supports the heat re-acclimation process.

citation: Frank DJ, King MJ, Dennard CD, Macnamara BN. Discriminant Function Analysis Reveals Which Combination of Measures from the NFL Scouting Combine Predict NFL Performance. Journal of Expertise. 2023;6(2):130-163.
abstract: This study applied discriminant function analysis (DFA) to 20 years of NFL Scouting Combine data (2000–2018) across 10 position groups to identify the best combination of physical attributes predicting draft status, starter frequency (games started, upper vs. lower quartile in first 5 years), and elite honors (multiple All-Pro/Pro Bowl selections). Using 8 Combine measures—including a novel arm-length-adjusted bench press work variable—the study found that combinations of traits predicted defensive success more consistently than offensive success, with 6 of 10 DFA models for defense finding multivariate predictors compared to 1 of 10 for offense. Speed (40-yard dash) emerged as a universal factor, while size variables (weight, height) and explosiveness measures (vertical, broad jump) contributed in position-specific combinations.
tldr: This is the largest and most methodologically rigorous analysis of NFL Scouting Combine predictive validity to date. The key insight for scouts and general managers: at most positions it is the right combination of physical traits—not any single measure—that predicts success. For defensive positions especially, DFA identified novel multivariate patterns that were missed by previous univariate approaches. Speed remains king at nearly every position, but big, fast edge rushers who can also broad jump (horizontal explosiveness) outperform those who are just fast or just big. For football staffs using Combine data in evaluation, this work provides a position-by-position roadmap of which trait combinations actually predict starter frequency and elite honors—versus which ones scouts simply appear to value during drafting.
methods: Secondary data analysis; Combine participants from 2000–2018 sourced from pro-football-reference.com; 10 position groups analyzed: QB, RB, WR, TE, OL, DL, edge rushers, LB, CB, S; predictors: 40-yard dash, 20-yard shuttle, 3-cone drill, height, weight, vertical jump, broad jump, and bench press work (arm-length-adjusted reps × force × distance in foot-pounds); success metrics: draft status, games started quartile (upper vs. lower, first 5 years), and multiple career honors (≥2 All-Pro or Pro Bowl selections); stepwise discriminant function analysis (DFA); multivariate outliers removed via Mahalanobis distance (p<0.001); predictors Winsorized at ±2.99 SD; 74% data completeness threshold for predictor inclusion; alpha=0.05 two-tailed.
findings: DFA successfully predicted draft status for 8 of 10 position groups (5–18% of between-group variance explained). For offensive positions, single measures typically drove models: RB speed (40-yard dash) predicted draft status, starter frequency, and elite honors; WR speed + vertical jump predicted draft; OL vertical jump predicted starter frequency; OL bench press work predicted elite status. For defensive positions, multivariate combinations were more common: edge rushers—speed, vertical, and weight predicted draft; weight and broad jump predicted starter frequency; speed and weight predicted elite honors. DFA models for starters correctly classified 61–70% of players at positions where models were significant. No Combine measures significantly predicted QB elite honors or WR elite honors.
limitations: Combine data explained only 5–18% of variance in most significant models; college performance and competition level—known predictors—were excluded to allow uniform analysis. Missing data required dropping participants, especially for 3-cone and 20-yard shuttle. Pre-draft position classifications may not match NFL positions played. Outcome variables (games started, honors) are sensitive to team success and do not capture position-specific contributions like blocking grades. No cognitive or personality measures included despite their likely relevance to NFL success. Arm length estimates used for bench press work calculation are approximations.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football scouting and coaching staffs should use Combine data as a combination-based supplement to film evaluation, not as a single-metric filter. At skill positions, scouts appear to be mostly using the right variables, but edge rushers who are big and explosive in horizontal direction (broad jump) are undervalued relative to those who combine size with vertical explosiveness. Defensive tackle speed and linebacker weight are overlooked predictors of performance. Scouts should be cautious about over-weighting vertical jump for edge rushers and cornerback strength, as these did not predict performance after controlling for other variables.
athleteDev: For NFL draft preparation and college player development, this study identifies which physical attribute combinations most reliably translate to professional success by position. Edge rushers should develop both speed and horizontal explosiveness (broad jump) not just vertical power. Linebackers benefit from combining speed with size. For offensive linemen, vertical jumping ability (lower body power) predicts starter frequency while upper body strength (bench press work) predicts elite performance—suggesting strength and power training should be balanced, not siloed, in OL development programs.
rtp: While not a RTP study, the Combine attribute profiles identified here have relevance for post-injury return decisions. Athletes returning from lower extremity injuries can be assessed against the position-specific athleticism benchmarks identified as performance-predictive—particularly broad jump (horizontal explosiveness) for edge rushers and vertical jump for offensive linemen and wide receivers—to determine whether they have regained the physical capabilities associated with successful NFL performance prior to full return.

citation: McGinniss A, Guinand LA, Vosbikian M, Ahmed I. Distal Biceps Ruptures in National Football League Players: Return to Play and Performance Analysis. Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery. 2021. doi:10.1016/j.jse.2020.11.010
abstract: This retrospective case-control study examined distal biceps ruptures in NFL players from the 2000–2016 seasons, focusing on return-to-play (RTP) rates and post-surgical performance. Thirty-five NFL players with surgically treated distal biceps ruptures were identified through publicly available sources and matched to a control group by position, age, experience, and performance score. Pre- and post-injury performance statistics and career length were compared between cohort and control groups using paired-samples Student t-tests.
tldr: Distal biceps ruptures surgically repaired in NFL players carry a high return-to-play rate (~94%), with an average time to return of approximately 325 days (about one year). Post-surgical performance scores across most positions are statistically comparable to pre-injury levels and to matched controls, meaning coaches and medical staff can counsel athletes that this injury, while prolonged, does not broadly diminish long-term performance. Offensive linemen are the most commonly affected position and show a modest but statistically significant reduction in games played per season post-surgery compared to controls, though their career length is not affected.
methods: Retrospective case-control study of NFL players from seasons 2000–2016 with surgically treated distal biceps ruptures identified via publicly available media, injury reports, and team websites confirmed by two independent searches. Thirty-five players were included. Each was matched to a control by position (CB, DE, DT, LB, TE, RB, S, OL), age (±1 year), experience, and performance score. Pre- vs. post-injury and cohort vs. control comparisons used paired-samples and independent-samples t-tests (p<0.05). Performance scores used a previously published standardized scoring system. Career length and games per season were also analyzed.
findings: 35 NFL players identified; 33 (94%) returned to sport at an average of 324.7 ± 112 days (351.4 ± 123.9 days per the abstract). Offensive linemen made up 35% (n=12) of the cohort; two OL (6%) failed to return. Average age at injury was 28.5 ± 3.5 years. Defensive tackles showed a significant drop in pre- vs. post-surgery performance scores (2.9 ± 0.7 vs. 1.7 ± 0.3, p=0.02), but no significant difference was found when DT cohort was compared to controls post-surgery. Offensive linemen played significantly fewer games per season post-surgery vs. controls (11.1 ± 3.4 vs. 13.6 ± 2.1, p=0.04). Career length post-surgery did not differ between OL and controls (p=0.79). No other positions showed significant differences in games played, career length, or performance scores.
limitations: Small cohort size due to rarity of the injury may have underpowered the study. Reliance on publicly available data introduces selection, reporting, and observer bias; surgical technique (1- vs. 2-incision) and specific operative details were not accessible from the NFL directly. No performance scoring system existed for offensive linemen, limiting analysis for that position group. Findings may not generalize beyond elite NFL athletes.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and performance staff should counsel players that surgical repair of distal biceps ruptures results in high RTP rates (~94%) but a prolonged absence of roughly one year. Performance upon return is generally preserved across positions. Offensive linemen may play fewer games per season post-surgery, likely due to the biomechanical demands of their position placing repetitive eccentric load on the biceps. Long-tenured players (8–9+ seasons) appear more susceptible, which may inform load management strategies for veteran linemen.
athleteDev: Offensive and defensive linemen are the most at-risk positions for distal biceps ruptures due to the high eccentric biceps loading inherent in blocking and hand-fighting. Strength and conditioning staff should monitor cumulative upper-extremity strain in veteran linemen and prioritize eccentric biceps strength work and forearm supination/flexion loading tolerance as preventive training strategies.
rtp: Average time to RTP after surgical distal biceps repair in NFL players is approximately 324–352 days (nearly a full calendar year). A 94% RTP rate supports aggressive surgical intervention. Return decisions should be guided by position-specific demands; offensive linemen may require extended monitoring given their reduced games-per-season post-surgery. Post-surgical performance is broadly comparable to pre-injury levels, supporting optimistic return-to-competition counseling.

citation: Kuhn AW, Coughlin MJ, McGonegle SJ, Herzog MM, Weir N, O'Cain CM, Anderson RB. Distal Tibiofibular Syndesmosis Injuries in the National Football League (NFL): A Spectrum of Pathology That Correlates With Time to Return to Full Participation. Sports Health. 2024. doi:10.1177/19417381241253223
abstract: This retrospective cross-sectional study evaluated 83 external rotation ankle injuries involving the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis in NFL players from the 2017–2019 seasons. All injuries were identified through the NFL Injury Surveillance Database, confirmed by video review, and subjected to comprehensive MRI review by a multidisciplinary team. Stepwise linear regression was used to identify MRI variables that predicted time to return to full participation.
tldr: High ankle sprains in the NFL are rarely isolated: only 13% of syndesmosis injuries occur without concomitant pathology, and most involve deltoid ligament or lateral ligament complex injury. What matters most for return-to-play timing is not the MRI grade of the syndesmosis itself, but the presence of an associated fracture or complete deep deltoid ligament tear/diastasis. Three injury clusters drive prognosis: fracture combinations (~250 days), complete deltoid tear or diastasis (~175 days), and all other syndesmosis injuries (~27 days). Staff and clinicians should look beyond the 'high ankle sprain' label and classify injury severity by concomitant findings to set accurate return timelines.
methods: Retrospective cross-sectional study. NFL Injury Surveillance Database queried for external rotation ankle injuries (2017–2019 pre-, regular-, and postseasons). Injuries confirmed by NFL Vision video review. MRI and radiograph/CT required within 30 days of injury onset. MRI reviewed by a musculoskeletal radiologist and two orthopaedic surgeons assessing AITFL, PITFL, IOM, deltoid ligament, lateral ligament complex, tendon pathology, osteochondral lesions, bone contusions, fractures, and diastasis. Stepwise linear regression (forward, backward) identified imaging predictors of days to return to full participation. 108 injuries met full imaging inclusion criteria; 83 classified as syndesmosis injuries.
findings: 83 syndesmosis injuries analyzed. Isolated syndesmosis injuries (n=11, 13%) were uncommon; most were combined with other ligamentous injury (n=65, 78%) or fractures (n=7, 8%). Overall median time to return to full participation was 30 days (IQR 19–73). Three injury groups emerged from regression: Group 1 (fracture combinations): median 250 days (IQR 142–266); Group 2 (complete deep deltoid tear or acute diastasis): median 175 days (IQR 20–248); Group 3 (all other syndesmosis injuries): median 27 days (IQR 18–46). One-way ANOVA confirmed significant between-group differences (p<0.01). Fractures required surgery 71% of the time; sprain combinations 17%; isolated injuries 9%.
limitations: MRI reviewed in tandem rather than independently, potentially introducing bias. Wide variability in MRI scanner strength and protocols across sites. Data limited to 2017–2019 seasons and game injuries only (no practice injuries). Study excluded players without adequate video confirmation of injury mechanism. Numerous factors affecting RTP beyond MRI were not modeled. Results are specific to elite NFL athletes and may not generalize broadly.
practicalImplications: NFL athletic trainers and team physicians should move beyond diagnosing 'high ankle sprains' as a single entity. MRI classification identifying associated fractures or complete deep deltoid tears/diastasis should directly inform return-to-play timelines—these athletes face 6–8+ month recoveries and are likely to require surgical intervention. The vast majority of syndesmosis injuries without these findings resolve in approximately 3–4 weeks. Accurate early imaging review should drive individualized communication with coaches and front offices about realistic availability windows.
athleteDev: Distal tibiofibular syndesmosis injuries in football carry a wide spectrum of severity with dramatically different timelines. Ankle injury rehabilitation and return-to-performance progressions should be risk-stratified by MRI findings rather than a blanket 'high ankle sprain' protocol. Athletes with concomitant deltoid involvement or diastasis require substantially longer timelines and should not be rushed through progressive loading progressions.
rtp: Syndesmosis injuries without fracture or complete deltoid tear have a median RTP of ~27 days and can be managed nonoperatively in most cases. Complete deep deltoid tears or acute diastasis extend median RTP to ~175 days and frequently require surgery. Syndesmosis-fracture combinations (Weber C, Maisonneuve) carry the longest recovery at ~250 days with 71% requiring surgical fixation. Early MRI with comprehensive ligamentous and fracture review is essential for accurate RTP prognosis in NFL players with external rotation ankle injuries.

citation: Lee TA, Lycke RJ, Lee PJ, Cudal CM, Torolski KJ, Bucherl SE, Leiva-Molano N, Auerbach PS, Talavage TM, Nauman EA. Distribution of Head Acceleration Events Varies by Position and Play Type in North American Football. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine. 2020;00:1-6. doi:10.1097/JSM.0000000000000778
abstract: This prospective pilot study evaluated the distribution and frequency of head acceleration events (HAEs) across positions, play types, and starting stances in 78 male postcollegiate North American football athletes at a skill development and professional recruitment camp. HAEs were measured using the xPatch sensor (X2 Biosystems) during up to three practice sessions and one exhibition game. Video footage of the exhibition game was used to analyze the effect of offensive linemen's starting stance (2-point 'up' vs. 3- or 4-point 'down') on HAE rates.
tldr: Offensive and defensive linemen absorb the greatest total number of head impacts in football, though most are lower magnitude (20–40 g). Tight ends and linebackers carry the highest proportion of high-magnitude impacts (above 60 g), with tight ends uniquely vulnerable due to dual blocking/receiving roles. A key actionable finding: offensive linemen who start in a 3- or 4-point 'down' stance have a significantly higher likelihood of sustaining a head impact than those in a 2-point 'up' stance, regardless of play type. Run plays generate nearly twice as many HAEs per play as pass plays. These data support rule changes eliminating down stances for linemen and reducing full-contact practice reps as immediate, practical strategies to reduce cumulative head exposure.
methods: Prospective cohort study. 78 male adult football athletes at a postcollegiate skill development camp. Up to 3 practice sessions and 1 exhibition game. HAEs measured using the xPatch sensor affixed behind the right ear. Events recorded when peak linear acceleration (PLA) exceeded 10 g; analysis included only events ≥20 g to exclude non-impact accelerations. Players categorized as DB, DL, LB, OL, TE, RB, WR, or skill (QB/K/P). Video footage from 8 cameras used to classify play type (run vs. pass) and OL/TE starting stance (up = 2-point; down = 3- or 4-point). Hit rates compared using chi-square tests with Bonferroni correction; distributions compared with Wilcoxon rank-sum test.
findings: 437 HAEs recorded across 3 practices; 272 HAEs during the exhibition game (265 during run or pass plays). OL had the most game HAEs (n=98), followed by DL (n=52). LBs had 9.68% and TEs had 18.75% of game HAEs above 60 g PLA—highest proportions of high-magnitude impacts. Position distributions differed significantly in both practice (p=0.011) and game (p=0.017). Run plays generated 5.57 ± 3.00 HAEs per play vs. 2.98 ± 1.65 for pass plays (p<0.001). Players in a down stance had a significantly higher likelihood of sustaining a HAE than those in an up stance (p=0.001). Down-stance players on run plays had the highest hit rate, significantly greater than up-stance players on run or down-stance players on pass plays.
limitations: Small sample size: only 2 full practice sessions and 1 game analyzed. Small TE group (n=3) limits validity of trends for that position. xPatch sensor has RMS and mean absolute errors of ~20%. Clack recognition algorithm not used, so non-impact events were not filtered by mechanism. Findings are from a postcollegiate recruitment camp and may not perfectly represent typical NFL or collegiate game scenarios.
practicalImplications: Football programs at all levels should consider transitioning offensive linemen to a 2-point stance to immediately reduce HAE rates without requiring rule changes by the governing body. Reducing full-contact practice frequency is another readily implementable strategy. Tight ends warrant special attention from medical and performance staff due to their uniquely high proportion of high-magnitude impacts. Coaches should be aware that run-heavy game plans generate substantially more head contact than pass-heavy schemes.
athleteDev: Athlete development staff should incorporate blocking technique coaching that minimizes head-first contact, particularly for OL and TE. Given that down-stance linemen are more frequently exposed to HAEs, teaching a quicker transition to an upright blocking posture may reduce cumulative head impact burden over a career. Tight end development should include specific neck strengthening and collision preparation given their disproportionate exposure to high-magnitude impacts.
rtp: While this study does not directly address concussion or return-to-play, its findings on HAE distribution have indirect RTP relevance: athletes returning from concussion who play OL, LB, or TE positions face the highest ongoing impact exposure. Post-concussion return protocols should account for position-specific impact risk, and modified practice participation (e.g., restricting full-contact down-stance reps for returning linemen) is a reasonable protective strategy during the RTP process.

citation: Gabler LF, Patton DA, Reynier KA, Barnett IJ, Miles AM, Dau NZ, Clugston JR, Cobian DG, Harmon KG, Kontos AP, Lynall RC, Mihalik JP, Moran RN, Terry DP, Mayer T, Solomon GS, Sills AK, Arbogast KB, Crandall JR. Distribution of position-specific head impact severities among professional and Division I collegiate American football athletes during games. BMJ Open Sport & Exercise Medicine. 2025;11:e002365. doi:10.1136/bmjsem-2024-002365
abstract: This study compared head impact severity distributions between NFL and NCAA Division I football players using instrumented mouthpieces (IMs) capable of measuring six degrees-of-freedom head kinematics during games. A total of 243 players (99 NFL from 12 clubs, 144 NCAA D-I from 8 Power Five teams) were instrumented across the 2019–2022 seasons. Head impact severity distributions across multiple kinematic metrics and three position groupings (linemen, hybrid, speed) were compared between leagues using permutation-based Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests with false discovery rate correction.
tldr: This landmark study provides the first rigorous comparison of head impact severity between NFL and Division I collegiate football players using instrumented mouthpieces—the gold standard measurement method. The key finding: head impact severity distributions are not significantly different between the two levels of play across all position groups and kinematic metrics (p≥0.320). Median peak linear accelerations were nearly identical: ~17 g for linemen, ~20–21 g for hybrid and speed positions in both leagues. This means D-I collegiate impact data can serve as a reliable proxy for NFL data, enabling combined datasets to accelerate position-specific helmet design and safety testing. For performance and medical staff, this reinforces that the biomechanical demands on players' heads are consistent from college to the pros.
methods: Prospective observational cohort study. 99 NFL players from 12 clubs (2019–2022) and 144 NCAA D-I players from 8 Power Five teams (2021–2022) wore custom-fit IMs with triaxial linear (±400 g) and angular (±20 krad/s²) accelerometers. Events triggered at >10 g linear acceleration. Kinematic data postprocessed with zero-phase 300 Hz filter, integrated for velocity, and transformed to head coordinate system. Head impacts classified using a machine learning model validated against video (precision 98.3%, recall 96.4%) combined with ambient light sensor to confirm IM was worn. NFL data synchronized to Next Gen Stats player tracking; NCAA to Catapult. Top 10% severity impacts reviewed manually. Distributions compared using permutation-based KS tests with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction across 18 comparisons. Position groupings: linemen (OL/DL), hybrid (TE/LB/RB), speed (WR/DB).
findings: 11,038 head impacts >10 g from 1,208 player-games across 286 player-seasons analyzed (NFL: 5,207 impacts from 547 player-games; NCAA: 5,831 from 661 player-games). No significant between-league differences in any kinematic metric for any position grouping after multiple comparison correction (p≥0.320 for all). Median PLA for linemen: 17.2 g (NFL) vs. 17.0 g (NCAA); hybrid: 20.7 g vs. 20.0 g; speed: 21.0 g vs. 20.8 g. Median differences across all metrics were within 13% of NFL median values. Individual player variability within positions was substantial (highest vs. lowest median PAA among linemen: 1.7x in NFL, 1.5x in NCAA), exceeding between-league differences.
limitations: Voluntary participation may introduce sampling bias, particularly for hybrid and speed positions which were underrepresented in the NFL. NFL players were not incentivized, potentially affecting compliance. NCAA data limited to Power Five teams and may not generalize to all collegiate programs. Study only evaluated game severity, not frequency of head impacts or practice environments. No head injury outcome data collected, limiting direct clinical inference. Video verification of impacts was not universally available for NCAA data.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs can now be confident that head impact severity data are transferable across levels of play. This supports use of a combined NFL-NCAA dataset for developing and evaluating position-specific helmets and protective equipment standards. Performance and medical staff should recognize that position-specific helmet recommendations developed from collegiate data are directly applicable to NFL players. The high within-position variability also suggests that individual player technique evaluations—not just position-level assessments—may be warranted for athletes with unusually high-severity impact profiles.
athleteDev: The similarity of head impact severity across NFL and D-I levels confirms that the biomechanical demands placed on athletes' heads are established early in competitive careers. Athlete development programs should begin implementing head impact mitigation strategies (technique coaching, position-specific helmet selection, contact limitation in practice) at the collegiate level with confidence that these interventions will translate to professional environments.
rtp: Although this study did not examine concussion outcomes directly, its findings establish position-specific impact severity baselines that are relevant to RTP decision-making. Speed positions (WR/DB) demonstrate the highest median impact severity (~21 g PLA), while linemen experience the highest total frequency at lower per-impact magnitudes. This profile should inform individualized RTP protocols and exposure management during staged return-to-contact progressions following concussion.

citation: Mihalik JP, Chandran A, Powell JR, Roby PR, Guskiewicz KM, Stemper BD, Shah AS, Rowson S, Duma S, Harezlak J, Riggen L, Broglio SP, McAllister TW, McCrea M, and CARE Consortium Investigators. Do Head Injury Biomechanics Predict Concussion Clinical Recovery in College American Football Players? Annals of Biomedical Engineering. 2020. doi:10.1007/s10439-020-02658-y
abstract: This multi-site NCAA-DoD CARE Consortium study examined whether head injury biomechanics—specifically injury-causing linear and rotational acceleration, season repetitive head impact exposure (RHIE), and injury day RHIE—predicted clinical recovery from concussion in 45 Division I college football players sustaining 51 incident concussions. Clinical outcomes included symptom resolution time (SRT), return-to-participation (RTP) time, and trajectories of symptom severity, balance (BESS), and mental status (SAC). Head impacts were measured using the HIT System; multivariable negative binomial regression models were employed.
tldr: Head impact biomechanics—including the magnitude of the injury-causing impact itself—do not reliably predict how fast a concussed college football player will recover clinically. The one exception is season-long repetitive head impact exposure (RHIE): athletes with greater cumulative head impact exposure across the season had 22–25% longer symptom resolution times and, counterintuitively, 20–28% shorter return-to-participation times (possibly reflecting institutional variation in RTP management). This means athletic trainers and team physicians should not use the 'size' of a concussive hit to predict recovery or set return timelines. Clinical factors—concussion history (86% longer RTP), playing position (offensive players resolve symptoms ~49% faster than defensive players), and impact location—carry more predictive weight than biomechanics alone.
methods: Multi-site prospective study within the NCAA-DoD CARE Consortium. 941 Division I football players from 4 civilian universities and 2 military service academies monitored during the study period; 45 athletes sustaining 51 incident concussions were analyzed. Head impacts measured using the HIT System (Riddell; 6 spring-loaded accelerometers measuring linear and rotational acceleration; triggered at ≥9.6 g). Concussion defined as a change in brain function following head trauma identified by team physicians and athletic trainers. Clinical measures collected at 5 time points: pre-injury baseline, time of injury (<6 h), 24–48 h post-injury, cleared for RTP, and unrestricted RTP. Symptom severity via SCAT3 (22-item, 0–132), balance via BESS (0–60), mental status via SAC (0–30). Season RHIE and injury day RHIE derived from cumulative impact data. Multivariable negative binomial regression models; adjusted for race/ethnicity, concussion history, medical history, BMI, event type, impact location.
findings: Median injurious linear acceleration: 66.7 g (IQR 48.2 g); rotational: 2963.0 rad/s² (IQR 2759.2 rad/s²). Median SRT: 6.1 days (IQR 5.8, n=45); median RTP: 12.3 days (IQR 7.8, n=36). Season RHIE predicted 22% longer SRT (Ratio 1.22, 95% CI [1.09, 1.36]) and 28% shorter RTP (Ratio 0.72, 95% CI [0.56, 0.93]) in multivariable models. No other biomechanical predictor (linear acceleration, rotational acceleration, injury day RHIE) significantly predicted SRT or RTP after covariate adjustment. Concussion history predicted 86% longer RTP (Ratio 1.86, 95% CI [1.05, 3.28]). Offensive players had SRTs 49% shorter than defensive players (Ratio 0.51, 95% CI [0.29, 0.92]). Side impacts predicted 54% shorter RTP vs. front impacts. Balance recovery varied by rotational acceleration for similar athletes but was not affected by linear acceleration, season RHIE, or injury day RHIE.
limitations: Small sample size (51 concussions from 45 athletes) limited multivariable model scope. HIT System is subject to known measurement error (~20% RMS). 52% of injuries were not immediately reported to clinical staff, potentially affecting impact identification. No computerized neurocognitive testing was included. Study limited to elite Division I athletes; findings cannot be generalized to other levels or sports. No formal power analysis performed. Participant retention across 5 time points was a pragmatic challenge in the multi-site design.
practicalImplications: Clinical staff should not use the biomechanical characteristics of a concussive impact to predict individual recovery or set RTP timelines—impact magnitude alone is insufficient. Athletes with a prior concussion history should receive more conservative management given their 86% longer RTP duration. Season-long head impact exposure (cumulative load) carries more prognostic relevance than single-event biomechanics, reinforcing the value of monitoring cumulative impact metrics throughout the season. Offensive vs. defensive position differences in SRT may reflect clinical management differences and warrant investigation at individual programs.
athleteDev: These findings underscore that subconcussive cumulative impact exposure across a season—more than any single injurious hit—is predictive of extended recovery timelines. Athlete development and sports science staff should track season RHIE for high-exposure athletes and use practice contact limitations to keep cumulative loads within safer ranges. Position-specific approaches to contact management are warranted given that defensive players showed significantly longer symptom resolution times.
rtp: Head injury biomechanics do not predict RTP time from concussion in college football players. Median RTP was 12.3 days. Greater season RHIE was associated with shorter RTP—possibly reflecting more aggressive clinical management decisions for athletes with low-exposure histories. Prior concussion history is the strongest clinical predictor of extended RTP (86% longer). Programs should base RTP decisions on standardized symptom resolution protocols rather than on injury-causing impact metrics, and should proactively flag athletes with concussion histories for extended monitoring.

citation: Aiello F, Di Claudio C, Fanchini M, Impellizzeri FM, McCall A, Sharp C, Brown SJ. Do non-contact injuries occur during high-speed running in elite football? Preliminary results from a new GPS and video-based method. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport. 2023. 10.1016/j.jsams.2023.07.007
abstract: This retrospective descriptive study evaluated the feasibility of combining GPS and video data to estimate the speed and acceleration at the time of non-contact injuries in elite football players. Data from 46 male elite Serie A players over three seasons were analysed, with training and matches video-recorded and external load measured via Catapult Vector S7 GPS. A total of 34 non-contact injuries were analysed using the Football Injury Inciting Circumstances Classification System (FIICCS), with inciting circumstances coded for speed, acceleration, and activity type.
tldr: This study provides the first GPS-plus-video method to quantify the exact running speed when non-contact injuries occur in elite soccer. The key finding is that nearly all hamstring injuries happened while players ran faster than 25 km/h — above 80% of their individual maximal speed. This means practitioners should not limit high-speed running exposure as an injury prevention strategy; instead, they should systematically prepare players to tolerate and decelerate from maximal sprint velocities. The method itself is a practical template for any club with GPS and video infrastructure to conduct standardised injury circumstance analysis.
methods: Retrospective descriptive study. 46 male elite players (mean age 26.6 ± 4.3 years, height 185 ± 6.5 cm, weight 80.8 ± 6.3 kg) from one Serie A football club over three seasons. External load collected via Catapult Vector S7 GPS (10 Hz). Training and matches video-recorded via fixed AXIS P1448-LE camera system and TV broadcasts. Injuries diagnosed by team physician. Inciting circumstances classified using FIICCS. Speed and average acceleration computed manually from GPS data for short-duration activities. Descriptive statistics reported as median and IQR.
findings: 34 non-contact injuries were analysed (34 of 68 total had complete data). Of 17 hamstring injuries, 16 occurred during running with a median peak speed of 29.28 km/h (IQR: 26.61–31.13 km/h), corresponding to 87.55% of maximal speed (IQR: 78.5–89.75%). All 17 hamstring running injuries occurred at peak speeds >25 km/h. Players were accelerating <2 m/s² in 8 cases and >2 m/s² in 3 cases (median acceleration 1.74 m/s²); decelerating >2 m/s² in 4 cases and <2 m/s² in 1 case. Both quadriceps injuries occurred during kicking. Adductor injuries occurred during acceleration, deceleration, and kicking. Most injuries (82%) occurred during matches; 97% occurred in players who had played from the start of the session.
limitations: Retrospective design prevented systematic player interviews immediately post-injury, requiring assumption that injuries occurred at peak speed. Acceleration data for short activity periods had to be calculated manually (Δv/Δt) as the software could not extract it automatically. 37% (30/80) of originally reported non-contact injuries could not be analysed due to missing GPS or video data. Reliability of the FIICCS classification system was not formally evaluated. Data from a single club only, limiting generalisability.
practicalImplications: Limiting players' high-speed running exposure is unlikely to reduce hamstring injury risk since most injuries occur at speeds >25 km/h and above 80% of individual maximal speed. NFL and college football staff should ensure players regularly experience and are conditioned for maximal sprint speeds and high-speed deceleration in training. Combining GPS and video to classify injury inciting circumstances is feasible and provides actionable data for designing injury prevention and rehabilitation protocols. Training programmes should prepare players for decelerating from sprint speeds, not just accelerating to them.
athleteDev: Football players at all development levels should be progressively exposed to high-speed running (>25 km/h) and sprint-to-deceleration sequences in training. The finding that 97% of injuries occurred in players who started the session suggests fatigue management and late-game conditioning are important athlete development targets. Periodised speed development that includes maximal-velocity work and high-intensity deceleration loading is indicated.
rtp: Return-to-play progressions after hamstring, adductor, or quadriceps injury should include running exposures that reach and exceed 80% of the athlete's individual maximal speed before full clearance, as this is the zone in which most non-contact injuries occur. Video-GPS analysis of the original injury inciting circumstance can guide task-specific RTP criteria. Deceleration capacity from sprint speed should be specifically tested before return to full training.

citation: Pimenta R, Antunes H, Lopes T, Veloso A. Do Repeated Sprints Affect the Biceps Femoris Long Head Architecture in Football Players with and without an Injury History? — A Retrospective Study. Biology. 2023;12:96. 10.3390/biology12010096
abstract: NOTE: This file has a truncated filename ('retrospective st.pdf') but contains identical content to ID 151. This retrospective study compared biceps femoris long head (BFlh) muscle architecture — fascicle length (FL), pennation angle (PA), and muscle thickness (MT) — between 12 professional football players with a history of BFlh strain injury and 20 healthy controls, before and after a 10 × 30 m repeated sprint protocol. Ultrasound B-mode imaging was used to assess architecture at rest and during active contraction (20% MVIC) pre- and post-sprint task, and knee flexor peak torque was measured via dynamometry.
tldr: This study demonstrates that previously injured football players maintain shorter BFlh fascicle lengths and greater pennation angles compared to healthy controls and their own contralateral limbs, even after returning to full training and competition. Intriguingly, the previously injured players were actually faster during repeated sprints. These architectural differences — likely a consequence of the original injury rather than a pre-existing risk factor — mean the injured limb may generate less force at the long muscle lengths required during sprinting, potentially increasing re-injury risk. Repeated sprint exercise itself had limited impact on BFlh architecture in both groups. For football staff, this reinforces the importance of long-term monitoring of BFlh fascicle length after hamstring injury, even in fully 'cleared' players.
methods: Retrospective study; 32 male professional football players (age 25.03 ± 3.72 years, height 1.79 ± 0.07 m, mass 74.69 ± 8.67 kg). Injury group: 12 players with unilateral BFlh strain history within last 11.7 ± 7.4 months (range 2–23 months). Control group: 20 players with no BFlh history. All returned to pre-injury training and competition levels. BFlh architecture (FL, PA, MT) assessed via two Aixplorer v11 ultrasound scanners at ~50% femur length. Protocol: 30-min rest, warm-up, 2 resting videos per limb, active videos at 20% MVIC, then 10 × 30 m repeated sprint task (30 s rest between sprints), followed by repeat measurements and 2 MVIC. Dynamometry measured knee flexor peak torque (1000 Hz). Data analysed with IBM SPSS 27.0; two-way mixed repeated measures ANOVA.
findings: Injured players had significantly shorter FL at rest (p = 0.014; η²p = 0.196) and in active state (p < 0.001; η²p = 0.413), and greater PA at rest (p = 0.002; η²p = 0.307) and active state (p < 0.001; η²p = 0.368) vs. controls. Intra-individual: injured limbs had shorter FL at rest (p = 0.012; η²p = 0.519) and active state (p = 0.039; η²p = 0.332) and greater PA in passive (p < 0.001; η²p = 0.732) and active states (p = 0.018; η²p = 0.412) vs. contralateral limbs. Injured players were significantly faster during sprints (7.00 ± 0.25 vs. 6.82 ± 0.19 m/s; p = 0.026). No significant differences in peak torque between groups. Sprint task had limited impact on BFlh architectural parameters; contralateral FL decreased and PA increased post-sprint in resting conditions for both groups.
limitations: Results apply only to senior professional male football players. The static ultrasound technique may overestimate FL by ~0.5 cm (especially for FL >7 cm) due to the transducer width and linear extrapolation method. Cannot determine whether shorter BFlh FL is a consequence of injury or a pre-existing risk factor due to retrospective design. Muscle and tendon stiffness were not measured. Small sample sizes.
practicalImplications: Football staff should not assume BFlh architecture has normalised after a player returns to full training following hamstring injury. Shorter fascicle lengths in the previously injured limb persist and may reduce force-generating capacity at the long muscle lengths encountered during sprinting. Regular ultrasound monitoring of BFlh FL after hamstring injury — including comparison to the contralateral limb — is recommended. The absence of between-group differences in peak torque suggests isometric strength testing alone is insufficient to detect residual structural deficits post-injury.
athleteDev: Developmental football players who sustain hamstring strain injuries may carry residual BFlh architectural changes that predispose to re-injury. Programmes targeting fascicle length development — such as long-length eccentric training (e.g., Nordic curls, Romanian deadlifts at full range) — should be incorporated into post-injury development plans even after medical clearance. Monitoring BFlh FL via ultrasound longitudinally may identify players at elevated re-injury risk.
rtp: Return-to-play decision-making after BFlh strain should include assessment of BFlh fascicle length via ultrasound, not just strength or functional performance testing. The finding that injured players had shorter FL and greater PA than their contralateral limb even months after RTP suggests structural normalisation is not guaranteed by standard RTP protocols. Consider bilateral ultrasound assessment as a supplementary RTP criterion, with the goal of reducing the FL asymmetry between limbs before clearing for full competition exposure.

citation: Pimenta R, Antunes H, Lopes T, Veloso A. Do Repeated Sprints Affect the Biceps Femoris Long Head Architecture in Football Players with and without an Injury History? — A Retrospective Study. Biology. 2023;12:96. 10.3390/biology12010096
