citation: Crow J, Semciw A, Couch J, Pizzari T. Does a recent hamstring muscle injury affect the timing of muscle activation during high speed overground running in professional Australian football players? Physical Therapy in Sport. 2020;43:188–194. 10.1016/j.ptsp.2020.03.005
abstract: This cohort study investigated whether the temporal characteristics of hamstring and gluteal muscle activation are altered during high-speed overground running in professional Australian Football players following hamstring muscle injury. Seven players with a recent hamstring injury (HS group; injury within past 6 months) and eight uninjured controls (CON group) from the same AFL club performed high-speed runs at approximately 90% of perceived maximum speed on a 100 m grass track. Surface EMG recorded muscle onset timing, offset timing, and duration of activation for the biceps femoris, medial hamstrings, and gluteus maximus.
tldr: Professional AFL players who completed comprehensive rehabilitation after a hamstring injury showed no differences in the timing of hamstring or gluteal muscle activation during high-speed running compared to uninjured teammates. This reassuring finding suggests that when rehabilitation is thorough — including high-speed running progressions — the neuromuscular sequencing of hamstring activation during sprinting is likely restored. For football staff, this underscores that the persistent altered muscle timing patterns reported in recreational athletes following hamstring injury may be a rehabilitation quality issue rather than an inevitable consequence of injury, supporting investment in high-quality, sport-specific RTP protocols.
methods: Cohort study; field-based testing. 15 male professional AFL players from one club: 7 with hamstring injury in the preceding 6 months (HS group; mean 108 ± 46 days since strain), 8 uninjured controls (CON group). All injuries MRI-confirmed. Participants ran 100 m at 60%, 70%, 80%, 90% of perceived maximum speed with 100 m walk recovery, then completed one practice trial. Two testing sessions within 7 days. Surface EMG via Delsys Trigno Wireless system (CMRR >80 dB; 2000 Hz; band-pass 20–450 Hz) on gluteus maximus, biceps femoris, medial hamstrings. Footswitches on plantar hallux and inter-phalangeal joint. GPS (MinimaxX Team 2.5, Catapult) for running speed. Two middle-20 m strides per trial selected for analysis (10 strides × 5 trials per participant). Muscle onset/offset at 15% peak amplitude threshold; duration as percentage of gait cycle. Mann–Whitney U tests and effect sizes (z-score/√n).
findings: No statistically significant differences between HS and CON groups for muscle onset, offset, or duration of activation for gluteus maximus, biceps femoris, or medial hamstrings (all p > 0.05). Groups were matched for age (HS: 22.3 ± 1.6; CON: 21.9 ± 1.6 years), height, weight, and average running speed (HS: 7.3 ± 0.3 m/s; CON: 7.2 ± 0.4 m/s). The grand ensemble EMG curves for all three muscles showed nearly identical patterns between groups throughout the gait cycle. These null results were consistent across both testing sessions.
limitations: Small sample from a single club limits generalisability. Could not control for training volume in the week preceding testing. EMG sensor dislodgement from one participant's medial hamstrings (CON group) reduced that muscle's sample to n=7 in CON. Study used onset/offset amplitude threshold approach rather than normalised amplitude, so differences in activation level rather than timing could have been missed. Overground GPS-confirmed running speeds (~7.3 m/s) may not represent true maximal sprint velocity. Environmental conditions (temperature, wind, ground hardness) were not fully controlled.
practicalImplications: After comprehensive hamstring rehabilitation, professional football players appear to restore normal neuromuscular timing during high-speed running. This provides confidence that well-rehabilitated players are not carrying covert neuromuscular deficits at the point of RTP. Football staff should use this as validation for investing in thorough, progressive rehabilitation that includes sport-speed running rather than settling for pain-free jogging. Residual activation timing deficits identified in recreational athletes may reflect inadequate rehabilitation rather than unavoidable injury sequelae.
athleteDev: Young football players recovering from hamstring strain should progress through a full high-speed running rehabilitation continuum. The data suggest that those who complete comprehensive rehabilitation programmes restore normal muscle activation patterns, indicating the nervous system is highly adaptable with appropriate loading. Coaches should not restrict athletes from high-speed running in late-stage rehabilitation out of fear of altered motor patterns, as this may ironically delay neuromuscular recovery.
rtp: This study supports returning players to full high-speed running after comprehensive hamstring rehabilitation without persistent concern about altered muscle timing patterns. RTP criteria should include demonstrated ability to run at high speeds (>7 m/s) without restriction, with confidence that normal gluteal and hamstring activation sequencing is likely restored. Practical implication for NFL/college football: do not rely on neuromuscular timing as an RTP screen unless the athlete is known to have had incomplete rehabilitation; instead focus on strength, range of motion, and sport-specific running load tolerance.

citation: Guitart-Trench M, Sanchez-Sanchez J, Valle X, Garcia-Unanue J, Cos F, Alonso-Callejo A, Rodas G, Felipe JL. Does accumulated physical load in different time windows affect hamstring injuries in elite football players? Research in Sports Medicine. 2025. 10.1080/15438627.2025.2468799
abstract: This descriptive longitudinal study investigated how accumulated training load over 7-, 14-, and 28-day time windows influences the incidence of hamstring injuries in 343 elite academy football players (2nd team, U-19, U-18) from a Spanish La Liga club competing in UEFA Champions League across four seasons (2017/18–2020/21). External load variables — total distance (TD), high-speed running (HSR), accelerations, decelerations, sprint count, and high metabolic load (HML) — were collected via GPS (WIMU PRO) and compared between injury blocks and pre-injury blocks for each time window. The acute:chronic workload ratio (ACWR) was also analysed.
tldr: Only the 28-day accumulated load window reliably discriminated between injured and non-injured periods in elite youth/development football players — the 7- and 14-day windows showed no significant differences. Players sustained hamstring injuries after 28-day periods of significantly higher total distance, HSR, sprint counts, acceleration/deceleration load, and high metabolic load compared to the preceding 28-day block. The ACWR was not associated with injury severity. For football staff this means that short-term workload spikes captured in weekly monitoring may be insufficient — chronic 28-day load accumulation, particularly in high-speed and sprint volume, should be the primary monitoring target for hamstring injury risk management.
methods: Descriptive longitudinal study; 343 male academy players from three teams of one Spanish La Liga club (2nd Team, U-19, U-18) across four seasons (2017/18 to 2020/21). Total 27,895 h training sessions, 1,488 h friendly matches, 6,551 h official matches. GPS data collected via WIMU PRO (RealtrackSystems, Spain; ICC x: 0.65, y: 0.85). External load variables: TD, MIR (18–21 km/h), HIR (21–24 km/h), HSR (>21 km/h), sprint distance (>24 km/h), sprint count, HI-ACC (>3 m/s²), HI-DCC (<−3 m/s²), HML (>25 W/kg²). Time windows: 7, 14, 28 days; injury block vs. pre-injury block. Injuries per UEFA consensus guidelines; classified using OSICS_10. Linear mixed models for repeated measurement; independent t-test for group comparisons; ACWR rolling 7:28. IBM SPSS 28.0.
findings: The 28-day time window showed significant differences between injury and pre-injury blocks for all analysed variables (p < 0.05): TD (+13,605.61 m; ES: 0.50), HSR (+725.41 m; ES: 0.48), HI-ACC (+119.42 m; ES: 0.49), HI-DCC (+133.99 m; ES: 0.48), sprint count (+18.81; ES: 0.48), HML (+2,689.54 m; ES: 0.55). Speed zone distances at 18–21 km/h (+581.83 m; ES: 0.51), 21–24 km/h (+333.08 m; ES: 0.52), and >24 km/h (+204.40 m; ES: 0.37) were also significantly higher. The 7- and 14-day windows showed no significant differences (p > 0.05). Hamstring muscle injury players showed higher load than medical attention players across all time windows for HSR and sprint variables. ACWR was not significantly different between injury and medical attention groups.
limitations: Study confined to academy players (2nd team and youth squads) from a single club, limiting generalisability to senior professionals. Goalkeepers were excluded. Individual variability in load response was not fully addressed despite linear mixed model use. Injuries classified as grade 0 (not forcing absence) were included alongside time-loss injuries, which may dilute findings. ACWR methodology has been criticised for its statistical properties in the broader literature.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning coaches in football should extend their workload monitoring window to 28 days for hamstring injury risk assessment rather than relying exclusively on acute weekly loads. When the 28-day accumulated load — particularly HSR distance, sprint count, and HML — spikes significantly above the preceding 28-day period, hamstring injury risk is elevated. Weekly fluctuations (7-day windows) are insufficient predictors. Staff should compare current 28-day blocks to the prior 28-day block for each player, not to a seasonal average, when making rotation or load management decisions.
athleteDev: Development players (U-18, U-19, reserve teams) accumulating high chronic loads over 28-day blocks — especially in sprint and high-speed running volume — face elevated hamstring injury risk. Programming for these age groups should build sprint and high-speed running capacity progressively across the season, avoiding abrupt 28-day load spikes. Periodisation models should account for match congestion periods, which can compress recovery and cause undetected chronic load accumulation in shorter monitoring windows.
rtp: When returning a player from hamstring injury to full training and match play, the accumulated load of the 28-day re-introduction period should be carefully managed to avoid surpassing the chronic load baseline from the prior 28-day period. The absence of ACWR differences between injury groups suggests ratio-based monitoring alone is insufficient for RTP load management decisions; absolute 28-day volume targets — particularly for HSR and sprint distance — should be established and tracked for each athlete returning from hamstring injury.

citation: Lapré MA, Palazzolo EM. Does Draft Currency Promote Competitive Balance? An Empirical Investigation of the National Football League 2002–2021. Journal of Sports Economics. 2024;1-23. doi:10.1177/15270025241264238
abstract: This study empirically investigates whether the NFL draft promotes competitive balance by examining whether draft-pick value (draft currency) increases the probability of reaching the playoffs across the 2002–2021 seasons. The authors distinguish between original draft currency (value of picks assigned before any trades) and final draft currency (value of picks used after all trades) using eight different draft-value curves. Logistic regression models with team-strength controls show that original draft currency does not affect playoff probability, but final draft currency (reflecting pick trading) does—with a delay of 2–3 years. Teams that outperformed others in trading draft picks, most notably the New England Patriots, perpetuated competitive imbalance by exploiting outdated pick-value frameworks used by most teams.
tldr: The NFL draft's intent to promote competitive balance fails in practice because what matters is not where you pick but how smartly you trade. Original draft position has no effect on reaching the playoffs, but teams that acquire more total draft value through trading are significantly more likely to reach the playoffs 2–3 years later. Most teams use McCoy's outdated Pick Value Chart (PVC), which overvalues early picks relative to more accurate models. Teams like New England that recognized this mispricing gained systematic advantages and remained dominant for over two decades. For football front offices and performance staff, this study reinforces that roster building through strategic draft-pick trading—guided by empirically derived draft-value curves—is more predictive of playoff success than simply having an early pick.
methods: Retrospective analysis of all NFL drafts and game results for the 1998–2021 seasons (focused on 2002–2021 post-realignment to 32 teams). Primary data from pro-football-reference.com. Eight draft-value curves compared: McCoy's PVC, Massey-Thaler MT1 (1983–2008) and MT2 (2001–2008), Stuart's PFRAV, Schuckers' APVC, and Lopez's Average, Star, and Blended curves. Original and final draft currency computed for each team-year. Team strength measured via least squares ratings (scoring margin-based). Logistic regression (equation 1) with random effects clustered by team estimated probability of making the playoffs, controlling for prior team ratings (up to 4 lags), strength of schedule, new head coach, division, and first-round QB selections. Dataset: 635 team-season observations (32 teams, 2002–2021; Cleveland Browns and Houston Texans have fewer due to expansion). Robustness tests included team dummies, compensatory-pick-only models, Patriots exclusion, and win percentage as dependent variable.
findings: Original draft currency was not statistically significant in any of the 10 models across all 8 draft-value curves (Table 1), indicating the assigned draft position does not influence playoff probability. Final draft currency (after pick trades) was significant for the Star, Blended, PFRAV, APVC, Average, and Linear draft-value curves (Table 2), primarily at lags 2 and 3 years. Model fit (log pseudolikelihood) was substantially better for models using less-steep draft-value curves (PFRAV, APVC, Average, Linear) than for PVC-based models. Final draft currency is strongly predicted by original draft currency (β≈1.00, R²=0.67 for PVC) but varies more when using APVC (R²=0.29) and Average (R²=0.28), reflecting that some teams systematically gain or lose value in trades. The New England Patriots had the least original draft currency under APVC but ended up with more final draft currency than 21 of 31 other teams on average, contributing to 17 playoff appearances over 20 seasons. An average team improving its final draft currency from 2.6 to 2.9 (APVC) increases its estimated playoff probability from 33.8% to 38.9% (15% increase).
limitations: The authors cannot fully control for trades involving veteran players alongside draft picks, so final draft currency coefficients may be biased downward. Positional depth within the draft and team-specific roster needs are not modeled. The analysis focuses on a single dependent variable (playoff appearance) and is underpowered to study Super Bowl appearance or divisional round advancement due to small sample sizes. The surplus value curve's critical dependence on 1994–2008 compensation data limits its applicability post-2011 CBA. Results may not generalize beyond the 32-team, post-2002-realignment NFL structure.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football front offices should replace McCoy's PVC—still widely used in negotiations—with empirically derived, less-steep draft-value curves (APVC, Average, Linear, PFRAV) that better capture actual player value by draft position. Teams using updated valuation frameworks will systematically acquire more draft value in trades, which translates into measurably higher playoff probability 2–3 years later. General managers and football operations staff should treat draft-pick trading as a capital allocation problem: the total value of picks acquired post-trade is a stronger predictor of organizational success than pick position. Performance directors should understand that late-round picks are undervalued by the PVC, making them advantageous assets to acquire.
athleteDev: For college football programs and NFL teams with draft responsibilities, this study underscores the importance of talent identification across all draft rounds rather than over-investing in top picks. Because less-steep value curves (PFRAV, Average) correlate better with playoff success, organizations that develop late-round picks effectively gain disproportionate value. Athlete development infrastructure—including depth chart management, position flexibility training, and accelerated development pipelines for later-round selections—is a strategic competitive advantage. Programs should build systems that reduce developmental risk for mid-to-late round picks, where true market inefficiencies exist relative to conventional pick valuation.
rtp: Not directly applicable. This is a sports economics study and does not address player health, injury, or return-to-play.

citation: Young TS, Chatham LS, Carpenter RD, Yakacki CM. Does pad displacement correlate to impact severity metrics in football helmets? Proc IMechE Part P: J Sports Engineering and Technology. 2024;1-13. doi:10.1177/17543371241280709
abstract: This computational study used finite element (FE) models of three NFL-tested football helmets (2016 Riddell Speed Classic, 2016 Xenith X2E, 2017 Vicis Zero1) to investigate whether helmet pad displacement—separated into normal and tangential components—correlates with impact severity metrics (HIC, DAMAGE, HARM) across six impact locations and three speeds per NFL testing protocol. When evaluated across all locations combined, normal displacement did not correlate strongly with any severity metric (R²<0.44); however, at the side upper (SU) location, normal displacement correlated strongly with HARM, HIC, and PRLA (R²=0.79–0.80). Tangential displacement was not found to correlate with any impact severity metric regardless of location, and helmets that maximized normal displacement without bottoming out generally performed better on severity metrics.
tldr: Helmet pad normal displacement is a meaningful indicator of impact severity—but only at specific impact locations, most notably the side upper (SU) region, which carries the highest weight in the NFL's Helmet Performance Score. A helmet that maximizes normal displacement without the pad fully densifying (bottoming out) will produce lower HIC and HARM scores. Tangential displacement is largely irrelevant to severity metrics. For equipment managers and performance staff, this study provides mechanistic insight into why certain helmets outperform others in NFL testing: the geometry and deformation mechanics of the internal padding system matter more than outer shell shape. The Riddell helmet showed lowest peak normal displacements, while Vicis showed the highest HIC despite comparable displacements due to its buckling column design densifying earlier. When selecting helmets, understanding pad displacement characteristics by impact location—not just overall performance scores—may guide more informed decisions.
methods: FE models of three helmets (2016 Riddell Speed Classic, 2016 Xenith X2E, 2017 Vicis Zero1) from BioCore LLC were used in LS-DYNA simulations per NFL testing protocol. The Hybrid III (HIII) headform and neck assembly were paired with a linear impactor. Six impact locations: side (C), rear (D), side upper (SU), oblique front (OF), facemask side (FMS), facemask center oblique (FMCO) at impact speeds of 5.5, 7.4, and 9.3 m/s. MATLAB code extracted time history data for three nodes from the outer pad surface and three from the inner pad surface to calculate peak normal and tangential displacement per impact. Impact severity metrics calculated: HIC15 (Head Injury Criterion, 15 ms window), DAMAGE (Diffuse Axonal Multi-Axis General Evaluation), HARM (Head Acceleration Response Metric, weighted combination of HIC and DAMAGE). Model validation used CORA scores: Riddell 0.79, Xenith 0.79, Vicis 0.74. Correlations between peak displacement components and severity metrics assessed using R² values by location and combined.
findings: Normal displacement correlated strongly with severity metrics at the SU location (HARM R²=0.79, HIC R²=0.80, PRLA R²=0.79) but weakly at other locations (OF: HARM R²=0.06, HIC R²=0.00). Across all locations combined, R²=0.33–0.44 for normal displacement versus severity metrics. Tangential displacement showed weak correlations across all locations (R²<0.2 for all metrics except PRAA at D location, R²=0.65). Peak normal displacement increased with impact speed for most locations; maximum deformations ranged 12–37mm across helmets at highest speed. At the rear location, Vicis showed highest HIC despite comparable displacements due to premature pad densification from buckling column design. Riddell had lowest normal displacements and HIC was variable by location; Xenith showed lowest HIC at the OF location. At the SU location, all three helmets reached similar peak normal displacements but Vicis showed highest HIC (Figure 6c) due to acceleration time-history shape. The hypothesis that helmets maximizing normal displacement without bottoming out will perform better was supported at SU and OF but not uniformly at all locations.
limitations: Only three impact speeds per location were tested, limiting the ability to statistically assess correlations (three data points per helmet per location). FE models are several years old and have largely been replaced by newer helmet versions. FE model definitions for rear location may not fully represent real-world conditions due to omission of rear nape of head geometry. Tangential displacement is calculated as a resultant without directional information. HIC, DAMAGE, and HARM each have inherent limitations in capturing all aspects of concussion risk. Results depend on specific materials and geometries in the BioCore FE models and may not generalize to all helmet designs.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football equipment managers should understand that impact location is critical when interpreting helmet performance scores. A helmet that performs well at the side upper (SU) location—the most heavily weighted in NFL HPS rankings—will benefit most from maximizing normal pad displacement without bottoming out. The Vicis buckling column design, while innovative, may reach pad densification earlier than traditional foam designs, which can paradoxically increase HIC at some locations. Equipment staff should use NFL helmet testing data alongside this biomechanical framework to select helmets that balance protection across all impact zones, not only those with the highest aggregate HPS score. Design engineers should prioritize pad systems that sustain energy absorption (displacement) across a range of impact speeds before full densification.
athleteDev: For football programs selecting helmets for developmental athletes, this study indicates that helmet fit and the internal pad-to-head interface—ensuring pads make full contact and displace effectively during impact—are critical for optimal protection. Helmets with larger internal offset (like Vicis) may provide less effective energy absorption if the design leads to premature densification at moderate impact speeds typical of youth and collegiate play. Position-specific helmet selection considerations are supported by the location-dependent nature of impact severity correlations; linemen experiencing frequent frontal impacts may benefit from helmets with different pad characteristics than skill players experiencing lateral impacts.
rtp: While this study does not directly address return-to-play, it provides mechanistic context relevant to concussion prevention protocols. The finding that helmet pad normal displacement correlates with HARM scores at high-risk impact locations (SU) reinforces that proper helmet fit and pad condition are critical components of concussion risk management. Equipment staff conducting return-to-play evaluations should include helmet inspection—checking pad integrity and displacement capacity—as part of the clearance process, particularly after a concussive impact. Helmets whose pads have experienced full densification from a prior impact may have reduced protective capacity for subsequent impacts.

citation: Card RK, Harrington JM, Reyes NP, Huish EG Jr, Pettegrew J. Does Playing Surface Affect the Risk of Anterior Cruciate Ligament Injuries in the National Football League? A Look at the 2017-2021 NFL Season. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2023. doi:10.1080/00913847.2023.2178817
abstract: This epidemiological study examined the prevalence and incidence of ACL injuries across natural grass, synthetic turf, and GrassMaster (hybrid) surfaces in the NFL during the 2017/18 through 2021/22 seasons using publicly available data. A total of 173 ACL ruptures with identifiable playing surfaces were recorded across 1,603 games: 73 on grass, 85 on turf, and 15 on GrassMaster. ACL injury rates were 0.097 per game on grass, 0.115 on turf, and 0.134 on GrassMaster, yielding a non-natural surface injury rate of 0.118. The injury rate ratio for non-natural vs. natural grass surfaces was 1.211 (21.1% higher risk), and the odds ratio was 1.239 (95% CI 0.900–1.704, p=0.189), which did not reach statistical significance. NOTE: This file (ID 157, filename ending '...NFL season.pdf') and ID 158 (filename ending '...NFL seasons.pdf') are identical in content — same authors, DOI, abstract, results, and data tables. The only difference is the filename: singular 'season' vs. plural 'seasons'. Both files appear to be the same accepted manuscript version.
tldr: Non-natural playing surfaces (synthetic turf and GrassMaster combined) carry a 21.1% higher ACL injury rate compared to natural grass in the NFL over five seasons (2017–2021), but this difference does not reach statistical significance (OR=1.239, 95% CI 0.900–1.704, p=0.189). GrassMaster hybrid surfaces showed the highest injury rate (0.134/game), followed by turf (0.115) and grass (0.097). The study is the first to include GrassMaster in NFL surface analysis. Despite the trend being consistent with prior literature, the lack of statistical significance—likely due to limited sample size and reliance on publicly available data (which captures only ~68% of true NFL ACL injuries)—means definitive conclusions cannot be drawn. Football operations staff should note the consistent trend and advocate for collaboration with the NFL on internal injury surveillance data to produce more definitive surface safety evidence. NOTE: IDs 157 and 158 are identical papers with different filenames.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological study using publicly available data (NFL team press releases, game reports, published injury reports) for NFL preseason, regular season, and playoff games from the 2017/18 through 2021/22 seasons. ACL injuries were included if they involved confirmed rupture or tear during a game where the playing surface could be identified; practice injuries and OTA injuries were excluded. Playing surfaces classified as: natural grass (Kentucky Bluegrass, Bermuda, or blended grass), synthetic turf (100% synthetic, multiple manufacturers), or GrassMaster (Tarkett Sports hybrid surface with synthetic fibers embedded in natural grass sub-base). ACL injuries recorded by field type (73 grass, 85 turf, 15 GrassMaster). Total games: 1,603 (752 grass, 739 turf, 112 GrassMaster). Injury rate defined as ACL ruptures per game. Injury rate ratios and odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals calculated; statistical analysis performed by a senior author (EH).
findings: 173 total ACL ruptures on identifiable surfaces across 1,603 games. Grass: 73 injuries (42.1%), 752 games; Turf: 85 injuries (49.1%), 739 games; GrassMaster: 15 injuries (8.7%), 112 games. ACL injury rates per game: Grass 0.097, Turf 0.115, GrassMaster 0.134, Non-natural combined 0.118. Injury rate ratio turf vs. grass: 1.185 (18.5% increased risk on turf). Injury rate ratio non-natural vs. grass: 1.211 (21.1% increased risk). OR for turf vs. grass: 1.209 (95% CI 0.869–1.683, p=0.261). OR for non-natural vs. grass: 1.239 (95% CI 0.900–1.704, p=0.189). No statistically significant difference in ACL injury risk between natural and non-natural surfaces. The trend is consistent with prior studies showing elevated ACL injury rates on synthetic surfaces.
limitations: Reliance on publicly available data, which captures approximately 68% of true NFL ACL injuries reported to NFL medical staff (per Inclan et al., 2022), may underestimate the true incidence and potentially introduce bias. Practice injuries were excluded due to inability to identify playing surface, even though many teams use both natural and synthetic practice surfaces. Only the game surface (not practice surface) was attributable to each injury. Study is limited to preseason, regular season, and playoff games. Sample size may be insufficient to detect statistically significant differences; the authors note that collaboration with the NFL to access internal medical records would substantially strengthen future analyses. The GrassMaster surface was used by only two NFL teams (Philadelphia Eagles and Green Bay Packers), limiting its representativeness.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football medical and performance staffs should be aware that a consistent—though not statistically significant—trend of 18–21% higher ACL injury rates exists on non-natural playing surfaces compared to grass in the NFL. GrassMaster hybrid surfaces showed the highest per-game injury rate in this dataset. Teams playing home games on synthetic surfaces should consider this trend when designing ACL prevention programs and load management strategies around surface type. Advocacy for NFL-level access to internal injury surveillance data is needed to generate statistically powered studies. Until more definitive data is available, surface type should be considered one factor among many in individualized ACL risk profiling.
athleteDev: Youth through college football programs should consider playing surface as a contextual risk factor in ACL injury prevention programming. Athletes regularly training or competing on synthetic turf may benefit from surface-specific neuromuscular training that addresses the higher traction and altered lower extremity mechanics associated with artificial surfaces. Footwear optimization (cleat type and length matched to surface) is a modifiable variable that may reduce traction-related ACL injury risk. Programs with access to both surface types should consider assigning highest-intensity ACL-risk activities (cutting drills, reactive agility) to natural grass when feasible.
rtp: Surface type should be considered in return-to-play planning for NFL players recovering from ACL reconstruction. Given the trend toward higher ACL injury incidence on synthetic turf and GrassMaster, a conservative approach to first full-contact return on these surfaces is warranted. Progressive surface reintroduction—beginning with natural grass and advancing to synthetic turf—may be appropriate for athletes with a history of ACL injury, particularly when returning to competition at venues with non-natural surfaces. The approximately 10.75-month average RTP timeline for NFL ACL injuries should factor in surface type and the athlete's neuromuscular readiness for cutting and planting mechanics on high-traction artificial turf.

citation: Oleksy Ł, Mika A, Kuchciak M, Bril G, Kielnar R, Adamska O, Wolański P, Deszczyński M. Does Restricted Ankle Joint Mobility Influence Hamstring Muscle Strength, Work and Power in Football Players after ACL Reconstruction and Non-Injured Players?. J. Clin. Med. 2023;12(19):6330. https://doi.org/10.3390/jcm12196330
abstract: This study investigated how ankle dorsiflexion ROM limitation affects hamstring muscle Peak Torque/BW (%), Average Power (W), and Total Work (J) in 47 professional football players divided into a post-ACL reconstruction group (n=24) and an uninjured group (n=23). Players were sub-classified by the Weight-Bearing Lunge Test (WBLT) into normal (≥12 cm) and restricted (<12 cm) ankle dorsiflexion subgroups, and concentric isokinetic testing at 60°/s was performed on both limbs. Results showed that ankle dorsiflexion restriction had no significant effect on hamstring strength variables within either group, with poor and non-significant correlations between WBLT and all strength measures. AUC values below 0.5 across all variables confirmed that ankle dorsiflexion ROM has no diagnostic accuracy for hamstring muscle strength.
tldr: Ankle dorsiflexion restriction does not weaken hamstring muscles in football players, whether or not they have undergone ACL reconstruction. Post-ACL reconstruction players showed significantly lower hamstring Peak Torque/BW and Average Power compared to uninjured players, but within both groups, restricted ankle mobility did not further impair hamstring strength, power, or total work. Coaches and sports scientists should not assume limited ankle dorsiflexion is a primary driver of hamstring weakness; prevention programs should target a broad spectrum of physical techniques beyond ankle mobility interventions.
methods: Cross-sectional study of 47 professional football players (Group 1: n=24 post-ACL reconstruction 2-3 years prior, cleared for RTS; Group 2: n=23 injury-free for 3 years; mean age ~22.6 years). Ankle dorsiflexion was measured with the WBLT (cut-off 12 cm). Hamstring Peak Torque/BW (%), Average Power (W), and Total Work (J) were assessed via concentric isokinetic dynamometry (Biodex System 4) at 60°/s, 10 repetitions per leg. MANOVA, Pearson correlations, and ROC/AUC analyses were used.
findings: Post-ACL players had significantly lower Peak Torque/BW (Group 1 injured leg: 121 ± ~25% vs Group 2 right leg: 164 ± ~30%, p<0.006) and Average Power compared to uninjured players. Within both groups, no significant differences were found between normal and restricted ankle dorsiflexion subgroups for any strength variable (all p>0.05). Pearson correlations between WBLT and all strength variables were poor (r range: -0.18 to 0.26, all non-significant). AUC values ranged from 0.40 to 0.65, all near or below 0.5, indicating no diagnostic utility.
limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. Longitudinal monitoring of both muscle strength and ankle dorsiflexion ROM over a season would provide more robust conclusions. The sample was limited to professional regional-level players, limiting generalizability to elite or other competitive levels.
practicalImplications: Football programs should not rely solely on ankle dorsiflexion assessments to identify athletes at risk for hamstring weakness. Post-ACL reconstruction players exhibit persistent bilateral hamstring strength deficits that require targeted rehabilitation regardless of ankle mobility status. Injury prevention programming should include a broad spectrum of physical training techniques addressing multiple risk factors beyond ankle joint mobility.
athleteDev: Hamstring strength development programs for football players should be prioritized independently of ankle dorsiflexion status. Athletes returning from ACL reconstruction demonstrate lasting hamstring strength deficits bilaterally, reinforcing the need for comprehensive lower-limb conditioning protocols throughout development. Screening should include isokinetic hamstring strength testing in addition to ankle mobility assessments.
rtp: Football players cleared for RTS after ACL reconstruction continue to show significantly reduced hamstring Peak Torque/BW and Average Power compared to uninjured peers, indicating that RTS clearance does not equate to full hamstring strength recovery. RTP protocols should include objective hamstring strength benchmarks and should not be conflated with ankle mobility restoration. Bilateral hamstring strength deficits persisting 2-3 years post-reconstruction highlight the need for extended post-clearance monitoring.

practicalImplications: Football programs should not rely solely on ankle dorsiflexion assessments to identify athletes at risk for hamstring weakness. Post-ACL reconstruction players exhibit persistent bilateral hamstring strength deficits that require targeted rehabilitation regardless of ankle mobility status. Injury prevention programming should include a broad spectrum of physical training techniques addressing multiple risk factors beyond ankle joint mobility.
athleteDev: Hamstring strength development programs for football players should be prioritized independently of ankle dorsiflexion status. Athletes returning from ACL reconstruction demonstrate lasting hamstring strength deficits bilaterally, reinforcing the need for comprehensive lower-limb conditioning protocols throughout development. Screening should include isokinetic hamstring strength testing in addition to ankle mobility assessments.
rtp: Football players cleared for RTS after ACL reconstruction continue to show significantly reduced hamstring Peak Torque/BW and Average Power compared to uninjured peers, indicating that RTS clearance does not equate to full hamstring strength recovery. RTP protocols should include objective hamstring strength benchmarks and should not be conflated with ankle mobility restoration. Bilateral hamstring strength deficits persisting 2-3 years post-reconstruction highlight the need for extended post-clearance monitoring.

citation: Silva DS, Boullosa D, Pereira EVM, Alves MDJ, Fernandes MSS, Aidar FJ, dos Santos LF, de Souza RF. Does the sequence of plyometric and dynamic stretching exercises influence subsequent sprint performance? A randomized crossover intervention study. Biol Sport. 2024;41(2):13–18. https://doi.org/10.5114/biolsport.2024.129485
abstract: This randomized crossover study examined the acute effects of exercise sequence — dynamic stretching followed by drop jumps (DS+DJ) versus drop jumps followed by dynamic stretching (DJ+DS) — on sprint performance in 13 competitive male track-and-field athletes (sprinters and jumpers). Athletes completed a standardized warm-up and then one of three conditions (DS+DJ, DJ+DS, or control) before performing 40-m sprints analyzed in 0–20 m and 20–40 m phases. The DS+DJ sequence improved final acceleration (20–40 m) speed compared to baseline, while DJ+DS worsened initial acceleration (0–20 m), and neither sequence improved overall 40-m performance.
tldr: For sports performance coaches using warm-up potentiation strategies, the order of dynamic stretching and plyometric exercises matters: performing dynamic stretching before drop jumps (DS+DJ) improved final sprint acceleration (20–40 m) by approximately 0.12 m/s, while reversing the order (DJ+DS) actually impaired initial acceleration. Athletes with lower baseline lower-limb power (lower CMJ height) benefited the most from the DS+DJ sequence, suggesting this strategy is especially valuable for less powerful athletes. This simple, equipment-free protocol is directly applicable in practice and competitive settings.
methods: Randomized crossover design with 13 competitive male sprint and jump athletes (19±2 years; CMJ height 39.0±3.0 cm; 100-m PB 11.4±0.6 s). Five sessions over 3 weeks: 2 familiarization, 3 experimental (72 hours apart). Conditions: DS+DJ (2 dynamic stretches × 15 reps/leg + 3 drop jumps at optimal height), DJ+DS (reverse order), and control. Sprint performance assessed via MySprint app at 240 fps over 40 m with split times at 0–20 m and 20–40 m. CMJ assessed via Chronojump contact mat. Two-way repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni post-hoc; Pearson correlation between CMJ and PAPE response.
findings: No significant effect of any condition on overall 40-m sprint performance. The DS+DJ condition significantly improved 20–40 m velocity vs. baseline (8.91±0.35 vs. 8.79±0.43 m/s; p=0.015). The DJ+DS condition significantly worsened 0–20 m velocity vs. baseline (6.22±0.26 vs. 6.26±0.25 m/s; p=0.002). A significant negative correlation was found between CMJ height and the percentage improvement in final acceleration in the DS+DJ condition (r=-0.741; p=0.004), indicating athletes with lower power benefited most.
limitations: Small sample size (n=13 after exclusions from an initial 16). All participants were competitive track-and-field athletes (sprinters and jumpers), limiting generalizability to team sport athletes. Only two dynamic stretching exercises and three drop jumps were used; results may differ with different exercise volumes or types. The study did not measure neuromuscular mechanisms (EMG, force-velocity characteristics) to explain the observed sequence effects.
practicalImplications: Football coaches and sports performance staff can use DS followed by DJ (DS+DJ) as a simple, equipment-free warm-up potentiation strategy to enhance final-phase sprint acceleration. This is particularly relevant for skill positions requiring top-end speed after an initial acceleration phase. Athletes with lower lower-limb power levels benefit most, making this strategy valuable for developing players. The reverse sequence (DJ then DS) should be avoided as it can impair initial acceleration, which is critical in football contexts.
athleteDev: Implementing DS+DJ sequencing in warm-up protocols can serve as a development tool for football skill positions (WR, DB, RB) requiring explosive acceleration in the 20–40 m range. Athletes with lower CMJ heights — often younger or less trained players — show the greatest performance enhancement, suggesting this PAPE strategy is especially valuable during developmental stages. Coaches should individualize potentiation warm-up strategies based on assessed lower-limb power levels.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play populations. However, the emphasis on exercise sequencing during warm-up may be relevant for athletes returning from lower-limb injuries who are reintegrating sprint-based activities. Caution is warranted: the DJ+DS sequence impaired initial acceleration, which could stress healing tissue in RTP athletes; DS+DJ may represent a safer potentiation strategy for athletes in the later stages of return-to-activity progressions.

citation: Bishay AE, Fitch SW, Iheagwaram CS, Williams KL, Naranjo C, Sills TK, Fitch RW, Pasquina PF, McAllister TW, McCrea MA, Broglio SP, Terry DP, Zuckerman SL; CARE Consortium Investigators. Does Timing of Concussion in Season Affect Incidence and Recovery in Division I Collegiate Football Players? A CARE Consortium Study. J Head Trauma Rehabil. 2026;Vol 00, No 0:1–10. https://doi.org/10.1097/HTR.0000000000001146
abstract: This prospective cohort study from the CARE Consortium investigated whether the timing of concussion within a football season (preseason, early, middle, or late-season) is associated with concussion incidence and recovery outcomes (time to symptom resolution [SR] and return-to-play [RTP]) in 682 Division I collegiate football players across 21 institutions (2014–2020). Concussion incidence was highest during preseason (4.73 per 100 players) compared to all regular-season phases (~2.2 per 100). Early and late-season concussions had significantly longer time to SR compared to preseason (HR=0.582 and 0.634, respectively), and early-season concussions also had a lower hazard of RTP (HR=0.703).
tldr: In NCAA Division I football, preseason carries the highest risk of concussion — nearly double the rate of any regular-season phase — but preseason concussions resolve fastest (median 5.7 days to SR). Concussions sustained during the early and late regular season take significantly longer to resolve (median 6.6 and 6.9 days to SR, respectively), and early-season concussions also prolong return-to-play. Football medical staff should increase concussion surveillance and prevention during preseason, while recognizing that early- and late-season concussions may require extended management windows, possibly due to academic demands, cumulative fatigue, or competitive pressures.
methods: Prospective cohort study using the CARE Consortium multi-institution database (30 universities, 2014–2020). Final sample: 682 Division I football players across 21 institutions who sustained concussions during preseason or regular-season. Season phases were defined as preseason (August), early (Sep 1–29), middle (Sep 30–Oct 28), and late-season (Oct 29–Nov 27) on a per-team basis. Concussion incidence expressed per 100 players. Time to SR and RTP analyzed with Kaplan-Meier survival curves, log-rank tests, and Cox proportional hazards regression with covariates including age, SCAT3 TSS, LOC, amnesia, ADHD, and psychological disorders.
findings: Preseason incidence: 4.73/100 players (41.9% of concussions); early: 2.19, middle: 2.31, late: 2.17/100 (χ²=104.6, p<0.001). Kaplan-Meier showed significant SR differences (log-rank p=0.012): median SR was preseason 5.7 days, early 6.6, middle 4.8, late 6.9 days. Cox regression: early-season concussions had 41.8% reduced SR hazard (HR=0.582, p<0.001); late-season had 35.6% reduced SR hazard (HR=0.634, p=0.002) vs preseason. No overall RTP differences (log-rank p=0.300), but early-season concussions had 29.7% reduced RTP hazard (HR=0.703, p=0.040) vs preseason. Higher SCAT3 TSS and delayed SCAT3 testing also predicted longer SR and RTP.
limitations: Arbitrary season phase cutoffs limit direct comparisons with studies using different definitions. Lack of exposure data prevents precise incidence rate calculations. Observational design cannot establish causality or distinguish mechanisms (e.g., training load, cumulative head impacts, academic demands). Sample limited to Division I football; results may not generalize to other levels or sports. Concussion management protocols evolved over the 2014–2020 study period, potentially influencing reported outcomes.
practicalImplications: Football programs should implement heightened concussion surveillance, prevention protocols, and head impact monitoring during preseason — the highest-risk period — including attention to conditioning status, practice intensity, and helmet-fit protocols. Athletic trainers should be aware that early- and late-season concussions may require longer management timelines, potentially due to academic load, cumulative fatigue, and playoff pressure. Symptom severity (SCAT3 TSS) at 24 hours post-injury remains a key prognostic indicator regardless of season phase.
athleteDev: Preseason represents the highest concussion incidence period for collegiate football players, likely linked to elevated head impact exposure, deconditioning from the off-season, and intense competition for roster spots. Programs should prioritize preseason neuromuscular conditioning, proper tackling technique, and gradual ramp-up of contact to reduce early-season concussion burden. Player wellness and concussion literacy education at the start of each season phase is essential.
rtp: Timing of concussion within the season significantly affects RTP trajectory: early-season concussions showed a 30% lower hazard of RTP compared to preseason, with median RTP ranging from 10.7 to 12.7 days across phases. Medical staff should not expect uniform RTP timelines across the season; late-season concussions in particular may be complicated by cumulative fatigue and playoff stakes. Post-injury SCAT3 symptom severity is the strongest clinical predictor of prolonged RTP and should anchor all return-to-play decision-making.

citation: Cairns JT, Kolstad AT, Galarneau J-M, van den Berg CA, Jordan MJ, Schneider KJ, Pasanen K, Emery CA. Down, set, record: Assessing warm-up structure and use of neuromuscular training exercises in Canadian adolescent tackle football. International Journal of Sports Science & Coaching. 2025;1–11. https://doi.org/10.1177/17479541251373073
abstract: This cross-sectional video-analysis study examined warm-up structure and neuromuscular training (NMT) component use across 46 warm-up sessions (23 practice, 23 game) from nine adolescent (ages 14–17) tackle football teams in Calgary, Canada during a nine-week 2022 season. Researchers coded warm-up footage for duration and exercise type across NMT components (aerobic, strength, agility, balance/coordination, head-and-neck control) and non-NMT components (dynamic and static stretching). Teams spent a median of 456.9 seconds total in warm-up with 266.7 seconds actively exercising, and 80% of exercises were aerobic (51%) or dynamic stretching (29%), while NMT-specific components (strength 9%, agility 3%, balance/coordination 1%, head-and-neck control 1%) were substantially underutilized.
tldr: Canadian adolescent tackle football coaches are not implementing evidence-based neuromuscular training (NMT) warm-up programs, which have been shown to reduce musculoskeletal injuries by >35% and concussions by 59% in adolescent sports. Warm-ups are dominated by aerobic running and dynamic stretching, while strength, agility, balance/coordination, and head-and-neck control exercises — critical NMT components — collectively account for less than 10% of all exercises. Head-and-neck control exercises, which are especially important for concussion prevention in a collision sport, were used by only one team. Football programs should restructure warm-ups to include all five NMT components and extend active exercise time to the recommended 10–15 minutes.
methods: Cross-sectional video-analysis sub-study within the SHRed Concussions research program. Nine tackle football teams (Calgary Spring Football Association, April–June 2022) were filmed across 46 warm-ups (23 practice, 23 game) over a 9-week season. Dartfish software (v10.1) was used to code exercise components and durations. A full factorial mixed effects linear regression model compared warm-up structure by session type (game vs practice) and season period (weeks 1–3, 4–6, 7–9), using restricted maximum likelihood estimation and Kenward-Roger degrees of freedom.
findings: Median total warm-up time was 456.9 s (25th–75th percentile: 276.8–532.3 s); median active exercise time was 266.7 s (193.5–326.9 s). Aerobic exercises comprised 51% (283/550) and dynamic stretching 29% (158/550) of all exercises. NMT components: strength 9% (51 exercises), agility 3% (15), balance/coordination 1% (3), head-and-neck control 1% (6). No significant differences in warm-up structure between game and practice sessions. The only statistically significant seasonal change was that aerobic exercise time was 42.2 s longer in the middle season period (weeks 4–6) than the beginning (p=0.02). Only one team consistently used head-and-neck control exercises.
limitations: Minimum of 4 warm-ups filmed per team due to feasibility constraints limited precision of estimates. Presence of the research team may have altered coach behavior. Only team-structured warm-ups were examined, excluding individual player warm-ups. Exercise fidelity was not assessed. Some exercises were classified into a single component category despite potential overlap. Sample limited to Calgary adolescent male teams, limiting generalizability.
practicalImplications: Tackle football programs must substantially restructure their warm-up protocols to incorporate all NMT components — particularly strength, agility, balance/coordination, and head-and-neck control — which are currently near-absent. Coaches should target 10–15 minutes of active NMT-based warm-up per session, 2–3 times per week, consistent with dose-response evidence. Head-and-neck control exercises should be standard given football's second-highest sport concussion rate. The video-analysis methodology used in this study provides a practical tool for coaching staff to audit their own warm-up practices.
athleteDev: Adolescent tackle football players (ages 14–17) are not receiving evidence-based NMT warm-up preparation that develops strength, agility, balance/coordination, and head-and-neck control — all critical athletic attributes in a collision sport. Coaches should replace excess aerobic running time with structured NMT exercises targeting these components. Implementation of a tackle football-specific NMT program (analogous to FIFA 11+ or Rugby Activate) would provide a structured framework for coaches without specialized strength and conditioning knowledge.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play populations. However, the near-absence of head-and-neck control and neuromuscular strength exercises in current warm-up practice has direct implications for concussion and musculoskeletal injury prevention, both of which affect RTP timelines. Programs that implement NMT warm-ups with head-and-neck control components and strength exercises may reduce primary concussion incidence and lower-limb injury rates, thereby reducing the frequency of RTP scenarios that football medical staff must manage.

citation: Stemper BD, Shah AS, Pintar FA, et al. Drill-Specific Head Impacts in Collegiate Football Practice: Implications for Reducing 'Friendly Fire' Exposure. Ann Biomed Eng. 2017;45(12):2648-2662. doi: 10.1007/s10439-017-1898-y
abstract: This study quantified head impact biomechanics by specific drill type in collegiate football practice to identify which drills contribute most to cumulative head impact exposure. Head impact data were collected using helmet-mounted accelerometers during practices across a collegiate football season. Drills were categorized and head impact frequency and magnitude were compared across drill types to identify opportunities for reducing practice-based head impact exposure.
tldr: Not all football practice drills carry equal concussion risk. This study found that certain full-contact drills — particularly those involving teammate-on-teammate ('friendly fire') contact — account for a disproportionate share of head impacts during practice. Coaches can meaningfully reduce cumulative head impact exposure by limiting or modifying these specific high-impact drills without eliminating overall football preparation.
methods: Helmet-mounted accelerometer systems recorded head impact kinematics in collegiate football players across an entire practice season. Each impact was tagged to a specific drill type. Linear and rotational accelerations were analyzed by drill category, and cumulative exposure metrics were calculated. Drills were classified to identify those generating the most frequent and highest-magnitude impacts.
findings: Certain contact-based drills contributed disproportionately to cumulative head impact burden. Teammate-on-teammate contact ('friendly fire') drills generated significant impact exposure. High-magnitude impacts were concentrated in a subset of drill types, suggesting that targeted drill modification could substantially reduce overall practice head impact exposure.
limitations: The study was conducted at a single institution, limiting generalizability across programs with different practice schemes. Accelerometer-based systems may not perfectly replicate true head kinematics. Drills were self-reported or observed by research staff, which may introduce classification inconsistency. The study did not directly link drill-specific impacts to concussion outcomes.
practicalImplications: Football programs can reduce cumulative head impact exposure by identifying and modifying or eliminating high-impact 'friendly fire' drills from practice schedules. Staff should audit their practice script to quantify which drills generate the most head impacts and consider substituting equivalent skill-development activities with lower contact intensity, consistent with evolving NCAA and NFL practice contact limitations.
athleteDev: Skill development can be maintained by replacing high-contact friendly-fire drills with technique-focused or thud-level alternatives. This is particularly relevant for linemen and linebackers who absorb the highest practice impact loads. Periodizing full-contact drill exposure across the season — heaviest early, tapering mid-season — can preserve development while protecting long-term neurological health.
rtp: Quantifying drill-specific impact exposure provides a framework for progressive return-to-practice protocols after concussion. Players returning from head injury can be reintegrated beginning with no-contact and drill-contact activities, avoiding the high-impact friendly-fire drills identified in this study until fully cleared, reducing re-injury risk during the vulnerable post-concussion window.

citation: Benson EM, Elphingstone JW, Paul KD, Schick S, Shihab YA, Barlow D, Ponce BA, Brabston EW, Momaya AM. Eccentric Hamstring Strength Imbalance among Football and Soccer Athletes. South Med J. 2024;117(4):214-219. doi: 10.14423/SMJ.0000000000001674
abstract: This cross-sectional study compared eccentric hamstring strength imbalances across 631 soccer and American football athletes at high school, collegiate, and professional levels using the NordBord Hamstring Testing System. The study measured maximum hamstring force, torque, and work imbalances between limbs. High school soccer athletes demonstrated significantly greater hamstring imbalances than football athletes, and imbalances decreased with advancing skill level in soccer but not in football.
tldr: High school soccer athletes show substantially greater interlimb eccentric hamstring imbalances than football athletes, placing them at elevated risk for hamstring strain injury. Imbalances improve with age and competitive level in soccer (likely reflecting more structured eccentric training), but football athletes show no significant change across skill levels. Single-sport specialization in high school is associated with greater work imbalances than multisport participation. Football programs that already emphasize weight training may be inadvertently protecting players from hamstring asymmetry.
methods: Cross-sectional study enrolling 631 athletes during preseason 2021, including high school (n=459), collegiate (n=149), and professional (n=24) male soccer and football players plus female soccer athletes. Nordic hamstring exercise was performed on the NordBord device. Maximum force (N), torque (N·m), and work (N·s) imbalances between legs were computed. One-way ANOVA with LSD post-hoc tests and chi-square tests compared groups; statistical significance set at P<0.05.
findings: High school soccer players showed significantly greater torque imbalance (P=0.03) and work imbalance (P<0.01) than football players. Male soccer athletes at high school had significantly higher maximum force (P=0.018), torque (P=0.035), and work (P=0.033) imbalances than college/professional counterparts. Female high school soccer players had significantly higher torque (P=0.045) and work (P=0.001) imbalances than collegiate females. Football athletes showed no significant imbalance changes across skill levels. Single-sport high school athletes had significantly greater work imbalances than multisport athletes (9.1% vs 7.0%, P=0.012). HSI rates were higher in soccer (11.7%) than football (4.5%, P=0.004).
limitations: Pre-measurement activity was not controlled. Imbalances were not stratified by field position due to insufficient statistical power. Cross-sectional design prevents causal conclusions about whether improved balance results from skill-level advancement or self-selection. Study was conducted at limited institutions and may not generalize broadly.
practicalImplications: Football strength and conditioning staff should incorporate eccentric hamstring testing (NordBord or similar) as a screening tool, particularly for high school athletes and positions requiring explosive acceleration/deceleration (DBs, WRs). Force imbalances exceeding 20% should trigger targeted single-leg eccentric programming. Nordic curl protocols have demonstrated 50-70% reduction in initial HSI risk and 85% reduction in reinjury rates and should be considered standard injury prevention for all skill-position players.
athleteDev: Early enrollment of football players in structured weight training — already common in most programs — appears protective against eccentric hamstring imbalance. Multisport participation in high school is associated with better hamstring balance and should be encouraged where possible. For positions with high sprint demands (DBs, WRs, RBs), single-leg deadlifts, Nordic curls, and single-leg Romanian chair holds should be integrated into year-round strength programs to address asymmetry proactively.
rtp: Athletes returning from hamstring strain injury should be cleared using NordBord testing to confirm limb symmetry before full sport return. Work imbalance >20% between legs indicates elevated re-injury risk (reported recurrence rates of 14-34%). A progressive eccentric loading protocol — including Nordic curls — should be completed before RTP clearance, given the 85% reinjury risk reduction demonstrated with this training approach.

citation: Pfaller AY, Brooks MA, Hetzel S, McGuine TA. Effect of a New Rule Limiting Full Contact Practice on the Incidence of Sport-Related Concussion in High School Football Players. Am J Sports Med. 2019;47(10):2294-2299. doi: 10.1177/0363546519860120
abstract: This cohort study examined whether a Wisconsin Interscholastic Athletic Association (WIAA) rule change limiting full-contact practice duration reduced sport-related concussion (SRC) rates in high school football. Data from 2081 players in pre-rule seasons (2012-2013) were compared to 945 players in the post-rule 2014 season. Athletic trainers tracked athlete exposures and concussion incidence across practice and competition settings.
tldr: Limiting full-contact practice time by rule reduced high school football practice concussions by 57%, with no corresponding increase in game-time concussions. This is strong evidence that targeted contact restrictions during practice are effective for concussion reduction without compromising game-readiness or technique. The data also show that overall concussion rates trended down (1.58 to 1.28 per 1000 AEs), though the overall change was not statistically significant, likely due to sample size. For football programs at any level, reducing unnecessary full-contact repetitions in practice is one of the most evidence-backed interventions available.
methods: Prospective cohort study across Wisconsin high schools: 34 schools in 2012, 18 in 2013 (pre-rule), and 26 schools in 2014 (post-rule). Athletic trainers classified practice sessions as full-contact, drill-contact, or no-contact, and recorded all athlete exposures (AEs) and SRC diagnoses. SRC defined per American Academy of Neurology guidelines. Chi-square tests compared concussion rates; Wilcoxon rank sum tests compared days lost.
findings: Practice SRC rate fell 57% after the rule change: from 0.76 per 1000 AEs (2012-2013) to 0.33 per 1000 AEs in 2014 (P=0.003). Game concussion rate was unchanged (5.81 vs 5.74 per 1000 AEs, P=0.999). Overall concussion rate trended down from 1.58 to 1.28 per 1000 AEs but was not statistically significant (P=0.139). Days lost to SRC were not significantly different (13 vs 14 days, P=0.967). No difference in concussion rates by prior football experience, suggesting less-experienced players were not harmed by reduced contact practice.
limitations: Head impact magnitude was not directly measured, so volume reduction in total head impacts is inferred. Classification of practice contact type by athletic trainers may introduce bias. Study was underpowered to detect significant overall concussion rate change. Other injury types were not tracked. Adherence to the new rule was assumed but not directly verified. Time-series design is susceptible to secular trends including changing concussion awareness and clinical practice.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs should use this evidence to support limiting unnecessary full-contact repetitions in practice, consistent with existing NFLPA collective bargaining agreement restrictions on padded practices. The argument that full contact in practice is required to prevent injury during games is not supported by this data — game concussion rates did not rise after contact was restricted. Programs should audit their practice scripts to classify contact levels and minimize full-contact time to what is strictly necessary for competitive preparation.
athleteDev: Players at all experience levels — including those with less prior football experience — showed no disadvantage from reduced full-contact practice, suggesting that technical skill development is preserved with drill-contact and thud-level activities. Programs should develop practice structures that achieve necessary positional skill development within no-contact and drill-contact frameworks as much as possible, reserving full-contact for specific installation and game-simulation periods.
rtp: The study indirectly informs RTP protocols by demonstrating that concussed players who miss practice but return to game competition do not face elevated concussion risk when the practice environment has reduced contact levels. Graduated RTP progressions that begin with no-contact and drill-contact activities are consistent with the practice structure that reduced overall concussion incidence in this study.

citation: Murphy CP, Frangiamore SJ, Mannava S, Sanchez A, Beiter ER, Whalen JM, Price MD, LaPrade RF, Provencher MT. Effect of Anterior Glenoid Labral Tears and Glenoid Bone Loss at the NFL Combine on Future NFL Performance. Orthop J Sports Med. 2018;6(7):2325967118784884. doi: 10.1177/2325967118784884
abstract: This retrospective cohort study reviewed all 2285 NFL Combine participants from 2009 to 2015 to determine whether MRI-confirmed anterior glenoid labral tears and associated injuries affected future NFL performance. Of 2285 players, 206 (9%) had anterior labral tears confirmed by MRI; 908 players without surgery history or significant missed time served as controls. NFL draft position, games played, games started, and snap percentage across the first two NFL seasons were compared between injured players and controls.
tldr: Having an anterior labral tear (Bankart lesion) confirmed at the NFL Combine does not independently reduce games played, games started, or snap percentage in a player's first two NFL seasons. However, labral tears occurring alongside other shoulder injuries (Hill-Sachs, SLAP, glenoid bone loss, rotator cuff tear) significantly drop a player's draft position — meaning teams penalize these athletes in the draft even though the combined injuries don't appear to hurt on-field performance. Glenoid bone loss specifically lowered draft position but did not affect playing time. Surgical versus non-surgical management showed no performance differences. This data is relevant for evaluating college athletes at the NFL Combine and making clearance and draft decisions.
methods: Retrospective cohort study of NFL Combine participants 2009-2015. MRI scans reviewed independently by two fellowship-trained orthopaedic surgeons for anterior labral tears, associated injuries (SLAP, glenoid bone loss, Hill-Sachs, rotator cuff tear, HAGL, combined posterior tears), and surgical evidence. Control group: players with no surgery history and no more than 2 missed collegiate games. Performance metrics (draft position, games played, games started, year 1 and year 2 snap %) collected from NFL.com and Pro Football Focus. Mann-Whitney U tests, chi-square, and Fisher exact tests used for comparisons.
findings: 206 of 2285 players (9%) had MRI-confirmed anterior labral tears; 20 were bilateral (226 total affected shoulders). Players with anterior labral tears did not perform significantly worse than controls in draft position (P=0.259), games played (P=0.391), games started (P=0.486), or snap percentage year 1 (P=0.268) or year 2 (P=0.757). Concomitant injuries significantly worsened draft position vs isolated labral tears (170.1 vs 95.8 pick, P=0.003). Glenoid bone loss reduced draft position (175.5 vs 95.8, P=0.001) but not playing metrics. Severity of bone loss did not significantly affect any metric. Operative vs nonoperative management showed no significant performance differences.
limitations: Retrospective design; not all players underwent shoulder MRI at the Combine, potentially missing asymptomatic tears. MR angiography and 3D CT were not consistently available, limiting sensitivity for certain lesions and bone loss quantification. Physical examination findings were not correlated to imaging. Confounding variables (collegiate performance, combine testing, interview, team needs) could affect draft position. Follow-up limited to first 2 NFL seasons; some players had only 2-year data available.
practicalImplications: NFL and college medical staffs should recognize that an anterior labral tear — even with associated injuries — does not independently predict reduced on-field performance in the first two years of professional play. Teams may be over-penalizing athletes in the draft for labral findings that do not translate to playing time differences. For collegiate athletes being evaluated for professional opportunities, appropriate surgical management of instability before the Combine does not appear to disadvantage performance outcomes relative to nonoperative care.
athleteDev: Athletes with anterior labral tears or a history of shoulder instability can be developed for professional play with appropriate management. The 9% prevalence of MRI-confirmed labral tears at the Combine reflects the high contact demands of collegiate football. Strength and conditioning programs emphasizing rotator cuff and scapular stabilizer conditioning may reduce recurrence risk for athletes returning from labral injury. Positions with highest prevalence (defensive backs, defensive linemen, linebackers) warrant proactive shoulder screening.
rtp: The lack of significant performance differences between operatively and nonoperatively managed labral tears suggests both pathways can lead to successful return to elite competition. Players returning from anterior labral repair should follow validated progressive RTP protocols with attention to end-range external rotation loading before clearing for full-contact practice. The presence of concomitant injuries (Hill-Sachs, glenoid bone loss) warrants more conservative RTP timelines and may require surgical stabilization to reduce recurrent dislocation risk in contact sport athletes.

citation: Reams N, Hayward RA, Kutcher JS, Burke JF. Effect of Concussion on Performance of National Football League Players. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2017;12(8):1100-1106. doi: 10.1123/ijspp.2016-0508
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined 1,882 NFL skill-position players (running backs, quarterbacks, wide receivers, tight ends) from 2007-2010 to determine whether concussion affects on-field performance as measured by Defense-Adjusted Yards Above Replacement (DYAR). Players reported on the weekly NFL injury report with concussion were compared to controls with other head/neck injuries on performance changes relative to each player's baseline. Fixed-effects regression models estimated performance before and after injury.
tldr: NFL skill-position players who were cleared to return to play after concussion performed at their pre-injury baseline level — there was no measurable decline in game performance upon return. However, players showed significantly reduced DYAR performance in the one and two weeks prior to their concussion appearing on the official injury report, which may indicate delayed diagnosis, underreporting, or a multi-hit cumulative injury phenomenon. For performance and medical staff, this suggests current return-to-play criteria are capturing functional recovery adequately, but earlier detection of subclinical concussion effects may be an important opportunity to improve athlete protection.
methods: Retrospective cohort study of 1,882 NFL skill-position players over 2007-2010 regular seasons. Concussion identified via weekly NFL injury report. Controls: players with other head/neck injuries. Primary outcome: Defense-Adjusted Yards Above Replacement (DYAR) from Football Outsiders, a context-adjusted performance metric. Multi-level fixed-effects linear regression estimated DYAR changes at weeks -4 through +4 relative to the injury report. Sensitivity analyses adjusted for plays per game, season week, player age, years in the league, and weeks missed.
findings: 140 concussions and 57 control injuries identified. Upon return to play (week 0 and subsequent weeks), concussed players performed at baseline DYAR — no significant decline post-injury. However, DYAR was significantly lower 2 weeks (-7.12, 95% CI -13.0 to -1.31) and 1 week (-12.2, 95% CI -17.9 to -6.5) before the injury report, suggesting pre-report performance decrement. In the week of the injury report, concussed players performed slightly better than controls (DYAR difference +11.2, p=0.06). Results were robust to sensitivity analyses.
limitations: Performance is not a guaranteed sensitive marker of neurological recovery; lingering effects may exist that do not manifest as DYAR changes. DYAR is a team-context-dependent metric that cannot fully isolate individual performance. NFL injury report diagnoses are non-specific and subject to underreporting (a 2012 poll found 56% of NFL players would hide concussion symptoms). Control group contamination (misclassified concussions) is possible. Study predates modern concussion awareness and management advances.
practicalImplications: NFL performance and medical staff can take some reassurance that the formal return-to-play protocol, as practiced during 2007-2010, was associated with players returning to baseline performance. However, the decline in performance detected 1-2 weeks before official concussion diagnosis should prompt earlier recognition efforts — sideline performance monitoring, weekly tracking of objective metrics, and low-threshold reporting culture. Performance-based metrics may complement traditional symptom-based concussion screening as an adjunct monitoring tool.
athleteDev: The multi-hit hypothesis suggested by pre-diagnosis performance declines has implications for practice design: reducing cumulative head impact exposure in practice reduces the probability of subclinical injury that may compound into diagnosed concussion. Tracking weekly objective performance metrics for skill-position players (yards per route run, completion percentage, etc.) may help identify players experiencing subclinical neurological effects before a formal concussion manifests.
rtp: Players cleared through formal concussion protocols returned to baseline DYAR performance, supporting the validity of symptom-based and neurocognitive RTP criteria for NFL skill-position players. Staff should not assume a cleared player will underperform — but should continue to monitor performance metrics and symptom recurrence in the weeks following RTP. The finding that performance declines precede official diagnosis underscores the importance of a low-threshold reporting environment and proactive monitoring throughout the season to enable earlier intervention.

citation: Jildeh TR, Okoroha KR, Taylor KA, Buckley P, Mehta S, Mehran N, Moutzouros V. Effect of Concussions on the Performance of Running Backs and Wide Receivers in the National Football League. Am J Sports Med. 2019. DOI: 10.1177/0363546519864581
abstract: This cohort study examined the effect of concussions on the performance of NFL running backs and wide receivers over 4 seasons (2012-2015). A total of 38 concussed players were compared to 139 matched controls using an offensive power rating metric (total yards/10 + touchdowns x 6). Player performance was tracked up to 3 seasons before and after the injury season to assess acute and longitudinal changes.
tldr: NFL running backs and wide receivers who sustained concussions returned to play at a very high rate (89% within the same season, missing ~1.5 games on average) and maintained performance levels comparable to uninjured controls both acutely and up to 3 years post-injury. Despite common concern, concussions did not measurably degrade on-field offensive production as measured by power rating. This is reassuring for staff managing return-to-play timelines but does not diminish the importance of rigorous concussion protocols given documented long-term neurological risks.
methods: Cohort study (Level 3 evidence). NFL injury reports, player profiles, and game summaries were used to identify 38 running backs/wide receivers who sustained concussions during 2012-2015 seasons. A matched control group of 139 players without concussion history was assembled. Performance was quantified using an offensive power rating (total yards/10 + 6 x touchdowns) calculated for 3 seasons before and after the index injury season. Repeated measures analyses controlled for age and NFL experience. Statistical significance set at p < .05.
findings: 89% of concussed players (34/38) returned to play within the same season, missing a mean of 1.5 ± 0.9 games; average time to RTP was 18.9 ± 7.6 days. Power ratings were statistically similar between concussed and control players at all time points. No significant difference in 1-year power rating delta (-1.2 ± 4.8 vs -1.1 ± 3.9, P=.199) or 3-year delta (-3.6 ± 8.0 vs -3.0 ± 4.5, P=.219). Within-season pre- vs. post-injury performance did not significantly differ (8.1 ± 4.3 vs 7.8 ± 3.7, P=.41). Two retirements were directly attributed to concussion.
limitations: Injury data were collected from publicly available sources and may be subject to inaccuracies; concussions may be underreported due to athlete self-reporting and organizational variance. Study was limited to running backs and wide receivers only, limiting generalizability. History of prior concussion was not controlled. The power rating metric may oversimplify multifactorial human performance. A time-period bias existed between concussion and control groups.
practicalImplications: NFL staff can have reasonable confidence that running backs and wide receivers who follow proper concussion protocols will return to comparable performance levels. The high RTP rate (~89% same season) supports aggressive but protocol-driven rehabilitation. The longer RTP timeline (~19 days) vs. earlier eras likely reflects improved medical standards and should be maintained. Staff should not compress return timelines based on performance pressure, given documented long-term neurological risks even when short-term performance is preserved.
athleteDev: Young skill position players who sustain concussions should not be expected to suffer long-term performance decline if managed properly. Development pipelines should include standardized concussion protocols; tracking offensive metrics (yards, touchdowns, power rating) over multiple seasons is a useful tool for objectively monitoring post-concussion recovery in skill players.
rtp: This study directly informs RTP decisions for running backs and wide receivers post-concussion. The ~19-day average RTP and 1.5-game absence is a useful benchmark for NFL medical staffs. All 38 players eventually returned to NFL competition, confirming that high-level return is achievable.
