RTP criteria should prioritize neurological clearance over performance metrics, as performance did not decline upon return when proper protocols were followed.

citation: Omari AM, Paul RW, Fliegel B, Osman A, Bishop ME, Erickson BJ, Alberta FG. Effect of COVID-19 on Injury Rates and Timing in the National Football League. Orthop J Sports Med. 2022;10(6):23259671221098749. DOI: 10.1177/23259671221098749
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study compared ACL, Achilles tendon, and hamstring tendon injury rates in the NFL between the pre-pandemic seasons (2017-2019) and the COVID-19-affected 2020 season, during which the entire preseason was cancelled and roster sizes were reduced. Data were collected from publicly available sources for 181 ACL, 87 Achilles tendon, and 633 hamstring tendon injuries across 2017-2020. Statistical comparisons used ANOVA, t-tests, and chi-square analysis.
tldr: The cancellation of the 2020 NFL preseason due to COVID-19 was associated with significant increases in Achilles tendon (+35%) and hamstring tendon (+25%) injuries during the regular season, while ACL injuries remained constant. Injuries that occurred in weeks 1-4 of the 2020 season mirrored the pattern typically seen during preseason in prior years, suggesting displaced injury risk rather than eliminated risk. For football staff, this underscores that a structured preseason is critical for injury prevention — removing it shifts soft-tissue injury risk into the regular season rather than preventing it.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study (Level 4 evidence). Publicly available data were collected for all NFL players sustaining ACL, Achilles tendon, or hamstring tendon injuries between April 1, 2017 and March 31, 2021. Player characteristics, career workload, and season-of-injury workload were assessed. ANOVA and independent-samples t-tests compared 2017-2019 averages to 2020. Chi-square tests were used for categorical data. Significance set at p < .05.
findings: Achilles tendon injuries were significantly higher in 2020 vs. 2017-2019 average (27 vs. 20, P=.024). Hamstring tendon injuries were significantly higher in 2020 vs. 2017-2019 average (186 vs. 149, P<.001). ACL injuries remained constant (43 vs. 46 average, P=.175). In 2020, ACL injuries were most common in weeks 1-4 (34.9%) rather than preseason as in prior years (52.9%). Athletes sustaining ACL injuries in 2020 played significantly more regular season games before injury (4.40 vs. 2.78, P=.042). No significant differences in player demographics or career workloads between seasons.
limitations: Publicly available databases may underreport minor injuries. Specific training program details for each team or player were unavailable, preventing conclusions about type of training regimen and injury risk. Numerous confounding factors related to the COVID-19 pandemic itself (mental health, access to facilities, individual training variability) could not be controlled. Causality cannot be established from this observational design.
practicalImplications: Football staff should treat preseason training and competition as essential injury-prevention infrastructure. The 2020 data suggest that when preseason is removed, soft-tissue injury risk (particularly hamstring and Achilles) is not eliminated but shifts into the early regular season. For NFL/college programs facing disrupted off-seasons (lockouts, pandemics, shortened camps), medical and strength staff should implement enhanced ramp-up protocols, increase monitoring of at-risk tissues (Achilles, hamstrings), and consider graduated loading programs to substitute for lost preseason exposure.
athleteDev: Pre-training camp conditioning is critical for reducing injury risk in professional athletes. Young players entering the league or returning from extended absences require structured progressive loading before high-intensity competition. The finding that hamstring injuries are most common early in the regular season (weeks 1-4) regardless of season type highlights the importance of consistent year-round conditioning and sport-specific movement preparation.
rtp: Athletes returning from extended training gaps (analogous to the COVID lockdown) face elevated risk of Achilles and hamstring tendon injuries. RTP protocols after prolonged inactivity or disrupted training should emphasize gradual reintroduction of sport-specific loads, with particular attention to eccentric hamstring loading and Achilles tendon preparation. The first 4 weeks after return to competition appear to be the highest-risk window for these injuries.

citation: Ellens S, Carey DL, Gastin PB, Varley MC. Effect of Data-Processing Methods on Acceleration Summary Metrics of GNSS Devices in Elite Australian Football. Sensors. 2024;24:4383. DOI: 10.3390/s24134383
abstract: This study measured differences in commonly used GNSS acceleration summary metrics (number of efforts and distance in medium/high-intensity zones) under three data processing protocols — raw, custom-processed (4th-order Butterworth filter at 2 Hz), and manufacturer-processed — using data from 38 elite Australian Football League players across 14 matches during the 2019 season. Linear mixed models were used to compare the effect of processing method on acceleration and deceleration metrics.
tldr: The same GNSS device produced dramatically different acceleration summary metrics depending on how the data were processed — manufacturer software reported up to 184 times less distance and 89 times fewer high-intensity efforts than raw data. These are not small rounding differences; they represent fundamentally different pictures of athlete workload. Any football performance staff using GPS/GNSS wearables must understand which processing pipeline their software uses and must not compare metrics across different software versions or platforms without accounting for this effect. Establishing internal normative data with a consistent processing method is essential.
methods: 38 elite male AFL players from one team were tracked with a 10-Hz GNSS device (Catapult Vector S7) during 14 matches in 2019. Three processing methods were compared: (I) raw (central difference on unsmoothed Doppler-shift speed data), (II) custom-processed (4th-order zero-lag Butterworth filter at 2 Hz cutoff, then central difference), and (III) manufacturer-processed (Catapult Openfield v2.7.1 default settings). Metrics analyzed: number of acceleration/deceleration efforts and distance covered in medium (±2 m/s²) and high (±3 m/s²) intensity zones. Linear mixed models with random effects for player and game were used. Sample size: 262 player files.
findings: Manufacturer-processed data had lowest reported distance and efforts; raw data had highest. For high-acceleration zone distance, raw exceeded manufacturer by 1373 m on average (p<0.001); custom exceeded manufacturer by 413 m (p<0.001). For high-acceleration zone efforts, raw reported 89.7x more efforts than manufacturer (p<0.001); custom reported 55.7x more (p<0.001). Raw data exceeded custom processing by 1.61x for high-acceleration efforts (p<0.001). Medium-intensity zone differences were also highly significant across all comparisons (all p<0.001). Within each processing method, medium-zone distance generally exceeded high-zone distance except in raw data.
limitations: Data were collected from only one GNSS device type (Catapult Vector S7) and one manufacturer software version (Openfield 2.7.1), limiting generalizability to other devices and platforms. Only one AFL team was studied. The study used GNSS-derived acceleration rather than accelerometer-derived acceleration, which may differ. Future research should include local positioning systems and optical tracking systems to allow cross-system comparison.
practicalImplications: Football performance staff using GPS/GNSS tracking technology must be aware that processing method profoundly alters acceleration summary metrics. Comparing data across software updates, different devices, or different teams using different platforms is scientifically invalid without accounting for processing differences. Staff should document their processing pipeline, maintain consistency over time, and develop internal normative databases rather than relying on cross-study or cross-platform benchmarks. When selecting thresholds for training load monitoring, practitioners should validate that their chosen method adequately captures the acceleration demands of their specific sport.
athleteDev: Acceleration and deceleration metrics derived from GNSS devices are widely used to quantify training load and competition demands in football. However, this study demonstrates that the metric values themselves are artefacts of processing methodology as much as actual player movement. Athlete development staff should build load monitoring systems using a consistent, documented processing pipeline and should educate themselves on whether their device's manufacturer software uses strong smoothing (which dramatically underestimates high-intensity efforts). Custom processing on raw data may provide a more accurate representation of true acceleration demands.
rtp: When tracking an athlete's return to play using GNSS-derived acceleration metrics, any change in device firmware, software version, or processing pipeline will invalidate direct comparisons to pre-injury baselines. RTP protocols that use acceleration load thresholds (e.g., number of high-intensity efforts) must be established and monitored using a consistent processing method throughout the rehabilitation period. Staff should be cautious about setting RTP thresholds based on published literature if the processing methods differ from those used in-house.

citation: Langan SP, Martin DG, Thomas L, Good A, Funk JR, Barker R, Deaton S, Guevel S, Crandall JR, Sills A, Myers BS, Arbogast KB, Casa DJ. Effect of newly required American football headgear on thermoregulation during exercise in the heat. Sports Engineering. 2025;28:19. DOI: 10.1007/s12283-025-00498-4
abstract: This study evaluated whether the NFL-mandated Guardian Cap NXT (GC NXT) soft-shell helmet cover accentuates hyperthermia during exercise heat stress using both a sweating thermal manikin and 12 male human volunteers (>91 kg) exercising in a chamber at 40°C, 40% humidity with ~800 W/m² simulated solar radiation. The manikin assessed heat loss and radiant heat absorption across four helmet models with and without the GC NXT; the human study measured rectal temperature, skin temperature, heart rate, sweat rate, and subjective measures across five 90-minute exercise trials.
tldr: The NFL's required Guardian Cap NXT helmet cover does not meaningfully worsen core body temperature, heart rate, or sweat rate during intense exercise in the heat compared to standard helmets alone. The manikin showed a small (2.3%) reduction in heat loss with the GC NXT, but this was dwarfed by differences between helmet models. In humans, no objective thermoregulatory differences were detected across helmet conditions, though players subjectively felt cooler with white GC NXTs. For NFL/college staff, this means the mandated GC NXT should not increase exertional heat illness risk, but white caps may provide a modest comfort benefit over black caps in hot weather.
methods: Two-phase study. Phase 1: Sweating head manikin at the Textile Protection and Comfort Center (NC State University) tested 4 helmet models (Riddell SpeedFlex, Schutt F7 UR2, Schutt Air XP Pro Q11 LTD, VICIS ZERO2-R) with and without GC NXT for thermal resistance, evaporative resistance, and radiant heat absorption. Phase 2: Randomized crossover design with 12 males (age 18-35, >91 kg) completing 5 exercise trials in an environmental chamber (40°C, 40% RH, ~800 W/m² solar radiation) wearing different helmet/GC NXT combinations. Exercise protocol: 15-min repeating bouts (5-min walk, 5-min jog, 5 x 20-s sprint/40-s rest) for 90 min or until Tr reached 40°C. Outcome measures: rectal temperature (Tr), mean skin temperature, helmet interior temperature, heart rate, sweat rate, RPE, thermal sensation, fatigue.
findings: Manikin: GC NXT reduced calculated heat loss by 4.85 W/m² (2.3%) across all helmets (p=0.0283), but effect size was small (Hedges' g*=0.13) compared to helmet model variation. GC NXT reduced radiant heat absorption vs. bare helmet (p<0.02). Human study: No significant differences in Tr (p=0.731), mean skin temperature, heart rate (average p=0.673; peak p=0.359), sweat rate (p=0.180), or body mass loss (p=0.441) between any headwear conditions. Interior helmet temperature was 5-10°C lower with GC NXT vs. bare helmet (p<0.001). At 75 min, thermal sensation was lower with white GC NXT vs. control (p=0.007) and vs. black GC NXT (p=0.011).
limitations: Human study participants were not blinded to cap color, introducing potential placebo effects on subjective measures. Only male participants >91 kg were tested, limiting generalizability to smaller players, females, or youth. The study was conducted under laboratory-simulated solar conditions which may not perfectly replicate outdoor environments. Conflict of interest: authors affiliated with Biocore LLC (GC NXT developer) were involved in testing, though separate laboratories conducted manikin and human studies.
practicalImplications: Football staff and medical personnel can be reassured that the mandated Guardian Cap NXT does not significantly increase exertional heat illness risk during intense exercise in hot conditions. Heat illness prevention protocols used with standard helmets should be equally effective with the GC NXT. Choosing white over black GC NXTs may reduce subjective thermal discomfort at the end of long practices. Helmet model choice has a much greater effect on thermoregulation than GC NXT presence — this should be considered when selecting equipment for hot-weather training environments.
athleteDev: The GC NXT's lack of thermoregulatory impact means it can be worn during summer training camps and hot-weather practices without additional heat illness precautions beyond standard protocol. Player welfare decisions about heat illness should focus on environmental conditions, workload, hydration, and acclimatization status rather than GC NXT use. Education for players and staff should clarify that wearing the cap is not a meaningful heat risk, reducing potential non-compliance.
rtp: Players returning from exertional heat illness (EHI) who are required to wear a GC NXT can do so without concern that the cap will significantly elevate thermoregulatory stress above baseline. The interior helmet temperature reduction (5-10°C lower with GC NXT) may even be marginally beneficial in hot conditions. Standard EHI return-to-sport protocols are appropriate for players wearing any of the tested helmet/GC NXT combinations.

citation: Burke J, Geller JS, Perez JR, Naik K, Vidal AF, Baraga MG, Kaplan LD. Effect of Passing Plays on Injury Rates in the National Football League. J Strength Cond Res. 2021;35(12S):S1-S4. DOI: 10.1519/JSC.0000000000004139
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study analyzed data from official NFL gamebooks for all 32 teams across the 2013-2016 regular seasons to determine whether play type (pass vs. run) affected in-game injury rates. There were 2,721 in-game injuries recorded with an overall rate of 1.33 injuries per team per game. Multivariable logistic regression controlled for weather conditions and temperature.
tldr: Passing plays carry a 40% higher odds of in-game injury than running plays in the NFL, primarily driven by elevated injury risk for quarterbacks (6.9x), receivers (5.0x), and defensive backs (2.3x). Passing plays are particularly associated with concussions (1.7x) and core/trunk injuries (1.7x). As passing plays now account for ~60% of NFL plays, this trend has direct injury prevention implications. For football staff, this means play-calling tendencies and practice design should consider the injury risk profile of different play types, especially regarding high-risk positions and concussion management.
methods: Retrospective descriptive epidemiology study using data extracted from official NFL gamebooks for all 32 teams across the 2013-2016 regular seasons. All 2,721 in-game injuries not occurring on special teams were included. Play type (run vs. pass), injury location (lower extremity, upper extremity, pelvis/hip, trunk/core, head/neck, concussion), and player position (QB, runner, receiver, lineman, linebacker, defensive back) were recorded. Injury location was verified through the following week's injury reports. Multivariable logistic regression controlled for weather conditions and temperature. Statistical significance set at p < 0.05.
findings: Passing plays comprised 55.7% of total plays. Passing plays had significantly higher odds of injury than running plays (OR 1.4, 95% CI: 1.3-1.5, p<0.0001) after controlling for weather and temperature. By injury location: concussion (OR 1.7, 95% CI: 1.2-2.4, p=0.002), core/trunk injuries (OR 1.7, 95% CI: 1.1-2.6, p=0.01), upper extremity (OR 1.4, 95% CI: 1.1-1.9, p=0.02); no effect on lower extremity (OR 1.0, p=0.70). By position: quarterbacks (OR 6.9), receivers (OR 5.0), and defensive backs (OR 2.3) had the greatest increased risk on passing plays; runners (OR 0.4) and linemen (OR 0.7) had decreased risk on passing plays.
limitations: The gamebook-derived injury rate (1.33/team/game) is lower than NFL-reported rates because minor injuries not requiring immediate medical attention are underreported. Study captures only in-game injuries, not practice injuries. Injury severity was not recorded in gamebooks. The study covers 2013-2016 and may not reflect more recent rule changes or evolving play trends. Player age was not recorded in gamebooks.
practicalImplications: Football staff should recognize that as passing plays increase (from 38% in 1978 to 60% by 2018), the overall in-game injury burden increases proportionally — particularly for concussions and trunk/core injuries. Practice designs that emphasize passing routes and coverages at high intensity should be weighted against the injury risk data. Specific attention should be paid to conditioning quarterbacks, receivers, and defensive backs for the collisions common in passing situations. Core and trunk strengthening programs for wide receivers and defensive backs may help mitigate the elevated trunk/core injury rate on passing plays.
athleteDev: Speed-position players (wide receivers, defensive backs, quarterbacks) face dramatically elevated injury risk during passing plays — the primary play type in modern football. Development programs for these players should incorporate collision preparation, core/trunk stability training, and concussion recognition education. Given that receivers face a 5x greater injury odds on passing plays, physical preparation must reflect the actual demands and risk profile of their role, not just speed and agility metrics.
rtp: Injury epidemiology by play type is useful for RTP decision-making. A running back (lower passing-play injury risk, OR 0.4 on pass plays) returning from injury can be more safely reintegrated in run-heavy offensive packages. Conversely, a wide receiver or defensive back cleared for RTP should be progressively reintroduced through practice exposure on passing plays before returning to full game competition, given the substantially elevated injury odds these players face during pass situations.

citation: Connolly JG, Nathanson JT, Sobotka S, Haider S, Gometz A, Lovell M, Choudhri T. Effect of Playing and Training at Altitude on Concussion Incidence in Professional Football. Orthop J Sports Med. 2018;6(12):2325967118794928. DOI: 10.1177/2325967118794928
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study examined whether the protective effects of altitude on NFL concussion incidence are due to acute exposure (the 'slosh effect') or chronic adaptations to living and training at altitude. Data from 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons (1920 team exposures, 569 concussions) were subdivided into four groups based on whether teams trained and played at high altitude (≥644 feet) or low altitude. Teams that trained AND played at high altitude (high-high group) had a 28% lower concussion rate (0.20/exposure) than the overall rate (0.29/exposure), while teams from low altitude playing at high altitude showed no significant reduction.
tldr: NFL teams that both train and play at altitude above 644 feet have significantly fewer concussions than all other groups—a 28% reduction versus the league average. This protective effect appears to require chronic altitude adaptation, not just acute exposure, since low-altitude teams visiting high-altitude venues showed no benefit. Conversely, high-altitude teams traveling to play at low altitude had the highest concussion rate (0.36/exposure), suggesting the protection is quickly lost when players descend. For football programs, this data challenges the simple 'slosh effect' hypothesis and implies that long-term physiological adaptation—not just the game environment—is the key variable. Away teams also had significantly more concussions than home teams regardless of altitude.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using publicly available data from Frontline's Concussion Watch covering 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons (weeks 1–16). A total of 1920 team exposures across 960 games were analyzed. Teams were categorized into four altitude groups: low-altitude training/playing below 644 ft (low-low), low-altitude training/playing above 644 ft (low-high), high-altitude training/playing below 644 ft (high-low), and high-altitude training/playing above 644 ft (high-high). Stadium elevations were approximated via web-based tools. Statistics included Spearman rho, Wilcoxon signed rank, unpaired t-tests, and one-way and two-way ANOVA.
findings: Total of 569 concussions across 1920 team exposures (overall rate 0.29/exposure). Away teams had significantly higher concussion rates than home teams (0.32 vs. 0.27/exposure; P=.03). The high-high group had a significantly lower concussion rate (0.20/exposure) compared with low-low (0.31; P<.05), high-low (0.36; P<.05), and overall (P<.05). The low-high group (0.32/exposure) was not significantly different from other groups. No dose-response relationship between altitude and concussion rate was found (R²=0.005). Quartile analysis showed no significant differences across altitude tiers.
limitations: Use of the binary 644-foot threshold may oversimplify a physiological spectrum; teams near this threshold (e.g., Green Bay at 640 ft vs. Indianapolis at 714 ft) may experience negligible differences. Altitudes studied are below the 2000-foot threshold where most documented physiological adaptations occur. Preseason altitude training was not accounted for. Data sourced from a public aggregator subject to underreporting. Small sample of high-altitude NFL teams (n=9 stadiums) limits statistical precision.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs based at altitude may carry an intrinsic concussion-risk advantage that diminishes when teams travel to lower elevations. This finding does not suggest teams should artificially simulate altitude but highlights environmental factors that may influence head injury protocols. Medical and performance staffs at high-altitude programs should be aware that this protective effect appears to dissipate rapidly upon descending, which may have implications for travel scheduling and pregame concussion baselines for away games. Low-altitude programs playing at high-altitude venues should not expect acute protection.
athleteDev: The altitude-concussion relationship is relevant primarily for programs located at elevation (e.g., Denver, Colorado Springs, Salt Lake City). For athlete development, the chronic physiological adaptations to altitude—including erythropoietic, vascular, and potentially neuroprotective changes—may have broader implications for training quality and recovery. Programs at low altitude would not be expected to gain concussion-risk benefits from short altitude training camps based on these data.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play protocols, but the finding that high-altitude teams playing away games had the highest concussion rate (0.36/exposure) raises the possibility that altitude descent could transiently increase concussion vulnerability. Clinicians managing RTP for players from altitude-based teams should consider whether rapid environmental changes after travel could influence concussion symptom presentation or risk of re-injury in the short term.

citation: Connolly JG, Nathanson JT, Sobotka S, Haider S, Gometz A, Lovell M, Choudhri T. Effect of Playing and Training at Altitude on Concussion Incidence in Professional Football. Orthop J Sports Med. 2018;6(12):2325967118794928. DOI: 10.1177/2325967118794928
abstract: NOTE: This entry (ID 175) is the same paper as ID 174 — identical content, authors, journal, and DOI. The two files differ only in filename casing and spacing (ID 174 uses lowercase with a double space before 'altitude'; ID 175 uses title case with single spaces). This descriptive epidemiology study examined whether the protective effects of altitude on NFL concussion incidence are due to acute exposure or chronic adaptations. Data from 2012–2015 NFL seasons showed teams that trained AND played at high altitude (≥644 ft) had a 28% lower concussion rate than the league average.
tldr: This is a duplicate file of ID 174 with a different filename casing. NFL teams that both train and play at altitude above 644 feet have significantly fewer concussions (28% reduction vs. league average). The protection requires chronic altitude adaptation—not acute exposure—since low-altitude teams visiting high-altitude venues gained no benefit. High-altitude teams playing away at low-altitude venues had the highest concussion rates, suggesting protection disappears rapidly upon descent. Away teams had more concussions than home teams regardless of altitude.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using publicly available data from Frontline's Concussion Watch covering 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons (weeks 1–16). A total of 1920 team exposures across 960 games were analyzed. Teams were categorized into four altitude groups based on training and game venue elevation relative to a 644-foot threshold. Stadium elevations were approximated via web-based tools. Statistics included Spearman rho, Wilcoxon signed rank, unpaired t-tests, and one-way and two-way ANOVA.
findings: Total of 569 concussions across 1920 team exposures (overall rate 0.29/exposure). Away teams had significantly higher concussion rates than home teams (0.32 vs. 0.27/exposure; P=.03). High-altitude teams playing at high altitude had a significantly lower concussion rate (0.20/exposure) compared with low-low (0.31; P<.05), high-low (0.36; P<.05), and overall (P<.05). Low-high teams (0.32/exposure) showed no significant difference from other groups. No dose-response relationship between stadium altitude and concussion rate was found (R²=0.005).
limitations: Use of the binary 644-foot threshold may oversimplify a physiological spectrum. Altitudes studied are below the 2000-foot threshold where most documented physiological adaptations occur. Preseason altitude training not accounted for. Data from a public aggregator subject to underreporting. Small sample of high-altitude NFL teams (n=9 stadiums).
practicalImplications: Same as ID 174: NFL and college football programs based at altitude may have an intrinsic concussion-risk advantage that diminishes when teams travel to lower elevations. Medical and performance staffs at high-altitude programs should note that protection appears to dissipate rapidly upon descent. Low-altitude programs visiting high-altitude venues should not expect acute concussion protection for their players.
athleteDev: Same as ID 174: Chronic altitude adaptation—not short-term exposure—appears to drive the concussion-protective effect. For athlete development, programs at elevation may benefit from related physiological adaptations (erythropoietic, vascular) that could support training quality and recovery. Short altitude training camps are unlikely to replicate these benefits based on current data.
rtp: Same as ID 174: This study does not directly address RTP protocols, but the finding that high-altitude teams playing away games had the highest concussion rate (0.36/exposure) suggests altitude descent could transiently increase concussion vulnerability. Clinicians managing RTP for altitude-based players should consider whether rapid environmental changes after travel could influence concussion symptom presentation or re-injury risk.

citation: Timmins RG, Bourne MN, Hickey JT, Maniar N, Tofari PJ, Williams MD, Opar DA. Effect of Prior Injury on Changes to Biceps Femoris Architecture across an Australian Football League Season. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2017;49(10):2102–2109. DOI: 10.1249/MSS.0000000000001333
abstract: This observational cohort study examined in-season changes in biceps femoris long head (BFlh) fascicle length in 30 elite Australian Football League players—12 with a prior unilateral hamstring strain injury (HSI) and 18 controls without HSI history. BFlh architecture was assessed via 2D ultrasound six times across the 23-week 2016 AFL season. Previously injured limbs had shorter fascicles than controls at all time points throughout the season. All groups showed an early-season increase in fascicle length followed by progressive shortening, with athletes who had prior HSI ending the season with shorter fascicles than they started with.
tldr: AFL players with a prior hamstring strain injury end the season with shorter biceps femoris fascicles than they began with—in both the previously injured and the contralateral uninjured limb. Players without injury history maintain fascicle length across the full season. All athletes see a brief early-season fascicle lengthening (peaking around week 5), but the subsequent shortening is more severe and sustained in previously injured athletes. The previously injured limb was shorter than controls at every measurement point throughout the year. This suggests that prior hamstring injury impairs structural adaptation across a full competitive season, likely due to residual neuromuscular inhibition, and that injury history must be a central consideration in any in-season hamstring management program.
methods: Observational retrospective cohort study during the 2016 AFL season (23 competitive weeks). Thirty elite male AFL players from two clubs participated: 18 controls (no HSI history) and 12 with a prior unilateral BFlh strain in the preceding 12 months. BFlh muscle thickness, pennation angle, and fascicle length were assessed via 2D B-mode ultrasound (12 MHz, GE Vivid-i) at weeks 1, 5, 9, 13, 17, and 23. Fascicle length was estimated using the validated Blazevich equation. Log-transformed data and Cohen's d effect sizes with 90% CIs were used; effects ≥0.2 = small, ≥0.5 = moderate, ≥0.8 = large.
findings: Previously injured limbs had shorter fascicles than controls at all time points (moderate to large effects; d range: −1.15 to −0.77). All groups peaked in fascicle length at week 5. The control group's fascicle length was similar at season start and end (trivial change). Previously injured limbs ended the season shorter than they started (moderate effect; d=−0.75). Contralateral uninjured limbs also ended shorter than at week 1 (small to moderate effects). The contralateral uninjured limb had shorter fascicles than controls from weeks 9–23 (moderate to large effects; d range: −0.87 to −0.54). Fascicle length peaked at week 5 across all groups then progressively shortened through week 23.
limitations: No preseason architecture data were collected (November–February), so fascicle changes during that period are unknown. No concurrent collection of match/training load, internal/external load, or resistance training variables. Two-dimensional ultrasound requires fascicle length estimation since full fascicles exceed the probe field of view (typical error ~0.30 cm). Three athletes sustained new HSIs during the study, requiring removal from later time points. Relatively small sample (n=30) from two clubs.
practicalImplications: For football programs, this study reinforces that prior hamstring injury is a chronic risk factor that persists structurally throughout an entire season—not just in the immediate post-injury period. Staff should use in-season ultrasound screening to identify players with short BFlh fascicles (particularly those with prior HSI) as candidates for targeted eccentric loading. The early in-season window (weeks 1–5) appears to be the period most responsive to fascicle lengthening interventions. After week 5, fascicles progressively shorten through the remainder of the season, making in-season eccentric hamstring maintenance critical.
athleteDev: Athletes with a prior hamstring strain show blunted structural adaptation to season demands compared to uninjured peers—a finding consistent with residual neuromuscular inhibition impairing the eccentric stimulus needed to add sarcomeres in series. Eccentric loading programs (e.g., Nordic hamstring curl, hip extension exercises) during the early in-season period and throughout the off-season are important tools for restoring BFlh fascicle length in previously injured athletes. Coaches should integrate systematic hamstring architecture monitoring into annual screening, recognizing that the contralateral 'uninjured' limb also shows reduced adaptation following unilateral HSI.
rtp: This study highlights that returning a player to play after hamstring strain injury does not reset the structural deficits in BFlh fascicle length. The previously injured limb maintained shorter fascicles than controls at every in-season assessment point, even when the athlete was cleared for full participation. RTP decisions should incorporate fascicle length assessment via ultrasound alongside traditional strength and functional criteria. Athletes with persistently short fascicles post-RTP should be considered for continued eccentric loading prescription and heightened hamstring strain recurrence monitoring throughout the season.

citation: Medeiros TM, Ribeiro-Alvares JB, Fritsch CG, Oliveira GS, Severo-Silveira L, Pappas E, Baroni BM. Effect of Weekly Training Frequency With the Nordic Hamstring Exercise on Muscle-Strain Risk Factors in Football Players: A Randomized Trial. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2020; (Ahead of Print). DOI: 10.1123/ijspp.2018-0780
abstract: This randomized trial compared the effects of performing the Nordic hamstring exercise (NHE) once versus twice per week across an 8-week program on hamstring strain injury (HSI) risk factors in 32 high-level football (soccer) players aged 18–23 years. Isokinetic strength (knee flexor/extensor peak torques, H:Q ratios) and BFlh muscle architecture (thickness, pennation angle, fascicle length) were assessed before and after the programs. Only the twice-weekly group demonstrated significant improvements in hamstring eccentric and concentric strength and H:Q ratios, while both groups achieved similar increases in BFlh muscle thickness and fascicle length.
tldr: For high-level football players, two NHE sessions per week are needed to achieve hamstring strength gains, whereas one session per week is sufficient to increase biceps femoris fascicle length and muscle thickness. The twice-weekly group improved hamstring eccentric peak torque by ~11% and concentric torque significantly, while the once-weekly group showed no isokinetic strength change. Both groups increased BFlh fascicle length by ~8–9% (~1 cm). This has direct practical value: during congested match schedules when two weekly NHE sessions are not feasible, a single session can still preserve or enhance muscle architecture—a modifiable HSI risk factor. However, for full hamstring strengthening, two weekly sessions should be the minimum target.
methods: Randomized trial with 32 male high-level football players (under-20 and under-23 from a national premier division club; ages 18–23 years) completing an 8-week NHE program. Group 1 (G1, n=15) performed NHE once per week; Group 2 (G2, n=17) performed NHE twice per week. Both groups followed the same progressive periodization (sets/reps per session). Isokinetic knee flexor/extensor strength (Biodex, 60°/s) and BFlh architecture (B-mode ultrasound, GE Vivid-i, 13 MHz) were assessed pre- and post-training. Analysis of covariance with baseline as covariate, independent t-tests for percentage changes, and Cohen's d effect sizes were used.
findings: G2 had significantly higher hamstring concentric peak torque post-training than G1 (155–164 vs. 149–158 N·m; P=.043; ES=0.27). Trends favored G2 for hamstring eccentric peak torque (212–234 vs. 198–221 N·m; P=.098; ES=0.37), H:Q conventional ratio (P=.089; ES=0.31), and H:Q functional ratio (P=.076; ES=0.50). G2 showed significantly larger percentage changes in hamstring eccentric and concentric torque and H:Q functional ratio (P≤.028; large ESs). No between-group differences were found for BFlh thickness (P=.864), pennation angle (P=.289), or fascicle length (P=.406). Both groups increased BFlh thickness (small-moderate ESs) and fascicle length (~8–9%; small ESs) within-group.
limitations: No control group (without NHE) due to club requirements; prior evidence suggests outcomes would remain unchanged in a non-NHE group. High dropout rate due to in-season injuries (15 of 54 excluded), reducing sample size. Baseline strength values differed between groups by up to 8% in some measures, potentially giving G2 greater room for improvement despite statistical covariate adjustment. Only left limb was assessed for muscle architecture. Compliance was 100% by study design (minimum thresholds applied), which may not reflect real-world adherence.
practicalImplications: Football coaching and medical staff should prioritize at least two NHE sessions per week to develop meaningful hamstring strength adaptations in high-level players. During match-congested periods where two sessions are impractical, a single weekly NHE session can still meaningfully increase BFlh fascicle length—preserving an important structural risk factor. This pragmatic dose-response finding helps staff make evidence-based decisions about NHE frequency when full evidence-based protocols (3x/week for 10 weeks) are not feasible. Programs should view the NHE as a year-round tool rather than a preseason-only intervention.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, this study clarifies the dose of NHE needed to drive different adaptations: muscle architecture (fascicle length, thickness) responds to even low weekly volumes, while strength gains require higher frequency. Given that short BFlh fascicles and eccentric hamstring weakness are independent predictors of future HSI, programs should design NHE progressions that achieve both targets. For younger or developing players who may have lower baseline strength, the twice-weekly protocol is especially important to build the strength reserve needed to withstand high-speed running demands.
rtp: During rehabilitation and return to play after hamstring strain injury, this study suggests that even a once-weekly NHE may be sufficient to begin restoring BFlh fascicle length—a key structural adaptation for reinjury prevention. However, achieving meaningful eccentric strength recovery prior to clearance for full training and competition requires at least twice-weekly NHE sessions. RTP criteria should include both fascicle length normalization (via ultrasound) and eccentric strength symmetry, with NHE frequency titrated accordingly as the athlete progresses through the rehabilitation continuum.

citation: Gillen ZM, Burch RF, Saucier DN, Strawderman L, Luczak T, Piroli A, Petway AJ, Rath T. Effects of a Strength and Conditioning Offseason Program on Countermovement Jump Ground Reaction Forces in Division I American Football Players. J Strength Cond Res. 2023;XX(X):1–10. DOI: 10.1519/JSC.0000000000004600
abstract: This pre-post repeated-measures study examined the effects of a 10-week NCAA Division I football offseason strength and conditioning program on ground reaction forces (GRFs) during bilateral countermovement jumps (CMJ) and single-leg jumps (SLJ) in 16 players. Force plates quantified unweighting, braking, propulsive, and performance metrics, along with a jump efficiency index. CMJ propulsive phase duration decreased while peak braking power, peak propulsive power, mean propulsive force, and jump height increased. SLJ improvements included peak braking power, force at the low position, braking RFD, eccentric force, peak propulsive power, mean propulsive force, and jump height in both legs. Jump efficiency improved for both CMJ and SLJ.
tldr: Ten weeks of an offseason S&C program significantly improved vertical jump performance in Division I football players across multiple GRF metrics for both bilateral and unilateral jumps. Key findings: CMJ jump height increased (~5%), propulsive phase duration decreased (indicating more explosive force application), and the CMJ efficiency index improved (force at the low position closer to peak force). Single-leg jumps showed even broader improvements, including braking RFD, eccentric force, and propulsive power for both legs. This study is the first to document pre-post GRF changes from an offseason program in this population, providing a detailed framework for how S&C professionals can use force plate data to quantify program effectiveness—beyond simple jump height—and monitor eccentric and concentric adaptations that transfer to sprint, change-of-direction, and injury-resilience outcomes on the field.
methods: Pre-post repeated-measures design with 16 NCAA Division I American football players (height 187.01±3.05 cm; body mass 102.33±9.33 kg) who completed ≥90% of a 10-week offseason S&C program (resistance training, plyometrics, speed/agility, anaerobic conditioning; 4 sessions/week with a deload in week 6). Players performed 3 CMJ and 3 SLJ (each leg) attempts on a single AMTI AccuGait force plate (1 kHz) at pre- and post-testing. GRFs were processed offline in LabVIEW to calculate unweighting, braking, propulsive, and performance metrics including a jump efficiency index. Dependent samples t-tests (CMJ) and two-way repeated-measures ANOVA (leg × time for SLJ) were used; alpha=0.05.
findings: Body mass increased significantly (p=0.043; d=0.553). CMJ: peak braking power increased (p=0.023; d=0.545), propulsive phase duration decreased (p=0.007; d=0.691), peak propulsive power increased (p<0.001; d=1.201), mean propulsive force increased (p=0.012; d=0.630), and jump height increased (p=0.005; d=0.727). CMJ efficiency index improved (p=0.016; d=0.676; 0.07→0.04). SLJ (both legs): peak braking power (p=0.044; η²=0.245), force at low position (p<0.001; η²=0.662), braking RFD (p<0.001; η²=0.587), eccentric force (p=0.030; η²=0.276), peak propulsive power (p=0.001; η²=0.555), mean propulsive force (p=0.026; η²=0.288), and jump height (p=0.002; η²=0.469) all increased. SLJ efficiency index improved (p=0.001; η²=0.552). No significant leg × time interactions were found.
limitations: Small sample size (n=16) limits position-specific analyses. No control group without S&C training. Jump efficiency index is relatively understudied; reliability data are not yet established and individual responses were variable (5 subjects did not improve CMJ efficiency). Sprint and change-of-direction performance were not concurrently assessed to confirm transfer of jump improvements. Body mass increased, which may confound absolute force interpretations for some metrics. The specific contribution of individual program components (resistance training vs. plyometrics vs. conditioning) cannot be isolated.
practicalImplications: This study validates the use of bilateral and unilateral force plate CMJ assessments as sensitive tools for monitoring offseason S&C program effectiveness in collegiate football players. Strength and conditioning coaches should track GRF metrics—particularly braking phase variables, propulsive power, and the efficiency index—not just jump height, as these provide mechanistic insight into eccentric and concentric adaptations. SLJ assessments appear especially sensitive to training-induced braking and eccentric force changes and can also inform injury risk monitoring. A 10-week offseason block is sufficient to produce meaningful, statistically significant improvements across multiple GRF metrics, supporting its use as a standard assessment window.
athleteDev: The improvements in eccentric (braking) and concentric (propulsive) GRF metrics after 10 weeks reflect enhanced stretch-shortening cycle efficiency, which is foundational for sprint acceleration, cutting, and jumping performance in football. The jump efficiency index—measuring how close force at the low position is to peak force—improved from 7% to 4% difference for the CMJ, suggesting athletes better compressed the 'spring' of the countermovement. For linemen and skill positions alike, tracking these changes across offseason blocks can guide individualized programming decisions and help coaches identify athletes with 'energy leakage' during the eccentric phase that may limit explosive performance.
rtp: The SLJ has been identified in the literature as a potential indicator of knee injury risk and return-to-sport status after ACL reconstruction (Kotsifaki et al., 2022, cited within). This study demonstrates that SLJ GRF metrics improve meaningfully after an offseason program, establishing training-response benchmarks relevant to return-to-play progressions. For athletes recovering from knee injuries—the most common injury type in American football—regular force plate SLJ assessments can help sports medicine staff objectively track recovery of eccentric and propulsive force capabilities and determine readiness for return to full practice and competition.

citation: Morvant BA, Heintz EC, Foreman JJ. Effects of Age and Travel on National Football League Quarterback Performance: A Correlational Study. International Journal of Kinesiology & Sports Science. 2021;9(1):45-50. 10.7575/aiac.ijkss.v.9n.1p.45
abstract: This correlational study examined the effects of age, travel distance, and their interaction on NFL quarterback passer ratings using data from the 2018 season (N=527 quarterback-game observations). OLS regression was used to predict quarterback ratings from age, distance traveled, season week, weeks since bye week, and an age-distance interaction term. Results showed that season week, weeks since bye week, and distance traveled all had significant negative effects on quarterback rating, while the age-performance relationship was nonlinear and the impact of travel differed by quarterback age.
tldr: NFL travel distance negatively affects quarterback performance, but this effect is age-dependent: younger quarterbacks (e.g., 23-year-olds) show the sharpest rating drops after long road trips, while older quarterbacks (mid-30s and beyond) actually tend to perform better after long-distance travel, possibly due to more developed travel-management strategies and higher cognitive experience. Teams and coaches should account for quarterback age when planning travel logistics and managing preparation windows before away games.
methods: Correlational research design using publicly available data from pro-football-reference.com. Sample: 527 quarterback-game observations from the 2018 NFL season. Variables: quarterback rating (dependent), age, age-squared, distance traveled (miles), weeks since bye week, season week, and an age-distance interaction. Ordinary least squares (OLS) regression analyzed both the full season (including playoffs, n=527) and regular season only (n=503).
findings: Season week (p=0.04), weeks since bye week (p=0.041), and distance traveled (p=0.031) had significant negative effects on quarterback rating. The age-performance relationship was nonlinear (p=0.018): ratings declined through an athlete's 20s then increased through the 30s and 40s. The age-distance interaction (p=0.072) showed that younger QBs' ratings decreased with farther travel while older QBs' ratings increased. For example, 23-year-old Baker Mayfield dropped from 100.1 to 70.7 after traveling ~2,500 miles, while 36-year-old Ben Roethlisberger improved from 96.26 to 130.2 over the same distance.
limitations: Data from only one NFL season (2018) limits generalizability. Only quarterback position was examined, precluding insights for other positions. Direction of travel (east vs. west) was not included, which prior research suggests matters. Causal mechanisms underlying the age-travel interaction are speculative and cannot be confirmed with this correlational design.
practicalImplications: NFL coaching and performance staffs should factor quarterback age into travel preparation protocols. Younger quarterbacks may benefit most from structured jet-lag mitigation routines (optimized hydration, sleep scheduling, light exposure management). Bye week timing meaningfully affects performance in subsequent weeks, reinforcing the value of maximizing recovery during bye weeks. Scheduling departments should consider age-travel dynamics when planning road game preparation timelines.
athleteDev: The non-linear age-performance curve observed for quarterbacks — declining through the 20s before rebounding in the 30s — suggests cognitive development and experience accumulation may offset physical decline at the QB position. Younger developing quarterbacks should be given structured education and experience with long-distance travel management as part of their professional development curriculum.
rtp: The negative effect of accumulated season weeks and time since bye week on QB performance highlights how cumulative fatigue compounds over a season. For return-to-play planning, the timing of a player's re-entry relative to recent travel demands and proximity to the last bye week should be factored into readiness assessments, particularly for cognitively demanding skill positions.

citation: Johnson DA, Cable TG, Funnell MP, Peden DL, Thorley J, Ferreira de Cunha M, Reynolds KM, Harris L, Wood M, Chavez-O'Reilly T, Carrington J, Bailey SJ, Clifford T, Heaney LM, James LJ. Effects of Cannabidiol Ingestion on Thermoregulatory and Inflammatory Responses to Treadmill Exercise in the Heat in Recreationally Active Males. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2024. 10.1249/MSS.0000000000003568
abstract: This double-blind, randomized crossover study investigated whether acute CBD ingestion (298 mg) could attenuate thermoregulatory and inflammatory responses to 1 hour of treadmill exercise at 60-65% VO2peak in 32°C, 50% relative humidity in 13 recreationally active males. Core temperature, skin temperature, heart rate, sweat loss, plasma intestinal damage marker (I-FABP), monocyte activation (CD14), and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-alpha) were assessed at multiple timepoints. CBD did not affect thermoregulation but produced minor immunosuppressive effects, reducing CD14 and peak IL-6 responses post-exercise.
tldr: A single 298 mg dose of CBD taken roughly 105 minutes before exercise did not reduce core temperature or improve thermoregulation during 1 hour of treadmill running in the heat, but it did modestly blunt post-exercise immune activation markers (CD14 and IL-6). For sports performance staff considering CBD use during summer training or heat exposure, current evidence does not support it as a thermoprotective strategy, though its marginal anti-inflammatory effects warrant continued investigation under more severe heat stress conditions relevant to NFL training camp environments.
methods: Randomized, double-blind, counterbalanced crossover design. Thirteen recreationally active males (age 25±5 y; VO2peak 50.4±3.2 mL/kg/min; body mass 79.5±8.9 kg). Participants ingested 298 mg CBD isolate or placebo (hemp oil capsules) 105 minutes before 1 hour treadmill exercise at 60-65% VO2peak in an environmental chamber at 32°C and 50% relative humidity. Blood samples collected at baseline, pre-exercise, 20 min post-exercise, and 90 min post-exercise for I-FABP, CD14, IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-alpha. Core temperature (rectal), skin temperature, heart rate, RPE, sweat loss, and plasma osmolality also measured. At least 1 week washout between conditions.
findings: Core temperature increased similarly in both conditions (delta 1.69±0.48°C CBD vs 1.79±0.53°C placebo; p>0.05). No condition differences in heart rate, skin temperature, sweat loss, RPE, or plasma osmolality. Plasma CD14 was 1776 (463-3090) pg/mL lower in CBD vs placebo 90 min post-exercise (p=0.049). Peak IL-6 concentration was 0.8 (-1.1, -0.3) pg/mL lower in CBD (p=0.050); IL-6 AUC difference was -113 (-172, 27) pg/mL·270min (p=0.054). No significant differences in IL-8 or TNF-alpha AUC between conditions. Plasma CBD Cmax was 190±59 ng/mL occurring 90 min post-exercise.
limitations: Small sample size (n=13), all male, recreationally active (not well-trained athletes), limiting generalizability to elite populations. Exercise intensity and duration were modest, producing limited gastrointestinal damage (I-FABP did not exceed baseline). A trial-order effect was detected for I-FABP AUC and IL-6 AUC (when outliers excluded), suggesting the washout period may have been insufficient. CBD absorption was highly variable and peak plasma CBD occurred after exercise cessation, potentially limiting effects during exercise. Findings may not translate to females or more prolonged/severe heat stress.
practicalImplications: CBD does not appear to confer thermoprotective benefits during moderate-intensity exercise in the heat for recreationally active males, so it cannot currently be recommended as a strategy to reduce heat illness risk during summer football camp. However, its mild attenuation of post-exercise monocyte activation (CD14) and IL-6 may be relevant in contexts of high inflammatory load. Staff should be aware that CBD pharmacokinetics are highly variable, that peak plasma concentrations arrive well after ingestion, and that current consumer-recommended doses (10-150 mg/day in most nations) are substantially lower than the 298 mg dose used here.
athleteDev: The finding that CBD did not impair any physiological or perceptual response to exercise in the heat — and produced minor anti-inflammatory effects — suggests it is unlikely to negatively affect training adaptation or performance in athletes who choose to use it recreationally. However, evidence for a meaningful benefit to athletic development is currently insufficient. Athletes curious about CBD should be counseled on the high variability of absorption and the lack of evidence for thermoregulatory benefit.
rtp: The marginal reduction in post-exercise CD14 and IL-6 with CBD raises the hypothesis that it could assist in managing systemic inflammation during return-to-play from soft tissue injuries. However, the effect sizes observed here were small and occurred under only modest inflammatory stress. Clinical application to RTP is premature without further evidence. Anti-doping status should always be confirmed as CBD product purity varies and contamination with prohibited cannabinoids is a documented risk.

citation: Costello JP II, Wagner JD, Dahl VA, Cohen JL, Reuter AM, Kaplan LD. Effects of COVID-19 on Rate of Injury and Position-Specific Injury During the 2020 National Football League Season. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. 2023. 10.1519/JSC.0000000000004610
abstract: This retrospective epidemiological study compared NFL injury rates during the 2020 COVID-affected season against a 5-season baseline (2015-2019). A total of 5,597 injuries were identified across all seasons. Relative to prior seasons, the 2020 regular season showed significantly elevated overall injury risk, particularly during the first two thirds of the season, as well as increased postseason injury risk. Specific increases in hamstring, groin/hip, and abdominal/core injuries were found, with position-specific spikes in cornerbacks (hamstring), guards (hip/groin), safeties (Achilles), running backs (quadriceps and hamstring), and defensive tackles (arm injuries).
tldr: The elimination of the 2020 NFL preseason and COVID-19-related restrictions on in-person training resulted in a 30.8% overall increase in regular-season injury risk compared to the prior 5-season average, with the largest spikes in soft tissue injuries early in the season (Week 1 RR=7.33). This study strongly supports the principle that proper preseason conditioning and gradual workload progression are essential injury prevention strategies. For football staff, the findings reinforce that virtual-only offseason programs are insufficient substitutes for structured in-person training, and that specific positions (cornerbacks, safeties, running backs, guards, defensive tackles) carry the highest risk when traditional preseason preparation is disrupted.
methods: Retrospective review of all NFL injuries from the 2015-2020 seasons using publicly available data from NFL.com, Pro-Football-Reference.com, ProsportsTransactions.com, and ESPN.com. An injury was defined as any event causing a player to miss the next game or be listed as do-not-return in elimination games. Athlete exposure (AE) was calculated assuming 3 live practices and 1 game per week per team. Relative risk (RR) with 95% CI was calculated comparing 2020 season injury rates to the 2015-2019 mean. Statistical analyses used SPSS v28, including two-tailed t-tests, chi-square tests, and one-way ANOVA.
findings: Total injuries: 993 in 2020 vs. 879-962 per season from 2015-2019. Regular season overall RR=1.308 (p=0.0005). Week 1 RR=7.33 (p=0.0024). W1-6 RR=1.964 (p=0.0304); W7-12 RR=1.8909 (p=0.0208); W13-17 no significant change. Postseason overall RR=1.1444 (p<0.0001). Anatomic increases: abdominal/core RR=1.248 (p=0.0011), groin/hip RR=2.534 (p=0.0019), hamstring RR=3.644 (p=0.0143). Position-specific increases: cornerback overall RR=3.99 (p<0.0001), guard overall RR=1.1913 (p=0.0136). Specific anatomic-position combinations with significant increases included CB hamstring, G hip/groin, S Achilles, RB hamstring, RB quadriceps, and DT arm. No change in proportion of game vs. practice injuries.
limitations: Retrospective design relying on publicly available data with potential for misreporting. No access to electronic medical records limited injury detail. Player- and team-specific variability in offseason training routines during COVID-19 could not be controlled. Roster size rule changes and COVID-related schedule disturbances introduced variability in AE calculations. Causal attribution to preseason elimination vs. other COVID-related factors cannot be definitively established.
practicalImplications: The dramatic injury spike in Week 1 and across the first half of the 2020 season provides strong evidence that canceling preseason games and replacing in-person preparation with virtual training creates unacceptable injury risk for NFL players. Performance and sports medicine staff should resist pressure to eliminate or substantially reduce preseason physical preparation and should ensure that any offseason disruption is followed by a structured return-to-full-load protocol before the regular season begins. Position groups most at risk for soft tissue injuries following disrupted preparation include cornerbacks (hamstring), safeties (Achilles), running backs (hamstring and quadriceps), and guards (hip/groin).
athleteDev: The findings underscore the critical role of sport-specific preseason conditioning in injury prevention. Hamstring, hip/groin, Achilles, and quadriceps injuries — all of which increased in 2020 — are well-documented sequelae of deconditioning in explosive, sprint-dependent athletes. Strength and conditioning programs for football must include progressive eccentric hamstring loading, hip mobility work, and Achilles tendon load tolerance training as core preseason elements, particularly after any period of reduced training.
rtp: The study is directly relevant to RTP protocols following extended absences (e.g., injury, suspension, holdout). An athlete returning to full NFL practice after weeks of limited training mirrors the physiological conditions of the 2020 preseason-less return: elevated soft tissue injury risk, especially for hamstrings, hip flexors, Achilles, and quadriceps. RTP programs should include a phased workload ramp-up over multiple weeks, position-specific movement preparation, and close monitoring of soft tissue injury symptoms, particularly during the first 6-12 weeks of return.

citation: Moreno-Pérez V, Del Coso J, Romero-Rodríguez D, Marcé-Hernández L, Peñaranda M, Madruga-Parera M. Effects of home confinement due to COVID-19 pandemic on eccentric hamstring muscle strength in football players. Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports. 2020;30:2010-2012. 10.1111/sms.13768
abstract: This prospective observational study measured eccentric hamstring muscle strength at three timepoints (14, 28, and 49 days) during COVID-19 home confinement in 30 semi-professional football (soccer) players who had been confined since March 14, 2020. Strength was assessed using the Nordic hamstring exercise and a validated video-based smartphone method. Despite players following prescribed home training programs from their teams' S&C staff, eccentric hamstring strength declined significantly and progressively throughout confinement.
tldr: Even when semi-professional soccer players followed prescribed home training programs during COVID-19 confinement, eccentric hamstring strength declined by 8% after 28 days and by 14.1% after 49 days of isolation. This is the first study to quantify detraining-related hamstring weakness during confinement and provides a clear warning for football staff: extended periods away from sport-specific training — including sprinting, acceleration, and deceleration — substantially increase hamstring injury risk upon return. The authors recommend a structured transition period before return to official competition, calibrated to the length of confinement.
methods: Prospective observational letter study with 30 semi-professional football (soccer) players from a Spanish Soccer League team. Confinement began March 14, 2020 for 7 weeks. Players followed prescribed home programs including strength training with bodyweight loads, proprioception, and high-intensity intermittent routines. Eccentric hamstring strength measured using the Nordic hamstring exercise at 14, 28, and 49 days post-confinement onset. A validated video-based smartphone method (Nordics App) was used to calculate Nordic break-point angle and hamstring force. Measurements taken at the same time of day following a rest day. Test-retest reliability: CV = 1.2±3.2% for break-point angle.
findings: Bilateral Nordic break-point angle decreased by 1.9% at 28 days and by 4.4% at 49 days compared to 14-day values (p<0.05). These angular reductions corresponded to eccentric hamstring strength decreases of 8.0% at 28 days and 14.1% at 49 days of confinement (p<0.01). Similar patterns were observed in both dominant and non-dominant limbs. Reductions occurred despite players maintaining prescribed home training programs targeting lower limb strength, suggesting body-weight-only training is insufficient to maintain football-specific hamstring strength.
limitations: Letter to the editor with a small sample (n=30) from a single semi-professional team; findings may not fully generalize to professional or elite players. The comparison reference is the 14-day timepoint rather than pre-confinement baseline, which may underestimate total decline if strength was already decreasing in the first two weeks. Only eccentric hamstring strength was measured; other muscle groups (quadriceps, hip flexors, calf) were not quantified. No control group of non-confined players was available.
practicalImplications: Football performance and medical staff must build a structured return-to-play loading period between the end of any prolonged off-site or virtual training phase and the onset of full-contact practice. Given that 14.1% eccentric hamstring strength loss accumulated over just 7 weeks of home training, teams returning from extended absences should incorporate progressive Nordic hamstring exercise volume, on-field sprint progressions, and acceleration/deceleration work over a minimum 2-4 week bridge period before resuming full competitive load.
athleteDev: The study confirms that football-specific physical qualities — particularly the eccentric hamstring strength required during sprinting — cannot be maintained through bodyweight home programs alone. This has direct implications for NFL offseason programming: extended virtual-only or limited-facility periods should be supplemented with accessible eccentric hamstring loading tools (e.g., portable Nordic benches, resistance bands for hamstring curls). Athletes should be individually screened upon return with the Nordic hamstring test or equivalent eccentric assessment before full sprint-based practice is cleared.
rtp: A 14.1% reduction in eccentric hamstring strength after 7 weeks of limited training represents a clinically meaningful increase in hamstring strain injury risk. Return-to-play protocols following any extended training disruption should include a mandatory eccentric hamstring strength assessment baseline and a graded re-loading program before high-speed sprint work is reintroduced. This is particularly critical for positions with high sprint demands (wide receivers, cornerbacks, running backs, safeties) and for athletes with a prior hamstring injury history.

citation: Flatt AA, Allen JR, Keith CM, Esco MR. Effects of Position and Injury Status on Associations Between Preseason Workload and Heart-Rate Variability Profiles in American College Football Players. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. 2025. 10.1123/ijspp.2024-0469
abstract: This observational study quantified position-specific associations between HRV profile characteristics (LnRMSSD mean and coefficient of variation) and GPS/accelerometer-derived external workload parameters in 27 Division I college football players (15 skill, 12 midskill) during preseason training camp, while accounting for injury-related participation status (15 full go, 12 go as can). Skill players who consistently performed the highest total workloads exhibited the most stable HRV (lowest LnRMSSD CV), while midskill players with the highest total workloads showed the greatest HRV instability. A moderate effect-size reduction in LnRMSSD was observed in players with go-as-can participation status.
tldr: During preseason camp at a national championship-winning Division I program, HRV stability responded oppositely to high training loads depending on position: skill players (WRs, DBs) who ran the most showed the most stable HRV, while midskill players (LBs, RBs, TEs) who accumulated the highest total loads showed the greatest day-to-day HRV fluctuation. This position-specific pattern was independent of injury status, though players with minor injuries tended to have moderately lower HRV. For football performance staff, interpreting HRV data without accounting for position group risks misclassifying well-adapted aerobically fit skill players and potentially overtaxed heavy midskill players.
methods: Observational study of 27 NCAA Division I football players from a national championship-winning program during a 13-day preseason camp (15 training sessions). Skill group (n=15): 8 DBs and 7 WRs (height 187.5±4.3 cm, weight 89.8±5.3 kg). Midskill group (n=12): 5 LBs, 4 RBs, 3 TEs (height 188.9±5.7 cm, weight 104.6±6.8 kg). Linemen excluded due to limited wearable tracker availability. Participation status: full go (n=15) or go as can (n=12). HRV (LnRMSSD) measured daily via 55-second photoplethysmography recordings (ithlete app) pre-practice. External workload from Catapult GPS/accelerometers: total distance, high-speed (19.4-25.8 km/h) and sprint (>25.8 km/h) distances, peak velocity, Player Load, and inertial movement analysis (IMA). Camp mean and CV calculated for all metrics. Multiple regression used to test position x workload interactions with injury status as covariate.
findings: Skill players ran significantly more total distance (5712 vs 4902 m, p<0.001), high-speed distance (316 vs 171 m, p<0.0001), and sprint distance (95 vs 38 m, p<0.0001) than midskill players. Skill LnRMSSD CV was inversely associated with total distance (r=-0.67, p<0.05) and Player Load (r=-0.56, p<0.05), and positively with total distance CV (r=0.61, p<0.05). Midskill LnRMSSD CV was positively associated with total distance (r=0.60, p<0.05), Player Load (r=0.63, p<0.05), and high speed CV (r=0.68, p<0.05). Multiple regression confirmed significant position x total distance (interaction p=0.002, adjusted r2=0.30) and position x Player Load (interaction p=0.002, adjusted r2=0.29) interactions independent of injury status. LnRMSSD was moderately greater in full-go vs go-as-can players (ES=0.73, p<0.07) with no statistically significant between-group workload differences.
limitations: Small sample size (n=27) from a single team, limiting generalizability. Linemen were excluded due to tracker availability constraints. Injury status was determined subjectively by sports medicine staff; players may not consistently self-report issues. HRV collected at the training facility rather than post-waking, increasing noise. Aerobic fitness and plasma volume — likely explanatory variables — were not directly measured. Lack of internal load data (RPE, session-RPE) is a further limitation. The potential bidirectional relationship between injury and HRV could not be fully explored.
practicalImplications: Football performance staff using HRV monitoring during preseason camp should interpret LnRMSSD CV in the context of position group. A skill player maintaining stable HRV while accumulating high total workloads likely reflects high aerobic fitness and positive adaptation — intervention is probably not warranted. Conversely, a midskill player (LB, RB, TE) showing increasing LnRMSSD CV alongside high total workload may be experiencing fatigue-related autonomic dysregulation and could benefit from workload reduction, especially if high-intensity output is also declining. Injury-related participation status should be considered when comparing average LnRMSSD across individuals.
athleteDev: The study provides a framework for individualized HRV monitoring in college football. High aerobic fitness in skill players appears to buffer cardiac-autonomic responses to high training loads during intense preseason camp in heat — suggesting that aerobic development should be a priority for skill position athletes entering camp. For midskill players, particularly larger LBs who accumulate very high total loads, the data suggest a potential training tolerance ceiling that, when exceeded, produces autonomic stress detectable via HRV. This has implications for optimizing long-term player health and development by tailoring camp workloads to position-specific physiological profiles.
rtp: The observation that players with go-as-can (minor injury) status showed moderately lower LnRMSSD than fully participating peers (ES=0.73, p<0.07) supports the hypothesis that minor musculoskeletal injuries suppress cardiac parasympathetic activity. HRV monitoring may therefore serve as an adjunct tool in RTP decisions — sustained suppression of LnRMSSD in an injured player could signal inadequate recovery before clearing for full participation. However, the current evidence is insufficient to establish HRV thresholds for RTP clearance, and this should be a focus of future research.

citation: Horgan BG, West NP, Tee N, Halson SL, Drinkwater EJ, Chapman DW, Haff GG. Effect of repeated post-resistance exercise cold or hot water immersion on in-season inflammatory responses in academy rugby players: a randomised controlled cross-over design. European Journal of Applied Physiology. 2024. doi:10.1007/s00421-024-05424-3
abstract: This randomised controlled cross-over study investigated the effects of repeated post-resistance exercise cold water immersion (CWI, 15°C×15 min) versus hot water immersion (HWI, 39°C×15 min) versus a control (CON, static stretching) on systemic inflammatory and hormone responses in 18 male academy Super Rugby players across a 12-week in-season programme. Blood measures were collected fasted and in the morning at baseline and every four weeks. Linear mixed-effects models were used to analyse treatment, time, and interaction effects across the biomarkers.
tldr: Both CWI and HWI can be used post-resistance exercise during the competitive season in team sport athletes without compromising adaptive inflammatory responses. CWI produced small reductions in muscle damage (CK) and increased heat shock protein HSP-72, suggesting a thermoregulatory adaptive response. HWI produced moderate reductions in CK and the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-1ra, while increasing anabolic growth factors IGF-1 and PDGF-BB. Practitioners can prescribe either modality during in-season phases, with HWI providing an anabolic signalling advantage and CWI supporting thermoregulatory adaptation.
methods: Randomised controlled cross-over design; 18 male academy Super Rugby players (age 19.9±1.5 y, mass 98.3±10.7 kg); 12-week programme divided into three 4-week blocks (CON, CWI at 15°C, HWI at 39°C), each 15 min whole-body post-resistance exercise twice weekly; fasted morning blood samples at baseline and end of each 4-week block for CK, myoglobin, IL-1ra, IL-1b, IL-4, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, IL-17a, TNF-α, IGF-1, IGFBP-3, PDGF-BB, HSP-72, testosterone, and cortisol; linear mixed-effects models for analysis.
findings: CWI significantly reduced CK compared to CON (p=0.025, g=0.05) and HWI reduced CK compared to CON (p<0.001, g=0.62). HWI decreased IL-1ra compared to CON (p=0.013, g=0.59). HWI increased growth factors IGF-1 and PDGF-BB compared to CON and CWI (p<0.001–0.026, g=0.06–0.17). CWI increased HSP-72 compared to HWI (p=0.004, g=0.46). No significant differences in testosterone, cortisol, or pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1b, IL-6, IL-8, IL-17a, TNF-α) were observed between interventions.
limitations: Small sample size (n=18) and a single time-point blood measurement at the end of each 4-week block may not capture the full kinetics of the biomarkers. Dietary standardisation was not fully controlled, which may influence inflammatory and hormonal responses. Subjects could not be blinded to temperature conditions. Pro-inflammatory cytokine responses were influenced by percent body fat as a covariate, limiting direct comparisons.
practicalImplications: Both CWI and HWI are safe and viable post-resistance exercise recovery strategies during the competitive season in highly trained athletes. HWI may be favoured when anabolic adaptation is a priority (via IGF-1 and PDGF-BB elevation), while CWI may be preferred when thermoregulatory stress adaptation (HSP-72) is desired. Practitioners should consider individualising water immersion temperature based on athlete goals and responses across a competitive block.
athleteDev: For football players in training blocks requiring simultaneous strength development and recovery, HWI post-resistance exercise may support anabolic signalling pathways (IGF-1, PDGF-BB) that underpin muscular adaptation, while still reducing muscle damage markers. CWI remains effective for blunting exercise-induced muscle damage (CK) without substantially impairing adaptive inflammatory responses, making both viable tools in periodised recovery plans for developing athletes.
rtp: During return-to-play from soft tissue injury, water immersion strategies can be strategically periodised. CWI may be appropriate in early stages to attenuate muscle damage and inflammation, while HWI may support the anabolic and growth factor environment needed for tissue remodelling and muscular reconditioning in later rehabilitation stages. Neither modality significantly alters testosterone or cortisol, suggesting minimal hormonal disruption during RTP programmes.

citation: Clements E, Ehrmann F, Clark A, Jones M, McCall A, Duffield R. Effects of Prematch Travel and Training on Physical and Technical Performance in International Football Matches. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. 2024 (Ahead of Print). doi:10.1123/ijspp.2023-0511
abstract: This study examined the relationship between prematch travel variables (duration, direction, time-zone change, arrival time) and in-camp training load on ensuing physical and technical match performance in 68 national-team footballers competing in 108 international matches. GPS-derived match running data and Opta technical statistics were aligned with travel records and 3-day training load sums. Linear mixed models assessed the relationships, accounting for player, match, and position as random effects.
tldr: Prematch international travel had minimal effects on physical and technical match performance when players arrived at least 48 hours before kickoff. Eastward travel was associated with small increases in total and very high-speed distance and a 5% reduction in pass accuracy, though this likely reflects playing against higher-ranked opponents rather than travel fatigue per se. The most actionable finding is that training high-speed running in the 3 days following arrival was positively related to match high-speed running output, meaning readiness to train on arrival should be the primary focus for national team management.
methods: Observational study; 68 male senior national team footballers (age 25.4±3.6 y); N=108 matches (October 2018–March 2023, including 2022 FIFA World Cup qualifying); GPS (STATSports Apex) for physical data; Opta for technical data; travel variables: duration (0.85–31 h), direction, time-zone difference, hours from arrival to kickoff; 3-day training load sum prior to each match; linear mixed models with player ID, match ID, and position as random effects.
findings: Travel variables explained little variance in outcomes (R²=0.02–0.16). Eastward travel was associated with more total distance (+3.003 m/min, p=0.042) and very high-speed distance (+0.336 m/min, p=0.030) per match, and a 5% reduction in pass accuracy (p=0.012). Greater time-zone difference was associated with more match decelerations (p=0.027). Higher training high-speed distance was associated with greater match high-speed distance (p=0.004) and more decelerations (p<0.001). Higher training total distance was associated with fewer match decelerations (p=0.002). 95% of athletes arrived ≥48 hours before matches.
limitations: Single national team limits generalisability to teams with different travel strategies. Small sample (n=103 data points after exclusions); only 2 of 14 matches were against higher-ranked opponents; 71% of matches resulted in wins, potentially masking performance decrements. Jet lag mitigation strategies were not controlled. No control over athlete behaviour before, during, or after travel.
practicalImplications: National team football staff should prioritise having players arrive at least 48 hours (ideally longer for multi-time-zone travel) before international matches to minimise travel-related performance risk.
