Because training high-speed running loads in the 3 days prior to matches relate to match running outputs, staff should monitor and optimise athlete readiness to train upon arrival. When early arrival is unavoidable, jet lag management strategies (light therapy, sleep hygiene, strategic napping) should be implemented.
athleteDev: For football players required to travel for national team duty, managing the transition from club to national team training is critical. Building high-speed running exposure into the first 3 days of camp, contingent on athlete recovery status, supports match-day running performance. Position-specific training plans should be adapted to the travel demands experienced by each player upon arrival.
rtp: The finding that travel had minimal impact on performance when 48+ hours of recovery was allowed is relevant for players returning to competition after medical travel or injury rehabilitation away from base. Clinicians should allow adequate recovery windows after air travel when scheduling return-to-play progression milestones, particularly for high-speed running and technical skill reassessment.

citation: Stergiopoulos DC, Botonis PG, Miliotis P, Zavvos AX, Ntalapera SD, Papaleontіou GL, Pilatis GA, Soukara EK, Geladas ND. Effects of Simulated Short-Haul Flights on Sport-Specific Performance, Sleep, and Psychophysiological Responses in Football Players. Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports. 2025;35:e70169. doi:10.1111/sms.70169
abstract: This randomised crossover study examined the effects of two simulated short-haul air travels (4-hour and 2-hour SHAT) compared to a control condition (no travel) on sport-specific performance, sleep, and psychophysiological responses in 19 highly trained male football players. Flights were simulated in a hypobaric chamber at 2438 m altitude equivalent and were completed at 08:00. Measures included football-specific aerobic capacity (Hoff test), sprint performance (10m, RAST), CMJ, technical skills (LSPT), sleep (actigraphy), HRV, salivary cortisol, and sIgA.
tldr: Even short-haul air travel of 2–4 hours impairs football-specific performance the day after travel, primarily due to sleep loss the night before the flight rather than the physiological stressors of the flight itself. Both 4-h and 2-h simulated flights reduced total sleep time by over 100 minutes the night before departure (early morning flights), and next-day performance suffered across aerobic endurance, sprint ability, CMJ, and technical skill. The 4-h flight additionally suppressed HRV and sIgA on the day of and day after travel. Club staff should avoid booking early-morning flights to prevent pre-flight sleep disruption.
methods: Randomised crossover; 19 high-level male football players (age 21.3±4.2 y, VO2max Yo-Yo Level 1: 1823±325 m); two simulated short-haul flights (4-h and 2-h) in a hypobaric chamber (8,000 ft / 2438 m altitude equivalent) at 08:00 one week apart, followed by a control (CON); measures at baseline (D-1), day of flight (DF), and day after (D+1); Hoff test (total distance covered and blood lactate), 10-m sprint, RAST (4×35 m), CMJ, LSPT; actigraphy sleep monitoring; HRV (LnRMSSD, Polar V800); salivary sIgA and cortisol; Profile of Mood States; linear mixed models.
findings: Total sleep time was reduced by ~114 min (4-h) and ~107 min (2-h) the night before the flight (p<0.01). Hoff test distance decreased ~5–6% post-both flights vs. baseline (p=0.02 and p<0.01). RSA mean and total time increased ~3.5% after both flights (p<0.001). CMJ decreased ~8.5% at +3h and ~5.5% at D+1 for both flights (p<0.001). LSPT performance decreased only after the 4-h flight (p<0.05). 10-m sprint declined 6% after both flights (p<0.001). sIgA was reduced on DF and D+1 for both flights (p<0.05). HRV (LnRMSSD) decreased only on the day of the 4-h flight (p<0.05, d=-0.64). Mood states and salivary cortisol were unaffected.
limitations: Simulated flights in a hypobaric chamber may underestimate real-world travel stressors (transfers, psychological stress, nutrition variability). Both flights departed at 08:00, limiting generalisation to other departure times. Small sample (n=19). Study did not isolate sleep deprivation from hypoxic exposure or somatic inertia. No female athletes included.
practicalImplications: Football club administrators and sports science staff should avoid scheduling early-morning flights before competition days, as the associated sleep loss is the primary driver of next-day performance impairment. Longer flights (≥4 h) additionally suppress autonomic (HRV) and immune (sIgA) function, increasing infection risk. Strategies to preserve pre-travel sleep — including travel timing adjustments and sleep hygiene protocols — are more impactful than managing in-flight physiological stressors.
athleteDev: For football players who frequently travel for domestic or European competition, accrued sleep debt from repeated early-morning short-haul flights may compound within a congested schedule. Monitoring HRV and sIgA alongside sleep actigraphy can help identify players at elevated risk of immune suppression and performance decline after travel-heavy periods. Training load on post-travel days should be reduced.
rtp: Returning players who have recently travelled (particularly early-morning short-haul flights) should have RTP physical testing and high-intensity training sessions scheduled to allow for at least one full recovery night after travel. Reduced aerobic performance, sprint capacity, and neuromuscular output (CMJ) on the day following travel suggest that early post-travel assessments may under-represent true baseline function.

citation: Sancese A, Taylor L, Walsh G, Byrd E, Delextrat A. Effects of sprint versus strength training on risk factors for hamstring injury in football players. Journal of Sports Medicine and Physical Fitness. 2023;63(4):580-7. doi:10.23736/S0022-4707.22.14529-9
abstract: This randomised controlled trial compared 4 weeks of in-season sprint training (5–6×30–40 m, twice weekly) versus Nordic hamstring exercise (NHE) training on a battery of modifiable hamstring strain injury (HSI) risk factors in 18 male university football players. Isokinetic dynamometry assessed eccentric hamstring peak torque, the functional H:Q ratio, angle of peak torque, rate of torque development, and hamstring torque at near-extension angles. Sprint mechanics (30 m time, F0, V0, Pmax, RFpeak) were also assessed pre- and post-intervention.
tldr: Both 4-week in-season sprint training and Nordic hamstring exercise programs improved key hamstring injury risk factors, but through different mechanisms. Both groups showed increased eccentric peak torque at 60°/s and a protective rightward shift in the angle of peak torque toward knee extension. The NHE group uniquely improved eccentric strength at faster velocities (180°/s), while the sprint group uniquely improved rate of torque development — the ability to quickly generate force — which may be particularly protective during the late swing phase of sprinting. This supports a personalised approach: players with low RTD may benefit most from sprint training, while those with low eccentric strength may benefit more from NHE.
methods: Randomised controlled trial; 18 male university football players (age 20.9±2.5 y, 75.8±9.1 kg); sprint group (n=9) performed 5–6×30–40 m twice weekly; NHE group (n=7 after dropouts) performed 2×5 to 3×6 reps twice weekly; both interventions added at end of regular practice; 4-week in-season intervention; isokinetic dynamometry (Biodex) at 60°/s and 180°/s for Hecc, Qcon, Hecc:Qcon, APT, RTD200, torque at 30°/20°/10°; sprint mechanics via MySprint app (F0, V0, Pmax, RFpeak); mixed-design ANOVA with repeated measures.
findings: Both groups showed significant increases in Hecc at 60°/s (+8–9.9%; p<0.05) and rightward shifts in APT at 60°/s (−32–36%; p<0.05). Torque at 10° improved in both legs (+21–31%; p<0.05) and at 20° in the dominant leg (+15%; p<0.05) in both groups. Only the NHE group showed a significant increase in Hecc at 180°/s (+24.5%; p=0.035, d=0.89). Only the sprint group showed a significant improvement in RTD200 of the dominant leg at 60°/s (+29.4%; p=0.004, d=1.01). No significant changes in sprint performance (30-m time) or sprint mechanics (F0, V0, Pmax, RFpeak) were found in either group.
limitations: Small sample size (n=18) may have underpowered secondary outcomes. Post-testing in December coincided with high physical fatigue and academic pressure that may have limited adaptations. Potential confounders (hamstring flexibility, pelvic tilt) were not measured. Sprint training was unloaded, which may explain lack of improvements in sprint mechanics; resisted sprint training may be needed to improve F0.
practicalImplications: Both sprint training and NHE are practical, low-resource in-season options for reducing hamstring strain injury risk in football players. A personalised approach is recommended: use sprint training for players with low RTD (rapid force development deficits) and NHE for those with low peak eccentric hamstring strength. The concurrent shift in angle of peak torque toward knee extension with both modalities supports their use in protective conditioning programs at any level of the sport.
athleteDev: In-season hamstring injury prevention for football players should incorporate either sprint training or NHE based on individual risk factor profiling. Sprint training added post-practice twice weekly for 4 weeks can meaningfully improve RTD and shift the APT toward protective extended angles without requiring equipment. These programs are accessible for sub-elite and collegiate football programmes where performance facilities may be limited.
rtp: Following hamstring strain injury, the choice between NHE and sprint training during the later phases of return to play should be guided by the player's residual deficits. Players with reduced eccentric peak torque (particularly at faster velocities) may prioritise NHE, while those with deficits in rate of torque development — common in previously strained hamstrings — may benefit more from progressive sprint training. Both interventions shifted the APT toward extended positions relevant to the injury mechanism during sprinting.

citation: Brown HA, Chalmers S, Topham TH, Clark B, Jowett A, Meyer T, Jay O, Périard JD. Efficacy of the FIFA cooling break heat policy during an intermittent treadmill football simulation in hot conditions in trained males. British Journal of Sports Medicine. 2024;0:1-8. doi:10.1136/bjsports-2024-108131
abstract: This randomised crossover study evaluated the efficacy of the FIFA cooling break heat policy in 12 trained males completing five 90-minute intermittent treadmill football match simulations at 40°C and 41% relative humidity (32°C WBGT). Five cooling configurations were compared: regular match (REG), 3-min breaks without cooling (BRKno-cool), 3-min breaks with cooling (BRKcool: current FIFA policy — chilled fluid and ice towel), 5-min extended half-time without breaks (ExtHTonly), and 3-min cooling breaks combined with extended half-time (ExtHTcool). Rectal temperature (Tre), heart rate, whole-body sweat rate, and RPE were recorded.
tldr: The current FIFA 3-minute cooling break policy (chilled fluid ingestion plus ice towel at 30 and 75 minutes) meaningfully reduces core temperature, heart rate, and perceived exertion during football in hot conditions. Combining the cooling breaks with a 5-minute extended half-time provides additional benefit. Simply stopping play without cooling (BRKno-cool) or extending half-time alone (ExtHTonly) provides minimal protection. For football in extreme heat (≥32°C WBGT), the FIFA cooling break protocol is an effective, low-cost strategy and should be implemented as intended — with cold fluids and ice towels, not just a rest break.
methods: Randomised crossover; 12 trained males (age 27±6 y, VO2peak 61±7 mL/kg/min); 5 experimental trials in 40°C±1°C, 41% RH (32°C WBGT); 10.7 km total distance across two 45-min halves; REG (no breaks), BRKno-cool (3-min break, no cooling), BRKcool (3-min break with 5 mL/kg of 13°C water and ice towel), ExtHTonly (20-min half-time, no mid-half breaks), ExtHTcool (BRKcool + 20-min half-time); outcomes: rectal temperature (telemetric capsule), heart rate, whole-body sweat rate, RPE (Borg), thermal comfort; linear mixed-effects models.
findings: Final Tre was lower in BRKno-cool (−0.20°C, p=0.038), BRKcool (−0.39°C, p<0.001), and ExtHTcool (−0.40°C, p<0.001) vs. REG (39.1°C). Mean Tre was lowest in ExtHTcool (38.2°C), then BRKcool (38.3°C), BRKno-cool and ExtHTonly (38.4°C), and REG (38.5°C). Mean heart rate was lower during BRKcool (−6 beats/min) and ExtHTcool (−7 beats/min) vs. REG (p<0.001). Whole-body sweat rate was similar across all trials (1.7±0.5 L/h). Fluid intake was ~645 mL higher in BRKcool and ExtHTcool vs. other conditions. RPE was lower in BRKcool (−0.4, p=0.004) and ExtHTcool (−0.5, p=0.002) vs. REG. ExtHTonly alone did not significantly reduce final Tre or RPE vs. REG.
limitations: Laboratory treadmill setting may underestimate real-world thermal strain due to absence of solar radiation and differences in air velocity. External workload was fixed, preventing self-regulation of effort. Fluid intake was prescribed rather than ad libitum. Study only included males; a separate female study was conducted. Results may not extend to harsher or more variable outdoor environments.
practicalImplications: Football staff and medical personnel should ensure the FIFA cooling break protocol is implemented as designed — ice-water towels and chilled fluids are essential components, not optional accessories. Simply pausing play without cooling tools provides substantially less benefit. Extending half-time by 5 minutes in combination with cooling breaks provides the greatest thermal and cardiovascular protection and should be adopted when logistically feasible in hot match conditions (≥32°C WBGT). Adequate cold fluid availability (~1.2 L per player) should be planned at each cooling break station.
athleteDev: For preseason or training in hot environments, implementing brief cooling breaks with cold fluids and ice towels during high-intensity sessions can reduce cumulative thermal load and attenuate perceived exertion, potentially allowing for better training quality and session completion. Heat acclimatisation combined with structured cooling breaks may be the most effective combination for developing athletes training in warm climates.
rtp: Players returning from heat-related illness or those with known elevated heat strain risk (e.g., post-exertional heat stroke) should have structured cooling breaks incorporated into their RTP training progression. The cooling break strategy tested in this study reduced both core temperature and cardiovascular strain without altering sweat rate, making it safe and practical for monitored progressive exposure to exercise in the heat during rehabilitation.

citation: Ferrari F, Stein R, Milani M. Electrocardiographic diversity in football players: how distinct ethnic patterns shape athletes' hearts. Eur J Prev Cardiol. 2026;33:6–7.
abstract: This expert interview presents findings from a series of four studies examining ECG patterns in over 6,000 Brazilian male soccer players across two decades. The research addresses the underrepresentation of mixed-race and ethnically diverse athletes in current international ECG interpretation guidelines. Key topics include the low prevalence of abnormal ECGs (3%), early repolarization patterns by ethnicity, and the rarity of the 'African/Afro-Caribbean' ECG pattern in Brazilian players. The authors argue that Brazil's high genetic admixture dilutes distinct ECG patterns seen in more homogeneous populations and that current guidelines inadequately account for admixed populations.
tldr: Brazilian football players—including Black and mixed-race athletes—show a lower rate of ECG abnormalities (3%) and a high prevalence of early repolarization (40%) compared to more homogeneous African cohorts, likely due to Brazil's extensive genetic admixture. Early repolarization in athletes appears benign in this context, while inferolateral T-wave inversion still warrants further cardiac workup. These findings highlight the need to update international ECG screening criteria to reflect ethnically diverse, admixed populations.
methods: Expert interview format summarizing findings from four peer-reviewed studies (B-Pro Foot ECG study) on resting ECGs of male Brazilian soccer players from multiple clubs across Brazil. Data were collected over approximately two decades from more than 6,000 athletes. In addition to ECGs, imaging data (echocardiography) were collected to assess structural correlates of ECG findings. Early repolarization was classified into seven distinct morphologies. Athletes were followed for a mean of four years.
findings: 97% of ECGs were normal across ethnically diverse Brazilian players. Early repolarization was present in 40% of players overall, with higher rates in Black (48%) and mixed-race (41%) athletes. The classic J-wave with ascending ST-segment was the most common morphology and was benign. Higher-risk patterns (horizontal ST-segment, inferolateral J-waves) were rare. No sudden cardiac deaths occurred during four years of follow-up. The 'African/Afro-Caribbean' ECG pattern was rare in Brazilian Black and mixed-race players and, when present, was not associated with structural abnormalities. ECG abnormalities became more common with age (26–35 years).
limitations: All studies were limited to male athletes; female footballer data were not yet published at the time of the interview. The mixed-race category in Brazil is heterogeneous, making precise genetic comparisons difficult. The interview format limits direct access to full study methodology and data tables. Only Brazilian football players were studied, limiting generalizability to other admixed or non-Brazilian populations.
practicalImplications: Clinicians screening ethnically diverse or admixed athlete populations should not over-interpret early repolarization as pathological—it is extremely common in Brazilian athletes and was not associated with adverse cardiac events. Inferolateral T-wave inversion, however, should always prompt further evaluation including cardiac MRI even when echocardiogram is normal. International ECG screening criteria should be updated to better reflect admixed populations to reduce unnecessary testing and false positives.
athleteDev: For development programs working with ethnically diverse rosters—including significant proportions of Black, mixed-race, or non-European athletes—standard ECG interpretation guidelines may over-flag benign training-related adaptations. Understanding that early repolarization is highly prevalent and likely benign in this population allows medical staff to avoid unnecessary disqualifications or investigations that could disrupt athlete development pathways.
rtp: No specific RTP decision framework was outlined, but the study findings support that early repolarization alone is insufficient grounds to restrict return to play. Inferolateral T-wave inversion warrants cardiac MRI before clearance, even with a normal echocardiogram. These principles are relevant for pre-participation screening decisions and for evaluating athletes flagged during routine cardiac screening.

citation: Bergstein VE, Agarwal AR, Solon LF, Mikula JD, Best MJ, LaPorte DM. Epidemiologic Trends in hand injuries in the National Football League from 2009-2010 to 2019-2020. Phys Sportsmed. 2023. DOI: 10.1080/00913847.2023.2286942.
abstract: This retrospective study characterized the prevalence, anatomic distribution, and severity of hand injuries (wrist, metacarpus, finger, thumb) in NFL players over 11 seasons (2009–2010 to 2019–2020) using the publicly available Pro Football Reference database. Of 6,127 players, 847 (13.8%) sustained at least one hand injury, totaling 825 injuries. Hand injuries decreased in prevalence by 65.6% over the study period, coinciding with increased NFL player safety rule implementation. Quarterbacks had the highest prevalence and severity of hand injuries across all anatomic locations. Wrist injuries were the most severe, with the lowest return-to-play rate (93.3%), most players on injured reserve (28.6%), and most games missed (mean 2.5).
tldr: NFL hand injuries declined 65.6% over 11 seasons (2009–2019), a trend that coincides with the implementation of 39 new player safety rules—16 of which directly involve the hands. Quarterbacks are disproportionately affected across all hand injury subtypes due to the demands of throwing and the vulnerability during tackles. Wrist injuries are the most severe hand injuries by all severity metrics. These findings support position-specific injury prevention protocols and continued advocacy for hand-protective rule changes in the NFL.
methods: Retrospective review of the publicly available Pro Football Reference database for all 32 NFL teams from the 2009–2010 through 2019–2020 seasons. Hand injuries were subclassified as finger, hand (metacarpus), thumb, or wrist. Players excluded if any injury not related to the hand was the reason for games missed. Variables analyzed included injury anatomic location, player position, games missed (mean and SD), percentage of players placed on injured reserve (IR), and percentage returning to play (RTP). Data analyzed using Microsoft Excel.
findings: 825 total hand injuries were identified across 726 players (11.85%). Injury distribution: wrist 23.75%, metacarpus 34.90%, finger 18.18%, thumb 23.15%. Overall RTP rate was 97.40%; mean games missed was 1.70 (SD 3.31); 14.77% placed on IR. Wrist injuries had the lowest RTP (93.33%), highest mean games missed (2.48, SD 1.96), and highest IR rate (28.57%). Finger injuries had the lowest mean games missed (0.72) and lowest IR rate (4.61%). Quarterbacks had the highest prevalence of hand injuries at all anatomic locations (26.67% overall). Hand injuries declined 65.6% from 2009 to 2019, with the steepest decline after 2012 when most new hand-relevant safety rules took effect.
limitations: Data sourced from a public database that classifies injuries only by general anatomic location, precluding specific injury type identification (e.g., fracture vs. ligament tear). Injuries classified as 'other' receive no anatomic designation, creating potential underrepresentation. Underreporting of hand injuries is likely given financial disincentives for NFL players to disclose injuries. Some position groups had very small sample sizes, limiting generalizability of position-specific findings. Study did not explore associations or risk factors contributing to hand injury occurrence.
practicalImplications: Medical and coaching staff should implement position-specific hand injury prevention protocols, with special emphasis on quarterbacks who face the highest prevalence and severity. Wrist injuries warrant heightened clinical attention given their association with prolonged absence and IR placement. The correlation between rule changes and declining hand injury prevalence supports advocacy for continued adoption of player safety rules involving hand and blocking mechanics.
athleteDev: For skill players—especially quarterbacks and wide receivers—hand and wrist injury prevention should be integrated into sport-specific training and protective equipment protocols. The high RTP rate for most hand injuries (97.4%) is reassuring for development staff, but wrist injuries may require modified training plans and extended timelines. Position-specific screening at the combine and preseason may help identify at-risk athletes for targeted prehabilitation.
rtp: Overall RTP rate across all hand injuries was 97.4%, with an average of 1.7 games missed. Wrist injuries had the lowest RTP rate (93.3%) and highest IR placement (28.6%), suggesting they carry the greatest risk of season-ending outcomes. Finger injuries had the fastest recovery (mean 0.72 games missed). Return-to-play planning should account for position demands: quarterbacks, who rely heavily on hand function for throwing mechanics, may require longer functional recovery timelines even after apparent clinical healing.

citation: Williams JZ, Singichetti B, Li H, Xiang H, Klingele KE, Yang J. Epidemiological patterns of initial and subsequent injuries in collegiate football athletes. Am J Sports Med. 2017. DOI: 10.1177/0363546516685317.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study examined rates of initial and subsequent injuries among collegiate Division I football players at two universities from 2007 to 2011, using a methodologically rigorous approach that calculated athlete-exposures (AEs) separately for initial and subsequent injuries. Of 365 enrolled players, 238 injuries occurred in 159 players. The overall injury rate was 35.2 per 10,000 AEs. Subsequent injuries occurred at a rate 1.4 times higher than initial injuries (45.3 vs. 31.7 per 10,000 AEs). The rate of subsequent head/neck/face injuries was double that of initial injuries, and wide receivers and defensive linemen had significantly elevated subsequent injury rates.
tldr: Once a collegiate football player sustains an initial injury, their risk of a subsequent injury increases approximately 40–50%, with the rate rising further with each successive injury. Head, neck, and face injuries—predominantly concussions—showed the highest rate escalation (2x) after an initial injury. Wide receivers and defensive linemen are at the greatest positional risk for subsequent injuries. This study provides a methodologically superior estimate of subsequent injury risk by using position-specific AEs, which prior studies did not adequately separate.
methods: Prospective cohort study using data from collegiate football players at two Division I universities (2007–2011). A total of 365 players were enrolled. Athletic trainers reported injuries via the Sports Injury Monitoring System (SIMS). Injury was defined as an event during games or practices requiring medical attention and resulting in at least one day of missed play. AEs for initial injuries were counted from enrollment to first injury; AEs for subsequent injuries were counted only from return to play after the prior injury. Poisson regression was used to calculate injury rate ratios (RR) with 95% confidence intervals, adjusting for covariates including age, grade, BMI, position, years of experience, and 12-month injury history.
findings: Total AEs: 67,564; overall injury rate: 35.2 per 10,000 AEs. Initial injury rate: 31.7; subsequent injury rate: 45.3 per 10,000 AEs (RR = 1.4; 95% CI 1.1–1.9). Game RR = 1.8 (95% CI 1.1–3.0). Rates for second through sixth injuries were 41.1, 77.5, 151.5, 116.7, and 64.9 per 10,000 AEs respectively. Subsequent head/neck/face injury rate was 10.9 vs. 5.6 per 10,000 AEs for initial injuries (RR = 2.0; 95% CI 1.1–3.5); 63% of these were concussions. Wide receivers had subsequent injury RR = 2.2 (95% CI 1.3–3.8); defensive linemen RR = 2.1 (95% CI 1.1–3.9). Prior 12-month injury history was strongly associated with subsequent injury (RR = 4.0 overall).
limitations: Only two Division I football programs were studied, limiting generalizability. Small sample sizes prevented detailed analysis of specific risk factors and how second injury characteristics predict third injury risk. AE recording did not allow separation of players who played from those who only attended competitions, likely underestimating game injury rates. Many athletes had pre-enrollment injury histories, meaning their 'initial' study injury may have been a higher-order injury in reality. Recurrent injuries (same type and site) were rare (n=6), precluding separate analysis.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and team physicians should treat any injury as a significant marker for future injury risk, particularly for head/neck/face injuries and in wide receiver and defensive lineman positions. Prior 12-month injury history should be a key screening variable in pre-participation evaluations. Subsequent injury prevention protocols—including load management, biomechanical screening, and targeted rehabilitation—should be prioritized for previously injured athletes, especially those who missed more than 3 months due to an initial injury.
athleteDev: Injury history is one of the strongest predictors of future injury in collegiate football. Development staff should factor prior injuries into training load decisions and return-to-play clearance standards. The finding that injury rate escalates with each successive injury argues for conservative return standards and structured reconditioning rather than rushing athletes back to full participation. Wide receivers and defensive linemen warrant close monitoring given their disproportionately elevated subsequent injury risk.
rtp: The study underscores that returning a player to play after an initial injury significantly elevates their risk of a subsequent injury—1.4x overall and 1.8x during games. Head/neck/face injuries, particularly concussions, showed a 2x subsequent injury rate, reinforcing the importance of conservative concussion RTP protocols. Players who lost 3 months of play following their first injury had the highest proportion of second injuries (50%), suggesting extended recovery timelines may reflect injury severity that persists as ongoing vulnerability.

citation: McHale KJ, Vopat BG, Beaulieu-Jones BR, Sanchez G, Whalen JM, McDonald LS, DiGiovanni CW, Theodore GH, Provencher MT. Epidemiology and outcomes of Lisfranc injuries identified at the National Football League Scouting Combine. Am J Sports Med. 2017;45(8):1901–1908.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined the epidemiology, treatment patterns, and NFL career impact of Lisfranc injuries identified at the annual NFL Scouting Combine from 2009 to 2015. Of 2,162 Combine participants, 41 (1.9%) had a prior Lisfranc injury; 63.4% were managed operatively. Quarterbacks were at significantly elevated risk (OR 4.4). Lisfranc-injured players had worse NFL draft position, played fewer games, and had shorter early NFL career productivity compared to matched controls. Residual Lisfranc joint displacement greater than 2 mm was associated with significantly worse NFL performance outcomes.
tldr: Lisfranc injuries identified at the NFL Scouting Combine adversely affect draft status and early NFL career performance. Players with prior Lisfranc injuries played significantly fewer games in their first two NFL seasons (16.9 vs. 23.3) and had worse draft positions than matched controls. Residual joint displacement greater than 2 mm is the key radiographic predictor of poor outcomes—athletes with greater displacement had far fewer games played and started. Surgically treated players also fared worse in the draft than those managed nonoperatively, likely reflecting underlying injury severity.
methods: Retrospective review of medical records from all NFL Scouting Combine participants (2009–2015) who had a prior Lisfranc injury identified during comprehensive medical evaluation. Epidemiological characteristics, player position, treatment methods (operative vs. nonoperative), and missed collegiate games were recorded. NFL performance outcomes (draft position, career length ≥2 years, games played and started in first 2 NFL seasons) were analyzed for 2009–2013 Combine participants and compared with position-matched controls (no midfoot injury, no prior surgery, ≤2 missed college games). Radiographic analysis of Combine weightbearing AP foot radiographs assessed residual Lisfranc joint displacement versus the contralateral foot. Logistic regression and two-sample t-tests were used for statistical analysis.
findings: 41 of 2,162 (1.9%) Combine participants had prior Lisfranc injuries. Quarterbacks had significantly elevated risk (OR 4.4; P=.004). Of 41 injuries, 26 (63.4%) were treated operatively (76.9% ORIF with screws/plates, 19.2% suspensory fixation, 3.8% arthrodesis). Mean missed collegiate games: 2.9 ± 4.9 (operative 4.3 vs. nonoperative 0.5; P=.008). Compared to controls, Lisfranc-injured players had worse draft position (142 vs. 111.3; P=.04) and fewer games played (16.9 vs. 23.3; P=.001). Operatively managed players had higher undrafted rates (38.5% vs. 13.3%; P=.04). 41.5% of athletes had residual displacement >2 mm; these players had significantly worse draft position (156.9 vs. 111.2; P=.009) and fewer games played (14.4 vs. 23.3; P=.001) and started (3.1 vs. 10.5; P=.03) vs. controls. 31.7% had radiographic evidence of midfoot degenerative joint disease.
limitations: Retrospective design relying on Combine records; some Lisfranc injuries may have been missed. Initial injury radiographs unavailable, preventing assessment of injury severity at time of occurrence and preventing differentiation between purely ligamentous and fracture-associated injuries. Study was underpowered to differentiate outcomes by specific operative technique. A prior Lisfranc injury may decrease the probability of receiving a Combine invitation, introducing selection bias. Overall NFL career success is multifactorial and not fully captured by the variables studied.
practicalImplications: Radiographic assessment of residual Lisfranc joint displacement versus the contralateral foot at the time of evaluation is critical—greater than 2 mm residual displacement is a significant predictor of worse draft position and reduced NFL game participation. Medical staff evaluating Combine or pre-draft athletes should compare bilateral weightbearing AP radiographs. Achieving anatomic reduction (≤2 mm) during operative management is essential for preserving career longevity. Quarterbacks warrant particular attention given their 4.4x elevated odds of Lisfranc injury.
athleteDev: Lisfranc injuries in developing football players—particularly quarterbacks—carry significant career implications. Residual midfoot instability or displacement directly reduces the number of games an athlete can participate in early in their professional career. For development programs, ensuring complete rehabilitation and confirming radiographic healing before return to full football activities is essential. Athletes with residual displacement above 2 mm may need revised performance expectations and potentially revision surgery.
rtp: None of the 24 Lisfranc-injured Combine participants missed an NFL game during their first two professional seasons due to a foot reinjury, suggesting adequate short-term RTP once athletes reach the professional level. However, despite RTP, these athletes played significantly fewer games overall, indicating a persistent performance deficit. Operative management was associated with significantly more missed collegiate games (4.3 vs. 0.5 for nonoperative), reflecting prolonged initial recovery timelines. Residual displacement >2 mm should serve as a radiographic benchmark guiding clearance decisions before return to competitive football.

citation: Desai SS, Dent CS, Hodgens BH, Rizzo MG, Barnhill SW, Allegra PR, Popkin CA, Aiyer AA. Epidemiology and outcomes of ankle injuries in the National Football League. Orthop J Sports Med. 2022;10(6):23259671221101056.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study examined ankle injuries in NFL players across three seasons (2015–2017) using the Pro Football Reference database, and assessed the effect of those injuries on performance metrics over the following two seasons. Among 5,963 NFL players, 668 ankle injuries were identified (average cumulative incidence 11.2%). Return-to-play rate was 91%. A performance analysis cohort of 159 players showed a statistically significant mean decrease in power rating (PR) of 22% in the first post-injury season and 27% in the second, with significant declines in PR per game played as well as total games played. All position groups and experience categories showed performance decline.
tldr: Ankle injuries in NFL players—despite a high 91% return-to-play rate—cause significant and lasting performance declines across multiple seasons. Players averaged a 22% drop in power rating in year one post-injury and a 27% drop in year two, with fewer games played and lower per-game output across all positions and experience levels. Running backs showed the largest performance decline (31–47%), while quarterbacks showed the smallest. These findings underscore that ankle injuries carry long-term career performance consequences beyond time missed.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological study using the Pro Football Reference database for NFL seasons 2015–2017. A total of 668 ankle injuries in 5,963 players were identified. Cumulative incidence, demographics, and RTP were recorded. For performance analysis, 159 players met inclusion criteria (started ≥8 games in preinjury season, returned to play, played in both post-injury seasons, excluded OL/ST positions). Power ratings (PRs) were calculated as: (offensive yards/10) + (touchdowns × 6) + (combined tackles) + (sacks × 2) + (interceptions × 2). PRs from preinjury season (Y-1) were compared to two postinjury seasons (Y+1, Y+2). Subgroup analyses by position, injury type, and experience category. Paired t-tests and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests were used.
findings: 668 ankle injuries identified; cumulative incidence 11.2%; RTP 91%. Mean overall PR decreased from 96.95 (Y-1) to 76.10 (Y+1; -22%; P<.001) and 70.93 (Y+2; -27%; P<.001). Mean games played declined from 14.76 to 13.19 (Y+1; P<.001) and 12.50 (Y+2; P<.001). Mean PR per game decreased from 6.70 to 5.75 (Y+1; -14%; P<.001) and 5.54 (Y+2; -17%; P<.001). Running backs showed the largest PR decline (DPR2%=-47%); quarterbacks showed the smallest and non-significant decline. All confirmed ankle sprains (n=100) showed statistically significant PR declines. Performance declines were significant across all experience categories (early, intermediate, veteran). OL/ST were excluded from performance analysis.
limitations: Most ankle injuries were unspecified in type; only 51% were confirmed sprains and 4% confirmed fractures. Specific injury patterns, operative vs. nonoperative management, and number of games missed during the index season were not captured. Additional non-ankle injuries during post-index seasons may have contributed to performance decline. OL and ST positions were excluded from performance analysis. Small QB subgroup (n=5) was underpowered. The use of NFL injury reports may underestimate true cumulative incidence due to players playing through unreported injuries.
practicalImplications: Ankle injuries in NFL players should not be viewed as minor events even when players successfully return to play. The sustained decline in per-game performance output over two seasons suggests incomplete functional recovery that current RTP protocols may not adequately address. Enhanced post-return rehabilitation targeting speed, agility, and proprioception—especially for running backs, wide receivers, linebackers, and defensive backs—may help reduce this performance gap. Medical staff should implement multi-season performance monitoring after ankle injuries.
athleteDev: Ankle injury prevention and comprehensive rehabilitation should be cornerstones of NFL player development programs. The finding that even veteran players (≥7 years) show statistically significant performance decline after ankle injuries (DPR1=-20%, DPR2=-22%) suggests that experience and physical maturity do not confer protection from long-term functional impact. Development staff should integrate ankle stability training, proprioceptive conditioning, and load management into year-round programming for all skill positions.
rtp: 91% of NFL players with ankle injuries returned to play (defined as playing at least 1 snap in a subsequent game), which compares favorably to other lower extremity injuries (ACL: 79%, Achilles rupture: 68%). However, RTP rates alone are misleading—players who returned still experienced 22–27% declines in total performance and 14–17% declines in per-game output across the following two seasons. RTP criteria should incorporate functional performance benchmarks beyond clinical healing, particularly speed and agility assessments, to better predict readiness for full competitive contribution.

citation: Palmieri-Smith RM, Mack CD, Brophy RH, Owens BD, Herzog MM, Beynnon BD, Spindler KP, Wojtys EM. Epidemiology of anterior cruciate ligament tears in the National Football League. Am J Sports Med. 2021. DOI: 10.1177/03635465211010146
abstract: This retrospective descriptive epidemiology study examined all ACL injuries entered into the NFL leaguewide electronic health record system from the 2015–2019 seasons (n = 16,403 eligible player-seasons). A total of 314 ACL injuries occurred over 5 years (mean 62/year). The overall 1-season injury risk was 1.9% (95% CI 1.7%–2.1%). Most tears were complete (90%) and nearly all complete tears were treated with reconstruction. The majority of injuries occurred during games (63%) and the preseason (40%). Special teams players had the highest game injury rate (7.6 per 10,000 player-plays), and punt plays had the highest play-type rate (2.19 per 1000 plays). Players with ≤3 years of NFL experience sustained 75% of all tears. Preseason game rates (6.1) were more than double regular season rates (2.7) per 10,000 player-plays.
tldr: NFL ACL injuries average 62 per year, with the highest risk during preseason games and special teams play. Less experienced players (≤3 years) account for 75% of tears. These findings suggest targeted prevention efforts should focus on early-career players, the preseason period, and special teams units.
methods: Retrospective descriptive epidemiology study using the NFL's centralized leaguewide electronic health record (EHR) for seasons 2015–2019. Incidence was calculated as 1-season injury risk (exact binomial distribution) and rates per 10,000 player-plays (Poisson regression). Covariates included injured side, severity, treatment, timing, injury setting, play type, and player demographics including years of NFL service.
findings: 314 ACL injuries occurred over 5 seasons (mean 62/year); 1-season risk 1.9%. Right and left knee equally affected. 90% were complete tears; 100% of complete tears treated with ACL reconstruction. 63% of injuries occurred in games; preseason game rate (6.1/10,000 player-plays) was 2.3x higher than regular season (2.7). Special teams players had the highest game rate (7.6/10,000 player-plays); punt plays the highest play-type rate (2.19/1000 plays). Players with ≤3 years of experience accounted for 75% of injuries. 35% of all ACL injuries occurred during training camp through preseason week 2.
limitations: Pre-NFL ACL injury history not consistently captured, precluding recurrence analysis. Practice participation/exposure data unavailable, preventing practice incidence rate calculations. Weight, height, and BMI recorded at time of signing, not at injury. Partial vs. complete designation made by team medical staff without mandatory imaging confirmation.
practicalImplications: Prevention programs targeting ACL injury should prioritize early-career NFL players and the preseason period. Special teams units—particularly punt coverage—warrant specific attention given their disproportionately high injury rates. Early identification of anatomic risk factors and individualized conditioning progressions during training camp may reduce risk. Rule changes or practice modifications during the first two weeks of preseason could have the greatest population-level impact.
athleteDev: Younger players (rookies and 1-year veterans) are at highest ACL risk, suggesting that the transition to elite NFL-level practice and game intensity is a critical vulnerability window. Progressive loading protocols, strength and neuromuscular training targeting knee stability, and monitored preseason ramp-up plans are particularly important for players entering their first three NFL seasons.
rtp: Approximately two-thirds of NFL players return to play following ACL reconstruction, though many perform at a lower level than pre-injury. Complete tears were universally treated with reconstruction. Return-to-play programming should account for position-specific demands, with special teams players potentially requiring additional sport-specific preparation given the change-of-direction and collision loads involved.

citation: Chung AS, Makovicka JL, Hassebrock JD, Patel KA, Tummala SV, Deckey DG, Hydrick TC, Rubel NC, Chhabra A. Epidemiology of cervical injuries in NCAA football players. Spine. 2019;44(12):848–854. DOI: 10.1097/BRS.0000000000003008
abstract: A descriptive epidemiology study using the NCAA Injury Surveillance Program (ISP) database for the 2009–2010 to 2013–2014 academic years. An estimated 7,496 cervical spine injuries were identified across all NCAA football divisions. The overall injury rate was 2.91 per 10,000 athlete-exposures (AEs). Stingers (brachial plexus injuries) were the most common injury type (1.87/10,000 AEs), followed by cervical strains (0.80/10,000 AEs). Injuries were approximately nine times more likely during competition than practice. The regular season had the highest injury rate (3.30/10,000 AEs). Division I athletes had the highest rates. Linebackers (20.3%) and defensive linemen (18.2%) sustained the most injuries. Contact mechanisms accounted for 90.8% of injuries. The majority of players (64.4%) returned to play within 24 hours; only 2.8% were out more than 21 days. No players required surgery.
tldr: Cervical spine injuries in NCAA football players are mostly minor—stingers dominate, and nearly two-thirds of athletes return to play within 24 hours. Serious injuries such as fractures and disc injuries are rare but carry long return-to-play timelines. Linebackers and defensive linemen are highest-risk positions and should be targeted with neck strengthening, tackling technique, and protective equipment interventions.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using the NCAA-ISP database (2009–2010 through 2013–2014). Poststratification sample weights based on sport, division, and academic year were applied to each injury and AE. Injury rates were reported per 10,000 AEs overall and by event type (practice vs. competition) and time of season (preseason, regular season, postseason). Injury rate ratios (IRRs) with 95% CIs were calculated; CIs not containing 1.0 considered significant. Participation restriction time reported as intervals (<24 h, 1–6 d, 7–21 d, >21 d).
findings: Estimated 7,496 cervical spine injuries over 5 academic years at a rate of 2.91/10,000 AEs. Stingers were most common (1.87/10,000 AEs); cervical strains second (0.80/10,000 AEs). Competition injury rate (14.82/10,000 AEs) was 9.1x higher than practice rate (1.62/10,000 AEs). Regular season rate (3.30) exceeded preseason (2.27; IRR 0.69) and postseason (1.27; IRR 0.39). Division I had highest rates. Linebackers accounted for 20.3% and defensive ends/tackles 18.2% of injuries. Contact mechanism: 90.8%. New injuries: 85.6%. 64.4% returned within 24 hours; 2.8% out >21 days. Cervical disc injuries associated with longest absences (41.6% out 7–21 d; 29.2% out >21 d). No surgeries required.
limitations: Retrospective data reliant on athletic trainer documentation accuracy. NCAA-ISP is a voluntary convenience sample, introducing potential selection bias and limiting generalizability to all NCAA programs. Use of weighted estimates may have biased statistical significance. More severe injuries were rare, limiting power for those subgroup analyses.
practicalImplications: Prevention efforts should be concentrated during the regular season when cervical spine injuries are most likely, and should focus on linebackers and defensive linemen given their elevated risk. Promotion of safe tackling technique, appropriate helmet fit and design, and preventive rehabilitation targeting neck strength are key strategies. While most injuries are transient, cervical disc injuries can result in extended absences and require conservative management timelines.
athleteDev: Cervical spine injuries are an occupational hazard of high-contact football positions. Youth and collegiate players in linebacker and defensive line roles should be taught proper tackling and blocking mechanics early in development to minimize axial loading and hyperextension mechanisms. Neck strengthening programs integrated into regular training may reduce injury severity and recurrence.
rtp: The majority of cervical spine injuries (64.4%) resolved within 24 hours, and no players required surgical intervention during this study period. Cervical disc injuries carried the longest absences (>21 days in 29.2% of cases). RTP decision-making should be individualized, with more severe pathologies (fractures, disc injuries, spinal stenosis) requiring comprehensive medical clearance protocols before return.

citation: Mack CD, Solomon G, Covassin T, Theodore N, Cárdenas J, Sills A. Epidemiology of concussion in the National Football League, 2015-2019. Sports Health. 2021. DOI: 10.1177/19417381211011446
abstract: A retrospective descriptive epidemiology study using NFL electronic health record (EHR) data from all 32 clubs across the 2015–2019 seasons. A total of 1,302 concussions were identified among 1,004 players. Approximately 80% occurred in game settings. Average annual in-season game concussion incidence decreased 23% from 230.7/season (2015–2017) to 177.0/season (2018–2019; P<0.01). The 5-year one-season concussion risk averaged 7.4%. Preseason game rates (70.6/100 games) were slightly higher than regular season rates (61.7/100 games). In regular season games, 790 concussions occurred across 4,657,243 player-plays at a rate of 1.70/10,000 player-plays. Kickoffs had the highest rate per play (0.69/100 plays). Defensive secondary and offensive linemen had the highest counts; tight ends and wide receivers had the highest rates per 10,000 player-plays.
tldr: NFL concussions declined 23% after targeted rule changes and education initiatives implemented before the 2018 season, demonstrating that systematic interventions can meaningfully reduce concussion incidence. Kickoffs and special teams plays remain the highest-risk play types per exposure. Tight ends and wide receivers have the highest concussion rates by position, while running backs and linebackers are most exposed during special teams plays.
methods: Retrospective observational study using the NFL's centralized EHR system (2015–2019) linked to the NFL Game Statistics and Information System for player-play level exposure data. Concussion incidence described by year, season, play type, and roster position. One-season concussion risk calculated using the exact binomial distribution. Game-related rates per 10,000 player-plays calculated using Poisson distribution; IRRs and 95% CIs reported. In-depth game analyses restricted to regular season. Repeat concussion risk calculated within single seasons.
findings: 1,302 concussions in 1,004 players over 5 seasons. 80.3% in games; 17.0% in practice; 2.7% conditioning/other. Regular season had most concussions (n=790; 60.7%; 61.7/100 games). Preseason game rate slightly higher (70.6/100 games). Game concussions declined 23% from 2015–2017 to 2018–2019 (P<0.01). Overall 5-year regular season rate: 1.70/10,000 player-plays. Kickoffs had the highest rate per play (0.69/100 plays). Defensive secondary had highest count (n=227; 28.7%); tight ends had highest rate (2.81/10,000 player-plays). Running backs and linebackers had the highest proportion of concussions on special teams plays (32.7% and 32.3%). Defensive secondary concussion rate declined 41% from 2015 to 2019. One-season risk: 7.4% average; repeat concussion risk 6.8%.
limitations: Concussion is difficult to diagnose definitively; underreporting remains possible despite mandated EHR use. Risk calculations based on player signings rather than static roster size may underestimate risk relative to AE-based methods. Practice participation data unavailable, precluding practice concussion rate calculations. Observational design cannot establish causality between rule changes and incidence reduction.
practicalImplications: The 23% reduction in game concussions following the 2018 rule changes (kickoff modifications, helmet restrictions, head-down tackle ban, blindside block elimination) demonstrates that evidence-based rule and equipment changes can reduce concussion incidence at elite levels. Kickoffs and special teams play remain the highest per-exposure risk activities and should continue to be targets for further rule modification. Helmet performance ratings should guide equipment selection at all levels of football.
athleteDev: Concussion risk is elevated in early career high-speed and high-contact scenarios. Educating young players on proper tackling technique—particularly avoiding lowering the head on contact—and ensuring they wear high-performing helmets are foundational developmental priorities. Wide receivers and tight ends have elevated positional rates and may benefit from position-specific contact preparation and concussion awareness.
rtp: Approximately 6.8% of concussed NFL players sustained a repeat concussion within the same season. A thorough graduated RTP protocol is essential. The NFL's protocol includes standardized symptom assessment, exertional testing, and neurological clearance before return to full practice and competition. The high repeat concussion rate underscores the importance of conservative management and avoidance of premature return.

citation: Pietsch S, Green B, Schache AG, Pizzari T. Epidemiology of quadriceps muscle strain injuries in elite male Australian football players. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2023;00:1–11. DOI: 10.1111/sms.14542
abstract: This study described the epidemiology of quadriceps muscle strain injuries (QMSIs) in AFL players using data from the Soft Tissue Injury Registry of the AFL (STRAFL) across seven consecutive seasons (2014–2020) from all 18 AFL teams. A total of 164 QMSIs from 122 players were reported (134 index; 30 re-injuries). Almost all QMSIs (91.3%) involved the rectus femoris. Half (48.4%) were caused by kicking and most commonly affected the dominant kicking leg (72%). The majority occurred during training (64.6%). Mean return to play (RTP) was 25.4 days (95% CI 22.6–28.2). Rectus femoris injuries took approximately 14 days longer to RTP than vastii injuries (p=0.001). QMSIs with a kicking mechanism had the longest RTP of all injury mechanisms. All 30 re-injuries involved the rectus femoris and most occurred within 6 months of the preceding injury (70%).
tldr: In AFL players, quadriceps strains are predominantly rectus femoris injuries caused by kicking, affecting the dominant leg, and most often occurring in training. They carry a mean RTP of about 25 days, with kicking-mechanism and rectus femoris injuries taking the longest. Re-injuries exclusively involve the rectus femoris, primarily from kicking, and occur early in recovery—emphasizing the need for careful RTP criteria and targeted rehabilitation.
methods: Retrospective analysis of QMSI data reported to the STRAFL across seven seasons (2014–2020) from all 18 AFL teams. Injuries were classified as index or re-injury. Player demographics, injury circumstances (activity, mechanism, timing), MRI reports (primary muscle), and recovery milestones were extracted. Descriptive frequency distributions presented. Recovery outcomes compared across injury type, primary muscle, and mechanism using Mann–Whitney U-tests; Kruskal–Wallis tests for multi-group comparisons. Effect sizes calculated (r = z/√n). Significance level p<0.05.
findings: 164 QMSIs from 122 players over 7 seasons (23.4 QMSIs/year; 1.5 per club per year). 134 index injuries (81.7%), 30 re-injuries (18.3%). 91.3% involved rectus femoris on MRI. 48.4% from kicking; 31% running-related. 72% affected dominant leg. 64.6% occurred at training. Mean RTP: 25.4 days (95% CI 22.6–28.2). Rectus femoris injuries took ~14 days longer RTP than vastii injuries (p=0.001). Kicking-mechanism injuries had longest RTP (H=8.185, p=0.02). All re-injuries involved rectus femoris; 63% from kicking; 70% occurred within 6 months of preceding injury. Competitive season injury frequency highest May–July (middle of season). Re-injury cumulative incidence: 53% within 2 months.
limitations: STRAFL captures only injured players, so incidence rates relative to total exposure cannot be calculated. Voluntary reporting may have resulted in underrepresentation of injuries, particularly pre-season. MRI reports were unavailable for 15.9% of cases, limiting primary muscle analysis in those instances. Data were recorded only for elite male AFL players, limiting generalizability to other populations or female athletes.
practicalImplications: Given that rectus femoris injuries from kicking dominate QMSIs in AFL and carry the longest recovery times, rehabilitation programs should specifically address eccentric strength and load tolerance of the rectus femoris through kicking-specific movement patterns. Strict RTP criteria—particularly for kicking loads—are warranted, as 53% of re-injuries occurred within 2 months. Training environments should be carefully managed given that 64.6% of QMSIs occur at training, and kicking workloads during training likely exceed match loads.
athleteDev: Kicking skill development is central to AFL, but the rectus femoris undergoes large rapid length changes during the kick, predisposing it to strain. Athletes should develop adequate eccentric quad strength, hip flexor control, and progressive kicking load exposure across their development. Identifying dominant-leg asymmetry and prior QMSI history should be part of routine screening, as these factors elevate re-injury risk.
rtp: Mean RTP was 25.4 days overall, but rectus femoris injuries averaged approximately 14 days longer than vastii injuries. Kicking-mechanism injuries had the longest RTP. Re-injury risk is highest within the first 2 months (53% cumulative incidence), indicating that full kicking load tolerance—not just pain-free walking or sprint speed—should be confirmed before RTP. A graduated progression through running, sprinting, and sport-specific kicking workloads is recommended.

citation: Anderson MJJ, Mack CD, Herzog MM, Levine WN. Epidemiology of shoulder instability in the National Football League. Orthop J Sports Med. 2021;9(5):23259671211007743. DOI: 10.1177/23259671211007743
abstract: A retrospective observational descriptive epidemiology study examining all documented missed-time shoulder instability injuries in the NFL from the 2012 through 2017 seasons. Over 6 years, 355 players sustained 403 missed-time shoulder instability injuries (average 67/year). Most injuries occurred during games (65%) via contact (85%). The overall game incidence rate was 3.6 per 100,000 player-plays, highest during the preseason (4.9). After excluding unspecified events, 70% were subluxations and 30% were dislocations; 75% of dislocations were anterior, while subluxations were more evenly split between anterior (45%) and posterior (52%). Median missed time was 47 days for dislocation vs. 13 days for subluxation. Quarterbacks and offensive linemen most commonly experienced posterior instability (73% and 53%); all other positions predominantly had anterior instability.
tldr: Shoulder instability in the NFL averages 67 missed-time injuries per year, with subluxations roughly twice as common as dislocations but dislocations causing far more missed time. Posterior instability is more prevalent in NFL players than previously recognized—especially in quarterbacks and offensive linemen—likely due to sport-specific blocking and throwing mechanics. Preseason carries the highest instability rates, and quarterbacks have the highest per-play game rate.
methods: Retrospective observational study using NFL injury databases (ISS 2012–2014; EMR 2015–2017) covering all 32 teams. Only missed-time shoulder instability injuries were included to maintain consistency across both data systems. Injuries classified by type (subluxation, dislocation), direction (anterior, posterior, inferior, multidirectional), laterality, injury setting, and mechanism. Missed time defined as days from injury to return to full participation. Game incidence rates calculated per 100,000 player-plays using NFL Game Statistics and Information System data. Descriptive statistics and median IQR reported for missed time.
findings: 403 missed-time instability injuries in 355 players; average 67/year; 11% suffered multiple episodes. Left shoulder more affected (57% vs. 43%). 65% of injuries in games, 85% contact mechanism. Overall game rate: 3.6/100,000 player-plays; preseason highest (4.9). Excluding unspecified: 70% subluxations, 30% dislocations. Directions (excluding unspecified): 75% of dislocations anterior; subluxations nearly evenly split anterior (45%) vs. posterior (52%). Posterior instability accounted for ~40% of all specified injuries. Median missed time: 47 days (dislocation) vs. 13 days (subluxation). Quarterbacks highest game rate by position (5.5/100,000 player-plays). QBs: 73% posterior instability; OL: 53% posterior. All other positions: predominantly anterior. Inferior/multidirectional instability rare but associated with greatest missed time.
limitations: Only missed-time injuries included, underestimating true prevalence—particularly for subluxations. ISS (2012–2014) and EMR (2015–2017) differed in reporting criteria, necessitating separate analysis. Incidence analysis limited to games due to unavailable practice exposure data. Return-to-play dates missing for 26% of athletes, limiting missed-time analysis. Injury characterization relied on athletic trainer reporting without mandatory imaging confirmation. Player movement in/out of NFL complicates RTP percentage calculations.
practicalImplications: The high proportion of posterior shoulder instability in NFL players—particularly offensive linemen and quarterbacks—should prompt clinicians to include posterior capsulolabral assessment in these athletes and not assume anterior pathology. Preseason shoulder instability rates are highest, consistent with deconditioning and full-contact drill patterns. Dislocation requires substantially longer recovery than subluxation; treatment planning and roster management should account for this. Posterior shoulder strengthening and sport-specific motor control training are important preventive components.
athleteDev: Offensive linemen and quarterbacks develop posterior instability through repetitive sport-specific loading—blocking mechanics with the shoulder flexed, adducted, and internally rotated, and the late cocking/follow-through phases of throwing. Developing posterior capsulolabral strength and rotator cuff endurance as part of systematic shoulder development programs can reduce chronic posterior loading stress. Early identification of labral laxity or pain during position-specific drills can allow for early intervention.
rtp: Median RTP after subluxation was 13 days; after dislocation 47 days. Approximately 92.8% of NFL players successfully return to full competition after in-season shoulder instability per publicly available data. Inferior and multidirectional instability carried the greatest missed time. Return-to-play decision-making should be informed by instability type and direction, surgical vs. nonoperative treatment pathway, and position-specific shoulder demands—particularly for quarterbacks who require full throwing motion and offensive linemen who require forceful blocking mechanics.

citation: Lamplot JD, Petit C, Lee R, Mack CD, Herzog MM, Myer GD, Hammond K. Epidemiology of Stingers in the National Football League, 2015-2019. Sports Health. 2024. doi:10.1177/19417381231223413
abstract: This retrospective epidemiological study analyzed all in-game stingers (transient traumatic neuropraxia of the brachial plexus or cervical nerve root) recorded in the NFL electronic health record system from 2015 to 2019. A total of 691 in-game stingers occurred over 5 seasons (mean 138.2/year). Incidence rate was 11.48 per 100,000 player-plays overall, higher during the regular season (12.26) than preseason (8.87). Running backs (18.00) and linebackers (15.87) had the highest incidence by position. The average single-season injury risk was 3.74%. Overall, 76.41% of athletes missed no additional time; mean time missed was 4.79 days. Concomitant injuries were uncommon (7.09%), most frequently shoulder injuries, cervical spine injuries, and concussions.
tldr: Over five NFL seasons (2015-2019), stingers occurred at a stable rate of ~138 per year and were most common in running backs and linebackers during regular-season games. The vast majority of affected players missed no additional time beyond the game of injury. While concomitant injuries were rare, concussion and cervical spine injuries did occur and must be ruled out before return to play — a critical reminder for sideline medical staff.
methods: Retrospective epidemiology study. All in-game stinger injuries (coded as 'neck brachial plexus stretch' or 'neck brachial plexus compression') from the NFL centralized electronic health record system were aggregated for the 2015-2019 seasons across all 32 clubs. Injury data were integrated with NFL Game Statistical and Information System (GSIS) play-participation data. Incidence rates per 100,000 player-plays were calculated using the Poisson distribution. Descriptive statistics and Poisson regression were used; significance accepted at p < 0.05.
findings: 691 total in-game stingers over 5 seasons; average single-season risk 3.74%. Regular-season incidence (12.26/100,000 player-plays) significantly exceeded preseason (8.87, p < 0.01). Highest incidence among running backs (18.00), linebackers (15.87), and defensive linemen (14.78). 76.41% of athletes missed no additional time; mean time missed was 4.79 days. Brachial plexus compression injuries resulted in longer mean time loss (5.98 days) than stretch injuries (4.20 days). Recurrence history had no effect on time missed. Concomitant injuries occurred in 7.09%: shoulder (40%), cervical spine (31%), concussion (22%).
limitations: Findings may not generalize to collegiate, high school, or youth football athletes. Underreporting is likely given the injury's transient nature and players' career concerns. The number of in-game plays missed during the injury game was not available. Player age was not available, precluding age-mechanism analysis. Cervical nerve root compression as a distinct mechanism could not be isolated due to EHR coding structure. MRI and imaging data were not available. Permanent neurologic deficits could not be assessed; only time-missed data were available. Injury recurrence before NFL participation was not captured.
practicalImplications: Medical staff should be prepared to efficiently perform concurrent concussion and cervical spine injury evaluations whenever a stinger is diagnosed, as co-occurring injuries — though uncommon — do occur. Understanding that most NFL stingers resolve without time loss can assist clinicians in managing player and coaching staff expectations. Running backs and linebackers represent the highest-risk positions and may benefit from targeted neck-strengthening and contact-technique education.
athleteDev: Running backs and linebackers sustain stingers at more than double the rate of quarterbacks and wide receivers, attributable to high-velocity collision patterns. Position-specific training that strengthens the cervical musculature and refines contact mechanics — particularly for players who initiate or absorb high-velocity collisions — may reduce stinger incidence and recurrence over a career.
rtp: Return to play after a stinger requires complete resolution of symptoms including full painless cervical range of motion and full extremity strength. Concurrent concussion evaluation is mandated by NFL protocol when a stinger is diagnosed. Cervical spine injury must also be cleared before clearance. The 2018 NFL helmet-lowering rule did not appear to reduce stinger incidence, highlighting the continued need for RTP vigilance in high-contact positions.

citation: Knapik DM, Mack CD, Lee R, Heersink W, Tanenbaum JE, Wetzel RJ, Voos JE. Epidemiology of Tibial Fractures in Professional American Football Athletes From 2013 to 2019. Orthop J Sports Med. 2021;9(11):23259671211040878. doi:10.1177/23259671211040878
abstract: This retrospective case series reviewed the NFL injury database (ISS 2013-2014; EHR 2015-2019) to identify all tibial fractures over seven seasons. A total of 64 fractures in 60 athletes were identified. Median time lost was 74 days. Defensive secondary athletes had the highest proportion (16%), followed by running backs (14%). The majority (61%) occurred during regular-season games, predominantly in the final 4 weeks (weeks 13-17). Being tackled with a direct impact to the leg was the most common mechanism. Lateral tibial plateau fractures were most frequent; distal tibial fractures caused the greatest time loss (mean 175 days). Surgery was required in 48% of EHR-era injuries; surgical cases missed a median of 232 days versus 56 days for conservatively managed cases.
tldr: Over seven NFL seasons, tibial fractures were uncommon but highly consequential, causing a median of 74 days lost and up to nearly a full year for surgically managed cases. Defensive backs and running backs were most affected, with most fractures occurring during the final four weeks of the regular season via direct contact during tackling. This late-season clustering points to fatigue-related neuromuscular breakdown as a likely contributor, underscoring the value of load management and neuromuscular maintenance late in the season.
methods: Retrospective case series using the NFL Injury Surveillance System (ISS) for 2013-2014 and the centralized electronic health record (EHR) system for 2015-2019. All tibial fractures were identified; purely ligamentous injuries and bony contusions without definite fracture were excluded. Variables collected included athlete characteristics, injury timing, fracture type and characteristics, associated injuries, management (operative vs conservative), and days missed. Surgical procedures were identified via CPT codes reviewed by an IQVIA analytics team. Descriptive statistics were calculated with SPSS v23.
findings: 64 tibial fractures in 60 athletes; median days lost 74 (IQR 34-153). Defensive secondary (16%) and running backs (14%) had highest injury counts. 61% occurred during regular-season games; 51% of those in weeks 13-17. Most common activity: being tackled (27%); most common mechanism: direct impact (58%). Lateral tibial plateau fractures were most common (46%); distal tibial fractures caused greatest time loss (mean 175 days). 48% of EHR-era injuries required surgery; surgical cases lost median 232 days vs 56 days for conservative management. Open fractures occurred in 4%; intra-articular involvement in 43% of distal tibial fractures.
limitations: Retrospective design depends on completeness of NFL ISS and EHR documentation. Data quality improved after EHR launch, so 2013-2014 season data (ISS) were excluded from surgical and RTP analyses. Days missed was unavailable in 36% of injuries. Data on athletes who left the NFL after fracture were not collected. Overall incidence rates by position exposure were not calculated. Mechanism and activity data were entered by athletic trainers without systematic video validation. Imaging and operative reports were not available for review. Results are specific to professional football and may not generalize to other sports or competition levels.
practicalImplications: Medical staff should have high suspicion for concomitant ligamentous and meniscal injuries in athletes with lateral tibial plateau fractures given the 29% co-injury rate. For fractures amenable to intramedullary nailing, earlier return-to-sport can be anticipated versus those requiring ORIF. Late-season fatigue-related injury clustering suggests value in monitoring neuromuscular readiness in the final weeks of the season to reduce severe lower extremity injury risk.
athleteDev: Defensive secondary and running back athletes face the highest tibial fracture risk, reflecting the open-field tackling demands of these positions. Position-specific conditioning that emphasizes neuromuscular control, dynamic balance, and proper tackling mechanics — especially during the high-fatigue late-season period — may reduce fracture risk. Monitoring cumulative load and incorporating deload strategies in weeks 12-17 may be warranted.
rtp: Tibial fractures carry significant RTP timelines: median 74 days overall, 232 days for surgical cases. Intramedullary nailing has demonstrated 90% RTP rates with longer career longevity than ORIF. Distal tibial fractures with intra-articular extension and concomitant fibular fracture carry the longest RTP timeline. Staged functional rehabilitation with clearance criteria for full sport participation is critical given the high rate of surgical intervention in this population.

citation: Hoge C, Sabbagh R, Morgan M, Grawe BM. Epidemiology of Youth and High School American Football-Related Injuries Presenting to United States Emergency Departments: 2010-2019. Physician Sportsmed. 2021. doi:10.1080/00913847.2021.1931980
abstract: This cross-sectional retrospective study queried the NEISS database (2010-2019) to compare football-related ED injury characteristics and rates in youth (ages 6-12) and high school (ages 14-18) athletes. A total of 2,827,751 weighted national estimates were identified from 89,236 unique cases. High school athletes accounted for 59.87% of injuries. The overall ED injury risk was 16.17 per 1,000 participants/year for high school athletes versus 11.97 for youth. Both groups showed significant decreases in injury risk over the decade (p < 0.0001). Head/neck and hand/wrist injuries were most common in both groups. High school athletes were almost twice as likely to present with knee injuries and 2.37 times more likely with shoulder injuries. Youth athletes were 2.5 times more likely to sustain shoulder fractures while high school athletes had over 5 times the risk of dislocations.
tldr: Across a decade of national ED surveillance data, football injury risk for both youth and high school athletes significantly declined — suggesting that rule changes, equipment improvements, and coaching technique initiatives since 2010 are showing measurable impact. However, high school athletes carry a substantially higher overall injury burden, with nearly double the concussion risk and over 5 times the dislocation risk compared to youth players. Injury prevention efforts must be age-group-specific, targeting knee and shoulder injuries in high school athletes and fractures in youth players.
methods: Cross-sectional retrospective study querying the Consumer Product Safety Commission's NEISS database from 2010-2019 for football-related injuries (Product Code 1211) in ages 6-12 (youth) and 14-18 (high school). Age 13 was excluded to minimize overlap. National weighted estimates and 95% CIs were calculated using IBM SPSS v27. Chi-square with odds ratios assessed bivariate associations. Poisson regression evaluated trends in injury risk, participation counts, and weighted estimates over time. Injury risk was calculated as injuries per 1,000 participants/year using SFIA (youth) and NFHS (high school) participation data.
findings: 2,827,751 weighted national estimates of football ED injuries (2010-2019); high school athletes comprised 59.87%. Overall injury risk: 16.17/1,000/year (high school) vs 11.97/1,000/year (youth). Both groups showed significant annual decreases (5.27%/year youth; 4.92%/year high school; p < 0.0001). Head/neck and hand/wrist were the most common injury sites in both groups. High school athletes were ~2x more likely to present with knee injuries and 2.37x more likely with shoulder injuries per 1,000/year. Youth athletes were 2.5x more likely to sustain shoulder fractures; high school athletes were ~10x more likely to sustain shoulder dislocations. High school athletes had almost double concussion risk and over 5x the dislocation risk compared to youth per year.
limitations: NEISS only captures injuries presenting to EDs, underrepresenting total injury burden and emphasizing more severe injuries. The database codes only the most severe injury per encounter; multiple injuries in one visit are not captured. Participation data (SFIA/NFHS) account only for core or team participants, not recreational players, potentially exaggerating injury rates. NFHS participation data were unavailable for 2019-2020 due to COVID-19. The study is limited to ages 6-12 and 14-18, excluding age 13.
practicalImplications: The declining injury risk trend since 2010 provides support for continued investment in rule changes, improved equipment, and coaching education as effective public health interventions. However, the persistent age-related differences in injury patterns — particularly the high school athletes' elevated concussion and dislocation risk — indicate that prevention programming must be age-differentiated. High school programs should emphasize shoulder and knee stability, while youth programs should focus on bone health and fracture-risk awareness.
athleteDev: Youth football athletes are at higher relative risk for skeletal fractures (including shoulder fractures) due to open physes and immature neuromuscular control, while high school athletes have greater risk for ligamentous and soft tissue injuries such as dislocations and sprains consistent with skeletally maturing bodies. Developmental programming should account for these physiological differences, incorporating age-appropriate contact progressions, movement skill development, and load management.
rtp: This study does not directly address RTP timelines, but the high rates of concussion, knee, and shoulder injuries in high school football players — conditions associated with significant recovery periods — underscore the importance of structured RTP protocols at the youth and scholastic levels. Concussion rates were nearly double in high school compared to youth athletes, making standardized concussion assessment and graduated RTP protocols especially critical at the high school level.

citation: Binney ZO, Bachynski KE. Estimating the prevalence at death of CTE neuropathology among professional football players. Neurology. 2019;92:43-45. doi:10.1212/WNL.0000000000006699
abstract: This brief methodological analysis used bias analysis to estimate the plausible range of CTE prevalence at death among former NFL players, contextualizing the widely-reported 99% finding from a 2017 JAMA brain bank study (Mez et al.). The authors obtained a fully enumerated cohort of 1,142 NFL players who died between February 2008 and May 2016 from Pro-Football-Reference.com. By modeling the selection bias inherent in voluntary brain donation — where symptomatic players are more likely to donate — the estimated true CTE prevalence ranges from 9.6% to near 100% depending on the assumed donation probability among those with CTE. At a 50% donation probability, prevalence is estimated at 19.3%; at 90%, it is 10.7%. CTE prevalence remains high under most plausible scenarios.
tldr: The frequently cited '99% of NFL players have CTE' statistic from the largest brain bank study is heavily influenced by selection bias, as symptomatic players are far more likely to donate their brains.
