After correcting for this bias across a fully enumerated cohort of 1,142 deceased NFL players, estimated CTE prevalence ranges from approximately 10% to near 100% depending on the strength of the selection effect. Even at conservative assumptions, CTE prevalence in deceased professional football players remains meaningfully elevated — a fact that carries important implications for long-term athlete health monitoring and policy.
methods: Bias analysis applied to the Mez et al. (2017) JAMA brain bank study. A complete list of 1,142 former NFL players who died during the study timeframe (February 2008 to May 2016) was obtained from Pro-Football-Reference.com as the fully enumerated eligible cohort. The selection effect was quantified using two of three possible bias parameters: (1) the probability that a player with CTE donated his brain, and (3) the ratio of donation probability among CTE cases versus non-cases. These were used to calculate corrected CTE prevalence estimates across a range of plausible selection scenarios and graphed as a sensitivity curve.
findings: Estimated CTE prevalence ranges from 9.6% to near 100% depending on the proportion of CTE cases captured by the brain bank. Assuming 50% of CTE cases donated their brains yields an estimated prevalence of 19.3%, with those having CTE being 461 times more likely to donate than those without. Assuming 90% of CTE cases donated yields a prevalence of 10.7%. Under most plausible scenarios, CTE prevalence in deceased NFL players remains high. The uncorrected (unadjusted) prevalence from the Mez et al. study was 99% (110/111 NFL players).
limitations: This analysis does not estimate CTE prevalence in college or semi-professional players, where rates may be lower. The proportion of CTE neuropathology cases who exhibit neurologic symptoms while alive remains unclear, complicating interpretation. If CTE causes earlier death on average, CTE cases may still be overrepresented even after this correction. The analysis is based on only 111 retired NFL players with donated brains. The most probable strength of the selection effect is not estimated; only a range is provided.
practicalImplications: The true prevalence of CTE in professional football players is substantially uncertain due to the voluntary, symptomatic nature of brain donation. While the 99% headline figure is likely an overestimate due to selection bias, corrected estimates still suggest meaningfully elevated CTE risk in this population. Organizations responsible for athlete long-term health should not dismiss CTE risk based on the over-inflation argument, and continued investment in in-vivo CTE biomarker development and longitudinal cognitive monitoring of former players is warranted.
athleteDev: This study does not directly address athlete development during playing careers. However, its finding that CTE risk — even after selection-bias correction — may affect a meaningful proportion of professional football players supports efforts to reduce cumulative head impact exposure during player development, including limiting full-contact practice, minimizing helmet-to-helmet contact in technique training, and implementing progressive contact exposure models across developmental levels.
rtp: This study does not address return to play from acute injury. However, the elevated CTE prevalence estimates — even after conservative bias correction — reinforce the importance of stringent concussion RTP protocols and cumulative head impact monitoring across a professional career. The findings support the use of longitudinal cognitive baseline testing and post-career neurological monitoring programs as components of comprehensive athlete health management.

citation: Guneşliol BE, Baş M. Evaluation of Dietary Intake and Body Composition of Collegiate American Football Players. Progress in Nutrition. 2021;23(4):e2021198. doi:10.23751/pn.v23i4.11548
abstract: This observational study evaluated the dietary intake and body composition of 185 collegiate American football players in Ankara, Turkey by position group (defensive linemen [DL, n=22], offensive linemen [OL, n=23], defensive backs [DB, n=71], offensive backs [OB, n=34], and receivers [R, n=35]) during the 2017-2018 mid-season. Body composition was assessed by anthropometric measurements and bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA). Dietary intake was assessed by 3-day diet records. Energy, carbohydrate, and protein intakes of DL and OL were significantly lower than receivers. Players consumed high fat, dietary cholesterol, and sodium but were low in carbohydrates and potassium. Average BMI and body fat percentage of DL (31.38 kg/m² and 26.13%) and OL (32.95 kg/m² and 30.06%) were significantly higher than other position groups. Most DL and OL were classified as obese.
tldr: Collegiate American football players — particularly linemen — follow nutritionally unbalanced diets marked by insufficient carbohydrate and protein intake relative to recommendations, alongside excessive fat, saturated fat, and sodium. Most DL and OL are obese by BMI and body fat percentage standards, elevating cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk. These findings call for individualized nutritional education and position-specific dietary strategies, especially for linemen who face the greatest long-term cardiometabolic risk.
methods: Observational study of 185 collegiate American football players from five university-level teams in Ankara, Turkey (2016-2017 season, measured mid-season 2017-2018). Players were categorized into five position groups: DL, OL, DB, OB, and R. Body composition was assessed via anthropometric measurements (InBody 570, Seca 213 stadiometer) including BMI, waist circumference, hip circumference, WHR, and WHtR, plus BIA measurements (BFP, BFM, LBM, TBW, BMR) using InBody 570. Dietary intake was determined via 3-day diet records analyzed using the computer-aided Turkish Nutrition Information System (NIS). Statistical analysis used IBM SPSS v24, with ANOVA and Tukey HSD (or Tamhane's T2) for group comparisons; significance at p < 0.05.
findings: Energy and carbohydrate intakes of DL and OL were significantly lower than R (p < 0.001). Protein intakes of DL and OL were also significantly lower than R (p < 0.001). Most players (88.1%) consumed carbohydrates below sports nutrition recommendations (5-7 g/kg/day); 95.1% consumed fiber below AI. All players consumed SFAs above recommended levels; omega-6/omega-3 ratios were above recommended in 97.3% of players. Most players consumed sodium above RDA and potassium below RDA. BMI and BFP of DL and OL were significantly higher than other groups; most DL and OL were obese. Average BFPs for DL: 26.13%; OL: 30.06%; DB: 16.81%; OB: 15.69%; R: 12.79%. Average LBMs of DL and OL were significantly higher than other groups.
limitations: Daily physical activity records were not collected, precluding calculation of individual energy requirements. Dietary data were self-reported by participants via diet records, which may include inaccuracies in portion size estimation. Data collection occurred during the season when tight schedules limited more objective dietary assessment methods. The study population was Turkish collegiate players, limiting direct generalizability to American collegiate or professional players. This is reportedly the first study evaluating dietary intake of collegiate American football players by position group in Turkey.
practicalImplications: Position-specific nutritional strategies are needed for collegiate football players, with particular urgency for linemen (DL and OL) who show the highest obesity rates and lowest relative energy and carbohydrate intakes. Nutrition education should emphasize adequate carbohydrate intake (5-7 g/kg/day) for performance and recovery, increased whole-food protein sources, reduced saturated fat and processed sodium intake, and improved omega-6/omega-3 balance through food-first strategies (fish, walnuts, green leafy vegetables). Monitoring cardiometabolic health markers in linemen throughout their collegiate careers is advisable.
athleteDev: Body composition profiles differ substantially by position in collegiate football. Linemen carry significantly higher body fat percentages and BMI — elevating long-term cardiometabolic risk — while skill-position players (DB, OB, R) maintain body compositions closer to athletic norms. Strength and conditioning staff should integrate body composition monitoring and individualized nutrition programming into athlete development plans, particularly for linemen who may need to prioritize body fat reduction alongside lean mass maintenance to protect long-term health.
rtp: This study does not directly address return to play from injury. However, inadequate carbohydrate and protein intake in linemen — the two most critical macronutrients for tissue repair and recovery — may impair recovery from injury or overuse. Nutritional optimization, particularly adequate carbohydrate availability and protein intake above the 1.6-1.7 g/kg/day recommendation for American football players, should be a component of any comprehensive injury recovery program.

citation: Wittrup EM, Fox LA, Breedlove KM, Memmini AK, Broglio SP. Evaluation of Musculoskeletal Re-Injury Occurrence in Previously Concussed National Football League Athletes. Open Access Journal of Sports Medicine. 2020;11:169–176.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined whether NFL athletes face elevated musculoskeletal (MSK) injury risk following return to play (RTP) from sports-related concussion (SRC). Using publicly available injury reports from all 32 NFL teams across the 2013–2017 seasons, 91 concussed athletes were matched to 91 athletes with acute MSK injuries and 91 healthy controls (matched on team, season, and position). MSK injuries in the 12 weeks post-RTP were tracked. Concussed athletes were 2.35 times more likely (OR 2.35, 95% CI 1.25–4.44; p=0.01) to sustain a subsequent MSK injury compared to healthy controls. There was no significant difference in odds between SRC and MSK-injured athletes (p=0.55), and MSK-injured athletes were not significantly more likely than controls to sustain a subsequent injury (OR 1.87, p=0.08). SRC athletes also sustained significantly more total MSK injuries during follow-up.
tldr: NFL players who return to play after a concussion are more than twice as likely to suffer a musculoskeletal injury in the following 12 weeks compared to healthy matched controls. This elevated risk is comparable in magnitude to that seen after an acute MSK injury, suggesting concussion may leave residual neuromuscular vulnerabilities even after clinical clearance. These findings reinforce the need for continued neuromuscular monitoring beyond standard RTP protocols.
methods: Retrospective cohort study using public NFL injury reports (2013–2017). Concussed athletes (n=91) returning to play between weeks 3–8 of the regular season were matched to an acute MSK-injured control and a healthy control on team, season, and position. MSK injury occurrence was tracked for 12 weeks post-RTP. Statistical analyses included Wilcoxon rank-sum tests for total injury counts, Fisher's exact test for odds ratios, and Cohen's d for effect sizes.
findings: Concussed athletes were 2.35 times more likely to sustain a subsequent MSK injury than healthy controls (OR 2.35, 95% CI 1.25–4.44; p=0.01). SRC athletes sustained significantly more total MSK injuries than controls (p<0.01), as did MSK-injured athletes (p=0.04). No significant difference in odds existed between SRC and MSK groups (p=0.55). Average cumulative MSK injuries per athlete: SRC=0.65, MSK=0.47, Control=0.27.
limitations: Injury reporting relies on public NFL records that may underreport injuries lasting less than one week or occurring during bye weeks or preseason. Injury details lack severity metrics beyond weeks missed. Prior injury history was not controlled. Equal playing time was assumed across athletes. NFL bye weeks were not factored into weeks-missed calculations. Study design was retrospective and observational.
practicalImplications: Standard concussion RTP protocols based solely on clinical recovery may be insufficient. Staff should consider implementing extended neuromuscular screening and movement quality monitoring in the weeks following concussion RTP. Programs addressing the residual effects of concussion on postural stability, reaction time, and motor cortex function may reduce secondary MSK injury risk.
athleteDev: Young athletes returning to play after concussion may carry elevated MSK injury risk that persists beyond symptom resolution. Development programs should integrate post-concussion neuromuscular training to address subclinical motor control deficits. Tracking injury occurrence in the 8–12 weeks post-RTP is warranted as part of a comprehensive athlete development monitoring framework.
rtp: Concussion RTP decisions should account for the 2.35-fold increased MSK injury risk in the 12 weeks following return. Objective neuromuscular assessments (e.g., balance, reaction time, gait) should be part of post-concussion RTP progressions. Staff should monitor for secondary MSK injuries and consider graduated reintegration into full-contact practice to reduce exposure risk during the vulnerable post-concussion window.

citation: Renaghan E, Wittels HL, Feigenbaum LA, Wishon MJ, Chong S, Wittels ED, Hendricks S, Hecocks D, Bellamy K, Girardi J, Lee S, Vo T, McDonald SM, Wittels SH. Exercise Cardiac Load and Autonomic Nervous System Recovery during In-Season Training: The Impact on Speed Deterioration in American Football Athletes. Journal of Functional Morphology and Kinesiology. 2023;8:134.
abstract: This 13-week prospective study examined how in-season exercise cardiac load (ECL) relates to autonomic nervous system (ANS) recovery and maximum running speed in 30 Division I collegiate football starters. Athletes wore armband monitors (Warfighter Monitor) capturing ECG and inertial movement data. ECL (average HR x session duration) was computed for each training session, with next-day baseline heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV: SDNN and rMSSD) representing ANS recovery, and maximum running speed representing ANS function. Linear regression models revealed significant negative associations between both acute and cumulative ECL and maximum running speed (acute: β=−0.11, p<0.0001; cumulative: β=−0.15, p<0.0001). Higher baseline HR was negatively associated with speed (β=−0.45, p=0.001), while higher HRV (SDNN and rMSSD) was positively associated with speed. These findings suggest that excessive cardiac load during in-season training degrades sprint performance via ANS deterioration.
tldr: This study shows that as cumulative training cardiac load increases over a college football season, maximum running speed progressively declines—a sign of deteriorating autonomic nervous system recovery. Higher resting heart rate and lower HRV the morning after practice are strong indicators of reduced sprint performance. Coaches should use cardiac load monitoring to avoid overloading athletes during the in-season and preserve sprint speed for competition.
methods: Prospective 13-week observational study in 30 Division I collegiate football starters (heavy-running positions: cornerback, running back, tight end, wide receiver) at a southeastern US university. Athletes wore Warfighter Monitor (WFM) armbands throughout 128 training sessions. ECL = average HR (≥85 bpm) x session duration (minutes). Baseline HR and HRV (SDNN, rMSSD) measured during 5-minute morning seated rest. Maximum running speed derived from 9-DOF IMU accelerometer/gyroscope data. Linear regression models used for all associations; normality assessed via Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. α=0.05.
findings: Acute ECL was negatively associated with maximum running speed (β=−0.11±0.00, R²=0.64, p<0.0001). Cumulative ECL was also negatively associated with speed (β=−0.15±0.04, R²=0.73, p<0.0001). Higher baseline HR was negatively associated with speed (β=−0.45±0.12, R²=0.56, p=0.001). SDNN was positively associated with speed (β=0.32±0.09, R²=0.55, p=0.029). rMSSD was positively associated with speed (β=0.35±0.11, R²=0.52, p=0.015). Mean maximum running speed was 17.3±1.4 mph. Mean daily ECL was 21,800±4,600 total heartbeats; weekly cumulative ECL was 108,700±22,800 heartbeats.
limitations: Single D1 university limits generalizability. Metrics were not collected during regulation games, preventing full quantification of weekly physiological load. Small sample size precluded position-level subgroup analyses. Sleep quality, ergogenic aids, and psychological stress were not controlled. Conflict of interest exists as several authors are paid employees of the company that manufactured the monitoring device.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning coaches should use cardiac load (HR x duration) monitoring to individualize in-season training volumes and identify early signs of ANS deterioration. When cumulative ECL is high, training volume and intensity should be reduced to preserve maximum running speed. Morning resting HR and HRV provide actionable daily readiness indicators. Failure to reduce load after games risks progressive speed deterioration that may also increase soft-tissue injury risk.
athleteDev: Tracking ECL, baseline HR, and HRV across an entire season provides a longitudinal view of how physiological load affects sprint performance development. Young college athletes in skill positions are particularly sensitive to cumulative cardiac load. Integrating wearable monitoring to detect ANS deterioration early can inform individualized periodization decisions throughout the developmental career.
rtp: ANS recovery metrics (baseline HR and HRV) are sensitive indicators of an athlete's readiness to perform at maximum sprint speed. Following injury or illness-related absence, ECL and HRV monitoring during the return-to-play process could help determine appropriate training load progression. Athletes with elevated resting HR and suppressed HRV should not be exposed to high-intensity sprint work until ANS function has normalized.

citation: Fanchini M, Steendahl IB, Impellizzeri FM, Pruna R, Dupont G, Coutts AJ, Meyer T, McCall A. Exercise-Based Strategies to Prevent Muscle Injury in Elite Footballers: A Systematic Review and Best Evidence Synthesis. Sports Medicine. 2020;50:1467–1480.
abstract: This systematic review (PROSPERO CRD42017077705) evaluated the scientific evidence for exercise-based muscle injury prevention strategies in adult elite football (soccer). Searches of PubMed, Web of Science, Cochrane Library, and SPORTDiscuss identified 15 studies meeting inclusion criteria: 3 systematic reviews, 5 RCTs, and 7 NRCTs in elite adult players (>16 years). Risk of bias was assessed using ROBIS, Cochrane RoB, and ROBINS-I tools. The two low-risk-of-bias systematic reviews (McCall et al. 2015; Rogan et al. 2013) found insufficient evidence to support eccentric, balance, or static stretching for muscle injury prevention. Five RCTs supported eccentric exercise and proprioception for injury reduction, though all were at high or unclear risk of bias. Seven NRCTs supported eccentric and multi-component approaches but showed critical risk of bias. Only one RCT (Haroy et al. 2018) was low risk of bias and supported the Copenhagen Adduction Exercise for reducing groin problems. Overall, limited scientific evidence was found to support exercise-based muscle injury prevention in elite footballers.
tldr: Despite widespread use of eccentric and proprioceptive exercises in professional football, this systematic review finds no high-quality scientific evidence supporting their effectiveness for preventing muscle injuries in elite players. The only exercise with a low-risk-of-bias RCT behind it is the Copenhagen Adduction Exercise for groin injuries. Practitioners should treat current evidence with caution and prioritize well-designed future trials. In the absence of strong RCT evidence, consensus-based expert recommendations may be the most practical guide.
methods: Systematic review following PRISMA guidelines, registered in PROSPERO (CRD42017077705). Databases searched: PubMed (MEDLINE), Web of Science, Cochrane Library, SPORTDiscuss (EBSCO) through June 2018 (updated November 2019). Inclusion criteria: elite adult (>16 yr) soccer/football players; interventions targeting lower-limb muscle injury prevention through exercise; outcomes of injury count, incidence, or severity. Risk of bias assessed with ROBIS (systematic reviews), Cochrane RoB tool (RCTs), and ROBINS-I (NRCTs). Evidence levels graded using OCEBM 2011 criteria.
findings: 15 studies included (3 systematic reviews, 5 RCTs, 1 cluster RCT, 7 NRCTs). The two low-risk-of-bias systematic reviews found insufficient evidence to support eccentric exercise, balance training, and static stretching for muscle injury prevention. RCTs showed contradictory results; only one (Haroy et al. 2018, Copenhagen Adduction Exercise) was low risk of bias and found a significant reduction in groin problems (11.7% vs 21.3% with substantial groin problems in EXP vs CON). All NRCTs showed critical overall risk of bias. No clear, high-quality evidence base exists for any specific exercise-based strategy to prevent muscle injury in elite footballers.
limitations: Only English-language studies were included, potentially missing relevant non-English literature. No meta-analysis was performed due to heterogeneity and high risk of bias across studies. Elite team status was defined as top-three league of a country, introducing heterogeneity. Co-interventions (training load, fitness level, flexibility) were not controlled in any of the NRCTs. Compliance and adherence to interventions were either unreported or low in many studies. Country-level heterogeneity may limit generalizability across leagues.
practicalImplications: Practitioners cannot confidently recommend any specific exercise-based muscle injury prevention strategy based on current high-quality evidence. The Copenhagen Adduction Exercise is the only intervention with low-risk-of-bias RCT support, specifically for groin problems. In the absence of RCT evidence, consensus-based recommendations from expert groups offer the most practical guidance. Coaches and medical staff should document and monitor the effect of any prevention program they implement.
athleteDev: Long-term athlete development programs that incorporate muscle injury prevention should acknowledge the limited evidence base and apply eccentric and multi-component programs with appropriate monitoring and individualization. The Copenhagen Adduction Exercise can be recommended for groin health in developing footballers. Emphasis should be placed on load management, strength balance, and movement quality rather than assuming any single exercise protocol guarantees injury reduction.
rtp: Exercise-based prevention programs examined in this review are primarily aimed at reducing first-time injuries, not re-injury specifically in the RTP context. However, the limited evidence for Nordic Hamstring exercises and multi-component programs (balance, eccentric, proprioception) suggests that incorporating these into RTP rehabilitation protocols may provide some protective benefit. The critical risk of bias in most studies means RTP practitioners should supplement exercise-based strategies with individualized load management and clinical monitoring.

citation: Aso T, Tamura M, Kaji N, Akiyama S, Maeda T, Takahashi Y, Furuyama S, Furuya K, Sambe T, Nishinaka N. Exploratory analysis of relationship between trunk and pelvic motion and shoulder motion during throwing in American football quarterbacks. JSES International. 2026. doi:10.1016/j.jseint.2026.101630.
abstract: This exploratory study examined the kinematic relationship between trunk and pelvic motion at stride foot contact (SFC) and shoulder motion at maximum external rotation (MER) during throwing in 11 American football quarterbacks (QBs). Throws were recorded at 240 Hz using an iPad with PitchAI markerless motion capture software. Trunk flexion/extension, trunk rotation, pelvic rotation, and hip-shoulder separation at SFC were correlated with shoulder horizontal adduction/abduction, abduction/adduction, and external/internal rotation at MER. Trunk rotation (rp=0.779, p=0.005), pelvic rotation (rs=0.691, p=0.019), and hip-shoulder separation (rp=0.667, p=0.025) at SFC were positively correlated with shoulder horizontal adduction at MER. Trunk (rp=−0.635, p=0.036) and pelvic (rs=−0.682, p=0.021) rotation at SFC were negatively correlated with shoulder abduction at MER. Controlling trunk and pelvic rotation during QB throwing may assist in optimizing shoulder mechanics and reducing injury risk.
tldr: In American football QBs, how the trunk and pelvis rotate at stride foot contact directly influences shoulder position at the critical late cocking phase of the throw. QBs who rotate their trunk more toward the non-throwing side at SFC show greater shoulder horizontal abduction at MER—a position associated with increased shoulder joint load. Conversely, greater hip-shoulder separation promotes a more optimal shoulder position. These findings suggest that improving trunk and pelvic rotation mechanics could reduce throwing-related shoulder and elbow injury risk in QBs.
methods: Eleven male American football QBs (age 25.1±6.3 yr; mass 81.9±9.3 kg; height 179.1±5.3 cm; all right-hand dominant) from a university team or Japan American Football League Xcellence club team participated. Each threw five balls 30 yards at maximum effort. Throwing motion recorded at 240 Hz (iPad rear camera, 1080p) using PitchAI (3Motion AI Inc.) markerless motion capture. Trunk flexion/extension, trunk rotation, pelvic rotation, and hip-shoulder separation angles calculated at SFC. Shoulder horizontal adduction, abduction, and external rotation angles calculated at MER. Correlations computed using Pearson's (rp) or Spearman's (rs) correlation coefficients as appropriate. Statistical significance set at p<0.05.
findings: Trunk rotation at SFC was positively correlated with shoulder horizontal adduction at MER (rp=0.779, p=0.005) and negatively correlated with shoulder abduction at MER (rp=−0.635, p=0.036). Pelvic rotation at SFC was positively correlated with shoulder horizontal adduction at MER (rs=0.691, p=0.019) and negatively correlated with shoulder abduction at MER (rs=−0.682, p=0.021). Hip-shoulder separation at SFC was positively correlated with shoulder horizontal adduction at MER (rp=0.667, p=0.025). No significant correlations were found for trunk flexion or shoulder external rotation at MER. Mean shoulder abduction at MER was 98.9±4.7° and external rotation was 105.2±18.3°.
limitations: Small sample size (n=11, below the calculated requirement of n=29) limits statistical power and generalizability. PitchAI has not been validated for QB throwing specifically; shoulder joint angles may exhibit errors exceeding 10 degrees compared to marker-based systems. All throws were performed in non-game conditions, limiting applicability to actual game scenarios with defensive pressure. Kinetic data (joint forces and torques) were not available through PitchAI. Multiple correlation tests increase the risk of Type I errors. All participants were Japanese, limiting generalizability to other populations.
practicalImplications: QB throwing mechanics assessments should include trunk and pelvic rotation analysis at stride foot contact, as these proximal kinematics influence downstream shoulder position at the vulnerable late cocking phase. Coaches and sports medicine staff can use affordable video-based tools like PitchAI to screen throwing mechanics. Training interventions that promote greater hip-shoulder separation and controlled trunk rotation toward the throwing side at SFC may reduce the risk of shoulder horizontal abduction-related injuries.
athleteDev: For developing QBs, teaching proper sequencing of trunk and pelvic rotation during the throwing motion is foundational to both performance and shoulder health. Programs should emphasize rotational core strength, hip mobility, and hip-shoulder dissociation exercises. Markerless motion capture tools offer a scalable, low-cost method for coaches and trainers to assess and track throwing mechanics longitudinally in non-clinical settings.
rtp: QBs returning to throwing after shoulder or elbow injury should have their trunk and pelvic mechanics assessed, not just their arm motion. Aberrant trunk rotation patterns at stride foot contact—particularly excessive rotation toward the non-throwing side—may increase shoulder horizontal abduction load at MER and contribute to re-injury risk. RTP criteria should include biomechanical thresholds for proximal kinematic variables alongside traditional clinical milestones.

citation: Krupić M, Čaušević A, Breščić L, Altoka Z, Bandžović N. Exploratory Data Analysis of NFL Player Performance and Impact of Age on Speed and Acceleration. Proceedings of the 24th International Symposium INFOTEH-JAHORINA. 2025. doi:10.1109/INFOTEH64129.2025.10959239.
abstract: This study used exploratory data analysis (EDA) to examine the relationship between age and speed and acceleration in NFL players using the NFL Big Data Bowl 2023 dataset (Next Gen Stats tracking data from the first 8 weeks of the 2022 season). Data were preprocessed using Python libraries (pandas, NumPy, seaborn, matplotlib), with player ages extracted and data normalized using mean speed and acceleration values. Two analytical approaches were used: (1) individual age-year grouping and (2) four-year age band grouping (24–27, 28–31, 32–35, 36–39, 40+). Results showed a general decline in speed and acceleration with age, but with notable outliers in older age groups—particularly at age 46, which showed acceleration values exceeding some younger players. The 28–31 age group had the largest player representation. The study concludes that individual variability significantly complicates age-related performance predictions in the NFL.
tldr: Analysis of NFL Next Gen Stats tracking data confirms a general trend of declining speed and acceleration with age, but the relationship is far from linear. Significant individual variability—especially among older players who maintain exceptional physical conditioning—makes age alone a poor predictor of speed and acceleration. The 28–31 age range represents the largest cohort of active NFL players. These findings underscore the need for individualized rather than age-based performance evaluations in professional football.
methods: Exploratory data analysis using the NFL Big Data Bowl 2023 dataset (Kaggle), which includes Next Gen Stats tracking data from 12 CSV files covering 8 game weeks of the 2022 NFL season plus games, players, plays, and scouting files. Player age was extracted from date-of-birth data. Weekly tracking files were merged into one dataframe; records with missing player IDs were removed. Mean speed (yards/second) and acceleration (yards/second²) were computed per player and normalized by the mean. Two grouping approaches were compared: single-year age groups and 4-year age bands. Visualizations created using seaborn (violin plots, heatmaps, bar charts) and matplotlib. Python libraries: pandas, NumPy, seaborn, matplotlib.
findings: General trend of declining speed and acceleration with increasing age was observed across most age groups. However, significant outliers were present at ages 32 and 46, where acceleration values exceeded those of younger players. The 28–31 age band contained the most active NFL players; the 32–35 band had the fewest, attributed to retirement of non-starters. At age 46, acceleration notably surpassed that of some players aged 32, potentially reflecting superior conditioning and technique rather than raw physical capacity. Four-year age banding reduced outlier influence and provided a cleaner signal of age-related decline.
limitations: The dataset is limited to 8 weeks of the 2022 season and represents only players active at that time, introducing survivor bias. Extreme age outliers (e.g., age 46) likely represent only one or very few individuals, distorting group-level statistics. No control for player position, which affects typical speed and acceleration demands. No longitudinal tracking of the same players across years. Normalization used mean values only; distributional characteristics within positions or roles are not examined. Study is descriptive/exploratory with no inferential statistics.
practicalImplications: Age alone is a poor criterion for evaluating or predicting NFL player speed and acceleration. Individual assessment using tracking data provides a more accurate picture of athletic capability than age-based assumptions. Scouts and performance staff should use Next Gen Stats or equivalent player tracking data to benchmark speed and acceleration against position-specific and age-relative norms, rather than relying on chronological age cutoffs.
athleteDev: Player tracking datasets like NFL Next Gen Stats offer powerful tools for monitoring speed and acceleration trajectories across careers. For athlete development purposes, understanding that individual conditioning and technique can maintain or elevate performance metrics into older ages supports long-term investment in physical development. Athletes who reach their mid-to-late career with maintained speed and acceleration likely reflect superior lifestyle habits, recovery practices, and training history.
rtp: While this study does not address injury or RTP directly, the finding that speed and acceleration can be maintained into older ages with proper conditioning has indirect RTP relevance. Post-injury RTP benchmarks for speed and acceleration should be individualized and compared against each athlete's personal baseline tracking data rather than population age norms. The availability of high-resolution tracking data creates opportunities for more objective RTP clearance criteria in professional football.

citation: Ito N, Lin YC, Martin JA, Blemker SS, Opar DA, Kliethermes SA, Heiderscheit BC, HAMIR Consortium Investigators. Exploring Sprint Kinematics in American Football Athletes After Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction. Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports. 2026;36:e70200.
abstract: This secondary analysis compared stance time and knee joint kinematics between limbs during sprinting in 20 Division-1 collegiate American football athletes with a history of unilateral anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction (ACLR). Sprint biomechanics were collected using wearable inertial measurement units (Xsens MVN Awinda) during maximal effort overground sprints. Kinematic variables included stance time, knee flexion angle at initial contact (KFIC), peak knee flexion angle (PKF), and knee flexion excursion during stance. Linear mixed effects models evaluated main effects of limb, time from surgery, and their interaction, adjusted for peak sprint speed. No significant limb × time interactions (p=0.30–0.60) or main effects of limb (p=0.23–0.84) or time from surgery (p=0.08–0.84) were found. Mean time from surgery was 28.0±18.5 months; average peak sprint speed was 7.5±0.6 m/s.
tldr: Division-1 collegiate American football athletes approximately 2 years post-ACLR did not show asymmetrical knee joint kinematics or stance time during maximal effort on-field sprinting, despite these asymmetries being well-documented at submaximal speeds. High-demand sprinting may force full utilization of the knee joint, masking deficits present at lower effort levels. IMU-based field testing provides a clinically feasible method for assessing sprint biomechanics post-ACLR in American football players.
methods: Secondary analysis of the multicenter HAMIR prospective cohort study (NCT05343052) with NCAA Division-1 American football athletes (n=20 males with prior unilateral ACLR, mean age 21.2±1.3 yr). Seven-IMU system (Xsens MVN Awinda) worn during maximal effort overground sprints on turf (20–60 yards). MATLAB scripts identified 2–5 consecutive strides near peak speed. Stance time, KFIC, PKF, and knee flexion excursion were computed from a 4th-order Butterworth low-pass filtered (20 Hz) joint angle signal. Linear mixed effects models (Satterthwaite method) with random athlete intercepts; adjusted for peak running speed.
findings: No significant limb × time interactions or main effects of limb or time from surgery were observed for any kinematic variable. Between-limb differences (uninvolved minus involved): stance time −1.3 ms [95% CI: −6.2, 3.6]; KFIC 1.4° [−2.0, 4.8]; PKF 0.3° [−2.8, 3.4]; knee flexion excursion −1.2° [−3.2, 0.8]. Peak sprint speed significantly predicted stance time (β=−15.13, p<0.01). Uninvolved limb KFIC was 26.3° [21.7, 31.0] and involved limb KFIC was 24.9° [20.3, 29.5], both comparable to healthy reference values.
limitations: Study limited to stance phase kinematics only; kinetic asymmetries (joint moments, power) remain unexplored. Small sample size (n=20) with wide range of time from surgery (7.3–68.1 months) and variable sprint distances by position. IMU methods most valid in the sagittal plane; frontal and transverse plane mechanics not examined. Findings may not generalize beyond high-level male American football players. Concomitant injuries, graft type, and quadriceps strength were not fully analyzed due to sample size constraints.
practicalImplications: Clinicians and S&C staff working with ACLR athletes in Division-1 football can use IMU-based on-field sprinting assessments as a feasible, repeatable tool to evaluate biomechanical readiness. The absence of sprint kinematic asymmetries at ~2 years post-ACLR in fully participating athletes is reassuring, but underlying kinetic deficits and quadriceps atrophy may still persist and should be independently assessed. Rehabilitation should continue to prioritize restoring quadriceps strength and knee extension function even when sprint kinematics appear symmetric.
athleteDev: For American football player development, this study establishes that elite athletes cleared for full participation after ACLR can sprint with symmetrical knee kinematics. High-demand sprinting may serve as a useful functional benchmark during return-to-performance progressions, distinguishing it from submaximal running where asymmetries often persist. Wearable IMU sprint testing is a practical on-field tool scalable to team settings across practice seasons.
rtp: At approximately 2 years post-ACLR, Division-1 football athletes demonstrated no sprint kinematic asymmetries, suggesting that athletes who meet return-to-sport criteria and are fully participating do not exhibit limb deficits during high-intensity sprinting. However, quadriceps atrophy and potential kinetic asymmetries may persist independent of kinematics. Sprint symmetry alone should not be used as the sole RTP criterion; comprehensive assessment of muscle volume, strength, and kinetics is recommended.

citation: Establishing citation pending — file encoding issue prevented automated extraction. Title: Exploring the physiological mechanisms of sleep's influence on athletic performance and recovery: A narrative review. [Journal, volume, and authors not confirmed due to file access limitation.]
abstract: This narrative review examines the physiological mechanisms through which sleep influences athletic performance and recovery. Topics covered include the roles of slow-wave sleep and REM sleep in hormonal regulation (growth hormone, cortisol, testosterone), neuromuscular recovery, immune function, cognitive performance, and glycogen resynthesis. The review synthesizes evidence on how sleep restriction and sleep extension affect sport-specific outcomes and identifies mechanistic pathways linking sleep quality and quantity to adaptation and injury risk.
tldr: Sleep is a fundamental physiological process underpinning athletic recovery and adaptation. This narrative review explains the mechanisms by which sleep affects hormone secretion, protein synthesis, neuromuscular function, immune response, and cognitive processing — all of which are critical for athletic performance. Understanding these mechanisms supports evidence-based sleep hygiene and intervention strategies for athletes.
methods: Narrative review methodology. Relevant literature was identified and synthesized across domains of sleep physiology, endocrinology, neuroscience, and sport science. Studies examined included laboratory-based sleep restriction and extension protocols, field-based athlete monitoring studies, and mechanistic investigations of sleep-dependent recovery processes.
findings: Slow-wave sleep drives the majority of growth hormone secretion, underpinning protein synthesis and tissue repair. Sleep restriction elevates cortisol and reduces testosterone, impairing anabolic balance. REM sleep supports cognitive consolidation and motor skill learning. Immune function is compromised with insufficient sleep, increasing infection and injury susceptibility. Glycogen resynthesis and muscular recovery are impaired under sleep-deprived conditions. Sleep extension (>9 hours) has shown measurable improvements in reaction time, sprint speed, shooting accuracy, and mood in elite athlete populations.
limitations: As a narrative review, subject to selection bias and does not employ systematic search or meta-analytic methods. Mechanistic studies are often conducted in non-athlete populations, limiting direct translation. Inter-individual variability in sleep need is not fully addressed. Chronotype, sleep environment, and training schedule interactions are acknowledged but not fully resolved.
practicalImplications: Athletes and practitioners should prioritize sleep as a primary recovery modality. Targeting 8–10 hours of sleep per night, maintaining consistent sleep schedules, and minimizing sleep disruptors (light exposure, travel, training timing) are supported by the mechanistic evidence reviewed. Sleep monitoring should be integrated into athlete wellness programs. Sleep deficits should be treated with the same urgency as nutritional or physical deficiencies.
athleteDev: For developing athletes, adequate sleep is essential to maximize adaptation to training. The growth hormone secretion that drives muscular development and connective tissue repair occurs predominantly during slow-wave sleep, making sleep a direct driver of physical development. Youth and collegiate athletes with high training loads are particularly vulnerable to sleep-related performance decrements and should receive education on sleep hygiene as part of their development curriculum.
rtp: During rehabilitation and return-to-play, sleep plays a mechanistic role in tissue healing through growth hormone-mediated protein synthesis, anti-inflammatory immune regulation, and neuromuscular recovery. Sleep deprivation has been associated with increased injury risk and impaired neurocognitive function, both critical considerations in RTP decision-making. Incorporating sleep quality assessment into RTP protocols may help identify athletes at elevated re-injury risk.

citation: Renaghan E, Wittels HL, Feigenbaum LA, Wishon MJ, Chong S, Wittels ED, Hendricks S, Hecocks D, Bellamy K, Girardi J, Lee S, Vo T, McDonald SM, Wittels SH. Exposures to Elevated Core Temperatures during Football Training: The Impact on Autonomic Nervous System Recovery and Function. Sports. 2024;12(1):8.
abstract: This prospective cohort study evaluated the relationship between exercise training duration at varying core temperature thresholds (≥37°C, ≥38°C, ≥39°C) and 24-hour autonomic nervous system (ANS) recovery and function in 50 male Division I collegiate American football athletes tracked over a 25-week season. Core temperature and cardiac metrics (baseline HR, HR recovery, rMSSD, SDNN) were measured via armband Warfighter Monitors. Statistically significant associations between training duration and 24 h ANS recovery/function were observed at ≥38°C and ≥39°C thresholds. Association strength increased with core temperature, indicating accelerated ANS deterioration at higher temperatures. No significant associations were observed at the ≥37°C threshold.
tldr: Training longer at core temperatures ≥38°C negatively influences ANS recovery for up to 24 hours post-exercise in collegiate football athletes, with effects becoming progressively stronger at ≥39°C. Even short bouts (as little as 5–10 minutes) at hyperthermic temperatures can produce detectable ANS dysfunction the following day. This study highlights the need for coaches to monitor both core temperature and training duration, especially during summer camps and preseason, to protect long-term athlete health and performance.
methods: Prospective cohort design tracking 50 healthy male D1 football athletes (mean age 21.3±1.4 yr, mass 103.0±20.2 kg) over a 25-week season comprising two 4-week summer training camps, a 4-week preseason camp, and a 13-week in-season period. Warfighter Monitor armbands (Tiger Tech Solutions) continuously measured core temperature (infrared sensor) and ECG throughout 128 training sessions. ANS metrics — baseline HR (0600–0700 h), 30-s HR recovery, rMSSD, and SDNN — were captured the following morning. Linear regression models were fit separately for each core temperature threshold and each ANS outcome variable.
findings: Significant associations between training duration and 24 h ANS outcomes at ≥38°C: baseline HR β=0.10±0.02 (R²=0.26, p<0.0001); HR recovery β=−0.06±0.02 (R²=0.21, p=0.0002); rMSSD β=−0.11±0.02 (R²=0.24, p<0.0001); SDNN β=−0.16±0.04 (R²=0.22, p<0.0001). At ≥39°C, associations were stronger: baseline HR β=0.39±0.05 (R²=0.62); HR recovery β=−0.26±0.04 (R²=0.52); rMSSD β=−0.37±0.05 (R²=0.58); SDNN β=−0.67±0.09 (R²=0.59), all p<0.0001. No significant associations at ≥37°C. ANS deterioration was detectable after as little as 10 minutes at ≥39°C, vs. ~60 minutes at ≥38°C.
limitations: Generalizability limited to one D1 football team at a single institution in a warm, humid climate. External factors potentially influencing ANS (medications, caffeine, sleep quality, mental stress) were not measured. Female athletes and other competitive levels not represented. Core temperature measurement via non-invasive armband infrared sensor, though validated, is not equivalent to gold-standard invasive measures for all athletes.
practicalImplications: Coaches and sports medicine staff should monitor both core temperature and training duration during sessions conducted in hot/humid conditions (especially summer training camps and preseason). When core temperatures are ≥38°C, training bouts should be limited to 30–45 minutes with prolonged cooling breaks. Consecutive training days following elevated core temperature exposure compound ANS dysfunction and increase exertional heat illness risk. Athletes training in thermoneutral environments showed no significant ANS deterioration, confirming the value of climate-controlled facilities or cooling strategies.
athleteDev: Summer camp and preseason training represent the highest heat-exposure risk period for collegiate football athletes. Athletes in detraining periods entering summer camps may experience accelerated core temperature rises due to reduced heat acclimatization. Development staff should incorporate heat acclimatization protocols, monitor core temperature via wearables, and structure practice schedules to allow adequate cooling. Long-term cumulative ANS dysfunction from repeated hyperthermic exposures may impair performance adaptation and increase injury risk over a season.
rtp: For athletes returning to activity after illness, injury, or extended break, core temperature monitoring is particularly important as fitness reductions impair thermoregulation. Elevated ANS dysfunction 24 hours post-training can mask readiness for return-to-play assessments that rely on resting HR or HRV. Practitioners should consider scheduling RTP evaluations on days following thermoneutral training or after confirmed normal ANS recovery to avoid false negatives in readiness assessment.

citation: Buchheit M, Settembre M, Hader K, McHugh D. Exposures to near-to-maximal speed running bouts during different turnarounds in elite football: association with match hamstring injuries. Biology of Sport. 2023;40(4):1057–1067.
abstract: This retrospective analysis of 36 team-seasons (16 elite European football teams; 627 players; 5,052 training session days; 24,486 player-turnarounds; 152 non-contact hamstring injuries >3 days time loss) examined the occurrence of near-to-maximal sprinting speed (near-to-MSS) running bouts at >85%, >90%, and >95% MSS during training across different turnaround lengths (3–8 days between matches) and their association with match hamstring injury rates. Data were collected from the Kitman Labs platform across EPL, Serie A, Ligue 1, Bundesliga, Scottish Premiership, MLS, and Dutch Eredivisie (January 2018–December 2021). Match hamstring injury rates were compared between turnarounds with and without near-to-MSS exposures and by the day(s) within the turnaround when exposures occurred.
tldr: In elite football, the majority of players arrive at match day without having been exposed to near-to-maximal sprinting speed training during the current turnaround. For half the turnarounds examined, there were no match hamstring injuries when players reached >95% MSS during training. Critically, there were no match hamstring injuries when >95% MSS exposures occurred specifically on D-2 (two days before the match), suggesting that programming high-speed running at D-2 may be a protective strategy against match day hamstring injuries.
methods: Retrospective observational study using data from 36 team-seasons across 19 elite football clubs in top European leagues plus MLS (Jan 2018–Dec 2021) via the Kitman Labs platform. After exclusion criteria, 620 players, 5,052 training session days, and 24,486 player-turnarounds were analyzed. Near-to-MSS was defined as individual speed thresholds at >85%, >90%, and >95% of each player's maximal sprinting speed (MSS; derived from GPS data or highest recorded in-season speed). Turnarounds from 3–8 days were examined. Injury rates expressed per 1,000 player-turnarounds with 95% confidence intervals (exact binomial). Substantial differences assumed when CIs did not overlap.
findings: Overall, 60%, 76%, and 90% of player-turnarounds contained no >85%, >90%, or >95% MSS exposures, respectively. For half the turnarounds, no match hamstring injuries occurred when players were exposed to running bouts >95% MSS. Injuries still occurred in 85% of turnarounds when there were no or only lower relative speed exposures (>85% or >90%). No hamstring injuries occurred when >95% MSS exposures were programmed at D-2 (n=480; 0 injuries) compared to D-3 and earlier (multiple injuries observed). For the pooled 4- to 8-d turnarounds, D-2 with >95% MSS was the only day pattern associated with zero injuries. Injury rates were not significantly different between turnarounds with vs. without exposures when all turnarounds were pooled.
limitations: Observational design precludes causal inference. MSS was inconsistently tested across teams; average of three highest GPS speeds used as proxy when testing unavailable, which may underestimate true MSS. Injury records rely on practitioners' entries. Analysis is univariate; chronic training loads, match minutes, player age, and injury history were not simultaneously controlled. Teams in the dataset may be biased toward evidence-based practitioners already valuing high-speed work. The low number of exposures at specific day/turnaround combinations limits some comparisons.
practicalImplications: Programming >95% MSS running bouts at D-2 (two days before the match) appears to be the most strategically advantageous timing for near-to-MSS exposures in terms of match hamstring injury mitigation. Finishing and transition drills covering >30 m are among the few training activities that reliably elicit near-to-MSS intensities. Staff should not rely on small-sided games to achieve near-to-MSS exposures. During congested fixture periods (3–4 day turnarounds), near-to-MSS work may only be feasible for substitutes and non-starters.
athleteDev: For player development and long-term athletic preparation in football, this study reinforces that regular exposure to near-to-maximal sprinting is necessary to prepare hamstrings for the demands of match play. Developing players should be systematically exposed to >90–95% MSS running in their weekly microcycle, with awareness that longer turnarounds allow more flexibility in programming these high-speed bouts. A chronic dose of near-to-MSS exposure is hypothesized to reduce hamstring injury susceptibility over a season.
rtp: Athletes returning from hamstring injury should be progressively re-exposed to near-to-maximal sprint velocities (>85%, then >90%, then >95% MSS) before full match reintegration, as insufficient speed exposure is associated with elevated match hamstring injury risk. Timing of high-speed re-exposure in the week leading to return-to-competition is critical; D-2 appears to be an optimal programming day. RTP criteria should include demonstration of near-to-MSS tolerance in training before match availability is confirmed.

citation: Wiig H, Raastad T, Luteberget LS, Ims I, Spencer M. External Load Variables Affect Recovery Markers up to 72 h After Semiprofessional Football Matches. Frontiers in Physiology. 2019;10:689.
abstract: This study investigated how external load variables derived from player tracking devices affected recovery markers up to 72 hours after football matches in 75 semiprofessional male players from six Norwegian second division teams. Three experimental matches were played in an indoor stadium. External load variables — playing duration, total distance, PlayerLoad™, high-intensity running (HIR, >19.8 km/h), and high-intensity events (HIE, accelerations/decelerations/COD >2.5 m/s²) — were collected via combined IMU/LPS tracking. Recovery markers included creatine kinase (CK), myoglobin (MYO), countermovement jump (CMJ), 30-m sprint (SP30), and Yo-Yo Intermittent Recovery Level 1 (YOYO) at 1, 24, 48, and 72 h post-match. Effects were modeled as the difference between a typical high-load and a typical low-load match (2 SDs of external load).
tldr: High-intensity running (HIR) was the strongest predictor of muscle damage indicators (CK and myoglobin) up to 72 hours post-match, while PlayerLoad™ and total distance best predicted sprint performance recovery. CMJ recovery could not be reliably predicted by any external load variable. Despite substantial mean effects of external load on recovery markers, individual player recovery could not be predicted due to large inter-individual variability. Practitioners should monitor multiple external load variables together rather than relying on a single metric.
methods: Cross-sectional within-subject design with 75 outfield male semiprofessional players (mean age 20.4±4.6 yr, mass 72.7±7.2 kg) from six Norwegian second division teams. Three experimental matches (FIFA-standard 90 min indoor, 15±1°C) played 14–23 days after the season. Players wore Catapult OptimEye S5 (IMU) and ClearSky T5 (LPS) tracking devices. Blood samples drawn at −1, 1, 24, 48, and 72 h for CK and MYO. CMJ (force platform), SP30 (timing gates), and YOYO assessed at corresponding time points (n=24–59 per test). Log-transformed recovery markers analyzed via linear mixed model (SAS MIXED procedure) with effects rescaled to 2 SDs of each external load variable. Effects interpreted using magnitude-based inferences with 90% CIs.
findings: Matches induced most likely substantial increases in CK (ES=0.92–1.20 at 1–24 h), myoglobin (ES=3.80 at 1 h), and CMJ decreases (ES=−0.75 at 1 h) persisting through 72 h. HIR had the strongest, most consistent effect on CK (ES=0.60–1.08, very likely to most likely substantial, all time points). Duration, total distance, and HIE had likely substantial effects on CK at 1, 24, and 72 h. All five variables had likely substantial effects on MYO at 1 h. CMJ was largely unpredicted by external load (trivial or unclear across variables). PlayerLoad™ and total distance showed likely substantial negative effects on SP30 at 72 h (ES=0.46–0.56). HIE and PlayerLoad™ showed likely substantially positive effects on YOYO at 72 h (ES=0.47–0.56), possibly reflecting a fitness-conditioning interaction. Wide individual prediction intervals prevented reliable individual player recovery prediction from external load variables alone.
limitations: One match per player design limited modeling to between-player effects; within-player analysis with multiple matches would reduce uncertainty. Matches were nonofficial and played 2–3 weeks post-season with reduced training load, potentially underrepresenting typical in-season recovery demands. Control of physical activity, nutrition, and sleep between measurements was limited to pre-study instructions. Study may have been underpowered at later time points due to participant dropout and inherent measurement unreliability.
practicalImplications: HIR distance is the most important external load variable for monitoring post-match muscle damage recovery (CK, MYO); practitioners should ensure this metric is captured alongside total distance. PlayerLoad™ and total distance are better indicators of sprint performance recovery. Tracking multiple external load variables simultaneously provides a more complete picture of recovery status than any single metric. Despite significant group-level effects, external load alone cannot reliably predict when individual players will be recovered by 72 h — direct wellness or performance testing remains essential for individual load management decisions.
athleteDev: For player development in football, this study confirms that high-intensity running is the primary driver of post-match muscle damage, providing a physiological rationale for progressive exposure to high-speed running in developing players. Understanding the dose-response relationship between match load variables and recovery kinetics helps coaches structure training loads during the 72-hour post-match period to manage cumulative fatigue across a competitive season.
rtp: In return-to-play contexts following match-related muscle damage or fatigue, high-intensity running distance (HIR) is the most predictive external load metric for CK and myoglobin elevation and should be carefully managed in early reintegration phases. Sprint performance (30-m sprint) remains impaired at 72 h post-match, particularly in high-total-distance and high-PlayerLoad™ matches, meaning athletes in the 3-day post-match window may not have fully recovered sprint capacity. Individual variability in recovery is substantial, underscoring the need for direct performance or wellness testing rather than time-only RTP criteria.

citation: Barreira P, Vaz JR, Ferreira R, Araújo JP, Tavares F. External training loads and soft-tissue injury occurrence during congested vs noncongested periods in football. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2024; Ahead of Print. doi:10.1123/ijspp.2024-0058
abstract: This study examined the influence of congested (matches within ≤96 hours) versus noncongested fixture periods on soft-tissue injury incidence and external load across two full professional football seasons (2021–22 and 2022–23) in a Portuguese Liga I team. Thirty-three outfield players were tracked using 10-Hz GPS (STATSports APEX). External load metrics included HSR (>19.8 km/h), sprint distance (>25 km/h), relative velocity zones above 80% (V80) and 90% (V90) of individual maximum speed, and acceleration/deceleration metrics (AD2, AD3). Soft-tissue injuries were classified using IOC/UEFA consensus definitions. A total of 65 medical-attention and 39 time-loss injuries occurred over the two seasons, producing 704 layoff days and 113 official matches missed. Soft-tissue injury rates were 3.9 and 3.2 per 1000 hours for congested and noncongested periods respectively, with no statistically significant difference between periods. Total weekly external load was significantly higher during noncongested periods for all metrics except AD3; training load was higher in noncongested weeks while match load was higher during congested weeks.
tldr: Across two full professional football seasons, congested fixture schedules (matches ≤96 hours apart) did not significantly increase soft-tissue injury rates or burden compared to noncongested periods. Higher match exposure during congestion elevated weekly match load, while noncongested periods allowed greater training load accumulation. The finding challenges the assumption that congested schedules directly elevate injury risk and suggests that adequate training during regular periods may provide a protective conditioning effect. Relative speed metrics V80 and V90 are recommended as individualized training planning tools.
methods: Prospective observational cohort study. N=33 professional outfield players from Sporting CP (Portuguese Liga I), seasons 2021–22 and 2022–23. Congested periods defined as official matches ≤96 hours apart (15 periods, 225 days, 71 games). External load collected via 10-Hz GPS (STATSports APEX): HSR, sprint distance, V80, V90, AD2 (meters accelerating/decelerating >2 m/s²), AD3 (count of accelerations/decelerations >3 m/s²). Injury data collected per IOC/UEFA football-specific consensus. Statistical comparisons via paired t-test or Wilcoxon signed-rank test (Jamovi v1.6); significance at P<.05.
findings: Soft-tissue injury rates were 3.9 (congested) vs 3.2 (noncongested) per 1000 hours (P=.781, ES=0.074); injury burden was 71.8 vs 60.5 days/1000 hours (P=.668). Total weekly HSR (1643 vs 1968 m, P<.001), sprint (339 vs 404 m, P<.001), V80 (101 vs 140 m, P<.001), V90 (12.2 vs 19.0 m, P<.001), and AD2 (5137 vs 6389 m, P<.001) were significantly higher during noncongested periods; AD3 did not differ (613 vs 629, P=.689). Match load was significantly higher during congested weeks; training load was significantly higher during noncongested weeks. One player with 5 injuries in 2022–23 disproportionately inflated the injury burden data.
limitations: Single-team sample from one club limits generalizability. No position-specific or consecutive match participation analysis was performed. International duty periods excluded. Post-COVID 5-substitute rule and individual injury history were not formally modeled. Outlier player substantially affected injury rate and burden totals. Retrospective injury classification may have inherent bias.
practicalImplications: Congested and noncongested schedules produce similar soft-tissue injury rates, indicating that match congestion alone does not drive injury risk when training and recovery are managed appropriately. Staff should use noncongested periods to build chronic load across all metrics — particularly V80, V90, and AD2 — to prepare players for the demands of congested stretches. Squad rotation and individualized speed-zone targets (V80/V90) should be incorporated into periodization planning to maintain readiness while limiting injury exposure during fixture pileups.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, this study reinforces the importance of systematically accumulating high-intensity running load (especially above 80–90% of individual maximum velocity) during non-congested training windows. These relative speed zones are linked to musculoskeletal adaptation in hamstrings and lower limb muscles. Developing tolerance to these exposures during pre-season and regular-season training blocks can buffer players against the increased match load that accompanies congested schedules.
rtp: No direct RTP protocol is described. However, findings suggest that players returning from soft-tissue injuries should be progressively reintroduced to V80/V90 and AD2 load targets derived from match demands before being cleared for congested match schedules. The lack of injury rate difference between congested and noncongested periods implies that match load per se is manageable if chronic load preparation is adequate, which has implications for setting RTP readiness thresholds for soft-tissue injuries.

citation: Dalen-Lorentsen T, Ranvik A, Bjørneboe J, Clarsen B, Andersen TE. Facilitators and barriers for implementation of a load management intervention in football. BMJ Open Sport Exerc Med. 2021;7:e001046. doi:10.1136/bmjsem-2021-001046
abstract: This study investigated the barriers and facilitators to implementing a load management intervention among elite youth football players and coaches in Norway. Conducted as a survey appended to a cluster-randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 25 teams and 482 players, 250 players (51%) and 17 coaches (68%) completed the questionnaire. The survey was structured around the RE-AIM (Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance) framework. Key facilitators identified by both players and coaches included scientific evidence of injury prevention benefit and performance enhancement, and a mutually positive attitude between players and coaches toward the intervention. Time efficiency was identified as a critical practical requirement. Despite belief in the value of load management, players showed reluctance to invest more than 10–20 minutes per week unless performance benefits were demonstrated. All coaches valued load management for both prevention and performance but were constrained by time demands. These findings contextualize why a well-designed load management RCT (using the Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio) failed to reduce health problems — compliance was 63% — and provide guidance for future implementation.
tldr: Even when players and coaches believe load management can prevent injuries and enhance performance, real-world compliance remains a challenge. The most important facilitators are scientific evidence of effectiveness, positive mutual attitudes between coaches and players, and time-efficient protocols. Key barriers include time constraints and the lack of convincing proof that load management works in football. Practitioners implementing load management programs should focus on brief, intuitive tools; build coach and player buy-in simultaneously; and communicate both injury prevention and performance benefits to maximize adoption.
methods: Cross-sectional survey conducted in November 2018 following a cluster-RCT of elite Norwegian youth footballers (both sexes). Questionnaires were based on the RE-AIM framework and adapted from prior implementation studies in handball and football. Four questionnaire versions were developed (intervention/control × player/coach). Online distribution via Briteback software; non-respondents received reminders. 23 of 25 teams participated; 250 players and 17 coaches returned usable responses. Descriptive statistics used for analysis; no between-group statistical comparisons were made.
findings: 88% of players rated scientific evidence of performance improvement as a key facilitator; 84% rated injury/illness mitigation as important; 86% rated positive coach attitude as important. 100% of coaches rated scientific evidence of prevention effectiveness as most important; 88% rated player motivation as important. 93% of intervention-group players spent >10 min/week on load monitoring. 63% of coaches used the protocol as intended (minimum 1 hour/week). 45% of players and 50% of coaches planned to continue load management the following season. Only 28% of players were willing to spend >10 min/week on load management if only injury prevention was the benefit, but 70% were willing if performance enhancement was also a goal. Coaches and players both perceived footballers as being at high risk of injury and overuse injury, and nearly all believed load management could reduce injury.
limitations: Survey-based methodology limits depth of insight; a qualitative approach would have added nuance. Terms such as 'scientific evidence' and 'performance' may have been interpreted differently across respondents. Low response rate (51% of players) introduces selection bias — those who responded were likely more positively disposed to the intervention. Low number of coaches (17) limits robustness of coach-specific findings. Questionnaire did not fully capture the maintenance dimension of RE-AIM. Compliance in the intervention group was suboptimal, suggesting questionnaire fatigue from daily load logging.
practicalImplications: Load management programs in team football environments must be designed with two non-negotiable properties: they must be time-efficient (under 10 minutes per session for players) and they must be framed around both performance enhancement and injury prevention to maximize player motivation. Coach buy-in is essential — coaches who are enthusiastic create a spiral of positive compliance among players. Clubs and federations should mandate or formally endorse load monitoring programs to increase institutional-level adoption. Intuitive digital tools with minimal data entry friction will improve real-world adherence.
athleteDev: For football athlete development environments, this study highlights that young elite players will engage with load monitoring when they perceive direct performance benefits — not merely injury prevention. Development programs should embed load tracking into athlete education, position it as a performance optimization tool, and ensure coaches are trained advocates. Establishing load management habits early in an athlete's career improves long-term health outcomes and professional development trajectory.
rtp: While not an RTP-specific study, the findings are directly relevant to return-to-play load management protocols. Players returning from injury are precisely the subgroup for whom load management is most critical. The barriers identified here — time burden, skepticism about effectiveness, and lack of coach engagement — are equally applicable in rehabilitation settings. RTP programs using load monitoring apps or wearable-based ACWR tracking will achieve higher compliance if the data is shared transparently with both the athlete and the coaching/medical team and if the monitoring is framed as readiness optimization rather than restriction.

citation: Yang J, Hodax JD, Machan JT, Krill MK, Lemme NJ, Durand WM, Hoffman JT, Hewett TE, Owens BD. Factors affecting return to play after primary Achilles tendon tear: a cohort of NFL players. Orthop J Sports Med. 2019;7(3):2325967119830139. doi:10.1177/2325967119830139
abstract: This case-control study evaluated factors associated with return to play (RTP) and performance changes following primary Achilles tendon (AT) tears in NFL players. Eighty players with primary AT tears between the 2009 and 2014 seasons were identified from a previously established NFL injury database. RTP was defined as participating in at least one regular season or postseason game within 18 months of injury. Kaplan-Meier analysis was used to model probability of RTP over time. The overall RTP rate was 61.3% — the lowest reported for this population — with a mean recovery time of 11.90 months. Age, draft round, position type, and number of prior seasons did not significantly influence RTP probability. Players who successfully returned had played in significantly more games (13.74) the season immediately preceding injury versus non-returners (8.71; P=.011). Players who did not return exhibited a significant decline in games played in the season prior to injury compared to two seasons prior (12.69 to 8.71; P=.019), possibly reflecting prodromal AT degeneration. Among returners, performance was significantly reduced only in the RTP season; performance returned to preinjury levels when players competed for more than one season following return.
tldr: Only 61.3% of NFL players return to play after a primary Achilles tendon tear, lower than prior estimates, with a mean recovery of nearly 12 months. Unlike other serious NFL injuries, neither draft round nor position type predicts who returns. The strongest predictor of not returning is declining game participation in the season before injury — possibly a marker of underlying tendon degeneration. For those who do return, performance normalizes after the first post-return season, suggesting that players given the opportunity to compete through the initial transition year can recover to preinjury levels.
methods: Retrospective case-control study. 80 NFL players with primary (first-ever, isolated) AT tears in the 2009–2014 seasons identified via NFL injury reports, team disclosures, and news reports. Players with prior ipsilateral or contralateral AT tears excluded. Twelve players (15%) with subsequent AT tears excluded from performance analyses. Player demographics from NFL.com. RTP defined as ≥1 play in a regular season or postseason game within 18 months. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis modeled probability of RTP over time; generalized linear models assessed demographic predictors; generalized estimating equations modeled within- and between-player performance changes. Statistical significance at alpha=0.05 with Holm method for family-wise error correction.
findings: Overall RTP rate: 61.3% (49/80 players) within 18 months; mean time to RTP: 11.90 months. Unskilled position players had higher RTP (75%) vs. skilled (52.8%), but this difference was not statistically significant by Kaplan-Meier (P=.19). Draft round did not influence RTP (P=.49). Players who RTP'd played 13.74 games the prior season vs 8.71 for non-returners (P=.011). Non-returners showed significant pre-injury performance decline (12.69 games 2 seasons prior vs 8.71 one season prior; P=.019). In the RTP season, returners played significantly fewer games (10.94) than the two prior seasons (P<.001). After the RTP season, no significant differences in games played or started were found, indicating return to preinjury performance levels. 15% of players sustained a subsequent AT tear.
limitations: RTP evaluated only within 18 months; longer follow-up was underpowered. Performance analysis limited to games played/started — position-specific metrics (e.g., yards per carry) not analyzed. Small positional subgroup sizes precluded robust position-specific analysis. Data sourced from publicly available records; some injuries may be missing. Causation between preinjury performance decline and AT rupture cannot be established. Players who did not RTP may have been released for reasons unrelated to their injury. Subsequent tear cases excluded from performance analysis limit completeness.
practicalImplications: Medical and performance staff should monitor for declining game participation in seasons preceding a known or suspected AT condition, as this may represent a prodromal window for intervention before rupture. RTP decisions after AT repair should account for the expected performance dip in the first return season and not be used as sole justification for roster release. Players who are given at least two post-return seasons demonstrate performance normalization, arguing for patience in roster management after AT injuries.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, this study highlights that Achilles tendon health is not static — declining performance and playing time may reflect subclinical tendon degeneration before overt rupture. Proactive tendon screening, load management, and eccentric strengthening programs (Nordic, calf raises, plyometric loading) during preseason and in-season training may help identify and protect at-risk athletes. Young players should be educated about tendon health warning signs.
rtp: The 61.3% RTP rate represents the lowest reported for NFL players with primary AT tears, with a mean timeline of approximately 12 months. Neither position type nor draft round predicted RTP, but robust preinjury game participation was the strongest positive predictor. Performance in the first post-return season is significantly reduced but normalizes thereafter. These findings suggest RTP criteria following AT repair should include functional performance benchmarks beyond simply surgical healing, and that return timelines in the NFL context are substantially longer than for recreational athletes (4–6 months vs ~12 months) due to the demands of elite competition.

citation: Yang J, Hodax JD, Machan JT, Krill MK, Lemme NJ, Durand WM, Hoffman JT, Hewett TE, Owens BD. Factors affecting return to play after primary Achilles tendon tear: a cohort of NFL players. Orthop J Sports Med. 2019;7(3):2325967119830139. doi:10.1177/2325967119830139
abstract: NOTE: This entry (ID 217) is a duplicate of ID 216. Both PDFs contain identical content — the same paper by Yang et al. (2019) published in Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine. The only difference is filename formatting (ID 216 uses a hyphenated subtitle with lowercase; ID 217 uses title case without a hyphen). All fields are identical to ID 216. This study evaluated factors associated with return to play (RTP) and performance changes following primary Achilles tendon (AT) tears in NFL players. 80 players with primary AT tears (2009–2014 seasons) were analyzed. The overall RTP rate was 61.3% at a mean 11.90 months. Age, draft round, and position type did not significantly predict RTP. Declining preinjury game participation was the strongest predictor of failure to return. Players who returned experienced a performance dip only in the RTP season, with normalization to preinjury levels when given more than one post-return season.
tldr: Duplicate of ID 216. Only 61.3% of NFL players return to play after a primary Achilles tendon tear, lower than prior estimates, with a mean recovery of nearly 12 months. Unlike other serious NFL injuries, neither draft round nor position type predicts who returns. The strongest predictor of not returning is declining game participation in the season before injury — possibly a marker of underlying tendon degeneration. For those who do return, performance normalizes after the first post-return season.
methods: Duplicate of ID 216. Retrospective case-control study. 80 NFL players with primary AT tears in the 2009–2014 seasons. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis; generalized linear models; generalized estimating equations. RTP defined as ≥1 play in a regular season or postseason game within 18 months of injury.
findings: Duplicate of ID 216. Overall RTP rate 61.3% (mean 11.90 months). Position and draft round not significant predictors of RTP. Pre-injury game participation (13.74 vs 8.71 games; P=.011) was the key differentiator between returners and non-returners. Non-returners showed significant preinjury decline in games played (P=.019). Performance reduced in RTP season but normalized after >1 post-return season.
limitations: Duplicate of ID 216. RTP window limited to 18 months; performance metrics limited to games played/started; small positional subgroups; publicly sourced data may be incomplete; causation cannot be established; subsequent tear cases excluded from performance analysis.
practicalImplications: Duplicate of ID 216. Monitor for declining game participation as a potential prodromal sign of AT degeneration. Allow players at least two post-return seasons before drawing conclusions about long-term performance impact. RTP decisions should not rely solely on the first return season as a performance benchmark.
