athleteDev: Duplicate of ID 216. Proactive Achilles tendon screening, eccentric loading programs, and load monitoring during development phases can help identify and protect at-risk football athletes before overt rupture occurs.
rtp: Duplicate of ID 216. RTP rate of 61.3% at approximately 12 months is the lowest reported for NFL players with primary AT tears. Preinjury activity level is the strongest RTP predictor. First post-return season performance is reduced but subsequent seasons normalize, supporting patience in roster decisions and comprehensive functional RTP criteria beyond surgical healing timelines.

citation: Brophy RH, Wojtys EM, Mack CD, Hawaldar K, Herzog MM, Owens BD. Factors associated with the mechanism of ACL tears in the National Football League: a video-based analysis. Orthop J Sports Med. 2021;9(11):23259671211053301. doi:10.1177/23259671211053301
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study used video analysis to characterize the mechanism of ACL tears in NFL games over six consecutive seasons (2014–2019). Two fellowship-trained sports medicine orthopaedic surgeons independently reviewed 140 video-confirmed ACL tears from the NFL's centralized electronic health record, classifying each as direct contact (to injured lower extremity), indirect contact (contact elsewhere), or no contact. The study found that 70% of NFL ACL tears occurred via noncontact mechanism (44% indirect contact, 26% no contact), with only 30% involving direct contact to the knee. However, injury mechanism varied substantially by position: 70% of offensive linemen's ACL tears were from direct contact, while wide receivers had zero direct contact ACL tears. Elevated BMI (≥35 kg/m²) was associated with greater likelihood of direct contact injury (53% vs 24%; P<.01), largely explained by the high BMI of linemen. Rookies had the fewest direct contact tears. Timing effects were observed — direct contact was most common in the third quarter (44%) and least common in the fourth quarter (13%; P<.01). Surface type (synthetic vs. natural grass) did not significantly affect the distribution of injury mechanism.
tldr: Most ACL tears in NFL games occur via noncontact or indirect contact mechanisms, but position matters enormously: offensive linemen sustain the majority of their ACL tears via direct contact at the line of scrimmage, while skill positions like wide receivers experience almost exclusively noncontact ACL tears. This means that injury prevention strategies cannot be one-size-fits-all in American football — linemen may benefit from bracing interventions while skill players need neuromuscular training programs targeting noncontact ACL risk. Understanding mechanism by position is essential for designing targeted prevention programs.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using prospective NFL injury surveillance data (2014–2019 regular and postseason games). ACL tears identified via the NFL centralized electronic health record (EHR). Film archives reviewed to identify 142 video-confirmed injuries; 140 were included after exclusions (2 events not visualizable). Two orthopaedic surgeons with sports medicine fellowship training independently reviewed videos and classified mechanism as direct contact, indirect contact, or no contact. Additional abstraction: player engaged with opponent, limb position, cleat-turf engagement, contact location. Interrater reliability evaluated via Cohen kappa (n=31 double-reviewed; kappa=0.80). Additional variables (BMI, years of service, surface type, game timing) extracted from NFL EHR and Game Statistics and Information System. Fisher exact chi-square tests for group comparisons; significance at P<.05.
findings: Of 140 ACL tears: 30% direct contact, 44% indirect contact, 26% no contact. Offensive linemen: 70% direct contact. Wide receivers: 0% direct contact. Defensive secondary: 8% direct contact. BMI ≥35 kg/m²: 53% direct contact vs 24% for BMI <35 kg/m² (P<.01); this finding confounded by position. Among non-linemen with BMI ≥35, only 1 of 9 tears was direct contact. Rookies: 18% direct contact vs 32% non-rookies (P=.22, NS). Third quarter: highest direct contact rate (44%); fourth quarter: lowest (13%; P<.01). Middle 8 weeks (weeks 5–12) of regular season: higher direct contact rate (38%) vs first/last 4 weeks (22%; P=.06). No significant difference in noncontact mechanism by surface type (synthetic 72% vs natural grass 69%).
limitations: Excludes practice (n=132) and preseason game (n=81) ACL tears, limiting generalizability to all ACL injuries. Cannot calculate injury rates or relative risks — descriptive study only. Small positional subgroup sizes limit ability to fully disentangle BMI vs position effects. Video quality varied; exact moment of injury could not always be determined with certainty. No practice-related injuries captured, although game ACLs exceed practice ACLs in frequency (>60% occur in games). Interrater reliability was high (kappa=0.80) but 4 injuries required adjudication. Study funded through NFL research relationship, which may introduce institutional bias.
practicalImplications: ACL injury prevention in American football must be position-stratified. Offensive linemen, who sustain the majority of direct contact ACL tears at the line of scrimmage, represent a population that may benefit from protective knee bracing. Skill position players — particularly wide receivers, defensive backs, and linebackers — sustain predominantly noncontact ACL tears and are the appropriate target for neuromuscular and movement-quality-based prevention programs (e.g., FIFA 11+ equivalents). Teams should not apply a single prevention protocol uniformly across all positions. Performance staff should also be aware that fatigue-related noncontact mechanisms may be elevated in the fourth quarter, supporting the importance of conditioning and rotation management late in games.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, this study confirms that noncontact ACL risk is the dominant concern for skill positions throughout their careers, while linemen face a distinct contact-mediated risk profile. Developmental programs for skill position players should prioritize landing mechanics, deceleration technique, single-leg stability, hip and hamstring strength, and knee valgus control as injury prevention foundations. For linemen at all levels, technique coaching in blocking and controlled engagement — alongside appropriate bracing consideration — should be integrated into position-specific development curricula.
rtp: While this study characterizes injury mechanism rather than RTP outcomes, the findings have important RTP implications. Understanding that a player's ACL tear was contact-driven (especially for linemen) versus noncontact has ramifications for rehabilitation emphasis and re-injury prevention. A lineman returning from a direct-contact ACL tear may benefit from bracing post-RTP, while a skill player returning from a noncontact ACL tear requires neuromuscular readiness benchmarks to address the movement deficits that predisposed to the original injury. Position-specific mechanism awareness should be incorporated into RTP decision frameworks for NFL and elite football players.

citation: Baugh CM, Gedlaman MA, Daneshvar DH, Kroshus E. Factors Influencing College Football Players' Beliefs About Incurring Football-Related Dementia. Orthop J Sports Med. 2021;9(4):23259671211001129.
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined factors influencing 296 NCAA Division I Power 5 college football players' beliefs about whether they would develop football-related dementia (CTE, Alzheimer's disease). Approximately 10% of athletes believed football-related dementia was likely in their future. Logistic regressions evaluated associations between this belief and athletic/demographic characteristics, football and general health risk postures, and cognitive reflection test scores. Skill players had lower odds than linemen of believing dementia was likely (OR 0.35; 95% CI 0.14-0.89). Each additional suspected concussion increased odds by 24% (OR 1.24; 95% CI 1.07-1.45). Higher cognitive reflection test scores were positively associated with believing dementia was likely (OR 1.57; 95% CI 1.12-2.21).
tldr: Only about 1 in 10 college football players believes he will develop football-related dementia. Players who are linemen, have more suspected concussions, and score higher on deliberative thinking tests are more likely to hold this belief. Risk communication strategies that appeal to experiential rather than deliberative reasoning may be more effective with this population.
methods: Cross-sectional survey of 296 NCAA Division I Power 5 football players from 4 teams (spring 2017). Measured: position, years of football, suspected/diagnosed concussion history, 7-point Likert perceived risk of CTE/AD/dementia, composite acute football injury risk perceptions, chronic health risk perceptions, risk-benefit calculations, DOSPERT health subscale, and the 3-item cognitive reflection test (CRT). Logistic regressions with robust standard errors (clustered by team) evaluated associations with the binary outcome of believing football-related dementia was likely.
findings: 66.8% of players thought football-related dementia unlikely; 22.9% were unsure; 10.3% thought it likely. Skill players had 0.35x the odds of linemen believing dementia was likely. Each additional suspected concussion raised odds by 24%. Greater perceived likelihood of future football injury and chronic health problems were positively associated with believing dementia was likely, while believing long-term benefits outweigh risks was inversely associated. Higher CRT score (OR 1.57) was positively associated with believing dementia was likely. Diagnosed concussions, years of football, race, and parental education were not significantly associated.
limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. Only 4 of 65 recruited teams participated, potentially limiting generalizability, though participating and non-participating teams were similar on observable characteristics. No concussion definition was provided, allowing varying conceptualizations. Only deliberative risk perceptions were assessed; experiential and affective perceptions were not. Numeracy was not evaluated. Findings apply only to male college football players.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine clinicians are well-positioned to facilitate individualized conversations about long-term health risks with athletes. Because athletes higher in deliberative thinking are more likely to already recognize dementia risk, risk communication strategies targeting the broader athlete population should emphasize experiential or 'gist'-based messaging rather than probabilistic statistics. Institutions have an ethical obligation to ensure athletes are appropriately informed of participation risks.
athleteDev: Understanding athletes' risk perceptions can inform how athletic departments and sports medicine staff structure educational programming about long-term brain health. Linemen — who had lower perceived risk — may require more targeted education about cumulative head impact exposure and associated neurological risks. Concussion history tracking and counseling should include discussion of long-term neurodegenerative risk.
rtp: Athletes who have sustained more suspected concussions are more likely to perceive long-term dementia risk, suggesting a potential opening for clinicians to engage in longitudinal risk counseling during post-concussion return-to-play encounters. Clinicians should use RTP visits not only to clear athletes for return but also to reinforce awareness of cumulative long-term brain health risks.

citation: Fullagar HHK, Delaney J, Duffield R, Murray A. Factors influencing home advantage in American collegiate football. Sci Med Football. 2019;3(2):163-168.
abstract: This study examined home advantage (HA) in NCAA Division I football and the factors contributing to it using game data from two non-consecutive seasons (2013 and 2016; 719 in-conference games; 222 teams). Outcome measures were points differential, penalty differential, and penalty yardage differential. Linear mixed models assessed the effects of game location, travel distance (quartiles), time zone direction of travel, and crowd size. Playing at home was associated with a ~5.3-point advantage (ES = 0.58). Away travel beyond 484 km produced a moderate disadvantage (~5-7 points). Westward time zone crossings yielded a ~7.5-point disadvantage. Larger crowds produced a ~6-point advantage for home teams and an ~8.7-point disadvantage for away teams per 2 SD change in crowd size.
tldr: Home field advantage in college football is worth about 5 points and is driven primarily by crowd size and travel distance. Travel beyond 300 miles consistently hurts away teams by 5-7 points, and westward travel across time zones is especially costly. Crowd noise may play a key role, as larger crowds disproportionately hurt visiting teams. These findings can guide travel and periodization planning for away games.
methods: Retrospective analysis of in-conference games from the 2013 and 2016 NCAA Division I football seasons (n=719 games, 222 teams) using commercial statistics provider data (Sport Source Analytics). Travel distances calculated via Google Maps; time zones via the R geonames package. Linear mixed models with team as a random effect. Outcomes converted to effect sizes (ES) by dividing by between-team SD. Effects considered real if likelihood exceeded 75%. Travel categorized as Short (<483 km), Moderate (484-836 km), Long (837-1331 km), and Very Long (>1332 km).
findings: Home teams averaged a 5.34-point advantage (ES = 0.58, 100% likelihood). No significant penalty differential was found between home and away teams. Away travel <483 km produced no meaningful disadvantage (ES = -0.23, 56% likelihood). All distances >484 km produced moderate disadvantages (ES = -0.61 to -0.77, 99-100% likelihood). Crossing time zones westward produced a ~7.5-point disadvantage (ES = -0.82). Eastward travel showed no clear disadvantage. A 2 SD change in crowd size (~60,000 people) was worth ~11 points to home teams and ~8.7 points against away teams. Per 10,000 fans, away teams were disadvantaged by ~4 points and home teams advantaged by ~2 points.
limitations: Data were retrospective and from only two non-consecutive seasons, limiting causal inference and temporal generalizability. Only in-conference games at non-neutral venues were analyzed, excluding bowl games and non-conference matchups. Penalty differentials did not differ significantly, suggesting referee bias was not a strong HA factor in this dataset. Psychological factors like motivation and confidence could not be quantified. Differences in team strength across conferences and geographic regions may confound crowd-HA relationships.
practicalImplications: Travel exceeding 300 miles should trigger enhanced logistical and recovery planning for away games. Westward cross-time-zone travel appears particularly disruptive and may warrant earlier departure, adjusted sleep schedules, or light exposure protocols. Practitioners should consider incorporating crowd noise simulation into pre-game practice for high-attendance away environments. The roughly 5-point home advantage is comparable to a successful field goal and is practically meaningful for in-season game preparation.
athleteDev: College football players aged 18-22 may be more susceptible to travel disruption than professional athletes due to less developed pre-competition routines. Programs should systematically expose younger players to away-game preparation strategies, including managing environmental unfamiliarity and crowd noise, as part of their athletic development.
rtp: While not a return-to-play study, travel-related fatigue and circadian disruption may compound recovery timelines for athletes playing away games following injury. Clinicians overseeing injured athletes should account for scheduled long-distance away travel when projecting return-to-play readiness, particularly for travel involving westward time zone crossings.

citation: Mitchell A, Holding C, Greig M. Factors influencing optimum countermovement jump performance and movement strategy in Championship professional football players: implications for player profiling. Res Sports Med. 2020. DOI: 10.1080/15438627.2020.1860049.
abstract: This study examined single-leg countermovement jump (CMJ) performance and movement strategy in 36 professional outfield players from an English Championship football club. Players were retrospectively classified as Best (n=10) or Worst (n=10) performers based on CMJ jump height and flight time:contraction time (FT:CT) ratio. Movement strategy was quantified through force-time history metrics differentiating eccentric and concentric phases. Best performers demonstrated greater rate of force development in both phases (P ≤ 0.033), with concentric impulse the strongest predictor of jump height. FT:CT ratio differentiated best performers as using a shallower countermovement (P = 0.002), with concentric RFD the strongest predictor of good FT:CT ratio. Jump height alone can mask ineffectual eccentric mechanics; movement strategy via time ratio is advocated as the key profiling indicator.
tldr: In professional soccer/football players, jump height alone is an insufficient profiling metric because athletes can achieve acceptable jump heights through compensatory strategies that mask eccentric deficits. The flight time:contraction time ratio reveals true movement quality, with the best performers characterized by a shallower countermovement and greater concentric rate of force development. Practitioners should prioritize movement strategy metrics over gross outcome measures when assessing readiness and return to play.
methods: Thirty-six full-time professional outfield players from a Championship football club (age 23.3 ± 5.6 yrs, weight 78.1 ± 1.9 kg) performed 10 single-leg CMJs on a dual PASPORT force plate system (NMP ForceDecks) 5 days post-match, +5 days from a scheduled rest day. Mean scores across 5 dominant limb trials were calculated. Players ranked on jump height and FT:CT ratio independently; top 10 vs. bottom 10 formed Best and Worst groups. Force-time variables included peak force, impulse, RFD, phase duration, force at zero velocity, and CMJ depth for eccentric and concentric phases. Univariate general linear models and linear regression analyses were used.
findings: Best performers had significantly higher jump height (36.24 ± 1.53 cm vs. 20.15 ± 1.56 cm; P < 0.001, η²=0.968) and higher FT:CT ratio (1.04 ± 0.17 vs. 0.35 ± 0.09; P < 0.001, η²=0.880). When classified by jump height, Best performers showed significantly higher EccRFD (P=0.006), concentric impulse, PkForce, and RFD (P ≤ 0.034). When classified by FT:CT ratio, Best performers had greater eccentric PkForce and RFD (P ≤ 0.002), shorter eccentric phase duration, shallower CMJ depth (P=0.002), and greater concentric PkForce and RFD (P < 0.001). Concentric RFD was the strongest predictor of FT:CT ratio (r=0.88 in Best performers). Worst performers showed strong associations with greater eccentric phase metrics and deeper countermovement, suggesting compensatory strategy.
limitations: Study limited to outfield male players at one professional club; findings may not generalize to other populations, female athletes, or other sports. Only the single-leg CMJ was assessed; double-leg CMJ findings may differ. The group stratification compared the top and bottom 10 of 36 players, meaning the 'worst' performers are still professional footballers. Jump height calculated from flight time is susceptible to body position changes at landing. Future work should examine unloading and braking sub-phases separately and consider single-subject longitudinal approaches.
practicalImplications: Practitioners without force platforms can approximate movement quality using a countermovement:rocket jump height ratio as a proxy for stretch-shortening cycle efficacy. Profiling should incorporate FT:CT ratio alongside jump height to avoid misclassifying athletes with compensatory eccentric strategies as adequately prepared. Training interventions should target concentric rate of force development while ensuring eccentric phase quality is not neglected.
athleteDev: Single-leg CMJ profiling with force-time analysis provides individualized neuromuscular data to guide personalized training prescription for football athletes. Athletes with poor FT:CT ratios showing deep countermovements and extended eccentric phases may benefit from targeted SSC and reactive strength training. Regular single-leg CMJ assessment is logistically feasible and sensitive enough to detect training adaptations and fatigue-related changes in movement strategy.
rtp: Jump height returning to pre-injury levels during rehabilitation may mask persistent neuromuscular deficits if movement strategy is not assessed. The FT:CT ratio is sensitive to previous injury and can identify eccentric strength deficiencies that are a risk factor for hamstring strains, ACL injuries, and ankle injuries — all common in football. Return-to-play clearance should include FT:CT ratio criteria alongside gross performance outcome thresholds.

citation: Farnell A. False Start? An Analysis of NFL Penalties With and Without Crowds. J Sports Econ. 2023. DOI: 10.1177/15270025211148997.
abstract: Using play-by-play data from 108,059 NFL snaps across the 2018-2020 regular seasons (including COVID-19-restricted 2020 season with dramatically reduced crowds), this study examined whether crowd absence affected Quarterback ability to manipulate opposing defenses via cadence. Logistic regression models estimated the probability of pre-snap line-of-scrimmage penalties (offensive false starts; defensive offside, encroachment, and neutral zone infractions) as a function of stadium capacity utilization, home/away status, and controls. Results showed that false starts by offenses were not significantly affected by crowd absence. However, home defenses were significantly less likely to commit defensive pre-snap penalties when crowds were larger, consistent with crowd noise drowning out the visiting Quarterback's cadence.
tldr: Using the COVID-19 natural experiment of crowd-free NFL games, this study finds that crowd noise meaningfully reduces home defensive penalties by making it harder for the visiting Quarterback's cadence to fool the defense. False start penalties by offenses were unaffected by crowd size. This isolates a specific crowd mechanism — communication disruption — that contributes to home advantage, beyond any referee bias explanation.
methods: Play-by-play data from NFL Savant and attendance data from Pro Football Reference for 2018-2020 regular seasons (n=108,059 snaps after excluding international games, kneels, and kickoffs). Outcome variables: binary indicators for offensive false start penalties and defensive line-of-scrimmage penalties (offside, encroachment, neutral zone infraction). Key predictor: stadium capacity utilization (Percent Full). Logistic regression models estimated separately for offensive and defensive penalties, with controls for quarter, minute, down, yards to go, yard line, score difference, closed roof, and offense/defense team fixed effects. Interaction terms included Percent Full × Home Team. Robustness checks used binary empty/near-empty stadium indicators and excluded games with backup Quarterbacks.
findings: Home teams enjoyed a ~5-point advantage consistent with prior literature. For false start penalties, higher capacity utilization was positively associated with false starts in baseline models, but the interaction with offense-at-home was not significant, indicating crowd effects on false starts did not differ between home and away offenses. For defensive penalties, the interaction of Percent Full × Defense at Home was negative and significant (marginal effect: -0.003 percentage points per 1% capacity increase), meaning home defenses committed fewer defensive penalties with larger crowds, while away defenses showed no significant crowd effect. Robustness checks with empty/near-empty dummies confirmed: empty or close-to-empty stadiums were associated with significantly more defensive penalties called against the home defense (interaction coefficients negative and significant). Quarterback absence did not explain findings.
limitations: Study relies on the COVID-19 natural experiment, which introduced confounders beyond crowd absence (changed training regimes, isolation protocols, backup QB usage). Cannot perfectly identify individual QB fixed effects due to data limitations, though offense team fixed effects are used as a proxy. Crowd noise is the assumed mechanism but cannot be directly measured. The study covers only three NFL seasons and only pre-snap non-discretionary penalties; findings may not generalize to all penalty types or other sports. The 2021 season was excluded due to rule changes (17-game season).
practicalImplications: Crowd noise confers a measurable defensive advantage for home teams by disrupting the visiting Quarterback's ability to use cadence manipulation. NFL teams seeking to maximize home advantage should actively encourage crowd noise on defensive snaps, particularly on 3rd and 4th down situations where cadence deception is most impactful. Away teams preparing for loud road environments should dedicate practice time to simulating crowd noise to maintain cadence communication with the offensive line.
athleteDev: Effective communication between a Quarterback and his offense under crowd noise conditions is a trainable skill. Offensive linemen and skill players should practice snap count recognition under noise during training camp and in-season preparation for loud away environments. Teams that rely heavily on no-huddle or up-tempo offenses are particularly dependent on this communication skill.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play clinical decision-making. However, the study demonstrates that environmental stressors (crowd noise) measurably impact player execution of pre-snap communication tasks, suggesting that rehabilitation and return-to-play protocols for players in communication-critical roles (e.g., Quarterbacks, offensive linemen) should consider cognitive and communication readiness under simulated game-environment stressors.

citation: Bergstein VE, Lu AZ, Hodgens BH, Bodendorfer BM, DeFroda SF, Kaplan J, Aiyer AA. Fantasy football points capture performance declines in National Football League offensive skill players following an ankle injury. J Orthop. 2024. DOI: 10.1016/j.jor.2024.03.030.
abstract: This retrospective study used publicly available databases (Pro Football Reference; FantasyData) to evaluate the impact of ankle injuries on NFL offensive skill player performance using fantasy football points as a composite performance metric. Among 303 offensive skill players who sustained ankle sprains, high ankle sprains, or bone bruises from 2009-2020, average fantasy points per game (PPG) and total season fantasy points decreased significantly in the season following injury (p<0.0001). Route runners (running backs, wide receivers, tight ends) showed significant decreases across all performance metrics. Quarterbacks showed no significant performance decline except reduced games played and fewer interceptions. RTP time and injury type did not significantly influence PPG or total fantasy points after adjustment.
tldr: Ankle injuries cause significant, measurable performance declines in NFL offensive skill players that persist through the following season, particularly in route runners (RBs, WRs, TEs). Fantasy football points serve as a validated composite performance metric that effectively captures these multidimensional declines. Quarterbacks are relatively unaffected in terms of passing production. These findings should inform RTP decision-making and rehabilitation protocols targeting the biomechanical demands unique to route-running positions.
methods: Retrospective cohort study using Pro Football Reference injury data (2009-2020 seasons) to identify offensive skill players with ankle sprains, high ankle sprains, or bone bruises. Exclusions: multiple injuries in one season, <3 games played or zero fantasy points pre-injury, no return to play. 303 players included (17 QBs, 106 RBs, 55 TEs, 125 WRs). Fantasy points and position-specific metrics collected from FantasyData for the season before and after the injury year. Each injured player matched 1:1 to a healthy control by position, age, and BMI. Paired T-tests compared pre/post performance; ANCOVA assessed effects of RTP time and injury type. Post-hoc Bonferroni correction applied for injury type comparisons.
findings: Overall injured cohort: games played decreased from 13.25 to 9.89 (p<0.0001); PPG decreased from 6.20 to 4.77 (p<0.0001); total season fantasy points decreased from 85.15 to 62.97 (p<0.0001). Matched controls showed no significant changes. Route runners showed significant decreases in all metrics: rush yards, receptions, receiving yards, touchdowns, PPG, and total fantasy points (all p<0.0001). QBs showed no significant decline in passing yards, TDs, rush yards, or PPG (all p>0.10); only games played (p=0.0033) and interceptions (p=0.029) decreased. RTP time and injury type both confounded games-played changes but did not significantly affect PPG or total fantasy points after ANCOVA adjustment.
limitations: Retrospective design allows only associations, not causation. No access to NFL electronic medical records; injury data derived from media-reported NFL injury reports (possible under- or over-reporting). Fantasy football points are not a previously validated composite performance measure and involve differential weighting of performance components. Players with injuries prior to the 2009 study start date were not tracked. Mechanism of injury (contact vs. non-contact) and playing conditions were not available in the database. Some ankle injuries may have been classified as 'other' in the database, potentially undercounting ankle injuries.
practicalImplications: Ankle injuries produce significant season-long performance decrements in NFL route runners that are not fully explained by severity or RTP time, suggesting residual neuromuscular or proprioceptive deficits after clinical clearance. Medical staff should consider extended rehabilitation protocols specifically targeting ankle stability, proprioception, and the biomechanical demands of cutting and route running. Return-to-play criteria may need to incorporate position-specific functional performance benchmarks beyond standard clinical milestones.
athleteDev: The high prevalence of ankle injuries — with 53.2% of NFL Combine attendees having prior ankle injury history — underscores the importance of ankle injury prevention programming throughout a player's development. Running backs, tight ends, and wide receivers face the highest ankle injury rates and show the greatest performance impact, making them priority populations for preventive ankle strengthening, proprioceptive training, and load management strategies.
rtp: Route runners (RBs, WRs, TEs) demonstrate significant declines in every tracked performance metric in the season following ankle injury regardless of RTP timing or injury type, suggesting that current RTP protocols may be insufficient to restore full functional capacity in these athletes. Future research should focus on optimizing NFL rehabilitation protocols specifically for route runners, potentially incorporating performance-based RTP criteria using position-specific on-field metrics or composite measures like fantasy points as outcome benchmarks.

citation: Eichner ER. Fatal Exertional Heat Stroke in Football: The Coaches Are the Culprits. Curr Sports Med Rep. 2019;18(7):251-252.
abstract: This commentary reviews fatal exertional heat stroke (EHS) cases in college football from 2014–2018, arguing that reckless coach-mandated conditioning drills—particularly high-intensity sprints for large linemen in the heat on early training days—are the primary cause. Four college football player deaths and two severe near-fatal cases are described in detail, with core temperatures at hospital ranging from 105°F to 108°F. The author advocates for ice-water immersion as the immediate treatment, with a 100% survival rate documented at the Falmouth Road Race over 18 years. The article concludes that fatal EHS in football is preventable and that coaches bear primary responsibility for creating the conditions that lead to these deaths.
tldr: Fatal exertional heat stroke in football is entirely preventable, yet since 2000 it has killed over 40 high school and at least 10 college players. Nearly every death occurs not during game play but during coach-mandated conditioning drills. Large linemen forced to run high-volume sprints on early training days in heat are at extreme risk because their bodies generate heat far faster than they can dissipate it. Immediate cold-water immersion is life-saving; the key is early recognition (fuzzy thinking, erratic behavior) before physical collapse occurs.
methods: Narrative commentary and case review. The author describes six detailed case vignettes of college football players who suffered EHS during conditioning drills (2013–2018), summarizes epidemiological data on EHS deaths in high school and college football since 2000, and references evidence on cold-water immersion efficacy from the Falmouth Road Race (274 cases over 18 years, 100% survival).
findings: Since 2000, EHS has killed more than 40 high school football players and at least 10 college football players, almost all during conditioning rather than game play. Four college football players died from EHS in the five years preceding publication (2014–2018). Core temperatures at hospital ranged from 106°F to 108°F in fatal cases. Two survivors experienced severe multi-organ failure (liver transplant, rhabdomyolysis with CK ~1,000,000 IU/L, prolonged ICU stays). Immediate cold-water immersion at the Falmouth Road Race achieved a 100% survival rate across 274 EHS cases. Cooling is effective even with full football gear in place.
limitations: This is an opinion/commentary piece, not a systematic study. Case data are drawn from media reports and published case series rather than a controlled data set. No incidence rates are formally calculated. The causal attribution to coaches is the author's interpretation rather than a formal epidemiological finding.
practicalImplications: Coaches must eliminate or radically modify high-volume sprint conditioning sessions for large linemen, especially on day 1 of preseason in the heat. Staff should 'bird-dog the big guys,' watching for earliest EHS signs (confusion, odd behavior) and act before collapse. Ice-water immersion tubs must be immediately accessible at all practices. Coaches and athletic trainers must be trained to recognize EHS early and act without delay, as >90-minute delays to hospital treatment resulted in death.
athleteDev: Large-bodied linemen are at disproportionate EHS risk because their high mass generates heat rapidly while their surface-area-to-mass ratio limits heat dissipation. Conditioning programs for these athletes must be designed around this physiological reality. Volume and intensity of early-preseason sprinting must be appropriate for body size, fitness level, heat acclimatization status, and ambient conditions. Progressive acclimatization protocols should replace single high-intensity conditioning sessions at the start of camp.
rtp: Athletes who survive EHS face prolonged recovery from multi-organ damage, rhabdomyolysis, renal and liver injury, and neurological sequelae. Return to sport after severe EHS requires formal heat tolerance testing before clearance, as demonstrated in one case where a survivor was deemed medically unfit to return to college football despite recovery of fitness. Staff should be aware that even surviving athletes may not be cleared for full football participation.

citation: McCormick WF, Lomis MJ, Yeager MT, Tsavaris NJ, Rogers CD. Field Surface Type and Season-Ending Lower Extremity Injury in NFL Players. Transl Sports Med. 2024;2024:6832213.
abstract: This study compared the incidence of in-game season-ending lower extremity injuries on natural and artificial playing surfaces in the NFL during the 2020, 2021, and 2022 seasons. Using publicly available data from multiple sports reference databases, researchers identified 199 season-ending lower extremity injuries across 891 games. Injury categories included knee ligament tears (ACL, PCL, LCL, MCL), knee tendon tears, leg/ankle fractures, and Achilles tendon ruptures. Injury rates were calculated per game played on each surface and compared using two-sample T-tests. No statistically significant difference in season-ending lower extremity injury rates was found between artificial turf (0.242/game) and natural turf (0.199/game) when accounting for the greater number of games played on artificial surfaces.
tldr: Despite widespread perception that artificial turf causes more serious injuries, this three-season NFL study found no statistically significant difference in season-ending lower extremity injury rates between artificial and natural turf when injury counts are normalized for games played on each surface. Artificial turf did account for 60% of injuries, but it also hosted 56% of games. The apparent elevated raw injury count on turf reflects greater utilization, not inherently greater risk. Surface hardness, player preference, and specific injury subtypes may still warrant continued monitoring.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological study using publicly available NFL injury data from the 2020, 2021, and 2022 regular seasons and preseason games. Season-ending injuries involving the knee, lower leg, or ankle were identified through fantasydata.com, pro-football-reference.com, spotrac.com, ESPN.com, and NFL.com. Field surface at each stadium was classified as artificial (any percentage of artificial fiber) or natural grass (100% natural). Only in-game injuries were included; practice, off-season, and postseason injuries were excluded. Two-sample T-tests compared injury rates per game between surfaces for each season and the combined three-year period.
findings: Over three seasons, 199 season-ending lower extremity injuries occurred across 891 games. Of these, 79 (39.7%) occurred on natural turf and 120 (60.3%) on artificial turf. Games were distributed as 396 on natural turf (44.4%) and 495 on artificial turf (55.6%). Injury rate per game: natural turf 0.199 (95% CI 0.155–0.241), artificial turf 0.242 (95% CI 0.191–0.275). Two-tailed T-tests produced p-values of 0.72 (2020), 0.90 (2021), 0.19 (2022), and 0.25 for the full three-year period—none statistically significant. Knee ligament tears were the most common season-ending injury on both surfaces each year.
limitations: Only three seasons of data were analyzed, limiting statistical power for subcategorization by injury type. The study used exclusively publicly available data, preventing contact vs. non-contact injury classification. Hybrid surfaces were classified as artificial, which may not fully reflect playing characteristics. T-tests do not control for confounding variables such as team, weather, game phase, or player position. Season-ending injuries represent only a small fraction of total NFL injuries, so findings do not generalize to all injury types.
practicalImplications: The oft-cited narrative that playing on artificial turf inherently causes more serious injuries is not supported when accounting for the volume of play on each surface. Organizations making field surface decisions should not rely solely on raw injury counts. However, monitoring for surface hardness and specific injury subcategories (e.g., ACL tears, non-contact injuries) should continue, as subgroup differences may exist. Player-reported preferences regarding surface feel and playability should also factor into surface selection even when not quantifiable in injury data.
athleteDev: For NFL athletes training and competing on artificial turf, the overall risk of a season-ending lower extremity injury does not appear statistically higher than on natural grass when exposure is equated. Strength and conditioning staff can use this data to contextualize player concerns without dismissing them. Individual surface variation (hardness, drainage, fiber type) may affect injury risk more than surface classification alone, suggesting ongoing monitoring at the facility level is warranted.
rtp: No surface-specific return-to-play protocols are indicated by this data, as rates of season-ending injuries (ACL tears, Achilles ruptures, fractures) did not significantly differ between surfaces. Clinicians should not modify RTP timelines based solely on the surface on which an injury occurred. Future research incorporating contact mechanism, surface subtype, and non-contact injury classification would provide more actionable RTP guidance.

citation: Orchard JW, Chaker Jomaa M, Orchard JJ, Rae K, Hoffman DT, Reddin T, Driscoll T. Fifteen-week window for recurrent muscle strains in football: a prospective cohort of 3600 muscle strains over 23 years in professional Australian rules football. Br J Sports Med. 2020;54(10):1185-1190.
abstract: This study analyzed 23 years of Australian Football League (AFL) injury data (1992–2014) to quantify recurrence risk and identify risk factors for the four major muscle strain types—hamstring, quadriceps, calf, and groin. A total of 3,647 muscle strains occurred across 272,759 player matches. Binary logistic regression identified recent injury history (within 8 weeks) as the dominant risk factor for all four strain types. Week-by-week recurrence rate analysis demonstrated that elevated reinjury risk follows a logarithmic decay pattern and persists for at least 15 weeks after return to play for hamstring and quadriceps strains, 18 weeks for calf, and 19 weeks for groin. First-match-back recurrence rates were 9% (hamstring), 5% (quadriceps), 2% (calf), and 6% (groin).
tldr: After a muscle strain, a footballer's recurrence risk is dramatically elevated—9% for hamstrings, 6% for groin, and 5% for quadriceps in the very first match back—and this elevated risk does not fully resolve for approximately 15 weeks following return to play. Prior injury to the same muscle is by far the strongest predictor, with adjusted odds ratios of 13–25x for recent injury history. This 15-week window where players are 'carrying' a prior injury represents a critical management period that spans more than half an AFL season and should inform return-to-play decision-making and monitoring in all football codes.
methods: Prospective cohort study using the AFL Injury Surveillance System database from 1992 to 2014. The unit of analysis was the 'player match' (n = 272,759). Muscle strains causing a missed match were classified as hamstring, quadriceps, calf, or groin. Multivariable binary logistic regression with forward stepwise selection identified independent risk factors including recent history (≤8 weeks), non-recent history (>8 weeks) of each strain type, histories of ACL/cartilage/ankle/concussion/lumbar injuries, age (dichotomized at 24 years), indigenous race, match level, and substitute rule era (2011–2014). Week-by-week recurrence rates were plotted and fit with logarithmic trendlines to determine when risk returned to baseline.
findings: 3,647 muscle strains (1,932 hamstring, 418 quadriceps, 458 calf, 839 groin) occurred across 272,759 player matches. Recent history (≤8 weeks) was the strongest risk factor: adjusted ORs of 13.1 (hamstring), 13.3 (calf), 25.2 (quadriceps), and 20.6 (groin). Non-recent history ORs: 3.5 (hamstring), 4.4 (calf), 5.2 (quadriceps), 3.5 (groin). First-match-back recurrence rates: 9% hamstring, 5% quadriceps, 2% calf, 6% groin. Elevated recurrence risk persisted for 15 weeks (hamstring, quadriceps), 18 weeks (calf), and 19 weeks (groin). Recent hamstring injury elevated subsequent quadriceps (OR 1.8) and calf strain risk (OR 1.8). Age was an independent risk factor for calf strains only (OR 1.6 for age ≥24). Indigenous race was associated with hamstring strain. During the substitute rule era (2011–2014), hamstring, quadriceps, and groin strains decreased but calf strains increased.
limitations: Injury side was not analyzed due to incomplete recording. Specific muscle within each group (e.g., biceps femoris vs. semimembranosus) was not distinguished. Injury history prior to AFL listing was not captured. Player workload, sprint volume, and real-time movement data were not included, despite being known risk factors. Repeated inclusion of each player across multiple matches introduces dependency between observations, potentially narrowing confidence intervals. Sparse data bias is possible given the large number of binary risk factor combinations. The AFL plays one match per week, limiting generalizability to codes with greater fixture congestion.
practicalImplications: Return-to-play clearance after muscle strain should not be treated as a binary event. The 15-week elevated risk window means players require careful monitoring and potentially modified load management well beyond symptom resolution and initial return to training. Staff should communicate this extended risk window to athletes and coaches when making RTP decisions. Recurrence in the first match back (up to 9% for hamstrings) is sufficiently high to justify conservative load progression protocols for the initial weeks after return.
athleteDev: For football athletes at all levels, a prior muscle strain is the single most powerful risk factor for future strain—far outweighing age, workload, or other variables in this dataset. Development programs should implement longitudinal injury tracking to flag athletes with prior strain history for targeted screening, prehabilitation, and individualized load planning. Cross-muscle risk (hamstring strain elevating quadriceps and calf risk) suggests monitoring adjacent muscle groups after any lower limb strain. Younger players (under 24) are relatively protected from calf strains, but not from hamstring or groin strains.
rtp: The data provide a quantitative framework for RTP decision-making: hamstring and quadriceps risk remains elevated for 15 weeks post-return, calf for 18 weeks, and groin for 19 weeks. The highest single-match recurrence risk is in the first game back (up to 9% for hamstring). RTP protocols should incorporate progressive return with enhanced monitoring across this entire window. Clinicians should explicitly inform athletes and coaches that RTP does not equal full biological resolution of injury risk, and that the player is 'carrying' the injury throughout the 15-week window.

citation: Weber AE, Bolia IK, Korber S, Mayfield CK, Lindsay A, Rosen J, McMannes S, Romano R, Tibone JE, Gamradt SC. Five-Year Surveillance of Vitamin D Levels in NCAA Division I Football Players: Risk Factors for Failed Supplementation. Orthop J Sports Med. 2021;9(1):2325967120975100.
abstract: This study reports results of a 5-year vitamin D surveillance program in 272 NCAA Division I football players at USC between 2012 and 2017. Serum 25(OH)D levels were measured annually at the start of each season; athletes with insufficiency (<32 ng/mL) received vitamin D3 alone (50,000 IU/week) or combined D3/D2. The prevalence of vitamin D insufficiency decreased significantly from 55.5% in 2012–2013 to 30.7% in 2016–2017 (P=.033), and mean 25(OH)D rose from 36.3 to 40.5 ng/mL (P<.001). Black athletes had the lowest mean vitamin D levels; quarterbacks had the highest. Among athletes with insufficiency who completed 12 months of supplementation, vitamin D3 alone had a significantly higher conversion rate (48.1%) than combined D3/D2 (22.2%; P=.034).
tldr: Nearly half of NCAA Division I football players begin each season with insufficient vitamin D levels, but a structured annual screening and supplementation program can cut that prevalence by more than half over five years. Black athletes have consistently lower vitamin D levels and higher rates of supplementation failure. Vitamin D3 alone is significantly more effective than combined D3/D2 therapy for restoring sufficient levels. Annual testing at the start of preseason, with D3-only supplementation for those found insufficient, is the evidence-supported approach for this population.
methods: Prospective cohort study; Level of evidence 3. Serum 25(OH)D was measured annually at the start of each season (June–August) from 2012 to 2017 in all athletes on the USC football roster (n=272 total analyzed). Insufficiency was defined as <32 ng/mL. Insufficient athletes received vitamin D3 (50,000 IU/week) or combined D3 (2,000 IU/day) + D2 (50,000 IU/week) per the treating physician's preference. Program efficacy was assessed by comparing insufficiency prevalence in the first two vs. last two years. Subgroup analysis of athletes with complete 12-month supplementation data evaluated factors associated with failed supplementation (failure to convert to ≥32 ng/mL) using Fisher exact and chi-square tests for categorical variables including age, BMI, race, position, and supplement type.
findings: Prevalence of vitamin D insufficiency decreased from 55.5% (2012–2013) to 30.7% (2016–2017; P=.033). Mean 25(OH)D rose from 36.3 ng/mL (2012) to 40.5 ng/mL (2017; P<.001), though the increase was nonlinear. By the final year, returning athletes had 16.2% insufficiency vs. 55.6% among incoming athletes (P<.001). White/non-Hispanic athletes had the highest mean 25(OH)D (43.47 ng/mL); Black athletes had the lowest (31.02 ng/mL; P<.001). Quarterbacks had the highest positional mean (47.48 ng/mL); running backs had the lowest (30.7 ng/mL; P<.001). In the supplementation subgroup (n=82), conversion success: D3 alone 48.1% vs. D3/D2 combined 22.2% (P=.034). No significant differences in age, BMI, race, or position between successful and failed converters.
limitations: Athlete compliance with supplementation relied on nutritionist administration but could not be fully guaranteed. Two supplementation regimens were used based on physician preference rather than randomization, introducing selection bias. Sun exposure at time of testing was not recorded, which could significantly affect baseline levels. Players who left the team after initial testing were not tracked. Prior supplementation history was not recorded. Subgroup analysis for failed supplementation was limited to athletes with complete data (n=82), reducing statistical power for race and position subanalyses. No clinical outcomes (injury rates, performance) were measured.
practicalImplications: Football programs should implement annual preseason vitamin D screening for all athletes. Vitamin D3 monotherapy (50,000 IU/week) should be the preferred supplementation protocol over combined D3/D2 regimens based on superior conversion rates. Black athletes warrant close monitoring and potentially more aggressive supplementation given consistently lower baseline levels and higher rates of supplementation failure. Incoming freshmen and transfers are at particularly high risk of insufficiency and should be prioritized for early testing and treatment. Compliance infrastructure (e.g., nutritionist-administered supplementation) improves program effectiveness.
athleteDev: Vitamin D insufficiency is highly prevalent in NCAA Division I football players, particularly among Black athletes, running backs, and incoming athletes. Given associations between vitamin D insufficiency and stress fractures, muscle strains, hormonal imbalance, and immune function, maintaining adequate levels is an important component of athlete health management. Programs should build annual screening and supplementation into standard preseason medical workflows, recognizing that new athletes arriving each year will reset the insufficiency prevalence without ongoing surveillance.
rtp: Low vitamin D levels have been associated in other studies with lower extremity muscle strains and core muscle injuries in NFL players. While this study did not measure injury outcomes directly, optimizing vitamin D status through supplementation may contribute to injury prevention and support recovery. Athletes presenting with recurrent lower extremity or core muscle injuries should have vitamin D status assessed as part of their workup. Ensuring sufficient 25(OH)D (≥32 ng/mL) before return to full training is a reasonable clinical consideration.

citation: Bedard G, Lawrence DW. Five-Year Trends in Reported National Football League Injuries. Clin J Sport Med. 2019;29(1):1-6.
abstract: This prospective descriptive epidemiological study analyzed official NFL injury report data across five regular seasons (2012–2016) to determine all-cause injury, concussion, and knee injury incidence trends. A total of 10,934 injuries were identified across 2,460 team games (TGs). The all-cause injury rate was 444.47 per 100 TGs (404.07 per 1,000 athletes at risk [AAR]); concussion incidence was 30.57 per 100 TGs (27.79 per 1,000 AAR). Defensive backs had the highest injury rates of all positions in every season. Significant findings included a decrease in all-cause and knee injury incidence in 2016 vs. 2015, and an increase in concussion incidence in 2015 vs. 2014. These trends were evaluated in context of rule changes implemented between seasons.
tldr: Over five NFL seasons (2012–2016), all-cause injury rates are high but trending downward, with a significant drop in 2016 coinciding with rule changes targeting dangerous blocking and tackling. Concussion rates spiked significantly in 2015—likely reflecting improved detection from new spotter rules and medical timeout protocols rather than a true increase in events. The knee and ankle remain the most frequently injured body areas, and defensive backs sustain the most injuries of any position group. Continued rule changes and concussion education programs are needed across all levels of football.
methods: Prospective descriptive epidemiological study. Official NFL injury reports were collected weekly for all 32 teams across the 2012–2013 through 2016–2017 regular seasons. A new injury was defined as any condition not reported in the prior week's injury report. Concussion was defined as any injury coded as concussion or head injury. Injuries were expressed as counts per team game (TG) and per 1,000 athletes at risk (AAR; 11 players per TG). The Kruskal–Wallis rank-sum test with Tukey and Nemenyi post hoc comparisons was used to assess year-by-year differences. Analysis included 2,460 of 2,560 total possible TGs (96.1%), with Week 17 reports available only for the 12 playoff-qualifying teams.
findings: 10,934 total injuries over 5 seasons; 752 (6.9%) were concussions. Lower extremity injuries accounted for 62.3% of all injuries. Top 5 injury locations: knee (17.2%), ankle (13.6%), shoulder (8.8%), hamstring (8.4%), concussion (6.9%). All-cause injury rate: 444.47/100 TGs (404.07/1,000 AAR). Concussion incidence: 30.57/100 TGs (27.79/1,000 AAR). All-cause injury rates were significantly lower in 2016 vs. 2015 (396.75 vs. 512.20/100 TGs; P<0.001). Knee injury incidence was significantly lower in 2016 vs. 2015 (62.40 vs. 86.38/100 TGs; P=0.029). Concussion incidence increased significantly from 2014 to 2015 (22.97 to 36.59/100 TGs; P=0.012). Defensive backs had the highest all-cause injury count in every season. Kickers/punters had the lowest.
limitations: All injuries were assumed to have occurred during games rather than practice, potentially overestimating game-specific injury burden. Only publicly available team-reported data were used; validity and reliability of team reporting cannot be fully verified despite NFL reporting mandates. New vs. recurrent injuries could not be reliably distinguished. The study is limited to reported injuries and may not capture all actual injuries. Selection bias is possible as Week 17 injury reports were only available for playoff teams. No mechanism (contact vs. non-contact) or severity data beyond injury report status were available.
practicalImplications: The significant reduction in all-cause and knee injuries in 2016 following specific rule changes (elimination of chop blocks, expanded horse collar rule) demonstrates that targeted rule modifications can reduce injury rates at the elite level, with potential downstream influence on rules at the college and high school levels. The spike in concussion reporting in 2015 following implementation of independent athletic trainer spotters and medical timeouts illustrates that improved detection infrastructure increases reported incidence even if true event rates are unchanged—a finding relevant to interpreting injury surveillance data at all levels.
athleteDev: Defensive backs face the highest all-cause injury burden of any position group in the NFL, followed by linebackers, defensive linemen, and offensive linemen. Position-specific injury exposure data can inform targeted prehabilitation, strength and conditioning programming, and load management strategies. The high rate of knee and ankle injuries across all positions reinforces the importance of lower extremity injury prevention programs (e.g., neuromuscular training, ACL prevention protocols) as a standard component of NFL and collegiate football preparation.
rtp: Knee injuries (17.2% of all injuries) and Achilles/tendon injuries are the most frequent season-impacting injuries in the NFL. The year-to-year variability in injury incidence—particularly the significant knee injury reduction in 2016—demonstrates that environmental factors (rules, playing surface) influence RTP population dynamics. Medical staff should track league-wide rule changes that may affect injury mechanisms and adjust clinical protocols and RTP timelines accordingly. The high concussion incidence (6.9% of all injuries, 27.79/1,000 AAR) demands robust concussion protocols and sideline detection infrastructure at all levels of play.

citation: Clements, E., Ehrmann, F., Clark, A., Jones, M., McCall, A., & Duffield, R. (2023). Flight path and scheduling effects on perceived jet lag, fatigue, and sleep in footballers traveling to and from national teams. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. https://doi.org/10.1123/ijspp.2023-0027
abstract: This study examined posttravel perceptual responses of national-team footballers (soccer) following different flight paths, arrival/departure times, and trip contexts. Details of 396 flights from 68 national-team players were obtained. Each player provided ratings of perceptual fatigue, sleep, soreness, stress, and jet lag for 2 days before and after each trip. Linear mixed models compared the pretravel with posttravel change in perceptual responses based on flight path, context, and schedule. Perceived jet-lag ratings were more responsive to travel variables (R2 = .48) than other perceptual ratings (R2 < .26). Asia-to-Europe and Europe-to-Australia travel produced the highest jet-lag ratings. Fatigue and sleep scores were worst following Europe-to-Australia travel. Return travel from national team to club produced poorer perceptual responses than all other contexts. Daytime arrivals (11 AM–5 PM) resulted in better perceptual outcomes than early-morning or late-night arrivals.
tldr: Perceived jet-lag ratings are more sensitive to travel demands than general wellness scales in national-team footballers. Europe-to-Australia and Asia-to-Europe routes are most disruptive. Returning to clubs after national team duties is harder on players than traveling to camp. Scheduling arrivals during daytime (11 AM–5 PM) yields the best perceptual recovery outcomes.
methods: Observational longitudinal study using 396 verified flights from 68 senior male national-team soccer players (March 2018–July 2022). Players completed daily perceptual questionnaires (7-point Likert scales for fatigue, sleep, soreness, stress; 10-point jet-lag scale) for 2 days pre- and up to 3 days post-travel. Flight paths were categorized by departure/arrival continent, trip context (outbound, transition, return), and arrival/departure time windows. Linear mixed models with player as random effect were built stepwise; post hoc pairwise comparisons used estimated marginal means.
findings: Jet-lag scores were most sensitive to travel variables (R2 = .48). Asia-to-Europe (p < .05) and Europe-to-Australia (p < .001) produced highest jet-lag ratings. Fatigue was worst after Asia-to-Europe and Europe-to-Australia. Sleep was worst after Europe-to-Australia. Return travel (national team to club) produced poorer total wellness, fatigue, sleep, and soreness than outbound or transition travel (p < .05). Daytime arrivals yielded significantly lower total wellness, fatigue, sleep, and soreness than morning arrivals; night arrivals produced highest stress scores. Older players reported higher jet lag; more experienced players reported lower jet lag.
limitations: Limited control over athlete behavior before, during, or after travel. Low R2 values for wellness scales suggest external factors beyond travel affect scores. Pretravel baseline used a single-day measure, increasing susceptibility to noise. Perceived jet-lag ratings collected at a single daily time point. Insufficient Australia-to-Europe trip data due to match scheduling and COVID-related venue changes.
practicalImplications: Use jet-lag ratings (not just wellness scales) to monitor national-team travel stress. Prioritize travel management interventions for Europe-to-Australia and Asia-to-Europe routes. Provide additional recovery support when athletes return to clubs post-camp. Where logistically possible, schedule arrivals between 11 AM and 5 PM. Develop individualized travel strategies for younger or less-experienced players who show greater jet-lag vulnerability.
athleteDev: Travel load accumulation during multi-trip national team windows can significantly impair perceived recovery. Development of travel literacy and routine (sleep hygiene, light exposure, nutrition timing) in younger national-team players may reduce jet-lag susceptibility over time.
rtp: Return travel after national-team camp represents a distinct recovery challenge beyond the match itself. Practitioners managing athletes returning from international duty should factor in travel-induced fatigue layered on top of match load when planning RTP timelines and training loads in the days following return.

citation: Park, G., & Kent, R. (2020). Foot shape analysis of professional American Football players. Footwear Science. https://doi.org/10.1080/19424280.2020.1769203
abstract: This study quantified the foot shape of NFL players to identify characteristics that inform position-specific footwear design. Statistical shape analysis (SSA) was performed on 3D scans of 290 NFL players' feet from 4 teams during preseason training camps. Eight foot shape measurements were defined to reflect the principal components (PCs) of shape variability. The first three PCs explained 82.3% of foot shape variation: foot volume/size (34.4%), medial longitudinal arch shape with foot length (26.2%), and foot curvature (21.7%). Mean foot length was 293 ± 13.7 mm; width 110.6 ± 6.8 mm. Significant between-position differences were observed primarily in size-related measures. Flat foot prevalence (zero arch depth) was higher in linemen and hybrid groups than skill players.
tldr: NFL players' feet vary most in overall size, arch shape, and curvature—factors that conventional Brannock devices do not fully capture. Linemen and hybrid players are larger in all dimensions and more likely to have flat feet than skill players. This data should inform position-specific last design and footwear selection to optimize fit, performance, and injury prevention at the elite level.
methods: A subset of 290 left-foot 3D scans (from 1,451 total scanned across all 32 NFL teams) was extracted from 4 teams during preseason training camps. Scans were collected with a laser foot scanner (INFOOT USB IFU-S-01) at 1 mm resolution, processed in Meshmixer v3.5. Correlation-based PCA was performed on 240 pseudo-landmarks from 8 transverse cross-sectional contours. Eight foot measurements (foot length, width, arch length, heel width, mid-foot width, ball girth, arch depth, flare angle) were defined and compared across three position groups (linemen, n = 83; hybrid, n = 68; skill, n = 135) using statistical comparisons.
findings: First three PCs explained 82.3% of shape variation: PC1 = foot volume/size (34.4%), PC2 = arch shape with foot length (26.2%), PC3 = foot curvature (21.7%). All measurements except arch depth and flare angle differed significantly between position groups (p < .05). Linemen had the largest feet overall (length 303.7 mm, width 117.0 mm, ball girth 277.0 mm). Flat foot prevalence (arch depth = 0) was greater in linemen and hybrid groups than skill players. Longer feet associated with lower arch height, consistent with prior literature.
limitations: Only left-foot scans were analyzed. Sample was a subset of 4 teams from the full 32-team database, though weight and height distributions were comparable to the full NFL population. Kickers and punters were excluded due to insufficient scan numbers. Scans captured static unloaded foot shape; dynamic loading during play may alter effective foot dimensions.
practicalImplications: NFL footwear design should be position-specific, accounting for differences in foot size and arch shape between linemen, hybrid, and skill players. Flat-foot prevalence in linemen and hybrid players supports dedicated arch support and last modifications for those groups. The 8 measurements identified here provide a practical framework for footwear fitting systems beyond the traditional Brannock device.
athleteDev: Understanding position-specific foot morphology in American football players can guide footwear selection during player development pipelines. Proper footwear fit improves comfort, performance, and reduces injury risk from conditions such as fifth metatarsal fractures, turf toe, Lisfranc injuries, and blisters, which are relevant across developmental and elite levels.
rtp: Foot shape data can inform orthotics and footwear modifications for athletes returning from foot and ankle injuries. Position-specific arch and curvature profiles should be considered when prescribing return-to-play footwear to ensure mechanical support is appropriate for the demands of the player's position.

citation: Cosio, P. L., Moreno-Simonet, L., Fernández, D., Lloret, M., Padulles, X., Padulles, J. M., Farran-Codina, A., Rodas, G., & Cadefau, J. A. (2024). Football (soccer) match-derived hamstring muscles residual fatigue can be monitored using early rate of torque development. European Journal of Applied Physiology. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00421-024-05694-x
abstract: This study investigated whether a soccer match affects the rapid force-generating capacity of the hamstring muscles using rate of torque development (RTD) and serum biomarkers. Nineteen male, regional first-division soccer players (age 20.9 ± 2.0 years) were assessed before and at 24, 48, and 72 hours post-match. Hamstring MVIC torque and RTD were measured via the 90°hip:20°knee isometric posterior chain (IPC) test. Serum biomarkers included CK, sMtCK, transaminases, MDA, and irisin. Early RTD (0–50 ms, 0–100 ms) remained significantly reduced at MD+3 in both dominant and non-dominant hamstrings (p < 0.001, g = −1.24 and −1.06). MVIC torque was unrecovered at MD+2 but recovered at MD+3. Neuromuscular deficits were coupled with elevated myocyte structural and mitochondrial damage biomarkers persisting through MD+3.
tldr: Early rate of torque development (RTD at 0–50 ms and 0–100 ms) is a more sensitive indicator of hamstring residual fatigue than MVIC torque alone after a soccer match. Hamstring rapid force-generating capacity is not fully recovered within 3 days post-match. The 90°hip:20°knee IPC test provides a practical, portable, cost-effective tool for monitoring hamstring recovery during congested schedules, and findings support the IOC recommendation of at least 96 hours between matches to allow adequate recovery.
methods: Single-group repeated measures design in 19 male regional first-division soccer players. Neuromuscular assessments (MVIC torque and RTD at 0–50, 0–100, 0–250 ms and peak RTD) were performed using the 90°hip:20°knee IPC test with a custom rigid strain gauge before the match (MD), and at MD+1 (24h), MD+2 (48h), and MD+3 (72h). Match load was monitored via 10-Hz IMU devices. Serum biomarkers (CK, CK/ALT, CK/AST, sMtCK, MDA, irisin) were collected at each time point. Statistical analysis included two-way repeated measures ANOVA for neuromuscular data and one-way ANOVA for biomarkers, with post hoc Bonferroni-corrected paired t-tests.
findings: Early RTD0–50 and RTD0–100 remained significantly reduced at MD+3 (p < 0.001, g = −1.24 and −1.06) in both dominant and non-dominant hamstrings. MVIC torque was unrecovered at MD+2 (p = 0.004, g = −0.91; p = 0.002, g = −0.98) but recovered by MD+3. RTD0–250 and RTDpeak did not change significantly. Normalized RTD (expressed as % MVIC torque/s) also showed significant decreases at 0–50 and 0–100 ms intervals through MD+3. CK peaked at MD+1 (+499%), remained elevated through MD+3 (+124%). sMtCK, CK/ALT, CK/AST, MDA, and irisin were all elevated through MD+3, confirming sustained structural and oxidative muscle damage.
limitations: The strain gauge had a low baseline noise amplitude and lacked 1000 Hz sampling frequency as recommended by Maffiuletti et al. (2016); however, recent evidence shows this does not substantially affect reliability at the frequencies used. Small sample size (n = 19) in a single competitive standard (regional first division). Monitoring was limited to 72 hours; full recovery timeline beyond 72 hours was not captured. Individual nutritional intake between meals was not fully controlled despite dietary standardization around the match.
practicalImplications: Use the 90°hip:20°knee IPC test and early RTD metrics (0–50 ms, 0–100 ms) as a non-exhaustive, cost-effective, portable tool to track hamstring recovery in soccer players during congested schedules. Early RTD deficits persist beyond MVIC recovery and represent a distinct, clinically relevant neuromuscular target. Practitioners should not rely on MVIC torque alone when clearing players from post-match hamstring fatigue, particularly given the risk of hamstring strain injury during sprinting with impaired rapid force production.
athleteDev: Monitoring hamstring rapid force-generating capacity through RTD is important for long-term athlete management in soccer. Developing hamstring strengthening programs that target rapid force production (e.g., flywheel, sprint-specific eccentric work) may help buffer post-match RTD decrements and reduce hamstring strain injury incidence across high-volume developmental and professional schedules.
rtp: Early RTD at 0–50 ms and 0–100 ms should be incorporated into hamstring RTP criteria following hamstring strain injury, as these metrics are more sensitive indicators of residual neuromuscular deficit than peak torque alone. Players returning to full match participation within 72 hours of a prior match face elevated hamstring injury risk due to unresolved rapid force-generating deficits and persistent biochemical markers of muscle damage.

citation: Schuth, G., Szigeti, G., Dobreff, G., Pašić, A., Gabbett, T., Szilas, A., & Pavlik, G. (2024). Football movement profile-based creatine kinase prediction performs similarly to GPS derived machine learning models in National team soccer players. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. https://doi.org/10.1123/ijspp.2024-0077
abstract: This study compared GPS/IMA-derived and Football Movement Profile (FMP)-derived machine learning models for predicting next-day creatine kinase (CK) in national-team soccer players, and compared general versus individualized prediction models. A total of 444 national-team male soccer players (U15 to senior) were monitored during 2019–2022 with 10-Hz GPS units, and morning CK was measured from whole blood, yielding 8,570 data points. Machine learning models were built using GPS+IMA, FMP, or combined parameters. The FMP model (R2 = .60, RMSE = 144.6 U/L) performed similarly to GPS (R2 = .62, RMSE = 141.2 U/L) and the combination (R2 = .62, RMSE = 140.3 U/L). General models outperformed individualized models (R2 = .57 vs .37 on average).
tldr: Six FMP parameters (standing, walking, medium running, high running, medium dynamic, high dynamic durations) can predict next-day CK with accuracy comparable to GPS and inertial sensor-derived machine learning models in national-team soccer players. Because FMP is based on inertial sensors rather than GPS satellites, it works indoors and in large stadiums and allows live monitoring during training and matches. General population-level models outperform individualized models when large datasets are available.
methods: Observational study (2019–2022) in 444 elite male national-team soccer players (U15 to senior; age 19.2 ± 4.7 years). GPS (10 Hz, Catapult S7/G7) and capillary blood CK (morning fasted, Reflotron Plus and Tascom SimplexTas 101) were collected across 4 training camp schedule types. Machine learning models (linear regression, ridge, lasso, gradient boosting, random forest) were built using GPS+IMA, FMP, or combined parameters to predict absolute next-day CK. Walk-forward cross-validation (6-fold, time-ordered) was used. Individualized models were built for 161 players with ≥20 data points. SHAP values characterized feature importance.
findings: FMP-only model: R2 = .60, MAE = 88.2 U/L, RMSE = 144.6 U/L, MAPE = 0.346. GPS-only: R2 = .62, MAE = 85.6 U/L, RMSE = 141.1 U/L. GPS+FMP combined: R2 = .62, MAE = 85.3 U/L, RMSE = 140.3 U/L. Differences between the three input sets were negligible. Actual CK was the top predictor (SHAP). FMP dynamic duration metrics (medium-high, high) were the most important FMP features. General models outperformed individualized models in 73% of cases (R2 .57–.62 vs .37). Players with >70 data points showed improved individual model performance but still did not surpass general model accuracy.
limitations: Wide age range (14–33 years) may introduce biological maturity confounds, though most U15 players were post-peak height velocity. CK measurement systems changed mid-study; a correction equation was developed and validated. Individual nutrition between standardized meals could not be fully controlled. Recovery protocols (ice bath, contrast bath) were standardized but may have influenced CK kinetics. Time between last session and morning CK draw could not be precisely controlled.
practicalImplications: Practitioners can use FMP-based monitoring (6 simple categories) as a practical alternative to GPS for predicting next-day muscle damage when GPS is unavailable (indoors, large stadiums). Live FMP monitoring enables immediate feedback to coaches, allowing real-time load adjustments. General population models with large datasets are more reliable than individualized models for most players, though individualized models may be worth pursuing if >70 matched data points per player are available.
athleteDev: FMP-based CK prediction can assist national team staff in managing training load during congested camp schedules across all age groups (U15 to senior). Identifying players at elevated CK risk based on their movement profile enables proactive periodization and reduced injury exposure during developmental national team programs.
rtp: CK prediction models can inform post-match recovery protocols and return-to-train decisions in congested national team schedules. By predicting next-day muscle damage from match or training load data, staff can identify players who may not tolerate full training intensity on subsequent days, supporting structured RTP progressions following muscle damage events.

citation: Ashley, C. D., Lopez, R. M., & Tritsch, A. J. (2023). Football practices in hot environments impact subsequent days' hydration. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. https://doi.org/10.1519/JSC.0000000000004600
abstract: This study examined the accuracy of urinary hydration measures and carryover effects of consecutive outdoor football practices in hot conditions (mean WBGT = 31.3 ± 1.8°C) in 31 male high school football players (age 16 ± 1 years) across 7 outdoor practices over 2 preseason weeks. Urine color (Ucol) and urine-specific gravity (USG) were compared against percent body mass loss (%BML) using ROC analysis. Neither Ucol nor USG was a valid standalone predictor of ≥2% BML (AUC = 0.55 and 0.49; sensitivity/specificity both ~60%/46%). However, Ucol accurately identified USG > 1.020 (AUC = 0.85). Significant carryover effects of Ucol were found between practices with less than 24 hours recovery, including 2-a-day sessions. %BML was significantly greater after 2-a-day practices.
tldr: Back-to-back and twice-daily football practices in hot conditions produce cumulative hydration deficits measurable via urine color carryover. Neither urine color nor urine-specific gravity alone reliably detects ≥2% body mass loss in this population, though urine color effectively tracks USG. Athletes must actively replenish fluids during and between sessions—especially during 2-a-day preseason structures—to maintain euhydration and reduce exertional heat illness risk.
methods: Observational field study in 31 male high school football players during the first 2 weeks of preseason (7 outdoor practices analyzed; mean WBGT 31.3 ± 1.8°C). Pre- and post-practice urine samples were collected for Ucol (8-color Armstrong scale) and USG (refractometry). Seminude body mass was measured pre and post each practice to calculate %BML. ROC curve analysis assessed Ucol and USG sensitivity/specificity for detecting ≥2% BML. Paired-samples t-tests assessed postpractice to next prepractice carryover. Repeated-measures ANOVA assessed carryover trends across all practices.
findings: ROC analysis: AUC for Ucol to detect ≥2% BML = 0.55 (p = 0.53), USG AUC = 0.49 (p = 0.90); neither was significant. Sensitivity for both = 60%; specificity = 48.8% (Ucol) and 44% (USG). Ucol significantly detected USG > 1.020 (AUC = 0.85, sensitivity 82.5%, specificity 67.9%). Significant Ucol carryover effects between practices with <24 hours recovery (practices 2→3, 6→7AM, 7AM→7PM, 10→11; p ≤ 0.004; effect sizes 0.65–1.10). Significant USG carryover only from practice 2 to 3 (p = 0.011, ES = 0.51). No significant body mass carryover. %BML significantly greater after 2-a-day practices (7PM practice: 1.48 ± 0.75%; practice 10: 1.32 ± 0.72%) versus early practices and baseline (~0.59–0.82%).
limitations: Convenience sample from one high school varsity team limits generalizability. Small sample size (n = 31) may have reduced power to detect smaller carryover effects. Mixed indoor/outdoor practice schedule meant only 7 of 11 practices were analyzed. %BML rarely exceeded 2% in this sample, limiting the utility of testing urinary measures against that threshold. Individual variations in dietary and fluid intake between practices could not be fully controlled.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and strength coaches managing preseason American football in hot environments should track urine color trends across consecutive practice days rather than relying on a single snapshot.
