Fluid replenishment protocols must be reinforced especially after 2-a-day practices and when less than 24 hours separates sessions. Urine color can serve as a practical proxy for USG in field settings without refractometer access. Cumulative hydration deficits across a preseason week are a tangible exertional heat illness risk factor that requires proactive management.
athleteDev: High school and developing football players practicing in hot environments accumulate hydration deficits across back-to-back sessions. Educating young athletes on consistent fluid intake routines—before, during, and after each practice—is a key component of heat illness prevention during preseason development periods. Equipment additions (pads, helmets) added progressively across preseason further increase sweat loss and compound hydration challenges.
rtp: Athletes returning to full-contact or full-load practices after a heat-related illness or in hot preseason environments should be monitored with serial urinary hydration markers (Ucol, USG) and body mass checks across consecutive days. Residual hypohydration from previous practices can impair thermoregulation, increasing risk during RTP progressions in hot conditions; ensuring euhydration before each session is a prerequisite for safe return.

citation: Wusk, Z., & Rowson, S. (2022). Football Shoulder Pad Design and Its Effect on Head Kinematics in Shoulder-to-Helmet Impacts. Annals of Biomedical Engineering. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10439-022-03063-3
abstract: Shoulder-to-helmet (STH) impacts account for approximately 20% of concussions in football, yet no published research had examined football shoulder pad design in relation to STH impacts. This study compared an unmodified Douglas SW24 flat pad (control) to a modified version with added Vinyl Nitrile 600 closed-cell foam at the top flap, interior arch, and shoulder resting point. Twenty participants with varsity-level football experience each performed six impacts against a helmeted Hybrid III dummy equipped with a NOCSAE instrumented head, yielding 111 analyzable trials. The modified shoulder pad produced statistically significant reductions in peak linear acceleration (31%), peak rotational acceleration (28%), and peak rotational velocity (10%), while increasing impact duration by 40%. Impact response corridors were developed for both conditions to support future controlled lab replication of STH impacts.
tldr: Adding foam padding to football shoulder pads meaningfully reduces the head kinematics generated during shoulder-to-helmet impacts. A 31% drop in peak linear acceleration and a 28% drop in rotational acceleration were observed at realistic impact speeds, suggesting that shoulder pad design—an area currently without performance standards—is a viable lever for reducing concussion risk in football. Impact response corridors developed in this study can guide future higher-speed testing to evaluate padding performance at concussive impact levels.
methods: Prospective lab study; 20 male participants with varsity football experience each delivered 6 impacts (3 per pad condition) to the upper-side of a helmeted Hybrid III 50th-percentile dummy suspended at 40 degrees. Participants used a 5-m runway before striking. Head kinematics captured at 20,000 Hz via three linear accelerometers and three angular rate sensors (NOCSAE head). High-speed cameras (960 fps) measured impact speed and contact location. Nine impacts removed due to off-target contact. Paired one-sided t-tests (alpha = 0.05) compared conditions; impact response corridors built using a modified Maltese method.
findings: Modified (foam-added) shoulder pad vs. control: peak linear acceleration reduced 31% (29.4 g to 20.3 g, p<0.001); peak rotational acceleration reduced 28% (1960 to 1396 rad/s², p<0.001); peak rotational velocity reduced 10% (22.8 to 20.4 rad/s, p<0.001); impact duration increased 40% (24.8 to 34.8 ms, p<0.001). Average impact speed was consistent across conditions (5.8 m/s). Control pad rotational velocities approached published concussive thresholds (~22 rad/s).
limitations: Impact speeds (mean 5.8 m/s) were below concussive levels reported in NFL studies (~9.3 m/s), limiting generalizability to high-severity impacts. Hybrid III dummy biofidelity for lateral STH impacts is uncertain. Variability in participant striking technique, though mitigated by paired analysis design. Ordered/learning effects not fully controlled. No existing shoulder pad performance standards for comparison.
practicalImplications: Shoulder pad design is an underexplored, actionable target for concussion risk reduction in football. Adding closed-cell foam at the top flap, arch, and resting point of existing pads is a low-cost modification that meaningfully reduces head kinematics in shoulder-to-helmet impacts. Programs should advocate for shoulder pad performance standards similar to those for helmets. Future higher-speed testing is needed before definitive concussion-risk claims can be made.
athleteDev: For developing players at all levels, shoulder pad design may contribute to long-term head safety. Programs should consider padding quality in shoulder pad selection criteria, not just fit or position-specific coverage. Coaches and equipment managers should be aware that STH impacts—now comprising ~22% of NFL concussions—are an increasing share of head contact events as helmet-to-helmet rules tighten.
rtp: Not directly applicable to RTP decision-making. However, the study underscores that modifiable equipment factors (shoulder pad design) contribute to concussion exposure, which has downstream implications for cumulative head impact burden and RTP thresholds. Players with concussion history may benefit from upgraded shoulder pad padding as a secondary prevention measure.

citation: Eichner, E. R. (2018). Football Team Rhabdomyolysis: The Pain Beats the Gain and the Coach Is to Blame. Current Sports Medicine Reports, 17(5), 142–143.
abstract: This clinical commentary reviews 17 outbreaks of team exertional rhabdomyolysis (ER) across multiple sports over recent years, with particular focus on seven football outbreaks at college and high school levels. Documented outbreaks occurred at Iowa (2011, 13 hospitalized), Maine (2012), Oregon (2017), Nebraska (2018), and multiple high school programs. The paper identifies a consistent causal pattern: novel, high-volume exercise performed immediately after a period of deconditioning (typically winter or summer break), directed by coaches motivated to 'toughen up' players following a losing season. The author argues that in each case, institutional and coaching decisions—not individual player failure—were the proximate cause of ER outbreaks.
tldr: Team exertional rhabdomyolysis (ER) in football is a preventable, coach-driven phenomenon. Every documented outbreak shares the same profile: novel overexertion (too much, too soon, too fast) after a deconditioning break, often as punishment or to assert a new coaching philosophy. Strength and conditioning staff must resist pressure to implement extreme novel loading in the first sessions after any break, as the combination of deconditioning and unfamiliar high-volume exercise reliably precipitates ER requiring hospitalization.
methods: Narrative clinical review describing 17 team ER outbreaks across college and high school sports. The author synthesizes published case reports, conference presentations, and media-documented incidents. Cases span football (7), swimming (2), lacrosse (2), soccer (1), track (1), basketball (1), softball (1), volleyball (1), and golf (1). Epidemiological patterns across outbreaks are described qualitatively.
findings: All 17 outbreaks shared the same etiology: novel overexertion following a break from training. Football outbreaks predominantly involved squat-based or weightlifting protocols administered in the first 1-3 days after winter break. Multiple outbreaks required fasciotomies for compartment syndrome. Coaches consistently blamed athletes rather than accepting institutional responsibility. Programs with new coaches or following losing seasons were disproportionately represented. The Iowa outbreak (100 timed back squats at 50% 1RM on day 2 of return) produced 13 hospitalizations—the largest single football ER event documented.
limitations: Narrative review without systematic search methodology. Reliance on published case reports and media accounts introduces selection bias toward more severe or publicized outbreaks. Causality is inferred from temporal and contextual patterns rather than prospective study design. Individual player-level physiological risk factors are not analyzed.
practicalImplications: The first training session after any break (winter, summer, postseason) should never introduce novel, high-volume exercises at near-maximal intensity. Strength and conditioning staff must follow evidence-based return-to-training progressions regardless of coaching pressure. Institutions should have explicit rhabdomyolysis prevention protocols including graduated loading after deconditioning periods. Early recognition of ER (severe muscle pain and weakness 12-48 hours post-exercise, dark urine) and immediate medical referral is essential. Culture change is needed so coaches view ER outbreaks as institutional failures, not player weakness.
athleteDev: Young football athletes are particularly vulnerable to ER because they may push through pain to avoid social stigma or dismissal from the team. Strength and conditioning programs must implement standardized return-to-training ramp protocols after all breaks exceeding one week. Novel movements (e.g., back squats, plyometrics) should be introduced at low volumes before increasing load or repetitions, regardless of prior training history.
rtp: Athletes who suffer ER require medical clearance before returning to full training. Severe cases involving compartment syndrome and fasciotomy have prolonged RTP timelines. Recurrence risk is elevated in players with prior ER history, and graduated loading should be especially conservative on subsequent return-to-play after breaks. Institutions should document all ER cases to identify repeat patterns and modify programming accordingly.

citation: Hecksteden, A., Schmartz, G. P., Egyptien, Y., aus der Fünten, K., Keller, A., & Meyer, T. (2022). Forecasting football injuries by combining screening, monitoring and machine learning. Science and Medicine in Football. https://doi.org/10.1080/24733938.2022.2095006
abstract: Injury risk management in football requires identifying players and circumstances associated with elevated injury likelihood. Prior work has typically applied either screening (time-stable characteristics) or monitoring (volatile daily factors) data separately, limiting predictive scope. This prospective observational cohort study developed and validated a machine learning injury forecasting model integrating both data types in 88 male professional football (soccer) players from 4 German 3rd and 4th division teams during the 2019/2020 season. Gradient boosting with ROSE upsampling within player-level leave-one-out cross-validation was used to predict acute, non-contact, time-loss injuries on a daily basis. The integrated model achieved a cross-validated ROC-AUC of 0.61 and a holdout test set AUC of 0.62, outperforming comparator models using monitoring data alone.
tldr: Combining periodic fitness screening data with daily monitoring data in a gradient-boosted machine learning model produces better injury forecasting accuracy than monitoring alone in professional football. The model's ROC-AUC of ~0.62 on a holdout test set indicates fair but not clinically sufficient discrimination. Key predictors included pain during the Football Injury Movement Screen (SIMS_pain), match day exposure, and post-RTP vulnerability windows. Prospective external validation is required before practical deployment, but the integration of screening and monitoring data represents a methodologically sound step toward actionable injury risk tools.
methods: Prospective observational cohort study; 88 male professional football players from 4 teams (German 3rd/4th division), 2019/2020 season (June 2019 to March 2020, truncated at COVID-19 shutdown). Data collected: basic player info, periodic screening (endurance capacity, 30m sprint, body composition, SIMS movement screen), daily monitoring (session RPE training load, subjective recovery/stress scale), exposure type (match vs. training), and injury recording per Fuller consensus. Gradient boosting (R) with ROSE upsampling integrated into player-level LOOCV. 11 explanatory variables preselected a priori. Holdout test set = 25% of players, stratified by injury history. Ancillary analyses probed leaky folds, upsampling effects, and round-robin robustness.
findings: 51 criterion injuries (acute, non-contact, time-loss) in 88 players over the study period. Main model cross-validated ROC-AUC = 0.61 [0.60–0.62]; holdout test set AUC = 0.62 [0.49–0.76]. Comparator models (monitoring only: AUC 0.56 on test set; monitoring + basic info: 0.52 on test set) were inferior, supporting value of integrating screening data. Top predictors: SIMS_pain, match day, post-RTP period, striker position, team-average sessionRPE. Leaky-folds analysis confirmed that ignoring hierarchical data structure inflates cross-validation AUC to 0.88—highlighting a major methodological risk in prior published injury ML models. Approximately 50% of criterion injuries concentrated in the 12.5% of time-points with highest predicted risk.
limitations: Small absolute number of criterion injuries (n=51) limits model stability; substantial variability in round-robin repetitions confirms lack of robustness. Single season data from one country's lower professional divisions limits generalizability. COVID-19 truncated the study period. Model miscalibration (overestimation of injury risk) due to upsampling. No prospective temporal validation attempted. Feature engineering and modeling choices must be adapted for each club's specific data collection infrastructure.
practicalImplications: Integrating periodic fitness screening with daily monitoring data via machine learning offers a methodologically sound framework for injury risk stratification in professional football. Programs already collecting both data types can leverage this approach without additional testing burden. However, current accuracy (~0.62 AUC) is insufficient for high-stakes clinical decisions (e.g., mandatory rest); the model's value lies in flagging elevated-risk periods to prompt clinical conversations, not in replacing human judgment. Prioritizing vigilance during match days, post-RTP windows, and in players with movement screen pain findings is supported by the feature importance results.
athleteDev: The framework is relevant for development programs collecting screening and wellness data. Movement screen pain (SIMS_pain) and match day exposure were among the top injury risk signals, suggesting that even in younger developmental players, systematic integration of physical screening with daily load data could improve injury surveillance. Programs should invest in standardized data collection infrastructure to enable future ML-based risk tools as sample sizes accumulate.
rtp: The post-RTP vulnerability window was identified as one of the most important predictors of subsequent injury in this model. Specifically, a 4-week period following return to full availability carried elevated risk. This empirically supports conservative monitoring protocols in the month after RTP, with heightened attention to training load, subjective recovery, and movement quality during this window.

citation: Nguyen, Q., Jiang, R., Ellingwood, M., & Yurko, R. (2025). Fractional tackles: leveraging player tracking data for within-play tackling evaluation in American football. Scientific Reports, 15, 2148. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-85993-1
abstract: Traditional tackling statistics in American football (solo tackles, assists, combined tackles) are discrete, manually recorded outcomes that undercount defensive contributions—particularly for linemen—and overstate the contributions credited to a single tackler. Using NFL Next Gen Stats player tracking data from the 2024 Big Data Bowl (weeks 1-9 of the 2022 NFL season, 5,539 run plays by running backs), this paper introduces fractional tackles: a continuous, model-free metric that credits defenders for slowing the ball-carrier's forward motion toward the end zone during contact windows within a play. The metric identifies contact windows (consecutive frames where a defender is within 1.5 yards of the ball-carrier), assigns a value to each window based on velocity reduction, and distributes credit across all involved defenders proportionally by frame and player count.
tldr: Fractional tackles is a new, data-driven defensive metric derived from NFL player tracking data that overcomes the binary limitations of traditional tackle counts. By measuring how much each defender contributes to slowing the ball-carrier's forward velocity during contact, the metric credits defenders who are not officially recorded as tacklers—especially defensive linemen who consistently disrupt plays but rarely receive statistical recognition. The metric shows strong correlation with traditional tackles (r=0.93) but greater stability across time and more measurable information for players without conventional box-score statistics.
methods: NFL Big Data Bowl 2024 tracking data used (10 Hz, 22 players + ball tracked per play); 5,539 run plays by running backs from weeks 1-9 of the 2022 NFL regular season. Contact windows defined as consecutive frames where a defender is within 1.5 yards of the ball-carrier (threshold covering ~95% of tackle distances in the data). Contact window value = (starting velocity - ending velocity) / peak pre-contact velocity, with adjustments for post-contact velocity recovery. Window value distributed equally across frames; frame value distributed equally across defenders in contact. Fractional tackles summed per defender per window and aggregated across plays. Stability assessed by correlating first-4-week vs. last-5-week totals. Compared to traditional combined tackles (total tackles + assists/2).
findings: 7,453 contact windows identified across 5,539 plays; average window length 1.28 seconds. Top fractional tackles leaders were dominated by linebackers (ILBs, OLBs) but defensive linemen showed notably higher average fractional tackles per play than their traditional tackle counts suggest. Fractional tackles correlated strongly with traditional combined tackles (r=0.93, 95% CI: 0.92–0.94) but were systematically lower, indicating conventional statistics overstate single-defender credit. Fractional tackles demonstrated greater stability (predictability of future from past performance) than combined tackles: overall r=0.69 vs. 0.59 across the season, with especially stronger within-position stability for linebackers (0.73 vs. 0.64). The metric credits 19,691 unique players across the study period, more than double the 7,720 with at least one recorded tackle.
limitations: Analysis limited to run plays by running backs (weeks 1-9 of one season), limiting generalizability to pass plays, other ball-carrier types, and full seasons. Contact windows are empirically defined from 2D tracking data; true physical contact cannot be confirmed without pose estimation. Credit attribution assumes equal distribution across defenders per frame, which simplifies actual contribution weighting. Ball-carrier velocity recovery between windows not fully credited to subsequent defenders. Framework is player-agnostic in contact window definition and does not account for player-specific traits (e.g., arm length) that affect contact radius.
practicalImplications: Fractional tackles provides coaching and analytics staffs with a more granular, objective view of defensive contribution than traditional tackle counts. Defensive linemen—a position group historically underserved by box-score statistics—can now be evaluated on measurable stopping contribution per play. The metric can inform game planning (identifying defenders most effective at halting specific ball-carriers), contract negotiations, and draft evaluation. Because it is model-free and derived from publicly available tracking data, it can be implemented in any data pipeline with access to player positioning.
athleteDev: For developing defensive players, fractional tackles offers a more informative feedback loop than binary tackle/no-tackle designations. Linemen and interior defenders who routinely reduce ball-carrier momentum but are not credited with official tackles can have their contributions quantified, supporting more accurate development assessments and reducing positional evaluation bias toward high-tackle-count positions like linebackers and safeties.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play contexts. However, the framework's ability to quantify within-play physical engagement could theoretically be adapted to monitor a returning player's on-field contribution and physical activity levels during gradual reintegration, providing objective data on participation intensity beyond simple snap count or playing time metrics.

citation: Palermi, S., Vittadini, F., Pedret, C., Vecchiato, M., Della Villa, F., Corsini, A., Jokela, A., & Lempainen, L. (2025). From the Field to the Screen: A Scoping Review of Video Analysis as a Tool for Understanding Thigh Muscle-Tendon Injuries in Football. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine. https://doi.org/10.1097/JSM.0000000000001377
abstract: Thigh muscle-tendon injuries are among the most prevalent and costly injuries in professional football, with hamstrings, adductors, quadriceps (rectus femoris), and calf muscles collectively affected. This scoping review synthesized data from 10 studies published 2010-2025 that used video analysis to investigate thigh muscle-tendon injury mechanisms in football players. Studies were quality-appraised using the QA-SIVAS scale. The review identified noncontact and indirect contact as the predominant mechanisms for severe injuries. Common injury scenarios included sprinting-induced eccentric hamstring strains, kicking-related rectus femoris injuries, and closed kinetic chain adductor longus injuries. Video analysis is positioned as an essential tool for understanding injury biomechanics, informing prevention, and guiding rehabilitation—with AI-enhanced video analysis identified as a critical future direction.
tldr: Video analysis of professional football injuries consistently shows that severe thigh muscle-tendon injuries are primarily noncontact or indirect-contact events driven by eccentric loading during high-speed running, kicking, and sudden directional changes. Hamstring injuries predominantly occur during sprinting (acceleration/deceleration), rectus femoris injuries during kicking and sprinting, and adductor injuries during complex closed and open kinetic chain movements. Prevention strategies should target eccentric strength in these positions and movement patterns, not simply limit high-speed running exposure—as many injuries occur during short accelerations and unexpected situations, not necessarily at maximal sprint speed.
methods: Scoping review methodology; comprehensive search of PubMed, Scopus, Cochrane, Embase, SportDiscuss, and Google Scholar from inception to February 2025, supplemented by AI-assisted preliminary mapping via ResearchRabbit. Inclusion criteria: video analysis as a primary method, thigh muscle-tendon injuries in professional football, acute injuries only. Ten studies met inclusion criteria. Quality assessed with the 18-item QA-SIVAS scale. Data extracted: objectives, number of injuries analyzed, muscle groups, and main injury mechanism findings.
findings: All 10 included studies found that severe thigh muscle-tendon injuries are predominantly noncontact (54-83% across studies). Hamstring: sprint-related (acceleration/deceleration) and stretch-related (CKC) mechanisms; biceps femoris long head most affected; injuries occur at high horizontal speeds but also during short accelerations <10m (86% involved running per Vermeulen et al.). Rectus femoris: kicking is the dominant mechanism (80% per Jokela et al.); complete ruptures common with kicking/sprinting. Adductor longus: change of direction (35%), kicking (29%), reaching (24%), jumping (12%); predominantly CKC movements with hip extension, abduction, external rotation. Gender differences noted: women have higher proportion of noncontact injuries during running and kicking than men. Eccentric muscle activation during rapid loading is consistently implicated across all injury types.
limitations: Evidence classified as Level 3 (Oxford Centre for Evidence-Based Medicine)—observational studies preclude causal inference. Methodological variability across studies (QA-SIVAS scores varied; no control groups in any study). Findings specific to professional football; limited applicability to amateur or youth levels. Only 10 studies included, restricting breadth of conclusions. Reliance on peer-reviewed journals may introduce publication bias. Practical implementation of video-based injury prevention in real-world settings faces cost and infrastructure barriers.
practicalImplications: Prevention programs should emphasize eccentric strengthening of hamstrings (Nordic curls, Romanian deadlifts), hip adductors, and quadriceps rather than solely limiting high-speed running volumes—as injuries commonly occur at submaximal speeds and during unexpected situational demands. Kicking mechanics and load management during kicking-intensive training phases are particularly relevant for rectus femoris injury prevention. Video analysis of training and match footage should be incorporated into injury surveillance programs to identify individual movement patterns associated with elevated injury risk. Return-to-play protocols should assess movement quality in the specific kinematic patterns (sprinting, kicking, CKC reaching) associated with each injury type.
athleteDev: Developing football athletes should progressively build eccentric strength capacity in hamstrings, adductors, and quadriceps from early in their career to withstand the high eccentric demands of sprinting and kicking. Position-specific loading patterns should inform training design: wide receivers and midfielders face greater hamstring sprint demands; forwards and attacking midfielders face higher rectus femoris kicking loads. Video-based movement screening can identify athletes whose running and kicking mechanics place excessive eccentric demand on specific muscle groups.
rtp: Video analysis findings directly inform RTP criteria for thigh muscle-tendon injuries. Functional RTP assessments should include sport-specific movement tests corresponding to the injury mechanism: maximal sprint at progressive speeds for hamstring injuries, kicking mechanics assessment for rectus femoris injuries, and CKC loading tests (change of direction, lunging) for adductor injuries. The review supports monitoring movement quality—not just pain absence or strength ratios—as a criterion for progressive RTP in professional football.

citation: Teramoto, M., Cushman, D. M., Cross, C. L., Curtiss, H. M., & Willick, S. E. (2017). Game schedules and rate of concussions in the National Football League. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine, 5(11), 2325967117740862.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study examined whether unconventional NFL game schedules—including Thursday games (3 days of rest) and overseas games—are associated with an increased rate of concussions. Using PBS Frontline Concussion Watch data, 582 concussions were analyzed across the 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons. Chi-square goodness-of-fit tests and logistic regression were used to examine the relationship between days of rest, game location, bye week timing, and concussion rates. No significant association was found between concussion rates and days of rest, game location, or bye week timing. Significantly more concussions occurred in the second half of the season (56.2% vs. 43.8%, P < .01).
tldr: Contrary to public concern, Thursday Night Football and overseas games do not increase NFL players' concussion risk. The only notable finding was a higher concussion rate in the second half of the season, suggesting cumulative fatigue may be a more meaningful risk factor than schedule format. Team medical staffs should heighten vigilance later in the season rather than specifically around short-rest games.
methods: Descriptive epidemiological study using publicly available data from PBS Frontline Concussion Watch and Pro-Football-Reference.com. Analyzed 582 concussions across 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons. Chi-square goodness-of-fit tests compared observed concussion distributions by days of rest (3, 5–7, 9–14 days) and game location (home, away, overseas) to expected distributions based on actual game scheduling proportions. Two-way contingency table analysis and logistic regression assessed repeat concussion risk, controlling for player position.
findings: No significant association between concussion rate and days of rest (P = .17), game location (P = .23), or bye week timing by team or opponent (P > .05). Game schedules were not predictive of repeat concussions (P > .05). Significantly more concussions occurred in the second half of the season than the first half (56.2% vs. 43.8%, P < .01). Non-significant trends included ~15 more concussions than expected after 9–14 days of rest and 39 more in away games than home games.
limitations: Reliance on a publicly available database rather than the NFL Injury Surveillance System may have resulted in missed or unverified concussions. Small sample size reduced statistical power, particularly for Thursday and overseas games. Individual player factors (condition, skill level, prior concussion history) were not controlled. Observational design prevents causal inference. Concussion underreporting in football is a known issue.
practicalImplications: Current NFL game schedules—including Thursday Night Football and international games—do not appear to independently elevate concussion risk. Scheduling policy changes related to player safety should focus on other risk factors. Medical and training staffs may consider increased concussion monitoring during the second half of the season when cumulative fatigue is highest.
athleteDev: For player development contexts, findings suggest that schedule density (short rest) is less influential on head injury risk than cumulative seasonal load. Programs should incorporate fatigue management across the full season rather than focusing primarily on short-week preparations.
rtp: The absence of a significant relationship between days of rest and repeat concussions provides some reassurance when managing return-to-play timelines around short rest weeks. However, clinicians should remain vigilant for players returning late in the season when overall concussion rates are elevated.

citation: Khalil, L. S., Lindsay-Rivera, K. G., Abbas, M. J., Shah, S., Tandron, M., Ferris, A., & Okoroha, K. R. (2021). Game utilization and performance following RTP from ACL reconstruction does not influence a subsequent second ACL injury in National Football League players. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation, 3(5), e1377–e1385.
abstract: This retrospective case-control study examined whether in-game utilization or performance following primary ACL reconstruction differs between NFL players who subsequently sustain a second ACL injury (retear group, n=13) and those who do not (tear group, n=32). Players who underwent ACL reconstruction between 2013 and 2017 were identified from public sources. Game utilization (games played, games started, snap counts) and season approximate value (SAV) were compared across three seasons post-RTP relative to the pre-injury baseline season. No significant differences were found between groups in utilization or performance metrics at any time point.
tldr: How much an NFL player plays after ACL reconstruction does not predict whether they will suffer a second ACL tear. Both groups showed similar declines in game usage and performance after RTP. The one meaningful predictor of return to starting lineup was draft pick position, suggesting organizational investment—not individual workload—drives post-ACL playing time decisions. Clinicians cannot use in-game utilization as a risk stratification tool for second ACL injury.
methods: Retrospective case-control study of NFL ACL injuries from 2012/2013 to 2017/2018 seasons. Players were identified via publicly available sources and classified into 'tear' (primary ACL only, n=32) and 'retear' (subsequent ipsilateral or contralateral ACL injury, n=13) groups. Game utilization statistics (games played, games started, snap counts) and season approximate value (SAV) were collected from pro-football-reference.com, ESPN.com, and NFL.com. Data were analyzed for the season prior to injury (baseline) and three seasons following RTP. Statistical comparisons used independent t-tests, Wilcoxon rank sum tests, chi-square tests, Fisher's exact tests, and repeated-measures analysis. Relative percentages used pre-injury baseline as reference.
findings: No significant differences in games played, games started, snap counts, or SAV between tear and retear groups across all three post-RTP seasons (P > .05 for all comparisons). Both groups demonstrated similar decreases in all utilization metrics relative to pre-injury baseline. Tear players averaged >100% of baseline for most metrics; retear players generally failed to reach 100% but differences were non-significant. Draft pick position was positively correlated with the relative percentage of games started in the first post-RTP season (r = .554, P = .017). Return to play rate was 73.3% following primary ACL reconstruction.
limitations: Retrospective design with data from public internet sources introduces potential for incomplete or inaccurate injury classification. Cannot be generalized to non-NFL athlete populations or other levels of play. Concomitant injuries (meniscal, cartilage), surgical technique, graft choice, and rehabilitation protocols were not standardized or controlled. Sample size was too small to independently analyze ipsilateral vs. contralateral reinjuries. No power analysis conducted. Players with pre-NFL ACL history were excluded, limiting generalizability.
practicalImplications: Game utilization after ACL reconstruction cannot serve as a modifiable risk factor or risk screening tool for second ACL injury in NFL players. Organizational decisions about returning high-draft-pick players to starting roles appear to be independent of reinjury risk. Clinicians and performance staff should not restrict or modulate game utilization based on reinjury prevention rationale alone. Other biomechanical, neuromuscular, and psychological factors should be prioritized in return-to-sport screening.
athleteDev: For player development after ACL reconstruction, draft position—a proxy for organizational investment and perceived talent—was the primary factor predicting return to a starting role. Development programs should focus on optimizing neuromuscular readiness and psychological readiness for RTP rather than managing game minutes as a reinjury prevention strategy, since load management did not differentiate those who re-tore from those who did not.
rtp: Game utilization and performance metrics following primary ACL reconstruction are not predictive of a subsequent second ACL tear. Return-to-play protocols should not use post-RTP snap counts or games started as risk stratification criteria. The similar decline in performance metrics in both tear and retear groups suggests that standard RTP decision-making should focus on objective functional and strength criteria rather than graduated game exposure as injury prevention.

citation: Roberts, S. S. H., Urwin, C. S., Regan, J., Bowe, S. J., & Warmington, S. A. (2025). Game-day temperatures are predictive of National Football League game outcomes when teams from different climates compete against each other. Temperature. Advance online publication.
abstract: This observational study examined whether game-day air temperature recorded at NFL stadia predicts game outcomes when teams from cool climates (>39° N latitude, North teams, n=17) compete against teams from warm climates (<39° N, South teams, n=15). Data from 1,011 regular-season and playoff games across eight NFL seasons (2017–2025) were extracted from official NFL Gamebooks. Mixed effects logistic regression and mixed effects models tested whether temperature associated with win probability and point differential, respectively, while controlling for season year, home team ID, and end-season rank differential.
tldr: When NFL teams from cold-climate cities (north of 39°N) play against warm-climate teams, their win probability and point differential both decline as game-day temperature rises—even after accounting for home field advantage. Each 1°C increase in temperature reduces a northern team's odds of winning by roughly 2–3%. The effect is strongest for games played in the South, suggesting that players and staff from warmer regions may have behavioral or psychological adaptations to heat that confer a competitive advantage.
methods: Historical game data from eight NFL seasons (2017–2025) collected from NFL Gamebooks. Teams categorized as North (>39° N, n=17) or South (<39° N, n=15) based on home stadium latitude. Analyzed 1,011 games with reported game-day temperature. Mixed effects logistic regression modeled North team win probability per 1°C temperature increase; mixed effects models examined North team point differential per 1°C increase. Models run for all games combined and separately by game location (North vs. South). Random effects included season year, home team ID, and end-season rank differential. Humidity and game location added as covariates in follow-up models.
findings: North team win probability declined significantly as game-day temperature increased (OR = 0.974, 95% CI [0.951, 0.997], p = .026), representing a 2–3% reduction per 1°C rise. This association held when controlling for humidity and game location. North team point differential was negatively associated with temperature (β = -0.175, 95% CI [-0.286, -0.063], p = .002), but this became non-significant when game location was included as a covariate. By game location, North team point differential was significantly associated with temperature for games played in South locations (β = -0.152, p = .001) but not North locations (p = .318). North teams won 59% of games played in the North but only 49% of games played in the South.
limitations: The 39°N latitude cutoff for North/South classification is somewhat arbitrary. Data limited to eight seasons from official NFL Gamebooks. Difficult to fully control for inherent differences in team quality across games, though random effects were included. Home time-zone and circadian misalignment were not controlled. Temperature recording methodology at NFL stadiums is not publicly standardized. Observational design cannot establish causal mechanisms.
practicalImplications: Teams based in cold-climate cities face a measurable competitive disadvantage when playing in warmer environments. Preparation strategies for warm-weather away games should include proactive heat acclimatization protocols, hydration planning (including potential IV hyperhydration), and in-game cooling strategies. Staff familiar with warmer conditions may have optimized behavioral routines that contribute to their teams' advantage. Northern teams should treat warm-weather road trips as a specific performance challenge requiring targeted environmental preparation.
athleteDev: NFL players from northern franchises competing in warm environments may benefit from heat exposure training during the preseason or before warm-weather road trips. Psychological preparation—reducing perceptual discomfort and reinforcing confidence in warm conditions—may also be underutilized. Teams should audit their thermal management protocols for warm-weather games as a competitive edge opportunity.
rtp: While not directly an RTP study, findings are relevant for scheduling return-to-competition after injury: players returning to play in unfamiliar climate conditions (particularly warm environments after cold-climate training) may face additional physiological and cognitive demands. Rehabilitation and reconditioning programs should consider environmental acclimatization as part of full RTP readiness, especially for travel to warm-weather venues.

citation: Foster, G., O'Reilly, N., Devereux, J. B., & Shundi, M. (2023). General manager and head coach exits in the NBA and the NFL. Sport, Business and Management: An International Journal. Advance online publication.
abstract: This study investigated why head coaches and general managers (GMs) exit their positions in the NBA and NFL over a 30-year period. Using 891 GM and coach annual observations from NBA clubs (1988–2018) and 949 observations from NFL clubs (1987–2017), three hypotheses grounded in agency theory, upper-echelon theory, and institutional logic theory were examined. Methods included logit analysis for exit/retention modeling and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling of qualitative press quotations. Findings show that simultaneous GM and coach exits are strongly correlated, win-loss record is more predictive of coach exits than GM exits, and past organizational culture of turnover strongly predicts future exits.
tldr: In professional football and basketball, coaches are fired primarily based on losing records, while GMs are dismissed for a more complex mix of reasons beyond wins and losses. When one key executive leaves, the other is significantly more likely to follow—the 'cleaning house' effect is statistically real. Perhaps most practically, a club's past culture of firing coaches and GMs is the strongest predictor of future exits, meaning organizational instability tends to perpetuate itself and should be a key consideration for any executive evaluating a job offer.
methods: Quantitative and qualitative analysis using 30 years of data. Logit analysis modeled the probability of GM and coach exit (binary: start vs. not start following season with same club) as a function of win-loss record, playoff progression, simultaneous exit of the other role, and historical turnover frequency. Separate models run for NBA, NFL, and pooled samples. Qualitative analysis used Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling on a database of press quotations compiled from Sports Business Daily, Bleacher Report, and ESPN.com for all GM and coach exit events. Two topics emerged from LDA: 'Win-Loss Record' and 'Beyond Win-Loss Record.'
findings: Hypothesis 1 (partially supported): Win-loss record significantly predicts coach exits in both NBA and NFL, but is much weaker as a predictor for GM exits. Hypothesis 2 (supported): Simultaneous exits of GMs and coaches are significantly correlated—a coach exit significantly increases the probability of a GM exit and vice versa (the 'cleaning house' effect; P < .001 across models). Hypothesis 3 (supported): Past organizational turnover frequency is strongly negatively associated with retention—clubs with high historical exit rates continue to exhibit high exit rates (P < .001). NFL GM mean tenure was 5.27 seasons; NFL coach mean tenure was 4.14 seasons. Annual exit rates averaged 16.0% for NFL GMs and 21.3% for NFL coaches.
limitations: Club ownership and ownership changes—a likely key driver of GM and coach exits—could not be fully modeled due to data limitations. The exit/retention classification is binary and may not capture nuanced departures (voluntary vs. involuntary). Press quotation databases are sparse for early years of the study period due to limited online content. The 'Beyond Win-Loss' LDA topic is heterogeneous, reflecting the complex and varied nature of non-performance-related GM exits. Causal inference is not possible from this observational design.
practicalImplications: Organizations with a high historical rate of GM and coach turnover are likely to continue that pattern, creating instability that affects player recruitment, team culture, and long-term performance. Executives and coaches evaluating new opportunities should assess an organization's historical turnover culture as a key contractual risk factor. Clubs should consider that cleaning house rarely addresses the root cause (often ownership behavior) and may perpetuate dysfunction. Win-loss record remains the primary lever for head coach retention in both leagues.
athleteDev: Organizational leadership stability is an underappreciated factor in athlete development. Frequent GM and coach turnover disrupts player development pipelines, scouting philosophies, and long-term roster building. For football programs, evaluating the historical turnover culture of an organization before committing long-term is critical for players, agents, and incoming coaches seeking environments conducive to sustained development.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play from injury. However, organizational instability caused by frequent coaching and GM turnover can disrupt sports medicine infrastructure, rehabilitation protocols, and player health management continuity. Stable leadership environments are likely more conducive to consistent, evidence-based RTP decision-making.

citation: de Oliveira, D. M., Silva, A. K. A. C., Macedo, A. G., Fernandes, M. B., & Fernandes, E. V. (2025). Global research trends in sports nutrition and football over the last 20 years (2004–2024). Sports, 13, 365.
abstract: This scientometric review mapped global scientific production on sports nutrition applied to soccer (football) from 2004 to 2024. A database search across Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus yielded 2,636 documents, of which 526 original articles were included after removing duplicates, reviews, meta-analyses, and out-of-scope studies. Data were analyzed using Bibliometrix and VOSviewer software. The study identified publication trends, leading authors, journals, countries, collaboration networks, and thematic clusters. Annual publication growth rate was 14.6% with a 1,450% increase over the 20-year period. The United Kingdom led in total output and citations, with Nutrients as the top publishing journal.
tldr: Research on sports nutrition applied to soccer has grown dramatically over 20 years, shifting from a primary focus on hydration to increasingly complex topics including low energy availability, polyphenol supplementation, body composition, and recovery strategies. Underexplored areas—oxidative stress markers, beta-alanine supplementation, and low energy availability—represent high-priority gaps for future research. For practitioners, this landscape review highlights that the evidence base for individualized nutritional strategies in football is expanding rapidly, with particular attention warranted on energy deficiency and antioxidant support.
methods: Scientometric analysis of original research articles published from January 2004 to December 2024. Databases searched: Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus. Boolean search strategy combined nutritional terms (dietary intake, sports nutrition, energy availability, hydration, supplementation, macronutrient/micronutrient intake) with football/soccer terms. After deduplication and eligibility screening, 526 articles were included. Bibliometrix 4.3.2 (R 4.4.2) used for descriptive analyses, production trends, authorship, journal distribution (Bradford's Law), citation analysis, and country production. VOSviewer 1.6.20 used for keyword co-occurrence networks and author/institution collaboration mapping. Conceptual structure mapped via multiple correspondence analysis (MCA).
findings: Annual scientific production grew 1,450% from 2004 (4 articles) to 2024 (62 articles) at a 14.6% annual growth rate. Nutrients was the leading publication source (53 articles, 29.6% of total). Morton J. (n=12) and Maughan R. (n=10) were the most productive authors. Liverpool John Moores University was the primary institutional collaboration hub. United Kingdom led in both volume (245 articles, 46.6%) and citations (1,847). Early research focused on hydration and thermoregulation (2006–2012); later research expanded to macronutrient intake, body composition, and energy balance (2013–2020); most recent trends (2021–2024) emphasize low energy availability and polyphenol supplementation. Co-occurrence network identified underexplored topics including lipid peroxidation, alpha-tocopherol, beta-alanine supplementation, and oxidative stress markers.
limitations: Restricted to three databases; gray literature and non-indexed journals were excluded. The 20-year time frame, while comprehensive, may not capture pre-2004 foundational research. Exclusion criteria removed American football, Australian rules football, rugby, futsal, and adapted football studies. Scientometric methods identify patterns but do not evaluate the quality or methodological rigor of individual studies. Database coverage discrepancies may affect results.
practicalImplications: The field of sports nutrition in soccer is rapidly maturing toward individualized, performance-focused strategies. Practitioners should be aware of the growing evidence base for low energy availability as a risk factor in soccer players, polyphenol supplementation for recovery and oxidative stress management, and beta-alanine for high-intensity anaerobic performance. Hydration and carbohydrate periodization remain foundational, while emerging topics offer opportunities for evidence-based innovation in nutritional protocols.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, the scientometric landscape confirms that nutritional support should be integrated across multiple dimensions: hydration and thermoregulation, carbohydrate and energy availability management, supplementation (polyphenols, vitamin D, beta-alanine), and body composition monitoring via anthropometry. Programs should build nutritional protocols informed by the most cited research clusters and be aware of underexplored areas—particularly low energy availability in younger and female players—that may have meaningful developmental implications.
rtp: The growing research emphasis on recovery nutrition, oxidative stress management, and energy availability in soccer players has direct relevance to return-to-play contexts. Nutritional strategies targeting inflammation, muscle damage (polyphenols, antioxidants), and energy resynthesis (carbohydrates, total energy intake) should be integrated into RTP rehabilitation protocols. Low energy availability—an emerging focus of the field—may compromise recovery and tissue healing, making energy intake monitoring a key component of injury return management.

citation: Franettovich Smith MM, Bonacci J, Mendis MD, Christie C, Rotstein A, Hides JA. Gluteus medius activation during running is a risk factor for season hamstring injuries in elite footballers. J Sci Med Sport. 2016. doi:10.1016/j.jsams.2016.07.004
abstract: This prospective cohort study investigated whether size and activation of the gluteal muscles (GMED and GMAX) were risk factors for hamstring injury in 26 elite male Australian Football League (AFL) players. Bilateral GMED and GMAX muscle volume was measured via MRI and electromyographic (EMG) recordings were obtained during treadmill gait at 6, 12, and 15 km/h at the start of the season. Nine players (35%) sustained a hamstring injury during the season. Higher GMED muscle activity during running at 12 and 15 km/h was significantly associated with in-season hamstring injury (p=0.03, effect sizes 1.1–1.5). No significant differences were found for GMED volume, GMAX volume, or GMAX activation. ROC analyses identified average GMED activity cut-offs of 12.0% MVC at 12 km/h (AUC=0.791, sensitivity=77.8%, specificity=76.5%) and 13.4% MVC at 15 km/h (AUC=0.824, sensitivity=77.8%, specificity=82.4%) as best predictors of season hamstring injury.
tldr: Elite AFL players who went on to suffer a hamstring injury during the season showed significantly higher gluteus medius (GMED) activation during running — but not walking — compared to uninjured players. This suggests overactivity of the GMED during high-speed running may reflect compensatory lumbo-pelvic control deficits, creating conditions favorable for hamstring strain. Practitioners should assess GMED function during running-specific tasks and consider neuromuscular control of the hip-pelvis region as a modifiable target in hamstring injury prevention programs.
methods: Prospective cohort study of 26 elite male AFL players from one professional club. Pre-season bilateral GMED and GMAX muscle volumes were measured via 3 Tesla MRI (T2-weighted axial sequences). Surface EMG was recorded using bipolar electrodes (Noraxon Telemyo DTS) during treadmill running at 6, 12, and 15 km/h; EMG was normalized to maximal voluntary isometric contraction (%MVC). Ten consecutive strides per speed were analyzed for peak and average muscle activation. Injury incidence was tracked prospectively via club medical records. Linear mixed models, independent t-tests, and ROC analyses were used for statistical analysis.
findings: Nine of 26 players (34.6%) sustained an in-season hamstring injury. Players who were injured showed significantly higher average and peak GMED activation at 12 km/h (mean difference: 3.3% MVC average, 26.1 N peak) and 15 km/h (mean difference: 4.0% MVC average, 27.2 N peak) with effect sizes of 1.1–1.5 (large). No differences were found at walking speed (6 km/h). GMAX activation and muscle volume (GMED and GMAX) did not differ significantly between groups. ROC cut-offs identified average GMED activity above ~12–13% MVC during running as predictive of hamstring injury with 77–78% sensitivity.
limitations: Small sample size (n=26) from a single AFL club limits generalizability to female athletes, other sports, and skill levels. Only GMED and GMAX were assessed; other lumbo-pelvic muscles and hamstring activation itself were not measured. Hip strength and lower limb kinematics were not evaluated, limiting mechanistic interpretation. No baseline hamstring activation data were collected. The study cannot determine whether high GMED activation is a cause or consequence of underlying neuromuscular deficits.
practicalImplications: Measuring GMED activation during running (not walking) may serve as a useful screening tool for hamstring injury risk in team sports athletes. Athletes demonstrating higher GMED activation during high-speed running warrant closer attention in injury prevention programming. Neuromuscular control of the lumbo-pelvic region — specifically GMED — should be incorporated into hamstring injury prevention and rehabilitation strategies alongside traditional hamstring-focused work.
athleteDev: Assessment of GMED function during running-speed-specific tasks should be considered in pre-season athlete screening for football players. Lumbo-pelvic stability training targeting GMED neuromuscular control may help reduce hamstring injury risk in developing and elite players who demonstrate compensatory gluteal overactivity during high-speed running.
rtp: Players returning from hamstring injury should be assessed for GMED activation asymmetries during sport-specific running speeds before return-to-play clearance is granted. Rehabilitation protocols should address lumbo-pelvic neuromuscular control in addition to traditional hamstring strength and flexibility criteria, with running-specific testing used to confirm normalized GMED recruitment patterns.

citation: Lord C, Blazevich AJ, Drinkwater EJ, Ma'ayah F. Greater loss of horizontal force after a repeated-sprint test in footballers with a previous hamstring injury. J Sci Med Sport. 2018. doi:10.1016/j.jsams.2018.06.008
abstract: This study quantified changes in running kinetics and kinematics during a repeated-sprint test (RST) in football players with and without a previous unilateral hamstring injury, and examined the sensitivity and specificity with which these variables could identify previous hamstring injury. Forty semi-professional Western Australia State League footballers — 20 with previous unilateral hamstring injury and 20 without — completed a 10 x 6-second RST on a non-motorised treadmill dynamometer (Woodway Curve). Horizontal force (FH), vertical force (FV), contact time, and flight time were compared between previously injured and uninjured legs. Mean FH in the previously injured (kicking) leg decreased by 13% (p=0.001; ES=1.69) across the RST, compared to only 3% in uninjured legs. This between-leg difference in FH correctly identified 77% of previously injured legs (77% sensitivity, 85% specificity; AUC=0.846).
tldr: Footballers with a previous hamstring injury show a significantly greater fatigue-induced decline in horizontal ground reaction force (FH) during a repeated-sprint test compared to the non-injured leg or legs of uninjured players. A 13% drop in FH across 10 sprints in the injured leg — versus only 3% in uninjured legs — suggests persistent neuromuscular deficits that impair sprint endurance even after return to play. Repeated-sprint testing of FH asymmetry is a practical, football-specific screening tool with good discriminative ability (AUC=0.846) for identifying players with residual hamstring dysfunction.
methods: Cross-sectional study of 40 semi-professional footballers: 20 with previous unilateral hamstring injury to the kicking leg (injured group, IG) and 20 uninjured controls (UG). Players completed two familiarisation sessions followed by a 10 x 6-second RST on a Woodway Curve non-motorised treadmill with 24-second active recovery jogs between sprints. FH (propulsive), FV, contact time, and flight time were recorded at 200 Hz per leg across all sprints. Data were analysed using repeated-measures ANOVA. Binary logistic regression and ROC curve analyses assessed sensitivity and specificity of FH change for identifying previously injured legs.
findings: The kicking (injured) leg in the IG showed a 13% decrease in mean FH from sprint 1 to sprint 10 (p=0.001; ES=1.69), significantly greater than the 3% decline in the kicking leg of the UG (p=0.058; ES=0.28). Between-leg FH asymmetry increased substantially over the course of the RST in the IG but not in the UG. No significant between-group differences were observed for FV, contact time, or flight time. Binary logistic regression showed that the FH difference from RST1 to RST10 explained 39.8% of variance (Nagelkerke R²) and correctly classified 77% of previously injured legs with 85% specificity. ROC analysis identified an optimal FH cut-off of 11.5 N (AUC=0.846, p<0.001, 95% CI: 0.721–0.970).
limitations: Cross-sectional design cannot establish causality or determine fatigability prior to injury. All injured players had sustained injury to the kicking leg, limiting generalizability to non-kicking leg injuries. Players had varying rehabilitation histories and recovery times post-injury. The study cannot confirm whether FH deficits preceded injury or arose from it. The RST commenced from a jogging (rather than stationary) start, which may have reduced absolute FH magnitudes compared to other protocols.
practicalImplications: The repeated-sprint test using a non-motorised treadmill provides a practical, football-specific assessment of horizontal force fatigue that can identify persistent hamstring deficits after injury with 77% sensitivity and 85% specificity. Rehabilitation and return-to-play programs should target the reduction of inter-limb FH asymmetry, particularly under repeated high-intensity sprint conditions. Players cleared for return to play may still harbour meaningful hamstring deficits detectable only under fatigue, supporting the use of fatiguing sprint protocols as a final-stage RTP assessment tool.
athleteDev: Non-motorised treadmill repeated-sprint testing can be integrated into pre-season and in-season athletic screening to detect inter-limb horizontal force asymmetries in football players. Athletes showing greater fatigue-related FH asymmetry may benefit from targeted eccentric hamstring and hip extensor development to improve sprint endurance and reduce re-injury risk during the late stages of high-intensity match play.
rtp: A repeated-sprint test monitoring horizontal force production per leg is recommended as a sport-specific component of hamstring RTP assessment. Players who demonstrate a >11.5 N between-leg decline in FH across 10 sprints may not be ready for full return despite passing traditional strength tests. Rehabilitation should emphasise asymmetry reduction under fatigue, specifically targeting the eccentric knee flexor and hip extensor functions that underpin horizontal force production during sprinting.

citation: Esteve E, Rathleff MS, Hölmich P, Casals M, Clausen MB, Vicens-Bordas J, Pizzari T, Thorborg K. Groin problems from pre- to in-season: a prospective study on 386 male Spanish footballers. Res Sports Med. 2020. doi:10.1080/15438627.2020.1860044
abstract: This prospective cohort study investigated weekly prevalence of groin problems over a 3-week football pre-season compared to a 39-week competitive in-season in 17 amateur male Spanish football teams (386 players, tiers IV–VI). Players completed weekly groin-pain surveys and the HAGOS Sport/Recreation subscale; football staff registered time-loss groin injuries and team exposure. The average weekly prevalence of groin problems was 1.8 times higher (95% CI 1.6–2.0) during pre-season (21%) than in-season (12%). The elevated pre-season prevalence was driven by a higher rate of groin problems without time loss (19% vs. 10%; PR=1.8). Time-loss groin injury incidence and injury burden did not differ significantly between periods. HAGOS Sport/Rec scores were slightly but significantly lower pre-season among players reporting groin problems without time loss.
tldr: Groin problems are nearly twice as prevalent during football pre-season compared to the in-season, primarily because of a higher rate of non-time-loss groin pain. The spike in groin problems coincided with the transition from off-season to structured football training, emphasising that rapid load increases at the start of pre-season are a key risk period. Preventive measures — particularly hip adductor strengthening programmes — should be implemented proactively before and during pre-season rather than waiting for in-season injury surveillance to identify affected players.
methods: Prospective cohort study extending previously published in-season data (Esteve et al., 2020) with a 3-week pre-season monitoring period. A convenience sample of 17 amateur Spanish football teams (tiers IV–VI; 386 players) completed weekly online surveys asking about groin pain in the preceding week and the HAGOS Sport/Recreation subscale. Football staff registered time-loss groin injuries and team exposure in hours. Weekly groin problem prevalence was calculated for players with and without time loss. Prevalence ratios (PR) and prevalence differences (PD), incidence rate ratios (IRR), and linear mixed models for HAGOS scores were computed. Alpha level set at 5%.
findings: Average weekly prevalence of all groin problems was 21% pre-season vs. 12% in-season (PR=1.8; 95% CI 1.6–2.0; PD=9.0%). Groin problems without time loss were substantially higher pre-season (19% vs. 10%; PR=1.8; 95% CI 1.6–2.1). Time-loss groin injury prevalence (2.0% vs. 1.3%; PR=1.5; 95% CI 1.0–2.4) and time-loss injury incidence (1.2 vs. 0.9 per 1000 h) and burden were not significantly different. The highest weekly prevalence (28%) occurred in week 1 of pre-season. HAGOS Sport/Rec scores were marginally but significantly lower in players with non-time-loss groin problems during pre-season (mean difference: 3.1 points; 95% CI 0.3–6.0; P=0.03).
limitations: Team-level exposure registration led to slight underestimation of individual injury rates. Survey response rate during pre-season (75%) was lower than the in-season rate (93%). Only 3 weeks of pre-season data were captured despite teams having varying pre-season lengths (4–5 weeks). Findings may not generalise to extended pre-season periods or higher professional football levels. No controlled prevention strategies were initiated, so natural prevalence changes cannot be attributed to specific interventions.
practicalImplications: Pre-season groin pain should be considered a major injury management concern, not only in-season. Teams should conduct brief pre-season groin pain screening (two simple questions about past-season groin pain and its duration) to detect at-risk players early. Implementation of hip adductor strengthening programmes (e.g., the Copenhagen Adductor Programme) before and during pre-season is recommended to reduce the prevalence of groin problems through the subsequent in-season. Gradual progression of training loads from off-season to pre-season is critical.
athleteDev: Off-season should be used as an opportunity to build hip adductor capacity and address residual groin problems before pre-season loads resume. Players presenting with groin symptoms at the start of pre-season should be flagged using HAGOS questionnaires or the five-second squeeze test and managed proactively with adductor loading protocols to prevent escalation into time-loss injuries during the competitive season.
rtp: Players returning from groin or adductor injuries should be monitored closely during the high-load pre-season transition period, as this phase carries the highest weekly prevalence of groin problems. Specific return-to-training load thresholds and monitoring tools — including hip adductor strength tests (>15% reduction alert) and HAGOS scores (<70/100) — should be used to guide safe progression back to full training and match participation.

citation: Grosicki GJ, Langan SP, Bagley JR, Galpin AJ, Garner D, Hampton-Marcell JT, Allen JM, Robinson AT. Gut check: Unveiling the influence of acute exercise on the gut microbiota. Exp Physiol. 2023;1–15. doi:10.1113/EP091446
abstract: This comprehensive narrative review summarised 11 human studies (n=191; 147 males, 54 females) examining gut microbiota and faecal metabolite responses to acute and very short-term (<3 weeks) exercise. Literature was identified from PubMed and Web of Science (up to July 2023) using terms combining 'gut microbiome' AND 'acute exercise' OR 'short-term exercise'. The review evaluated evidence on changes in alpha-diversity, beta-diversity, individual microbial taxa and metabolic pathways, and faecal metabolites following single exercise bouts ranging from cycling and running to ultra-endurance events. Key conclusions are that acute aerobic exercise has: (i) limited effects on intra- and inter-individual gut microbial diversity; (ii) variable effects on specific microbial taxa likely driven by exercise intensity, duration, and interindividual differences; and (iii) numerous effects on microbial metabolic activity with potential implications for host health and performance.
tldr: A single bout of exercise causes modest, variable changes in gut microbial community structure but can meaningfully alter the metabolic output of gut microbes — including short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), bile acids, and amino acid metabolites — in ways that may benefit host health and athletic performance. The chronic gut health benefits of regular training are likely built upon these acute transient responses. Future research should prioritise rigorous experimental controls (diet, non-exercise controls, multiple sample time points) and larger, more diverse populations to clarify which specific exercise parameters drive the most beneficial microbiome adaptations.
methods: Narrative review with systematic literature search conducted in PubMed and Web of Science for all studies published in English combining 'gut microbiome' AND 'acute exercise' OR 'short-term exercise' up to July 2023. Eleven eligible human studies were identified and characterised across: study design, population characteristics (n=191), exercise stimulus, dietary control, non-exercising controls, microbiome measurement approach (16S rRNA amplicon sequencing or shotgun metagenomics), alpha- and beta-diversity findings, individual taxa changes, metabolic pathway shifts, and faecal metabolite changes. Evidence was synthesised narratively and methodological gaps and future research priorities were identified.
findings: Of 11 studies, 9 reported alpha-diversity findings — 5 found no change, 3 found increases (via Shannon Index) primarily after extreme exercise (ultra-marathon, transoceanic rowing, military ski), and 1 found a decrease. Six beta-diversity analyses showed mostly no significant change, with 2 reporting changes after extreme exercise. Nine of 11 studies reported changes in specific microbial taxa, with the most consistent finding being increased relative abundance of Veillonella (especially Veillonella atypica) following intense endurance exercise in highly trained athletes — a species that converts lactate to propionate. Four studies on faecal metabolites all reported substantial pre–post differences, including changes in amino acids, bile acids, carbohydrate metabolites, fatty acids, and nucleic acid-related compounds. Extreme exercise was the most potent driver of acute microbiome changes.
limitations: Only 11 studies with fewer than 200 total participants were identified, limiting statistical power and generalisability. Only 1 of 11 studies included a non-exercising control group. Dietary control was largely absent or not standardised. Most studies used 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing (lower resolution than shotgun metagenomics). Sample collection timing varied widely and was often imprecise relative to exercise. Substantial inter-individual variability in microbiome responses makes group-level findings difficult to apply individually. Only aerobic exercise modalities were studied; resistance exercise responses are essentially unknown.
practicalImplications: Sports practitioners should understand that acute exercise modestly impacts gut microbial diversity but can substantially alter microbial metabolite production, which may influence energy availability, immune function, and recovery. Designing training sessions to support gut health — including adequate hydration, avoiding extreme single-bout overload, and timing exercise relative to meals — may help optimise microbiome-mediated health benefits. Probiotic or dietary interventions timed around acute exercise bouts represent a promising area for future evidence-based performance nutrition strategies.
athleteDev: The finding that extreme endurance exercise transiently increases Veillonella abundance (converting lactate to propionate) suggests that training-induced gut microbiome adaptations may contribute to improved lactate clearance and energy metabolism in developing elite athletes. Progressive training load management — avoiding sudden spikes to extreme exercise — may help foster adaptive rather than disruptive acute microbiome responses as athletes develop their aerobic base.
rtp: Athletes returning from injury who resume training may experience acute changes in gut microbiota driven by the sudden reintroduction of exercise stress. While direct RTP implications are currently limited by sparse evidence, ensuring adequate dietary fibre intake, hydration, and gradual training load progression during the return-to-play continuum may support a gut microbiome environment conducive to tissue repair, immune modulation, and performance restoration.

citation: Ekstrand J, Walden M, Hagglund M. Hamstring injuries have increased by 4% annually in men's professional football, since 2001: a 13-year longitudinal analysis of the UEFA Elite Club Injury Study. Br J Sports Med. 2016;50(12):731–737. doi:10.1136/bjsports-2015-095359
abstract: This 13-year longitudinal analysis of the UEFA Elite Club Injury Study examined hamstring injury trends in men's professional football from 2001–02 to 2013–14 across up to 36 European clubs. The study tracked hamstring injury incidence, burden, severity, and recurrence rates over 13 seasons using standardised injury recording and team exposure data. Hamstring injury incidence increased significantly by approximately 4% per year over the 13-year period (p<0.001). Despite this concerning trend, injury burden (days lost per 1000 hours exposure) did not increase significantly, suggesting that average hamstring injury severity and/or return-to-play times may have improved over the period. Recurrence rates remained high and consistent throughout, accounting for approximately 16% of all hamstring injuries. The silver lining identified was that while incidence rose, clubs and medical teams appeared to improve management of individual injury episodes.
tldr: Hamstring injuries in elite European professional football increased by approximately 4% annually over a 13-year period — a significant and worsening epidemiological trend that has not been resolved by existing prevention efforts. However, injury burden (days lost per injury) did not increase proportionally, suggesting improved clinical management of individual episodes. The persistently high recurrence rate (~16%) indicates that return-to-play and rehabilitation standards need urgent improvement. These findings make a compelling case for mandatory adoption of evidence-based hamstring injury prevention programmes and more rigorous RTP criteria in elite football.
methods: Longitudinal prospective cohort study using data from the UEFA Elite Club Injury Study, one of the largest and longest-running sports injury surveillance programmes in professional football. Up to 36 professional European clubs participated across 13 consecutive seasons (2001–02 to 2013–14). Injury data were recorded by club medical staff using a standardised reporting system and exposure time was tracked as team training and match hours. Hamstring injuries were defined as any acute hamstring muscle injury causing absence from training or match play. Injury incidence (per 1000 h exposure), injury burden (days lost per 1000 h exposure), mean days absent, and recurrence rates were calculated. Time trends were assessed using Poisson regression analysis.
findings: Hamstring injury incidence increased by ~4% per year over 13 seasons (p<0.001), representing a substantial and statistically significant rise. Total hamstring injury burden did not increase significantly, indicating average time lost per injury may have improved. Recurrence rates remained consistently high at approximately 16% of all hamstring injuries across all seasons, with no significant improving trend. Hamstring injuries were the single most common injury type across all seasons, accounting for approximately 12% of all injuries. Match play carried a substantially higher injury rate than training. Despite awareness of the problem, no systematic reduction in incidence was achieved during the study period.
limitations: Injury surveillance relied on club medical staff reporting, which may introduce inter-club variability in recording practices. The study captured only time-loss injuries, potentially underestimating the full burden of hamstring problems. Specific injury mechanisms, anatomical locations, and MRI grading were not systematically captured in early seasons. Changes in training methods, match schedules, player populations, and prevention programme adoption were not controlled for. The silver lining interpretation (stable burden despite rising incidence) may also reflect earlier return-to-play decisions rather than genuine improvements in recovery.
practicalImplications: The persistent 4% annual increase in hamstring injury incidence across elite European clubs underscores the inadequacy of current prevention approaches at the population level. Sports medicine and performance staff should prioritise implementation of validated hamstring injury prevention programmes (e.g., the Nordic Hamstring Exercise protocol) and rigorous, criteria-based return-to-play frameworks to address both rising incidence and persistently high recurrence rates. Investment in surveillance, early detection, and evidence-based rehabilitation infrastructure is justified by the scale of the burden.
athleteDev: The growing hamstring injury epidemic in professional football highlights the need for systematic hamstring injury prevention to be embedded throughout the athletic development pathway — not only at the elite level. Young footballers should develop robust hamstring eccentric strength, sprint mechanics, and neuromuscular control as foundational qualities, with progressive loading across development phases to reduce lifetime hamstring injury risk before players reach elite competition.
rtp: With recurrence rates consistently around 16% across 13 seasons, return-to-play criteria for hamstring injuries in professional football are clearly insufficient. Objective, criteria-based RTP frameworks incorporating eccentric strength symmetry, running mechanics, sport-specific sprint loading, and functional testing should replace time-based or symptom-based clearance. The lack of improvement in recurrence rates over 13 years despite increased awareness represents a critical quality gap that must be addressed through standardised, evidence-based RTP protocols.

citation: Luxenburg D, Wasserman N, Geller J, Perez J, Burke J, Kaplan L. Hamstring injuries in the national football league: An epidemiological study. Journal of Orthopaedics. 2023;35:111–114.
abstract: NOTE: IDs 249 and 250 are the same paper. ID 249 is the journal pre-proof version (with cover page and '(1)' in filename); ID 250 is the final published version. Both contain identical content — the epidemiological study of hamstring strain injuries (HSIs) in the NFL from 2013–2016 regular season game books (n=78 HSIs). The study analyzed relative incidence by position (standardized incidence ratios), field surface (turf vs. grass), short rest (4-day weeks), and week of season. Linebackers (SIR 2.02) and Defensive Backs (SIR 1.62) had the highest relative incidence; Offensive and Defensive Linemen had the lowest (SIRs 0.28 and 0.29). No significant effect of field surface (p=0.082) or short rest (p=0.959) was found. Mean week of HSI was 8.05 in a 17-week season.
tldr: This NFL epidemiological study found that Linebackers and Defensive Backs sustain hamstring injuries at roughly twice the expected rate relative to their time on the field, while Offensive and Defensive Linemen are at substantially lower risk. Neither artificial turf nor playing on short rest (Thursday games) significantly increased hamstring injury rates compared to other injury types or to games played on normal rest. Hamstring injuries tended to occur slightly earlier in the season than other injuries. For football staff, position-specific monitoring and targeted prevention programs for skill-position players — particularly those who sprint, cut, and change direction frequently — are warranted.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological analysis using publicly available official NFL game books from the 2013–2016 regular seasons. Seventy-eight qualifying in-game hamstring strain injuries (strains, partial tears, complete tears) were identified and cross-referenced with Foxsports.com.
