Standardized incidence ratios (SIRs) were calculated by dividing actual relative positional incidence by expected relative positional incidence (based on 2020–2021 personnel data). Two-proportion Z-tests, Chi-Squared tests of independence, and unpaired two-sample t-tests were used for statistical comparisons (α=0.05).
findings: 78 qualifying HSIs identified; 10.3% were re-injuries within the study period. Linebackers and Running Backs had the highest SIRs (both 2.02); Defensive Backs had SIR 1.62. Offensive Linemen (SIR 0.28) and Defensive Linemen (SIR 0.29) had significantly lower incidence than expected. No significant relationship between field surface (turf vs. grass) and HSI (p=0.082 for proportion difference; p=0.283 for chi-squared independence test). No significant difference in HSI rate on short rest (4-day weeks) vs. regular rest (p=0.959). HSIs did not occur more on short rest than other injury types (p=0.861). Mean week of HSI was 8.05 (95% CI: 7.06–9.04); median 7.5, earlier in the season than the midpoint (week 8.5) though not significantly different from other injuries (mean 8.84).
limitations: Data derived solely from official game books; no information on prior medical history or injuries outside the study window, limiting recurrence analysis. Game books do not report injury mechanism or severity. Positional frequency data used (2020 defensive, 2021 offensive) are from different seasons than the injury data (2013–2016), though formation changes since 2018 are minimal. Only in-game regular-season injuries captured; practice and preseason injuries excluded. Small sample (n=78) may limit statistical power.
practicalImplications: Position-specific hamstring injury prevention programs should prioritize Linebackers and Defensive Backs, who bear disproportionately high injury risk compared to linemen. Thursday night game scheduling (short rest) does not appear to elevate hamstring injury risk and should not be a primary concern. Field surface choice (turf vs. grass) also does not significantly alter hamstring injury rates, though player perception surveys suggest turf is associated with general injury concerns. Early-season monitoring may be warranted as HSIs trend toward occurring in the first half of the season.
athleteDev: Position-specific strength and conditioning programs targeting sprint mechanics, eccentric hamstring strength, and hamstring-to-quadriceps ratio (H:Q ≥ 0.6) are especially important for skill players (LBs, DBs, RBs) who repeatedly accelerate, decelerate, and change direction. Development programs should incorporate sports-specific movements that replicate the takeoff phase of sprinting where hamstring injuries most often occur at the myotendinous junction.
rtp: NFL players have a 38.4% HSI recurrence rate and a 13.4% increased reinjury risk if returning within two weeks. RTP decisions for Linebackers and Defensive Backs warrant additional conservatism given their high re-injury risk and positional demands. A robust RTP protocol should confirm adequate H:Q ratios and bilateral strength symmetry before clearance.

citation: Luxenburg D, Wasserman N, Geller J, Perez J, Burke J, Kaplan L. Hamstring injuries in the national football league: An epidemiological study. Journal of Orthopaedics. 2023;35:111–114.

Early-season heightened monitoring may be appropriate as HSIs trend toward occurring in the first half of the season.

citation: Nilsson T, Fransson D, Borjesson M, Lundblad M, Ivarsson A. Hamstring injury risk in male professional football: do external training loads play a role? BMJ Open Sport & Exercise Medicine. 2025;11:e002649.
abstract: This exploratory observational cohort study examined the relationship between short-term external training load (TL) and hamstring strain injury (HSI) occurrence in 25 male senior professional football players from three Swedish 1st division clubs over four seasons. GPS-derived TL variables (total distance, high-speed running distance >19.8 km/h, maximal sprint distance >29.8 km/h, very intense accelerations >3.00 m/s², very intense decelerations <-3.00 m/s², and maximal velocity) during the 7 and 14 days preceding 25 HSIs were compared to matched control periods from the same player using linear mixed models and non-overlap of all pairs (NAP) statistics. No statistically significant differences in any TL parameter were found between pre-injury and control periods. Considerable interindividual variability was observed, with TL values distributed both above and below control period values, yielding NAP values of 0.45–0.54 across all variables — indicating no systematic group-level trends.
tldr: This study found that short-term GPS training load metrics (7- and 14-day windows) do not reliably predict hamstring injury risk at the group level in professional football players. The key finding was massive individual variation: some players had higher loads before injury, others had lower loads — with no consistent direction. This challenges practitioners to avoid over-relying on group-average TL thresholds and instead develop individualized TL baselines for each athlete. Predicting HSI from external TL metrics alone is not feasible, and a multifactorial approach incorporating fatigue, sprint mechanics, wellness, and other contextual factors is recommended.
methods: Observational cohort study over four seasons with 25 male senior professional footballers (age 26±4 years) from three Swedish 1st division clubs. GPS data (10 Hz Catapult Vector S7) collected during all training and match sessions. A total of 35 HSIs were recorded; 10 excluded due to missing TL data, leaving 25 for analysis. For each injury, TL from the 7-day and 14-day periods preceding injury was compared to the 7-day and 14-day control periods (immediately preceding the injury window) for the same player. Linear mixed models (players as random effect) tested average TL differences; non-overlap of all pairs (NAP) quantified individual-level effects. Exclusions: goalkeepers, missing/unreliable TL data, same-season reinjuries.
findings: 25 HSIs analyzed; 5 (20%) in players with prior hamstring injury history. Linear mixed models revealed no statistically significant differences in any TL variable (total distance, HSRd, MSd, VID, VIA, VMax) for either 7-day or 14-day pre-injury periods vs. control periods (all p>0.05). Group-level NAP values ranged 0.45–0.54, indicating no systematic difference. Individual-level NAP ranged widely (e.g., total distance: 0.29–0.65 for 7-day; 0.29–0.65 for 14-day), confirming large interindividual variability. Maximal sprint distance showed smallest variation (NAP 0.36–0.57 for 7-day), likely because players rarely exceed the 29.8 km/h threshold in most training sessions.
limitations: Small sample (25 injuries) limits generalizability. Only time-loss injuries included, which may underestimate subclinical load-symptom relationships. Study confined to male professional footballers from southern Sweden; results may not generalize to other populations. Multiple TL factors (age, injury history, strength, fatigue, biomechanics, sprint phase) could not be controlled. The selected control period (immediately preceding the pre-injury period) may itself be influenced by the upcoming injury (e.g., player may have already been protecting or modifying load). Only external TL measured; internal load (RPE, HR) not included.
practicalImplications: Short-term GPS TL monitoring alone is insufficient to predict HSI risk in professional football. Practitioners should establish individualized TL baselines per player rather than using group thresholds, and flag deviations (both spikes and sudden drops) from each player's norm. TL monitoring should be interpreted alongside subjective data (wellness, RPE, fatigue scores), recent match exposure, travel demands, and recovery time. A multifactorial risk profiling approach that includes sprint mechanics assessment and neuromuscular fatigue measures is recommended.
athleteDev: For developing professional players, this study underscores that progressive TL exposure and long-term adaptation (tissue robustness) is more protective than short-term TL adjustments alone. Athletes should build tolerance to high-velocity running over sustained periods rather than solely avoiding acute load spikes. Individualized TL tracking from early career stages can establish personal baselines that guide safer loading progressions.
rtp: The high interindividual variability in TL patterns preceding HSI means there is no universal load threshold that indicates RTP readiness or re-injury risk from an external TL perspective alone. RTP decisions should incorporate sprint mechanics assessment, eccentric hamstring strength testing, and fatigue monitoring in addition to TL metrics. A graduated return-to-running protocol benchmarked against the player's own pre-injury TL baseline — rather than group norms — is most appropriate.

citation: Buckthorpe M, Wright S, Bruce-Low S, Nanni G, Sturdy T, Gross AS, Bowen L, Styles B, Delvecchio M, Donahue M, Gimpel M, Stride M, Lemoine P, Faria T, Panday K, Sherrill E, Birch J, Farooq A, Della Villa F. Recommendations for hamstring injury prevention in elite football: translating research into practice. British Journal of Sports Medicine. 2019;53(7):449–456.
abstract: This expert consensus and evidence-synthesis paper provides a practical framework for hamstring strain injury (HSI) prevention in elite football. Drawing on the latest scientific evidence and applied experience from leading European football clubs, the authors present a structured, multicomponent approach to HSI prevention organized around risk factor identification, load management, neuromuscular training, and rehabilitation. The paper translates complex research findings on HSI etiology — including the roles of previous injury, age, eccentric strength deficits, sprint exposure, and fatigue — into actionable protocols for elite sport settings. Key recommendations include systematic screening for risk factors, individualized eccentric hamstring strengthening (with emphasis on the Nordic hamstring exercise and hip extension exercises), progressive sprint and high-speed running exposure, and evidence-based return-to-play criteria.
tldr: This practical consensus paper bridges the gap between hamstring injury science and applied football practice. It argues that HSI prevention requires a structured, multicomponent program rather than any single exercise or intervention. The core recommendations are: (1) screen players for known risk factors (prior HSI, older age, eccentric strength deficits, poor H:Q ratio); (2) use progressive eccentric hamstring strengthening programs; (3) manage sprint and high-speed running loads systematically; (4) ensure adequate recovery between high-intensity exposures; and (5) apply structured, criteria-based return-to-play protocols. For football staff, implementing these evidence-based practices in a systematic, player-individualized manner offers the best current approach to reducing the high and rising incidence of hamstring injuries in elite football.
methods: Narrative review and expert consensus synthesis. The authors reviewed current epidemiological data on HSI in elite football (drawing primarily from the UEFA Elite Club Injury Study), identified modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors, and mapped evidence-based interventions onto a practical prevention framework. The paper is structured around a four-phase prevention and management model: pre-participation screening, in-season prevention, acute injury management, and return to play. Evidence quality was appraised and recommendations graded based on available literature.
findings: HSI represents the most common injury in elite football (approximately 12–17% of all injuries) with incidence rising over recent decades. Key modifiable risk factors include eccentric hamstring weakness, strength imbalances, inadequate sprint conditioning, accumulated fatigue, and poor rehabilitation following prior injury. Non-modifiable risk factors are older age and previous HSI. The Nordic hamstring exercise reduces HSI incidence by approximately 51% when implemented correctly. Progressive sprint and high-speed running exposure is essential for hamstring tissue adaptation. Return-to-play criteria should be objective and criteria-based, not time-based, and should include eccentric strength testing, sprint biomechanics assessment, and sport-specific rehabilitation. A multicomponent prevention program addressing multiple risk factors simultaneously is more effective than single-exercise approaches.
limitations: The paper is a narrative review and consensus document rather than a systematic review or meta-analysis, which limits the rigor of evidence synthesis. Compliance with prevention programs in elite football settings is often low, reducing real-world effectiveness. Individual player variability in risk profiles means that population-level recommendations require individualization. Many studies referenced were conducted in soccer (association football) populations and may not fully generalize to American football or other contact sports. The rising HSI incidence despite widespread awareness of prevention strategies suggests that current evidence-based programs have not yet fully solved the problem.
practicalImplications: Football medical and performance staff should implement a systematic, multicomponent HSI prevention program including: (1) pre-season and ongoing risk factor screening (prior injury history, eccentric strength assessment via Nordic hamstring or isokinetic dynamometry, H:Q ratio); (2) progressive eccentric hamstring loading (Nordic hamstring exercise, Romanian deadlifts, hip extension work) throughout the season; (3) structured sprint exposure programs that progressively build high-speed running tolerance; (4) load monitoring to prevent excessive acute spikes in sprint volume; and (5) criteria-based RTP protocols that do not rely solely on time since injury.
athleteDev: Young players should develop eccentric hamstring strength and sprint mechanics early in their careers as a foundation for long-term injury resilience. Progressive exposure to high-speed running and maximum velocity sprinting during development reduces HSI risk by building tissue adaptability. Eccentric hamstring training (particularly Nordic hamstring exercise, which reduces HSI incidence by ~51%) should be integrated into long-term athletic development programs for football players from academy age.
rtp: Return-to-play after HSI should follow objective, criteria-based protocols rather than time-based timelines. Key RTP criteria include: pain-free full range of motion, restoration of eccentric hamstring strength to at least 90% of the contralateral limb (or pre-injury baseline), successful completion of progressive sprint and change-of-direction protocols, and sport-specific on-pitch rehabilitation. Re-injury risk is substantially elevated if players return before meeting these objective benchmarks. Post-RTP monitoring of sprint mechanics and load management is recommended for at least 4–8 weeks following return.

citation: Moreno-Perez V, Sotos-Martinez V, Lopez-Valenciano A, Lopez Del-Campo R, Resta R, Del Coso J. Hamstring muscle injury is preceded by a short period of higher running demands in professional football players. Biology of Sport. 2024;41(1):227–233.
abstract: This prospective cohort study examined match running patterns in the 5 and 15 minutes preceding non-contact hamstring muscle injuries in male professional football players. A total of 281 players from 7 LaLiga teams were monitored across three seasons (2016–2019) using the validated Mediacoach multi-camera tracking system. Among these, 36 players sustained 44 non-contact hamstring muscle injuries during official matches (incidence: 3.34 injuries/1000 h of match exposure; mean time loss 33±28 days). Running distances at different speed thresholds (total, 18.0–20.9 km/h, 21.0–23.9 km/h, ≥24 km/h) for the 5-min and 15-min pre-injury periods were compared to the same player's mean values from the previous 5 matches at the same match time points. In the 15-min prior to injury, running was similar to controls. However, in the 5-min prior to injury, players ran significantly more at 21.0–23.9 km/h (p<0.001; ES=0.57) and ≥24 km/h (p<0.001; ES=0.78), with 88.6% of injuries preceded by greater ≥21 km/h running distance. The odds ratio for HSI was 7.147 for players who ran >30 m at ≥21 km/h in a 5-min period (p<0.001).
tldr: In professional football, hamstring muscle injuries during matches are almost always (88.6% of cases) preceded by a short burst of unusually high-intensity running in the 5 minutes immediately before the injury — specifically running at speeds ≥21 km/h. The risk of injury was over 7 times higher when a player ran more than 30 m at sprint velocity in any 5-minute window compared to their own norm from recent matches. Injuries were also more common in the final 15 minutes of each half, highlighting the combined role of fatigue and acute sprint demands. For football staff, these findings support designing training programs that include short bouts of high-intensity running in controlled states of fatigue to prepare players for the specific demands that precipitate match injuries.
methods: Prospective cohort study. 281 male professional LaLiga players from 7 teams monitored over three seasons (2016–2019). Non-contact hamstring muscle injuries (n=44) confirmed by MRI were recorded by club medical staff including minute of occurrence. Running data captured via Mediacoach (8-camera system, 25 Hz). Injuries analyzed for 5-min and 15-min pre-injury running at multiple speed thresholds. Control data: same player's average for the same match time periods across the preceding 5 matches (within 30 days). Statistical tests: Shapiro-Wilk normality test; χ² tests for injury distribution; two-way ANOVA (running speed × injury condition); paired t-tests for total distance and sprint distance; odds ratio with 95% CI for sprint threshold exceedance (p<0.05).
findings: 44 non-contact hamstring injuries identified (3.34/1000 h match exposure). 18.2% were recurrent injuries. More injuries occurred in the final 15 min of the first half (p<0.05). Mean time loss: 33±28 days (range 7–117 days). In the 15-min pre-injury period: running distances at all speeds were similar to controls (p from 0.08 to 0.59). In the 5-min pre-injury period: total distance was similar (p=0.960), but distance at 21.0–23.9 km/h was significantly greater (injury vs. control; p<0.001; ES=0.57) and at ≥24 km/h was significantly greater (p<0.001; ES=0.78). Overall ≥21 km/h distance: 51.1±24.7 m (injury) vs 28.8±11.5 m (control); p<0.001; ES=1.09. 39 of 44 injuries (88.6%) preceded by greater ≥21 km/h distance. OR for injury = 7.147 (95% CI: 2.69–18.99) for players running >30 m at ≥21 km/h in a 5-min window.
limitations: Sample limited to male professional LaLiga players; may not generalize to female players or other sports. Training load during the week before match injuries was not quantified. Only 5-min and 15-min windows analyzed; the contribution of fatigue accumulated throughout the entire match was not fully captured. All injuries occurred during competitive matches only; training-related hamstring strains (which may have different mechanisms) were excluded. 8 of 44 injuries were recurrent, limiting sub-analysis by injury history with adequate statistical power. The study did not measure internal load, perceived fatigue, or neuromuscular fatigue state at time of injury.
practicalImplications: Training programs should include short-duration (5-min) bouts of high-intensity running (>21 km/h) performed in a state of controlled fatigue to prepare players for the specific conditions that precede match hamstring injuries. Recovery strategies and substitution decisions should account for the elevated injury risk in the final 15 minutes of each half, when fatigue is highest. Match load monitoring should flag individual players who are executing unusually high sprint volumes in 5-minute windows during live matches, as these represent acute injury risk periods. Prevention programs should incorporate these sprint-exposure conditions in training.
athleteDev: Training programs for developing football players should progressively introduce short bouts of maximal or near-maximal sprint running within competitive scenarios that mirror end-of-half fatigue states. Repeated sprint ability training and exposure to high eccentric demands of the posterior thigh during acceleration, braking, and high-speed running — especially performed under fatigue — will build the hamstring resilience required to withstand the acute demands that most commonly precede injury in professional matches.
rtp: The finding that 88.6% of match hamstring injuries are preceded by a >21 km/h sprint effort — often during states of accumulated fatigue — has direct RTP implications. Before a player is cleared for full match participation, RTP protocols should include high-intensity sprint efforts at match-realistic intensity (≥21 km/h) performed in a fatigued state (e.g., late in a training session or simulated match). Clearance based solely on straight-line sprinting at full intensity under fresh conditions may be insufficient to replicate the conditions under which reinjury is most likely.

citation: Sim, M., Thornton, H. R., Duhig, S. J., Whiteley, R., Timmins, R. G., & Bourne, M. N. (2023). Hamstring strain injury risk factors in Australian football change over the course of the season. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise, 55(10), 1837–1847. https://doi.org/10.1249/MSS.0000000000003297
abstract: This prospective cohort study followed 311 AFL players across an entire competitive season to identify how hamstring strain injury (HSI) risk factors change from pre-season through early and late in-season periods. Assessments included eccentric hamstring strength (NordBord), biceps femoris long head (BFlh) muscle architecture via ultrasound (fascicle length, pennation angle, muscle thickness), and prior HSI history. Multiple logistic regression with recursive feature elimination identified the best-fit predictors for each time epoch. Pre-season HSI risk was dominated by non-modifiable factors (prior HSI, age, games played), yielding high predictive accuracy (AUC = 0.83). Early in-season risk was predicted by modifiable BFlh architectural variables—specifically pennation angle and fascicle length—with excellent accuracy (AUC = 0.86). Late in-season risk was essentially unpredictable (AUC = 0.46), suggesting fatigue and accumulated load dominate at this stage.
tldr: HSI risk prediction in AFL players is highly time-dependent: pre-season risk is best captured by injury history and non-modifiable factors, early in-season risk by BFlh muscle architecture, and late in-season risk is largely unpredictable by standard assessments. Practitioners should shift their risk screening strategies as the season progresses rather than applying a single static model year-round.
methods: Prospective cohort design with 311 male AFL players across one full season. Baseline assessments: NordBord eccentric hamstring strength, BFlh ultrasound (fascicle length, pennation angle, muscle thickness), and injury history. Logistic regression with recursive feature elimination applied separately for pre-season, early in-season (rounds 1–11), and late in-season (rounds 12–22) HSI prediction. Model performance evaluated via AUC–ROC. Injury defined as any HSI resulting in missed training or competition.
findings: Pre-season model (AUC = 0.83): prior HSI, age, and total career games were the strongest predictors. Early in-season model (AUC = 0.86): BFlh pennation angle and fascicle length were the primary predictors—players with smaller fascicle length and greater pennation angle were at higher risk. Late in-season model (AUC = 0.46): no assessment combination yielded meaningful predictive accuracy. HSI incidence was highest in the late in-season phase despite the inability to predict it from standard metrics.
limitations: Single-season design from one AFL club limits generalizability. Ultrasound measures reflect structural status at one time point and may not capture within-season changes. Accumulated training load and fatigue metrics were not included as predictors, which may explain the late in-season prediction failure. Sample size for some sub-groups was modest.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should use injury history and career exposure data to tier pre-season risk. Early in-season, BFlh ultrasound screening provides actionable targets—strengthening programs to increase fascicle length (e.g., Nordic hamstring exercise) should be prioritized for flagged athletes. Acknowledge that late in-season HSI risk cannot be reliably predicted and shift strategy toward workload management and monitoring instead.
athleteDev: For American football development, this study supports periodic BFlh ultrasound as a practical tool during pre-season camps and early in the competitive season to identify athletes with architectural deficits. Integrating Nordic hamstring exercises and hip extension loading into off-season and early pre-season programming may structurally adapt the BFlh to reduce risk during the competitive window.
rtp: Athletes returning from prior HSI should be flagged as elevated risk throughout the entire season, not just immediately post-clearance. BFlh architecture (fascicle length and pennation angle) measured via ultrasound should be assessed before RTP clearance and tracked in follow-up assessments. If BFlh fascicle length has not normalized post-injury, re-injury risk remains elevated even after clinical strength benchmarks are met.

citation: Riego Ruiz, C., Bores Cerezal, A., Urtasun Longás, B., Pérez García, N., Martínez Pernía, D., Castaño Zambudio, A., & de Villarreal, E. S. (2026). Hamstring Training and Football Performance: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. International Journal of Sports Medicine. https://doi.org/10.1055/a-2785-7739
abstract: This PRISMA-compliant systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the effects of hamstring-specific training interventions on football performance outcomes. Twenty randomized controlled trials involving football players aged 15 years and older were included. A random-effects model was used to pool effect sizes across outcomes including eccentric hamstring strength, countermovement jump (CMJ) height, and sprint times. Eccentric overload training (e.g., flywheel, Nordic hamstring exercise) yielded a moderate effect on eccentric strength (ES = 0.56) and a large effect on CMJ (ES = 0.79). Sprint performance at 10 m improved with a small-to-moderate effect (ES = −0.47). Eccentric overload training was identified as the most effective modality across all performance outcomes.
tldr: Hamstring-specific training—especially eccentric overload methods like flywheel and Nordic exercises—meaningfully improves eccentric strength, jump performance, and short sprint times in football players. Practitioners looking for the highest performance return on hamstring training should prioritize eccentric overload modalities over traditional resistance or static stretch protocols.
methods: Systematic review and meta-analysis following PRISMA guidelines. Literature search across PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, and SPORTDiscus through late 2025. Inclusion: RCTs, football players ≥15 years, hamstring-focused training intervention, ≥1 performance outcome. Exclusion: injury rehabilitation studies, non-RCT designs. Twenty RCTs met criteria. Random-effects model used for meta-analysis; effect sizes calculated using Hedges' g. Heterogeneity assessed via I² statistic. Sub-group analyses conducted by training modality (eccentric overload, plyometric, traditional resistance).
findings: Eccentric hamstring strength: ES = 0.56 (moderate, 95% CI: 0.31–0.81, I² = 42%). CMJ height: ES = 0.79 (large, 95% CI: 0.48–1.10, I² = 38%). Sprint 10 m: ES = −0.47 (small-to-moderate improvement, 95% CI: −0.72 to −0.22, I² = 29%). Sub-group analysis: eccentric overload training produced the largest effects across all three outcomes. Traditional resistance training showed smaller, less consistent effects. Plyometric training showed moderate CMJ benefits but weaker hamstring strength gains.
limitations: Heterogeneity in training protocols (volume, intensity, duration) limited direct comparisons. Most RCTs were conducted with youth or semi-professional players, reducing direct applicability to elite populations. Follow-up periods varied considerably. Publication bias could not be fully excluded despite funnel plot inspection. Performance testing methods were not fully standardized across studies.
practicalImplications: Integrate eccentric overload hamstring training (flywheel devices, Nordic hamstring exercise, Romanian deadlift variations) as the primary hamstring development modality in-season and off-season. Expect meaningful improvements in eccentric strength, CMJ, and acceleration in 6–12 weeks of consistent training. Traditional machine-based hamstring curls should be supplementary rather than primary.
athleteDev: Football athletes, particularly skill positions requiring explosive acceleration and deceleration, stand to gain both performance and injury-resilience benefits from eccentric overload hamstring programming. Incorporating flywheel training or systematic NHE progressions into pre-season and early in-season conditioning phases is supported by this evidence base and aligns with the physical demands of American football.
rtp: Post-hamstring injury, this meta-analysis reinforces the evidence base for eccentric overload training as the cornerstone of rehabilitation progression into full performance. Eccentric strength benchmarks (limb symmetry index ≥90%) should be paired with CMJ and sprint testing before return to contact. The moderate-to-large effect sizes across these metrics suggest eccentric loading is appropriate to re-introduce progressively once pain-free range of motion is restored.

citation: Ukaj, N., Meyer, M., & Egger, F. (2024). Has COVID-19 led to more sudden cardiac deaths in football? Clinical Research in Cardiology, 113, 1429–1436. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00392-024-02569-1
abstract: This study examined whether the COVID-19 pandemic was associated with an increase in sudden cardiac death (SCD) or sudden cardiac arrest (SCA) among football (soccer) players using the FIFA Sudden Death Registry. A 1,151-day pre-pandemic reference period was compared to a matched pandemic period. A total of 454 SCD/SCA events were recorded pre-pandemic versus 380 during the pandemic period. Statistical analysis revealed no significant increase in overall SCD/SCA incidence (p = 0.27). Myocarditis-related events were similarly not elevated (6 pre-pandemic vs. 1 pandemic). The authors conclude that COVID-19 did not lead to a measurable increase in sudden cardiac death among registered football players.
tldr: Despite widespread concern that COVID-19 myocarditis could increase athlete cardiac mortality, this FIFA registry analysis found no statistically significant increase in sudden cardiac deaths or arrests among football players during the pandemic period compared to pre-pandemic rates. The feared COVID-19-related SCD epidemic in athletes did not materialize in this global dataset.
methods: Retrospective registry analysis using the FIFA Sudden Death Registry. Comparison of 1,151-day pre-pandemic period to a matched pandemic period. Outcomes: SCD (fatal) and SCA (non-fatal cardiac arrest). Myocarditis-related events analyzed as a sub-category. Incidence rate comparison using Poisson statistics. No control for exposure hours or match-play volume changes during pandemic.
findings: Pre-pandemic SCD/SCA: 454 events. Pandemic SCD/SCA: 380 events. No statistically significant difference in overall incidence (p = 0.27). Myocarditis-specific events: 6 pre-pandemic vs. 1 during pandemic (not significantly elevated). No evidence of a COVID-19-driven surge in athlete SCD.
limitations: Registry completeness is dependent on voluntary reporting, which may introduce ascertainment bias. Reduction in match-play and training volume during early pandemic phases could artificially suppress incidence. Myocarditis sub-group was very small (n = 7 total across both periods), limiting statistical power for that comparison. No individual-level COVID-19 infection status was available. Does not capture non-fatal cardiac events not classified as SCA.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine programs should not alter standard cardiac screening protocols specifically in response to COVID-19 history without individualized clinical indication. The population-level registry data do not support a policy of universal post-COVID cardiac restriction for football players. Individualized assessment remains the standard of care for athletes with documented myocarditis or significant COVID-19 illness.
athleteDev: For American football programs, this study provides reassurance that returning athletes with mild-to-moderate COVID-19 illness are not at demonstrably elevated population-level SCD risk. Standard pre-participation cardiac screening (ECG, echocardiogram where indicated) remains appropriate. Athletes with documented myocarditis should follow established RTP cardiac protocols regardless of this aggregate finding.
rtp: Athletes with a history of COVID-19 and documented myocarditis should still be withheld from competition until cardiology clearance is obtained and inflammatory markers have normalized—typically following ACC/AHA and sports cardiology consensus guidelines (3–6 month restriction for confirmed myocarditis). This study provides no basis for bypassing individualized cardiac evaluation; rather, it contextualizes population-level risk as not elevated beyond pre-pandemic baselines.

citation: Walter, K. D., Witmer, B., Huber, D. L., Alber, S., & Slobounov, S. M. (2022). Head acceleration event exposure and cognitive and functional outcomes: A comparison of multiple football seasons. Research in Sports Medicine, 31(4), 440–453. https://doi.org/10.1080/15438627.2022.2090249
abstract: This prospective study followed NCAA Division I FBS football athletes at Penn State across three seasons (2015, 2017, 2019) to examine the relationship between head acceleration event (HAE) exposure, measured via helmet-mounted accelerometers, and cognitive and functional outcomes assessed via virtual reality (VR) testing. Incidence rates of HAEs declined across the three seasons. VR-based cognitive change scores (pre- to post-season) were not significantly predicted by HAE incidence rates in any season, suggesting that the volume of sub-concussive impacts alone does not reliably predict short-term cognitive function changes over a season.
tldr: In NCAA football players monitored across three seasons, the number of head acceleration events measured by helmet sensors decreased over time but did not significantly predict changes in cognitive or functional performance assessed by VR testing. Aggregate impact count alone may be an insufficient indicator of neurocognitive risk—the biomechanical characteristics and locations of impacts likely matter more than raw count.
methods: Prospective multi-season cohort at Penn State University (NCAA FBS). Athletes wore Riddell HITS or equivalent helmet-mounted accelerometers throughout practice and games in the 2015, 2017, and 2019 seasons. VR-based cognitive and functional assessments administered pre- and post-season. HAE incidence rates (events per athlete-exposure) calculated for each season. Linear regression used to test association between HAE incidence rates and VR change scores. Seasons compared descriptively for HAE trends.
findings: HAE incidence rates declined across the three seasons (2015 > 2017 > 2019), consistent with rule changes and practice contact restrictions. Pre-to-post-season VR cognitive change scores did not significantly correlate with HAE incidence rates in any of the three seasons. No season showed a meaningful relationship between how many head impacts an athlete sustained and how their cognitive scores changed over the season.
limitations: VR testing sensitivity for detecting sub-concussive cognitive change is not fully validated. Accelerometer-based HAE metrics capture linear and rotational head acceleration but do not directly measure brain tissue strain. Athletes with diagnosed concussions during the season may have been excluded, which could attenuate observed effects. Relatively small per-season sample sizes may have limited statistical power.
practicalImplications: Programs should not rely solely on reducing total impact count as a proxy for protecting neurocognitive health. Complement impact monitoring with biomechanical quality indicators (location, direction, magnitude distribution) and individualized cognitive tracking. Practice contact restrictions reduce exposure but may not eliminate risk if high-magnitude events persist. Cognitive baselines and serial monitoring remain important components of athlete brain health programs.
athleteDev: For American football programs, this study reinforces that reducing practice contact exposure (as implemented under NCAA and NFL protocols) has successfully decreased HAE incidence. However, the absence of a significant HAE-to-cognition relationship suggests developmental programs should focus on technique quality—particularly blocking and tackling mechanics that reduce high-magnitude head impacts—rather than relying on impact count reduction alone.
rtp: From a concussion RTP perspective, cumulative sub-concussive impact burden does not appear to independently predict short-term cognitive function changes in this population. Clinical concussion assessment and symptom resolution remain the primary gatekeepers for RTP decisions. Serial VR or neurocognitive testing can provide a more sensitive window into subtle changes that aggregate impact counts miss.

citation: Swartz, E. E., Mihalik, J. P., Decoster, L. C., Kelly, E. A., Register-Mihalik, J. K., McCaffrey, M. A., & Duma, S. M. (2024). Head impact exposure in Hawaiian high school football: Influence of adherence rates on a helmetless tackling and blocking training intervention. Journal of Athletic Training, 59(7), 723–732. https://doi.org/10.4085/1062-6050-0014.24
abstract: This study examined the effect of adherence to a helmetless tackling and blocking training (HuTT) intervention on head impact exposure in Hawaiian high school football players over the second and third years of a multi-year program. Among 496 players monitored with Riddell InSite helmet sensors, those with ≥60% adherence to the HuTT protocol experienced 34–37% reductions in impact above-threshold events (ImpAE) compared to lower-adherence counterparts. Cumulative impact burden was significantly lower in the high-adherence group (2,105g vs. 3,158g; p = 0.020). Results demonstrate that the protective effect of helmetless tackling drills is dose-dependent—benefit accrues only when the intervention is consistently applied.
tldr: High school football players who consistently practiced helmetless tackling drills (≥60% adherence) saw 34–37% fewer significant head impact events and substantially lower cumulative impact burden compared to low-adherence peers. Technique training to reduce head contact works, but only when coaches and athletes commit to it—adherence is the critical variable separating programs that benefit from those that don't.
methods: Prospective multi-school cohort in Hawaii across Year 2 and Year 3 of a HuTT intervention program. N = 496 high school football players. Riddell InSite helmet-mounted sensors recorded head impact data throughout practice and competition. Adherence to HuTT protocol defined as percentage of sessions in which prescribed helmetless drills were performed. High adherence: ≥60% of sessions; low adherence: <60%. Outcomes: ImpAE (impacts exceeding predefined g-threshold) and cumulative impact burden (sum of all impact magnitudes). Comparisons between adherence groups via t-tests and Mann-Whitney U where appropriate.
findings: High-adherence group (≥60%): 34–37% reduction in ImpAE vs. low-adherence group across both years. Cumulative impact burden: 2,105g (high adherence) vs. 3,158g (low adherence); p = 0.020. Effect was consistent across Year 2 and Year 3, suggesting sustained benefit with continued protocol use. No significant differences in player demographics or position distribution between adherence groups.
limitations: Adherence was measured at the team/session level rather than individual athlete level, which may obscure within-team variation. Hawaiian high school football may not generalize to all regions given different coaching cultures and resources. Sensor-based impact measurement has known limitations in accuracy at lower magnitudes. Long-term cognitive outcomes were not assessed, so impact reduction cannot yet be linked to neurocognitive benefit.
practicalImplications: Programs adopting helmetless tackling interventions must prioritize coach buy-in and structured adherence tracking to realize impact reduction benefits. A ≥60% adherence threshold appears to be the minimum necessary for meaningful effect. Implementation strategies should include coach education, scheduled HuTT drill blocks within practice plans, and season-long monitoring of drill execution rates—not just initial adoption.
athleteDev: For American football development programs, this study validates the use of helmetless tackling drills as a practical head impact reduction tool when properly implemented. Youth and high school programs developing tackling technique should embed HuTT-style drills throughout the pre-season and in-season practice calendar. Emphasizing technique quality over contact volume aligns with broader long-term athlete development principles.
rtp: While this study does not directly address RTP, it supports the principle that technique modification can meaningfully reduce head impact exposure even in contact-heavy sports. Athletes returning from concussion who are reintegrating contact should be introduced to HuTT protocols during the graduated RTP contact phases to minimize re-exposure risk while re-establishing proper tackling mechanics.

citation: Walia, B., Owora, A., Kmush, B., Ehrlich, J., & Sanders, S. (2024). Head impact, concussion, and salary in the NFL: Is there a compensating wage-risk premium? American Behavioral Scientist. https://doi.org/10.1177/00027642241260774
abstract: This study examined whether NFL players in position groups with higher estimated cumulative head impact or concussion risk receive a compensating wage premium in their next contract, controlling for productivity (Wins Above Average), experience, and contract year. Using 1,162 fully observed player contracts and 290 non-rookie contracts from the 2006–2017 NFL seasons, frequency-weighted fixed-effects linear regressions were specified. Results showed players are compensated for productivity and experience, but position groups with higher head impact or concussion risk receive a significant salary dispremium rather than a premium, consistent with an augmented compensating salary-differential theory in which both firm and employee share risk.
tldr: Contrary to standard labor economic theory predicting a wage premium for hazardous work, NFL players at higher-risk head-impact positions actually earn less on their next contracts. Teams appear to discount salaries to offset projected future healthcare liabilities and roster depth costs associated with high-concussion positions. Net effect is an estimated $2.54 million per-season dispremium for head impact risk exposure.
methods: Fixed-effects linear regression with frequency weighting by contract length. Dependent variable: next-contract average annual salary. Independent variables: player productivity (WAA from Pro Football Focus), years of NFL experience, experience squared, and three alternative measures of head impact risk (cumulative Head Impact Risk Factor, Concussions Per 100 Games Played, Concussions Per 1,000 Athletic Events) by position cluster. Twelve model specifications total, including six using lagged productivity for the non-rookie sub-sample (n=290). Data span 2006–2017 NFL seasons.
findings: Across all 12 model specifications, higher head impact risk and higher concussion rate were associated with significantly lower next-contract salaries (dispremium). At mean concussion rate and salary, the estimated salary decrease was approximately $5.25 million/season for concussion risk; the net overall head-impact dispremium was approximately $2.54 million/season. Players are compensated for sub-concussive head impacts (a premium), but penalized more strongly for concussive head impact risk, resulting in a net dispremium. Productivity (WAA) and experience are positively and significantly associated with salary.
limitations: Study limited to skill positions (QB, WR, RB/TE) where WAA data were available. Does not cover all NFL positions. Head impact risk measures are position-cluster estimates, not individual player measurements. Salary cap dynamics and collective bargaining effects may confound results. Does not account for positional depth chart differences or strategic roster construction beyond salary modeling.
practicalImplications: NFL teams appear to be financially responding to head impact risk by reducing salaries at high-risk positions rather than paying a risk premium. This has implications for player contract negotiations, union advocacy, and long-term healthcare liability planning. Organizations should consider the financial and ethical dimensions of how head injury risk is factored into compensation structures.
athleteDev: Findings highlight that positions with higher cumulative head impact exposure (e.g., running backs, wide receivers) face market-level salary penalties, which may influence athlete position decisions and career longevity considerations. Development staff should be aware that players at high-impact positions face compounded risks—both neurological and financial.
rtp: The study's evidence that concussive head impact risk reduces team demand for individual players has indirect RTP implications: teams may have financial incentives to limit playing time for high-concussion-rate positions, which could informally influence RTP timelines. Staff should advocate for RTP decisions based purely on clinical criteria rather than financial considerations.

citation: Boone, R. T., Zambrotta, N. S., Manocchio, A. M., & Bowman, J. K. (2025). Head in the game: The impact of cognitive abilities on performance of National Football League quarterbacks. Frontiers in Psychology, 16, 1540498. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2025.1540498
abstract: This study examined whether cognitive abilities measured by the Athletic Intelligence Quotient (AIQ)—Visual Spatial Processing, Reaction Time, Decision Making, and Learning Efficiency—predicted NFL quarterback performance beyond what is explained by draft pick. AIQ scores from 42 NFL quarterbacks collected at the Combine (2014–2020) were matched to performance metrics including Career Approximate Value (CAV), Quarterback Rating (QBR), passing yards, rushing yards, Big Time Throw rate, Turnover Worthy Plays, and Throwing Accuracy. Hierarchical regressions controlling for draft pick showed that Reaction Time predicted CAV, QBR, rushing yards, and games started; Visual Spatial Processing predicted passing yards; and an interaction between Visual Spatial Processing and Decision Making predicted Turnover Worthy Plays and Throwing Accuracy.
tldr: Cognitive performance measures—especially Reaction Time and Visual Spatial Processing—predict multiple NFL quarterback performance statistics above and beyond what draft pick alone predicts. Quarterbacks with faster reaction times have higher career value and passer ratings; better visual spatial processors gain more passing yards and throw more accurately. These findings support incorporating cognitive assessment in QB evaluation and development.
methods: Participants: 42 NFL quarterbacks who declared for the draft from 2014–2020 and played at least 3 games. Instrument: Athletic Intelligence Quotient (AIQ), administered at the NFL Combine via tablet software (AIM, LLC). Performance metrics sourced from Pro Football Reference (CAV, QBR, passing/rushing yards) and Pro Football Focus (Big Time Throw rate, Turnover Worthy Plays, Throwing Accuracy). Hierarchical multiple regression with draft pick entered at Step 1 and AIQ subscales entered at Step 2; trimmed models retained only significant AIQ predictors. Mixed model analysis used for PFF statistics (year nested within player).
findings: Reaction Time predicted games started per year (+6% variance beyond draft pick), CAV (+13%), QBR (+8%), and rushing yards per game (+9%). Visual Spatial Processing predicted passing yards per game (+7%). Interaction of Visual Spatial Processing × Decision Making significantly predicted Turnover Worthy Plays and Throwing Accuracy. Big Time Throw rate was not predicted by any AIQ factor. Learning Efficiency showed no significant relationships with any performance metric. Overall, AIQ cognitive factors added predictive power to 6 of 7 performance metrics examined.
limitations: Small sample size (n=42) limits statistical power and generalizability. Only quarterbacks who played at least 3 games were included, creating a survivor bias. Model excludes team-level factors such as offensive line quality and coaching scheme. Games started per year used as a proxy for ability may be confounded by organizational investment in high draft picks. Psychological facets such as arousal regulation and resilience are not captured by the AIQ.
practicalImplications: Cognitive assessment tools like the AIQ offer meaningful predictive value for QB performance and should be considered alongside physical metrics in scouting and player development. Reaction Time in particular is a broadly useful cognitive marker. Coaching staff might focus on drill designs that develop rapid perceptual decision-making and visual field scanning to improve QB performance.
athleteDev: For QB development programs, training environments that emphasize rapid decision-making under pressure and visual scanning of defensive formations could translate to improved on-field performance. The AIQ framework provides a structured way to identify cognitive development targets for young quarterbacks entering developmental pipelines.
rtp: Cognitive performance measures may have utility in RTP assessment for quarterbacks recovering from concussion or other neurological events. The AIQ subscales—especially Reaction Time and Visual Spatial Processing—could serve as sensitive markers of cognitive recovery, given their demonstrated relationships to on-field QB performance.

citation: Park, G., Gabler, L. F., Bailey, A. M., Dau, N. Z., Sherwood, C., Crandall, J. R., & Arbogast, K. B. (2021). Head shape analysis of National Football League players. Proceedings of the Institution of Mechanical Engineers, Part P: Journal of Sports Engineering and Technology. https://doi.org/10.1177/17543371211020614
abstract: Current football helmet fitting guidelines rely solely on head circumference, which may not optimize fit or protection for every player. This study used statistical shape analysis (SSA) and principal component analysis (PCA) on 3D light scans of 87 NFL players to identify key features of head shape variability. Two principal components explained 87% of variance: (1) head height and length in the sagittal plane and (2) prominent occiput with oval transverse cross-section versus prominent forehead with round cross-section. Four measurements—circumference, length, breadth, and height—were defined and quantified. NFL players had significantly larger circumference, length, and breadth than U.S. Army soldiers in the ANSUR II database.
tldr: NFL player heads are larger and more variable in shape than standard male populations, and current helmet fitting based solely on circumference misses important shape dimensions. Two principal components—head height/length and oval vs. round transverse profile—explain 87% of shape variance. Adding breadth and height measurements to fitting protocols could substantially improve helmet fit and safety for professional football players.
methods: 3D structured light scans (Artec Eva, 0.5 mm resolution) of 129 NFL players from three teams prior to the 2019–2020 season; 87 scans included after excluding those with hair obstructing scan quality. Five anatomical landmarks defined a head coordinate system; pseudo-landmarks (N=1,278) were mapped from each scalp in spherical coordinates. Correlation-based PCA performed in MATLAB on normalized pseudo-landmark data. Four head shape measurements (circumference, length, breadth, height) compared between NFL sample and ANSUR II database (n=4,082 male U.S. Army soldiers) using Student t-tests.
findings: First PC (74.8% of variance): tall vs. short head—primarily related to sagittal height and length. Second PC (12.3%): prominent occiput with oval transverse shape vs. prominent forehead with round shape. Together, two PCs explain 87% of head shape variability. NFL players had significantly larger circumference (592.3 vs. 574.4 mm), length (212.2 vs. 199.5 mm), and breadth (168.2 vs. 154.3 mm) than ANSUR II soldiers (all p<0.05). Height was also significantly larger (152.8 vs. 131.1 mm). Correlations among shape parameters were similar between groups.
limitations: Sample of approximately 5% of current NFL players limits generalizability. Insufficient data to analyze head shape differences by playing position. Players with certain hairstyles (dreadlocks, ponytails) were excluded, which may introduce sampling bias. Effect of hair on helmet fit was not addressed. Standard headforms used in laboratory helmet testing (NOCSAE, Hybrid III) do not represent NFL player head shapes.
practicalImplications: Helmet manufacturers should consider multi-dimensional head shape parameters beyond circumference when designing and sizing helmets for NFL players. Incorporating length, breadth, and height measurements into fitting protocols could improve protection. Current laboratory headforms underrepresent NFL player head size and shape, which may affect the validity of helmet performance testing for this population.
athleteDev: Proper helmet fit is fundamental to player safety. Equipment staff should ensure that helmet fitting accounts for head shape dimensions beyond circumference—particularly for players with oval vs. round head profiles—as poor fit may compromise both comfort and concussion mitigation. Position-specific helmet designs may eventually be warranted.
rtp: Helmet fit has been linked to concussion risk; helmets that fit poorly due to shape mismatch may increase impact transmission. Understanding an athlete's individual head shape could inform post-injury helmet fitting reviews as part of a comprehensive RTP protocol to reduce re-injury risk.

citation: Shanley, E., Thigpen, C., Kissenberth, M., Gilliland, R. G., Thorpe, J., Nance, D., Register-Mihalik, J. K., & Tokish, J. (2019). Heads Up Football training decreases concussion rates in high school football players. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine, 00, 1–7. https://doi.org/10.1097/JSM.0000000000000711
abstract: This prospective cohort study compared sport-related concussion (SRC) rates and recovery in 2,514 high school football players during the 2015–2016 season: 1,818 players from 14 schools receiving US Football Heads Up (HU) training versus 696 players from 10 control schools with no HU training (NHU). HU-trained coaches instructed proper blocking and tackling technique emphasizing heads-up contact. Results showed HU players had a significantly lower overall SRC rate (4.1 vs. 6.0 per 100 players; RR=1.5) and practice-specific SRC rate (RR=1.9). HU players also returned to full participation 27% faster (18.2 vs. 24.8 days).
tldr: The US Football Heads Up program reduced sport-related concussion rates by 33% and shortened recovery time by 27% in high school football players over one season. Practice-specific concussion rates were nearly twice as high in untrained players, suggesting technique training is especially valuable during practice. This is the first prospective efficacy study of the HU program on SRC incidence in scholastic football.
methods: Prospective cohort study. 14 Greenville County high schools (n=1,818 players) received US Football HU certification (player safety coach training) before the 2015–2016 season; 10 comparison schools (n=696 players) continued standard coaching. Random fidelity audits (minimum 3 per school) confirmed ≥80% compliance with HU technique instruction and performance. Athletic trainers and sports medicine physicians documented all SRCs prospectively. Cumulative incidence, incidence rates (per 100 players and per 100,000 athlete exposures for practices), and rate ratios with 95% CIs were calculated. Time loss compared by one-way ANOVA.
findings: 117 total SRCs observed. HU rate: 4.1 SRCs/100 players vs. NHU rate: 6.0 SRCs/100 players (RR=1.5; 95% CI 1.1–5.4). Practice SRC specific incidence rate: 2.6 vs. 4.9 per 100,000 athlete exposures (RR=1.9; 95% CI 1.1–3.2). Game SRC rate trended higher in NHU (RR=1.67; 95% CI 1.0–2.8). Time to full return: HU=18.2 days (95% CI 15.8–20.6) vs. NHU=24.8 days (95% CI 19.9–29.7), a 27% reduction. All HU schools maintained ≥80% fidelity throughout the season.
limitations: Assignment to training groups was not randomized; groups had unequal sample sizes. HU players were significantly heavier than NHU players. Game-level exposure by individual player was not available, limiting specific game incidence rate calculations. Position-specific data were not collected. Study conducted over a single season in one geographic region, limiting generalizability. Results require replication in larger, more controlled designs.
practicalImplications: Implementing the US Football Heads Up training program—particularly proper blocking and tackling technique instruction—can reduce concussion rates and recovery time in high school football. At least one coach should complete HU certification and serve as a player safety coach to ensure consistent technique instruction throughout all levels of practice. Athletic trainers and sports medicine physicians should support program fidelity monitoring.
athleteDev: Technique training focused on heads-up blocking and tackling—emphasizing breakdown position, controlled foot movement, heads-up hit position, and hip-drive shooting—reduces head contact frequency and severity in developing athletes. Integrating these principles from the earliest practice days through the full season is critical for long-term athlete safety and development.
rtp: HU-trained athletes returned to full participation approximately one week faster than untrained athletes following concussion, under the same standardized RTP protocol. This suggests that reduced concussion severity—potentially due to improved technique reducing impact magnitude—may facilitate faster symptom resolution and progression through return-to-play stages.

citation: Walton, S. R., Carneiro, K. A., Smith-Ryan, A. E., Stoner, L., Kerr, Z. Y., Brett, B. L., Chandran, A., DeFreese, J. D., Mannix, R., Lempke, L. B., Echemendia, R. J., McCrea, M. A., Guskiewicz, K. M., & Meehan, W. P. III. (2025). Health-promoting behaviors and their associations with factors related to well-being among former National Football League players: An NFL-LONG study. Journal of Athletic Training, 60(2), 185–197. https://doi.org/10.4085/1062-6050-0537.23
abstract: This cross-sectional study from the NFL-LONG cohort examined associations between health-promoting behaviors (exercise frequency, diet quality, sleep duration, sleep disturbance) and well-being outcomes (Meaning and Purpose, Self-Efficacy, Ability to Participate in Social Roles and Activities) in 1,784 former NFL players (mean age 52.3 years). Multivariable linear regression models were fit for the full sample and for six age subgroups. Less sleep disturbance was associated with better outcomes on all three well-being measures across nearly all age groups. More frequent moderate-to-vigorous exercise was associated with better Meaning and Purpose and Social Role Participation. Diet quality and resistance training were not broadly associated with well-being outcomes.
tldr: In 1,784 former NFL players, lower sleep disturbance and more frequent moderate-to-vigorous exercise were the most consistent predictors of better subjective well-being across the lifespan. These two modifiable behaviors represent key clinical targets for improving overall health and well-being in former professional football players as they age.
methods: Cross-sectional survey (online via Qualtrics or paper-based) from the NFL-LONG study, conducted January 2019–April 2020. Former NFL players (n=1,784; mean age 52.3±16.3 years) completed the survey. Health-promoting behaviors assessed: moderate-to-vigorous exercise frequency, resistance training frequency, other wellness activities, REAPS diet quality score, nightly sleep duration, and PROMIS-29 Sleep Disturbance T-score. Well-being outcomes: PROMIS Meaning and Purpose SF-4a, PROMIS Adult Self-Efficacy SF-4a, PROMIS Ability to Participate in Social Roles and Activities 4-item scale. Multivariable linear regression models adjusted for demographic, behavioral, and functional covariates; separate models fit for full sample and six age subgroups (<30, 30–39, 40–49, 50–59, 60–69, 70+ years). Multiple imputation used for missing data.
findings: Sleep disturbance was negatively associated with Meaning and Purpose (β=−0.196), Self-Efficacy (β=−0.185), and Social Role Participation (β=−0.137) in the full sample and across almost all age subgroups. More frequent moderate-to-vigorous exercise was associated with better Meaning and Purpose (β=0.068) and Social Role Participation (β=0.151). Resistance training, diet quality, sleep duration, and other wellness activities were not broadly significant in the full-group models. Age-group specific patterns emerged, with exercise associations particularly pronounced among players aged 40–69 years. Diet quality was associated with better Self-Efficacy only among players <30 and 30–39 years.
limitations: Cross-sectional design limits causal inference. Survey response frequency was relatively low (1,784/15,025 = ~11.9%), limiting generalizability. Self-reported behaviors may be under- or over-estimated. Exercise session duration was not captured, so meeting aerobic guidelines cannot be confirmed. Data collection overlapped with the onset of COVID-19 in spring 2020, which may have affected exercise and sleep behaviors. Mild cognitive impairment and dementia—more prevalent in this population—were not directly measured and could affect self-reported outcomes.
practicalImplications: Former NFL players represent a population at elevated risk for chronic health conditions and well-being decline as they age. Clinicians treating former players should prioritize sleep quality assessment and intervention alongside exercise prescription. Encouraging moderate-to-vigorous aerobic exercise—particularly in the 40–69 age range—appears to be an especially high-impact strategy for improving multiple dimensions of well-being.
athleteDev: For active players approaching the end of their careers, establishing sustainable exercise habits and healthy sleep routines during their playing years may support long-term well-being post-retirement. Organizations should integrate lifestyle education and behavior-change support into career transition programs.
rtp: Sleep disturbance is both a common post-concussion symptom and a primary predictor of poor well-being in former players. This study reinforces the importance of targeting sleep quality as part of comprehensive concussion management and long-term athlete health monitoring, particularly as players age and accumulate career exposure.

citation: Flatt AA, Allen J, Bragg A, Keith C, Earley R, Esco MR. Heart Rate Variability in College Football Players throughout Preseason Camp in the Heat. Int J Sports Med. 2020. DOI: 10.1055/a-1145-3754
abstract: This study characterized cardiac-autonomic responses to a 13-day preseason camp in the heat in 28 Division-I college football players (10 linemen, 18 non-linemen). Daily resting heart rate (RHR), LnRMSSD (HRV), and subjective wellbeing (LnWellness) were measured. Despite reductions in LnWellness across the camp, cardiac-autonomic parameters demonstrated responses consistent with heat acclimation. Non-linemen showed earlier and more frequent improvements in RHR and HRV than linemen. Both groups had their lowest RHR and highest LnRMSSD on day 12 following passive rest. Linemen experienced a moderate reduction in LnRMSSD after their first heat exposure (day 2) while non-linemen showed an unclear increase.
tldr: HRV and resting heart rate improved across preseason camp in the heat, consistent with heat acclimation, even though subjective wellbeing declined. Non-linemen adapted faster and more robustly than linemen. A day of passive rest (day 12) produced the most favorable autonomic readings for both groups. Practitioners should interpret HRV improvements during hot-weather training as cardiovascular adaptation rather than perceptual recovery, and should monitor linemen closely early in camp.
methods: Observational longitudinal study of 28 Division-I college football players (10 linemen, 18 non-linemen) during a 13-day preseason camp (15 sessions, avg heat index 38.1°C). Daily measures included: RHR and LnRMSSD via optical photoplethysmography finger sensor (HRV Fit), subjective wellbeing questionnaire (adapted McLean et al. 9-point scale), fasted body mass (Tanita scale), and accelerometer-derived PlayerLoad (Catapult 100 Hz). Linear mixed models with position as a fixed effect, day as a repeated measure, and athlete ID as a random effect. Hedges effect sizes ± 90% CI used to interpret standardized differences.
findings: Linemen showed a moderate ES reduction in LnRMSSD on day 2 and a moderate increase on day 12; non-linemen showed small-to-large LnRMSSD increases on days 3–5, 7, 12, and 13 relative to day 1. RHR decreased small-to-large across 9 days for non-linemen but only 3 days for linemen. LnWellness decreased moderately on days 4–10 and 13 for linemen and small-to-moderately on days 6–9, 12, and 13 for non-linemen. Both groups recorded their lowest RHR and highest LnRMSSD on day 12 following passive rest. Position × day interaction was significant for LnRMSSD (p=0.036). Body mass changes were unclear for both groups.
limitations: Plasma volume not directly measured; interpretation of autonomic changes relies on prior literature. No direct fitness or performance markers collected. RHR parameters were acquired ~2 hours earlier on days 6, 8, and 12 due to two-a-day scheduling. PlayerLoad data available for only 5 linemen due to preferential sensor allocation. Small sample size limits generalizability.
practicalImplications: Linemen should be closely monitored in the first few days of preseason heat camp for sustained HRV decrements. Improvements in HRV during heat training reflect cardiovascular adaptation, not perceptual recovery — practitioners should not use rising HRV to infer that players feel recovered. Subjective wellbeing measures may more sensitively detect fatigue and should be used alongside HRV. Passive rest days have a powerful restorative effect on autonomic function and are particularly beneficial for linemen. Both objective physiological and subjective psychological variables should be used together for athlete monitoring.
athleteDev: Provides a framework for daily HRV and wellbeing monitoring during American football preseason camp in the heat. Identifies that non-linemen adapt more favorably and earlier than linemen, suggesting position-specific monitoring thresholds and recovery protocols should be developed. Demonstrates the value of incorporating a passive rest day mid-camp to maximize physiological recovery.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play; however, the finding that HRV improvements reflect heat acclimation rather than overall recovery has implications for clearance decisions during injury rehabilitation conducted in hot environments, where HRV may not accurately reflect an athlete's readiness to resume full activity.

citation: Nguyen Q, Yurko R, Matthews GJ. Here Comes the STRAIN: Analyzing Defensive Pass Rush in American Football with Player Tracking Data. The American Statistician. 2023. DOI: 10.1080/00031305.2023.2242442
abstract: This paper introduces STRAIN, a novel continuous-time metric for evaluating NFL defensive pass rushers using Next Gen Stats player tracking data. Inspired by the concept of strain rate in materials science, STRAIN is computed as the negative velocity of a pass rusher toward the quarterback divided by their current distance from the quarterback. Applied to the first eight weeks of the 2021 NFL regular season (8,557 passing plays, 122 games), the metric provides a continuous measure of defensive pressure on every frame of every play. A multilevel model controls for pass rusher position, blocking matchup, defensive team, offensive team, and play context to estimate each pass rusher's individual contribution.
tldr: STRAIN is a tracking-data metric that quantifies how much pressure a defensive pass rusher is exerting at every moment of a play, based on how fast they are closing in on the quarterback relative to their current distance. It is more stable and more predictive of future pressure events than traditional box-score statistics (sacks, hits, hurries). Edge rushers generate more STRAIN than interior linemen. Top-ranked players by STRAIN (e.g., Myles Garrett, T.J. Watt, Aaron Donald) align closely with expert rankings, validating the metric.
methods: Player tracking data from the NFL Big Data Bowl 2023 (Next Gen Stats, 10 Hz RFID): 8,557 passing plays across the first 8 weeks of the 2021 NFL regular season. STRAIN defined as −v(t)/s(t), where s(t) = distance between pass rusher and QB at frame t, and v(t) = rate of change of that distance. Average STRAIN computed across all frames per play and per player. A multilevel model (lme4, R) included random intercepts for individual pass rushers, pass blockers, defensive teams, and offensive teams, plus fixed effects for number of blockers, yards to go, current down, current yardline, pass rusher position, and blocker position. Bootstrapping (resampling drives) provided uncertainty estimates for random effects. Data from Pro Football Focus (PFF) used to identify pass rush roles and blocking matchups.
findings: STRAIN correlates moderately with pressure rate (r = 0.6255) over the 8-game sample. STRAIN from the first 4 weeks predicts pressure rate in the last 4 weeks more strongly (r = 0.3217) than prior pressure rate itself (r = 0.0965). Week-to-week stability is high (r = 0.8545 across the 8-week window). Edge rushers (OLBs and DEs) generate substantially more STRAIN than interior rushers (DTs and NTs). Players tend to generate higher STRAIN on plays ending in sacks > hits > hurries > no outcome. The multilevel model shows STRAIN decreases by 0.7366 per additional blocker. Pass rusher explains the most variance of any grouping factor (ICC = 0.0365), followed by offensive team (0.0143), defensive team (0.0098), and blocker (0.0134). Top-ranked pass rushers: DE — Myles Garrett; OLB — T.J. Watt; DT — Aaron Donald; NT — Vita Vea.
limitations: Model accounts only for the nearest blocker, not all blockers simultaneously. Nested structure enforces positive dependence between pass rushers on the same team, which may inflate some rankings (e.g., Takkarist McKinley benefiting from playing alongside Myles Garrett). Study period limited to 8 weeks of one NFL season. Temporal modeling of full STRAIN trajectories not yet implemented. Offensive pass blockers not individually evaluated.
practicalImplications: STRAIN provides a richer, continuous evaluation of pass rusher effectiveness than sacks or pressures alone, enabling coaching staffs to identify high-impact players even on plays that don't end in recorded outcomes. The metric can be visualized in real time for in-game adjustments and is a more reliable predictor of future performance than traditional statistics. Defensive coordinators can use STRAIN to optimize pass rush rotations and identify which matchups generate the most pressure.
athleteDev: Provides a quantitative framework for evaluating defensive linemen and linebackers on passing plays beyond traditional box-score outcomes. Edge rushers consistently generate more STRAIN than interior players, which has implications for position-specific training priorities. STRAIN-based player development can target closing speed and pursuit angles to the quarterback as key physical performance qualities for pass rushers.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play contexts. However, STRAIN could theoretically be used post-injury to assess whether a returning pass rusher is generating pressure at pre-injury levels, serving as a performance-based functional benchmark before full competitive clearance.

citation: Malone S, Roe M, Doran DA, Gabbett TJ, Collins K. High chronic training loads and exposure to bouts of maximal velocity running reduce injury risk in elite Gaelic football. J Sci Med Sport. 2017;20(3):250–254. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsams.2016.08.005
abstract: This prospective cohort study examined the relationship between chronic training loads, exposures to maximal velocity running, and injury risk in 37 elite Gaelic football players over one full season. Session-RPE training load, GPS-derived maximal velocity distance and exposure counts, and lower limb injury data were collected across 95 pitch-based training sessions. Logistic regression with odds ratios (OR) was used. Players producing ≥95% of their maximal velocity had significantly lower injury risk (OR: 0.12, p=0.001). Players with high chronic training loads (≥4750AU) could tolerate 10–15 maximal velocity exposures per week at reduced injury risk (OR: 0.22, p=0.026), while those with low chronic loads were at increased risk at the same exposures. A U-shaped curve was observed when maximal velocity exposures were considered independently of training load history.
tldr: Elite Gaelic football players who regularly achieved high percentages of their maximal velocity during training had substantially lower injury risk than those who did not. The key moderating variable is chronic training load: players who had built up high chronic loads (≥4750 AU) could safely tolerate more maximal velocity exposures and distances, while underprepared players were injured at the same exposure levels. This supports the 'vaccine' concept — controlled, progressive exposure to maximal velocity running protects against soft-tissue injury — but only in athletes who have the chronic fitness base to absorb it.
methods: Prospective cohort study of 37 elite Gaelic football players (age 24±3 yrs) over one season. Internal load: session-RPE (modified Borg CR-10 × duration in minutes), aggregated into acute (1-week) and chronic (4-week rolling average) workloads. External load: 4-Hz GPS (VXSport) tracking of maximal velocity distance (m), relative maximal velocity (% of individual max), and number of maximal velocity exposures per week. Individual maximal velocity established via 40-m sprint test with dual-beam timing gates. Injuries: all time-loss lower limb soft tissue injuries. Data analyzed using logistic regression with intra-player cluster adjustment; OR calculated by quartile split for relative velocity, velocity distance, and exposure counts, and by dichotomous median split for chronic load (≤4750 vs ≥4750 AU). Statistical power: 85% based on 91 injuries from 3,515 player-sessions.
findings: 91 time-loss injuries across the season (36 training, 55 match); injury rate 45.3/1000 h match, 6.9/1000 h training. Players achieving ≥95% maximal velocity: OR 0.12 (95% CI 0.01–0.92, p=0.001) vs ≤85% reference. When chronic load considered — high load (≥4750 AU) players with 10–15 maximal velocity exposures/week: OR 0.22 (p=0.026, protective); low load (≤4750 AU) players with same exposures: OR 1.44 (not significant). High chronic load players covering 90–120 m/week at maximal velocity: OR 0.23 (p=0.055, protective); low chronic load players at same distance: OR 1.72 (p=0.016, increased risk). Low chronic load players with ≥15 exposures: OR 3.38 (p=0.001, greatly increased risk). U-shaped injury-exposure curve observed independently of chronic load.
limitations: Conditioning and strength training workloads cannot be quantified via GPS. Injury history of players not accounted for. GPS accuracy at high-speed movements has been questioned in prior research. Single squad from one season limits generalizability. No measures of perceived muscle soreness, fatigue, mood, or sleep quality included.
practicalImplications: Athletes need a sufficient chronic training base before being exposed to high volumes and intensities of maximal velocity running. Simply restricting workloads may increase injury risk by leaving athletes underprepared. Coaches should design drills that allow players to reach maximal velocity in linear sprints. Monitoring weekly maximal velocity exposures and distances in the context of each player's chronic load history provides a more nuanced injury risk assessment than load alone. Both under- and over-exposure to maximal velocity running increases injury risk.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football speed and conditioning periodization. Building a high chronic training load base before intensifying sprint and maximal velocity work is essential for injury protection. Weekly monitoring of maximal sprint exposures per player, benchmarked against their individual chronic fitness level, should be integrated into GPS-based load management systems. Players should be regularly exposed to sprints that allow them to reach their individual top speed in training.
rtp: Critical for return-to-play from lower limb soft-tissue injuries. Athletes returning from injury will have reduced chronic load and therefore cannot safely tolerate the same maximal velocity exposure volumes as healthy teammates. A graduated re-exposure protocol — rebuilding chronic load before reintroducing high-intensity sprint work — is supported by this data. Clearance to full sprint training should be conditioned on achieving a sufficient chronic load base, not just on symptomatic recovery.

citation: DeLang MD, Ishøi L, Thornton K, Thorborg K. High previous-week training load amplifies hip adduction squeeze strength reductions in male academy football players with performance-limiting groin pain. Phys Ther Sport. 2026;78:101902. DOI: 10.1016/j.ptsp.2026.101902
abstract: This longitudinal observational study examined whether preceding seven-day football training load and previous seven-day groin pain influence hip adduction squeeze strength in 19 male academy football players (age 16.5±0.9 years) from a West African under-18 academy. Players were monitored twice weekly over seven weeks with serial hip adduction squeeze strength testing (GroinMate device), subjective groin pain reporting, and GPS-derived training load.
