Linear mixed-effects models stratified by groin pain presence showed that training load significantly influenced squeeze strength only when groin pain was present (estimate −0.13 N/kg, 95% CI [−0.20, −0.07], p<0.001), while pain-free players showed no training load effect (estimate −0.01, p=0.634).
tldr: When a football player has groin pain, higher training loads make their hip adduction squeeze strength measurably worse. Pain-free players, by contrast, tolerate high training loads with no meaningful change in adduction strength. This finding means that monitoring training load in the context of serial groin strength screening provides additive information — a player showing strength deficits who also had a high-load week is at compounded risk and may need load modification as part of their management.
methods: Longitudinal observational study with twice-weekly repeated measures over 7 weeks in-season (summer 2023). 19 male academy players (U-18, West Africa). Monitoring protocol (Monday and Thursday, prior to training): 1) Verbal subjective groin pain NRS (0–10) for the preceding 7 days, operationalized as 'performance-limiting'; 2) Hip adduction squeeze strength via GroinMate (CCAthletics, Denmark) in long-lever Copenhagen 5-second squeeze test position, one maximal isometric trial, normalized to body mass (N/kg); 3) GPS training load (Catapult) compiled as 7-day cumulative summaries — variables: total distance, high-speed running distance (>21 km/h), sprint distance (>25 km/h), accelerations + decelerations (>2.5 m/s²), duration. PCA reduced multicollinear GPS variables into a single composite load score (PC1: 81% variance, eigenvalue 4.06). Linear mixed-effects models (lme4, R 4.2.3) with training load and random player intercepts, stratified by pain presence.
findings: 17 of 19 players reported groin pain during the study; 119 of 266 observations (44.7%) were pain-associated. No time-loss groin injuries occurred. In the pain group: training load estimate = −0.13 N/kg (95% CI [−0.20, −0.07], p<0.001) per 1 SD increase in training load. In the no-pain group: estimate = −0.01 N/kg (95% CI [−0.06, 0.03], p=0.634). Pain-free players maintained adduction strength regardless of training load. The interaction between pain and training load was positive, supporting model stratification.
limitations: Small sample (n=19) from a single elite West African academy limits generalizability. Detection bias possible due to structured monitoring capturing subthreshold pain. No time-loss injuries captured during the study period. Training load not stratified by playing position. Individual cut-off points or predictive algorithms for at-risk players not yet feasible. Age range of 15–18 introduces maturational variability not accounted for in the analysis. No consideration of preceding 24-h fatigue on monitoring days.
practicalImplications: Serial monitoring of hip adduction squeeze strength combined with training load data provides richer early-detection information than either measure alone. When a player reports groin pain, practitioners should consider modifying training load as a primary intervention — not just exercise prescription — to break the cycle of strength degradation. A combined pain-load flag in a monitoring system could prioritize early intervention. Reducing football exposure is a legitimate and evidence-based pain modulation strategy.
athleteDev: Supports integrating GPS-based load monitoring with serial groin strength screening in academy-level football programs. Players with groin pain experiencing high training loads are at compounded risk of further strength deficits and eventual time-loss injury. Academy programs should build load modification protocols specifically for non-time-loss groin pain cases to prevent escalation.
rtp: Directly informs the management of players returning from or managing groin pain in active training environments. Elevated training load amplifies strength deficits in symptomatic players, suggesting that load reduction — not just rehabilitation exercise — should be a component of RTP protocols for groin conditions. A player's training load history in the week preceding an RTP test should be considered when interpreting adduction strength scores.

citation: Kang L, Okolo M, Cleary EJ, Lu Y, Iyer S, Levy BA, Hevesi M, Okoroha KR, Krych AJ. High Rates of Return to American Football After Primary Hip Arthroscopy for Femoroacetabular Impingement: A 2-Year Minimum Follow-up. Orthop J Sports Med. 2026;14(1):23259671251397513. DOI: 10.1177/23259671251397513
abstract: This retrospective case series from Mayo Clinic examined return-to-sport (RTS) rates and patient-reported outcomes in 38 active American football players (49 hips) who underwent primary hip arthroscopy with labral repair for femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) between 2010 and 2023. The cohort was predominantly high school (74%) and college (21%) athletes with a mean age of 18.1 years; 51% were linemen. At a mean 7.6-year follow-up, 88% of players achieved RTS (excluding those who did not return for nonmedical reasons). Significant improvements were observed in all patient-reported outcome measures (mHHS, HOS-ADL, HOS-Sport), all exceeding the minimal clinically important difference. The reoperation rate was 8%.
tldr: Amateur American football players — most of them high school or college athletes — achieve an 88% return-to-sport rate after hip arthroscopy for FAI when nonmedical reasons for not returning (graduation, changing priorities) are excluded. The overall return rate including nonmedical factors was only 61%, highlighting that life circumstances significantly affect football participation at these levels. Outcomes are excellent and durable at 7+ years. Linemen represent over half the surgical population, likely due to the extreme hip loading demands of their position. No factors — including BMI, age, position, or alpha angle — significantly predicted RTS.
methods: Retrospective analysis of a prospectively maintained institutional database (Mayo Clinic) from 2010–2023. Inclusion: active football players with primary hip arthroscopy for FAI with labral repair and intent to return to sport. Exclusion: follow-up <2 years, concomitant PAO, insufficient documentation, or opted out. 49 hips in 38 male players included. Data collected: demographics, radiographic parameters (lateral center-edge angle, Tonnis angle, alpha angle), surgical details, complications, reoperations. Minimum 2-year follow-up via REDCap and telephone interview for updated PROMs (mHHS, HOS-ADL, HOS-Sport, Tegner, VAS pain, satisfaction) and RTS status. RTS defined as participation in ≥1 competitive game at the same or lower level. MCID thresholds applied (mHHS 9.2, HOS-ADL 9.7, HOS-Sport 14.3). Logistic regression for RTS predictors. Statistical analysis in R.
findings: Mean age 18.1±3.0 yrs; 51% linemen; 74% high school, 21% college. Bilateral involvement in 29%. Mean alpha angle 59.8±12.1°. Labral repair in 100% of cases; femoroplasty in 100%; acetabuloplasty in 86%; capsular repair in 98%. RTS (excluding nonmedical): 23/26 (88%); all returned at same or higher level. Overall RTS including nonmedical non-returners: 61%. Mean follow-up 7.6±3.9 yrs: mHHS 90.2±12.6, HOS-ADL 94.5±6.8, HOS-Sport 88.7±17.0; satisfaction 8.7±1.7/10. Significant pre-to-post improvements in all PROMs (p≤.002), all exceeding MCID. 4 hips (8%) reoperated at mean 4.9 yrs. No perioperative complications. No statistically significant differences in outcomes between those who did and did not return. Logistic regression: no significant predictors of RTS (BMI, age, level of play, alpha angle, position).
limitations: PROMs collected from only 27/38 athletes (71%), introducing potential response bias. 12 athletes who did not return for nonmedical reasons may bias results. Small sample limits regression model interpretation. Procedures performed by multiple surgeons over 13 years with evolving techniques. Case series design; Level 4 evidence. Study population predominantly high school and college athletes, limiting direct applicability to professional American football.
practicalImplications: Football players undergoing hip arthroscopy for FAI can be counseled that they have an 88% chance of returning to play when medical factors are the primary consideration. Nonmedical factors (graduation, changing priorities) are important to address in shared decision-making, particularly for high school and college athletes. Outcomes are durable at 7+ years with high satisfaction. Rehabilitation protocols with clear milestones (jogging/plyometrics by 12 weeks, 75% LSI at 12 weeks, 90% LSI at 16 weeks) supported full return.
athleteDev: Linemen are disproportionately represented in football FAI surgical populations (51%) due to their crouched stance requiring maximal hip flexion and asymmetric loading. Higher BMI in linemen may increase compressive and shear forces on the hip. Screening programs for FAI symptoms should target linemen specifically. Early identification and management may prevent symptom progression and surgical necessity.
rtp: 88% RTS rate in amateur American football players after primary hip arthroscopy for FAI is comparable to NFL studies (84–93%). Standard rehabilitation protocol: partial weight-bearing and passive ROM for first 2 weeks, progressive weight-bearing and functional exercises thereafter, jogging and plyometrics by 12 weeks, with LSI targets of 75% at 12 weeks and 90% at 16 weeks before full RTS. No significant predictors of failure to return were identified, meaning individualized counseling regarding nonmedical factors is important alongside medical management.

citation: Marín, K., & Castellano, J. (2023). High-speed running distance and frequency in football training: When and how are they stimulated in a microcycle? International Journal of Sports Science & Coaching, 18(4), 1132–1141.
abstract: This study described the distribution of distance and frequency of high-speed running (HSR >21 km/h), very high-speed running (VHSR >24 km/h), and sprint (SPR >27 km/h) in professional football training across microcycles (Mi) of 4, 5, 6, and 7 days between matches. GPS data were collected from 23 La Liga players across 28 microcycles during the 2018–19 season. Central training days (MD-3, MD-4) accumulated the greatest high-speed locomotive demands regardless of microcycle duration, while MD-2 consistently showed the least stimulation. Longer microcycles (Mi7) had significantly higher HSR values on MD-4 and MD-5 compared to shorter microcycles.
tldr: Professional football teams follow an inverted-U weekly load pattern: high-speed running is concentrated in the mid-week acquisition sessions (MD-3 and MD-4), tapers before match day (with MD-2 as the lowest stimulus day), and is kept low immediately post-match. Total accumulated HSR volume does not increase proportionally when more days are available between matches, but longer microcycles push high-speed work further from the upcoming match (e.g., MD-5 in 7-day weeks). Staff should program sprint and high-speed running deliberately in mid-week to optimize adaptation while preserving freshness for competition.
methods: Descriptive, observational design. Twenty-three male professional players from one La Liga club were monitored via 10 Hz Catapult GPS units across 28 competitive microcycles (2018–19 season). Microcycles were classified by days between matches (Mi4, Mi5, Mi6, Mi7). HSR (>21 km/h), VHSR (>24 km/h), and SPR (>27 km/h) were quantified by total distance (m) and frequency (occurrences) per session. One-way ANOVA with Bonferroni/Dunnett's T3 post hoc tests; effect sizes reported as Cohen's d. Mixed linear models used for MD-day comparisons.
findings: No significant differences in total accumulated HSR, VHSR, or SPR volume across microcycle durations (Mi4–Mi7). Regardless of microcycle length, central days (MD-4, MD-3) received significantly greater locomotive demands than post-match (MD-7, MD-6) and pre-match (MD-2, MD-1) sessions, with MD-2 requiring the least stimulation (p<0.05). In 7-day microcycles, MD-4 and MD-5 showed significantly higher HSR/VHSR/SPR than shorter microcycles. MD-3 was consistently the day with the greatest stimulation across all microcycle lengths. Substantial intra-session sprint variability was noted, attributable to position, fitness status, and competition participation.
limitations: Single team and single coaching philosophy limits generalizability. No internal load metrics (e.g., heart rate, RPE) were included to contextualize physiological response. Testing/readiness and match performance data were not integrated. Large SD in sprint outputs reflects high inter-individual variability.
practicalImplications: High-speed running (HSR, VHSR, SPR) should be programmed primarily on mid-week days (MD-3 and MD-4), distanced from both the preceding and upcoming match to allow recovery and avoid compromising match-day readiness. When more days are available between matches, push high-speed work earlier (MD-5) rather than simply adding more total volume. MD-2 should consistently be the lowest-stimulus session. Coaches should adjust the timing of sprint stimuli based on microcycle length, not just overall volume.
athleteDev: For developing football athletes, this study supports the principle of horizontal alternation within the weekly microcycle: recovery sessions post-match, acquisition sessions mid-week (with sprint and high-speed running emphasis), and tapering pre-match. Building tolerance to high-speed running across the week at appropriate distances and frequencies may serve a protective function against injury and support long-term speed development.
rtp: The study underscores that MD-2 is the most protected session in professional programming. For athletes returning from injury, this session structure provides a logical integration framework: return players to mid-week sessions with graduated HSR exposure (beginning at MD-3 or MD-4 level intensities), progressing as they demonstrate tolerance, before full participation in match-day activities.

citation: Lacki, J. F. (2021). High training/competition ratio, less incidence of injury? Professional football calendar exploration. Apunts Sports Medicine, 56(209), 100338.
abstract: This 12-season prospective analysis of an Argentine professional football club (Defensa y Justicia, 2003–2014) investigated whether a high training-to-competition ratio is associated with lower injury incidence. The team competed in a single tournament (38 rounds/season) at approximately 3.85 matches/month, yielding a training/competition ratio of 8.4 training hours per match hour. Over 12 seasons, 797 injuries were recorded across 4,710 training hours and 715 competition hours. Overall injury incidence was 4.2 per 1,000 hours, compared to 30.5 (competition) and 5.8 (training) per 1,000 hours in UEFA Champions League data. Sixty-five percent of injuries resolved within 7 days.
tldr: Football teams competing in fewer matches per season with longer recovery-training cycles between games (5–8 days) show substantially lower injury incidence rates than European clubs playing in multiple concurrent competitions. This South American club's injury rate (4.2/1,000 h total; 2.3/1,000 h training; 20.5/1,000 h competition) was well below UEFA benchmarks (5.8 and 30.5/1,000 h respectively), and 65% of injuries resolved within one week—compared to only 27% in UEFA data. More training relative to competition may improve fitness, build structural resilience, and reduce severe injury burden.
methods: Prospective longitudinal injury surveillance over 12 seasons (2003–2014) for one Argentine First Division club. Daily medical records documented all injuries (defined per Dvorak & Junge 2000 as missed training/match time with anatomical diagnosis). Injury severity classified using the Paus modification (Grade I: 1–7 days; II: 1–3 weeks; III: 3–8 weeks; IV: >8 weeks). Incidence calculated as injuries per 1,000 participation hours for training and competition separately. Data compared to UEFA Champions League and English FA Championship injury reports.
findings: Injury incidence rate: 4.2/1,000 h (combined), 2.3/1,000 h training, 20.5/1,000 h competition. Training/competition ratio: 8.4:1 (vs. 6.3:1 in UEFA 2016/17). Average absence per injury: 21.4 h. Injury burden: 90 days/1,000 h (vs. 130 days/1,000 h in UEFA Champions League). Grade I injuries (1–7 days): 65% of all injuries. Muscle injuries most common (44.5%), followed by knee (17.3%) and ankle (16%). Hamstrings comprised 53.5% of muscle injuries. Non-contact injuries: 57.1%. Surgical procedures required in only 1.24% of cases.
limitations: Single club and coaching staff across 12 seasons limits generalizability. Training load recorded by time only (no GPS), which reduces precision. Illness-related absences were not recorded as injuries, which may explain some of the lower incidence compared to European studies. The Argentine league structure (single tournament, two annual cycles) differs substantially from European multi-competition formats.
practicalImplications: Sports calendars allowing 5–8 days between competitions and a training/competition ratio of approximately 8:1 may function as a protective factor for professional footballers. Administrators and governing bodies planning fixture schedules should consider injury burden data when designing competition formats. Clubs competing in congested schedules should proactively plan recovery, manage training load, and monitor injury surveillance metrics. Muscle injuries—especially hamstrings—remain the primary target for prevention regardless of schedule.
athleteDev: Athletes in development programs who train more than they compete may benefit from greater structural adaptation and lower injury risk. The data support the idea that higher chronic training loads relative to competition can prepare athletes to tolerate the demands of matches. Periodization models that prioritize training volume and progressive loading during lower-competition periods may produce more robust and injury-resilient athletes.
rtp: The finding that 65% of injuries resolved within 7 days—compared to 27% in UEFA populations—suggests that lower accumulated fatigue from congested schedules may facilitate faster recovery and return to play. Adequate recovery-training cycles between competitions (5–8 days) may support more complete physiological restoration, reducing re-injury risk. Return-to-play protocols should account for the team's competition frequency as a contextual factor in managing return timelines.

citation: Iverson, G. L., Merz, Z. C., & Terry, D. P. (2022). High-school football and midlife brain health problems. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine, 32(2), 86–94.
abstract: This cross-sectional online survey study examined whether middle-aged men (ages 35–55) who played high-school football experience worse brain health outcomes compared to men who played other contact sports, noncontact sports, or no sports. A total of 407 men were stratified by high-school sport history (football n=123, contact sports n=69, noncontact sports n=97, no sports n=118). Validated measures included the PHQ-8 for depression symptoms and BC-PSI for post-concussion-like symptoms. No significant group differences were found in lifetime mental health treatment, rates of depression, anxiety, concentration or memory problems, or post-concussion symptom totals. Football players reported significantly more lifetime concussions, higher rates of chronic pain and headache medication prescriptions, and more sleep problems in the past year.
tldr: Men who played high-school football (American) do not report meaningfully worse mental health or cognitive functioning in midlife compared to men who played other sports or no sports. While former football players had more lifetime concussions and higher rates of chronic pain and headache medication use, they did not differ from comparison groups on depression, anxiety, memory, or post-concussion symptoms. Sleep difficulties were more prevalent in the football group. These findings are consistent with six prior studies and do not support the idea that high-school football participation necessarily leads to poor brain health in middle age.
methods: Cross-sectional cohort study using an online survey via Amazon Mechanical Turk (mTurk). Participants: 407 US men aged 35–55. Groups: football (n=123), other contact sports (n=69), noncontact sports (n=97), no sports (n=118). Measures: PHQ-8 (depression), BC-PSI (post-concussion symptoms), and a custom survey on health history, current symptoms, concussion history, and medication use. Statistical analyses: one-way ANOVA, Kruskal-Wallis H tests, chi-square tests, Spearman/point-biserial correlations, and logistic regression. Validity items embedded to filter inattentive respondents.
findings: No significant group differences in: lifetime history of depression or anxiety medication, mental health treatment, rates of depression/anxiety/anger/concentration/memory/headache/chronic pain over the past year, PHQ-8 depression scores, or BC-PSI post-concussion symptom totals. Football players reported more lifetime concussions (mean 2.28 vs. 0.68–1.67 in other groups), higher rates of chronic pain medication prescription (66.7%), more headache medication prescriptions (26%), and greater sleep difficulties in the past year (39.0% vs. 20.6–30.5%). Predictors of memory problems: sleep difficulties (OR=3.19), anxiety (OR=2.90), concussion history (OR=2.33), and depression (OR=2.26); high-school football was not a significant predictor.
limitations: Self-reported data subject to recall and response bias. Consent language mentioning CTE may have introduced selection bias. Concussion history could not be independently verified. Multiple comparisons were not corrected for (though null findings dominate). The sample had a relatively high rate of depressive symptoms compared to the general population. Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. All participants were current US residents which may limit demographic generalizability.
practicalImplications: High-school football participation alone does not appear to predict worse brain health in midlife. Clinicians working with former football players should be aware that sleep difficulties, chronic pain, and headaches may be more prevalent in this population and should be assessed and treated. Depression, anxiety, sleep problems, and concussion history are the primary predictors of memory complaints—not sport participation per se. Evidence-based treatments for these comorbid conditions can substantially improve function and quality of life.
athleteDev: These data provide reassurance that high-school-level football participation is not inherently linked to midlife cognitive or mental health deficits. For athlete development programs, this supports continued participation at the high-school level with appropriate concussion management protocols in place. The elevated concussion history in the football group underscores the ongoing importance of concussion education, recognition, and safe return-to-play protocols at the youth and high-school levels.
rtp: The study indirectly supports the importance of thorough concussion management: former football players had significantly more lifetime concussions, which was a significant predictor of memory problems in midlife. Proper identification and management of concussions during an athlete's playing career may help mitigate long-term cognitive risk. Current sleep difficulties and anxiety were stronger predictors of memory problems than sport history, suggesting that RTP protocols should include mental health and sleep screening, particularly following concussive events.

citation: Mack, C. D., Hershman, E. B., Anderson, R. B., Coughlin, M. J., McNitt, A. S., Sendor, R. R., & Kent, R. W. (2019). Higher rates of lower extremity injury on synthetic turf compared with natural turf among National Football League athletes: Epidemiologic confirmation of a biomechanical hypothesis. The American Journal of Sports Medicine, 47(1), 189–196.
abstract: This cohort study examined whether lower extremity (LE) injury rates in NFL regular season games (2012–2016) were higher on synthetic turf than natural turf, epidemiologically testing a prior biomechanical hypothesis that synthetic surfaces generate greater shear force on the foot due to poor cleat release. All 32 NFL teams contributed injury data under mandated consistent reporting protocols. Poisson models estimated adjusted incidence rate ratios (IRRs). Synthetic turf was associated with a 16% increase in LE injuries per play (IRR 1.16; 95% CI 1.10–1.23). This effect was more pronounced for noncontact/surface contact injuries (IRR up to 2.03 for ankle/foot with ≥8 days missed), and increased as injury locations were more distal (closer to the playing surface).
tldr: NFL players are 16% more likely to sustain a lower extremity injury per play on synthetic turf than on natural grass, with the risk rising to over 100% greater for noncontact ankle and foot injuries requiring 8+ days off. The pattern of findings—stronger associations for distal injuries and noncontact mechanisms—is consistent with the biomechanical explanation that synthetic turf fails to release cleats at injurious torque levels. Natural turf's ability to divot under load serves as a force-limiting mechanism absent in synthetic surfaces. This has direct implications for surface selection, footwear choice, and field maintenance standards.
methods: Retrospective cohort study using NFL injury surveillance data from 2012–2016 regular season (1,280 games; 213,935 plays). All 32 teams reported injuries through mandated electronic health records. LE injuries causing any missed participation or ≥8 days missed were included; subcategories of knee and ankle/foot examined. Poisson regression models estimated crude and adjusted IRRs, controlling for surface hardness (Gmax) and field moisture. Secondary analysis restricted to noncontact/surface contact injuries. Sensitivity analyses excluded potentially heterogeneous synthetic fields (Seattle, New England, Dallas).
findings: 4,801 LE injuries occurred across 1,280 games. Synthetic turf: 16% more LE injuries per play vs. natural turf (IRR 1.16; 95% CI 1.10–1.23). Effect persisted for injuries with ≥8 days missed. Noncontact/surface contact injury IRRs: overall LE 1.27 (1.15–1.41), knee 1.46 (1.20–1.77), ankle/foot 1.68 (1.36–2.08). For ≥8 days missed ankle/foot noncontact injuries: IRR 2.03 (1.42–2.89). Contact injuries attributed to player-to-player contact showed no surface difference (IRR 1.06; 0.97–1.14). Adjustment for hardness and moisture did not meaningfully alter results. If natural turf rates applied to synthetic turf games in the study, approximately 319 fewer LE injuries would have been expected.
limitations: Findings are specific to NFL conditions (elite athletes, professional maintenance, certified surfaces) and may not generalize to other levels of play or sports. Injury mechanism classification is subjective and prone to sideline reporting error. Return-to-play data may be incomplete for players who leave the league. Variability in synthetic turf design, age, and maintenance across stadiums was not fully captured. The study cannot fully account for systematic behavioral differences in play style by surface type.
practicalImplications: NFL teams and athletic staff should prioritize footwear selection optimized for synthetic surfaces, as cleat pattern significantly affects torque and shear loading. Players on synthetic turf may benefit from additional lower limb protection. Stadium operators and synthetic turf manufacturers should pursue designs that allow cleat release at injurious load thresholds, similar to natural turf divoting. Teams playing significant portions of their schedule on synthetic turf should factor surface type into injury prevention programming, including extra emphasis on ankle and foot stability training.
athleteDev: The biomechanical mechanism identified—inadequate cleat release on synthetic turf generating excess shear force—is relevant at all levels of football. Youth and collegiate programs using synthetic fields should invest in surface-appropriate footwear education and should consider this injury risk when programming high-intensity change-of-direction and sprint work. Developing players who compete on synthetic surfaces may benefit from targeted ankle/foot strength and proprioception training to compensate for the elevated mechanical demands of synthetic fields.
rtp: The elevated ankle and foot injury rates on synthetic turf—particularly for noncontact mechanisms—are directly relevant to RTP decisions. Athletes returning from lower extremity injuries (especially ankle sprains, foot injuries, or knee ligament injuries) should be cautiously managed when returning to play on synthetic surfaces. Clinicians should consider surface type as part of RTP criteria, potentially requiring higher functional thresholds before clearing athletes to compete on synthetic turf. Footwear reassessment and surface-specific functional testing should be incorporated into RTP protocols.

citation: Lonie, T. A., Brade, C. J., Finucane, M. E., Jacques, A., & Grisbrook, T. L. (2019). Hip adduction and abduction strength and adduction-to-abduction ratio changes across an Australian Football League season. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport, 23(2), 130–135.
abstract: This cross-sectional repeated-measures study examined changes in isometric hip adduction strength, abduction strength, and adduction-to-abduction ratio across an Australian Football (AF) League season in 38 uninjured elite players from one AFL club. Maximal isometric hip strength was measured at four time points using a hand-held dynamometer with external belt fixation: start of pre-season (T1), end of pre-season (T2), mid-season (T3), and post-season (T4). Hip adduction strength and the adduction-to-abduction ratio were significantly greater at mid-season (T3) compared to pre-season (T1), while both adduction and abduction strength were significantly weaker at post-season (T4) versus T1. No differences were found between preferred and non-preferred kicking legs in adduction or abduction, though the adduction-to-abduction ratio of the preferred leg was significantly lower than the non-preferred leg at T3 and T4.
tldr: Hip adductor and abductor strength change meaningfully across an Australian Football season: adduction strength and the adduction-to-abduction ratio peak at mid-season before declining at post-season below pre-season values. Relying solely on pre-season hip strength testing to screen for groin injury risk or guide return-to-play criteria is insufficient—strength profiles shift considerably through the year. Regular in-season testing is needed to accurately reflect a player's current neuromuscular condition, and training load was not associated with these changes, suggesting that training volume alone does not explain the seasonal pattern.
methods: Cross-sectional repeated measures design. 38 elite AFL players from one club (2016 season). Maximal isometric hip adduction and abduction strength measured at T1 (start pre-season, November 2015), T2 (end pre-season, February 2016), T3 (mid-season, June 2016), and T4 (post-season, August 2016) using a hand-held dynamometer (PowerTrack II Commander, JTECH) with external belt fixation. Adduction-to-abduction ratio calculated by dividing maximal adduction by maximal abduction score. Training load quantified via session-RPE × duration. Adjusted linear mixed regression models with player characteristics (age, BM, AFL experience, games played prior to and in 2016) as covariates.
findings: Hip adduction strength was significantly greater at T3 vs. T1 (+22.71N, p<0.001) and significantly weaker at T4 vs. T1 (−18.6N, p=0.004). No significant difference between T1 and T2 for adduction. Hip abduction strength was significantly weaker at T4 vs. T1 (−24.67N, p<0.001); no difference between T1–T2 or T1–T3. Adduction-to-abduction ratio was significantly greater at T3 (+0.15N, p<0.001) vs. T1. No overall differences between preferred and non-preferred kicking legs in adduction (p=0.409) or abduction (p=0.602). However, at T3 and T4, the preferred leg adduction-to-abduction ratio was significantly lower than the non-preferred leg (T3: −0.14N, p=0.020; T4: −0.15N, p=0.019). Training load was not significantly associated with hip strength changes. Body mass was associated with abduction strength and the ratio.
limitations: Single club limits generalizability. Post-season player motivation and availability may have influenced T4 results (reduced effort anecdotally noted). Training load was not recorded during the off-season (prior to T1). The study was observational and cannot determine causation for the seasonal strength changes. No injured players were included, so findings apply only to uninjured athletes.
practicalImplications: Pre-season hip strength testing alone cannot serve as a reliable reference point for the entire season—values change significantly by mid-season and post-season. Strength and conditioning and sports medicine staff should implement regular in-season hip strength monitoring (e.g., mid-season and post-season) to track individual player changes. The preferred kicking leg shows a relatively lower adduction-to-abduction ratio at mid-season and post-season compared to the non-preferred leg, which may reflect asymmetric loading from kicking demands and could be relevant for groin injury risk stratification during the competitive season.
athleteDev: For developing AFL players, this study highlights that strength profiles are not static across a season and that pre-season screening captures only a single snapshot. Developmental programs should incorporate longitudinal strength monitoring to detect individual athletes whose adduction strength or adduction-to-abduction ratio deviates from expected seasonal trajectories. The groin-protective threshold of >80% adduction-to-abduction ratio may need to be evaluated relative to where a player is in their seasonal cycle, not just their pre-season value.
rtp: Return-to-play benchmarks for groin strain in AFL commonly use the uninjured limb as a reference, or target an adduction-to-abduction ratio of 90–100%. This study shows that hip strength symmetry and ratios fluctuate across the season in uninjured players, which has direct implications for RTP criteria. Using a pre-season value or a static normative reference may be misleading if a player's uninjured baseline has shifted. RTP assessments should ideally compare the recovering limb to in-season normative data for that athlete or to matched in-season data from uninjured teammates.

citation: Lonie TA, Brade CJ, Finucane ME, Jacques A, Grisbrook TL. Hip adduction and abduction strength and adduction-to-abduction ratio changes across an Australian Football League season. J Sci Med Sport. 2020;23(1):2-6.
abstract: This cross-sectional repeated-measures study examined changes in maximal isometric hip adduction and abduction strength, and the adduction-to-abduction ratio, in 38 uninjured elite Australian Football (AF) players at four time points across a season: start of pre-season (T1), end of pre-season (T2), mid-season (T3), and post-season (T4). Strength was measured using a hand-held dynamometer with external belt fixation. Hip adduction strength and the adduction-to-abduction ratio were significantly greater at T3 compared to T1 (adduction by 22.71 N, p<0.001; ratio by 0.15, p<0.001), while both adduction and abduction were significantly weaker at T4 compared to T1. No overall difference between preferred and non-preferred legs in adduction or abduction strength was found; however, at T3 and T4 the adduction-to-abduction ratio of the preferred kicking leg was significantly lower than the non-preferred leg. Training load measured via session-RPE was not significantly associated with hip strength changes.
tldr: Hip strength is not static across an AFL season — it rises meaningfully from pre-season to mid-season and drops post-season. Pre-season screening captures only a single snapshot. Because the preferred kicking leg develops a lower adduction-to-abduction ratio by mid-season (even in uninjured players), using the contralateral limb as a return-to-play reference requires caution. Regular in-season testing better reflects true player conditioning than a single pre-season measurement.
methods: Cross-sectional repeated measures. 38 uninjured elite AFL players (one club, 2016 playing list). Maximal isometric hip adduction and abduction strength measured at four time points (T1: November 2015 pre-season start; T2: February 2016 pre-season end; T3: June 2016 mid-season; T4: August 2016 post-season) using a hand-held dynamometer (HHD; PowerTrack II Commander, JTECH Medical) with external belt fixation following Thorborg et al. protocol. Three 5-second maximal contractions performed; highest value used. Adduction-to-abduction ratio calculated from peak scores. Training load quantified via session-RPE across three periods. Adjusted linear mixed regression models with player characteristics (training load, age, body mass, years of experience, games played) as covariates analysed changes over the season.
findings: Hip adduction strength was significantly greater at T3 vs T1 (+22.71 N, p<0.001) and significantly weaker at T4 vs T1 (-18.6 N, p=0.004). Hip abduction was significantly weaker at T4 vs T1 (-24.67 N, p<0.001). No significant difference between preferred and non-preferred legs overall in adduction (p=0.409) or abduction (p=0.602). A significant interaction between leg and time existed for the adduction-to-abduction ratio: at T3 the preferred kicking leg ratio was 0.14 lower (p=0.020) and at T4 it was 0.15 lower (p=0.019) than the non-preferred leg. Training load was not associated with strength changes. Body mass was associated with abduction strength (+0.989 N/kg, p=0.008) and inversely with the adduction-to-abduction ratio.
limitations: Single club sample limits generalisability. Post-season effort may have been reduced anecdotally. Training load was quantified via session-RPE only, which may not capture asymmetric sport-specific loading (e.g., kicking volume). The study cannot determine whether strength changes were driven by training adaptation, residual effects of previous injuries, or technique variation. No game-level GPS or kick-count data were collected to quantify limb-specific loading.
practicalImplications: A single pre-season hip strength screen does not accurately represent a player's condition throughout the season; regular in-season testing at multiple time points is recommended. The preferred kicking leg shows a lower adduction-to-abduction ratio by mid- and post-season even in uninjured players, meaning the uninjured limb may not be a reliable reference for return-to-play decisions following a unilateral groin strain. Session-RPE-based training load monitoring alone is insufficient to explain individual hip strength variation.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, this study underscores the need to monitor hip adduction and abduction strength longitudinally rather than relying solely on pre-season data. Strength and conditioning programming should account for the natural in-season rise in adductor strength and the asymmetries that develop in kicking-dominant athletes over a season. Incorporating bilateral hip strength testing at multiple seasonal phases supports more targeted loading and conditioning interventions.
rtp: When making return-to-play decisions following a unilateral groin strain, practitioners should be cautious about using the contralateral (non-injured) limb as the gold-standard comparator, as even uninjured players demonstrate significantly lower adduction-to-abduction ratios in the preferred kicking leg by mid- and post-season. Establishing player-specific in-season baselines provides more clinically meaningful RTP benchmarks than a single pre-season measurement.

citation: O'Connor C, Renaghan E, Bennett A, Ferrer L, Damodaran R, Geraghty P, Feigenbaum LA. Hip adduction and abduction strength values in NCAA Division I American football players: strength values and a comparison across position groups. Phys Ther Sport. 2025; doi:10.1016/j.ptsp.2025.01.004.
abstract: This cross-sectional study is the first to report maximal isometric hip adduction squeeze and abduction press strength values in NCAA Division I American football players, and to compare strength across position groups. Eighty-five male players from an ACC program were tested using the ForceFrame Strength Testing System in two positions: 0° hip-flexion long-lever and 0° hip-flexion short-lever. Absolute, relative (N/kg), and normalised (Nm/kg) strength values were reported. Players were grouped as lineman (offensive/defensive line), mid-position (tight end, running back, linebacker), or skill position (quarterback, wide receiver, defensive back, punter, kicker, snapper). ANOVA revealed significant differences in relative hip adduction and abduction strength between position groups (medium-to-large effect size, n²=0.116–0.284), with skill and mid-position players demonstrating significantly greater relative strength than linemen. Overall hip adduction:abduction ratios ranged from 0.68–0.88 (long-lever) and 1.05–1.09 (short-lever), notably lower than other field-sport cohorts.
tldr: NCAA Division I American football players demonstrate lower hip adduction and abduction relative strength — and markedly lower adduction:abduction ratios — compared to soccer, Gaelic football, and rugby union athletes. Lineman-position players show the lowest relative hip strength of all position groups despite similar absolute values, suggesting their large body mass masks meaningful strength deficits relative to sport demands. These findings establish a first reference dataset and highlight a potential groin injury risk that warrants targeted hip strengthening, particularly for linemen.
methods: Cross-sectional study. 85 male NCAA Division I football players (mean age 20.0±1.79 years; mass 108.3±23.69 kg; height 188.7±6.57 cm) from one ACC program. Testing conducted at the university training facility at the start of summer pre-season. ForceFrame Strength Testing System (VALD Performance) used for maximum isometric hip adduction squeeze and abduction press in two positions: 0° hip-flexion long-lever (malleoli against force pads) and 0° hip-flexion short-lever (femoral condyles against force pads). Two maximal 5-second trials per test position; peak value used. Absolute (N), relative (N/kg), and normalised (Nm/kg) values reported bilaterally and unilaterally. Players grouped into lineman, mid-position, and skill-position categories. ANOVA with Bonferroni post-hoc compared position groups; pain during testing recorded on a 0–10 NPRS.
findings: ANOVA revealed significant differences (p≤0.05) in relative hip adduction and abduction strength between position groups with medium-to-large effect sizes (n²=0.116–0.284). Skill and mid-position players had significantly greater relative hip adduction squeeze and abduction press strength than linemen across both testing positions (differences of 0.37–1.01 N/kg in adduction, 0.37–1.01 N/kg in abduction). No significant differences were found between skill and mid-position groups. Absolute strength values were not significantly different between position groups. Hip adduction:abduction ratios ranged from 0.68–0.88 in the long-lever position and 1.05–1.09 in the short-lever position — lower than comparable field-sport cohorts. The lineman group showed an adduction:abduction ratio of 0.68±0.21 in the long-lever position, falling below the 0.80 threshold associated with elevated groin injury risk.
limitations: Data collected from a single NCAA Division I program; results may reflect that specific programme's strength and conditioning practices rather than the broader population. Testing conducted at the start of summer pre-season; values may change as the season progresses. The convenience sample limits generalisability across Division I programs. Players with prior hip/groin injury were included to reflect the true playing population, potentially influencing strength values.
practicalImplications: This study provides the first hip strength reference values for American football players using a gold-standard ForceFrame testing protocol, enabling practitioners to benchmark individual athletes. The notably low hip adduction:abduction ratios — especially in linemen (0.68) — suggest a population-wide risk for hip/groin injury that may be underappreciated. Routine hip strength screening and targeted hip adductor and abductor strengthening programmes, particularly for lineman-position players, are warranted to reduce groin injury risk and improve player availability.
athleteDev: Position-specific differences in relative hip strength should inform training programme design. Linemen, who have the lowest relative adduction and abduction strength despite their mass, may benefit most from targeted hip adductor/abductor strengthening incorporated into their strength and conditioning. Skill and mid-position athletes, who perform more high-speed running and change-of-direction, appear to develop greater relative hip strength naturally through those movement demands, but should still be individually screened against normative references.
rtp: The low hip adduction:abduction ratios observed in this American football cohort — particularly the 0.68 ratio in linemen, below the 0.80 injury-risk threshold — suggest that return-to-play criteria used in other field sports (e.g., achieving a ratio ≥0.90) may be relevant targets for post-groin-injury clearance in American football. Position-specific normative data from this study can be used to set individualized RTP benchmarks for players recovering from hip/groin injuries.

citation: Koyama T, Saita Y, Tateishi T, Tanita T, Ukita H, Shima T, Itaya N, Yaguchi H, Yamamoto N, Mori Y, Aizawa T, Nagao M. Hip internal rotation restriction and heel raise mechanics as risk factors for Jones fractures in youth football players. Asia Pac J Sports Med Arthrosc Rehabil Technol. 2025;42:15-20.
abstract: This cross-sectional study investigated the prevalence and risk factors for Jones fractures (stress fractures of the proximal metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal) among 232 male youth football players from 20 top-level high school teams in Japan during the 2024 tournament. Medical examinations combined ultrasound and portable X-ray imaging with physical testing including hip internal rotation (HIR) range of motion, single heel raise (SHR) weight-bearing position, ankle lunge test, straight leg raise, single-leg sit-to-stand, toe grip strength, and hip abductor strength. Eighteen players (7.8%) tested positive on ultrasound and seven (3.0%) had confirmed fractures on X-ray; 11 had a fracture history. Restricted HIR of <30° was significantly associated with Jones fracture risk (Crude OR 3.74, p=0.01). Combined restricted HIR <30° with lateral weight-bearing during SHR further elevated risk (Crude OR 4.02, p=0.007), and this combination remained significant after adjustment for height, weight, and dominant foot (Adjusted OR 3.87, p=0.009).
tldr: Youth football players with restricted hip internal rotation (<30°) who also bear weight on the lateral side of the foot during a single heel raise face approximately four times the risk of Jones fracture compared to unaffected peers. Restricted HIR induces femoral/tibial external rotation and foot supination, increasing lateral metatarsal loading. Screening these two simple clinical measures can identify high-risk athletes for targeted preventive interventions before a complete fracture develops.
methods: Cross-sectional medical examination study. 232 male high school football players (mean age 16±1 years) from 20 top-level Japanese teams examined during a 2024 tournament. Fracture identification: ultrasound (hump sign or beak sign) confirmed by ≥2 physicians; positive cases confirmed by portable dental X-ray device; fractures classified as true fractures. Physical examinations by 32 physical therapists included: HIR range of motion (prone active rotation measured by digital inclinometer), single heel raise weight-bearing position (neutral vs. lateral), ankle lunge test (cm), straight leg raise angle, single-leg sit-to-stand time, toe grip strength, and hip abductor strength. Questionnaire collected height, weight, dominant foot, and fracture history. Statistical analysis: Wilcoxon signed-rank test (within-injury-group bilateral comparison), Mann-Whitney U test (injury vs. control groups), ROC curve for HIR cutoff, univariate and multivariate logistic regression adjusted for height, weight, and dominant foot. IRB approval: Juntendo University (M16-0167).
findings: 18 of 232 players (7.8%) positive on ultrasound; 7 (3.0%) confirmed true fractures on X-ray; 11 had fracture history. Injury group: 16 limbs with fractures or fracture history; control group: 422 limbs. HIR was significantly lower in the injury group vs. controls (27.0° vs. 35.0°, p=0.02). Lateral weight-bearing during SHR trended higher in the injury group (75% vs. 53%, p=0.08). HIR <30° associated with Crude OR 3.74 (p=0.01), Adjusted OR 3.77 (p=0.01). Lateral weight-bearing alone: Crude OR 2.65 (p=0.096). Combined HIR <30° and lateral SHR weight-bearing: Crude OR 4.02 (p=0.007), Adjusted OR 3.87 (p=0.009). ROC AUC for HIR=0.68; cutoff 30° yielded sensitivity 62.5%, specificity 65.7%. SL-STS time was significantly longer on the injured vs. contralateral limb (p=0.008).
limitations: Small sample of confirmed fractures (n=16 injury limbs) may have reduced statistical power and contributed to the modest ROC AUC. Cross-sectional design from a single tournament prevents causal inference. Timing of physical examination varied (pre- vs. post-match), introducing possible fatigue effects. Ultrasound sensitivity is uncertain; negative findings assessed by only one examiner. Multiple PTs performed examinations, introducing potential inter-rater variability despite use of measurement instruments.
practicalImplications: A simple two-test screen — active HIR range of motion in prone and weight-bearing position during a single heel raise — can identify youth football players at elevated Jones fracture risk. Players with HIR <30° and lateral SHR weight-bearing are approximately four times more likely to sustain a Jones fracture. Early screening followed by targeted hip flexibility work (e.g., caudal mobilisation with movement) and peroneal/intrinsic foot muscle strengthening may reduce fracture incidence. Early ultrasound detection of incomplete fractures allows treatment while maintaining training participation.
athleteDev: Jones fractures are particularly prevalent among high school and college-aged football players and carry a high refracture rate (up to 67% with conservative management). Youth players should be screened for restricted HIR — potentially linked to cam morphology that becomes increasingly prevalent between ages 12–19 — and corrective flexibility and foot strengthening programmes should be implemented early. Playing on artificial turf and shoe design are additional modifiable extrinsic risk factors to address in youth football settings.
rtp: Surgical treatment for Jones fractures in athletes yields return-to-play times of 8–10 weeks with a 5.6% refracture rate, substantially better than conservative management (18.5% refracture rate). Preventing complete fracture progression through early detection (ultrasound/extracorporeal shock wave therapy while training continues) is preferable. Players cleared to return should demonstrate corrected lateral weight-bearing mechanics during heel raise and normalised HIR before full return to high-intensity football activities to minimise refracture risk.

citation: Valente HG, Oliveira RR, Baroni BM. How are hamstring strain injuries managed in elite men's football clubs? A survey with 62 Brazilian physical therapists. Phys Ther Sport. 2023;61:73-81.
abstract: This cross-sectional survey study describes the assessment and rehabilitation practices of physical therapists managing hamstring strain injury (HSI) in elite Brazilian men's football. Sixty-two physical therapists from 35 of 40 eligible clubs (87.5% representativeness) competing in the 2021 Brazilian Championship Series A and B responded. Despite heterogeneous assessment practices, all respondents used imaging exams, applied injury classification systems (most commonly Munich Consensus, 45.2%), and evaluated pain, range of motion, muscle strength, and functional status. Rehabilitation programs were typically divided into 3–4 phases. All respondents applied electrophysical agents and stretching; 98.4% used strengthening exercises (93.5% including eccentrics); 96.8% used manual therapy; 95.2% applied football-specific functional exercises; and 93.5% incorporated lumbopelvic stabilisation exercises. Muscle strength was the most frequently reported RTP criterion (71% of respondents).
tldr: In elite Brazilian football, HSI rehabilitation is near-universally multimodal — combining electrophysical agents, manual therapy, progressive strengthening (almost always with eccentric overload), lumbopelvic stabilisation, and sport-specific exercises — yet meaningful heterogeneity exists in assessment depth and classification. Many practitioners rely on subjective range-of-motion and manual strength testing despite evidence supporting objective tools. Muscle strength is the dominant RTP criterion, aligning with expert consensus, but sprint and functional performance criteria are also frequently used.
methods: Cross-sectional study using an online questionnaire developed by the authors and delivered in Brazilian Portuguese. Participants: physical therapists from clubs in the 2021 Brazilian Men's Football Championship Series A (20 clubs) and Series B (20 clubs); 95 invited, 62 responded (65% adherence) from 35 clubs (87.5% representativeness). Questionnaire covered 30 questions in three sections: workplace structure/organisation, HSI assessment practices, and HSI rehabilitation practices. Distributed via WhatsApp Messenger or email; data collected March–December 2021. Researchers followed up with participants to clarify ambiguous responses. Descriptive statistics (frequency distributions, means, SDs) computed in Microsoft Excel. Ethics approval: Federal University of Health Sciences of Porto Alegre (#4.838.243).
findings: All 62 respondents used imaging exams; 100% used injury classification systems (Munich Consensus 45.2%, British Classification 24.2%). Initial evaluation timing varied: 35.5% preferred 12–24 h post-injury, 29% first 12 h. All assessed pain, strength, and functional status. Strength testing: 83.9% used manual resistance testing; only 35.5% used hand-held dynamometry. Range-of-motion assessment: 58.1% quantitative, 56.5% subjective. 90.3% organised rehabilitation into phases (3 phases: 45.2%; 4 phases: 33.9%). All used electrophysical agents (analgesic currents 93.5%, photobiomodulation 88.7%, cryotherapy 87.1%). Manual therapy: 96.8% (myofascial release 95.2%). Strengthening: 98.4%; eccentric exercises 93.5%. Lumbopelvic stabilisation: 93.5%. Football-specific exercises: 95.2%. Periodic rehabilitation assessments: 100% (pain 83.9%, strength 82.3%). RTP criteria: muscle strength 71%, sprint/high-speed running 58%, intense accelerations 43.5%, pain 43.5%.
limitations: Only Brazilian physical therapists participated; results may not reflect practices in other countries due to differences in professional training, culture, and club resources. Self-reported questionnaire data are subject to response bias and inconsistencies. The study captures perceptions and stated practices, which may differ from actual clinical practice. Female football clubs not included. Coordination of field transition phases between physical therapists and strength/conditioning coaches was not investigated.
practicalImplications: The near-universal adoption of eccentric strengthening (93.5%), lumbopelvic stabilisation (93.5%), and sport-specific exercises (95.2%) by elite Brazilian football PTs aligns well with current clinical practice guidelines. However, the low uptake of objective strength measurement tools (only 35.5% using HHD) and objective ROM quantification suggests a gap between available evidence and common practice. Departments should invest in training staff in objective assessment and implementing validated RTP criteria beyond subjective judgment. Electrophysical agents are widely used despite limited evidence; resources may be better directed towards supervised exercise.
athleteDev: The structured, phase-based rehabilitation approach (3–4 phases) and progressive incorporation of sport-specific football demands — including high-speed running, change of direction, sprints, jumps, and passing/kicking — reflects best-practice principles for returning athletes to full football performance. Early introduction of eccentric loading and lumbopelvic stabilisation exercises supports tissue remodelling and neuromuscular control critical for preventing re-injury. This survey provides a real-world benchmark for what comprehensive HSI rehabilitation looks like in elite football environments.
rtp: Muscle strength (71%) and sprint/high-speed running performance (58%) were the most commonly reported RTP criteria among elite Brazilian football physical therapists, broadly consistent with Delphi consensus recommendations. However, objective strength measurement tools were underutilised, potentially limiting the precision of RTP decisions. Staff should adopt standardised, objective RTP benchmarks — including hamstring strength symmetry, functional hop tests, and GPS-monitored sprint performance — to improve the reliability of RTP clearance and reduce the ~18% recurrence rate reported in the literature.

citation: Guitart M, Alonso-Callejo A, Rodas G, Cos F, Martin-Garcia A, Franquesa X, Carles B, Valle X, Yangüas X, Felipe JL. How do muscle injuries relate to return-to-performance metrics in male elite football players? Biol Sport. 2026;43:165-175.
abstract: This longitudinal prospective study examined how the type, location, and severity of hamstring and quadriceps muscle injuries relate to time elapsed for male elite football players to regain pre-injury performance levels in high-speed running (HSR, >21 km/h), sprint distance (>24 km/h), and maximum velocity (Vmax). Data were collected from 333 elite players from an elite Spanish La Liga club (male 2nd team, U-19, U-18) over five seasons (2017/18–2021/22). A total of 234 injuries (81 medical attention, 167 time-loss) were analysed using linear mixed models with 7-day pre- and post-injury time windows (Pre5–Pre1; Post1–Post5). Hamstring injuries significantly reduced Vmax and HSR post-RTP, with severe injuries requiring up to five weeks to recover; severe hamstring injuries produced a 1.43 km/h reduction in Vmax and a 32.90 m decrease in HSR in the first post-RTP week. Quadriceps injuries showed less impact, with only mild injuries producing a significant reduction in Vmax (−1.18 km/h) and HSR (−52.70 m) in the first week post-RTP.
tldr: Returning to the pitch after RTP criteria are met does not equate to returning to pre-injury performance. Severe hamstring injuries cause measurable deficits in maximum velocity and high-speed running for up to five weeks post-RTP, while quadriceps injuries resolve much faster. This study quantifies the performance gap and underscores that RTP and return-to-performance are distinct milestones that require different monitoring frameworks — particularly for hamstring injuries, where incomplete performance recovery may increase re-injury risk.
methods: Descriptive longitudinal prospective study. 333 elite male football players from three squads (Male 2nd Team, U-19, U-18) of one elite Spanish La Liga club over five seasons (2017/18–2021/22). 234 hamstring and quadriceps muscle injuries included (81 medical attention, 167 time-loss). Injuries classified per UEFA consensus by severity: medical attention (0 days), mild (1–7 days), moderate (8–28 days), severe (>28 days), and coded with OSICS-10. GPS external load data collected during all on-pitch training sessions and matches using WiMU PRO devices (RealtrackSystems). Performance variables: HSR distance (>21 km/h), Sprint distance (>24 km/h), and Vmax (km/h). 7-day time windows established before (Pre5–Pre1) and after RTP (Post1–Post5). Linear mixed models (LMM) fitted for each muscle group and injury severity with Time Windows as fixed effects, Days Off as numeric covariate, and Pre1 as reference level. Statistical analyses in R 4.2.2 with lme4 package. FC Barcelona Ethics Committee approval (#2019FCB28).
findings: Hamstring injuries (all severities) showed significant reductions in HSR during Post1 period (p<0.05) except moderate. Severe hamstring injuries produced significant reductions in Vmax at Post1 (−1.43 km/h, p=0.01), Post2 (−0.73, p=0.04), Post3 (−1.16, p=0.01), Post4 (−0.73, p=0.04), and Post5 (−0.90, p=0.02); and in Sprint distance at Post1 (−20.27 m, p=0.02). Severe hamstring injuries also required up to 5 weeks to recover Vmax to pre-injury levels. Quadriceps mild injuries: significant reduction in Vmax (−1.18 km/h, p=0.04) and HSR (−52.70 m, p=0.01) only in Post1. No significant performance changes for moderate or severe quadriceps injuries. Days Off was significantly associated with Vmax recovery in severe hamstring injuries (coefficient=0.00, p=0.01).
limitations: Data from a single elite club; game model and training methodology were consistent across teams but may limit generalisability. Some injury observations were missed due to player transfers or contract endings reducing follow-up completeness. Players who changed teams mid-season were included only for the period they trained/played at the club. Goalkeepers were excluded. The study does not capture neuromuscular testing or psychological readiness, which are important RTP/RTPerf components. Follow-up limited to 5 weeks post-RTP; longer-term recovery trajectories could not be assessed.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should distinguish between RTP (medical clearance to train/play) and return to performance (RTPerf — regaining pre-injury GPS metrics). For severe hamstring injuries, rehabilitation and performance staff must collaboratively plan a progressive reloading phase lasting at minimum 4–5 weeks post-RTP before expecting full performance restoration. Daily GPS-based monitoring of HSR, sprint distance, and Vmax is recommended to track individual recovery trajectories and adjust training loads accordingly, reducing risk of re-injury through premature high-intensity exposure.
athleteDev: This study highlights injury location as a critical determinant of performance recovery timelines: hamstring injuries require significantly longer performance restoration than quadriceps injuries. For elite football development programmes, integrating GPS performance monitoring into injury surveillance — not just return-to-play criteria — provides more nuanced information about when athletes are truly ready to compete at full intensity. Implementing coordinated rehabilitation and performance strategies that align with individual load histories is essential for maintaining squad availability and long-term athletic development.
rtp: RTP clearance based on clinical criteria alone is insufficient for hamstring injuries — particularly severe ones, where Vmax and HSR remain significantly below pre-injury levels for up to five weeks after returning to play. Return-to-performance should be treated as a separate, subsequent phase of rehabilitation with objective GPS performance benchmarks (pre-injury HSR, sprint distance, and Vmax averages) as clearance criteria. The first week post-RTP carries the greatest performance deficit and likely the highest re-injury risk, warranting conservative load management even after medical staff have cleared the player.

citation: Tribolet, R., Sheehan, W. B., Novak, A. R., Watsford, M. L., & Fransen, J. (2022). How does practice change across the season? A descriptive study of the training structures and practice activities implemented by a professional Australian football team. International Journal of Sports Science & Coaching, 17(1), 63–72.
abstract: This observational study examined practice activities used by eight professional coaches across 72 training sessions in one season within a professional Australian Football League (AFL) team. Coaches implemented seven practice types categorized as training-form (fundamentals, fitness, skills) and playing-form (breakdown, transition, line-specific, match simulation). Practice type frequencies differed significantly between pre- and in-season (χ²=109.6, p<0.001). In-season activities had longer durations and coaches implemented more playing-form practice in-season (62.0±25.4%) versus pre-season (52.4±19.8%). Training-form practice frequency was higher pre-season, while playing-form frequency was similar across phases. Coaches altered the frequency of practice types more than exposure time.
tldr: Professional AFL coaches shift practice structure across the season, emphasizing more training-form (fundamentals, fitness, skills) activities pre-season and transitioning toward more playing-form (match simulation, line-specific) activities in-season. The overall split of training-form to playing-form remained roughly equal across the whole season (51%/49%), but the type composition changed meaningfully. Skill practice dominated both phases. This data-driven view of coaching practice gives sport scientists a framework for understanding where and how to advocate for more representative learning design throughout the season.
methods: Observational study of one professional AFL club (48 male players). Data collected across 72 practice sessions (37 pre-season, 35 in-season) over one full season. Two cameras filmed each session; a single experienced observer coded time spent in each of seven coach-defined practice activity types. Practice types were reclassified by researchers as training-form or playing-form per Ford et al. frameworks. Chi-square tests of independence, paired samples t-tests, and effect size statistics (Cohen's d) were used. Intra-class correlation coefficients assessed coding reliability (r=0.97–0.99).
findings: Practice type frequencies differed significantly between pre- and in-season (χ²=109.6, p<0.001). The largest pre-season frequencies were skill (24.2%), fundamentals (17.5%), and fitness (15.2%); in-season leaders were skill (35.1%), match simulation (24.3%), and line-specific (19.5%). Fitness dropped to zero in-season. Practice duration was significantly longer in-season (8.0±0.4 vs 7.0±0.8 min, p=0.023, d=−0.17). Coaches altered training-form frequency significantly more than playing-form frequency between phases (χ²=30.8, p<0.001). The percentage of session time in playing-form activities was not significantly different between phases (in-season 62.0±25.4% vs pre-season 52.4±19.8%, p=0.112).
limitations: Data collected from a single professional AFL team, limiting generalizability to other teams or sports. Results are preliminary and rely on descriptive analysis. Variation in playing-form activities between pre- and in-season may reflect load management or scheduling constraints rather than intentional skill acquisition periodization. No individual player data were collected. Observer annotation was at the team rather than player level.
practicalImplications: Sport scientists and skill acquisition specialists can use this framework to objectively monitor training practice types and their exposures throughout the season. Quantifying training-form versus playing-form proportions can highlight opportunities to increase representative learning design. The finding that coaches naturally shift toward more game-specific activities in-season aligns with skill acquisition principles, but the persistently high proportion of training-form activities (especially isolated skill drills) across both phases suggests room to advocate for more perceptually rich, coupled practice environments.
athleteDev: AFL-context findings translate directly to American football development: coaches should progressively increase playing-form (scrimmage-style, situational) practice as competition approaches. Tracking the proportion of isolated drills versus representative game-play activities during training camp and regular season provides an objective coaching audit tool. Pre-season emphasis on fundamentals and fitness is appropriate, but integrating more small-sided and match-simulation activities earlier may accelerate perceptual-motor skill transfer.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play protocols. However, the classification framework (training-form vs. playing-form) could be applied to RTP progressions: athletes returning from injury should advance from isolated rehabilitation exercises (training-form analog) toward fully representative, contact-level match simulation (playing-form analog) in a structured, monitored sequence.

citation: Hecksteden, A., Berger, J., Mazzolari, R., Zhang, G., Meyer, T., Brink, M., Lemmink, K., Fritz, J., Keller, N., & Hauser, T. (2026). How to mitigate injury risk after return-to-play in elite football? Expert judgement meets data analytics. Science and Medicine in Football. Advance online publication.
abstract: This mixed-methods study leveraged synergies between expert practitioner knowledge and data analytics to identify options for reducing post-RTP injury risk in elite European football. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with 14 experienced practitioners (orthopaedic surgeons, physiotherapists, rehab/S&C coaches, sport scientists) from top European football. Thematic analysis identified four themes: (1) rehab time and its effect on injury risk, (2) rehabilitation strategy, (3) psychosocial factors, and (4) team structure. Data analytics used publicly available injury data (transfermarkt.com, 2023/24 season, n=8644 complete episodes) and Cox proportional hazards models with two alternative time axes (calendar days, football exposure in weighted hours) to scrutinize expert judgment regarding rehab time. Hazard ratios for pooled episodes were 0.886 (0.834–0.937) for calendar days and 1.046 (1.005–1.087) for football exposure.
tldr: Expert practitioners in elite European football believe injury risk post-RTP would be meaningfully lower with longer rehabilitation times, but data analytics reveal a more nuanced picture. Longer rehab time is associated with lower subsequent injury rates when measured in calendar days — however, this protective association disappears (and reverses) when time-at-risk is measured in football exposure hours post-RTP, suggesting that football exposure rather than calendar time may be the primary driver. The most actionable expert-identified improvements are logistically simple: smooth transitions between rehab phases, gradual load progression, psychosocial support, and consistent application of proven rehabilitation measures. Teams should not mistake early injury-free return exposures for evidence of low underlying risk.
methods: Mixed-methods sequential problem-solving design. Qualitative: semi-structured interviews (March–November 2023) with 14 elite European football practitioners; thematic analysis using Braun and Clarke's six-phase model. Quantitative: publicly sourced injury data from top European leagues (Austria, France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Spain, UK), season 2023/24, tracking complete index injury–RTP–subsequent injury episodes. Cox proportional hazards models with player ID as random effect; two time axes: calendar days and football exposure (match minutes + training minutes/8). Rehab time scaled within index injury diagnosis using interquartile range. ROC curves used to explore threshold effects.
findings: Four expert themes identified: (1) 'They return too early' — experts broadly agree longer rehab reduces injury risk, via tissue maturation, conditioning, and psychological readiness; (2) 'Mission command' — consistent, humble rehab strategy with tight feedback loops and full load achieved during guided rehab; (3) 'Psychosocial factors deserve more attention' — internal/external pressure and emotional recovery influence risk; (4) 'Small can be beautiful' — large medical teams carry communication and responsibility-overlap risks. Quantitative: HR for calendar days = 0.886 (95% CI 0.834–0.937), p<0.05 (lower injury rate with longer rehab). HR for football exposure = 1.046 (1.005–1.087), p<0.05 (higher injury rate per exposure hour with longer rehab). No threshold effect for rehab time found in ROC analysis. High football exposure post-RTP observed almost exclusively after early return.
limitations: Media-based injury data lacks detailed medical information (grading, tissue defect severity). Training times required estimation from literature values. Interviews not recorded verbatim; limited to salient terms and phrases. Small number of interviewees limits qualitative richness. Results specific to elite European football context. Analyses include only complete episodes with subsequent injury, which may underrepresent players with very long injury-free periods. Season distribution of RTP and football exposure could confound results.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should not interpret a negative association between rehab time and calendar-day injury rate as straightforward evidence that longer rehab causes lower injury risk — football exposure post-RTP is a key confounder. Early returners accumulate higher football exposure rapidly, which itself elevates risk. If early RTP is unavoidable, match playing time should be minimized in initial exposures. Rehab programs should use consistent, proven measures with tight feedback loops, achieve close-to-match loads before RTP, and provide psychosocial support. Communication quality within multi-disciplinary teams is critical.
athleteDev: For athlete development contexts, this study reinforces that accelerating return from injury carries real risk that may be masked when injury rate is expressed per calendar day rather than per unit of football exposure. Development-stage players may face additional vulnerability if they return to full competition loads before tissue healing and conditioning are complete. Building a culture of gradual, progressive loading and transparent communication between coaches and medical staff is a practical takeaway applicable across competitive levels.
rtp: Directly addresses RTP decision-making in elite football. Key findings: (1) experts universally believe longer rehab reduces reinjury risk, consistent with biological healing timelines; (2) data confirm lower injury rate per calendar day with longer rehab, but this association does not hold when expressed per football exposure hour post-RTP; (3) no clear threshold rehab duration was identified; (4) the combination of early RTP and high immediate football exposure is the highest-risk constellation. RTP decisions should weight exposure management post-clearance as critically as the clearance decision itself. Psychosocial readiness and load management during the post-RTP period warrant dedicated protocols.

citation: Rowell, A. E., Aughey, R. J., Hopkins, W. G., Stewart, A. M., & Cormack, S. J. (2017). Identification of sensitive measures of recovery after external load from football match play. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance, 12(7), 969–976.
abstract: This study investigated the sensitivity of countermovement-jump (CMJ) performance measures and salivary hormones (testosterone and cortisol) to levels of accelerometer-derived PlayerLoad during elite A League football match play. Eighteen elite male football players were measured before (27 h, 1 h) and after (0.5, 18, 42, 66, 90 h) three consecutive preseason matches. Match load was divided into tertiles: low (<500 au), medium (500–1000 au), and high (>1000 au). CMJ variables and hormonal concentrations were analyzed using a linear mixed model; sensitivity was quantified via t-statistics and magnitude-based inferences. The ratio of flight time to contraction time (FT:CT) was identified as more sensitive to match load than jump height alone, with reductions persisting to later post-match time points.
tldr: FT:CT — the ratio of flight time to contraction time during a countermovement jump — is the most sensitive CMJ variable for monitoring recovery after football match play, outperforming jump height by detecting neuromuscular fatigue that persists beyond what jump height reveals. Players accumulating more than 500 PlayerLoad units show meaningfully reduced CMJ performance for at least 42 hours post-match, suggesting practitioners should wait at least 42 hours before prescribing intensive training. Cortisol spikes 55–165% immediately post-match across all load levels, but the hormonal response shows substantial individual variability, limiting the utility of routine salivary hormone monitoring as a team-level recovery tool.
methods: Eighteen elite male A League football players (age 23.3±4.1 y) from one club were tested across three consecutive preseason matches. CMJ was performed on a force plate (Fitness Technology) with hands on hips; variables included jump height, FT:CT, peak velocity, peak/mean power, peak force, and contraction time. Saliva samples (2 mL passive drool, frozen at −80°C) were analyzed for cortisol (μg/dL) and testosterone (pg/mL) via ELISA. Accelerometer-derived PlayerLoad (100-Hz) quantified match load divided into three tertiles. Data analyzed with SAS linear mixed model (Proc Mixed); changes expressed as % ± 90% CI and standardized effects (ES). Nonclinical magnitude-based inference framework applied.
findings: Jump height was reduced in medium load at 0.5 h (10%±7%) and 18 h (7%±4%) post-match, and in high load at 0.5 h (16%±8%) and 18 h (9%±5%). FT:CT showed a 12%±7% reduction in high load at 0.5 h post, with reductions in medium and high load at 18 h, and FT:CT reductions persisting to 42 and 66 h post in some groups. Jump height changes were unclear at 42 h across all loads. Cortisol increased 55–165% across all load tertiles at 0.5 h post with substantial individual variability thereafter. Testosterone increased 17–20% across all loads at 0.5 h. T:C ratio was reduced (large/very-large effects) at 0.5 h post for all loads, suggesting an acute catabolic state, with unclear changes at 18 h and small-to-moderate increases at 42 h.
limitations: Small sample (n=18) from a single club limits generalizability. Testing was conducted during the preseason, which may not reflect in-season physiological state. Arbitrary division of PlayerLoad into tertiles may not fully discriminate individual responses; players within the same tertile could differ by ~500 au. Activity profile (speed distribution, number of sprints vs. continuous low-intensity work) within a given PlayerLoad was not controlled. Circadian variation in cortisol and testosterone could not be fully eliminated due to match timing constraints. Low-load group trained at 18 h post-match, potentially confounding recovery measures at later time points.
