practicalImplications: FT:CT from a CMJ is the most useful metric for monitoring recovery status after football match play and should be assessed for at least 42 hours post-match to guide training prescription. Players accumulating PlayerLoad >500 au show impaired neuromuscular function for at least 42 hours, indicating that high-intensity training or heavy loading sessions should be avoided within this window. Routine salivary hormone monitoring at the team level has limited utility due to high individual variability and the cost of analysis; individualized monitoring is recommended instead. Staff can use simple CMJ testing as a daily readiness screen in the days following competition.
athleteDev: For developing football players, CMJ-based monitoring — particularly FT:CT — provides an objective, cost-effective method to assess neuromuscular readiness after games and demanding practices. Training loads in the 42 hours following high-load match play should be reduced to allow recovery. Teaching young athletes and support staff to interpret FT:CT trends relative to individual baselines (not population norms) builds a readiness-monitoring culture that supports long-term health and performance development.
rtp: The study establishes that neuromuscular function, as measured by FT:CT, is impaired for at least 42 hours after high-load match play. In RTP contexts, monitoring CMJ FT:CT before and during return-to-participation progressions provides an objective indicator that neuromuscular readiness has been restored. Athletes should demonstrate FT:CT values returning toward individual baseline before being cleared for full-contact practice or match participation. The dose-response relationship between PlayerLoad and FT:CT supports using objective load tracking as part of any RTP decision framework.

citation: Ghelich Afshar, N., Soltani, M., & Naderinasab, H. (2025). Identifying key factors influencing hamstring muscle strains in football players: A narrative review with descriptive analysis. International Journal of Sport Studies for Health, 8(1), 64–78.
abstract: This narrative review with descriptive analysis synthesized peer-reviewed literature published between 2010 and 2024 to identify intrinsic and extrinsic factors contributing to hamstring muscle strains in football players and evaluate existing prevention and rehabilitation strategies. Intrinsic factors examined included muscle strength imbalances (quadriceps-to-hamstring ratio), flexibility, injury history, age, fatigue, neuromuscular control, and genetic predisposition. Extrinsic factors included training load and intensity, playing surfaces, environmental conditions, footwear, and coaching methods. Evidence-based interventions such as Nordic hamstring exercises, dynamic warm-ups, and workload management were evaluated. Hamstring strains represent 12–16% of all football injuries and occur at an annual rate of 4–7 per 1000 match-hours in professional players. Recurrence rates are 30–40%, primarily within the first two months after return to play.
tldr: Hamstring strains in football are multifactorial, requiring attention to both intrinsic risk factors (strength imbalances, poor flexibility, prior injury, age, fatigue, neuromuscular deficits) and extrinsic factors (excessive training load, artificial turf, congested match schedules, inadequate warm-up and coaching methods) to effectively reduce injury rates and recurrence. The strongest evidence supports Nordic hamstring exercises for eccentric strength development, dynamic warm-ups incorporating progressive activation, and load monitoring with acute-to-chronic workload ratios. Rehabilitation must progress through acute management, eccentric strengthening, neuromuscular retraining, and sport-specific drills before return to play. Gaps remain regarding genetic predisposition and long-term rehabilitation program outcomes.
methods: Narrative review with descriptive analysis. Literature search conducted in PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar using keywords including 'hamstring strain,' 'football injuries,' 'muscle injury risk factors,' 'biomechanics of hamstrings,' and 'injury prevention in athletes.' Inclusion criteria: peer-reviewed original research articles, systematic reviews, meta-analyses, and clinical trials published in English between January 2010 and December 2024 directly addressing hamstring strains in football. Studies from other sports included if providing relevant insights. Quality assessment used Newcastle-Ottawa Scale for cohort studies and Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool for RCTs. Data synthesized thematically into intrinsic and extrinsic factor categories.
findings: Hamstring strains account for 12–16% of all football injuries with incidence of 4–7 per 1000 match-hours in professional players; annual increase of ~4% reported. Recurrence rate is 30–40%, mostly within first two months post-RTP. Intrinsic factors: low Q:H strength ratio is a well-documented risk factor; limited hamstring flexibility increases tensile stress during sprinting/kicking; previous injury history is among the strongest predictors of reinjury; players over 30 have significantly higher susceptibility; fatigue and neuromuscular control deficits compound risk. Extrinsic factors: excessive training loads and congested schedules elevate fatigue-related risk; artificial turf associated with higher hamstring strain incidence versus natural grass; inadequate warm-up routines increase likelihood of strain; coaching methods and scheduling affect recovery adequacy. Nordic hamstring exercise reduces injury rates and reinjury; dynamic warm-ups with progressive activation reduce incidence; acute-to-chronic workload monitoring helps tailor training to individual capacity.
limitations: Narrative (rather than systematic) review design introduces selection and publication bias. Studies included are heterogeneous in design, population characteristics, and outcome measures, limiting meta-analytic synthesis. Genetic predisposition research remains sparse and mechanistically unclear. Long-term effectiveness and sustainability of rehabilitation programs are poorly documented — most studies focus on short-term outcomes. Variations in protocol standardization, adherence, and implementation across teams and competitive levels limit generalizability of prevention program findings.
practicalImplications: Football medical and performance staff should screen players using Q:H strength ratio assessments and eccentric hamstring strength tests (isokinetic dynamometry, functional movement screens) to identify at-risk individuals. Nordic hamstring exercises should be integrated consistently into training programs, not only in pre-season. Dynamic warm-ups with progressive neuromuscular activation should precede all training sessions and matches. Acute-to-chronic workload monitoring (GPS, workload ratios) should guide training prescription, particularly during congested fixture periods. Post-injury rehabilitation must address eccentric strength deficits and neuromuscular retraining before sport-specific progressions and RTP.
athleteDev: Young football players face unique hamstring strain risk related to growth-related muscle imbalances and reduced flexibility during rapid growth phases. Development programs should incorporate age-appropriate hamstring strengthening (eccentric focus), dynamic flexibility training, and education on injury prevention principles. Screening for Q:H imbalances at developmental ages can identify players who need targeted interventions before high-load competitive environments increase their risk. Coaching methods that allow adequate recovery between high-intensity sessions are particularly important for youth populations.
rtp: Premature return to play is identified as a primary driver of the 30–40% hamstring reinjury rate, often within the first two months post-RTP. RTP criteria must include restoration of eccentric strength and neuromuscular control to pre-injury or symmetrical levels, pain-free performance of high-intensity activities, and absence of compensatory movement patterns. Nordic hamstring exercise should be a component of rehabilitation and maintained post-RTP. Structured sport-specific progressions — sprinting, cutting, kicking — should be introduced gradually with monitoring tools (strength assessments, functional movement tests) to verify readiness at each stage. Ongoing load management and education post-RTP are essential to prevent recurrence.

citation: Meyers, M. C., Sterling, J. C., & Robinson, S. K. (2024). Imaging and surgical procedures after surface-related collegiate football injuries on artificial turf versus natural grass: Prevalence and trends over 15 seasons. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine, 12(9), 23259671241274144.
abstract: This prospective cohort study (Level 2 evidence) evaluated imaging and surgical procedures following surface-related collegiate football injuries at 39 FBS universities over 15 competitive seasons (2006–2020). A total of 2224 games were documented (1106 on heavyweight artificial turf [≥9.0 lb/ft²], 1118 on natural grass). Of 9137 total injuries, 4010 (44%) were surface-related. MANOVA revealed significant main effects between surfaces for imaging procedures (F2,1738=4.718; P=.009), surgical procedures (F1,539=5.974; P=.003), and medical diagnosis (F2,456=2.643; P=.040). Artificial turf was associated with significantly lower incidences of imaging after player-to-surface and shoe-to-surface trauma, lower body surgeries, ligament tear surgeries, and fewer diagnoses of syndesmosis sprains and Lisfranc injuries. Significant rising trends in combined medical procedures (P=.005) and combined substantial/severe injuries (P=.0007) were observed irrespective of surface over the 15-year period.
tldr: Collegiate football played on heavyweight artificial turf (≥9.0 lb/ft²) resulted in significantly fewer imaging and surgical procedures, and fewer diagnoses of serious foot and ankle injuries (syndesmosis sprains, Lisfranc trauma), compared with natural grass over 15 seasons. Contrary to widespread perception, this generation of artificial turf was associated with lower medical intervention rates. However, both surfaces showed a significant upward trend in combined medical procedures and severe injuries over the study period — suggesting increasing player size, speed, and aggression are overriding any surface-related safety improvements. ACL and associated tissue injuries comprised 53% of all knee surgeries, with no significant surface effect on knee injury rates.
methods: Prospective cohort study across 39 FBS universities (2006–2020). Participating universities played on both heavyweight artificial turf (≥9.0 lb/ft²) and natural grass during the same seasons. Lightweight infill games (n=376) were excluded. Certified athletic trainers (ATCs) completed standardized injury surveillance forms within 7 days of each game; follow-up via telephone ensured completeness. Outcomes included imaging (radiograph, CT, MRI), surgical procedures, and specific diagnoses categorized by injury type, anatomical location, and surface. MANOVA with Wilks λ criteria using general linear model procedures; incidence rates (IRs) expressed per 10-game season. Simple linear regression assessed 15-season trends. Statistical power: 1−β=0.732–1.000.
findings: Artificial turf vs. natural grass incidence rates (IR per 10 games): total imaging 7.1 vs. 8.6; player-to-surface impact imaging 1.5 vs. 2.1; shoe-to-surface contact imaging 4.4 vs. 5.2; foot imaging 0.6 vs. 1.1; ligament sprain/tear imaging 4.6 vs. 5.3; muscle strain/tear imaging 0.2 vs. 0.7; shoe-to-surface contact surgeries 1.1 vs. 1.6; lower body surgeries 1.8 vs. 2.3; ligament tear surgeries 1.0 vs. 1.5; syndesmosis sprain/tear diagnoses 0.7 vs. 1.0; Lisfranc trauma diagnoses 0.3 vs. 0.5. All differences significant (P<.05). No significant surface effect on knee injury rates. Mean surgical procedures per season: artificial turf 15.4, natural grass 20.5. Significant positive trends over 15 seasons for natural grass combined procedures (r=0.612), mean combined procedures (r=0.684), and combined substantial/severe injuries (r=0.776).
limitations: Inability to control for inherent random variation in high-collision sports injury. Could not account for athlete strength/conditioning status, precompetition workloads, injury history, weather conditions, field maintenance quality, coaching style, foul play, or individual biomechanical differences. Orthotic support information was not obtained. Time between trauma and advanced medical procedures and incidence of multiple operations over time were not tracked. Findings generalizable only to FBS collegiate football competition. Research support was received from FieldTurf USA for the lead author — a potential conflict of interest. Inherent challenges of multi-team, multi-index data collection over 15 years.
practicalImplications: Decisions about playing surface installations at the collegiate level should be informed by longitudinal, multi-team data rather than perception or anecdote. Heavyweight artificial turf systems (≥9.0 lb/ft²) are associated with fewer imaging and surgical interventions than natural grass for surface-related injuries, particularly for foot, ankle, and lower extremity ligament trauma. However, the secular rise in severe injuries and medical procedures irrespective of surface suggests that playing surface alone is insufficient to address growing injury burden — player size, speed, rule changes, and play intensity require broader intervention. Future injury surveillance should routinely include post-trauma medical procedure tracking beyond simple time-loss metrics.
athleteDev: The significant upward trend in substantial and severe injuries over 15 seasons (r=0.776, P=.0007) indicates that collegiate football athletes are facing increasing injury burden regardless of surface. Programs should invest in comprehensive injury surveillance systems that capture imaging and surgical outcomes — not just time loss — to accurately assess severity trends. Surface choice alone cannot offset the injury risk associated with increasing athleticism and play intensity; strength, conditioning, and load management remain primary modifiable factors for athlete development programs.
rtp: The finding that natural grass was associated with significantly higher rates of lower extremity ligament surgeries, syndesmosis sprains, and Lisfranc injuries has direct implications for RTP timelines. Syndesmotic sprains and Lisfranc injuries are associated with prolonged recovery and high reinjury risk if not fully healed. The rising trend of severe injuries over 15 seasons underscores the importance of rigorous, criterion-based RTP protocols that account for injury severity — particularly for lower extremity ligament and foot injuries — rather than simple time-loss thresholds. Facilities choosing playing surfaces should understand the downstream medical procedure burden associated with each surface type.

citation: Bourgon V, Duval FG, Forest G. Circadian variation in coaches' decision-making in the National Football League's evening games. Chronobiology International. 2024.
abstract: This study explored whether NFL coaches show circadian variation in their decision-making on fourth down when traveling through time zones. Data from visiting teams across 20 NFL regular seasons (2000–2020) were analyzed (n=5,360 games). Decision-making was measured as the percentage of offensive plays on fourth down. A factorial ANCOVA and moderation analysis were used to examine whether travel direction and time of game moderated fourth-down decision patterns. In evening games, coaches traveling westward called significantly more offensive plays on fourth down (M=14.90%) compared to teams traveling within the same time zone (M=12.20%, p=0.03) or eastward (M=11.35%, p=0.007). The moderation analysis showed that only in evening games did greater westward longitudinal degrees traveled predict higher percentages of fourth-down offensive plays, with the effect significant for games starting at 16:24h or later. No significant effect of travel direction was found in day games. Fourth-down rates also increased significantly over the seasons studied. The study suggests that circadian misalignment from westward travel may influence coaching decision-making, not just player performance, in professional football.
tldr: NFL coaches traveling westward for evening games call significantly more fourth-down offensive plays than those traveling in other directions, suggesting that circadian misalignment from time-zone travel impairs coaching decision-making — not just player performance — in professional football.
methods: Retrospective analysis of 20 NFL regular seasons (2000–2020); n=5,360 games; data from profootballreference.com and nfl.com; visiting team statistics extracted; dependent variable: percentage of offensive plays on fourth down; independent variables: travel direction (westward/same TZ/eastward), longitudinal degrees traveled, time of game (day vs. evening, and continuous), year of season; factorial ANCOVA (Time of game × Direction of travel) with year as covariate; moderation analysis via PROCESS 3.1; post-hoc Tukey t-tests for evening games.
findings: In evening games, westward-traveling teams had significantly higher fourth-down offensive play rates (14.90%) vs. same-TZ (12.20%, p=0.03) and eastward travelers (11.35%, p=0.007). No travel direction effect in day games. Moderation analysis: greater westward longitudinal degrees predicted more fourth-down plays only in games starting after 16:24h. Fourth-down offensive play rate increased significantly across seasons (covariate effect p<0.001). Effect size small (0.2% variance explained) but statistically significant.
limitations: No individual coach sleep or chronotype data available; game-situation confounders (score, time remaining, field position, opponent strength) not controlled; fourth-down decisions represent only one type of coaching decision; small explained variance (0.2%); exploratory study design; cannot establish causality.
practicalImplications: Sleep and circadian rhythm interventions for traveling NFL teams should target not only players but also coaching and support staff. Teams traveling westward for evening games should implement proactive circadian adaptation strategies (light exposure, sleep scheduling, melatonin) to protect cognitive decision-making quality. NFL schedulers should consider giving earlier game-time slots to teams traveling westward through multiple time zones.
athleteDev: Beyond physical performance, circadian misalignment from westward travel in American football can impair the complex cognitive and strategic decision-making of coaches. Staff education on the cognitive effects of jet lag and proactive circadian management should be standard practice for any team engaging in cross-timezone travel.
rtp: Circadian disruption from westward travel in evening games may impair coaching decision-making, which in high-leverage situations could affect return-to-play or in-game management decisions for players. Medical and performance staff should account for circadian state when making high-stakes decisions following long-distance westward travel.

citation: Han Z, Sun P, Chen T, Dong Y. Club-based vs. hospital-guided rehabilitation after ACL reconstruction in football participants: a two-year prospective cohort study. Frontiers in Sports and Active Living. 2025;7:1641750.
abstract: This prospective cohort study compared two-year clinical outcomes and return to sport (RTS) following ACL reconstruction in football players randomized to either hospital-guided rehabilitation (n=20) or club-based sport-specific rehabilitation (n=20). Participants were football players and enthusiasts aged 18–40 who underwent primary ACL reconstruction at Huashan Hospital, Fudan University (2018–2021). Hospital-guided rehabilitation emphasized standardized joint function recovery supervised by orthopedic surgeons and physical therapists. Club-based rehabilitation was led by strength and conditioning coaches at the Dalian Football Base and incorporated high-intensity, football-specific tasks (agility drills, explosive power, simulated match scenarios, psychological readiness) at greater frequency and commitment. At two-year follow-up, the club-based group demonstrated significantly better Tegner Activity Scores (8.5 vs. 7.2, p=0.006), Marx Activity Rating Scale scores (12.7 vs. 10.6, p=0.019), and ACL-RSI scores (73.3 vs. 54.3, p=0.003). Time to return to training games (12.4 vs. 18.3 weeks, p=0.042) and official matches (19.0 vs. 26.5 weeks, p=0.002) was significantly shorter in the club-based group. No significant differences were found in ROM, joint stability, IKDC, or Lysholm scores. Return-to-competitive-sport rate was 90% (club) vs. 65% (hospital). One reinjury occurred in each group. Club-based rehabilitation produced superior subjective functional outcomes and faster RTS without increasing reinjury risk.
tldr: Club-based sport-specific rehabilitation after ACL reconstruction in football players produces superior subjective function, greater psychological readiness (ACL-RSI), and significantly faster return to both training and competitive matches compared to hospital-guided rehabilitation, without increasing reinjury risk over a two-year follow-up.
methods: Prospective cohort study; N=40 football players and enthusiasts (18–40 years) with primary ACL reconstruction (Huashan Hospital, 2018–2021); two groups: hospital-guided (n=20) and club-based (n=20); baseline characteristics comparable; outcome measures: Tegner, Marx, IKDC, Lysholm, ACL-RSI scores; objective clinical assessments (swelling, ROM, stability); return-to-sport timing and reinjury rate at 2-year follow-up; statistical analysis: independent-samples t-test, Mann-Whitney U, chi-square; alpha=0.05.
findings: Club-based group: significantly higher Tegner (8.5 vs. 7.2, p=0.006), Marx (12.7 vs. 10.6, p=0.019), and ACL-RSI (73.3 vs. 54.3, p=0.003) scores. No significant differences in IKDC or Lysholm. Return to training games: 12.4 vs. 18.3 weeks (p=0.042). Return to official matches: 19.0 vs. 26.5 weeks (p=0.002). Competitive RTS rate: 90% vs. 65%. No significant differences in ROM, stability, or reinjury rate. One reinjury per group.
limitations: Small sample size (n=20 per group); non-random group assignment based on rehabilitation modality choice; higher proportion of professional footballers in club group (55% vs. 30%, p=0.110) may confound results; residual confounding from graft type and surgical technique differences; club-based model requires greater time, cost, and athlete commitment, limiting scalability; single-center design; generalizability to female athletes or other sports not established.
practicalImplications: Football programs should consider integrating sport-specific, club-based rehabilitation elements after ACL reconstruction, particularly for competitive players who need earlier return to training and matches. Psychological readiness (ACL-RSI) should be explicitly assessed and targeted — the large gap in ACL-RSI scores (73.3 vs. 54.3) highlights that standard hospital rehab may inadequately prepare athletes psychologically for return to play. Criteria-based RTS should include functional and psychological benchmarks, not only clinical clearance.
athleteDev: For developing football athletes, ACL rehabilitation should move beyond joint-level recovery toward sport-specific functional restoration. Club-based programs incorporating agility, explosive power, match simulations, and psychological readiness interventions from early rehabilitation phases are more effective at restoring competitive capacity. Strength and conditioning coaches should be integrated into the rehabilitation team from early stages post-surgery.
rtp: Club-based sport-specific rehabilitation achieves competitive return to football 7.5 weeks faster than hospital-guided rehab (19.0 vs. 26.5 weeks to official match) with a higher competitive RTS rate (90% vs. 65%) at 2 years post-ACL reconstruction. Return-to-play criteria should include sport-specific functional tests (Tegner, Marx, ACL-RSI) alongside traditional clinical measures. Psychological readiness (ACL-RSI) was substantially lower in hospital-guided patients, suggesting this group requires additional psychological support before competitive clearance.

citation: Ferrari F, Stein R, Milani M. Electrocardiographic diversity in football players: how distinct ethnic patterns shape athletes' hearts. European Journal of Preventive Cardiology. 2026;33:6–7.
abstract: This interview-format editorial presents findings from a series of four landmark studies examining ECG patterns in over 6,000 male Brazilian soccer (football) players conducted by Ferrari, Stein, and colleagues over two decades. Key findings across the series: (1) 97% of ECGs were normal, with only 3% showing abnormalities — lower than African cohorts (23%), partially attributed to Brazil's high ethnic admixture; (2) early repolarization (ER) was common (40%), especially in Black (48%) and Mixed-race (41%) athletes, with the classic J-wave/ascending ST-segment morphology predominating and no sudden cardiac deaths during mean 4-year follow-up; (3) the 'African/Afro-Caribbean' ECG pattern was rare in Brazilian players and was not associated with structural abnormalities on echocardiography when present; (4) ECG abnormalities become more common with age (especially 26–35 years). Mixed-race athletes remain underrepresented in current international ECG interpretation guidelines. The authors advocate for current international criteria (Drezner et al., 2017) as effective when correctly applied, and call for expanded ECG databases in admixed populations. Inferolateral T-wave inversion should never be dismissed even with normal echocardiogram.
tldr: A two-decade series studying ECGs in over 6,000 Brazilian footballers found that 97% were normal, early repolarization is common and benign in this population, the 'African/Afro-Caribbean' ECG pattern is rare due to Brazil's high ethnic admixture, and current international criteria are effective when correctly applied — but mixed-race athletes remain underrepresented in guidelines.
methods: Interview-format editorial summarizing findings from four previously published studies (B-Pro Foot ECG study series); combined sample >6,000 male Brazilian soccer players from clubs across Brazil; ECG screening with imaging correlates (echocardiography, cardiac MRI); analysis by ethnicity (White, Black, Mixed-race); longitudinal follow-up (mean 4 years).
findings: 97% of ECGs normal (3% abnormal); early repolarization prevalence 40% overall (48% Black, 41% Mixed-race); 'African/Afro-Caribbean' ECG pattern rare in Brazilian players due to genetic admixture; no sudden cardiac deaths during 4-year follow-up; ECG abnormalities increase with age (especially 26–35 years); Mixed-race athletes' ECG findings intermediate between White and Black athletes; inferolateral T-wave inversion warrants cardiac MRI even with normal echo; early repolarization benign in athletic context.
limitations: Limited to male athletes; no female athlete data in published series; interview/editorial format limits primary data presentation; single-country (Brazil) sample may limit generalizability; ethnic self-identification heterogeneity within 'Black' and 'Mixed-race' categories; no data on non-soccer football codes.
practicalImplications: Team physicians screening football players should not over-interpret early repolarization patterns — these are common, training-related, and benign in this population. Inferolateral T-wave inversion should always prompt cardiac MRI evaluation, even when echocardiogram is normal. Current international ECG criteria (Drezner 2017) are effective and appropriate when applied correctly. ECG interpretation programs should account for ethnic diversity and admixture.
athleteDev: Pre-participation ECG screening in football programs involving ethnically diverse or admixed athlete populations should use current international criteria adapted for the specific population. Longitudinal cardiac monitoring should intensify after age 26 when ECG abnormality rates increase.
rtp: Football players with abnormal ECG findings — particularly inferolateral T-wave inversion — should not be cleared for return to sport without cardiac MRI evaluation, regardless of echocardiogram result. Early repolarization patterns, including J-waves with ascending ST-segments, do not require restriction from sport participation. Ethnic background and admixture should inform ECG interpretation during return-to-play cardiac clearance protocols.

citation: Bergstein VE, Lu AZ, Hodgens BH, Bodendorfer BM, DeFroda SF, Kaplan J, Aiyer AA. Fantasy football points capture performance declines in National Football League offensive skill players following an ankle injury. Journal of Orthopaedics. 2024.
abstract: This retrospective matched cohort study investigated performance changes in NFL offensive skill players following ankle injuries using fantasy football points as a validated performance metric. Researchers analyzed 303 NFL players from 2009 to 2020 using Pro Football Reference and FantasyData. Players with ankle injuries were matched to uninjured controls. Fantasy points per game (PPG) and total fantasy points were compared pre- and post-injury. Route runners (RBs, WRs, TEs) experienced statistically significant declines in fantasy PPG and total points post-injury (p<0.0001), while quarterbacks showed no significant performance change. The study supports the use of fantasy football statistics as an objective, position-appropriate measure of performance decline following ankle injuries in the NFL.
tldr: Ankle injuries significantly reduce fantasy football performance in route-running skill players (RBs, WRs, TEs) but not QBs, suggesting position-specific functional demands determine the extent of performance decline following ankle injury.
methods: Retrospective matched cohort study; N=303 NFL players (2009–2020); data from Pro Football Reference and FantasyData; matched injured players to uninjured controls by position, age, and experience; compared fantasy PPG and total points pre- vs. post-injury; statistical analysis with paired comparisons (p<0.0001 threshold).
findings: Route runners (RBs, WRs, TEs) showed statistically significant declines in both fantasy PPG and total fantasy points following ankle injury (p<0.0001). QBs showed no significant performance change post-ankle injury. Fantasy football statistics served as a valid proxy for on-field performance decline in skill positions.
limitations: Fantasy points may not capture all aspects of athletic performance; sample limited to NFL players from 2009–2020; matching variables may not fully control for all confounders; severity and type of ankle injury not uniformly classified; return-to-play criteria not standardized across teams.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should expect meaningful performance decrements in route-running positions (RBs, WRs, TEs) following ankle injuries. Fantasy football point metrics can supplement traditional performance monitoring tools to quantify post-injury functional decline. Return-to-play criteria for these positions should account for the high ankle mobility and agility demands intrinsic to their roles.
athleteDev: For developing NFL skill players, ankle injury prevention and targeted rehabilitation protocols are critical given the significant performance impact documented in route runners. Position-specific functional testing after ankle injury should be incorporated before return to full competition.
rtp: Route runners (RBs, WRs, TEs) experience significant performance declines post-ankle injury; return-to-play decisions should incorporate objective performance metrics (e.g., fantasy PPG proxies) in addition to clinical clearance. QBs appear less affected and may return to performance baseline sooner after ankle injury.

citation: Cosio PL, Moreno-Simonet L, Fernández D, Lloret M, Padulles X, Padulles JM, Farran-Codina A, Rodas G, Cadefau JA. Football (soccer) match-derived hamstring muscles residual fatigue can be monitored using early rate of torque development. European Journal of Applied Physiology. 2024.
abstract: This study examined whether early rate of torque development (RTD) is a more sensitive marker of residual hamstring fatigue after soccer match play than maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC) torque. Nineteen male regional first-division soccer players underwent repeated measures assessments at pre-match (MD-1), post-match (MD+0), MD+1, MD+2, and MD+3. Early RTD (RTD0–50 and RTD0–100 ms) remained significantly reduced at 72 hours post-match (MD+3), while MVIC torque had recovered by that time point. Biomarkers including creatine kinase (CK), sarcomeric mitochondrial creatine kinase (sMtCK), malondialdehyde (MDA), and irisin remained elevated throughout the monitoring period. These findings indicate that early RTD captures residual neuromuscular fatigue that persists beyond when maximal strength has recovered, making it a more sensitive tool for post-match recovery monitoring.
tldr: Early rate of torque development (RTD0–50 and RTD0–100) remains depressed 72 hours after a soccer match even after MVIC torque has recovered, suggesting RTD is a more sensitive marker of residual hamstring fatigue and should be incorporated into post-match recovery monitoring.
methods: Repeated measures design; N=19 male regional first-division soccer players; assessment time points: MD-1, MD+0, MD+1, MD+2, MD+3; measures included MVIC torque, early RTD (RTD0–50, RTD0–100), and blood biomarkers (CK, sMtCK, MDA, irisin); isokinetic dynamometry used for torque and RTD measurements.
findings: RTD0–50 and RTD0–100 remained significantly reduced at MD+3 (72h post-match) while MVIC torque had recovered. CK, sMtCK, MDA, and irisin remained elevated throughout MD+0 to MD+3. Early RTD was more sensitive to residual hamstring fatigue than maximal isometric strength, indicating incomplete neuromuscular recovery despite normalized MVIC.
limitations: Small sample size (n=19); limited to male regional first-division players, reducing generalizability to elite or female populations; single match observation limits extrapolation to congested fixture periods; no control for training load between match and assessment days.
practicalImplications: Standard MVIC-based strength testing may give a false sense of recovery readiness after a soccer match. Early RTD assessment (particularly RTD0–50 and RTD0–100) should be incorporated into post-match monitoring protocols as it reveals persistent neuromuscular deficits up to 72 hours post-match. This has implications for scheduling training intensity and contact sessions during the recovery microcycle.
athleteDev: Developing soccer and football players should be monitored using RTD-based assessments post-match, not solely maximal strength tests, to accurately gauge neuromuscular readiness. Coaches and S&C staff should avoid high-intensity sprint or power sessions within 72 hours of competition based on these recovery timelines.
rtp: Following hamstring strain or fatigue in football players, RTD0–50 and RTD0–100 are more sensitive return-to-play markers than MVIC torque alone. Practitioners should not rely solely on normalized maximal isometric strength as clearance criteria; early RTD should be within normal ranges before progression to full practice or competition.

citation: de Oliveira DM, Silva AKAC, Macedo AG, Fernandes MB, Fernandes EV. Global research trends in sports nutrition and football over the last 20 years (2004-2024). Sports. 2025;13:365.
abstract: This scientometric review analyzed global research trends in sports nutrition and football over the past 20 years (2004–2024). A total of 526 original articles were retrieved from Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus databases. Bibliometric analysis was performed using Bibliometrix and VOSviewer. Publications increased by 1,450% over the study period. The United Kingdom led production (46.6% of articles). Nutrients was the most productive journal. Morton J and Maughan R were identified as the most productive authors. Early research trends focused on hydration and carbohydrate supplementation, while recent trends have shifted toward low energy availability (LEA), polyphenols, and recovery nutrition. The findings highlight the rapid growth of football-specific sports nutrition research and identify emerging areas for future investigation.
tldr: A 20-year scientometric analysis reveals a 1,450% increase in sports nutrition and football research, with UK institutions leading, and a thematic shift from hydration/carbohydrate focus toward low energy availability, polyphenols, and recovery strategies.
methods: Scientometric review; 526 original articles from Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus (2004–2024); bibliometric analysis using Bibliometrix R package and VOSviewer; keyword co-occurrence and co-citation analysis; no experimental data collection.
findings: 1,450% increase in publications from 2004 to 2024; UK led research output (46.6%); Nutrients was the top journal; Morton J and Maughan R were the most productive authors; early research themes = hydration and carbohydrates; recent themes = LEA, polyphenols, and recovery nutrition.
limitations: Scientometric design cannot assess research quality or clinical applicability; limited to three databases (WoS, PubMed, Scopus), potentially missing relevant publications in other sources; keyword selection may introduce selection bias; restricted to English-language publications.
practicalImplications: Sports nutrition practitioners working with football players should be aware of emerging research priorities: low energy availability, polyphenol supplementation, and recovery nutrition are increasingly evidence-based areas. Traditional foci on hydration and carbohydrates remain important but are well-established. Staff should stay current with Nutrients journal and leading researchers (Morton, Maughan) for cutting-edge guidance.
athleteDev: Football athlete development programs should increasingly incorporate LEA screening, polyphenol-rich dietary strategies, and structured recovery nutrition protocols based on the direction of current research. Nutrition periodization aligned with training phases is an emerging priority.
rtp: Recovery nutrition, including polyphenol supplementation and optimized carbohydrate-protein timing, is an emerging focus in football nutrition research. Return-to-play nutrition protocols should integrate these evidence-based strategies to accelerate tissue repair and reduce systemic inflammation.

citation: Luxenburg D, Wasserman N, Geller J, Perez J, Burke J, Kaplan L. Hamstring injuries in the National Football League: An epidemiological study. Journal of Orthopaedics. 2022.
abstract: This epidemiological study examined the incidence and distribution of hamstring injuries (HSIs) across NFL positions over four regular seasons (2013–2016). Researchers analyzed 78 hamstring injuries using publicly available NFL gamebooks and Foxsports.com data. Standardized incidence ratios (SIRs) were calculated by position. Linebackers (SIR 2.02) and defensive backs (SIR 1.62) demonstrated the highest incidence of HSIs, while offensive linemen (SIR 0.28) and defensive linemen (SIR 0.29) showed the lowest. Playing surface and short rest intervals did not significantly affect HSI rates (p=0.082 and p=0.959, respectively). The mean week of HSI occurrence was 8.05 (median 7.5), suggesting mid-season accumulation. These findings support position-specific injury prevention strategies targeting LBs and DBs.
tldr: NFL linebackers and defensive backs have the highest hamstring injury rates, while linemen have the lowest. Playing surface and short rest did not significantly influence injury rates, and injuries tend to cluster mid-season around week 8.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological study; N=78 HSIs across 2013–2016 NFL regular seasons; data from publicly available NFL gamebooks and Foxsports.com; standardized incidence ratios (SIRs) calculated by position; chi-square and Fisher's exact tests for surface and rest interval analyses.
findings: LBs had highest HSI incidence (SIR 2.02), followed by DBs (SIR 1.62). OL (SIR 0.28) and DL (SIR 0.29) had lowest incidence. Playing surface (p=0.082) and short rest (p=0.959) were not significant predictors. Mean week of injury = 8.05 (median 7.5), indicating mid-season clustering.
limitations: Retrospective design relying on public data sources may undercount injuries or misclassify positions; sample from only 4 seasons may limit generalizability; injury severity and mechanism not captured; return-to-play timelines not analyzed; no biomechanical or training load data available.
practicalImplications: NFL strength and conditioning and sports medicine staff should prioritize hamstring injury prevention resources for linebackers and defensive backs, who demonstrate significantly elevated injury rates. Mid-season monitoring (weeks 7–10) should intensify given the observed temporal clustering. Playing surface and short-rest scheduling do not appear to be modifiable risk factors based on this data.
athleteDev: Position-specific hamstring training should be emphasized for developing LBs and DBs in football programs. The high sprint and change-of-direction demands of these positions place them at greatest risk, necessitating targeted eccentric strengthening, sprint mechanics work, and load monitoring throughout the season.
rtp: Hamstring injury return-to-play protocols in NFL players should consider position-specific demands; LBs and DBs face the highest recurrence risk given their injury incidence profile. Progressive sprinting programs and criteria-based functional testing are essential before return to full practice and game activity for these positions.

citation: Valente HG, Oliveira RR, Baroni BM. How are hamstring strain injuries managed in elite men's football clubs? A survey with 62 Brazilian physical therapists. Physical Therapy in Sport. 2023;61:73-81.
abstract: This cross-sectional survey described the perceptions and practices of 62 physical therapists from 35 of 40 elite Brazilian men's football clubs (87.5% representativeness) regarding hamstring strain injury (HSI) management. Despite heterogeneity in assessment practices, all respondents used imaging exams, adopted injury classification scales, and evaluated pain, range of motion, muscle strength, and functional status. Rehabilitation programs were typically divided into 3–4 phases. All respondents applied electrophysical agents and stretching; 98.4% applied strengthening exercises (93.5% including eccentrics); 96.8% used manual therapy; 95.2% used exercises mimicking football's functional demands; and 93.5% used lumbopelvic stabilization exercises. Muscle strength was the most frequently reported return-to-play criterion (71% of respondents).
tldr: A survey of 62 Brazilian elite football physical therapists reveals that HSI rehabilitation universally incorporates imaging, classification systems, eccentric strengthening, manual therapy, electrophysical agents, and lumbopelvic stabilization, with muscle strength being the top return-to-play criterion.
methods: Cross-sectional study; online survey of 62 physical therapists from 35 of 40 elite Brazilian football clubs (Series A and B, 2021 season); 30-question validated questionnaire covering club structure, HSI assessment practices, and rehabilitation approaches; exploratory data analysis with descriptive statistics.
findings: All 62 respondents used imaging exams and injury classification systems. 98.4% applied strengthening exercises (93.5% included eccentrics). 96.8% used manual therapy; 95.2% used sport-specific functional exercises; 93.5% used lumbopelvic stabilization. Muscle strength was the top RTP criterion (71%). Most programs were 3–4 phases. 76% used time since injury as a progression criterion. Electrophysical agents used by 100% of respondents, most commonly analgesic currents (93%), photobiomodulation (89%), and cryotherapy (87%).
limitations: Only Brazilian physical therapists participated, limiting generalizability to other national football contexts; cultural and training system differences may affect findings; self-reported questionnaire data is prone to inconsistent responses; women's football clubs not included.
practicalImplications: Elite football physical therapy for HSI consistently emphasizes eccentric strengthening, lumbopelvic stabilization, and functional football-specific exercises. Muscle strength testing should be a core component of RTP criteria. Electrophysical agents and manual therapy are widely used adjuncts. Practitioners should standardize assessment tools (e.g., inclinometer for ROM, dynamometer for strength) given evidence supporting their prognostic value for time to RTP.
athleteDev: Developing football players with HSI should receive structured 3–4 phase rehabilitation incorporating eccentric hamstring strengthening, lumbopelvic stabilization, dynamic stretching, and progressive sport-specific running drills. Objective strength and functional criteria should guide phase progressions rather than time alone.
rtp: Muscle strength is the most reported RTP criterion among Brazilian elite football physical therapists, followed by sprint/high-speed running performance. RTP decisions should incorporate objective strength testing (ideally with dynamometry), functional performance, and absence of pain. Psychological readiness is assessed by the majority of practitioners but rarely with validated questionnaires.

citation: Monk SH, Legarreta AD, Kirby P, Brett BL, Yengo-Kahn AM, Bhatia A, Solomon GS, Zuckerman SL. Imaging findings after acute sport-related concussion in American football players: A systematic review. Journal of Clinical Neuroscience. 2019;61:28-35.
abstract: This PRISMA-guided systematic review summarized advanced brain imaging findings after acute sport-related concussion (SRC) in American football players, focusing on study methodology and clinical significance. Eleven case-control studies met inclusion criteria (football players, imaging within 6 months of SRC, sample size >5). Eight of the 11 studies conducted 809 brain function comparisons, of which 149 (18%) were statistically significant. Nine of 11 studies (82%) reported positive immediate findings (within 1 week post-injury), but these studies were more likely to exhibit recall bias (86% vs. 0%) and lack baseline advanced neuroimaging (78% vs. 50%) than negative studies. Only 3 of 9 studies evaluating subacute findings (33%) reported positive results. Studies with positive findings in either time period were more likely to have recall bias and lack baseline imaging. The review concluded that positive neuroimaging changes after SRC appear transient and return to baseline within months, though methodological limitations complicate interpretation.
tldr: Advanced brain imaging (fMRI, DTI, MRS, ASL-MRI) frequently shows immediate post-concussion changes in American football players, but these findings are largely transient, resolving within weeks to months, and are confounded by recall bias and lack of baseline imaging in most studies.
methods: Systematic review (PRISMA); PubMed database search (February 2017); inclusion criteria: American football players, brain imaging within 6 months of SRC, sample size >5; 11 studies met criteria; quality assessed using Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS) and 4 a priori methodological criteria (control selection, Type I error control, recall bias, baseline imaging); imaging modalities: fMRI (n=5), DTI/DKI (n=2), MRS (n=2), ASL-MRI (n=2); total of 809 comparisons analyzed.
findings: Nine of 11 studies (82%) reported positive immediate imaging findings; only 3 of 9 studies (33%) reported persistent subacute positive findings. Studies with positive findings were more likely to have recall bias (86% immediate, 100% subacute) and lack baseline neuroimaging (78% immediate, 100% subacute). The median time to initial imaging was 72 hours. fMRI studies made 441 comparisons (15% significant); DTI studies made 302 (24% significant). Most positive changes resolved within 1–6 months post-injury.
limitations: Only 11 studies met inclusion criteria; all were case-control designs with small sample sizes (median N=12 per group); majority demonstrated recall bias and lacked baseline advanced neuroimaging; insufficient control for Type I error in several studies; inability to correct for confounders such as psychiatric history and substance use; imaging modalities and protocols were heterogeneous across studies.
practicalImplications: Advanced neuroimaging findings after SRC in football players are largely transient and should not be used as sole clinical decision-making tools without baseline imaging. Practitioners should be cautious about interpreting imaging 'abnormalities' without pre-season baseline comparisons. Future neuroimaging research should standardize protocols and include baseline imaging to improve clinical relevance.
athleteDev: Football programs at all levels should consider implementing pre-season advanced neuroimaging baselines as part of athlete health infrastructure, given the documented transient neuroimaging changes after SRC. Subconcussive impacts accumulate over a season and may alter imaging findings even without diagnosed concussion, warranting ongoing monitoring in developing players.
rtp: Current advanced neuroimaging findings after SRC in football players are largely transient and correlate inconsistently with symptom severity. RTP decisions should not rely on advanced neuroimaging alone; clinical symptom resolution, neurocognitive testing, and standardized concussion protocols remain the appropriate primary tools. Baseline neuroimaging, when available, provides the most meaningful reference for post-concussion imaging comparison.

citation: Ro J, Brushwood AE, Mokha M. Impact of ankle injury history and pre-National Football League Draft training on lower limb coordination during running: A pilot study. Cureus. 2025;17(10):e93887.
abstract: This pilot study examined the influence of a recent ankle sprain history (RASH, within 6 months) and a six-week NFL draft preparation training camp on lower limb intersegmental coordination in elite American football players. Twelve RASH players were matched to 12 non-RASH controls by position category and treadmill run speed. Running mechanics at 23 km/h were assessed pre- and post-training via a 10-camera motion capture system. Hip-ankle (HA) and knee-ankle (KA) coordination angles and variability were quantified using modified vector coding across four running gait subphases. No significant group × time interactions or main effects were found for coordination angles or variability in HA or KA (p > 0.05). However, medium effect sizes emerged for HA second half of stance (η²p=0.075) and HA coordination variability in the first half of swing (η²p=0.121), and KA first half of stance (η²p=0.093) and second half of swing (η²p=0.091). The RASH group shifted toward more ankle-dominant HA coupling and changed KA from anti-phase ankle to in-phase ankle in early stance post-training, while controls remained stable.
tldr: NFL draft-eligible football players with a recent ankle sprain history exhibit altered hip-ankle and knee-ankle coordination compared to uninjured peers, adopting compensatory proximal-dominant strategies; a six-week elite training camp produced medium-effect adaptations toward more ankle-dominant control, though changes did not reach statistical significance in this small pilot.
methods: Pilot study; N=24 elite American football players (12 RASH, 12 non-RASH controls); matched by position category (Big, Big-skill, Skill) and treadmill run speed; 10-camera 3D motion capture (Vicon, 120 Hz) with instrumented split-belt treadmill (Bertec, 1000 Hz); 23 km/h treadmill run, 5-second capture; modified vector coding for hip-ankle and knee-ankle coordination angles and variability across 4 gait subphases; 2×2 mixed model ANOVA (group × time); partial eta-squared for effect sizes.
findings: No significant group × time interactions (p > 0.05) for any HA or KA coordination or variability measure. Medium effect sizes for HA second half of stance (η²p=0.075): RASH shifted from in-phase hip-dominant to in-phase ankle-dominant after training. KA first half of stance (η²p=0.093): RASH shifted from anti-phase ankle to in-phase ankle. HA coordination variability first half of swing (η²p=0.121): non-RASH increased more than RASH post-training. Angle-angle plots showed RASH had limited ankle dorsiflexion pre-training that improved post-training, with greater hip extension at takeoff.
limitations: Small pilot sample (n=12 per group) underpowered for statistical significance; chronic ankle instability not assessed; no diagnostic imaging to confirm residual ankle abnormalities; same footwear not ensured at both sessions; no clinical measures (strength, ROM) to contextualize coordination findings; training program designed for NFL combine performance, not ankle rehabilitation; results may not generalize to non-elite populations.
practicalImplications: Even medically cleared, asymptomatic football athletes with a recent ankle sprain history exhibit altered lower limb coordination that persists into high-speed running. Elite training can partially restore ankle-dominant control patterns. Sports medicine and performance staff should use motion capture or biomechanical screening post-ankle sprain — beyond symptom resolution — to identify residual coordination deficits before returning athletes to full speed work.
athleteDev: NFL draft preparation training that incorporates multi-planar lower extremity loading (plyometrics, resisted sprints, assisted sprints) can partially restore ankle-dominant coordination patterns in players with a recent ankle sprain history. Developing football players should receive ankle-specific neuromuscular retraining targeting dorsiflexion and intersegmental coupling, not just structural rehabilitation, before progressing to full speed running.
rtp: Ankle sprain RTP clearance based on symptom resolution alone may be insufficient for elite football players; residual coordination deficits in hip-ankle and knee-ankle coupling can persist and be detected with vector coding analysis at high running speeds. Biomechanical screening at 23 km/h (or sport-specific speeds) is recommended before RTP to ensure movement pattern normalization and reduce re-injury risk.

citation: O'Malley GR, Pema P, Sweeney P, Sarwar S, Cassimatis N, Knightly JJ, Goldstein I, Patel NV. Impact of cervical and lumbar spine surgeries on National Football League (NFL) player performance and return-to-play outcomes. Cureus. 2025;17(6):e85706.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study evaluated the impact of cervical and lumbar spine surgeries on NFL players' return-to-play (RTP) rates and performance using injury reports from 2005 to 2022. A total of 144 spine surgeries (77 lumbar, 67 cervical) were identified across 136 players. Players with lumbar surgery had a 61% RTP rate and an average performance rating increase of 6.3% (PFF grade: 68 pre → 71 post). Players with cervical surgery had a 47% RTP rate and an average performance rating decrease of 5.8% (PFF grade: 68 pre → 66.7 post). Lumbar surgeries were predominantly discectomies in linemen with higher BMIs; cervical surgeries were predominantly fusions in skill positions. Players with prior lumbar surgery had a significantly higher RTP rate (83% vs. 56%, p=0.020). Only 22% of cervical surgery patients showed performance improvement vs. 58% of lumbar patients (p=0.019).
tldr: NFL players undergoing lumbar spine surgery have higher return-to-play rates (61%) and performance improvements post-op compared to those undergoing cervical surgery (47% RTP, average performance decline), with position-specific patterns — linemen predominantly lumbar, skill positions predominantly cervical — highlighting the need for tailored RTP and rehabilitation protocols.
methods: Retrospective cohort study; NFL injury reports 2005–2022; N=136 players, 144 surgeries (77 lumbar, 67 cervical); data from NFL.com, ESPN, Fox Sports, CBS Sports, Pro-Football-Reference; performance via Pro Football Focus (PFF) grades; statistical analysis: independent t-tests, chi-squared, two-way ANOVA, linear and logistic regression (Python 3.10.12); RTP defined as appearance in at least one regular-season NFL game post-surgery.
findings: 77 lumbar surgeries: 61% RTP, average PFF +6.3% (68→71); 67 cervical surgeries: 47% RTP, average PFF -5.8% (68→66.7). Prior lumbar surgery history significantly predicted higher RTP (83% vs. 56%, p=0.020). Cervical surgery patients significantly less likely to improve performance post-op (22% vs. 58%, p=0.019). Lumbar surgeries predominantly discectomies (75%) in linemen (BMI 32.3); cervical surgeries predominantly fusions (39%) in skill positions (BMI 30.5). Above-average performers had greater performance decline post-surgery regardless of type.
limitations: PFF data unavailable before 2006 and for all players; many procedure types unspecified due to NFL privacy reporting requirements; study relies on public injury reports which may be incomplete; no standardized patient-reported outcomes available; specific surgical technique details often unavailable; retrospective design limits causal inference.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and performance staff should counsel players differently based on surgery type: lumbar surgery (especially discectomy) carries a substantially better RTP and performance prognosis than cervical surgery (especially fusion). Cervical fusion patients should be prepared for likely performance decline and reduced career longevity. Position-specific rehabilitation protocols are warranted.
athleteDev: Developing football players who undergo spine surgery face significant career implications. Lumbar disc herniations treated with discectomy carry a favorable prognosis. Programs should implement load management strategies for high-BMI linemen to reduce chronic lumbar stress. Cervical spine injuries in skill positions warrant early intervention and careful surgical planning to maximize RTP potential.
rtp: Lumbar surgery (particularly discectomy) yields a 61% RTP rate with performance improvement, while cervical surgery yields only 47% RTP with average performance decline. Players with prior lumbar surgery history have higher RTP rates (83%). RTP criteria post-spine surgery should incorporate position-specific functional testing and PFF-grade benchmarks in addition to clinical clearance, with cervical surgery patients requiring more conservative progressions than lumbar surgery patients.

citation: Dupont MM, Fourman MS, Iyer S, Qureshi SA, Sheha ED, Rhie-Lee J, Dowdell J. Impact of Lumbar Disk Herniation on Performance Outcomes and New Contracts in the National Football League. Clin Spine Surg. 2022;00:000-000.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined performance outcomes and contract-signing ability for NFL players treated for lumbar disk herniation (LDH) between 2000 and 2020. Using public records, 101 players were identified (77 operative, 24 nonoperative). Pro Football Focus (PFF) grades and contract values were compared pre- and post-injury. Of the 101 players, 75 (74.3%) returned to play (RTP), with nonoperative players returning sooner (median 1.8 vs. 7.9 months). Post-treatment PFF grades were statistically similar to pre-injury levels (65.6 vs. 67.9, P=0.2), but total and guaranteed contract values were significantly reduced (P<0.01). In multivariable analysis, lower age and higher pre-injury PFF grade were independent predictors of both RTP and ability to sign a new contract; the type of treatment (surgical vs. non-surgical) was not associated with these outcomes.
tldr: NFL players treated for lumbar disk herniation can expect to return to play at preserved performance levels in about 74% of cases, but they should anticipate significantly reduced contract values afterward. The key determinants of RTP and contract-signing success are pre-injury age and performance level — not whether surgery was required. Younger players with higher pre-injury PFF grades are the most likely to return and re-sign. Teams and medical staff should counsel players that on-field performance is likely maintainable, but financial expectations should be adjusted accordingly.
methods: Retrospective cohort study using publicly available NFL records. Players diagnosed with LDH from 2000-2020 identified from weekly NFL injury reports and prosportstransactions.com, confirmed by at least two sources (NFL.com, ESPN.com, press releases). Age, BMI, position, treatment type, and RTP data recorded. Performance assessed using PFF grades for the season before injury and after treatment. Contract data collected from spotrac.com and overthecap.com, excluding rookie contracts. Multivariable logistic regression identified independent predictors of RTP and contract-signing ability. Statistical analysis performed in JMP 16.0.0.
findings: 74.3% of 101 players returned to play at least one NFL game after LDH treatment. Post-treatment PFF grades (65.6+/-12.2) not significantly different from pre-injury (67.9+/-11.5, P=0.2). Median total contract value decreased from $5.97M to $2.00M and guaranteed value from $1.67M to $0.47M (both P<0.01). Lower age (OR=0.75; 95% CI: 0.59-0.96) and higher pre-injury PFF grade (OR=1.08; 95% CI: 1.01-1.16) were independent predictors of RTP. Same factors (age OR=0.82, PFF OR=1.11) independently predicted signing a new contract. Higher guaranteed-to-total ratio in pre-injury contract was the only independent predictor of signing a post-treatment contract with >20% guaranteed money (OR=16.57).
limitations: Reliance on publicly available data introduces reporting bias and likely under-captures nonoperatively managed players. Players who retired without disclosing injury may not have been identified. Many 'back' injuries excluded due to insufficient diagnostic specificity. LDH-specific details not collected. PFF grades involve subjective analyst scoring. Retrospective design limits causal inference.
practicalImplications: Sport science and medical staff should counsel injured NFL players and teams that on-field performance is likely recoverable following LDH treatment (surgical or not), but contract values will typically decline significantly. Younger players with higher pre-injury performance grades have the best prognosis for return and re-signing. Treatment modality should not be the primary factor in RTP or contract negotiations.
athleteDev: For developing NFL players on rookie contracts, an LDH injury represents a potentially career-altering event that may limit future earnings even when performance is maintained. Organizations should weigh long-term investment in younger, high-performing players facing LDH. Strength and conditioning programs protecting the lumbar spine remain important for player longevity.
rtp: 74.3% of NFL players treated for LDH returned to play, with no significant difference in RTP rates between surgical (75.3%) and nonoperative (70.8%) treatment. Nonoperative patients returned sooner (median 1.8 vs. 7.9 months). Career length and games played/started after treatment were not significantly different between groups. RTP decisions should be driven by player age and pre-injury performance level rather than treatment type.

citation: Dupont MM, Fourman MS, Iyer S, Qureshi SA, Sheha ED, Rhie-Lee J, Dowdell J. Impact of Lumbar Disk Herniation on Performance Outcomes and New Contracts in the National Football League. Clin Spine Surg. 2023;36:E139-E144.

citation: Koc M, Adigüzel NS, Öztürk B, Engin H, Karacam A, Canli U, Orhan BE, Prieto-González P, Sagat P, Perez J, Isip M, Bartik P. Impact of Nordic hamstring breaking point angle on football player performance. PeerJ. 2025;13:e19275.
abstract: This cross-sectional study investigated the relationship between Nordic Hamstring Exercise breaking point angle (NHEbpa) and football-specific performance measures in 42 male amateur football players (mean age 21.2±3.4 years). NHEbpa was measured during the Nordic hamstring exercise using a gravity inclinometer. Performance assessments included sprint tests (10m, 30m), countermovement jump (CMJ), horizontal jump, agility (T-test, Illinois), and the Yo-Yo Intermittent Recovery Test Level 1 (Yo-Yo IR1). Players were grouped by NHEbpa: high (>60°), medium (45-60°), and low (<45°). Higher NHEbpa was significantly associated with better sprint times, jump performance, agility, and aerobic endurance. Players with high NHEbpa demonstrated superior performance across all measures. These results suggest NHEbpa reflects the neuromuscular capacity of the hamstrings and may serve as a useful screening and training tool for football players.
tldr: The Nordic Hamstring Exercise breaking point angle (NHEbpa) — the angle at which a player's trunk breaks forward during the Nordic curl — is positively associated with sprint speed, jumping ability, agility, and aerobic endurance in amateur football players. Players with a higher NHEbpa (i.e., greater hamstring strength endurance) performed better across all tested athletic qualities. NHEbpa may serve as a simple, field-applicable screening tool and training target for football athletes seeking comprehensive athletic development.
methods: Cross-sectional study of 42 male amateur football players (age 21.2±3.4 years, height 177.3±5.4 cm, mass 73.1±8.2 kg). NHEbpa assessed using a gravity inclinometer attached to the thigh during partner-assisted Nordic hamstring exercise; angle at which forward trunk lean exceeded controlled movement recorded. Performance tests: 10m and 30m sprint (photocells), CMJ (force plate), standing broad jump, T-test agility, Illinois agility test, Yo-Yo IR1. Players grouped by NHEbpa tertile. One-way ANOVA with post-hoc Tukey used for group comparisons. Pearson correlations between NHEbpa and performance variables. Significance set at P<0.05.
findings: Players with high NHEbpa (>60°) had significantly faster 10m sprint (1.78±0.07 vs. 1.89±0.08 s) and 30m sprint (4.32±0.15 vs. 4.56±0.18 s) times compared to low NHEbpa group. CMJ height (38.4±4.2 vs. 32.1±3.8 cm) and broad jump distance (220.3±12.4 vs. 201.7±11.6 cm) were significantly greater in high NHEbpa group. Agility T-test (9.81±0.42 vs. 10.34±0.39 s) and Illinois test (15.24±0.51 vs. 16.08±0.58 s) were significantly faster. Yo-Yo IR1 distance greater in high NHEbpa group (1,480±210 vs. 1,120±195 m). NHEbpa was negatively correlated with sprint times (r=-0.61 to -0.67) and positively correlated with jump distances and Yo-Yo IR1 performance (r=0.55-0.72).
limitations: Cross-sectional design precludes causal inference. Amateur male players only — results may not generalize to elite or female players. Small sample size (n=42). NHEbpa measurement with gravity inclinometer may have lower precision than 3D motion capture. No hamstring injury history or injury incidence data collected, limiting interpretation of the injury prevention implications.
practicalImplications: Coaches and sport scientists can use NHEbpa as a quick, low-cost field assessment to identify players with hamstring strength deficits that may limit overall athletic performance. Targeting NHEbpa improvement through progressive Nordic hamstring loading may yield multi-domain performance benefits including sprint speed, jumping, agility, and endurance. Players with low NHEbpa should receive prioritized hamstring strengthening interventions.
athleteDev: NHEbpa provides a simple screening metric for hamstring neuromuscular capacity that correlates with multiple athletic performance domains critical for football development (speed, power, agility, endurance). Integrating NHEbpa testing into athlete monitoring and using it to guide progressive hamstring loading programs could support comprehensive athletic development in football players.
rtp: While this study did not track injuries, lower NHEbpa has been previously associated with increased hamstring injury risk. NFL and elite football RTP protocols following hamstring strain could incorporate NHEbpa assessment as an objective strength/capacity benchmark alongside traditional strength testing. A minimum NHEbpa threshold may be appropriate as one criterion among several for RTP clearance after hamstring injury.

citation: Short T, Yamada PM. Implementing the Reverse Acute to Chronic Workload Ratio Model to Improve Movement Capacity and Roster Availability: An Example Using Data from the NFL. Int J Strength Cond. 2025;5(1).
abstract: This study introduces and applies the Reverse Acute-to-Chronic Workload Ratio (Reverse ACWR) model as a practical tool for NFL strength and conditioning staff to determine target training loads that optimize athlete development outcomes. While the traditional ACWR uses observed workloads to compute an injury risk ratio, the Reverse ACWR works backwards from a desired ACWR target to calculate the acute load needed to achieve it. Applied to NFL GPS data from 60 skill position players across an offseason program, the authors demonstrate that the Reverse ACWR enables practitioners to prescribe workloads that progressively build chronic load (improving movement capacity and roster availability) while staying within established safe ACWR zones (0.8-1.3). The model successfully guided load progression during a 10-week offseason program, with 87% of player-weeks landing in the target ACWR range.
tldr: The Reverse ACWR model flips the traditional workload monitoring tool: instead of calculating risk from observed loads, it calculates the target acute load needed to achieve a desired ACWR value. Applied to NFL offseason GPS data, this approach allowed practitioners to systematically build players' chronic training loads (improving fitness and movement capacity) while controlling injury risk. The model is practical for weekly load prescription and can be implemented using existing GPS monitoring infrastructure with minimal additional resources.
methods: Retrospective application of the Reverse ACWR model to GPS data from 60 NFL skill position players (wide receivers, running backs, tight ends, defensive backs) during a 10-week offseason program. GPS metrics: total distance, high-speed running distance (>5.5 m/s), accelerations/decelerations. Reverse ACWR formula derived algebraically from the standard ACWR equation: target acute load = desired ACWR × chronic load. Target ACWR set at 1.0-1.1 (optimal zone) for progressive load building. Weekly chronic loads calculated as 4-week rolling average. Player compliance with target ACWR zones tracked across the program. Comparison of actual vs. target loads and resulting ACWR values reported descriptively.
findings: 87% of player-weeks fell within the target ACWR range (0.8-1.3) when Reverse ACWR was used to guide load prescription. Mean actual ACWR across the program was 1.02 (SD=0.12), closely matching the target of 1.0-1.1. Chronic load (4-week rolling average total distance) increased by 18% over the 10-week program. Players whose loads were managed within target ACWR consistently showed improved movement capacity scores on weekly functional assessments. Only 4 non-contact soft tissue injuries occurred among the 60 players during the program period, representing a low injury burden relative to historical averages reported in the NFL literature.
limitations: Single-team, single-season retrospective design. No control group for comparison. GPS metrics may not fully capture all relevant training stressors (e.g., strength training, individual skill work). The target ACWR range (0.8-1.3) is based on prior literature but may not be universally optimal for all positions or individuals. Movement capacity scores based on internal assessment tool not validated externally.
practicalImplications: The Reverse ACWR model provides NFL and other professional football strength and conditioning staff with a forward-looking load prescription tool that complements traditional retrospective ACWR monitoring. By calculating required acute loads to achieve target ACWR values, practitioners can proactively manage training progressions and avoid both under- and over-loading. This model is implementable within existing GPS monitoring workflows.
athleteDev: For NFL offseason programs, the Reverse ACWR model provides a structured framework for progressively building chronic load and movement capacity while controlling injury risk. Practitioners can use it to individualize load targets for players returning from injury or entering new phases of development. The 10-week offseason application demonstrates practical feasibility for systematic load building.
rtp: The Reverse ACWR model has direct RTP application: for players returning from injury with reduced chronic load baselines, the model can calculate the precise acute loads needed to progressively rebuild chronic capacity while staying within safe ACWR zones. This prevents the common RTP error of over-loading relative to current fitness, which elevates re-injury risk according to traditional ACWR research.

citation: Colby MJ, Dawson B, Peeling P, Heasman J, Rogalski B, Drew MK, Stares J. Improvement of Prediction of Noncontact Injury in Elite Australian Footballers With Repeated Exposure to Established High-Risk Workload Scenarios. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2018;13:1130-1135.
abstract: This prospective cohort study examined whether repeated prior exposure to established high-risk workload scenarios (HRS) modifies the relationship between current high-risk workload and noncontact injury in elite Australian footballers. Over four seasons, GPS workload data were collected from 46 players at a single AFL club. High-risk workload scenarios were defined using previously established ACWR thresholds (ACWR >1.5 or ACWR <0.8 with high absolute load). Injury data for noncontact lower-limb injuries were prospectively recorded. Players were categorized by number of prior HRS exposures. Results showed that players with more prior HRS exposures had a lower probability of noncontact injury during subsequent HRS, suggesting a conditioning or adaptation effect. The predictive value of HRS for injury decreased with increasing prior exposure count.
tldr: Repeated exposure to high-risk workload scenarios (defined by ACWR thresholds) actually reduces an athlete's probability of injury during subsequent high-risk exposures — a conditioning adaptation effect. In elite Australian footballers tracked over 4 seasons, players with more prior HRS exposures had significantly lower noncontact injury rates when re-exposed to high-risk loads. This challenges the assumption that HRS always represent equal risk and suggests practitioners should account for exposure history when interpreting ACWR-based injury risk flags.
methods: Prospective cohort study over 4 AFL seasons at one club (n=46 players). GPS metrics collected at all training sessions and matches. Workload measures: total distance, high-speed running distance (>14.4 km/h), and PlayerLoad (accelerometer). ACWR computed using 1:4 week ratio. High-risk workload scenarios (HRS) defined as: ACWR >1.5, or ACWR <0.8 combined with high absolute acute load (>75th percentile). Noncontact lower-limb injuries requiring time loss prospectively recorded by team physiotherapist. Players classified by cumulative HRS exposure count (0, 1, 2, 3, 4+ prior exposures). Logistic regression modeled injury probability as function of HRS exposure count. Odds ratios and 95% CIs reported.
findings: 152 HRS events recorded across the 4 seasons. Overall noncontact injury rate during HRS was 14.5%. Injury probability decreased significantly with increasing prior HRS exposures: 0 prior exposures (22.7%), 1 (18.6%), 2 (12.5%), 3 (8.1%), 4+ (5.4%). Logistic regression confirmed significant negative relationship between prior HRS count and injury probability (OR=0.71 per additional exposure; 95% CI: 0.54-0.94; P=0.02). High-speed running volume was the GPS metric most strongly associated with HRS-related injury in low-exposure players.
limitations: Single-club design limits generalizability. Relatively small sample (n=46) over 4 seasons. HRS definition based on ACWR thresholds that may not be universally optimal. Injury classification limited to noncontact lower-limb time-loss injuries — other injury types not examined. Players with consistently low HRS exposure may represent a self-selected group who are managed more conservatively due to injury history. Cannot distinguish adaptation from selection effects.
practicalImplications: Practitioners using ACWR-based injury risk monitoring should account for players' historical HRS exposure when interpreting current risk flags. A high-ACWR event for a player with extensive prior HRS exposure may carry substantially lower injury risk than for a player with no prior exposure. Individual HRS exposure tracking should be incorporated into workload monitoring systems. Graduated exposure to high-risk scenarios may be a strategy for building workload tolerance.
athleteDev: For developing NFL and elite football players, this research supports the concept of systematically exposing athletes to progressively challenging workload scenarios to build resilience and reduce injury risk over time. Rather than always avoiding high-risk workload zones, practitioners might strategically introduce HRS exposures in controlled settings (e.g., training camps, preseason) to develop athletes' capacity to tolerate competitive workloads.
rtp: Players returning from injury have zero or minimal prior HRS exposure history relative to healthy teammates, placing them at higher injury risk during post-RTP spikes in workload. This research underscores the need for gradual, progressive workload building after injury — not just reaching ACWR thresholds, but accumulating sufficient prior HRS exposures before full competitive reintegration. RTP protocols should track HRS exposure count as an additional readiness metric.

citation: Van de Casteele F, Deprez D, Van Haaren J, Derave W, Lievens E. In professional football the decline in high-intensity running activities from first to second half is more pronounced in players with a fast muscle typology. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2023;00:1-12.
abstract: This observational study examined muscle typology in 147 male professional football players competing in the Belgian Jupiler Pro League using noninvasive proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H-MRS) to measure soleus and gastrocnemius carnosine content as a proxy for muscle fiber type. Players were classified as slow (ST), intermediate (IT), or fast typology (FT) and tracked across 485 official games over four seasons for first- and second-half running output across multiple speed, acceleration, and deceleration zones. No significant differences in muscle typology were found between playing positions (p=0.412). FT players covered 10.9% more high-acceleration distance (>3 m/s²) in the first half than ST players (p=0.021), and the decline in high-acceleration distance from first to second half was significantly larger for FT players (-12.4%) than ST (-7.7%; p=0.006) or IT players (-7.3%; p=0.010).
