FT players also tended to show larger declines in high-intensity running (>15 km/h), medium acceleration, and high deceleration zones compared to ST players, and FT players jumped higher than ST players on the countermovement jump.
tldr: Professional football players exhibit wide variation in muscle typology regardless of playing position, and players with a fast muscle typology (FT) show a meaningfully larger decline in high-intensity running and acceleration activities from the first to second half of matches compared to slow-typology (ST) players. This suggests FT players fatigue more toward the end of games. Coaches should monitor FT players for second-half performance drops and have quality substitutes ready for those positions, particularly given that deteriorating high-intensity output in FT players may also signal elevated muscle injury risk.
methods: Observational cohort study. Muscle typology estimated via 1H-MRS carnosine measurement in soleus and gastrocnemius of 147 Belgian first-division male players; players classified as ST (z-score ≤-0.5), IT (-0.5 to 0.5), or FT (≥0.5). Spatiotemporal tracking data collected via SportVU semiautomatic video system across 485 official games (2017-2021); only outfield players with ≥75 min playtime and ≥5 qualifying games were included (n=66). Distances per minute were compared between first and second half across 11 intensity zones including strolling, walking, jogging, running, high-speed, sprinting, medium/high acceleration, and medium/high deceleration. Off-field testing included 10 m sprint, VO2peak, and countermovement jump. One-way ANOVA and Kruskal-Wallis tests used for between-group comparisons.
findings: No differences in muscle typology distribution were found between positions (p=0.412). FT players covered 10.9% more high-acceleration distance (>3 m/s²) in the first half than ST players (p=0.021). The decline in high-acceleration distance from first to second half was -12.4% (FT) vs -7.7% (ST; p=0.006) and -7.3% (IT; p=0.010). FT players tended to show larger second-half declines in high-intensity running (-11.2% vs -7.1%), high deceleration (-12.7% vs -8.1%), and medium acceleration (-11.5% vs -8.1%) compared to ST players (0.05<p<0.1). FT players jumped significantly higher than ST on CMJ without arm swing (45.2 vs 40.6 cm; p<0.001) and with arm swing (52.1 vs 48.2 cm; p=0.024). No differences were found in VO2peak or 10 m sprint performance between typology groups.
limitations: Playing position and physical fitness level were not controlled for in the match running output analysis. Contextual game factors (scoreline, opponent quality, home/away) were not accounted for, though averaging across ≥5 games per player was intended to minimize their influence. Off-field testing procedures were not standardized across clubs. The relatively small sample of players with sufficient match data (n=66) limited statistical power for some subgroup analyses.
practicalImplications: Coaches should consider players' muscle typology when making tactical and substitution decisions, as FT players appear to fatigue more in the second half and may require earlier replacement. Real-time GPS or tracking data monitoring of second-half high-intensity output in FT players can help identify fatigue onset. FT players should not be steered toward specific positions based on typology, as all typologies are present at all positions in professional football. FT players showing second-half performance drops may also be at elevated hamstring strain injury risk and warrant heightened monitoring.
athleteDev: Muscle typology assessment via 1H-MRS could be integrated into player profiling to understand individual fatigability and recovery needs. FT players who show explosive first-half output but larger second-half declines may benefit from training periodization that builds fatigue resistance or from substitution strategies that protect them from end-of-game injury exposure. CMJ performance correlates moderately with muscle typology and may serve as a practical proxy indicator for a faster fiber profile in talent identification and monitoring contexts.
rtp: Monitoring high-intensity running metrics in the second half of matches can help identify when FT players are experiencing fatigue, which has been linked to elevated hamstring strain injury risk. FT players whose high-intensity output deteriorates significantly during return-to-play progressions or training camp sessions may need extended recovery periods before full clearance. The finding that FT players require longer recovery from high-intensity exercise (per prior laboratory research) supports more conservative return-to-play timelines and workload management for fast-typology athletes.

citation: Nassis GP, Girard O, Chiampas GT, Krustrup P, Racinais S. In-match strategies to mitigate the effect of heat on football (soccer) players' health and performance. Br J Sports Med. 2024;0:1-2.
abstract: This editorial addresses the physiological challenges of playing football in hot conditions and provides evidence-based in-match cooling strategies relevant to the 2026 FIFA World Cup and beyond. Historical weather data indicate Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) values of 30-35°C are projected during afternoon match times in six 2026 World Cup host cities including Atlanta, Dallas, Houston, Kansas City, Miami, and Monterrey. Playing football in the heat elevates core temperature to 39.5-40.2°C rectal and up to 42°C in muscles, and cognitive function in complex tasks begins to decline once core temperature exceeds 38.7°C. The authors synthesize evidence on cooling strategies that can be applied during warm-up modification, half-time, and the 3-minute in-game cooling breaks to protect player health and maintain performance. Eight specific in-match strategies are recommended, ranging from adjusted warm-up structure to cold water immersion at half-time.
tldr: Playing football in hot and humid environments significantly elevates core and muscle temperatures, impairing high-speed running capacity and potentially compromising cognitive decision-making. With the 2026 FIFA World Cup scheduled for summer months across several hot US cities and Mexico, medical and performance staff need practical in-match cooling tools. Cold water immersion during half-time is the most effective method (reducing core temperature 0.25-0.35°C per minute), while ice towels, head and forearm cooling, and chilled fluid intake can help during shorter 3-minute cooling breaks. Coaches should also adapt game strategy and warm-up structure to reduce metabolic heat production in extreme conditions.
methods: Expert editorial synthesizing evidence from studies that closely replicated football exercise protocols including cooling breaks and half-time conditions. Authors reviewed historical WBGT data from 2012-2021 for 2026 World Cup host cities and compiled evidence on thermoregulatory physiology, cognitive effects of heat, and cooling intervention efficacy. Sources included randomized controlled studies on cold water immersion, ice towel application, head/hand/forearm cooling, fan and skin-wetting techniques, and chilled fluid consumption. Evidence quality was evaluated in the context of realistic football match scenarios.
findings: Rectal temperatures of 39.5-40.2°C and muscle temperatures up to 42°C are reported during football matches in the heat. Cognitive function declines in complex tasks above 38.7°C core temperature. High-speed running is compromised in heat but peak speed and passing accuracy may remain constant or improve. Cold water immersion (CWI) at half-time reduces core temperature by 0.25-0.35°C per minute and is the most effective cooling method. Ice towels covering large body surface are the second most effective (~0.1°C per minute). Combinations of pre- and mid-cooling techniques (ice towels, head/hand cooling) have been shown to improve second-half sprinting performance. Chilled water consumption alone or with neck ice towel application during 3-minute breaks helps manage hydration and thermal sensation when full core cooling is impractical.
limitations: Most available evidence is not directly applicable to football due to differences in exercise protocols, and only a limited number of studies have replicated actual football match conditions including structured cooling breaks and half-time intervals. Recommendations are primarily based on studies involving men; evidence for women and youth players is limited. Sex-based thermoregulatory differences exist, with women potentially at a disadvantage in dry heat due to lower sweat production. The challenge of additional playing time and multiple extra-time matches in tournaments played in hot environments has not been adequately studied.
practicalImplications: Medical and performance staff preparing for matches in hot conditions should prioritize half-time cold water immersion as the primary cooling intervention, with ice towels as the practical fallback. During 3-minute cooling breaks, focus should be on hydration, chilled fluid intake, and skin temperature reduction via ice towels around the neck and head or forearm cooling. Warm-up duration and intensity should be modified to limit pre-match core temperature increases. Coaches should consider adapting game tactics to reduce high-intensity activity volume in extreme heat. Scheduling decisions should involve medical personnel, and organizers should consider adding cooling breaks before extra time.
athleteDev: Athletes training and competing in hot environments should undergo formal heat acclimatization programs of 10-14 sessions maintaining core temperature above 38.5°C for 60 minutes per session to promote adequate physiological adaptations. Maintaining euhydration during matches in the heat can reduce internal temperature and improve physiological function relative to playing in a dehydrated state. Youth players competing in heat require additional protection, with cooling break thresholds recommended at WBGT ≥32°C rather than higher adult thresholds.
rtp: Players returning from injury or illness who have missed heat acclimatization training may have reduced heat tolerance and should be monitored carefully during initial exposures to hot match conditions. Core temperature monitoring and hydration status assessment during return-to-play progressions in hot weather can help ensure athletes are physiologically ready to sustain the demands of a full match. Cognitive function impairment at high core temperatures may delay complex skill execution during return-to-sport testing and should be accounted for in evaluation protocols.

citation: Kuitunen I, Immonen V, Pakarinen O, Mattila VM, Ponkilainen VT. Incidence of football injuries sustained on artificial turf compared to grass and other playing surfaces: a systematic review and meta-analysis. eClinicalMedicine. 2023;59:101956.
abstract: This systematic review and meta-analysis is the first to provide statistical pooled synthesis comparing football (soccer) injury incidence on artificial turf versus grass and other playing surfaces. A total of 1,447 studies were screened from PubMed, Scopus, SPORTDiscus, and Web of Science databases; 22 observational cohort studies conducted between 2001 and 2014 were ultimately included, covering a wide range of player levels, sexes, and age groups. A random effects model using mixed-effects Poisson regression was applied to calculate pooled incidence rate ratios (IRR). Overall injury incidence was 14% lower on artificial turf than on grass (IRR 0.86, CI 0.78-0.95), with similar findings in both men (IRR 0.82) and women (IRR 0.83). Professional players showed a lower injury incidence on artificial turf (IRR 0.79), while amateur players showed no significant difference. Non-contact injuries and muscle strains were less frequent on artificial turf, and pelvis/thigh and knee injury incidences were lower on artificial turf.
tldr: Despite widespread perception that artificial turf increases injury risk, this large systematic review and meta-analysis of 22 football-specific cohort studies found that overall injury incidence is approximately 14% lower on artificial turf than on natural grass. The benefit was consistent across both male and female players and was most pronounced in professional athletes. Muscle strains and non-contact injuries were specifically less frequent on artificial turf. These findings suggest injury risk should not be used as an argument against artificial turf when planning playing facilities, though high heterogeneity and limited control for confounders in primary studies mean evidence quality is rated low to very low.
methods: Systematic review and meta-analysis (PROSPERO: CRD42022371414) following PRISMA 2020 guidelines. Databases searched: PubMed, Scopus, SPORTDiscus, Web of Science (October 2022). Inclusion criteria: football-specific observational cohort studies reporting injuries per exposure time on artificial turf versus grass or other surfaces. Risk of bias assessed by Newcastle-Ottawa Scale. Pooled incidence rate ratios (IRR) with 95% CI calculated via mixed-effects Poisson regression with random study effects. Subgroup analyses by sex, level of play (professional/amateur), age group, injury mechanism, injury type, anatomical location, and geographical region. Sensitivity analyses for third-generation turf only and prospective studies only. Evidence quality rated by GRADE framework.
findings: Overall injury incidence was lower on artificial turf vs grass (20 studies; IRR 0.86, CI 0.78-0.95; I²=84%). Artificial turf showed higher injury incidence vs other playing surfaces (IRR 1.73, CI 1.25-2.41). Both men (IRR 0.82, CI 0.72-0.94) and women (IRR 0.83, CI 0.76-0.91) had lower injury incidence on artificial turf. Professional players had lower incidence on artificial turf (IRR 0.79, CI 0.70-0.90); no difference was found for amateur players (IRR 0.91, CI 0.77-1.09). Non-contact injuries (IRR 0.86) and muscle strains (IRR 0.79) were less frequent on artificial turf. Pelvis/thigh injuries were 28% less frequent (IRR 0.72) and knee injuries 23% less frequent (IRR 0.77) on artificial turf. No subgroup analysis showed increased injury incidence on artificial turf.
limitations: Only 5 of 22 included studies controlled for potential confounders in their analyses. Injury definitions varied substantially across studies (interruption of play vs. medical attention required vs. missed training/game time). High statistical heterogeneity (I²=84%) limits precision of pooled estimates. Studies were conducted between 2001 and 2014; one used second-generation turf and three did not specify generation. Few studies examined amateur, youth, and female populations, limiting generalizability. Subgroup analyses had wide confidence intervals due to small numbers of included studies.
practicalImplications: Injury risk should not be cited as a rationale for preventing the use of artificial turf in football facility planning or policy decisions. Third-generation artificial turf appears to be at least as safe as natural grass for professional football, and may be preferable in climates where maintaining grass quality is difficult. Medical staff and athletic trainers should update player and coach education to address cognitive bias against artificial turf, as player fear of injury on artificial turf appears to exceed actual risk. Muscle strain prevention programs remain important regardless of playing surface.
athleteDev: Facility planners for youth and community football programs should feel confident that third-generation artificial turf is a safe and practical surface alternative to grass, particularly in regions where natural grass quality is inconsistent. The lower incidence of muscle strains and non-contact injuries on artificial turf may reflect the more consistent and homogenous footing it provides. Coaches working with players transitioning between surfaces should emphasize surface-specific proprioceptive and movement preparation as a precautionary measure.
rtp: Players returning from lower extremity injuries, particularly thigh/hamstring strains and knee injuries, do not face elevated reinjury risk when returning on artificial turf compared to grass based on current evidence. Rehabilitation staff can plan return-to-play progressions on artificial turf with confidence that the surface does not independently increase risk. Monitoring of slide tackle frequency and pass selection patterns may be warranted, as some evidence suggests players slightly modify technique on artificial turf, which could affect RTP movement assessments.

citation: Mack CD, Kent RW, Coughlin MJ, Shiue KY, Weiss LJ, Jastifer JR, Wojtys EM, Anderson RB, on behalf of the NFL Musculoskeletal Committee. Incidence of lower extremity injury in the National Football League 2015 to 2018. Am J Sports Med. 2020;1-8.
abstract: This retrospective observational study using the NFL's centralized electronic health record (EHR) system analyzed all time-loss lower extremity injuries across the 2015-2018 seasons from all 32 NFL teams. An average of 2,006 time-loss lower extremity injuries were reported each season, representing a 41% single-season risk for any NFL player. Lower extremity injuries accounted for approximately 60% of all NFL injuries, with the knee (29.3%), ankle (22.4%), thigh (17.2%), and foot (9.1%) being most commonly affected. Hamstring strains were the most common individual injury, followed by lateral ankle sprains, adductor strains, high ankle sprains, and MCL tears. The total time-loss burden reached approximately 56,700 player-days lost per NFL season, with ACL tears generating the greatest burden (~12,400 player-days/season). Injury incidence did not meaningfully decrease over the four seasons studied.
tldr: Lower extremity injuries in the NFL are extremely common—approximately 41% of players sustain a time-loss lower extremity injury in any single season—and impose a staggering burden of roughly 56,700 player-days missed per year. The knee, ankle, and thigh are the most vulnerable regions. Hamstring strains are the most frequent injury by count while ACL tears create the most total time lost. Notably, injury rates were higher during the preseason than regular season (11.5 vs 9.4 per 10,000 player-plays), and running backs faced the highest game-time injury rate by position. These findings underscore the continued need for position-specific prevention strategies, better conditioning periodization, and evidence-based return-to-play protocols.
methods: Retrospective observational descriptive epidemiology study using the NFL's mandatory centralized EHR system, linked to the NFL Game Statistics and Information System (GSIS) for per-play exposure data. All 32 teams contributed data for the 2015-2018 seasons. Only time-loss injuries (resulting in removal from and inability to return to participation) were included; non-time-loss injuries and offseason injuries were excluded. Outcomes included 1-season risk, injury rate per 10,000 player-plays (games only), and days lost per injury (2015-2017 injuries tracked through 2018). Analyses stratified by season phase, team activity, body region, roster position, and specific injury type. Confidence intervals calculated using exact binomial distribution for risk and Poisson distribution for rates.
findings: Average of 2,006 time-loss lower extremity injuries per season; 1-season risk 41.0% (95% CI 40.1-41.8%). Most injuries occurred during games (58.7%) vs practice (37.3%). Regular season accounted for 56.6% of injuries; preseason 40.6%. Knee injuries most common (29.3%), followed by ankle (22.4%), thigh (17.2%), foot (9.1%). Hamstring strains most frequent specific injury (n=1,352; 9.1% seasonal risk). ACL tears imposed the greatest time-loss burden (~12,400 player-days/season; median 290 days missed). Preseason game injury rate was higher than regular season (11.5 vs 9.4 per 10,000 player-plays). Running backs had the highest game injury rate (16.0/10,000); defensive secondary and wide receivers had the highest seasonal risk (53.7% and 49.7%, respectively). Quarterbacks and punters/kickers had the lowest seasonal risk (18.2% and 16.2%).
limitations: Player exposure during practices could not be quantified at the per-play level, limiting rate calculations to game activities. Injury rates by position are based on roster position rather than actual position at time of injury, which may misclassify injuries that occurred during special teams play. Approximately 11% of lower extremity injuries had missing return dates, particularly for severe injuries like ACL tears (up to 40% missing), though imputation was used for burden metrics. Days-lost metrics reflect missed NFL participation time rather than full clinical recovery burden.
practicalImplications: The persistently high and non-declining injury burden reinforces the need for evidence-based prevention, training periodization, and return-to-play protocols. Preseason training camp carries disproportionately high injury risk, suggesting workload management and acclimation protocols are critical during this period. Position-specific programs are warranted: running backs, wide receivers, and defensive secondary players face the greatest per-play and seasonal risk and should receive targeted strength, conditioning, and movement screening. Playing surface is a modifiable risk factor—a 27% higher noncontact lower extremity injury rate on synthetic turf vs natural grass in the NFL has been previously documented.
athleteDev: High-school and collegiate programs should be aware that lower extremity injury rates are elevated during transition periods (e.g., preseason), and that workload management and adequate conditioning before return from the offseason are essential. Hamstring strain prevention should be a foundational element of any lower extremity injury prevention program given it is the most common time-loss injury. Running backs face significantly higher per-play injury rates than other positions and may benefit from specialized lower extremity loading and movement screening programs.
rtp: ACL tears and Achilles ruptures represent the highest individual time-loss burden (median >240 days) and require formal long-term return-to-play protocols. High ankle sprains carry a significantly longer recovery (median 21 days) than lateral ankle sprains (median 5 days) and should not be managed identically. Hamstring strains, while individually shorter in duration (median 11 days), are so common that they account for the second-greatest cumulative time-loss burden in the NFL (~5,800 player-days/season), reinforcing the need for robust hamstring-specific rehabilitation and clearance criteria before return to full activity.

citation: Bodendorfer BM, DeFroda SF, Shu HT, Knapik DM, Yang DS, Verma NN. Incidence of pectoralis major injuries has increased 40% over the last 22 National Football League seasons. Arthroscopy Sports Medicine and Rehabilitation. 2021;e1-e6.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined trends in pectoralis major (PM) injury incidence across 22 NFL seasons (1998-1999 through 2019-2020) using publicly available injury data from league reports and press releases. A total of 258 PM injuries were identified in 245 athletes. Overall incidence was 0.603 per 10,000 athlete-exposures and increased significantly over time by 0.039 per 10,000 athlete-exposures per year (R²=0.787, p<0.001), representing approximately a 40% increase from Period 1 (0.395/10,000) to Period 2 (0.893/10,000) following NFL tackling rule changes in 2009. Defensive athletes accounted for 64.7% of injuries, with linebackers most commonly affected. Complete tears were the most frequent injury type (51.2%). Surgical repair was performed in 48.8% of cases, with defensive linemen, defensive backs, linebackers, more recent year of injury, and shorter NFL experience identified as significant risk factors for operative management.
tldr: Pectoralis major (PM) injuries in the NFL have increased substantially over the past two decades, rising from approximately 0.40 to 0.89 injuries per 10,000 athlete-exposures between the first and second 11-year periods of the study—a trend that held regardless of tackling rule changes and appears to be a linear increase driven by the evolving size, speed, and contact demands of professional football. Defensive players, particularly linebackers and defensive linemen, are at greatest risk. Nearly half of PM injuries require surgical repair, with complete tears universally treated operatively. Clinical and performance staff should be aware of the high prevalence of in-game PM tears in defensive athletes and prepare appropriate surgical decision-making frameworks and return-to-sport protocols.
methods: Retrospective cohort study (Level of Evidence 3). Publicly available data from Pro-Football-Reference.com and ESPN NFL injury databases were queried to identify PM injuries reported as 'pectoral,' 'chest,' or 'shoulder' in weekly NFL injury reports from the 1998-1999 through 2019-2020 seasons. Press releases were used to verify inclusion. Two independent authors collected data, with disagreements resolved by consensus. Athlete characteristics (age, BMI, position, experience) and injury characteristics (setting, activity, grade, management) were recorded. Injury incidence calculated per 10,000 athlete-exposures assuming 53 players/team, 105 practices/season, 16 games/season, 32 teams. Linear regression assessed incidence trends. Multivariate logistic regression identified risk factors for operative management controlling for age, BMI, experience, position, year, and injury setting.
findings: 258 PM injuries in 245 athletes over 22 seasons; mean age 27.1 years, mean BMI 32.6. Incidence increased linearly by 0.039/10,000 athlete-exposures per year (R²=0.787, p<0.001). Incidence rose from 0.395 (Period 1: 1998-2008) to 0.893 (Period 2: 2009-2019) per 10,000 athlete-exposures (p<0.001). Defensive athletes accounted for 64.7% of injuries; linebackers (43.7% of defensive injuries) most commonly affected. Complete tears (Grade III) comprised 51.2% of injuries. Most injuries occurred in-game (76%), primarily during regular season (69.3%). Highest incidence occurred at the NFL Combine (122/10,000 athlete-exposures). Operative repair in 48.8%; 100% of Grade III tears were treated operatively. Risk factors for surgery: defensive lineman (OR 3.78), defensive back (OR 12.20), linebacker (OR 8.98), more recent injury year (OR 1.11/year), shorter NFL experience (OR 0.77 for older athletes).
limitations: Use of public databases may undercount injuries classified as 'undisclosed' or 'chest' injuries that cannot be confirmed as PM tears. The study cannot identify causal factors for the observed linear increase in PM injury incidence. Return-to-play and performance outcomes were not examined. Public injury reports may miss less severe PM injuries not publicly disclosed by teams, potentially underestimating true incidence compared to the NFL Injury Surveillance System (ISS) gold standard.
practicalImplications: The rising incidence of PM injuries in the NFL warrants increased attention to PM health screening, particularly among defensive players. Athletes with shorter NFL experience and those in defensive positions (linemen, linebackers, defensive backs) should receive specific education on PM injury risk, recognition, and management. Given that 100% of complete tears and 48.8% of all PM injuries undergo operative repair, medical and athletic training staff should have clear surgical referral pathways and post-operative return-to-sport protocols established. Acute repair yields better outcomes than chronic repair, emphasizing the importance of timely diagnosis.
athleteDev: The NFL Combine bench press requirement (225 lbs for maximum repetitions) is associated with a very high incidence of PM injury (122/10,000 athlete-exposures at the Combine). Strength and conditioning programs should incorporate PM-specific eccentric loading, injury screening, and appropriate periodization of heavy pressing movements, particularly for defensive linemen, linebackers, and defensive backs who are at highest risk. Young defensive players entering professional football should receive education about proper bench press technique and PM tear recognition given their elevated risk at the start of their careers.
rtp: All Grade III (complete) PM tears were managed operatively in this study, and surgical repair is associated with superior functional outcomes and strength recovery compared to nonoperative treatment. Acute repair is preferred over delayed repair for better functional outcomes and cosmesis satisfaction. Return-to-sport timelines for PM repairs in NFL athletes should account for the high physical demands of blocking and tackling, which place the arm in the vulnerable abducted, externally rotated position. Position-specific performance testing of PM strength and shoulder range of motion should be components of clearance criteria before return to full contact practice.

citation: Patel NK, Hanna A, Johns WL, Dhanota AS, Dodson CC, Pepe M. Incidence, Management, and Outcomes following Elbow Ulnar Collateral Ligament Injuries in Non-Quarterback Professional Football Players. JSES International. 2025;doi:10.1016/j.jseint.2025.04.010.
abstract: This retrospective descriptive epidemiology study examined ulnar collateral ligament (UCL) injuries in non-quarterback NFL players using the NFL Injury Surveillance System (NFLISS) from 2015 to 2022. A total of 268 isolated UCL injuries were identified across the 7-year period, averaging 33.5±4.2 per year. Injury severity was graded I-III based on clinical examination and MRI findings, with management type and time to return to play (RTP) recorded. Offensive and defensive linemen accounted for 56% of all injuries, and 98.5% of players were treated non-operatively. Significant differences in time missed were found between Grade I (1.1 days) and Grade II (12.4 days) injuries (p<0.05) and between Grade I and Grade III (26.3 days) injuries (p<0.05).
tldr: UCL injuries in non-quarterback NFL players are predominantly mild Grade I sprains affecting linemen, and the vast majority resolve with non-operative treatment without any time lost. However, Grade II and III injuries result in significantly longer absences — up to 26 days on average — so grading severity early helps staff set realistic return-to-play timelines and counsel players appropriately.
methods: Retrospective review of the NFLISS database from 2015–2022 for non-quarterback players with isolated UCL injuries. Injuries were graded I–III based on laxity on clinical examination or MRI. Player demographics, position, injury mechanism (contact vs. non-contact), timing (preseason/regular season/postseason), setting (practice vs. game), treatment type (operative vs. non-operative), and days missed were extracted. T-tests and Mann-Whitney U tests compared time missed by injury grade; significance set at p<0.05.
findings: 268 total isolated UCL injuries identified (33.5±4.2/year). Offensive and defensive linemen accounted for 56% (150/268) of injuries; offensive linemen alone were 33.5%. Grade distribution: 73% Grade I, 16.8% Grade II, 10.1% Grade III. 72% of players missed no time; of those who did, mean time missed was 5.4±24.2 days. Grade I vs. Grade II time missed: 1.1 vs. 12.4 days (p<0.05); Grade I vs. Grade III: 1.1 vs. 26.3 days (p<0.05). Only 4 players (1.5%) required surgery (3 linebackers, 1 special teams); surgical RTP averaged 4.7 months. All injuries occurred via contact mechanism.
limitations: Retrospective nature and reliance on NFLISS reporting by individual team medical staffs may underrepresent true incidence. Time missed may be confounded by injury timing within the season. MRI details (tear type, location, extent) were unavailable. Non-operative treatment specifics (bracing, PRP, duration) were not recorded. Patient-reported outcome data and return-to-performance measures are unavailable through NFLISS. Surgical indications, techniques, and long-term follow-up are unknown.
practicalImplications: Applied sport science staff should expect that most UCL elbow injuries in non-QB players will not result in missed time and can be managed non-operatively. Injury severity grading at the time of diagnosis is critical for setting RTP expectations: Grade I injuries typically allow same-week return, while Grade II injuries average nearly 2 weeks and Grade III injuries average nearly 4 weeks. Linemen and linebackers are highest-risk positions and should be monitored closely during contact-heavy blocks and drills.
athleteDev: Given that linemen sustain over half of all UCL injuries in the NFL through valgus contact forces at the elbow during blocking, position-specific elbow stability and load tolerance training should be integrated into athlete development programs. Strengthening the medial elbow stabilizers and teaching blocking mechanics that minimize valgus stress may reduce incidence, particularly for offensive linemen who represent the single most frequently injured position group.
rtp: UCL injury grade is the primary driver of RTP timeline in non-QB NFL players. Grade I sprains: near-zero time loss expected, return same week is routine. Grade II sprains: expect approximately 12 days absence, non-operative management. Grade III sprains: expect approximately 26 days absence, still typically non-operative. Surgical cases: RTP averaged 4.7 months, potentially reflecting repair vs. reconstruction differences. RTP is defined as full unrestricted practice or game participation.

citation: Cairns MA, Hasty EK, Herzog MM, Ostrum RF, Kerr ZY. Incidence, Severity, and Time Loss Associated With Collegiate Football Fractures, 2004-2005 to 2013-2014. Am J Sports Med. 2018;doi:10.1177/0363546517749914.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study analyzed fracture data from NCAA football programs reported to the NCAA Injury Surveillance Program (NCAA-ISP) during the 2004-2005 through 2013-2014 academic years. A total of 986 fractures were identified, representing 4.4% of all injuries during this period and an overall rate of 0.32 per 1000 athlete-exposures. Competition fractures occurred at 10.56 times the rate of practice fractures. The most common fracture sites were the hand/fingers (34.6%) and fibula (17.2%), while 62.5% of all fractures resulted in more than 21 days of time loss. Overall, 34.4% of fractures required surgical intervention, and surgically managed fractures were significantly more likely to result in extended time loss.
tldr: Fractures are relatively uncommon in collegiate football (4.4% of all injuries) but carry significant consequences — nearly two-thirds result in more than 3 weeks out of participation, and one-third require surgery. Hand and fibula fractures are most common, and surgically treated fractures are far more likely to cause extended time loss. Staff should anticipate prolonged absences and surgical referrals for lower extremity fractures in particular.
methods: Retrospective analysis of NCAA-ISP data from academic years 2004-2005 through 2013-2014, limited to injuries with a reported diagnosis of fracture. Data were collected electronically from an average of 43 schools per year by certified athletic trainers. Fracture rates per 1000 athlete-exposures (AEs), injury rate ratios (IRRs), injury proportion ratios (IPRs), and 95% CIs were calculated. Time loss was categorized as 1-6 days, 7-21 days, or >21 days. Statistical significance defined as 95% CI excluding 1.0.
findings: 986 total fractures reported; 50.7% in competition, 49.3% in practice. Competition fracture rate was 10.56x practice rate (1.80 vs 0.17 per 1000 AEs). Most common fractures: hand/fingers 34.6%, fibula 17.2%, foot 12.0%, shoulder 7.5%, arm/elbow 6.9%. 62.5% of all fractures resulted in >21 days time loss. 34.4% required surgery; surgically managed fractures were more likely to result in >21 days lost (85.0% vs 50.7%; IPR=1.68). 6.3% were recurrent; recurrent hand/finger fractures were more likely to require surgery (66.7% vs 27.2%; IPR=2.45). Lower leg fractures: 94.4% resulted in >21 days time loss, 53.8% required surgery.
limitations: Reliance on athletic trainer reporting introduces potential inaccuracy. No player position data available, which could influence surgery incidence and time loss. Time loss is not further stratified beyond 21 days. Timing of injury within the season may affect reported time loss. Surgical indications and specific procedures are not captured. Potential underreporting of surgeries performed after completion of collegiate career.
practicalImplications: Collegiate football medical and sport science staff should treat any lower extremity fracture as a presumptive multi-week absence requiring surgical evaluation. Hand and finger fractures, the most common type, have highly variable outcomes. Prevention initiatives targeting fracture reduction — particularly in competition settings where the rate is 10x that of practice — are warranted. Fifth metatarsal fractures have a notably high recurrence rate and surgical need, requiring cautious RTP protocols.
athleteDev: The 10-fold higher fracture rate in competition versus practice underscores the importance of progressive physical preparation and sport-specific loading during preseason. Hand and finger fractures at 34.6% of all fractures suggest that linemen and skill players exposed to high contact forces at the extremities should have protective equipment regularly evaluated. Lower leg fracture prevention programs and bone stress monitoring in high-load athletes may reduce season-ending injuries.
rtp: Fracture RTP in collegiate football is highly site-dependent. Hand/finger fractures may allow return within 1-6 days in some cases (~35%), but thumb and metacarpal fractures more often require 3+ weeks. Shoulder and arm/elbow fractures almost universally result in >21 days loss. Lower leg, ankle, and foot fractures causing >21 days loss in 85-95% of cases should receive extended RTP protocols. Recurrent fifth metatarsal fractures carry heightened surgical risk and demand careful graduated RTP.

citation: Posmanick B, Pinheiro R, Ameis D, Fay S. Income Taxes and Firm Competitiveness: A Case Study from the National Football League. International Journal of Financial Studies. 2025;13(4):212.
abstract: This economics study uses NFL teams from 1984 to 2000 to estimate the causal effect of state income taxes on team performance before and after implementation of the NFL salary cap in 1994. A difference-in-differences model was employed with college football teams as the control group, as college players during this era received no compensation and thus were not affected by tax rates. The Simple Rating System (SRS) served as the primary measure of team quality. Results show that higher state income taxes significantly reduced team quality as measured by SRS, with the effect substantially amplified after the salary cap was introduced. Decision tree analysis confirmed that teams in states with tax rates above approximately 7% shifted from being more likely to be above average (pre-cap) to more likely to be below average (post-cap).
tldr: NFL teams in high-income-tax states face a measurable competitive disadvantage — particularly after the 1994 salary cap — because player wages must be grossed up to offset tax burdens, effectively reducing the real value of the salary cap. For football organizations in high-tax states, this supports a roster-building strategy favoring the draft and trades over free agency, and highlights the structural economic challenge that state tax policy creates for competitive balance.
methods: Difference-in-differences (DID) regression using NFL (treatment) and college football FBS Division 1 (control) teams from 1984–2000. Salary cap implementation in 1994 served as the treatment. Dependent variables: team winning percentage and SRS. Key independent variables: top state income tax bracket rate, salary cap indicator (NFL teams post-1994), and their interaction term. Control variables: median household income, state population, strength of schedule, and team fixed effects. Three control groups tested: all FBS college teams, power conference teams only, and hand-matched state-level pairs. Decision tree analysis using Gini impurity minimization classified teams as above/below average SRS before and after the cap.
findings: Full sample DID: tax rate negatively associated with SRS (coefficient -31.45, p<0.1); Tax × Salary Cap interaction: -35.03 (p<0.1). Power conference sample: tax effect -54.95 (p<0.01), interaction -36.45 (p<0.1). Hand-matched sample: tax effect -51.27 (p<0.01), interaction -36.46 (p<0.05). Pre-cap decision tree: teams with tax rate ≥7.2% were above average 60% of the time. Post-cap decision tree: teams with tax rate ≥7.0% were above average only 36% of the time; teams with tax rate <7.0% were above average 58% of the time.
limitations: Study period limited to 1984–2000; may not reflect modern salary cap era. Parallel trends assumption cannot be directly tested post-treatment. Only state-level top marginal tax rates used; local city taxes and effective rates not incorporated. Individual player-level compensation data not used. The 'good-team discount' is acknowledged but not modeled.
practicalImplications: NFL front offices in high-tax states should factor state income tax rates into free agency negotiations, as teams may need to pay approximately 9.5% more in gross wages to attract equivalent talent. Roster-building strategy for high-tax teams should skew toward the draft and internal development rather than free agency bidding. Sport science staff in high-tax markets should be aware that effective player acquisition budgets may be constrained relative to low-tax competitors.
athleteDev: While not directly focused on athlete physical development, this study has relevance for team resource allocation: in high-tax states, salary cap efficiency demands greater return on investment from drafted and developed players. Player development programs in such organizations may face implicit pressure to maximize contributions from lower-cost, internally developed athletes, potentially increasing the relative value of robust sport science and athletic development infrastructure.
rtp: This paper does not address return-to-play protocols or injury management. It is an economics study focused on team competitiveness and fiscal policy. No RTP-relevant data or findings are present.

citation: Bi AS, Azam MT, Butler JJ, Alaia MJ, Jazrawi LM, Gonzalez-Lomas G, Kennedy JG. Increased incidence of acute Achilles tendon ruptures in the peri-pandemic COVID era with parallels to the 2021-22 NFL season. Knee Surgery, Sports Traumatology, Arthroscopy. 2023;doi:10.1007/s00167-023-07309-y.
abstract: This retrospective study investigated rates of acute Achilles tendon ruptures (AATRs) at a single urban academic institution in New York from 2017 to 2021, with a parallel analysis of NFL injured reserve data from the 2019-20 through 2021-22 regular seasons. A total of 1,431 AATRs were identified during the 5-year period, with 588 undergoing primary surgical repair. AATR incidence declined 7.5% from 2019 to 2020 during COVID quarantine, then rebounded 8.9% from 2020 to 2021 — exceeding pre-pandemic rates. Within the NFL, AATRs resulting in injured reserve designations increased annually from 11 (2019-20) to 17 (2020-21) to 24 (2021-22), mirroring the pattern seen in recreational athletes.
tldr: Extended periods of physical inactivity — whether from COVID quarantine or a canceled preseason — appear to prime the Achilles tendon for rupture upon return to activity. NFL data from 2019-2022 shows a steady and concerning rise in Achilles tendon injuries on injured reserve, with the 2021-22 season showing the highest count in recent history. Sport science staff should implement structured, progressive Achilles loading programs following any prolonged layoff to reduce rupture risk when athletes return to full competition.
methods: Retrospective review at NYU Langone Health using CPT codes for Achilles repair (27650, 27652, 27654) from January 2017 to December 2021. Non-operative AATRs identified via ICD-10 codes. Acute primary repairs only included; augmentations, reconstructions, and tendon transfers excluded. Total sports surgery volume used as denominator control. NFL analysis: retrospective review of publicly available injury reports for players placed on injured reserve due to Achilles injury in 2019-20, 2020-21, and 2021-22 regular seasons. Chi-square analysis conducted to compare proportions across years.
findings: Total AATRs: 1,431 over 5 years (2017: n=288, 2018: n=261, 2019: n=291, 2020: n=196, 2021: n=395). Surgical repairs: 2017: 124, 2018: 110, 2019: 130, 2020: 86, 2021: 138. 2021 had significantly higher proportion of Achilles repairs vs. 2020 (p=0.008), 2018 (p=0.007), 2017 (p=0.03), and 2017-2020 combined (p=0.009), but not vs. 2019. NFL IR-level AATRs rose from 11 (2019-20) to 17 (2020-21) to 24 (2021-22), a 118% increase over 3 seasons. Cohort: 75.7% male, avg age 43.2±14.4 years, avg BMI 28.6.
limitations: Single-institution study at an urban academic center; may not reflect national trends. CPT code capture only estimates AATR rate. Patient migration out of NYC during 2020 may have artificially lowered case counts. Non-operative management trends during COVID may have shifted cases. NFL data prior to 2019-20 relied on previously published studies. Only IR-designation injuries captured in NFL analysis.
practicalImplications: Sport science and medical staff should treat any prolonged period of inactivity as a high-risk window for Achilles tendon injury upon return. Progressive tendon loading protocols should be implemented before returning to full practice. Passive stretching has limited protective value; balance and proprioception training programs have demonstrated benefit. Tendon adaptation requires time, and rapid workload spikes following deconditioning are the primary danger.
athleteDev: Achilles tendon health should be a monitored component of return-to-activity programming after any extended layoff. Athletes with longer periods of inactivity (8+ weeks) may require dedicated Achilles-specific progressive loading phases — including eccentric protocols, isometrics, and plyometric progressions — before returning to full practice demands.
rtp: Following Achilles tendon rupture in NFL players, IR designation was virtually universal, indicating season-ending or multi-month absences. For players returning from quarantine or canceled preseason, graduated return protocols should include Achilles loading benchmarks before clearance for full-contact practice. The parallel between recreational athlete AATR increases post-COVID and NFL IR spikes in 2021-22 provides strong support for mandatory progressive Achilles preparation protocols after any prolonged layoff.

citation: Sclafani S, Frane N, Humphrey TJ, Miceli J, Trasolini R. Increased regular season soft tissue injury rates in National Football League players may be associated with the canceled 2020 NFL preseason due to COVID-19. Cureus. 2022;14(5):e24674.
abstract: This retrospective study compared soft tissue injury rates in the NFL's 2020-2021 regular season (canceled preseason) versus the 2018-2019 regular season (normal preseason) using publicly available injury report data. Eight soft tissue injury categories were analyzed: hamstring, groin, calf, quadriceps, thigh, knee-ACL, pectoral, and Achilles. Total injuries in 2020-2021 (n=2086) were substantially higher than in 2018-2019 (n=1370), with overall injury rates per 1000 athletic exposures of 88.57 vs. 58.17 (p<0.001). Hamstring, groin, calf, quadriceps, and thigh injuries were all significantly elevated in 2020-2021, while ACL, pectoral, and Achilles injuries did not reach statistical significance, possibly due to lower event rates or injury mechanism differences.
tldr: The canceled 2020 NFL preseason due to COVID-19 was associated with a 52% increase in total soft tissue injuries during the regular season, with hamstring, groin, calf, quad, and thigh injuries each significantly elevated. This parallels findings from the 2011 NFL lockout and strongly supports the protective role of preseason and gradual workload progression. For applied sport science staff, this is powerful evidence that any abrupt increase in training load following a deconditioning period dramatically elevates soft tissue injury risk.
methods: Retrospective review of publicly available NFL injury reports (NFL.com) for the 2020-2021 and 2018-2019 regular seasons. Injury data points defined by unique anatomic location and game status per weekly report. Injury rates calculated per 1000 athletic exposures (23,552 AEs/season: 46 active players × 2 teams × 256 games). Chi-square tests for categorical comparisons; Student's t-test for continuous variables. Eight soft tissue injury categories analyzed; all preseason, postseason injuries and illness designations excluded.
findings: Total injuries: 2018-19=1,370; 2020-21=2,086. Overall rate/1000 AEs: 58.17 vs 88.57 (p<0.001). Significantly higher in 2020-21: hamstring (9.98 vs 5.31, p=0.043), groin (5.56 vs 2.46, p=0.007), calf (4.08 vs 1.61, p=0.006), quadriceps (2.00 vs 0.72, p=0.030), thigh (1.23 vs 0.30, p=0.012). Non-significant: knee-ACL (p=0.251), pectoral (p=0.165), Achilles (p=0.214, identical 0.72 rate both seasons). Average duration of hamstring, groin, quadriceps, and thigh injuries was significantly shorter in 2020-21. No significant age differences between seasons.
limitations: Data integrity depends on accurate team-level injury reporting. Causality cannot be definitively established. Individual-level risk factor analysis not possible from population database. Variation in rest periods between games not captured. Team-specific return-to-play protocol differences not accounted for. Only 2 comparison seasons used. Cannot account for potential rule enforcement or gameplay changes.
practicalImplications: Strong empirical support for the protective role of NFL preseason training on regular season soft tissue injury rates. Sport science staff should advocate for maintaining preseason training continuity as the primary strategy to mitigate muscle strain injuries. Injury surveillance should be heightened in early weeks of any season following abbreviated preparation. Rapid workload increases from a low chronic baseline (acute:chronic workload imbalance) drive hamstring, groin, calf, and thigh injury risk.
athleteDev: Gradual workload progression is a foundational injury prevention principle for NFL athletes. Off-season programming that maintains a chronic training base — even during disruptions — is protective against early-season soft tissue breakdown. Hamstring eccentric strength, posterior chain flexibility, and sport-specific movement patterns are particularly vulnerable following deconditioning. Development programs should incorporate workload monitoring tools (acute:chronic ratios) to prevent dangerous spikes.
rtp: Players returning from extended inactivity are at significantly elevated risk for hamstring, groin, calf, quadriceps, and thigh injuries once full practice and game demands resume. RTP protocols following deconditioning periods should include structured tissue loading phases before full-contact clearance, with particular attention to hamstring eccentric strength deficits and posterior chain flexibility. The shorter average injury duration in 2020-21 suggests milder injuries on average, but the sheer volume increase still poses significant roster management challenges.

citation: Abed V, Fine R, Fine R, Conley C, Jacobs C, Stone AV. Increased Snap Counts Were Not Seen Prior to Lisfranc Injuries in the National Football League. Cureus. 2022;14(12):e32736.
abstract: This retrospective study examined 21 NFL players who sustained Lisfranc injuries during regular season games between 2013 and 2021 to determine whether increased snap counts contributed to injury risk. Player data including position, draft information, snap counts, approximate value (AV), injury timing, and foot laterality were collected from public online resources and the Pro Football Reference database. Injured players were matched to 32 controls based on position, age, BMI, seasons played, pre-injury AV, and snaps per game. Results showed that players played significantly fewer snaps on the day of injury compared to their season average (33.9 vs. 50.3 snaps; p<0.001), and there was no significant difference in career snap rates between the injury year and prior seasons (p=0.20). The majority of injuries (76.2%) occurred during the first eight games of the season, and no significant differences in return-to-play statistics were found between injured players and controls.
tldr: Lisfranc injuries in NFL players are not preceded by elevated snap counts or cumulative workload spikes, suggesting they are acute traumatic events rather than overuse injuries. Most occurred early in the regular season, which may point to pre-season preparation as a modifiable risk factor. Offensive and defensive linemen were disproportionately affected (42.8%), likely due to higher BMI and frequent foot-planting maneuvers. RTP rates and post-injury performance were comparable to matched controls, offering reassurance for roster planning.
methods: NFL players with Lisfranc injuries (2013-2021) were identified by cross-referencing public injury reports, press releases, and game summaries, verified by at least two sources. Inclusion required the injury to occur in-game and after at least one prior season. Snap counts and AV were retrieved from Pro Football Reference. A control group (n=32) was matched to the injured cohort (n=16 with RTP data) on position, age, BMI, pre-injury seasons, AV, and snaps per game. Statistical analysis used Wilcoxon rank-sum tests for skewed variables and Welch two-sample t-tests for continuous outcomes; significance set at p<0.05.
findings: 21 players met inclusion criteria; 18 (85.7%) underwent surgery. Players played significantly fewer snaps in the injury game vs. season average (33.9 +/- 21.9 vs. 50.3 +/- 15.8; p<0.001). No significant difference in career vs. injury-year snaps per game (45.7 +/- 17.1 vs. 50.3 +/- 15.8; p=0.20). Most injuries (76.2%) occurred in the first half of the season. Injury timing within games was most common in Q2 (38.1%) and Q4 (33.3%). Left foot injured twice as frequently as right (66.7% vs. 33.3%). No significant differences between injured players and controls for post-injury seasons, AV, or snaps per game.
limitations: Injury identification relied on public databases, so unreported cases would be missed. Surgical techniques, physical examination findings, and rehabilitation protocols could not be assessed without medical records. Sample size was small (n=21). Acute-chronic workload ratios could not be calculated from available data.
practicalImplications: Lisfranc injuries appear to be acute events not driven by workload accumulation, so snap count monitoring alone is unlikely to prevent them. Early-season injury clustering suggests pre-season conditioning and gradual return to full practice loads may be relevant mitigation strategies. Linemen require particular attention given their disproportionate injury rate.
athleteDev: Offensive and defensive linemen had the highest Lisfranc injury rates, likely related to high BMI and repetitive foot-planting demands. Footwear selection, field surface type, and pre-season conditioning programs targeting foot and ankle strength may be worth evaluating as preventive measures for linemen.
rtp: 85.7% of injured players required surgery, yet post-injury performance metrics (AV, snaps per game, seasons played) were not significantly different from matched controls. Most players (81.3%) returned to their same team. These data support optimistic RTP counseling after Lisfranc injury and surgery, with expected return to pre-injury performance levels.

citation: Wise P, Gallo RA. Increasing Incidence of Pectoralis Major Ruptures in NFL Players. Orthop J Sports Med. 2019;7(7 suppl 5):2325967119S00396.
abstract: This study examined the incidence and characteristics of pectoralis major ruptures in NFL players from 2010 to 2017 using publicly available reports from ESPN.com and NFL.com. Injury details including age, position, injury setting, PED suspension history, and NFL Combine performance data were collected and compared to position-matched peers. Over the eight-year period, 51 players sustained 55 total pectoralis major ruptures, representing a dramatic increase from only 10 complete ruptures reported in the NFL from 2000 to 2010. Defensive players accounted for 82% of injuries, and 87.3% required surgery. Return to play was achieved in 85.4% of athletes who had prior playing time. Combine metrics (bench press, arm length, shuttle time) for injured players were not significantly different from peers, and only 5.9% had prior PED suspensions.
tldr: Pectoralis major ruptures in the NFL have increased more than five-fold over comparable time periods, affecting predominantly defensive players at an average age of 26. The cause of this rise remains unexplained and appears unrelated to baseline strength, agility, arm length, or PED use. Over 85% of injured players who had prior playing time returned to game action, suggesting favorable RTP prognosis with surgical repair. Medical and strength staff should be aware of this rising injury trend, particularly for defensive linemen and linebackers.
methods: Public injury reports from ESPN.com and NFL.com were searched to identify pectoralis major injuries in NFL players from 2010 to 2017. For each injury, details about age, position played, injury setting (game vs. practice), PED suspension history, and NFL Combine performance (bench press reps, arm length, 20-yard shuttle) were recorded. Combine data were compared to position-matched peers at the same workout to assess relative performance. A prior study reported 10 complete ruptures from 2000 to 2010 for historical comparison.
findings: 51 NFL players sustained 55 pectoralis major ruptures from 2010 to 2017 (vs. 10 from 2000 to 2010). Defensive players comprised 82% (42 of 51) of injuries. Mean age at rupture was 26 +/- 1 years. 76.4% of ruptures occurred during games. 87.3% required surgery. Of those with prior playing time whose rupture occurred before the 2017 season, 85.4% returned to play. Injured players ranked at the 63rd percentile for bench press, 50th for arm length, and 52nd for 20-yard shuttle vs. position peers. Only 5.9% (3 of 51) had prior PED suspensions.
limitations: Injury identification depended on public reporting, which may undercount cases. Definitive surgical treatment could not be confirmed for 7 of 55 injuries. The study was descriptive without a formal control group. The mechanism of the incidence increase could not be determined from available data.
practicalImplications: The dramatic rise in pectoralis major ruptures in the NFL warrants attention from strength staff, athletic trainers, and team physicians. Given that relative strength and PED use do not explain the trend, other factors such as training methodologies, practice load, or equipment changes may warrant investigation. High RTP rates support surgical intervention when indicated.
athleteDev: Defensive players (82%) are at highest risk for pectoralis major rupture, likely due to the demands of hand-fighting, shedding blocks, and pass-rushing techniques that place the pectoralis under eccentric load. Monitoring bench press technique and progressive loading during in-season and off-season strength programs may help identify at-risk athletes.
rtp: 85.4% of NFL players with prior playing time returned to game action following pectoralis major rupture, most requiring surgical repair (87.3%). Medical staff should anticipate a prolonged recovery timeline but can counsel athletes and teams on favorable return-to-play prognosis. Monitoring post-RTP performance is warranted given the high-demand nature of defensive positions.

citation: Wang Z, Feng Z, Lu M, Sun J, Liu T, Zhang Q, Wang X. Independent effects of mental fatigue and drop height on drop jump performance in American football athletes: an exploration of central-peripheral interaction. PeerJ. 2026;14:e20574.
abstract: This study investigated the independent and interactive effects of mental fatigue (MF) and drop height on drop jump (DJ) performance in 12 male collegiate American football athletes using a within-subjects repeated-measures design. MF was induced via a 45-minute Stroop task, and DJ performance was tested at drop heights of 30, 40, and 50 cm using a Vicon 3D motion capture system and Kistler force platforms. Outcome measures included jump height, peak vertical ground reaction force (vGRF), rate of force development (RFD), average power output, reactive strength index (RSI), reactive strength ratio (RSR), and lower limb stiffness (Kleg). MF significantly reduced average power output, RSI, and RSR across all drop heights, suggesting impaired central nervous system efficiency. Increasing drop height above 30 cm significantly reduced jump height, RFD, and RSI, while increasing Kleg. No significant interaction between MF and drop height was found, indicating their effects operate through independent mechanisms.
tldr: Mental fatigue impairs power output and reactive strength in American football athletes during drop jumps, independent of drop height, while drop heights above 30 cm reduce jump performance metrics and increase leg stiffness. These findings suggest that cognitively demanding pre-training or pre-game activities (film study, video games, poor sleep) may meaningfully reduce athletes' explosive lower-body output. For applied staff, monitoring athletes' mental fatigue status before plyometric training sessions is warranted, and 30 cm may represent the optimal drop height for American football-specific DJ training given the sport's multidirectional demands.
methods: Twelve male American football athletes (age 22.2 +/- 1.9 years, height 181.4 +/- 3.1 cm, mass 80.1 +/- 8.5 kg) from Soochow University participated. MF was induced using a 45-minute computerized Stroop task; cognitive and effort workload were confirmed via NASA-TLX before and after. DJ tests were performed at 30, 40, and 50 cm drop heights under both baseline and MF conditions in a 2 x 3 repeated-measures ANOVA design. A Vicon infrared 3D motion capture system (8 cameras, 100 Hz) and dual Kistler force platforms (1000 Hz) captured kinematic and kinetic data. Outcome variables were calculated from force-time data and motion capture. Greenhouse-Geisser corrections and Bonferroni post-hoc tests were applied as needed; alpha = 0.05.
findings: MF induction was confirmed by significant increases in NASA-TLX cognitive demand (p<0.001) and effort (p=0.002). MF significantly reduced average power output (p<0.001), RSI (p=0.001), and RSR (p=0.001) across all heights. Drop height significantly affected jump height (p<0.001), RFD (p<0.001), RSI (p=0.001), and Kleg (p=0.002): 40 cm and 50 cm yielded lower jump height, RFD, and RSI but higher Kleg compared to 30 cm. No significant MF x height interaction was found for any variable. Jump height and vGRFpeak were not significantly affected by MF alone.
limitations: Small all-male sample (n=12) from a single institution limits generalizability. Drop heights were restricted to 30-50 cm, limiting extension to other heights. MF was assessed primarily via subjective workload scale without physiological markers. The control condition used no intervention rather than an active low-demand control task, which may limit internal validity.
practicalImplications: Applied sport science staff should consider scheduling high-intensity plyometric training when athletes are not mentally fatigued from prolonged cognitive tasks such as film study, meetings, or electronic device use. Pre-session fatigue screening (e.g., NASA-TLX, reaction time tests) could help identify days when plyometric volume or intensity should be reduced. Brain endurance training may be a viable strategy to mitigate MF effects.
athleteDev: For American football athlete development, 30 cm drop height appears to produce the most favorable combination of jump height, RFD, and RSI, aligning with the sport's demand for multidirectional explosive movements rather than single-direction maximum force. Coaches should avoid excessively high drop heights in DJ training and manage cognitive load on plyometric training days to maximize neuromuscular adaptations.
rtp: Mental fatigue-induced reductions in average power output, RSI, and RSR suggest that athletes returning from injury may be at risk for suboptimal neuromuscular performance if cognitively fatigued during RTP testing or training. RTP readiness assessments should account for the athlete's cognitive state; DJ testing performed after mentally demanding activities may underestimate true lower-limb explosive capacity.

citation: Isenmann E, Veit S, Flenker U, Lesch A, Lachenmeier DW, Diel P. Influence of short-term chronic oral cannabidiol application on muscle recovery and performance after an intensive training protocol - a randomized double-blind crossover study. J Int Soc Sports Nutr. 2024;21(1):2337252.
abstract: This three-arm double-blind randomized crossover study investigated the effects of short-term chronic cannabidiol (CBD) application on muscle recovery and performance in 17 well-trained athletes following a six-day high-intensity training protocol combining strength and interval running sessions. Subjects received 60 mg of CBD oil, CBD solubilisate, or placebo after each training session, with four-week washout periods between arms. Performance was assessed via countermovement jump (CMJ), 1-RM back squat, 1-RM bench press, and 1-mile run before and after the training week. Biomarkers for muscle damage (CK, myoglobin), inflammation (IL-6, IL-10), oxidative stress, and immune cell activity were measured. Athletes were classified as advanced (n=8) or highly advanced (n=9) based on relative squat performance. CBD oil was associated with reduced myoglobin concentration in advanced athletes (p=0.049), but no clear effects of either CBD product on performance parameters were found.
tldr: Short-term chronic CBD supplementation (60 mg/day) during an intensive training week produced minimal measurable benefits for muscle recovery or performance in well-trained athletes. CBD oil modestly reduced myoglobin in advanced athletes but had no impact on performance metrics or inflammatory markers. Critically, performance-level classification (advanced vs. highly advanced) was the strongest predictor of how athletes responded to the training protocol. For applied staff considering CBD supplementation protocols, current evidence does not support its routine use for recovery during high-load training blocks, and performance level should be considered when evaluating supplementation efficacy.
methods: 17 trained male athletes completed a three-arm randomized double-blind crossover trial with 4-week washout periods. The six-day training protocol included whole-body strength training on days 1, 3, 5 (back squat, bench press, lunges, dips, rows at 65-70% 1RM) and high-intensity interval running on days 2, 4, 6 (4 x 4 min at max effort). CBD oil (sublingual, 60 mg), CBD solubilisate (oral, 60 mg), or placebo was consumed after each session. Performance (CMJ via OptoJump, 1-RM squat and bench press, 1-mile run) and blood biomarkers (CK, myoglobin, IL-6, IL-10, OxLDL, immune cell ratios) were measured pre- and post-training week. Linear mixed-effects models were used for analysis.
findings: Both performance levels showed significant CK increases post-training (p=0.002-0.003). Myoglobin increased significantly only in advanced athletes under placebo (p=0.001); CBD oil significantly reduced this increase (p=0.049). CBD solubilisate did not reduce myoglobin. CMJ decreased significantly in advanced athletes under all treatments (p=0.015); highly advanced athletes showed a trend toward back squat decline. No significant changes in IL-6, IL-10, oxidative stress, or NLR were observed. PLR decreased in advanced athletes under placebo (p=0.047); CBD oil showed a trend toward inhibiting this decrease (p=0.083). No significant treatment effects on any performance parameter.
limitations: Eight subjects dropped out due to COVID-19, creating treatment order bias (highly advanced athletes disproportionately received CBD-solu first). Small group sizes (n=8-9 per level) limit statistical power. Blood CBD concentrations were not measured, so bioavailability differences between oil and solubilisate could not be quantified. The washout period (4 weeks) may have been insufficient given persistent adaptation effects observed in biomarkers.
practicalImplications: Applied staff should not expect meaningful recovery benefits from 60 mg/day CBD supplementation during high-intensity training blocks based on current evidence. The finding that athlete training status significantly modulates recovery responses highlights the need to individualize supplementation strategies. Future research should use longer washout periods, measure CBD plasma levels, and specify athlete performance classification to enable more definitive conclusions.
athleteDev: The training protocol (18 work sets per muscle group per week combined with HIIT running) reliably induced measurable muscle damage and performance decrements, confirming its utility as a physiological stress model. The differential response between advanced and highly advanced athletes to the same protocol underscores the importance of individualized training loads in football athlete development programs.
rtp: The study demonstrates that even well-trained athletes experience measurable muscle damage and CMJ decrements following high-volume training blocks; CBD supplementation does not meaningfully accelerate recovery of these performance markers. For RTP contexts, functional performance metrics like CMJ should be monitored after high-load training phases, and recovery timelines should not assume supplementation will accelerate readiness.

citation: Krause DA, Dueffert LG, Postma JL, Vogler ET, Walsh AJ, Hollman JH. Influence of Body Position on Shoulder and Trunk Muscle Activation During Resisted Isometric Shoulder External Rotation. Sports Health. 2018;10(5):407-412.
abstract: This quasi-experimental repeated-measures study examined the effects of body position on external rotation (ER) torque and electromyographic (EMG) activation of shoulder and trunk muscles in 25 healthy overhead recreational athletes performing resisted isometric shoulder ER in three positions: standing, side lying, and side plank. Surface EMG was recorded from the infraspinatus, posterior deltoid, middle trapezius, multifidi, and bilateral internal and external obliques, normalized to maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC). ER force was measured via handheld dynamometer. No significant differences in ER torque were found between positions, but significant EMG differences were found in 7 of 8 muscles. The side plank position produced significantly greater EMG activity in the infraspinatus, middle trapezius, and nondominant external and internal obliques compared to standing and side lying, supporting its use as an integrated shoulder and core exercise.
tldr: When performing shoulder external rotation strengthening, the side plank position produces greater activation of the rotator cuff (infraspinatus) and trunk muscles (obliques) compared to standing or side lying, without sacrificing ER torque output. For football athletes requiring both shoulder stability and core integration - particularly quarterbacks, linemen, and athletes in overhead rehab - the side plank ER exercise is the preferred position to simultaneously target shoulder and trunk musculature. All three positions adequately stimulate shoulder girdle muscles above the 50% MVIC threshold needed for strength gains.
methods: Twenty-five healthy overhead recreational athletes (9 men, 16 women; ages 22-31) participated in this IRB-approved study. Surface EMG electrodes (Bagnoli DE-3.1, Delsys) were placed on the infraspinatus, posterior deltoid, middle trapezius, multifidi, and bilateral external and internal obliques. A 5-second MVIC was recorded for each muscle to normalize signals. Participants performed resisted isometric shoulder ER in standing, side-lying, and side plank positions in randomized order, with ER force recorded via MicroFET 2 handheld dynamometer. Three trials per position were performed with 20-second rest between trials and 2-minute rest between positions. Peak EMG within a 100-ms window was used. Repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni corrections (alpha = 0.05) analyzed differences.
findings: No significant difference in ER torque between positions (standing: 29.5 +/- 10.0 N-m; side lying: 29.3 +/- 11.7 N-m; side plank: 29.3 +/- 10.0 N-m). Side plank produced significantly greater EMG than standing and side lying for: infraspinatus (mean difference vs. standing: 8.12% MVIC, p=0.02; vs. side lying: 13.11% MVIC, p=0.02), middle trapezius (vs. standing: 12.64%, p=0.01; vs. side lying: 11.09%, p=0.01), nondominant external oblique (vs. standing: 44.85%, p<0.01; vs. side lying: 43.39%, p<0.01), and nondominant internal oblique (vs. standing: 22.46%, p=0.03; vs. side lying: 36.43%, p<0.01). Only the side plank position exceeded the 50% MVIC threshold for trunk muscles.
limitations: The sample was young, healthy, and athletic, limiting generalizability to injured, elderly, or post-operative patients. The unexplained finding of greater infraspinatus and posterior deltoid EMG in side plank without increased torque may reflect EMG cross-talk from teres minor. The study examined only isometric ER and did not assess dynamic movement conditions.
practicalImplications: Clinicians and strength staff designing shoulder rehabilitation or injury prevention programs for overhead athletes should consider the side plank ER position when the goal is to simultaneously activate both rotator cuff and core musculature. For purely shoulder-focused strengthening, any of the three positions is adequate. Progression from standing to side plank ER represents a logical continuum that increases the core demand without reducing the shoulder training stimulus.
athleteDev: For football athletes - particularly quarterbacks and receivers who depend on shoulder stability and rotational core strength - integrating side plank ER exercises into shoulder prehabilitation and strengthening programs can address both shoulder and trunk stability simultaneously. This exercise is practical for team settings and progresses naturally from more basic shoulder ER variations.
rtp: The side plank ER position is recommended during the transition phase of shoulder rehabilitation before return to throwing or overhead activity, as it integrates trunk stability with rotator cuff strengthening. Given that all three positions achieved sufficient EMG stimulus for shoulder girdle strength gains, standing and side-lying ER remain appropriate early in rehabilitation when core demands need to be minimized, with progression to side plank as tolerance improves.

citation: Pommerell F, Boyas S, Samozino P, Morel B, Begue J, Rahmani A, Peyrot N. Influence of countermovement depth on net force, push-off time, vertical impulse and performance during jumping. Journal of Electromyography and Kinesiology. 2024;79:102945.
abstract: This study investigated how push-off distance (countermovement depth) influences maximal jump height and the relationships among vertical jump mechanical parameters in 17 CrossFit athletes performing countermovement jumps across four depth conditions. Results confirmed a quadratic relationship between push-off distance and maximal jump height, indicating an individual optimal depth that maximizes performance. Increasing push-off distance was associated with increased push-off time and a concomitant decrease in mean net vertical force. An almost perfect negative linear relationship was observed between mean net vertical external force and push-off time (r = −0.99), mathematically producing a quadratic relationship between relative net vertical impulse and push-off time (r = 1.00). Individual optimal push-off distances corresponded to mean knee angles of approximately 100–103°, though high inter-individual variability was noted.
tldr: Countermovement depth directly affects vertical jump performance through opposing changes in force production and push-off time. There is an individual-specific optimal depth that maximizes jump height, and this optimal depth is highly variable across athletes (knee angles ranging 76–144°). Practitioners should assess each athlete's preferred or optimal countermovement depth rather than applying a standardized depth, as the Fnet–tPO relationship emerges as a novel indicator of an individual's vertical jump performance capacity that can guide training program design.
methods: Seventeen CrossFit athletes performed maximal countermovement jumps at four countermovement depth conditions (0–30%, 30–50%, 50–70%, 70–100% of maximal depth) on dual force plates synchronized with a 10-camera motion capture system. Push-off distance, push-off time, mean net vertical force, vertical impulse, take-off velocity, and maximal jump height were calculated. Linear and quadratic regression analyses were applied to characterize relationships between mechanical parameters on both averaged and individual data.
findings: Push-off distance and maximal jump height were related by an almost perfect quadratic relationship (r = 1.00), confirming an individual optimal push-off distance. Relative net vertical force decreased linearly as push-off distance increased (r = −0.99), while push-off time increased linearly (r = 1.00). The negative linear relationship between Fnet and tPO resulted mathematically in a quadratic relationship between relative vertical impulse and push-off time (r = 1.00). Mean optimal push-off distance was 75 ± 21% of maximal depth, corresponding to a knee angle of 103 ± 21°.
limitations: The study was limited to CrossFit athletes, limiting generalizability to other sport populations. The range of push-off distances tested did not encompass the predicted optimal for 9 of 17 participants, requiring extrapolation beyond the experimental range. Only unloaded jumps were analyzed, and a single session design was used. The Fnet–tPO profile was described from a relatively small sample.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should assess individual optimal countermovement depth before using vertical jump as a performance test or training tool. Using a self-preferred depth is a practical alternative to determining the exact optimum. Resistance training at specific squat depths may shift the Fnet–tPO profile, potentially increasing maximal vertical impulse and jump height. Standardizing depth across athletes may not capture true maximal jump ability.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, the finding that an individual optimal countermovement depth exists supports athlete-specific jump training protocols. Programs targeting vertical jump improvement should individualize countermovement depth rather than prescribing a uniform standard, particularly for positions requiring maximal vertical power (e.g., receivers, defensive backs, linebackers).
