The Fnet–tPO profile concept may help monitor adaptations to depth-specific strength training over a season.
rtp: The quadratic relationship between push-off distance and jump height provides a framework for monitoring neuromuscular recovery during return-to-play. If an athlete's optimal countermovement depth or Fnet–tPO profile deviates from their pre-injury baseline, this may indicate incomplete neuromuscular recovery. Testing jump performance across multiple depths—rather than at a single standardized depth—could provide more sensitive RTP criteria for lower-extremity injuries affecting range of motion.

citation: Wannop JW, Schrier N, Worobets J, Stefanyshyn D. Influence of forefoot bending stiffness on American football performance and metatarsophalangeal joint bending angle. Sports Biomechanics. 2020. DOI: 10.1080/14763141.2020.1750682.
abstract: This study examined whether increasing forefoot bending stiffness in football cleats reduces metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint hyperextension and improves athletic performance in 10 varsity grid-iron football players. Athletes performed four NFL combine movements (5-10-5 agility, 5-m sprint, vertical jump, broad jump) in three footwear conditions: Soft (12.7 N/mm), Control (23.8 N/mm), and Stiff (42.2 N/mm) on an artificial turf surface. None of the footwear conditions significantly altered peak MTP bending angle across any movement. Performance improvements occurred only during the 5-m sprint acceleration, with both Control and Stiff conditions producing faster times than Soft, driven by increased horizontal ground reaction impulse and greater ankle joint power and net work in the Control condition.
tldr: Increasing forefoot bending stiffness in football cleats up to 42.2 N/mm does not reduce MTP joint bending enough to lower turf-toe injury risk in large football athletes, likely because their greater strength and body mass overcomes the stiffness. However, moderate-to-high stiffness does improve short sprint acceleration performance by enhancing ankle plantarflexor output. Teams selecting footwear for large football players should recognize that injury protection may require stiffness values beyond what is currently available commercially, while performance benefits during acceleration favor stiffer forefoot constructions.
methods: Ten university grid-iron football players (mean mass 96.2 kg) wore three prototype adidas cleats of identical construction but differing forefoot bending stiffness (Soft 12.7, Control 23.8, Stiff 42.2 N/mm) on an artificial turf surface in a randomized order. An 8-camera motion capture system (240 Hz) and embedded force platform (2400 Hz) recorded 3D kinematics and kinetics during 5 trials of each of 4 NFL combine drills. MTP bending angle, sprint time, agility time, vertical and horizontal jump distance, ground reaction forces, and ankle joint moments, impulses, work, and power were analyzed using one-way ANOVA (α = 0.10).
findings: No significant differences in peak MTP extension angle were found between footwear conditions for any movement. Sprint acceleration time was significantly improved in Control (p = 0.085) and Stiff (p = 0.094) versus Soft conditions. The Control shoe significantly increased horizontal ground reaction impulse (+4.1%), ankle angular impulse (+5.1%), net ankle joint work (+9.5%), and peak ankle joint power (+6.6%) compared to Soft. No significant differences were found for the agility drill, vertical jump, or broad jump.
limitations: Only 10 athletes were tested, reducing statistical power. The stiffness range tested (up to 42.2 N/mm) may be insufficient to produce meaningful MTP bending reductions in large, strong football players. Only the second footfall during the sprint was captured by the force platform, missing initial and later steps. The study did not examine subjective comfort ratings. Generalizability to other player sizes, positions, or surface types is uncertain.
practicalImplications: Footwear with moderate forefoot bending stiffness (~23–42 N/mm) can improve sprint acceleration in large football players by augmenting ankle plantarflexor output. To meaningfully reduce MTP hyperextension injury risk in large athletes, stiffness values greater than 42.2 N/mm may be necessary, potentially with non-linear stiffness designs that remain comfortable at low bending angles. Equipment staff and sports medicine teams should consider athlete body mass and strength when recommending or selecting football cleats for both performance and injury prevention purposes.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, stiffer forefoot cleats may provide a performance edge during initial sprint acceleration—a critical action in nearly every football play. Linemen and skill players who initiate from a three-point stance may benefit most from moderate-to-stiff forefoot constructions. Footwear selection should be individualized to athlete size, as the optimal stiffness may scale with body mass and plantarflexor strength capacity.
rtp: Athletes returning from MTP joint sprains (turf toe) may benefit from footwear with increased forefoot bending stiffness as a protective measure, though the data suggest that stiffness values used in current commercial cleats may be insufficient to fully protect large players. Until higher-stiffness options are validated, RTP footwear protocols should include rigid forefoot orthotic inserts or carbon fiber plates in addition to standard cleat selection, particularly for heavier athletes resuming explosive cutting and sprinting activities.

citation: Polletta J, Leung K, Diaz D, Branum N, Mokha M. Influence of interlimb lean muscle mass asymmetry on countermovement jump neuromuscular performance qualities among American football players. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. 2024;XX(X):000–000.
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined whether interlimb lean muscle mass (LMM) asymmetry was associated with countermovement jump (CMJ) performance or force/impulse asymmetry in 74 collegiate American football players preparing for the 2024 NFL Draft. Body composition was assessed via InBody 270 bioelectrical impedance analysis, and CMJ variables—including jump height, reactive strength index modified (RSImod), and eccentric/concentric peak force and impulse asymmetries—were measured on dual uniaxial force plates. Although right and left leg LMM were significantly different (p < 0.001), mean LMM asymmetry was low (<1%) and showed no significant correlations with any CMJ asymmetry variable, jump height, or RSImod. Notably, larger asymmetries were observed during the eccentric phase than the concentric phase of the CMJ.
tldr: In high-level American football players, the magnitude of interlimb lean muscle mass asymmetry does not predict CMJ force or impulse asymmetry, jump height, or reactive strength. This means body composition symmetry alone cannot be assumed to equal neuromuscular performance symmetry—athletes may appear balanced in muscle mass yet still display significant force asymmetries during jumping, particularly in the eccentric loading phase. Practitioners should use force plate monitoring to directly assess functional asymmetries rather than relying solely on body composition data, and should prioritize targeted eccentric neuromuscular training to reduce CMJ asymmetries that do negatively impact jump performance.
methods: Seventy-four male collegiate football players (age 23.0 ± 0.9 years; mass 104.9 ± 21.6 kg) preparing for the 2024 NFL Draft underwent body composition assessment using an InBody 270 multifrequency bioelectrical impedance analyzer to obtain right and left leg LMM. Subjects performed 3 maximal CMJs with a dowel across the shoulders on dual uniaxial force plates (VALD FD Lites, 1000 Hz). ForceDecks software calculated jump height, RSImod, and interlimb percentage asymmetry of eccentric peak force, eccentric deceleration impulse, concentric peak force, and concentric impulse. Pearson correlation coefficients assessed relationships between LMM asymmetry and CMJ variables.
findings: Right leg LMM was significantly greater than left (12.69 vs 12.59 kg, p < 0.001), but mean LMM asymmetry was only 0.67 ± 0.67%. No significant correlations were found between LMM asymmetry and eccentric peak force asymmetry (r = 0.001, p = 0.954), eccentric impulse asymmetry (r = 0.158, p = 0.180), concentric peak force asymmetry (r = −0.070, p = 0.553), concentric impulse asymmetry (r = 0.111, p = 0.347), jump height (r = −0.205, p = 0.08), or RSImod (r = −0.151, p = 0.199). Eccentric phase asymmetries (peak force 4.8%, impulse 5.3%) were greater than concentric phase asymmetries (peak force 3.0%, impulse 3.1%). CMJ kinetic asymmetries were negatively correlated with jump height and RSImod.
limitations: The study was cross-sectional, preventing causal inference. There was no control group from other sports or non-athletic populations to determine whether asymmetries are sport-specific. Limb dominance and injury history were not systematically recorded or controlled. Positional differences were not analyzed. The InBody 270 may underestimate or overestimate body composition compared to DXA. LMM asymmetries in this sample were very low overall, which may have limited the ability to detect relationships.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning coaches cannot assume that achieving LMM symmetry will translate to symmetrical force production during bilateral jump tasks. Force plate monitoring should be used independently of body composition assessments to identify and address functional asymmetries. When CMJ force asymmetries are present, targeted eccentric neuromuscular training is indicated—especially for the loading/braking phase. For athletes with low jump height and high LMM asymmetry, unilateral strength exercises targeting the weaker limb are recommended; for athletes with low jump height but symmetric LMM, bilateral power training may be more appropriate.
athleteDev: NFL Draft preparation programs for American football players should incorporate dual force plate CMJ testing as a standard evaluation tool to quantify eccentric and concentric force asymmetries by limb. The eccentric deceleration phase shows the largest asymmetries and has the greatest impact on jump performance, making it a priority target in training programs for players in explosive positions. Position-specific movement demands may contribute to asymmetry patterns that differ by offensive vs. defensive role.
rtp: CMJ force asymmetry assessment—particularly during the eccentric phase—provides more sensitive and specific return-to-play information than body composition symmetry alone. Athletes cleared for return based on LMM symmetry may still display meaningful force production asymmetries that affect performance and injury risk. Force plate eccentric impulse asymmetry thresholds (e.g., <10% for return to full practice, <15% for return to competition) should be incorporated into RTP criteria following lower-extremity injuries in football players.

citation: Ready LV, Li NY, Worobey S, Lemme NJ, Yang DS, Yang J, Krill M, Owens BD. Influence of preseason versus in-season play on Achilles tendon injuries in the National Football League. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine. 2021;9(12):23259671211056083.
abstract: This retrospective case series analyzed 101 documented Achilles tendon (AT) tears in NFL players between 2009 and 2016 to identify risk factors related to player experience level and season timing. Of all AT tears, 64% occurred during the preseason (including preseason games), and 29% of those preseason tears affected rookies despite rookies representing only approximately 25% of preseason athletes. Importantly, 100% of in-season tears affected non-rookie players. AT tear incidence more than doubled across the study period. A bimodal age distribution was observed, with highest rates at ages 24 and 36. Return to play occurred in 58% of all injured players, but rookies returned at a substantially lower rate (42%) than non-rookies (62%).
tldr: The preseason is the highest-risk period for Achilles tendon tears in the NFL, particularly for rookie players who are transitioning to the demands of professional football. Rookies accounted for a disproportionate share of preseason tears and none of the in-season tears, while veteran players bore all in-season ruptures—likely reflecting an overuse/fatigue pattern later in the season. Medical and strength staffs should implement preseason-specific AT injury prevention protocols targeting rookies, with emphasis on gradual workload progression, adequate recovery, and proprioceptive training to reduce the disproportionate burden this injury places on early-career players.
methods: NFL players with a diagnosed AT tear between 2009 and 2016 were identified through an established publicly available database. Variables collected included player position, season timing (preseason/in-season/postseason), age, height, weight, years in league, draft status, field type, air temperature, and game versus practice. Rookie proportion was estimated using draft pick counts and undrafted free agent signings relative to preseason roster size. Incidence was computed per 10,000 athlete-exposures assuming 53-man rosters, 105 practices, and 16 games per season. Generalized linear models, chi-square analyses, and t-tests were used.
findings: 101 AT tears were documented from 2009–2016, with incidence increasing from 0.341 to 0.731 per 10,000 athlete-exposures over the study period. 64% of tears occurred during preseason; 29% of preseason tears affected rookies, while 100% of in-season tears affected non-rookies. AT tears decreased by an estimated 1.18 per additional year of NFL experience (p < 0.001). Skilled position players (62%) had more tears than unskilled (34%); defensive end (19 tears) and linebacker (16) were most common positions. No significant differences in tear rates were found between grass and turf surfaces or between Sunday and Thursday games. Return-to-play rate was 42% for rookies vs. 62% for non-rookies.
limitations: Data relied on publicly available injury reports, which may be incomplete. The NFL does not publicly release exact rookie counts per year, requiring estimation. Sample size was limited to 101 tears over 7 years, reducing statistical power. The study was retrospective and could not control for training loads, prior injury history, or individual conditioning status. Player recovery rates in the NFL may not generalize to other populations. Only index ruptures were included, excluding recurrent injuries.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and strength staffs should treat the preseason as the highest-risk window for AT injuries, especially for first-year players. Prevention strategies should include load monitoring and gradual progression of running volume for rookies entering preseason camp, proprioceptive and calf-strengthening programs, and education about AT injury warning signs. The absence of significant surface or temperature effects suggests that environmental modifications are less impactful than workload management. Organizations should ensure that rookies receive at least equivalent—if not greater—preventative resources relative to veterans during preseason.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, the sharp decline in AT injury risk with increasing years of NFL experience suggests that developing robust AT tissue resilience is a multi-year adaptation process. Strength and conditioning programs for younger and newly professional players should include progressive Achilles tendon loading protocols throughout the year, not just during the season. Plyometric and eccentric calf training in the off-season may help condition AT tissue before the high-demand preseason environment. Position-specific risk profiling—prioritizing defensive ends and linebackers—can guide targeted prevention resources.
rtp: Rookies returning from AT ruptures face a significantly lower likelihood of NFL career continuation (42% vs. 62% for veterans), making early identification and aggressive preventative measures critical for this population. RTP protocols following AT rupture should account for the minimum 5–12 month timeline before return to full participation. Given the career impact of these injuries, RTP criteria should incorporate functional measures beyond time-based thresholds, including strength symmetry testing and sport-specific load progression. Teams should recognize that undrafted free agents—representing 36% of AT tear cases—may lack the contractual protection that incentivizes teams to invest in full rehabilitation.

citation: Pecci J, Sañudo B, Ramirez-Campillo R, Saez de Villarreal E. Influence of resistance training variables and the Nordic hamstring exercise on biceps femoris architectural adaptations in soccer players: a systematic review. Sports Health. 2025;XX(X). DOI: 10.1177/19417381251331607.
abstract: This systematic review with meta-analysis analyzed how resistance training variables affect biceps femoris long head (BFLH) muscle architecture in soccer players, focusing on the Nordic hamstring exercise (NHE). Six studies comprising 12 training groups and 168 participants were analyzed, examining the effects of NHE versus control, training frequency (1 vs. 2 days/week), and training volume. The NHE significantly increased BFLH fascicle length (p = 0.01), while changes in muscle thickness and pennation angle were nonsignificant. Training twice per week did not produce significantly greater architectural adaptations than once per week, though it showed higher effect sizes. High training volumes (>290 total repetitions, ≥57 repetitions/week) over 6–12 weeks produced significant fascicle length increases, whereas low volumes did not.
tldr: The Nordic hamstring exercise is effective at increasing biceps femoris fascicle length in soccer players, which is important because shorter fascicles are associated with higher hamstring injury risk. To achieve meaningful fascicle lengthening, players should accumulate more than 290 total NHE repetitions across the training program at a density of at least 57 repetitions per week, with at least 6 weeks of training and preferably 2 sessions per week. This dosing framework provides concrete, evidence-based programming guidance for strength and conditioning coaches seeking to reduce hamstring injury incidence in soccer and other field sport athletes including football.
methods: Five databases (PubMed, SPORTDiscus, Web of Science, PsycInfo, CINAHL) were searched from inception to January 2024. Studies were included if they involved competitive soccer players, contained a resistance training intervention with hamstring exercises of at least 4 weeks, and measured BFLH muscle architecture (muscle thickness, fascicle length, pennation angle) via ultrasound or MRI. Six studies met inclusion criteria. Statistical analysis used Hedges' g effect sizes, inverse-variance weighted random effects models, and I² for heterogeneity. Subgroup analyses compared NHE vs. control, training frequency, and training volume. Study quality was assessed using the TESTEX scale (mean score 11.33 ± 1.89, indicating high quality).
findings: NHE significantly improved BFLH fascicle length (p = 0.01) compared to control (onfield training only); muscle thickness showed a nonsignificant increase and pennation angle a nonsignificant decrease. Training frequency of 2 days/week was not significantly superior to 1 day/week for any architectural variable, though effect sizes favored higher frequency. High training volume (>290 total repetitions) produced significant fascicle length increases; low volume (<290 repetitions) did not. High-volume groups averaged 57 repetitions/week. The median training volume across all included interventions was 290 repetitions.
limitations: Only six studies met inclusion criteria, limiting the robustness of conclusions. All included studies used the NHE as the primary resistance exercise, making it impossible to compare hip-dominant vs. knee-dominant exercises or loaded NHE variations. Fascicle length was measured using 2D ultrasound prediction equations, which may overestimate true fascicle length due to curvature and limited transducer field of view. No study trained to muscular failure, so degree-of-effort effects could not be analyzed. Only one study included female players, limiting generalizability to women. Meta-regression could not be performed due to fewer than 10 studies per comparison.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning coaches should prescribe NHE programs with >290 total repetitions over 6–12 weeks at a density of at least 57 repetitions/week to achieve significant biceps femoris fascicle lengthening. Two training sessions per week is the recommended frequency, though one session per week may still produce meaningful architectural benefits when volume is equalized. Hip-dominant exercises such as the stiff-leg deadlift should be further investigated as potential complements to NHE, given their greater selective activation of the BFLH. The relationship between fascicle lengthening and hamstring injury incidence supports the use of NHE as a primary injury prevention tool in field sports.
athleteDev: Football athletes—particularly skill positions and linebackers subject to high-speed running demands—can benefit from structured NHE cycles targeting >290 total repetitions over a preseason or early in-season block. Incorporating NHE into strength programs can lengthen biceps femoris fascicles, which may reduce hamstring strain risk during the terminal swing phase of sprinting. Sprint-based eccentric training (as explored in one included study) produced even greater fascicle length improvements than NHE alone, suggesting value in combining sprint work with NHE in football conditioning programs.
rtp: Biceps femoris fascicle length is a modifiable structural risk factor for hamstring injury, and NHE-based programs can systematically increase fascicle length as part of return-to-play protocols following hamstring strains. Before full clearance for high-speed running, athletes recovering from biceps femoris injuries should complete a progressive NHE loading block targeting the dose thresholds identified in this review (>290 repetitions). Ultrasound measurement of fascicle length at baseline and post-rehabilitation can objectively confirm structural recovery and guide return-to-sport decisions alongside functional strength testing.

citation: Knurr KA, Cobian DG, Kliethermes SA, Joachim MR, Heiderscheit BC. Influence of running speed and time post-operatively on lower extremity work in collegiate athletes after anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2025;57(3):579–589.
abstract: This study examined how running speed and post-operative time affect lower extremity joint work during running in 56 collegiate athletes (32 ACL-reconstructed, 24 controls) at early (3.5–7 months) and late (8–13 months) post-operative time points across five treadmill speeds (2.68–4.47 m/s). Linear mixed effects models assessed limb symmetry in knee, hip, and ankle negative and positive work. At early post-op, the reconstructed limb showed a 67% deficit in knee negative work and 50% deficit in knee positive work, with compensatory increases in hip positive work. By late post-op, knee deficits improved to approximately 45% negative and 35% positive, and hip compensation resolved. Ankle deficits, present early, largely resolved by the late time point. Running speed influenced absolute work values but did not interact with limb or time point for most outcomes.
tldr: After ACLR, athletes show large knee work deficits (>60% early, ~45% late post-op) that persist well into return-to-sport timelines, with early hip compensation that resolves but ongoing knee asymmetry suggesting functional recovery lags behind clinical clearance criteria. Speed does not resolve these asymmetries.
methods: Cross-sectional study; 32 ACLR collegiate athletes (Division I) and 24 healthy controls; instrumented treadmill running at five speeds (2.68, 3.13, 3.58, 4.02, 4.47 m/s); 3D motion capture and force measurement; lower extremity joint work calculated from joint moment and angular velocity; linear mixed effects models with Bonferroni correction; limb symmetry index computed for each joint and phase.
findings: Reconstructed limb had 67% less knee negative work and ~50% less knee positive work at early post-op vs. contralateral limb; late post-op deficits remained (~45% negative, ~35% positive). Hip positive work was elevated early (compensation) but normalized by late post-op. Ankle deficits present early resolved by late post-op. Higher speeds increased absolute work values across joints. No speed × limb interaction was statistically significant for most outcomes, indicating deficits were speed-independent.
limitations: Cross-sectional design precludes within-athlete longitudinal tracking; no pre-injury baseline; heterogeneous graft types; small sample at some speed × time-point cells; did not assess sport-specific movements beyond straight-line running; control group was not matched for sport.
practicalImplications: Knee mechanical work deficits persist beyond typical RTS timelines (8–13 months), suggesting practitioners should not rely solely on time post-op when making return-to-sport decisions. Biomechanical assessment during running—especially knee work symmetry—should be a standard RTS criterion. Hip compensation early post-op may mask knee deficits in clinical assessments.
athleteDev: For football players post-ACLR, return-to-run progressions should include formal biomechanical screening at multiple speeds. Strength and neuromuscular training targeting knee extensor function during the stance phase may accelerate symmetry restoration. Athletes cleared at 6 months may still have clinically meaningful knee work deficits that increase reinjury risk.
rtp: Limb symmetry in knee negative and positive work during running should be quantified before RTS clearance in ACLR athletes. Persistent deficits at 8–13 months post-op indicate that time-based RTS criteria are insufficient. Clinicians and S&C staff should implement instrumented running assessments or isokinetic testing protocols that approximate joint work demands to better individualize RTS timing.

citation: Wannop JW, Kowalchuk S, Smith-Bregeda E, Aldahir P, Spratley EM, O'Cain CM, Park G, Stefanyshyn D. Influence of sport surface properties on utilized traction and lower extremity biomechanics of American football players. Appl Sci. 2026;16(5):2423.
abstract: This study investigated how three FieldTurf surfaces with differing traction properties affected utilized traction and lower extremity biomechanics during cutting movements in 16 university-level American football players. Participants performed 45° cuts, 90° cuts, and V-cuts wearing standardized football cleats on three artificial turf surfaces. A 15-camera Vicon motion capture system and Kistler force plates assessed frontal plane joint angles and moments at the ankle and knee, along with utilized traction (the resultant horizontal to vertical GRF ratio). Higher-traction surfaces produced flatter foot contact angles (more perpendicular cleat engagement), greater utilized traction, and elevated frontal plane ankle inversion and knee valgus moments. A traction threshold of approximately 1.6 was identified as necessary for optimal cutting mechanics. Positional differences emerged, with speed-position players (wide receivers, defensive backs) generating higher traction demands than power-position players (linemen).
tldr: Higher-traction artificial turf surfaces alter cutting biomechanics in football players by increasing frontal plane ankle and knee loading. A traction coefficient above ~1.6 appears necessary for optimal cutting, but very high traction may increase injury risk at the ankle and knee. Positional demands differ, with skill players generating higher traction forces.
methods: Within-subject repeated measures; 16 male university football players; three FieldTurf artificial surfaces with different traction ratings; three cutting tasks (45°, 90°, V-cut); 15-camera Vicon motion capture at 200 Hz; Kistler force plates at 2000 Hz; utilized traction = resultant horizontal GRF / vertical GRF; frontal plane ankle and knee angles and moments computed via inverse dynamics; mixed ANOVA with surface and cut type as within-subject factors; post-hoc Tukey tests.
findings: Higher-traction surfaces produced significantly greater utilized traction (p<0.05) and altered foot contact angle (flatter foot). Frontal plane ankle inversion moments and knee valgus moments were significantly elevated on high-traction surfaces. Optimal cutting mechanics required a traction threshold of ~1.6. Wide receivers and defensive backs produced higher traction demands than linemen. No significant surface effect on sagittal plane outcomes. V-cuts produced the highest traction demands across all surfaces.
limitations: Laboratory setting with force plates may not replicate field conditions; only male players tested; standardized cleats were used (no cleat × surface interaction); small sample (n=16); positions not equally represented; outdoor environmental factors (temperature, moisture, infill compression) not controlled.
practicalImplications: Turf selection for practice and competition facilities should consider traction profiles relative to positional demands. Facilities using high-traction artificial surfaces may inadvertently increase frontal plane ankle and knee loading during cutting, which could elevate soft tissue injury risk. Speed-position players may warrant specific footwear or surface pairing considerations.
athleteDev: Football speed-position players (WR, DB, RB) generate higher traction demands during cuts; their cleat-surface pairing should be evaluated periodically, especially when transitioning between surfaces. Frontal plane lower extremity strength (hip abductors, ankle invertors) may help buffer the increased joint moments on high-traction turf.
rtp: Athletes returning from ankle or knee injuries should undergo biomechanical assessment of cutting mechanics across different surfaces before full RTP, particularly if the team trains or competes on high-traction artificial turf. The elevated frontal plane loads on high-traction surfaces may represent a meaningful reinjury risk factor during RTP progressions.

citation: Soler A, Agulló F, Hernández-Davó J, Raya-González J, Del Coso J, González-Ródenas J, Moreno-Pérez V. Influence of the external workload on calf muscle strain injuries in professional soccer players: a pilot study. Sports Health. 2024;16(6):1015–1022.
abstract: This pilot study examined the relationship between GPS-derived external workload and calf muscle strain injuries in professional soccer players across two LaLiga seasons. Forty-one male players were monitored with GPS-accelerometer devices during all training sessions and matches. Sixteen calf injuries occurred in 10 players. Workload variables in the 7-day period preceding injury were compared to each player's habitual workload norms. Total distance (TD), high-metabolic load distance (HMLD), low-acceleration (LACC), high-acceleration (HACC), low-deceleration (LDEC), high-deceleration (HDEC), and very high-speed distance (VHSD) were all approximately 20% above habitual norms in the week before injury. Older and heavier players showed greater injury susceptibility. The reinjury rate was 68.7%, highlighting the recurrent nature of calf strains in this population.
tldr: Calf muscle strain injuries in professional soccer were preceded by approximately 20% above-normal GPS workloads across multiple variables in the prior week. Older, heavier players were at greater risk, and reinjury rates were very high (69%), underscoring the need for individualized load monitoring and targeted calf injury prevention programs.
methods: Prospective observational cohort; 41 male LaLiga professional soccer players; 2 competitive seasons; GPS-accelerometer devices (STATSports or equivalent) during all training and match sessions; 16 calf injuries identified via medical staff assessment; 7-day rolling workload window compared to individual habitual norms; GPS variables: TD, HMLD, LACC, HACC, LDEC, HDEC, VHSD; descriptive statistics and univariate comparisons between injury and non-injury weeks; pilot sample limits statistical power.
findings: All primary GPS variables were ~20% above each player's habitual norm in the 7-day window preceding calf injury. HMLD, HACC, and HDEC showed the largest relative elevations. Older players (>28 years) and heavier players had significantly more injuries. Reinjury rate was 68.7% (11 of 16 incidents were reinjuries). Match weeks had higher workloads than training-only weeks across all variables.
limitations: Pilot study with small injury sample (n=16 injuries); no control for match congestion, pitch type, or fatigue markers beyond GPS; individual habitual norm windows not standardized across players; no causal inference possible; positional grouping limited by sample size; GPS device models not fully standardized across two seasons.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should set individualized workload thresholds (~20% above habitual norms as a caution zone) for calf-intensive GPS variables, particularly HMLD, HACC, and HDEC. Players with prior calf injury history, older age, or higher body mass should receive heightened monitoring. Post-injury return plans must include structured calf loading to reduce the very high reinjury rate.
athleteDev: For football athletes, high-acceleration and high-deceleration volumes are particularly relevant calf injury risk signals. Monitoring weekly spikes in HACC and HDEC relative to individual baselines can help identify at-risk athletes before injury occurs. Older, more experienced players may need modified loading strategies during high-density training blocks.
rtp: Given the 68.7% reinjury rate for calf strains, RTP protocols must extend beyond symptom resolution to include structured progressive loading of calf tissues. GPS-based criteria for RTP should include normalization of HMLD, HACC, and HDEC relative to habitual norms before full training resumption. A graduated return to match-intensity external loads over 2–3 weeks post-clearance is strongly indicated.

citation: McMahon JJ, Jones PA, Suchomel TJ, Lake J, Comfort P. Influence of the reactive strength index modified on force– and power–time curves. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2018;13(4):534–541.
abstract: This study investigated how reactive strength index modified (RSImod) relates to force–time and power–time curve characteristics during the countermovement jump (CMJ) in 53 professional male rugby league players. Players were split into high-RSImod (top 20%, mean 0.53 m/s) and low-RSImod (bottom 20%, mean 0.27 m/s) groups. CMJs were performed on a Kistler force plate at 1000 Hz with temporal phase analysis (TPA) applied. High-RSImod players jumped higher (37.7 vs. 27.1 cm) with shorter time-to-takeoff (TTT: 0.707 vs. 0.812 s). Force, velocity, and power were greater throughout both eccentric and concentric phases in high-RSImod players. Concentric mean power explained 81% of variance in RSImod (R²=0.81), and eccentric mean power explained 66% (R²=0.66).
tldr: Athletes with high RSImod scores generate more force and power throughout the entire CMJ (both eccentric and concentric phases) and take off faster. Concentric power is the strongest correlate of RSImod, explaining 81% of its variance. RSImod is a robust summary metric that captures whole-jump neuromuscular quality.
methods: Cross-sectional; 53 professional male rugby league players; CMJ on Kistler 9286AA force platform at 1000 Hz; RSImod = jump height / time-to-takeoff; top and bottom 20% split (n≈10–11 per group); temporal phase analysis (TPA) dividing CMJ into unweighting, braking, propulsion, and flight phases; peak and mean force, velocity, power, and impulse computed per phase; Pearson correlations between RSImod and phase variables; independent t-tests between high/low groups; effect sizes (Cohen's d) reported.
findings: High-RSImod group: mean RSImod 0.53 m/s, jump height 37.7 cm, TTT 0.707 s. Low-RSImod group: mean RSImod 0.27 m/s, jump height 27.1 cm, TTT 0.812 s. High-RSImod athletes showed significantly greater eccentric peak force, eccentric mean power, concentric peak force, concentric mean power, and concentric mean velocity (all p<0.05, large effect sizes). Concentric mean power: R²=0.81 with RSImod. Eccentric mean power: R²=0.66 with RSImod. Braking impulse was not significantly different between groups.
limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference; rugby-specific sample limits generalizability to other sports; no female athletes; single testing session; no between-session reliability reported for TPA variables in this sample; top/bottom 20% split reduces sample size for group comparisons.
practicalImplications: RSImod is a simple, valid metric for characterizing CMJ neuromuscular quality. Practitioners can prioritize improving concentric power (e.g., via loaded jump training, Olympic lifting derivatives) to elevate RSImod. Athletes with low RSImod likely have deficits in both eccentric force application and concentric power expression, requiring targeted training across both phases.
athleteDev: Football athletes with low RSImod scores can be targeted for phase-specific CMJ training interventions. Eccentric overload training (e.g., flywheel, yielding isometrics) and concentric power development (hang power clean, jump squats) address the specific mechanical deficits associated with low RSImod. Tracking RSImod longitudinally provides a fast, reliable indicator of training adaptation.
rtp: RSImod from CMJ assessment provides a sensitive RTP monitoring metric following lower extremity injury. Comparison of RSImod to pre-injury baseline or normative values for the athlete's position identifies neuromuscular readiness gaps. The TPA breakdown allows practitioners to identify whether residual deficits are eccentric (braking) or concentric (propulsive) in nature, enabling targeted rehabilitation before clearance.

citation: Clements E, Ehrmann F, Clark A, Jones M, McCall A, Duffield R. Influence of travel demands and match load on recovery following postmatch travel in national-team footballers. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2025;20(3):412–420.
abstract: This study examined how postmatch travel demands and match external load affected recovery in 79 male international footballers across 258 trips over five years (2018–2023). STATSports Apex GPS devices monitored match load; perceptual wellness questionnaires assessed fatigue, muscle soreness, sleep quality, and recovery at match day (MD), MD+1, and MD+3. Travel was classified as transition (club to national team) or return-to-club. Return-to-club travel was associated with significantly greater fatigue (+0.491 AU) and worse recovery (−0.629 AU) compared to transition travel. Eastward travel direction impaired sleep quality at MD+1 and MD+3. Longer travel duration reduced MD+3 recovery. Later departure time (>6 PM) was associated with worse MD+3 sleep. Higher match external load (total distance, high-speed running) did not significantly interact with travel type for most recovery outcomes.
tldr: Return-to-club travel after international matches is substantially harder on recovery than transition travel, with eastward flights, longer durations, and late departures compounding fatigue and sleep disruption. Match load alone did not drive recovery; travel logistics are independently important targets for optimization in national-team management.
methods: Prospective observational cohort; 79 male international footballers; 258 travel trips, 2018–2023; STATSports Apex GPS for match external load (TD, HMLD, HSR, sprinting distance); perceptual wellness questionnaires (Hooper Index items) at MD, MD+1, MD+3; travel variables: direction (east/west), duration (hours), departure time (early/late); mixed-effects models with player as random effect; travel type (transition vs. return) as primary predictor; match load variables as covariates.
findings: Return-to-club travel: fatigue +0.491 AU higher (p<0.05), recovery −0.629 AU worse vs. transition. Eastward travel: significantly worse sleep at MD+1 and MD+3 (p<0.05). Travel duration >6 h: MD+3 recovery significantly reduced. Late departure (>6 PM): MD+3 sleep quality significantly worse. Match external load (TD, HSR) did not significantly moderate recovery outcomes when travel variables were in the model. Muscle soreness trajectories did not differ by travel type.
limitations: Observational design; no randomization of travel conditions; wellness data self-reported (perceptual); GPS data from matches only (no training data between trips); heterogeneous fixture schedules across 5 years; no objective sleep measures (actigraphy); confounding from individual differences in jet lag susceptibility not fully modeled.
practicalImplications: National team staff and club practitioners should coordinate travel logistics to minimize return-to-club travel burden: prioritize westward routing where possible, schedule earlier departures, and shorten layover durations. MD+1 and MD+3 training loads should be adjusted based on travel direction and duration, not only perceived fatigue or match load.
athleteDev: For football players participating in international competitions, travel logistics between club and national team assignments represent a modifiable recovery stressor. Clubs receiving players back from eastward international travel should implement sleep hygiene protocols and reduce MD+3 training intensity proactively. Tracking individual travel logs alongside wellness data enables more personalized recovery management.
rtp: International travel demands can extend effective recovery timelines beyond what match load alone would predict. Players returning from long-haul or eastward international duty may have suppressed readiness at MD+3 even if feeling symptomatically recovered. RTP or return-to-full-training decisions after international windows should incorporate travel direction and duration as contextual modifiers alongside standard wellness and load metrics.

citation: Weber ML, Dean JL, Hoffman NL, Broglio SP, McCrea M, McAllister TW, Schmidt JD, CARE Consortium Investigators. Influences of Mental Illness, Current Psychological State, and Concussion History on Baseline Concussion Assessment Performance. The American Journal of Sports Medicine. 2018;1-10.
abstract: This cross-sectional study from the NCAA-DoD CARE Consortium examined how mental illness history, current psychological state, and concussion history influence baseline concussion assessment performance in 8,652 collegiate student-athletes. Baseline assessments included the SCAT symptom checklist, Standardized Assessment of Concussion (SAC), ImPACT, Balance Error Scoring System (BESS), and the Brief Symptom Inventory-18 (BSI-18). Student-athletes with trait anxiety (n=59), depression (n=283), or comorbid anxiety with depression (n=68) reported significantly higher symptom counts, symptom severity, and psychological distress on BSI-18 subscores compared to those without these conditions. A moderate positive correlation was found between BSI-18 subscores and total symptom number and severity (rs=0.41-0.45, P<.001). Athletes with 4 or more prior concussions demonstrated significantly greater state anxiety, depression, and somatization on the BSI-18 compared to those with fewer concussions, indicating a dose-response relationship between concussion history and psychological distress.
tldr: Athletes with anxiety, depression, or comorbid anxiety-depression report more concussion symptoms and greater psychological distress at baseline than healthy peers — and this worsens with each additional concussion. Applied sport science staff must account for mental health history when interpreting baseline and post-injury concussion assessment scores, as these athletes may never match typical normative values. The BSI-18 is a practical tool for detecting psychological distress during baseline testing, and athletes with 4+ prior concussions warrant proactive monitoring and potential mental health referral.
methods: Cross-sectional study of 8,652 collegiate student-athletes (54.5% male, 45.5% female; mean age 19.4 years) from 22 NCAA-DoD CARE Consortium sites (January 2014 to September 2016). Baseline assessments included: SCAT symptom checklist (22-item Likert scale), SAC (orientation, immediate memory, concentration, delayed memory), ImPACT (verbal memory, visual memory, visual motor speed, reaction time), BESS (6 stances on firm/foam), and BSI-18 (state anxiety, depression, somatization subscores; 4-point Likert items rated over past 7 days). Trait mental illness was self-reported via physician-diagnosed history of anxiety and/or depression. Concussion history was self-reported (0, 1, 2, 3, 4+ concussions). Non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis tests and Mann-Whitney follow-up tests compared groups; Spearman rho correlations assessed psychological state-performance relationships; Cohen d effect sizes calculated. Reliable Change Indices (RCIs) were used to contextualize clinical meaningfulness.
findings: Athletes with trait anxiety, depression, or comorbid anxiety-depression had significantly higher SCAT symptom counts (mean 4.3-5.4 vs. 2.5) and symptom severity (8.1-12.4 vs. 4.1) compared to those without mental illness (P<.001; Cohen d=0.53-1.19). BSI-18 anxiety, depression, and somatization subscores were elevated in all mental illness groups (P<.001; Cohen d up to 1.61). Only individuals with trait anxiety showed significantly worse ImPACT visual memory (d=0.53) and visual motor speed (d=0.36) versus controls. Moderate positive correlations existed between all BSI-18 subscores and total symptom number (rs=0.42-0.45, P<.001) and severity (rs=0.41-0.45, P<.001). Students with 4+ prior concussions had significantly higher BSI-18 anxiety, depression, and somatization vs. all other concussion history groups (P=.003-.001). SCAT symptom severity RCI was exceeded between those with depression vs. no mental illness (mean difference 6.3 vs. RCI of 4), indicating clinically meaningful differences.
limitations: BSI-18 was not developed for athletic populations; some somatization items may be confounded by physical training fatigue. BSI-18 has fair-to-poor test-retest reliability due to its state measurement nature. Mental illness diagnosis and concussion history were self-reported without medical record verification; medication use was not recorded. Large sample size may have led to oversampling, producing statistically significant but clinically weak findings. Preconcussion psychological state was not assessed, preventing causal inference. Family history of psychiatric illness was not examined.
practicalImplications: Clinicians should not compare post-injury scores of athletes with anxiety or depression to population normative data, as these athletes may have systematically elevated baselines. BSI-18 administered at baseline can identify athletes in acute psychological distress who may need delayed neurocognitive testing. Athletes with 4+ prior concussions should be routinely screened for psychological distress and considered for mental health referral. Mental health history should be collected during pre-participation exams and factored into concussion management protocols.
athleteDev: Football athletes with diagnosed anxiety or depression may present with elevated symptom scores and greater psychological distress at baseline — not necessarily reflecting a concussion. Identifying these athletes early allows performance staff to contextualize neurocognitive baselines appropriately and build individualized monitoring plans. The link between cumulative concussion history and increasing psychological distress has implications for long-term monitoring of high-contact athletes throughout their careers.
rtp: When determining return-to-play after concussion, clinicians must account for a football athlete's pre-existing mental health history. Athletes with trait anxiety, depression, or comorbid conditions may not achieve normative symptom thresholds even when fully recovered, making individual baseline comparison (rather than normative data) essential. For athletes with 4+ prior concussions showing elevated BSI-18 distress scores, return-to-play decisions should incorporate mental health evaluation to ensure distress is not prolonging reported symptom burden.

citation: Weber ML, Dean JL, Hoffman NL, Broglio SP, McCrea M, McAllister TW, Schmidt JD, CARE Consortium Investigators. Influences of Mental Illness, Current Psychological State, and Concussion History on Baseline Concussion Assessment Performance. The American Journal of Sports Medicine. 2018;1-10.
abstract: NOTE: This is a duplicate of ID 329 (same paper, cleaner filename). This cross-sectional study from the NCAA-DoD CARE Consortium examined how mental illness history, current psychological state, and concussion history influence baseline concussion assessment performance in 8,652 collegiate student-athletes. Baseline assessments included the SCAT symptom checklist, SAC, ImPACT, BESS, and BSI-18. Concussion history was self-reported (0, 1, 2, 3, 4+). Non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis and Mann-Whitney tests compared groups; Spearman rho correlations assessed psychological state-performance relationships; Cohen d effect sizes and RCIs calculated.

Athletes with 4+ prior concussions had significantly higher BSI-18 anxiety, depression, and somatization vs. all other groups.

Mental illness diagnosis and concussion history were self-reported without medical record verification; medication use was not recorded. Large sample size may have produced statistically significant but clinically weak findings. Preconcussion psychological state was not assessed, preventing causal inference.

BSI-18 at baseline can identify athletes in acute psychological distress who may need delayed neurocognitive testing. Athletes with 4+ prior concussions should be routinely screened for psychological distress and considered for mental health referral.

Identifying these athletes early allows performance staff to contextualize neurocognitive baselines appropriately and build individualized monitoring plans.

Athletes with trait anxiety, depression, or comorbid conditions may not achieve normative symptom thresholds even when fully recovered, making individual baseline comparison essential. citation: Vella JD, Facer-Childs ER, Ogden J, Liddle S, Engelman A, Volchek I, King K. Influences on the mental health and well-being of retired professional athletes from high contact team sports: a mixed methods systematic review. British Journal of Sports Medicine. 2025;0:1-11.
abstract: This mixed-methods systematic review (PROSPERO CRD42023449114) synthesized 85 studies comprising 53,996 participants from six high contact team sports (NFL, CFL, AFL, football/soccer, ice hockey, rugby) to report prevalence of mental health symptoms and influencing factors in retired professional athletes. Prevalence ranged from 0.9% (smoking) to 68.8% (adverse alcohol use), with depression 3%-49% and anxiety 4.3%-42% across studies. Self-reported scores for depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbance were generally within normal/minimal ranges on validated scales, while mild or higher levels of stress and adverse alcohol use were observed. Key negative influencing factors included concussion history, pain, involuntary retirement, injury, neurological factors, and declining physical function. Both positive and negative effects were found for athletic identity, psychosocial support, retirement autonomy, life events, osteoarthritis, and cognitive function. The review found major underrepresentation of female athletes (0.46% of participants).
tldr: Retired professional football and rugby athletes face a complex landscape of mental health challenges, with adverse alcohol use being the most prevalent problem and depression and anxiety typically clustering within normal ranges on validated scales. Concussion history, chronic pain, involuntary retirement, and physical decline are the strongest drivers of poor mental health outcomes. Sport science and medical staff should advocate for robust transition planning, regular post-retirement check-ins, alumni support networks, and mental health literacy programs for players approaching and entering retirement.
methods: Mixed-methods systematic review following PRISMA 2020 guidelines registered on PROSPERO (CRD42023449114). Five databases (PsycINFO, Embase, Medline, SPORTDiscus, Scopus) searched July 2023 and March 2025 with no year/language restrictions. Included peer-reviewed studies of retired professional high-contact team sport athletes investigating mental health or influencing factors. Quality assessment: NIH tools for quantitative, CASP for qualitative. Two independent reviewers for abstract screening, full-text review, quality assessment, and data extraction. Quantitative data: prevalence, means/SDs, odds ratios, risk ratios, prevalence ratios. Qualitative data: themes and direct quotes. Meta-analysis not possible due to heterogeneity; narrative synthesis used.
findings: 85 studies included (86% quantitative, 12% qualitative, 2% mixed methods); 48% rated good quality. Prevalence: depression 3%-49%, anxiety 4.3%-42%, adverse alcohol use 6.4%-68.8%, stress 8.7%-26.9%, sleep disturbance 11%-35%, opioid use 7%-23.6%. Average scores for depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbance were within normal ranges; stress and alcohol use showed mild or higher levels. Concussion history negatively impacted mental health in 17/23 studies; undiagnosed concussions were strongest predictor of opioid misuse (aOR=4.25). Rate of physical function decline was the strongest longitudinal predictor of depression over 19 years. Involuntary retirement associated with higher distress (OR=2.10) and comorbid mental disorders (OR=1.89). Pain predicted depression (OR=1.38-3.5) and opioid use (OR=4.5). 3+ injuries associated with opioid use (OR=6.02).
limitations: Most included studies were cross-sectional with self-report measures, preventing causal inference. Study heterogeneity precluded meta-analysis. Female athletes were severely underrepresented (0.46% of all participants). Self-reported concussion and injury history introduces recall bias. Plethora of different measurement tools makes cross-study comparisons difficult. Limited longitudinal data reduces certainty about temporal associations. Most studies involved only males from North American professional sports.
practicalImplications: Sporting organizations should establish regular post-retirement check-ins and promote alumni social/support networks. Athletes approaching retirement should develop identities outside sport, plan professionally, and establish post-retirement daily routines. Mindfulness-based stress reduction programs have demonstrated efficacy in reducing stress, anxiety, and depression in retired athletes. Concussion history and physical decline should trigger proactive mental health screening. Opioid use monitoring is particularly warranted for retired athletes with high concussion counts and injury histories.
athleteDev: Football programs should integrate life skills and identity development training during athletes' active careers to reduce post-retirement identity crises and mental health vulnerability. Athletic identity — while valuable during play — can become a liability if it is the athlete's sole source of self-worth. Programs that develop athletes as whole persons (educational, social, vocational) can buffer against adverse mental health outcomes commonly seen after retirement from high-contact sports.
rtp: While this review focuses on retired athletes, its findings have direct RTP implications: the mental health consequences of repeated concussions, chronic pain, and declining physical function documented post-retirement suggest these factors require proactive management during active careers. Returning players with high concussion burdens or chronic pain to play without comprehensive mental health support may accelerate the negative trajectories documented in retired cohorts.

citation: Monseau AJ, Balcik BJ, Roberts L, Andrews R, Sharon MJ. Initially concealed concussion lowers in-game performance of NCAA Division I football players: A case series. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2020. DOI: 10.1080/00913847.2020.1763145.
abstract: This case series reported three NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision players who sustained sport-related concussions during games but did not immediately disclose their injuries. Concussion incidents were identified via video review, and in-game performance grades (PGs) scored by position coaches (0-100% scale) were compared before and after the concussive event within the same game. Player 1 (defensive lineman) dropped from 76 to 47 (-38%); Player 2 (defensive skill) dropped from 100 to 60 (-40%); Player 3 (offensive skill) dropped from 67 to 43 (-36%). All three also showed worsened SCAT-3 scores when formally evaluated. The authors propose that framing concussion-related performance decline in quantifiable team-relevant terms may motivate athletes and coaches to prioritize disclosure.
tldr: When football players sustain a concussion and hide it to stay in the game, their measurable in-game performance drops approximately 36-40% — meaning they are actively hurting the team, not helping it. This case series provides a compelling, coach-friendly argument for why concussion disclosure is in everyone's best interest. Sport science staff and athletic trainers can use this framing when educating athletes and coaches about the real-game costs of playing through concussion symptoms.
methods: Case series of three NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision players who did not report concussions immediately. Concussion plays were identified via video review. Primary outcome: performance grade (PG) — a 0-100% score generated by position coaches within 24 hours post-game based on correct pre-snap alignment, in-play execution, and action during the play. Pre-concussion and post-concussion PGs were compared within the same game to control for opponent quality, weather, and game scheme variables. Percent change from pre-concussion PG was calculated. Secondary outcomes: SCAT-3 components (symptom count/severity, SAC, balance, Maddocks score) compared to pre-season baselines. Coaches completed grading before being approached by study team to prevent bias.
findings: All three players demonstrated marked within-game performance declines post-concussion. Player 1 (defensive lineman, 2 prior concussions): pre-injury PG 76, post-injury PG 47, 38% drop; season average 75 (range 63-82). Player 2 (defensive skill, 1 prior concussion): pre-injury PG 100, post-injury PG 60, 40% drop; season average 89 (range 80-96). Player 3 (offensive skill, 0 prior concussions): pre-injury PG 67, post-injury PG 43, 36% drop. SCAT-3 post-injury vs. baseline: Player 1 showed 18/22 symptoms (baseline 6) and severity 64 (baseline 12); Player 3 showed 17/22 symptoms and severity 42 one week later with SAC dropping from 29 to 21. Recovery ranged from 6 days (Player 2) to 6 weeks (Player 3).
limitations: Only three cases with small post-injury play samples (especially Player 2 with only 5 post-injury plays). Position coach in Case 2 may have been biased by their health assessment of the player. Fatigue effects on later-game performance not fully controlled. Performance grading is not a scientifically validated instrument and grading criteria vary between position coaches. Cannot generalize findings from three cases. Selection bias toward more severe or detectable concussions. No matched controls or comparison group.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and team physicians should use performance-decline data as a practical education tool with players and coaches to reframe concussion disclosure: playing through a concussion does not help the team — it costs the team approximately 1 in 3 correct plays. Position coaches observing unusual player behavior should be empowered to alert medical staff. Sideline protocols should include coach-initiated removal triggers in addition to medical staff identification.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, this case series underscores that cognitive impairment from concussion directly compromises on-field execution — alignment, read, and action — the fundamental skills developed through practice. Coaches and performance staff can use this evidence to build a culture of transparency around brain injuries, framing disclosure not as weakness but as protecting individual performance and team outcomes. Pre-season concussion education should include position-specific performance data demonstrating objective game-level costs.
rtp: This case series provides objective performance-based evidence that a football player competing with an undisclosed concussion is substantially impaired in ways detectable by position coaches. For RTP decision-making, these findings reinforce current protocols requiring removal at time of suspected injury: delayed removal (as in Player 3, who waited 1 week to disclose) resulted in the longest recovery (6 weeks). Immediate removal from activity after concussion is associated with shorter recovery, and this study illustrates the real-game performance cost of non-compliance.

citation: Krystofiak J. Injection of Amniotic Membrane and Umbilical Cord Particulate for Muscle and Ligament Tears in Collegiate Football Athletes: A Single-Center, Retrospective Study. Orthopedic Research and Reviews. 2024;16:295-301.
abstract: This single-center, retrospective chart review evaluated amniotic membrane and umbilical cord (AM/UC) powder injection (Clarix Flo, 50 or 100 mg) as an adjunctive treatment for acute muscle and ligament tears in 10 consecutive Division I college football players treated between January 2022 and May 2023. Injuries included MCL tears (n=3), hamstring tears (n=6), and abdominal muscle avulsions (n=1); injury grades ranged from partial (grade 2) to complete (grade 3). All players received standard athletic training, physical therapy, activity modification, and NSAIDs alongside the AM/UC injection delivered under ultrasound guidance within 5 days of injury. Athletes achieved full return to play at an average of 29.6 +/- 15.2 days post-injury (range 16-69), with 80% returning within 4 weeks and no reinjuries or complications during 6 months of follow-up.
tldr: A novel placenta-derived biologic injection (amniotic membrane/umbilical cord powder) used alongside standard rehabilitation enabled 80% of collegiate football players with hamstring, MCL, and abdominal muscle tears to return to full play within 4 weeks, averaging about 30 days total — comparable to or faster than corticosteroid and PRP injection benchmarks, with zero reinjuries or complications over 6 months. Sports medicine staff should be aware of this emerging regenerative option, particularly for higher-grade muscle and ligament injuries where corticosteroids are contraindicated.
methods: Retrospective, single-center chart review of consecutive Division I college football players treated at Orthopedics and Sports Medicine, Livingston, NJ, January 2022 to May 2023. Inclusion: age 18+, active Division I football, traumatic injury, AM/UC injection treatment, 6 months follow-up, no prior injury within 1 year. Injury severity classified by modified Peetrons grading (0-3). All patients received athletic training modalities, physical therapy, activity modification, Indomethacin 75mg ER for 7 days, and either 50 mg or 100 mg AM/UC powder (Clarix Flo) reconstituted in 1% lidocaine, injected under ultrasound guidance within 5 days of injury. Primary outcome: time to full return to play (days). Secondary outcomes: time to limited RTP, proportion returning within 4 weeks, reinjury rate at 6 months, complications. Spearman rank correlation used for variable associations; p<0.05 significant.
findings: 10 athletes included: MCL tears (n=3), hamstring tears (n=6), abdominal avulsions (n=1). Average age 19.8 years; BMI 28.5 kg/m2; positions: wide receiver (3), offensive line (3), linebacker (1), running back (1), defensive line (1), defensive back (1). 60% had grade 2 partial tears; 20% grade 2-3; 20% grade 3 complete tears. Limited return to play: 19 +/- 5.7 days (range 10-29). Full return to play: 29.6 +/- 15.2 days (range 16-69); 80% within 4 weeks. MCL tears: full RTP 26.7 +/- 10.3 days. Hamstring tears: full RTP 33.3 +/- 17.8 days. Abdominal avulsion: full RTP day 16. Injury grade, BMI, and AM/UC dose were not significantly correlated with time to RTP (p=0.44, 0.22, 0.18). No reinjuries or complications during 6-month follow-up.
limitations: Small sample size (n=10) and absence of a control group limit causal conclusions. Retrospective design with heterogeneous injury grades, locations, and AM/UC doses. No randomization or blinding. Single center and single provider limits generalizability. Author disclosed speaker relationship with BioTissue (manufacturer of Clarix Flo), representing a potential conflict of interest. Historical comparison groups from literature used rather than concurrent controls. Prospective, randomized, blinded studies are needed.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine staff treating collegiate football players with acute hamstring, MCL, or abdominal muscle tears may consider AM/UC injection as an adjunctive option to standard rehabilitation, particularly for higher-grade injuries where corticosteroids are contraindicated and PRP evidence is inconsistent. The 80% within-4-weeks RTP rate and zero reinjury rate over 6 months are encouraging, though larger controlled trials are needed before broad adoption.
athleteDev: For football athlete development programs, reducing time-loss from muscle and ligament injuries is critical to maintaining development continuity and minimizing detraining. AM/UC injection as an adjunctive treatment to standard rehabilitation represents a potentially valuable tool for accelerating recovery from common football injuries (hamstring, MCL) while reducing re-injury risk. If validated in larger trials, this approach could enable athletes to return to full participation faster than current standard-of-care timelines.
rtp: This study directly addresses return-to-play timelines for three of the most common football injuries: hamstring tears, MCL sprains/tears, and abdominal muscle avulsions. With 80% of players returning within 4 weeks and no reinjuries over 6 months, AM/UC injection combined with standard rehabilitation produced RTP timelines competitive with corticosteroid injection (28-day average) and PRP injection (20-42 days), while being applicable to higher-grade injuries where corticosteroids are contraindicated. Football medical staff should track this emerging treatment option as a potential RTP optimization strategy for Grade 2-3 muscle and ligament injuries.

citation: Cosio PL, Moreno-Simonet L, Fernández D, Lloret M, Padulles X, Padulles JM, Farran-Codina A, Rodas G, Cadefau JA. Football (soccer) match-derived hamstring muscles residual fatigue can be monitored using early rate of torque development. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2024. doi:10.1007/s00421-024-05694-x
abstract: This study investigated whether early rate of torque development (RTD) is more sensitive than maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC) torque for detecting post-match hamstring fatigue in soccer players. Nineteen male regional first-division soccer players (age 20.9±2.0 years) underwent repeated measures assessments at pre-match (MD), MD+24h, MD+48h, and MD+72h using an isometric prone curl test (IPC; 90° hip, 20° knee flexion). RTD was measured at 0–50 ms (RTD0-50), 0–100 ms (RTD0-100), 0–250 ms (RTD0-250), and peak RTD. Serum biomarkers assessed concurrently included creatine kinase (CK), sarcomeric mitochondrial CK (sMtCK), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), malondialdehyde (MDA), and irisin. Results showed that while MVIC torque recovered to baseline by MD+72h, early RTD indices did not: RTD0-50 showed a large significant decrement (p<0.001, g=−1.24) and RTD0-100 remained depressed (p<0.001, g=−1.06) at MD+72h. All serum biomarkers remained elevated through MD+72h, indicating ongoing muscle damage and inflammation beyond the point of perceived MVIC recovery. RTD0-250 and RTDpeak recovered by MD+72h. The study concludes that early RTD windows (0–50 ms and 0–100 ms) detect residual neuromuscular fatigue that MVIC torque misses, and that 72 hours is insufficient for complete hamstring recovery following a competitive soccer match.
tldr: Standard isometric strength testing (MVIC) suggests hamstrings recover within 72 hours after a soccer match, but early rate of torque development (RTD0-50 and RTD0-100) reveals that explosive force capacity remains significantly impaired at MD+72h. This means players may appear 'strong' on conventional strength tests while still carrying meaningful neuromuscular fatigue. Serum biomarkers corroborate ongoing muscle stress. For return-to-play and load management decisions, RTD-based testing provides a more sensitive window into true hamstring readiness than MVIC alone.
methods: Single-group repeated measures design; n=19 male regional first-division soccer players (age 20.9±2.0y, height 178.3±5.5cm, mass 73.4±7.6kg). Isometric prone curl (IPC) test at 90° hip flexion and 20° knee flexion using a custom dynamometer. RTD computed over 0–50 ms, 0–100 ms, 0–250 ms intervals and peak RTD. Assessments at pre-match (MD), MD+24h, MD+48h, MD+72h. Venous blood draws at same time points for CK, sMtCK, ALT, AST, MDA, and irisin. Statistical analysis: repeated measures ANOVA with Hedges' g effect sizes; significance set at p<0.05.
findings: RTD0-50 was significantly reduced at all post-match time points versus baseline and remained unrecovered at MD+72h (p<0.001, g=−1.24). RTD0-100 similarly unrecovered at MD+72h (p<0.001, g=−1.06). RTD0-250 and RTDpeak recovered by MD+72h. MVIC torque recovered by MD+72h. CK, sMtCK, ALT, AST, MDA, and irisin were all elevated through MD+72h. Early RTD indices (0–50 ms and 0–100 ms) were significantly more sensitive markers of residual hamstring fatigue than MVIC torque, detecting neuromuscular deficits that MVIC testing did not capture.
limitations: Small sample (n=19) from a single regional division; all male; no control group; individual match demands not controlled; no imaging to confirm absence of structural injury; ecological validity limited as testing context differs from in-game contractions; reliability of IPC test at very early RTD windows not independently validated in this population.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should not rely solely on MVIC torque to clear hamstrings post-match; RTD0-50 and RTD0-100 provide a more sensitive readiness marker. A 72-hour recovery window is likely insufficient for full explosive hamstring capacity restoration. Incorporating early RTD assessment into weekly monitoring can better inform training load decisions, particularly regarding sprint and high-intensity work scheduled 3 days post-match. Serum biomarkers (CK, sMtCK) can complement RTD data as objective indicators of ongoing muscle stress.
athleteDev: For football players at all levels, this research underscores that hamstring explosive capacity — critical for sprinting and change of direction — lags behind maximal strength recovery after high-demand competition. Athlete development programs should include RTD-sensitive testing tools and build sufficient recovery windows (>72h) before high-velocity sprint sessions following competitive play. Monitoring early RTD across a season may help identify cumulative fatigue patterns before injury risk escalates.
rtp: Following hamstring strain injury, RTD0-50 and RTD0-100 indices should be incorporated alongside MVIC assessments in return-to-play criteria. An athlete demonstrating full MVIC recovery but deficient early RTD has not fully restored explosive neuromuscular function, which is directly implicated in re-injury during sprinting. Serum biomarkers provide a complementary systemic indicator of ongoing muscle damage. Clearance decisions should include early RTD symmetry thresholds, not only strength-based measures.

citation: de Oliveira DM, Silva AKAC, Macedo AG, Fernandes MB, Fernandes EV. Global research trends in sports nutrition and football over the last 20 years (2004–2024). Sports. 2025;13(10):365. doi:10.3390/sports13100365
abstract: This scientometric analysis mapped the global landscape of sports nutrition research applied to football (soccer) from 2004 to 2024. The authors retrieved 526 original articles from Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus, analyzing publication outputs, citations, author contributions, institutional and country affiliations, journal distribution, and keyword co-occurrence networks using Bibliometrix v5.0.1 and VOSviewer v1.6.20. Publication volume grew by approximately 1,450% over the study period. The UK was the dominant contributing country (46.6% of publications), with Morton J and Maughan R identified as the most prolific authors. The journal Nutrients accounted for 29.6% of all publications. Keyword trend analysis revealed temporal shifts in research focus: early work (2004–2014) concentrated on hydration and carbohydrate intake, while more recent literature (2015–2024) has pivoted toward low energy availability (LEA), polyphenols, antioxidants, and gut microbiome. Emerging and underexplored topics include oxidative stress, beta-alanine supplementation, and the consequences of LEA in football populations. The findings provide a structured overview of where football nutrition science has been and where productive research gaps remain.
tldr: A 20-year bibliometric analysis of football sports nutrition research reveals explosive growth in the field (1,450% increase in publications), a dominant UK research presence, and a clear thematic shift from hydration/carbohydrate focus toward low energy availability, polyphenols, and gut health. Practitioners benefit from knowing which nutritional topics have strong evidence bases (hydration, CHO) versus emerging areas requiring more research (LEA, beta-alanine, oxidative stress). This map helps prioritize evidence-based nutrition strategies while flagging where caution and continued monitoring of new literature are warranted.
methods: Scientometric analysis. Database search: Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus; 526 original articles; date range 2004–2024; search terms related to sports nutrition AND football/soccer. Bibliometric tools: Bibliometrix v5.0.1 (R package) for performance analysis and science mapping; VOSviewer v1.6.20 for keyword co-occurrence network visualization. Analyses included: annual publication trends, citation metrics, h-index by author, country and institutional collaboration networks, source journal analysis, keyword trend mapping (time-sliced co-occurrence).
findings: Publication volume increased ~1,450% from 2004 to 2024. The UK contributed 46.6% of all publications; Morton J and Maughan R were the most prolific individual authors. Nutrients was the top publishing journal (29.6% of articles). Early-period dominant keywords: hydration, carbohydrate, energy. Late-period emerging keywords: low energy availability, polyphenols, antioxidants, gut microbiome. Underexplored topics with low publication density but rising interest: oxidative stress, beta-alanine, LEA consequences in male and female football players. Co-authorship networks revealed concentrated UK–European collaboration clusters with limited South American and Asian representation despite large football populations in those regions.
limitations: Scientometric analyses describe publication patterns, not evidence quality; high-volume research areas may not reflect the highest-impact interventions. Keyword co-occurrence reflects author-chosen terminology which can vary across regions and time. Restricting to three databases may exclude regional journals. The 20-year window captures maturation of the field but cannot assess causality of trend drivers (e.g., whether publication growth reflects practitioner demand or funding availability).
practicalImplications: Applied sport scientists and nutritionists working in football can use this landscape map to identify the robustness of evidence underpinning common practices (hydration and CHO protocols have decades of support) versus newer strategies (LEA management, polyphenol supplementation) that require more individualized and cautious application. Awareness of underexplored topics (beta-alanine, oxidative stress) flags areas where practitioners should seek emerging trial data rather than relying on established protocols from other sports.
athleteDev: For long-term athlete development in football, the evidence landscape suggests carbohydrate periodization and hydration protocols remain the bedrock nutritional interventions. Emerging research on LEA is particularly relevant for youth and female athletes, where energy balance monitoring should be integrated into development programs. Polyphenol and antioxidant strategies show growing research interest, warranting consideration for recovery optimization in development pipelines.
rtp: Nutritional support during return-to-play is an area with limited specific research identified in this analysis. The established literature on carbohydrate and protein intake for muscle repair provides the primary evidence base. Practitioners should monitor emerging LEA and antioxidant/polyphenol literature for applications in injury recovery and tissue healing protocols, recognizing these areas are still accumulating evidence.

citation: Luxenburg D, Wasserman N, Geller J, Perez J, Burke J, Kaplan L. Hamstring injuries in the National Football League: An epidemiological study. J Orthop. 2022. doi:10.1016/j.jor.2022.11.010
abstract: This retrospective epidemiological study characterized the incidence, position-specific risk, surface type associations, and recurrence patterns of hamstring injuries in the NFL across the 2013–2016 regular seasons. Using official NFL gamebooks, the authors identified 78 documented hamstring injuries across 64 regular-season games per season. Standardized incidence ratios (SIR) were calculated by position group to determine relative risk compared to the overall population. Linebackers (LB) demonstrated the highest SIR (2.02), followed by defensive backs (DB, SIR=1.62), while offensive linemen (OL, SIR=0.28) and defensive linemen (DL, SIR=0.29) were at substantially lower risk. The majority of injuries (57.7%) occurred on artificial turf, though this difference did not reach statistical significance (p=0.082). No effect of short rest (e.g., Thursday games) on injury rate was identified (p=0.959). Mean injury week was 8.05 (median 7.5), suggesting a mid-season concentration. A recurrence rate of 10.3% was observed within the study period. The study highlights that speed/skill positions with high sprint demands carry the greatest hamstring injury risk in professional American football.
tldr: In the NFL from 2013–2016, hamstring injuries disproportionately affect linebackers (2× expected rate) and defensive backs (1.6× expected rate), reflecting the high-speed sprint demands of those positions.
