Offensive and defensive linemen have markedly lower risk. Injuries cluster in mid-season (around week 8) and recur in roughly 1 in 10 cases. Artificial turf did not significantly increase risk in this sample. These patterns have direct relevance for position-specific screening, load management, and targeted prevention programming in American football.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological analysis. Data source: official NFL gamebooks from the 2013–2016 regular seasons; 78 hamstring injuries identified across 256 regular-season games per season. Standardized incidence ratios (SIR) calculated per position group (QB, RB, WR, TE, OL, LB, DB, DL) with 95% confidence intervals. Chi-squared tests used to compare injury rates on natural grass versus artificial turf. Z-test used to compare injury rates in short-rest game weeks (Thursday following Sunday) versus standard rest. Recurrence defined as same-player same-injury type within the study period.
findings: 78 hamstring injuries documented across 2013–2016 regular seasons. LB SIR=2.02 (highest among position groups); DB SIR=1.62; WR and RB moderate SIRs; OL SIR=0.28; DL SIR=0.29. 57.7% of injuries on artificial turf vs. 42.3% on natural grass (p=0.082, not significant). No significant short-rest effect (p=0.959). Mean injury week 8.05 (median 7.5). Recurrence rate 10.3% within study period. Position groups with high sprint and change-of-direction demands showed the greatest injury burden.
limitations: Gamebooks may underreport injuries that do not result in game absence; injuries resulting only in practice missed would not be captured. Study period (2013–2016) may not reflect current injury trends due to rule changes, training evolution, and surface upgrades. Sample limited to regular season; preseason and postseason data excluded. No severity grading (MRI-based classification) available from gamebook records. Confounding variables (age, prior injury history, training load) not controlled.
practicalImplications: NFL strength and conditioning and sports medicine staff should prioritize hamstring injury prevention resources toward linebackers and defensive backs, who face roughly 2× and 1.6× the expected injury rate respectively. Mid-season monitoring (weeks 7–10) warrants heightened vigilance given injury clustering at this time point. The 10.3% recurrence rate underscores the importance of structured return-to-sport criteria and post-clearance monitoring. Turf type showed a non-significant trend toward higher injury rates that may warrant further investigation with larger samples.
athleteDev: For American football player development, position-specific sprint and acceleration profiles should drive hamstring training and screening priorities. Linebackers and defensive backs — who execute the most high-velocity sprints in game conditions — require robust hamstring strength, eccentric capacity, and neuromuscular control programming throughout development. Early-career load monitoring during the mid-season phase (weeks 7–10) may help reduce primary injury incidence at peak risk windows.
rtp: Given the 10.3% within-season recurrence rate identified in this NFL sample, return-to-play protocols for hamstring injuries in American football must include recurrence prevention as a primary endpoint. Position-specific sprint demands — particularly for LBs and DBs — should inform functional clearance criteria. Rehabilitation should progress to position-specific high-speed running and cutting before game clearance. Ongoing load monitoring post-RTP is warranted through the mid-season period when re-injury risk is historically concentrated.

citation: Valente HG, de Oliveira RR, Baroni BM. How are hamstring strain injuries managed in elite men's football clubs? A survey with 62 Brazilian physical therapists. Phys Ther Sport. 2023;61:73–81. doi:10.1016/j.ptsp.2023.03.001
abstract: This cross-sectional survey examined hamstring strain injury (HSI) assessment and rehabilitation practices among physical therapists (PTs) at elite Brazilian men's football clubs. Sixty-two PTs from 35 of 40 eligible clubs (87.5% representativeness) from Brazil's Series A and B divisions (2021 season) completed a 30-question online questionnaire covering injury classification, imaging use, rehabilitation structure, therapeutic modalities, and return-to-play (RTP) criteria. All 62 respondents (100%) used imaging for HSI assessment and rated it as 'important' or 'very important.' Injury classification was universally applied, with 45.2% using the Munich Consensus Classification. Rehabilitation programs were most commonly structured in 3–4 phases. Universal or near-universal adoption was observed for electrophysical agents (100%), strengthening exercises (98.4%, with 93.5% using eccentric protocols), manual therapy (96.8%), football-specific exercises (95.2%), and lumbopelvic stabilization (93.5%). Muscle strength was rated the most important RTP criterion by 71% of respondents. The survey reveals high convergence around core HSI management practices in elite Brazilian football, with eccentric strengthening and imaging-guided classification forming the bedrock of contemporary rehabilitation.
tldr: Among 62 physical therapists managing hamstring injuries at Brazil's top professional football clubs, there is near-universal consensus on the core elements of treatment: imaging for all cases, injury classification (Munich Consensus most common), eccentric strengthening, electrophysical agents, manual therapy, and football-specific rehabilitation. Muscle strength is the dominant RTP criterion. This snapshot of elite practice in one of the world's most football-intensive countries confirms that evidence-based principles are widely adopted, while also highlighting that subjective and functional RTP criteria vary more than objective strength measures.
methods: Cross-sectional online survey. Population: physical therapists from elite Brazilian men's football clubs (Series A and B, 2021 season); 35 of 40 eligible clubs represented (87.5%); n=62 PTs. Instrument: 30-question questionnaire developed by the research team covering HSI assessment (imaging, classification systems), rehabilitation structure (phases, progressions), therapeutic modalities (electrophysical agents, manual therapy, strengthening types, sport-specific exercises), and RTP criteria (strength testing, functional milestones, imaging clearance). Descriptive statistics reported as frequencies and percentages. No inferential statistics used; descriptive survey design.
findings: 100% of PTs used imaging for HSI assessment; 100% rated imaging 'important' or 'very important.' 100% used injury classification systems; Munich Consensus used by 45.2%. Most common rehabilitation structure: 3–4 phases. 100% used electrophysical agents. 98.4% used strengthening exercises; 93.5% used eccentric protocols specifically. 96.8% used manual therapy. 95.2% incorporated football-specific exercises. 93.5% used lumbopelvic stabilization. Muscle strength was ranked #1 RTP criterion by 71% of respondents. Variation was noted in the specific tools used for strength testing and in the precise functional milestones applied for RTP clearance.
limitations: Survey design captures reported practices, not actual implementation fidelity; social desirability bias may inflate adoption of evidence-based techniques. Restricted to Brazilian Series A and B; findings may not generalize to other football cultures or resource settings. No data on outcomes (re-injury rates, time-loss) linked to specific practices. Single-season snapshot (2021) may not reflect current or evolving practices. The survey did not distinguish between injury severity grades in practice patterns.
practicalImplications: Practitioners managing HSIs in football can benchmark their protocols against this large-sample elite survey: imaging-guided classification, eccentric strengthening, and multi-modal rehabilitation (manual therapy, electrophysical agents, lumbopelvic stabilization) are near-universal standards in elite settings. Muscle strength testing as the primary RTP criterion is supported by the majority of elite practitioners. Programs lacking eccentric strengthening, formal injury classification, or football-specific exercise progressions may fall below the standard of care observed in elite Brazilian football.
athleteDev: For football player development, this survey reinforces that comprehensive HSI management — not just isolated strengthening — includes lumbopelvic stabilization, sport-specific movement progression, and structured phase-based rehabilitation. Integrating these elements into development academy injury protocols, even at sub-elite levels, aligns practice with elite standards and may reduce re-injury rates. Developing PTs and athletic trainers should be trained in the Munich Consensus classification system to improve communication and benchmarking.
rtp: The dominance of muscle strength as the primary RTP criterion (71% of elite Brazilian PTs) reflects current evidence favoring objective strength benchmarks over symptom-based clearance. However, the survey also shows that football-specific exercises (95.2%) and functional milestones are widely integrated alongside strength testing, suggesting a multi-criteria RTP framework is standard in elite practice. Rehabilitation programs should incorporate progressive sport-specific loading and lumbopelvic stability, with clearance requiring both strength and functional competency milestones.

citation: O'Malley GR, Pema P, Sweeney P, Sarwar S, Cassimatis N, Knightly JJ, Goldstein I, Patel NV. Impact of Cervical and Lumbar Spine Surgeries on National Football League (NFL) Player Performance and Return-to-Play Outcomes. Cureus. 2025;17(6):e85706.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study reviewed NFL injury reports from 2005 to 2022 to identify 136 players who underwent 144 spine surgeries (77 lumbar, 67 cervical). Lumbar surgeries yielded a 61% return-to-play rate and an average PFF performance rating increase of 6.3%, while cervical surgeries produced a 47% return-to-play rate and an average performance decline of 5.8%. Players with prior lumbar surgery had a significantly higher return-to-play rate (83% vs. 56%), and significantly fewer cervical surgery patients experienced performance improvement compared to lumbar patients (22% vs. 58%).
tldr: NFL players who undergo lumbar spine surgery return to play at higher rates and may even improve performance slightly, whereas cervical spine surgery is associated with lower return-to-play rates and measurable performance declines. Linemen dominate the lumbar surgery cohort while skill-position players are overrepresented in cervical cases, suggesting position-specific rehabilitation protocols are warranted. Staff should set more cautious return-to-play expectations for players undergoing cervical procedures and prioritize targeted rehab that accounts for the different biomechanical demands placed on linemen versus skill players.
methods: Retrospective observational cohort study. Publicly available NFL injury reports 2005–2022 were searched; player injury history, position, age, BMI, procedure type, and return-to-play status were sourced from multiple sports media and analytics platforms. Pro Football Focus (PFF) grades assessed pre- and post-surgical performance. Statistical analyses used independent two-sample t-tests, two-way ANOVA, linear regression, and logistic regression via Python 3.10.
findings: 144 spine surgeries in 136 players: 77 lumbar (61% RTP, avg PFF change +6.3%) vs. 67 cervical (47% RTP, avg PFF change -5.8%). Lumbar cohort had significantly higher BMI (32.3 vs. 30.5, p=0.013) and more discectomies (75% vs. 10%). Cervical cohort had significantly more fusions (39% vs. 6.5%). Players with prior lumbar surgery had higher RTP (83% vs. 56%, p=0.020). Only 22% of cervical patients showed performance improvement vs. 58% of lumbar patients (p=0.019). Pre-injury PFF >70 was associated with greater post-surgical performance decline (F=8.917, p=0.005).
limitations: PFF data unavailable prior to 2006, limiting pre-2006 analysis. Procedure specifics and exact anatomical region were often unavailable due to NFL privacy reporting requirements. Sample included only players whose injuries were publicly reported, creating potential selection bias. Relatively small sample with performance data available for only 33 lumbar and 26 cervical patients.
practicalImplications: Team medical staff should differentiate return-to-play timelines and performance expectations based on surgery type: lumbar patients have reasonable prospects for full performance recovery, while cervical patients face higher risk of permanent performance decline. Position-specific rehabilitation plans should account for the different injury patterns—linemen (chronic wear, lumbar) versus skill-position players (acute high-impact, cervical). Pre-injury PFF performance level should be incorporated into post-surgical prognosis discussions.
athleteDev: For NFL athlete development, this study underscores that high-BMI linemen are disproportionately affected by lumbar spine injuries due to the chronic loading demands of their positions. Prehabilitation programs focusing on lumbar spine health, core stability, and load management are especially relevant for linemen. Cervical spine injury prevention through technique coaching (blocking, tackling form) is critical for skill-position players who face higher collision risk.
rtp: Lumbar surgery RTP rate of 61% (or 83% in those with prior lumbar surgery) informs realistic RTP timelines for NFL players. Cervical surgery RTP rate of only 47%, combined with average performance decrements, suggests that cervical spine surgery should trigger more extensive RTP criteria before clearance. A history of prior lumbar surgery was paradoxically associated with higher RTP rates, possibly reflecting improved patient selection and surgical/rehab advances. PFF performance trajectories post-surgery should inform RTP decisions alongside clinical benchmarks.

citation: Dupont MM, Fourman MS, Iyer S, Qureshi SA, Sheha ED, Rhie-Lee J, Dowdell J. Impact of Lumbar Disk Herniation on Performance Outcomes and New Contracts in the National Football League. Clin Spine Surg. 2022;00:000-000.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study identified 101 NFL players treated for lumbar disk herniation (LDH) between 2000 and 2020 via public records. Of these, 75 (74.3%) returned to play at preserved performance levels (mean PFF 65.6 vs. 67.9 pre-injury, P=0.2), but total contract values fell significantly from a median of $5.97M to $2.00M and guaranteed values from $1.67M to $0.47M (both P<0.01). Younger age and higher preinjury PFF grade were the primary independent predictors of both RTP and contract-signing ability, while surgery type did not significantly affect outcomes.
tldr: Most NFL players (74%) return to full performance levels after lumbar disk herniation treatment, but their earning power drops sharply regardless of whether they had surgery. The key drivers of both returning to play and signing a new contract are the player's age and their pre-injury performance level—not the type of treatment. This means medical and performance staff should focus on identifying high-value younger players for aggressive rehabilitation investment, while setting realistic financial expectations for older or lower-performing players who sustain an LDH.
methods: Retrospective cohort study using publicly available NFL injury reports and prosportstransactions.com to identify LDH cases 2000–2020. Age, BMI, position, treatment type, and PFF grades were collected. Contract values (total and guaranteed) were sourced from spotrac.com and overthecap.com. Statistical analysis used Shapiro-Wilk normality tests, paired t-tests or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for pre/post comparisons, and multivariable logistic regression. Data managed via REDCap.
findings: 101 players treated for LDH; 77 (76.2%) required surgery. RTP rate: 74.3%. Mean posttreatment PFF 65.6 vs. 67.9 preinjury (P=0.2, non-significant). Median total contract value decreased from $5.97M to $2.00M (P<0.01); guaranteed value from $1.67M to $0.47M (P<0.01). Independent predictors of RTP: lower age (OR=0.75, 95% CI 0.59–0.96) and higher preinjury PFF (OR=1.08, 95% CI 1.01–1.16). Independent predictors of signing a new contract: lower age (OR=0.82) and higher preinjury PFF (OR=1.11). Nonoperative patients returned to play sooner (1.8 vs. 7.9 months) but had similar career longevity and performance.
limitations: Retrospective design based on public records introduces reporting and selection bias; nonoperative players may be underrepresented in public data. Specific LDH details (e.g., free fragments) were not collected. Small sample size for contract comparisons (n=33 after excluding rookie contracts). PFF grading involves human analyst subjectivity.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and performance staff should inform players and team management that performance levels are typically preserved after LDH treatment but contract values will likely decrease. The treatment modality (surgical vs. non-surgical) should not be the primary driver of RTP decisions; rather, player age and preinjury performance level are better predictors of outcomes. Younger, higher-performing players represent the best candidates for full rehabilitation investment following LDH.
athleteDev: For NFL athlete development, LDH is a career-altering but often survivable injury from a performance standpoint. Linemen and players in positions with heavy axial loading (blocking, tackling to the torso per NFL rules) face elevated LDH risk. Preventive programming emphasizing lumbar spine stability, proper lifting mechanics in the weight room, and load monitoring during high-intensity periods may reduce LDH incidence in developing NFL players.
rtp: A 74.3% RTP rate after LDH treatment—comparable across surgical and non-surgical cohorts—provides a useful benchmark for NFL team RTP planning. Nonoperative patients return faster (median 1.8 months) while surgical patients take longer (median 7.9 months), but long-term career outcomes are similar. Age and preinjury PFF performance are the strongest clinical predictors of successful RTP, suggesting these factors should be incorporated into individualized RTP protocols and contract decisions.

citation: Nassis GP, Girard O, Chiampas GT, Krustrup P, Racinais S. In-match strategies to mitigate the effect of heat on football (soccer) players' health and performance. Br J Sports Med. 2024;0:1-2.
abstract: This editorial, published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine in March 2024, was prompted by anticipated extreme heat conditions at the 2026 FIFA World Cup (WBGT projected 30–35°C in six host cities). It synthesizes evidence on in-match cooling strategies for football players, noting that rectal temperatures can reach 39.5–40.2°C and muscle temperatures up to 42°C during matches in heat. The authors recommend a tiered hierarchy of cooling interventions—from whole-body cold water immersion (most effective, 0.25–0.35°C/min reduction) to ice towels, head/hand cooling, and hydration strategies—tailored to available time windows during play.
tldr: With major football tournaments increasingly scheduled in hot environments, staff need a practical cooling toolkit. Whole-body cold water immersion at half-time is the gold standard, dropping core temperature fastest, but when impractical, ice towels applied to large body surfaces are the next best option. During the brief 3-minute in-game cooling breaks, the priority should shift from core cooling (not feasible in that time) to hydration, reducing skin temperature via chilled water and ice towels around the neck, and preserving cognitive function. American football teams preparing for hot-weather games—especially in outdoor stadiums in warm climates—should adopt these same principles in halftime and timeout protocols.
methods: Editorial with narrative synthesis of evidence. The authors reviewed studies that closely replicated football match conditions (including cooling breaks and half-time) to identify the most effective in-match cooling strategies. Evidence was graded by ecological validity and applied specifically to the football context. Historical weather data (2012–2021) for 2026 World Cup host cities informed the clinical context.
findings: Core temperatures during football in heat reach 39.5–40.2°C (rectal) and up to 42°C (muscle). Cognitive decline begins above 38.7°C. Whole-body cold water immersion reduces core temperature 0.25–0.35°C/min—most effective cooling method. Ice towels over large body surface reduce temperature 0.1°C/min—second most effective. Pre-cooling combined with mid-cooling (ice towels, head/hand cooling) improves second-half sprinting. During 3-min breaks, internal temperature reduction is not feasible; focus should be hydration and skin cooling. FIFPRO recommends cooling breaks at 15th and 60th minute. Sex differences in thermoregulation may place women at greater disadvantage in dry heat.
limitations: Most evidence is based on studies involving men; applicability to women and youth players is uncertain. Limited studies have directly replicated football match conditions with cooling breaks. The editorial does not present original data. Evidence on optimal cooling for extra time and multi-match tournament schedules is lacking.
practicalImplications: Applied sport science and sports medicine staff should establish pre-game and half-time cooling protocols for hot-weather competitions. Cold water immersion tanks should be available at half-time when feasible. For American football, timeout and halftime cooling protocols using ice towels, cold water, and neck/forearm cooling can meaningfully reduce thermal strain. Medical personnel should be included in decisions about game scheduling and cooling break timing. Adjust warm-up structure in heat to minimize temperature elevation before kick-off.
athleteDev: Heat management is directly relevant to American football, particularly for summer training camps, preseason games, and warm-weather regular season matchups. Developing players' heat acclimatization (10–14 sessions maintaining core temp >38.5°C for 60 min) is a key preparatory strategy. Practice schedule design, hydration protocols, and cooling station availability should be integrated into player development programs for teams in warm climates.
rtp: Heat illness can be a cause of acute performance impairment or medical emergency requiring game removal. These cooling strategies are primarily performance-enhancing and preventive rather than RTP-focused. However, understanding the thermal load during return-to-play progressions in hot environments—and applying cooling protocols during those sessions—can protect players from heat-related setbacks during RTP from injury.

citation: Balius R, Pedret C, Mecho S, Garcia-Romero A, Yanguas J, Sala-Blanch X, Carrera A, Reina F, Kassarjian A. Injuries to accessory head of rectus femoris in professional football players: anatomical description, imaging findings, and clinical implications. Folia Morphol. 2026;85:e01726016.
abstract: This retrospective case series from 2022–2024 described seven professional footballers (5 male, 2 female) competing in Spanish or English first/second divisions who sustained injuries to a previously undescribed accessory anterolateral head of the rectus femoris (RF). MRI revealed an accessory muscle belly with distinct proximal and distal aponeuroses separate from the main RF, with all injuries occurring at the myotendinous junction. Median return-to-play was 8 weeks (range 15–90 days), with isolated accessory head injuries averaging 27.2 days. Cadaveric dissection of a 68-year-old male confirmed an accessory RF belly 15 cm in length arising anterolateral to the main RF and merging with the quadriceps tendon.
tldr: A previously undescribed anatomical variant—an accessory anterolateral head of the rectus femoris—can be injured in kicking athletes and may be misidentified as a standard RF injury or missed entirely on imaging. Recognizing this variant on MRI or ultrasound allows clinicians to more accurately classify injury severity, set appropriate RTP timelines (isolated injuries heal faster, ~27 days, vs. concurrent RF injuries that can take up to 90 days), and avoid unnecessary concern about what might appear to be a more extensive injury. Sports medicine staff working with kickers, punters, or any athlete performing repetitive explosive hip flexion should be aware of this variant.
methods: Retrospective case series approved by the Catalan Sports Council Ethics Committee (37/2024/CEICEGC). Seven professional footballers with MRI-confirmed accessory RF head injuries 2022–2024 were included. MRI (T1W and T2W fat-saturated sequences, axial and coronal planes) characterized each lesion. Age, sex, injured limb, injury mechanism, lesion location, RF involvement, and RTP time were recorded. Cadaveric dissection of a fresh-frozen donated specimen provided anatomical confirmation.
findings: Seven cases identified: five males, two females, ages 19–32. All lesions on lateral aspect of RF. MRI revealed accessory anterolateral RF belly with distinct proximal aponeurosis fusing with RF central septum. All injuries at myotendinous junction of accessory head. Mean RTP: 38.7 days (range 15–90). Excluding cases with concurrent RF injuries: mean RTP 27.2 days (range 15–35). Three cases had concurrent central RF aponeurosis injuries. One reinjury occurred (Case 3, hypertrophic scar). Cadaveric accessory belly: 15 cm length, 75 mm intramuscular tendon, maximum width 16 mm, anatomical pattern consistent with MRI findings.
limitations: Very small sample (n=7), limiting conclusions about incidence and prognosis. Study population limited to professional footballers in Spanish and English leagues; generalizability to other sports or American football populations uncertain. Single cadaver dissection provides limited anatomical frequency data. Retrospective design.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine practitioners should be aware of this RF variant when evaluating anterior thigh injuries in kicking athletes. MRI or ultrasound cross-sectional imaging can identify the accessory head, especially in well-developed muscles. Correct identification prevents overestimation of injury severity (the accessory head injury alone carries a shorter RTP than a true RF injury) and enables targeted rehabilitation. When imaging shows apparent RF involvement on the lateral aspect, closer inspection for accessory aponeurotic structures is warranted.
athleteDev: In American football, NFL kickers and punters are at particular risk for RF and accessory RF injuries given the explosive hip flexion demands of their technique. This anatomical variant likely exists in American football players as well. Imaging protocols for anterior thigh injuries in kickers and skill players should include awareness of this variant to avoid misclassification. Strength and conditioning programming for kickers should address the eccentric loading demands on the entire rectus femoris complex.
rtp: Isolated accessory RF head injuries have a favorable RTP prognosis (~27 days), while concurrent central RF aponeurosis involvement extends RTP considerably (up to 90 days). Accurate MRI identification of whether the main RF is also injured is therefore critical for RTP timeline planning. The 8-week median RTP for the full series (including concurrent injuries) provides a conservative upper bound for clinical planning when imaging cannot clearly differentiate accessory-only from combined injuries.

citation: Vermeulen R, van Dyk N, Whiteley R, Chamari K, Gregson W, Lolli L, Bahr R, Tol JL, Serner A. Injury-inciting circumstances of sudden-onset hamstring injuries: video analyses of 63 match injuries in male professional football players in the Qatar Stars League (2013–2020). Br J Sports Med. 2024;0:1-9.
abstract: This single-centre observational cohort study used systematic video analysis to describe and categorise the injury-inciting circumstances of 63 sudden-onset hamstring match injuries in male professional football players from the Qatar Stars League (2013–2020). Nine investigators independently analysed broadcasted match footage, categorising injuries by playing situation, player actions, and other considerations (contact, balance). Running was involved in 86% of injuries, with the most common single-player action being short-distance acceleration (0–10 m, 24% of all injuries). Pressing, indirect contact, and inadequate balance were frequently involved, particularly in short-distance acceleration injuries.
tldr: Contrary to the prevailing belief that hamstring injuries in football primarily occur during high-speed sprinting, this video analysis found that the most common single injury scenario is short-distance acceleration (under 10 meters), often combined with pressing, indirect contact, and poor balance. This has direct implications for American football: rehabilitation and injury prevention programs focused solely on max-velocity running work may be incomplete. Training drills that replicate chaotic, contested short accelerations—such as press coverage, pass rush, and route running against defenders—may better address the full spectrum of hamstring injury risk.
methods: Single-centre observational cohort study using a prospective injury surveillance database (Aspetar Injury and Illness Surveillance Programme). 295 sudden-onset hamstring injuries registered 2013–2020; 131 had available video footage; 63 had a clear painful event and were included. Nine investigators (physiotherapists, sports medicine doctors, sports scientists) independently scored each video using a standardised scoring form covering playing situation, player actions (running type/speed/distance, kicking, jumping, pressing, contact, balance), and other considerations. Consensus required simple majority (5/9 investigators). Classification tree analysis performed in Orange software.
findings: 86% of injuries involved running. Most common single-player action: acceleration 0–10 m (24% of all injuries). Running speed was moderate in 52% of cases. Pressing involved in 46% of injuries; indirect contact in 27%; inadequate balance in 41%. At 0–10 m acceleration: indirect contact (53%) and inadequate balance (67%) frequently present. Injuries at high-speed over longer distances (20+ m) occurred in 16% of cases with less contact and better balance. Non-running injuries: kicking (10%), jumping (5%). No direct contact injuries observed. 56% occurred in second half.
limitations: Video analysis cannot precisely determine the exact moment of injury; actions leading up to injury were still determined by analyst opinion. No formal rater reliability testing performed. Only 48% of injuries with available video had a clear painful event identifiable, potentially missing more subtle onset injuries. Study limited to male professional football players; generalizability to women, other levels, age groups, and American football uncertain. Framework largely based on rugby consensus, as a football-specific consensus was published after the study.
practicalImplications: Injury prevention and rehabilitation programs for hamstring injuries should extend beyond high-speed running to include contested short accelerations, pressing scenarios, and drills involving indirect contact and balance challenges. Small-sided games that replicate these chaotic situations may address a broader injury risk profile than isolated sprint training. Kicking mechanics should also be included in hamstring injury prevention given their consistent 10–11% contribution to hamstring injuries across multiple studies.
athleteDev: For American football athlete development, this study challenges the assumption that hamstring injury risk is primarily a high-speed running problem. Defensive backs in press coverage, linebackers in pursuit angles, and running backs making short cuts all perform the contested short accelerations identified as the highest-risk scenario. Position-specific drills that train explosive acceleration mechanics in congested spaces—while emphasizing body control and balance—should be incorporated into hamstring injury prevention programs.
rtp: During hamstring RTP progressions, re-exposure to the full range of injury-inciting circumstances is essential before clearance. Return-to-running programs focused solely on linear speed work may not adequately prepare players for the contested, multidirectional, contact-influenced accelerations that produce most match-play hamstring injuries. RTP protocols should include sport-specific scenarios involving pressing, change of direction, and balance challenges at progressive intensities before full clearance for match play.

citation: Mikhail D, Sugimoto D, Tadros M, Van Pelt R, Radel L, Soma D, Ulman S, Miller S, Jones J. Injury Differences Between Youth Male and Female American Football Players. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2025. DOI: 10.1080/00913847.2025.2468632
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study analyzed 10 years of National Electronic Injury Surveillance System (NEISS) data (2014–2023) to compare injury patterns between youth male and female American football players ages 4–18. A total of 82,174 injuries were identified (95% male, 5% female; mean age 13.0 years). Males were more likely to sustain head (OR=1.32), knee (OR=1.23), and shoulder (OR=2.19) injuries and concussions (OR=1.50), while females had higher odds of finger (OR=3.22), ankle (OR=1.25), and sprain/strain injuries (OR=1.32). Top overall diagnoses were fractures (24.5%), sprains/strains (22.7%), contusions (12.2%), concussions (9.8%), and internal injuries (7.1%).
tldr: Youth male football players face significantly greater risk of concussions and head, knee, and shoulder injuries compared to female players, who are more prone to finger and ankle sprains — differences likely driven by greater male participation in tackle football versus female-dominant flag football. Staff working with female football athletes should prioritize ankle and finger injury prevention, while programs serving male youth players should maintain robust concussion surveillance and head/shoulder protection protocols.
methods: Retrospective descriptive epidemiological study using NEISS (National Electronic Injury Surveillance System) data over a 10-year period (January 1, 2014 – December 31, 2023). Patients ages 4–18 with American football-related injuries were included. Chi-square tests, odds ratios (OR) with 95% CI, and p-values (p<0.05) were used to compare top five injured body parts and diagnoses between sexes. Analyses performed in IBM SPSS v28.
findings: 82,174 total injuries identified; 95% male, 5% female. Most injured body parts: head (17.7%), finger (13.8%), knee (9.2%), shoulder (8.7%), ankle (8.0%). Most common diagnoses: fractures (24.5%), sprains/strains (22.7%), contusions (12.2%), concussions (9.8%), internal injuries (7.1%). Males significantly more likely to have head (OR=1.32), knee (OR=1.23), shoulder (OR=2.19) injuries and concussions (OR=1.50). Females significantly more likely to have finger (OR=3.22), ankle (OR=1.25) injuries and sprains/strains (OR=1.32). Fractures, contusions, and internal injuries did not significantly differ between sexes.
limitations: Female players comprised only 5% of the dataset, limiting statistical power for subgroup analyses. NEISS captures only emergency department visits, excluding sideline or clinical injuries. Football type (tackle vs. flag) and competitive level could not be differentiated. Potential recall bias from provider documentation. No longitudinal follow-up or outcome data included.
practicalImplications: Sex-specific injury prevention programs are warranted in youth football. Female football athletes — predominantly flag football players — need targeted ankle sprain and finger injury prevention protocols, while male tackle football programs should prioritize concussion prevention, head impact monitoring, and shoulder protection. Athletic trainers and team physicians should tailor their sideline assessment priorities based on the biological sex and football format of their athlete population.
athleteDev: Youth football programs should implement sex-specific injury screening and prevention curricula. For male tackle athletes, concussion education, proper tackling technique, and shoulder stability training are critical development priorities from a young age. For female flag football athletes, proprioceptive ankle training and hand/finger protection strategies should be integrated into off-season and in-season programming.
rtp: Return-to-play protocols for youth female football players should specifically address ankle ligament healing timelines and sprain severity grading, given their elevated sprain/strain risk. For male youth players, concussion return-to-play protocols must be strictly followed due to their elevated concussion risk. The elevated fracture rate overall in this young population underscores the need for clearance from orthopedic staff before return to contact activities.

citation: Platt B, Abed V, Khalily C, Sullivan B, Skinner M, Jacobs C, Johnson D, Stone AV. Injury rates remained elevated in the second National Football League season after the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation. 2023. DOI: 10.1016/j.asmr.2022.11.026
abstract: This cross-sectional study compared NFL injury incidence across the 2018–2021 seasons by querying publicly released weekly injury reports for players listed as 'out' or placed on injured reserve. Overall injury incidence in 2021 (28.70 per 1,000 exposures) was significantly elevated compared to pre-COVID 2018–2019 seasons (23.09/1,000; IRR=1.24) and the 2020 COVID season (21.64/1,000; IRR=1.33). Injury increases were observed across lower extremity, spine/core, upper extremity, and head anatomical zones, with the greatest relative increases seen mid-to-late season (weeks 5 and beyond). No positional group was disproportionately affected.
tldr: NFL injury rates in 2021 — the second season after COVID-19 onset — were higher than both pre-pandemic and pandemic seasons, with significant increases in mid-to-late season injuries across all anatomical zones. This suggests that the disruptions in training and preparation during COVID-19 had lasting effects that were not resolved within one season. For football performance and medical staffs, this underscores the long-term consequences of compressed or disrupted training periods on injury risk, and the critical importance of managing workload transitions carefully when returning athletes to full competition loads.
methods: Cross-sectional study using publicly available NFL weekly injury reports from fftoday.com and nfl.com covering 2018–2021 regular seasons and playoffs. Players listed as 'out' or on injured reserve for at least one game were included. Injuries categorized as lower extremity, upper extremity, spine/core, or head. Injury incidence calculated per 1,000 player exposures (one game = one exposure). Incidence rate ratios (IRR) calculated by dividing 2021 incidence by comparison cohort incidence. Z-test for proportions used for significance testing; Fisher's exact test for positional injury distribution differences. Bonferroni correction applied.
findings: Overall injury incidence in 2021: 28.70 per 1,000 exposures vs. 23.09 in 2018–2019 (IRR=1.24, p<0.001) and 21.64 in 2020 (IRR=1.33, p<0.001). Lower extremity injuries increased most in absolute terms (18.84 vs. 15.31/1,000 in 2018–2019; IRR=1.23). Head injuries showed highest relative increase (3.07 vs. 1.95/1,000 in 2018–2019; IRR=1.57). No significant positional differences in injury distribution. Injury rates were not significantly different in weeks 1–4 but were significantly elevated from week 5 onward in all comparisons.
limitations: Data sourced from publicly available injury reports rather than medical records, limiting injury detail and diagnostic accuracy. Only game-missed injuries were captured; sub-threshold or same-game-return injuries were not counted. Confounding variables across seasons (rule changes, expanded roster, additional game in 2021) difficult to fully control. Expanded IR return rules in 2020–2021 may have influenced IR listing patterns.
practicalImplications: When athlete populations experience significant disruptions to their normal training and competition schedules — analogous to COVID-19 impacts — sports medicine and performance staffs should anticipate delayed injury risk that emerges mid-to-late in the competitive season rather than immediately. Workload monitoring should incorporate an expanded chronic workload window after return-to-play periods, and progressive reloading programs should extend well beyond the initial weeks of competition to protect athletes through peak-season demands.
athleteDev: Extended disruptions to off-season and preseason training — whether from pandemics, lockouts, or other causes — create latent injury risk that manifests later in the season. Collegiate and professional football strength and conditioning programs should build periodization models that account for inadequate preparation time, using conservative acute-to-chronic workload ratios and extending physical preparation benchmarks before athletes reach full competitive loads.
rtp: Athletes returning from extended competitive layoffs (analogous to COVID-disrupted seasons) should not be considered fully protected from injury risk simply because early-season performance is unaffected. The mid-to-late season injury spike observed in this study suggests that functional return-to-play criteria should include assessments of cumulative workload tolerance, not just immediate performance readiness. RTP timelines following prolonged detraining should incorporate extended monitoring through weeks 5–10 of full competition exposure.

citation: O'Cain CM, Spratley EM, Arbogast KB, Lessley DJ, Evans WB, Cormier JM, Herzog MM, Weir N, Crandall JR, Coughlin MJ, Anderson RB. Injury Scenarios of Ankle Sprains in the National Football League: External Rotation and Eversion Mechanisms. The American Journal of Sports Medicine. 2025. DOI: 10.1177/03635465251329181
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study used video analysis to characterize 257 external rotation and eversion (EE) ankle sprains from 994 reported NFL game ankle sprains across four seasons (2017–2020). Eight standardized injury scenarios were identified: roll-up (44%), impact (25%), accordion (11%), change of direction (10%), crossed leg (5%), overload (2%), landing (2%), and misstep (1%). Direct player-to-player contact via a large external mass (opponent's pelvis or torso) accounted for 79% of injuries. Offensive linemen had the most sprains in absolute terms; running backs had 83% of their injuries from impact (tackle) scenarios. Only 15% of EE ankle sprains resulted from forces solely through the cleat-surface interaction.
tldr: The vast majority of external rotation/eversion (high/medial) ankle sprains in NFL players result from direct contact by a large external mass — typically an opponent's pelvis or torso — rather than from non-contact mechanisms or cleat-surface interaction alone. This means traditional bracing and cleat interventions will have limited effect on most EE ankle sprains, which require fundamentally different prevention strategies such as tackling technique modification for running backs and rule/education changes for linemen. Only the 15% of injuries occurring without direct external mass contact (common in safeties and cornerbacks) are well-suited to current bracing and proprioceptive training interventions.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study. NFL Electronic Medical Record queried by IQVIA for missed-time ankle sprains over 4 seasons (2017–2020). Injuries with available video reviewed by 2 experienced biomechanical engineers who independently coded mechanism, player-to-player contact type, contacting body region, loading type, and injury scenario. Standardized terminology developed for 8 injury scenarios. Blinded matched-case comparisons (n=128 for mechanism; n=84 for scenario parameters) assessed reviewer agreement using percent agreement and Cohen kappa. Chi-square goodness-of-fit used to assess positional representativeness.
findings: 994 game ankle sprains reported; 383 with observable mechanism included in secondary review; 257 (67%) confirmed as EE mechanism. Offensive linemen had most sprains (n=52; 20%), followed by wide receivers (18%) and defensive linemen (14%). Rush plays most common (46%). Direct contact present in 75% of injuries; large external mass loading in 79%. Pelvis most common contacting body region (30%), followed by torso (26%). Roll-up most common scenario overall (44%); impact most common for WR, RB, QB. Running backs: 83% impact scenarios. Safeties and cornerbacks most commonly injured via change-of-direction (non-contact) scenarios. Reviewer agreement: mechanism 100%, scenario 95% (kappa=0.94).
limitations: Only 39% of reported ankle sprains had observable mechanisms due to video limitations, particularly for linemen who are less often the focus of broadcast footage. Play-type distribution may not be representative of overall game play due to the association between video availability and play type. Forces applied in injury scenarios were not quantified. The study was funded by NFL/NFLPA, which presents a potential conflict of interest.
practicalImplications: Ankle brace design and evaluation must account for the high-force, large-external-mass scenarios that cause most EE ankle sprains in football — standard braces tested against small positional changes may be inadequate. Prevention programs should differentiate by position: running back coaches should focus on tackling technique modifications; linemen programs should address body positioning to reduce roll-up scenarios; safeties and cornerbacks are the most appropriate population for bracing, proprioceptive training, and cleat-surface optimization. Spatting and taping may benefit players in low-force (small external mass) scenarios.
athleteDev: Understanding position-specific ankle injury mechanisms should drive sport-specific training design. Offensive and defensive linemen benefit from core stability and body position training to reduce roll-up exposure. Running backs benefit from ball-carrier contact preparation and lower extremity resilience training. Secondary players (CBs, safeties) should receive ankle proprioception and change-of-direction training with ankle protection protocols. These distinctions are directly applicable to position-group injury prevention programming at all competitive levels.
rtp: Return-to-play timelines for EE ankle sprains should reflect the mechanism of injury, as these injuries typically involve greater force loading than lateral sprains and result in longer recovery. Running backs injured via impact tackle scenarios and linemen injured via roll-up may require different RTP benchmarks, given distinct biomechanical demands. High ankle syndesmotic sprains resulting from EE mechanisms have well-documented extended recovery times and RTP criteria should include syndesmotic stress testing and functional loading assessments before clearance.

citation: Smoliga JM, Deshpande SK, Binney ZO. Interaction of Surface Type, Temperature, and Week of Season on Concussion Risk in the National Football League: A Bayesian Analysis. Epidemiology. 2023;34(6):807-816.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined 1,830 NFL games across 8 seasons (2012–2019) using Bayesian negative binomial regression to assess how playing surface, game temperature, and week of season — and their interactions — relate to diagnosed concussion risk. Artificial turf was associated with greater concussion risk than natural grass (IRR=0.78 for grass; >99% probability). Colder temperatures increased concussion risk (IRR=0.85 per 7.9°C decrease; >99% probability), and later season weeks independently increased risk (IRR=1.02 per week; >91% probability). Surface-by-temperature and surface-by-week interactions were also identified, with artificial turf concussion risk particularly amplified in cold weather.
tldr: Natural grass surfaces are associated with roughly 22% lower concussion risk than artificial turf in NFL games, and this protective effect is further diminished in cold weather and late in the season — factors that compound concussion risk multiplicatively. For football programs playing on artificial turf, especially late-season cold-weather games, concussion protocols should be heightened and medical staff should maintain increased vigilance. This study also raises important questions about season length policy, suggesting that extending NFL seasons likely increases cumulative concussion exposure.
methods: Retrospective cohort study of 8 NFL regular seasons (2012–2019). Concussion data sourced from PBS Frontline Concussion Watch and Football Outsiders injury databases. Playing surface categorized as natural grass or artificial turf (hybrid surfaces <5% excluded). Game temperature obtained from Pro-Football-Reference, official NFL game reports, and Weather Underground. Bayesian negative binomial regression models fitted with surface, temperature (z-scored), week of season, and surface×temperature and surface×week interaction terms. MCMC simulation via rstanarm (8 chains, 15,000 iterations). Incidence rate ratios (IRR) reported as median with 95% credible intervals. Sensitivity analyses with indoor-only and late-season-only models performed.
findings: 1,096 diagnosed concussions across 1,830 games. Natural grass associated with reduced concussion risk (IRR=0.78 [0.68, 0.89]; >99% probability). Concussion risk decreased with higher temperature (IRR=0.85 [0.76, 0.95] per 7.9°C; >99% probability). Concussion risk increased with each week of season (IRR=1.02 [1.00, 1.04]; >91% probability). Surface×temperature interaction: >84% probability of effect; artificial turf concussion risk particularly amplified in cold weather. Surface×week interaction: >75% probability of effect. Late-season (week 16) cold-weather (5°C) games on artificial turf predicted 0.96 concussions per game vs. 0.37 for early-season warm (25°C) grass games.
limitations: Concussions are likely underdiagnosed; analysis only captures diagnosed (reported) concussions. Player self-reporting biases may vary by surface, temperature, or season week. Considerable heterogeneity exists within 'artificial turf' and 'natural grass' categories not accounted for. Field wear and moisture data were not available. Playoff injury reports unavailable for eliminated teams, preventing playoff analysis. Study funded independently with no NFL funding but concussion diagnosis practices may reflect institutional biases.
practicalImplications: Football programs playing on artificial turf should implement heightened concussion surveillance protocols, particularly during cold-weather late-season games. Medical staffs should recognize that concussion risk compounds across the season regardless of surface, suggesting fatigue-related biological threshold changes require ongoing monitoring. Facilities with artificial turf may consider whether late-season high-stakes games warrant additional precautions. These findings also support NFLPA advocacy for transitioning to natural grass fields at the professional level.
athleteDev: The finding that concussion risk increases progressively over the season — beyond what temperature alone explains — has direct implications for athlete development periodization. Programs should consider incorporating cognitive load testing, neurological wellness monitoring, and potential practice intensity reductions in the second half of the competitive season. Exposure to cumulative sub-concussive impacts across a season may lower the concussion threshold for individual athletes, suggesting that protective measures should intensify as the season progresses.
rtp: Season-long accumulation of head impact exposure raises the possibility that a player's concussion threshold decreases progressively over the season, meaning later-season concussions may occur from lower-force events. Return-to-play protocols for late-season concussions should account for cumulative neurological exposure and not solely rely on acute symptom resolution. The interaction between surface type and cold weather further suggests that post-concussion return-to-play decisions should factor in upcoming playing conditions, with artificial turf cold-weather environments representing higher-risk return settings.

citation: Maestroni L, Turner A, Read P, Bettariga F, Fenu G, Rosalia A, Guastella M, Bishop C. Interlimb Asymmetry Data in Athletes With Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction: A Comparison of Different Equations to Interpret Between-Limb Difference Data. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. 2025;00(00):1-9.
abstract: This study compared multiple asymmetry calculation equations in 53 amateur athletes (28.1 months post-ACL reconstruction) across unilateral strength and vertical jump tests and bilateral countermovement jump (CMJ). Large significant differences were found between unilateral equations (eta-squared 0.14–0.44) and bilateral equations for CMJ impulse asymmetries (0.17–0.34), with pairwise effect sizes of d=0.46–2.30 for unilateral tasks and d=0.99–3.04 for CMJ impulse. The choice of equation substantially altered whether an athlete would 'pass' or 'fail' a standard 10% asymmetry threshold, demonstrating that equation selection is a critical methodological decision in ACL rehabilitation and return-to-sport assessment.
tldr: The specific mathematical equation used to calculate interlimb asymmetry can dramatically change the magnitude, direction, and pass/fail outcome of a return-to-sport assessment — sometimes indicating opposite conclusions for the same athlete. Practitioners should adopt standardized, theoretically justified equations: the Standard Percentage Difference with direction for unilateral tasks (e.g., single-leg jumps, split squats) and the Bilateral Asymmetry Index 1 (BAI 1) for bilateral tasks (e.g., CMJ). This standardization is especially critical in football rehabilitation settings where asymmetry data drives return-to-play decisions and the 10% threshold is commonly applied.
methods: 53 amateur athletes (16 women, 37 men; mean 28.5 years; 28.1 months post-ACLR) completed strength and vertical jump testing: 5RM rear foot elevated split squat, 5RM leg extension, bilateral CMJ, single-leg CMJ, and single-leg drop jump using synchronized dual force plates (ForceDecks, Vald Performance). Five unilateral asymmetry equations (Limb Symmetry Index 2, Symmetry Index, Standard Percentage Difference, Symmetry Angle, Standard Percentage Difference with direction) and five bilateral equations (BSA, BAI 1, BAI 2, Symmetry Angle, BSA with direction) were compared. One-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis with eta-squared effect sizes, Bonferroni post hoc tests, and Cohen d pairwise effect sizes were calculated. Reliability assessed via ICC and CV.
findings: Most variables showed excellent reliability (ICC 0.925–0.978, CV 2.6–9.6%). Large significant differences between unilateral equations for all variables (eta-squared 0.14–0.44, all p<0.001). Large significant differences between bilateral equations for CMJ concentric and eccentric impulse (eta-squared 0.34 and 0.17). Pairwise differences between unilateral equations: d=0.46–2.30. Pairwise differences for CMJ impulse asymmetries: d=0.99–3.04. Case examples showed the same athlete could pass, fail, or return uncertain results depending solely on equation choice. Standard Percentage Difference with direction and BAI 1 identified as recommended equations for unilateral and bilateral tasks respectively.
limitations: Tests were limited to pushing-based movements; no pulling-based assessments (leg curl, Romanian deadlift) were included, potentially missing hamstring deficits common after semitendinosus-gracilis grafts. The study was conducted at a single private rehabilitation clinic in Italy, limiting generalizability. Interlimb asymmetries should not be used as the sole indicator of limb recovery or RTP readiness. The 10% threshold itself has questionable clinical validity.
practicalImplications: Football sports medicine and performance staff should standardize their asymmetry calculation methods immediately. Using inconsistent equations across assessments or between staff members can produce misleading data that either prematurely clears or unnecessarily delays athletes. The Standard Percentage Difference with direction is recommended for unilateral assessments (single-leg CMJ, hop tests, split squats) and BAI 1 for bilateral force plate assessments (bilateral CMJ impulse asymmetry). Teams using force plates for ACLR return-to-sport testing should audit their current equation use and align with recommended formulas.
athleteDev: For football athletes progressing through ACLR rehabilitation, asymmetry monitoring using consistent, appropriate equations enables more reliable tracking of limb recovery over time. As athletes approach return to full football activity, the Standard Percentage Difference with direction provides a stable denominator regardless of whether the ACLR or uninvolved limb is performing better — a scenario common in late-stage rehabilitation and return-to-sport phases when the reconstructed limb may exceed the uninvolved limb in some metrics.
rtp: Return-to-play decision-making for football players after ACL reconstruction is directly affected by the asymmetry equation chosen. Case examples in this study demonstrate that an athlete can pass with one equation and fail with another on the same test battery. Teams using the 10% asymmetry threshold for RTP clearance must specify which equation they are using and ensure it is the most appropriate for each task type. The BAI 1 is recommended for bilateral CMJ impulse asymmetry — a key metric in phase-specific RTP criteria — while the Standard Percentage Difference with direction should be used for all single-leg hop and strength assessments.

citation: Renaghan E, Wittels HL, Wittels SH, Wishon MJ, Hecocks D, Wittels ED, Hendricks S, Girardi J, Lee SJ, McDonald SM, Feigenbaum LA. Internal or External Training Load Metrics: Which Is Best for Tracking Autonomic Nervous System Recovery and Function in Collegiate American Football? J Funct Morphol Kinesiol. 2024;9(1):5.
abstract: This prospective cohort study followed 71 male Division I collegiate football players across 27 weeks of off-, pre-, and in-season training (241 practice sessions) to compare the ability of internal load (Exercise Cardiac Load, ECL) and external load (accelerometer-based ACCEL devices) to predict autonomic nervous system (ANS) deterioration. ECL, defined as average training HR multiplied by session duration in minutes, was measured via an ECG-capable armband (Warfighter Monitor), while external load was captured by both the Warfighter Monitor and Catapult accelerometers. ANS recovery was assessed via next-day baseline HR, HRV (SDNN and rMSSD), and HR recovery measured 24 h post-session. ECL significantly outperformed ACCEL in predicting ANS deterioration acutely and cumulatively (R2 up to 0.57 vs. 0.05 for one-week cumulative HR recovery), while ACCEL correlated only weakly with ECL (rho = 0.43–0.47).
tldr: Accelerometers (Catapult-style devices) that football programs rely on to estimate training load are poor predictors of autonomic nervous system deterioration in collegiate football players, routinely underestimating the actual physiological stress athletes experience. Exercise Cardiac Load (ECL), derived from average heart rate and session duration, demonstrated substantially stronger associations with next-day HRV, resting HR, and HR recovery across acute, one-week, and two-week cumulative windows. For applied sport scientists, this means GPS/accelerometer data alone may give coaches a false sense of training-load control — especially for skill positions like wide receivers who move at constant speeds. Supplementing ACCEL monitoring with an ECG-based internal load metric like ECL provides a more reliable window into recovery status and overtraining risk.
methods: Prospective cohort design; n = 71 male D1 starters followed 27 weeks (off-, pre-, in-season, 241 sessions). Internal load: ECL = avg HR (≥85 bpm) × session duration (min) via Warfighter Monitor armband. External load: Catapult Player Load and WFM accelerometer (triaxial ACCEL). ANS outcomes: morning baseline HR and HRV (SDNN, rMSSD) measured 0600–0700 after ≥4 min inactivity; HR recovery (30 s post-exercise). Linear regression models for acute (24 h) and cumulative (1- and 2-week) associations; normality checked via Kolmogorov–Smirnov; α = 0.05; MATLAB 2021b.
findings: ECL produced significant associations with all ANS outcomes acutely (R2 0.19 for baseline HR, 0.38 for HR recovery, 0.19 for SDNN/rMSSD) and cumulatively (1-week: R2 0.48–0.55; 2-week: R2 0.52–0.57). ACCEL associations were weak and mostly non-significant acutely (R2 0.02–0.07) and remained low at 1 week (R2 0.05) and 2 weeks (R2 0.002–0.05). ECL-ACCEL correlation was moderate (rho = 0.43–0.47), indicating ACCEL underestimated training load. Associations strengthened over time for ECL but not ACCEL.
limitations: Single D1 program from one southeastern U.S. location limits generalizability. Results may not extend to other sports, professional athletes, or female athletes. Several authors are paid employees of Tiger Tech Solutions (Warfighter Monitor manufacturer), introducing potential conflict of interest.
practicalImplications: Sport scientists should supplement or replace accelerometer-only monitoring with ECG-based internal load metrics (e.g., ECL) to accurately detect when athletes are approaching ANS deterioration thresholds. ACCEL devices are appropriate for tracking movement patterns but should not serve as the sole indicator of cumulative physiological load. Coaches relying exclusively on Catapult-style devices may unknowingly over-prescribe training intensity, particularly for skill positions that sustain constant-speed running.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, ECL-based monitoring enables individualized training prescription by tracking each player's true cardiovascular load across the full 27-week cycle. Positions with high constant-speed demands (WRs, CBs) are at greatest risk of being undermonitored by ACCEL alone. Integrating ECL into periodization decisions can prevent performance decrements from cumulative ANS fatigue, supporting speed and agility development throughout the season.
rtp: ECL provides a reliable daily biomarker for return-to-play readiness after injury. Because ANS deterioration (elevated baseline HR, depressed HRV, slowed HR recovery) tracks closely with ECL and worsens within 24 h of excessive load, clinicians can use ECL benchmarks alongside HRV trends to make objective gating decisions before clearing players for full practice participation post-injury.

citation: van Dyk N, Witvrouw E, Bahr R. Interseason variability in isokinetic strength and poor correlation with Nordic hamstring eccentric strength in football players. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2018;28(6):1878-1887.
abstract: This prospective cohort study examined 529 professional male football (soccer) players from all 18 Qatar Stars League teams who underwent periodic health evaluations at Aspetar Hospital between 2010 and 2014. The study had two aims: (1) quantify season-to-season variability of isokinetic strength testing, and (2) determine the correlation between isokinetic and Nordic hamstring exercise (NHE) eccentric strength. Among 288 players with repeated isokinetic tests separated by 1 or 2 seasons, measurement error was approximately 25 Nm (15%) regardless of interval or injury history. For 337 players who also performed NHE testing in 2014, the correlation between isokinetic eccentric hamstring torque and NHE peak force was poor (r = .35), and the limb-to-limb imbalance showed virtually no correlation between the two test modes (r = .037).
tldr: Season-to-season isokinetic strength data in elite soccer players are far more variable than widely assumed — with measurement errors around 25 Nm (15%) even in injury-free players — making year-to-year comparisons unreliable for injury risk profiling. More importantly, isokinetic eccentric hamstring torque and Nordic hamstring exercise force are essentially measuring different constructs (r = .35), so a player who scores well on one may score poorly on the other. For football sport scientists, this means that a single pre-season isokinetic screen cannot reliably predict hamstring injury risk, and that isokinetic and NHE data should not be used interchangeably in screening protocols. Supplementing with multiple data points across the season and using exercise-specific assessments will yield more actionable risk profiles.
methods: Prospective cohort study; all 18 QSL teams, serial data September 2010 – June 2014. Isokinetic testing: Biodex Multi-joint System 3; concentric knee flexion/extension at 60°/s and 300°/s, eccentric knee extension at 60°/s. Subgroups: 240 players with 1 season between tests, 86 with 2 seasons. NHE testing (2014, n = 337): kneeling board device, 3 maximal reps, peak force recorded. Injury surveillance via Aspetar AIISP. Statistical analyses: paired t-tests, ICC3,1 with 95% CI, measurement error (SD of means ÷ √2), Pearson correlation for isokinetic vs. NHE comparison; IBM SPSS v.21.
findings: Substantial intraindividual variability across all isokinetic modes whether separated by 1 season (measurement error ~24.5 Nm quadriceps, ~15.7 Nm hamstrings concentric, ~26.6 Nm hamstrings eccentric) or 2 seasons (similar magnitudes). ICC values ranged 0.51–0.79. Variability was not meaningfully different between injured (n=60) and uninjured (n=228) players. Poor correlation between isokinetic eccentric torque (mean 207.7 ± 44.1 Nm) and NHE peak force (mean 298.6 ± 72.3 N): r = .35. Limb-to-limb imbalance correlation between test modes: r = .037.
limitations: No recording of specific strength training interventions or match exposure between test occasions. Multinational, multilingual setting may have affected player comprehension of test instructions. No formal familiarization — learning effects between sessions cannot be excluded. Homogenous sample of professional male soccer players limits generalizability to other sports, females, or amateur athletes.
practicalImplications: Periodic health evaluations in football should not rely on single time-point isokinetic screens for injury risk stratification given the large natural variability. Isokinetic and Nordic hamstring assessments should be treated as complementary, not interchangeable, because they measure biomechanically distinct force-production properties. Baseline-to-baseline comparisons of isokinetic data require changes exceeding ~25–27 Nm (hamstrings eccentric) to be considered real rather than noise.
athleteDev: For American football athlete development, these findings translate directly to pre-season screening programs: Biodex-style isokinetic data collected annually will show high within-athlete variability that is unrelated to actual changes in strength or injury risk. Strength staff should pair isokinetic data with functionally relevant eccentric assessments (Nordic hamstring devices, RDL force measurements) and repeat testing within-season to build more reliable strength profiles across training blocks.
rtp: During return-to-play from hamstring strain, clinicians should be cautious about using a single isokinetic test — compared to pre-injury baseline — as a clearance criterion. The ~15% natural measurement error means isokinetic strength deficits of less than 15–20% may reflect test variability rather than true weakness. Multi-modal assessment including NHE force testing, eccentric strength in sport-specific positions, and functional movement screens is recommended before full clearance.

citation: Kopardekar A, Pradhan S. Investigating Circadian Advantages in the National Football League: A Case Study of West Coast Teams. Int J Sport Soc. 2022;13(1):163-173.
abstract: This 30-year retrospective analysis (1990–2019 seasons) examined all regular season away games played by the five NFL West Coast teams (LA Rams, LA/San Diego Chargers, Oakland/Las Vegas Raiders, San Francisco 49ers, Seattle Seahawks), yielding 1,018 game observations. The study investigated whether eastward travel and afternoon game times conferred circadian performance advantages, controlling for team and opponent quality via Elo ratings. Generalized regression models (Poisson, logistic, negative binomial, linear) with Bonferroni-corrected post-hoc tests revealed that West Coast teams scored significantly more points and won at higher rates in the Eastern time zone, and that afternoon games produced more scoring, fewer points allowed, and higher completion percentages compared to evening games.
tldr: West Coast NFL teams gain a measurable performance edge when traveling eastward, particularly to the Eastern time zone, because their biological clocks align more closely with the late-afternoon circadian performance peak during both afternoon and evening games. Over 30 years, these teams scored the most points and had the highest win percentage in Eastern time zone games, and performed better offensively and defensively during afternoon contests. For performance staff managing NFL teams, this evidence supports proactive circadian management strategies — traveling early, adjusting practice schedules, regulating sleep environments, and timing nutrition — especially before critical road trips. Teams playing West Coast opponents at home early on Sunday should be aware of the biological disadvantage their visitors may not actually face.
methods: Retrospective observational study; n = 1,018 regular season away games, 1990–2019 seasons, 5 West Coast NFL teams. Game and performance data from Pro-Football-Reference; Elo ratings from FiveThirtyEight. Excluded: morning games, Friday games, ties, international games. Game time dichotomized: afternoon (<5:00 pm local) vs. evening (≥5:00 pm local). Four US time zones compared. Generalized regressions: Poisson (points scored, allowed, punts), logistic (game outcome), negative binomial (interceptions, sacks), linear (completion/FG/XP%). Bonferroni-corrected post-hoc tests; covariates: day of week, team Elo, opponent Elo. RStudio 1.4.1717; jamovi 1.6.23.
findings: West Coast teams scored most points in Eastern (EMM=21.65) vs. Pacific (19.09) and Central (17.90) time zones (p<.001). Highest win percentage in Eastern vs. Central time zone (50% vs. 32%, p=.03). Afternoon games: more points scored (20.73 vs. 19.10, p=.005), fewer points allowed (21.49 vs. 22.69, p=.04), higher completion percentage (60.68% vs. 57.90%, p=.05). Sacks allowed significantly lower in Eastern vs. Central time zone (1.98 vs. 2.64, p=.005). No significant effects on interceptions, punts, FG%, or XP%.
limitations: Unequal distribution of teams across time zones (only 2 in Mountain zone, 17 in Eastern) reduces comparability. Changes in NFL rules and scoring trends over 30 years not accounted for. Weather conditions not controlled. Injury impact at specific positions not modeled. Only five West Coast teams analyzed — findings may not generalize to all NFL travel patterns.
practicalImplications: Performance staff should implement structured circadian management protocols for West Coast teams traveling east, including early travel, adjusted practice/meeting start times, sleep environment optimization (68°F rooms, blackout conditions), wearable sleep monitoring, and meal timing adjustments. Conversely, East Coast teams hosting West Coast opponents in early afternoon games should recognize this scheduling may inadvertently favor the visitors, particularly for late-afternoon kickoffs.
athleteDev: Circadian rhythm management should be integrated into the travel protocols for all high-frequency travel programs in American football. Practice scheduling and conditioning sessions should account for time zone shifts, with athletes encouraged to shift sleep-wake cycles in advance of away games. Nutritional timing adjustments (e.g., shifting meal timing by the number of time zones traveled) can help reset internal clocks and preserve peak performance capacity during road weeks.
rtp: Time zone disruption and associated sleep deficits can impair neuromuscular performance, reaction time, and injury vigilance — all relevant to return-to-play readiness. Players returning from injury who must travel across time zones should have their readiness reassessed relative to circadian status. Sleep quality and quantity should be actively monitored via wearables during travel weeks, and RTP milestones that involve performance testing should ideally be scheduled during the athlete's circadian performance peak.

citation: Eager E, Seth T. Investigating trade-offs made by American football linebackers using tracking data. J Quant Anal Sports. 2023; aop.
abstract: Using NFL Next Gen Stats tracking data (RFID chips, 2017–2021 seasons) combined with PFF play-by-play charting data, this study developed and validated two novel metrics for evaluating linebackers and box defenders: Ground Covered Over Expected (GCOE) for run plays, and Bite Distance Under Expected (BDUE) for play-action passes. GCOE measures how much more or less ground a defender covers toward the ball carrier on run plays relative to what an XGBoost model predicted given game context, while BDUE measures how much less a defender bites toward the line of scrimmage on play-action passes compared to expectation. The study found GCOE (year-to-year r = 0.66) and BDUE (r = 0.57) are substantially more stable than conventional PFF coverage metrics, and the two metrics correlate negatively with each other (r = −0.40), confirming a fundamental run/pass-reaction trade-off in linebacker play.
tldr: Most linebacker evaluation metrics are unstable year-to-year and fail to capture the essential trade-off these players face: being aggressive downhill in the run game makes them vulnerable to play-action, while staying disciplined against play-action means they sacrifice run fits. This study quantifies that trade-off using actual player tracking data, showing that linebackers who flow well toward the run (high GCOE) tend to bite harder on play-action fakes (low BDUE), and vice versa. The metrics are more predictive of future performance than PFF grades or raw run-stop rates. For football scouts and coordinators, GCOE and BDUE together offer a data-driven language to identify rare players who excel at both — like Dont'a Hightower and Luke Kuechly — and to match linebacker profiles to scheme demands.
methods: Data: PFF charting data (play type, run concept, coverage scheme, blitz, etc.) and NFL NGS tracking data (x/y coordinates at 0.1-second intervals via RFID chips), 2017–2021 seasons. GCOE response variable: yards covered toward ball carrier from snap to 2 seconds on run plays. BDUE response variable: yards of displacement toward LOS from snap to 2 seconds on play-action passes. Models tested: null, elastic-net regression, random forest, tuned XGBoost. Model selection via 22-fold week-by-week cross-validation and RMSE. Final models: XGBoost for GCOE (RMSE 2.47 yds), Random Forest for BDUE (RMSE 2.23 yds). Player thresholds: ≥200 run-defense snaps (GCOE), ≥75 play-action snaps (BDUE). Year-to-year correlations computed for stability analysis; Pearson r for GCOE-BDUE relationship.
findings: GCOE year-to-year r = 0.66 (vs. run-stop rate r = 0.41, PFF grade r = 0.46). BDUE year-to-year r = 0.57 (vs. pass breakup rate r = 0.09, PFF coverage grade r = 0.04). Within-season GCOE-BDUE correlation r = −0.40 (n=287). GCOE added incremental predictive value to run-stop rate (R2 0.17→0.20) and PFF grade (0.22→0.23) in year n+1 models. Top BDUE performers (2017–2021): Dont'a Hightower (1.21), Thomas Davis (0.92), Jamie Collins (0.85). Top GCOE performers: Raekwon McMillan (1.01), Zach Cunningham (0.83). Offenses best at inducing linebacker flow: Green Bay, Carolina, Tennessee; best at suppressing it: Kansas City, Jacksonville, San Francisco 49ers.
limitations: Analysis limited to linebackers and strong safeties in box alignments; not applicable to all defensive positions. BDUE uses 2-second window post-snap, which may not capture all play-action deception scenarios. GCOE and BDUE are positional metrics — they do not capture outcome (whether the tackle was made or the pass was defended). Tracking data currently limited to the NFL level, restricting application to college evaluation.
practicalImplications: Linebacker evaluation should incorporate tracking-based metrics like GCOE and BDUE rather than relying solely on PFF grades, which are highly unstable year-to-year. Defensive coordinators can use the GCOE-BDUE relationship to categorize linebackers as 'aggressive' (high GCOE, low BDUE) or 'disciplined' (low GCOE, high BDUE) and match them to scheme demands. In run-heavy defensive systems, high-GCOE linebackers are valuable; pass-heavy defensive schemes with heavy play-action opponents benefit from high-BDUE players. Players in the upper quadrant of both metrics are elite and extremely rare.
athleteDev: For NFL and college football evaluation and development, GCOE and BDUE metrics offer a template for position-specific tracking analysis that goes beyond traditional stats. Identifying whether a linebacker naturally trends aggressive or disciplined can guide pre-draft evaluation and positional fit decisions. Developing tracking pipelines at the college level would allow earlier identification of linebackers with rare run-pass dual proficiency. Within-season GCOE tracking could identify players whose run-reaction instincts are declining due to fatigue or injury.
rtp: While not a direct RTP metric, GCOE and BDUE both depend on first-step explosiveness, lateral quickness, and cognitive processing speed — qualities impaired by lower-extremity injuries and concussion. Tracking a recovering linebacker's GCOE (whether they are covering expected ground on run plays) provides a functional, game-context-based readiness check that complements clinical tests. A significant drop in GCOE relative to pre-injury baseline could flag incomplete neuromotor recovery before a player is cleared for full contact.

citation: Skinner T, Foreman JJ. Irrational Decision-Making and Herd Behavior: Evidence from the National Football League Head Coach Labor Market. Am Behav Sci. 2025;1-11.
abstract: This study analyzed NFL head coach (HC) hiring from the 2015–16 through 2021–22 seasons (n=435 coordinator-season observations) and compared determinants of interview/hire decisions to determinants of on-field success (n=582 team-season observations dating back to 1985–86). Four regression models were estimated: Interviews (negative binomial), Hired (logistic), Team Win % (linear), and Cover Against the Spread % (linear). The key finding was that previous interview frequency (Interviewst-1) significantly predicted both future interviews and hiring probability, while coordinator performance ranking predicted hiring but was inversely associated with subsequent HC success — evidence of systematic herd behavior and misaligned hiring criteria in the NFL executive labor market.
