tldr: NFL teams largely hire head coaches based on which candidates other teams are already interviewing, not based on what actually predicts winning. A coordinator's defensive/offensive performance ranking increases their odds of getting hired but is inversely associated with actual success as a head coach — suggesting that the skills required to run a successful unit are different from those needed to lead an entire organization. Each prior interview a candidate received increased the probability of future hiring by over 2%, such that a coach interviewed by 7 teams had a 27% higher hiring probability the following year. For front office staff, this is a cautionary finding about groupthink in executive hiring: structured, independent evaluation processes leveraging research-based criteria (rather than market signaling) would likely yield better head coaching hires.
methods: Panel data study; 435 coordinator-season observations (Interview and Hired models, 2015–16 to 2021–22) and 582 team-season observations (Team Win % and Cover ATS % models, 1985–86 to 2021–22) restricted to HCs hired from coordinator roles. Interview/hired data sourced from profootballrumors.com annual HC tracking. Four models: negative binomial regression (Interviews), logistic regression (Hired), linear regression with random effects (Team Win %, Cover ATS %). Clustered standard errors in Hired, Team Win %, and Cover ATS % models. Controls: age, race, NFL playing experience, HC/coordinator experience, offensive coordinator indicator, schedule strength, coordinator performance ranking, player quality variables, prior team success.
findings: Interviewst-1 was positive and significant in both Interview (β=0.285, p<.01) and Hired (β=0.339, p<.05) models, confirming herd behavior in HC appointments. Coor. Perf. Ranking was negative and significant in Interviews (β=−0.049, p<.01) and Hired (β=−0.080, p<.05) but positive and significant in Team Win % (β=0.366, p<.01) and Cover ATS % (β=0.185, p<.10) models — demonstrating that better coordinators are more likely to be hired but perform worse as HCs. NFL Entry Level Exp. significantly reduced Team Win % (β=−1.729, p<.01) and Cover ATS % (β=−1.275, p<.01). Younger teams performed better with younger coaches; older teams performed better with older coaches (Age × Avg. Player Age interaction, p<.05). Older coaches were significantly less likely to be interviewed despite no negative effect on team win % for age-matched rosters.
limitations: Study limited to offensive and defensive coordinators as candidates, representing 64.9% of hired HCs — position coaches and other pathways excluded. Data from profootballrumors.com relies on media citations, which may not capture all genuine candidates. Rooney Rule compliance (sham interviews) may confound interview count data. Results may not generalize beyond NFL to college football or other professional leagues.
practicalImplications: NFL organizations seeking to optimize head coaching hires should implement structured selection processes that evaluate coordinators on criteria empirically tied to HC success (e.g., player development track records, organizational management skills) rather than relying on market signaling from other teams' interview lists. Coordinator unit performance rankings are unreliable HC success predictors because managing one side of the ball requires different skills than leading an entire franchise. Human resource professionals and analytics staff should be involved in the executive hiring process to reduce irrational decision-making driven by reputational concerns.
athleteDev: While not directly about on-field athlete development, the organizational behavior implications are relevant: head coaches hired via herd behavior rather than empirical criteria may be less equipped to build cultures, develop young players, and make sound in-game decisions. Program directors at all levels should recognize that coordinator success does not automatically translate to effective head coaching, and should identify candidates with demonstrated organizational leadership and player development skills as well as unit performance.
rtp: Organizational decision-making quality, including head coach selection, shapes the culture and protocols around athlete health. Coaches hired through irrational processes may be less receptive to sports medicine input on return-to-play decisions. Programs that implement evidence-based hiring processes aligned with organizational best practices tend to create environments where medical and performance staff have appropriate authority in athlete health decisions, supporting safer and more systematic RTP protocols.

citation: Rogge N, Rowley P. Is it all about the stars? Spending on star players and success in the National Football League. Omega. 2025. doi:10.1016/j.omega.2025.103433
abstract: This study examined whether spending on star players drives team success in the NFL from 2011 to 2020 using Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA). Four DEA models evaluated overall payroll efficiency, offensive star spending efficiency, defensive star spending efficiency, and quarterback spending efficiency across all 32 NFL teams. Star players were defined as those earning above the positional median salary. Efficiency scores were regressed against team success metrics including win-loss record, playoff appearances, and Super Bowl outcomes. The analysis found that payroll efficiency — not raw spending — was the primary driver of team success. Defensive star spending efficiency showed the strongest association with playoff and championship outcomes. Teams that efficiently allocated resources to defensive stars outperformed those that simply spent the most on offensive stars or quarterbacks.
tldr: Using Data Envelopment Analysis on 10 years of NFL salary and performance data, this study shows that it's not how much you spend on star players, but how efficiently you allocate that spending — particularly on the defensive side — that predicts playoff and Super Bowl success.
methods: Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) applied to salary cap and performance data for all 32 NFL teams from 2011 to 2020. Four DEA models constructed: overall payroll efficiency, offensive star spending, defensive star spending, and QB spending. Star player threshold defined as above positional median salary. Efficiency scores regressed against win-loss record, playoff appearances, and Super Bowl outcomes.
findings: Payroll efficiency was a stronger predictor of success than absolute spending levels. Defensive star spending efficiency had the strongest association with playoff appearances and Super Bowl wins. Teams with high overall DEA efficiency scores were more likely to reach the postseason. QB spending efficiency alone was not a significant predictor of team success.
limitations: Study relies on publicly available salary cap data which may not fully capture actual player compensation (signing bonuses, restructured deals). DEA models require assumptions about inputs/outputs. Causal inference is limited by observational design. Star player threshold based on median salary may not fully capture market value.
practicalImplications: NFL front offices and salary cap managers should prioritize efficiency in resource allocation over total spending. Investing heavily in a few marquee players at the expense of roster depth may be suboptimal. Defensive star efficiency appears to be a particularly high-leverage area for competitive success.
athleteDev: Highlights the organizational and roster-construction dimension of player development — identifying and retaining high-efficiency defensive contributors may yield greater team success than chasing the highest-priced offensive stars.

citation: Safar Cherati A, Khalifeh Soltani S, Moghadam N, et al. Is there a relationship between lower-extremity injuries and foot postures in professional football players? A prospective cohort study. Science and Medicine in Football. 2020. doi:10.1080/24733938.2020.1870711
abstract: This prospective cohort study investigated associations between foot posture and lower-extremity injuries in 420 Iranian professional football (soccer) players over the 2015–2016 season. Foot posture was assessed using four methods: the Foot Posture Index (FPI), optical foot scanning, static arch index, and navicular drop test. Players were followed throughout the season and 155 lower-extremity injuries were recorded. Pronated foot posture was associated with a relative risk of 1.8 to 2.3 for hamstring injuries. Abnormal foot posture was associated with medial collateral ligament (MCL) injuries. Flat foot was linked to time-loss injuries requiring absence from training or competition. The study supports routine foot posture assessment as part of pre-season injury screening in professional football.
tldr: In a season-long study of 420 professional football players, pronated foot posture increased hamstring injury risk nearly 2-fold and flat foot was linked to time-loss injuries — supporting pre-season foot screening as a standard injury risk assessment tool.
methods: Prospective cohort design. 420 Iranian professional football players assessed pre-season using four foot posture measurement tools: Foot Posture Index (FPI-6), optical foot scanning, static arch index, and navicular drop test. Players followed throughout the 2015–2016 competitive season. Lower-extremity injuries recorded by team medical staff. Relative risk (RR) calculated for injury associations by foot type.
findings: 155 lower-extremity injuries recorded over the season. Pronated foot posture associated with hamstring injuries (RR 1.8–2.3 depending on measurement tool). Abnormal foot posture (particularly pronation) associated with MCL injuries. Flat foot linked to time-loss injuries. No significant relationship between supinated foot type and specific injury categories. FPI and navicular drop test showed the strongest predictive associations.
limitations: Study conducted in Iranian professional football — generalizability to American football players is limited. Foot posture assessment methods vary in reliability. Study does not control for all confounding variables (e.g., training load, previous injury history). Injury definitions and recording methods may introduce ascertainment bias.
practicalImplications: Pre-season foot posture screening using FPI or navicular drop test can identify players at elevated risk for hamstring and MCL injuries. Athletes with pronated or flat foot morphology may benefit from orthotic interventions, targeted strength programming for intrinsic foot muscles, or modified loading progressions.
athleteDev: Foot posture screening should be integrated into annual physical evaluations for football athletes. Corrective interventions for pronated or flat foot types — including orthotic support and foot/ankle strengthening — may reduce lower-extremity injury burden across a season.
rtp: Abnormal foot posture (pronation, flat foot) is a risk factor for hamstring and MCL injuries. Post-injury evaluation should include foot posture reassessment, and RTP protocols may benefit from orthotic consultation to reduce reinjury risk.

citation: Luo EJ, Anastasio AT, Stauffer T, et al. Jones Fracture in the National Football League. Sports. 2024;12(1):7. doi:10.3390/sports12010007
abstract: This PRISMA-compliant systematic review examined outcomes of Jones fractures (proximal 5th metatarsal fractures) in NFL players. Six retrospective studies meeting inclusion criteria were analyzed, encompassing 285 Jones fractures. All identified players achieved return to play (RTP). Time to RTP ranged from 6 to 27 weeks depending on surgical technique and augmentation. Refracture rates ranged from 4% to 12%. The primary surgical treatment was intramedullary (IM) screw fixation, with some studies reporting use of bone marrow aspirate concentrate (BMAC) and demineralized bone matrix (DBM) as biological augmentation. The review found no statistically significant difference in RTP rates across surgical techniques, though augmentation may reduce refracture risk.
tldr: A systematic review of 285 Jones fractures in NFL players found a 100% return-to-play rate, with time to return ranging 6–27 weeks. Intramedullary screw fixation is the standard surgical approach, and biological augmentation (BMAC/DBM) may reduce the 4–12% refracture risk.
methods: PRISMA-compliant systematic review. Literature search of PubMed, MEDLINE, and Cochrane databases for studies reporting Jones fracture outcomes in NFL players. Six retrospective cohort studies met inclusion criteria. Data extracted on fracture classification, surgical technique, time to RTP, RTP rate, and refracture rate. Qualitative synthesis performed; meta-analysis not conducted due to heterogeneity.
findings: 285 Jones fractures identified across 6 studies. Return to play rate: 100%. Mean time to RTP: ranged 6–27 weeks across studies. Refracture rate: 4–12%. Primary surgical intervention: IM screw fixation. Biological augmentation (BMAC, DBM) used in some cases with potential reduction in refracture risk. No significant differences in RTP rates between surgical technique variations.
limitations: All included studies were retrospective in design, limiting causal inference. Heterogeneity in fracture classification, surgical technique, and outcome reporting precluded meta-analysis. Small sample sizes in individual studies. Selection bias possible as only published cases captured. Long-term outcomes (career longevity, chronic pain) not well reported.
practicalImplications: NFL medical staff can counsel players with Jones fractures that full return to play is expected with appropriate surgical management. Recovery timelines of 6–27 weeks should be communicated to coaches and front offices for roster planning. Biological augmentation should be considered to minimize refracture risk in high-demand positions.
athleteDev: Jones fractures are a known occupational risk in football given the cutting and lateral loading demands of the sport. Preventive strategies including footwear optimization, load monitoring, and bone health monitoring (Vitamin D, calcium) may reduce incidence in high-risk players.
rtp: 100% RTP expected following surgical IM screw fixation for Jones fractures in NFL players. RTP timeline: 6–27 weeks. Refracture risk 4–12% warrants post-RTP monitoring and consideration of biological augmentation at time of surgery. RTP criteria should include full weight-bearing, pain-free sport-specific movement, and imaging confirmation of healing.

citation: Johnson QR, Yang Y, Cabarkapa D, et al. Key Performance Indicators for College American Football Starters: An Exploratory Study. Journal of Functional Morphology and Kinesiology. 2025;10(1):19. doi:10.3390/jfmk10010019
abstract: This exploratory cross-sectional study profiled key performance indicators (KPIs) in 16 NCAA Division-II college American football starters during pre-season 2022. Measures included body composition (InBody 270 BIA), joint kinematics during bilateral squat using the DARI markerless motion capture system, isometric mid-thigh pull (IMTP) on force plates, countermovement jump (CMJ), and multi-rebound jump (MRJ). Significant position-group differences were found in body composition (p=0.004), muscular strength (p=0.01), and muscular power (p=0.03). No significant differences were found in joint kinematics between position groups, suggesting movement quality may be a shared KPI across all starters regardless of position. The study provides a reference dataset for position-specific performance profiling in college football.
tldr: In 16 NCAA Division-II football starters, body composition, muscular strength (IMTP), and power (CMJ/MRJ) differed significantly by position group, but joint kinematics during bilateral squat did not — suggesting movement quality is a universal performance standard while strength and power benchmarks should be position-specific.
methods: Cross-sectional observational design. 16 NCAA Division-II American football starters assessed during 2022 pre-season. Body composition: InBody 270 bioelectrical impedance analysis. Joint kinematics: DARI markerless motion capture system during bilateral squat. Muscular strength: isometric mid-thigh pull (IMTP) on dual force plates. Muscular power: countermovement jump (CMJ) and multi-rebound jump (MRJ) on force plates. One-way ANOVA with post-hoc testing used to assess position group differences.
findings: Significant position-group differences in body composition (p=0.004), muscular strength via IMTP (p=0.01), and muscular power via CMJ/MRJ (p=0.03). No significant differences in joint kinematics between position groups (p>0.05). Linemen showed greatest body mass and IMTP peak force; skill positions showed greatest relative power output. Movement quality (bilateral squat kinematics) was comparable across all starter position groups.
limitations: Small sample (n=16) limits statistical power and generalizability. Single institution NCAA Division-II cohort — findings may not apply to Power Five or NFL-level athletes. DARI system reliability in field settings not fully established. Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference or longitudinal tracking.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning staff should use position-specific benchmarks for body composition, IMTP, and CMJ/MRJ when evaluating college football athletes. Joint kinematics quality targets (bilateral squat) can be applied universally across positions. The DARI markerless system provides a practical, rapid field-based movement screen for team settings.
athleteDev: This study supports a position-specific approach to strength and power development benchmarks while using universal movement quality standards. Pre-season profiling using IMTP, CMJ, MRJ, and markerless motion capture can identify developmental gaps in college football starters and guide individualized training prescriptions.
rtp: IMTP, CMJ, and MRJ force-plate assessments provide objective strength and power benchmarks that can anchor RTP criteria for injured college football players. Joint kinematics screening via markerless motion capture offers a practical, no-marker-required movement quality assessment for post-injury clearance.

citation: Labbe A, Foret W, Troy M, Page P, Savoie F "Buddy." Kinematic Sequencing of the Football Pass Using Inertial Motion Analysis. Int J Sports Phys Ther. 2025;20(8):1214-1221.
abstract: This pilot descriptive cross-sectional study used wireless Noraxon Ultium IMUs (16 sensors) on 8 right-handed quarterbacks (4 high school, 4 collegiate; mean age 16.4 years) performing 9 drop-back passes at 10, 20, and 30 yards. Kinematic data were analyzed at four points of interest — foot contact, maximal external rotation (MaxER), ball release, and maximal internal rotation — using Noraxon MyoMotion 3.18 software. Shoulder abduction and external rotation peaked at 112° and 134° at MaxER, elbow flexion peaked at 100°, and minimal hip-shoulder separation (11°) occurred at ball release, with trunk variables converging to near-neutral just before release.
tldr: This pilot study provides the first IMU-based kinematic sequence data for football quarterbacks, showing that lower body posture remains relatively stable throughout the throw while the trunk shifts away from the throwing side and converges to near-neutral at ball release. Hip-shoulder separation and trunk rotation patterns mirror key principles from baseball pitching research but differ in elbow extension angle and trunk involvement. For QB development staff, these normative angles provide a baseline for pre-season assessment, injury risk monitoring, and rehabilitation benchmarks — particularly for shoulder ER range, lumbar extension load during cocking, and the importance of coordinated hip-trunk sequencing for power generation.
methods: Pilot descriptive cross-sectional study. Eight healthy, right-handed, pain-free quarterbacks (4 high school, 4 collegiate) with at least 2 years of experience performed 3 drop-back passes at each of 3 distances (10, 20, 30 yards) for 9 passes per participant. Sixteen Noraxon Ultium IMUs were placed on lower extremities, trunk, and upper extremities. Kinematic sequence algorithm in Noraxon MyoMotion 3.18 identified joint angles at four points of interest. Mean angles were calculated for shoulder abduction/ER, elbow flexion, knee flexion, trunk inclination/rotation, lumbar extension, pelvis rotation, and hip-shoulder separation.
findings: Shoulder abduction and ER peaked at 112° and 134° at MaxER; elbow flexion peaked at 100°. Shoulder ER at ball release was 56°, elbow extended from 99° to 31°. Stride knee flexion averaged 29°–46°, peaking at ball release. Trunk averaged 23° lumbar extension at MaxER. Trunk rotation began at 40° rightward at foot contact and reversed to 21° leftward at MaxER. Hip-shoulder separation maintained ~20° in cocking phase, quickly reversed to 20° favoring left trunk in follow-through, with only 11° separation at ball release. All trunk measures converged near neutral just before ball release.
limitations: Small sample (n=8) of only right-handed high school and collegiate QBs limits generalizability. Throwing distances were not analyzed separately. IMU limitations include potential skin artifact, electromagnetic interference, and signal drift. IMU data is not directly comparable to gold-standard optical motion capture. The controlled setting cannot replicate real-game spontaneous throwing mechanics.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine professionals can use these normative kinematic angles as baselines for pre-season QB assessments. High shoulder ER (~134°) underscores the need for shoulder flexibility and stability training. Sequencing shoulder strengthening with opposite hip activation through the kinetic chain facilitates hip-shoulder separation. Monitoring hip-shoulder separation and changes in trunk rotation patterns can inform both performance enhancement and injury prevention strategies throughout the season. IMU technology enables repeated field-based assessments without lab constraints.
athleteDev: For QB development, this study establishes field-accessible kinematic benchmarks: ~112° shoulder abduction, ~134° ER at MaxER, and ~46° peak knee flexion. Coaches can use IMU assessments to evaluate throwing mechanics at multiple distances during pre-season. Trunk rotation of ~60° total in the transverse plane and hip-shoulder separation principles should guide QB-specific strength and mobility programming. Lumbar extension demands (~23°) during cocking highlight the importance of core and thoracic mobility in QB training.
rtp: For QBs returning from shoulder or elbow injuries, these normative IMU-derived angles provide reference targets: ~134° shoulder ER, ~100° elbow flexion at cocking, and ~56° shoulder ER at ball release. Progressive achievement of these kinematic benchmarks can serve as objective milestones in return-to-throw protocols. The study's pre-season assessment model supports using IMU-based kinematic profiling to establish individual baselines for comparing post-injury mechanics before clearing for full competition.

citation: Josse CM, Morin J-B, Clark KP, Greenshields JT, Wellman AD. Kinetics and kinematics of initial sprint acceleration performance in Division I collegiate American football players. J Sports Sci. 2025. doi:10.1080/02640414.2025.2567779.
abstract: This pilot study collected the first direct measurements of kinetic and kinematic variables associated with initial sprint acceleration in 123 male NCAA Division I football players categorized into Skill (DB, WR, RB), Big-Skill (TE, OLB, ILB, QB), and Lineman (OL, DL) groups. Players performed maximal sprints of 20–30 m from a three-point stance; kinetics were derived from a linear motorized encoder and force-velocity-power (FVP) profiles were calculated. Kinematics were derived from slow-motion video at 240 Hz analyzed in Kinovea. Maximal power (Pmax) had the largest relationship with 0–10 m time across all position groups (r = −0.60 to −0.90), while key kinematic variables at toe-off and touchdown also showed large significant relationships with Pmax.
tldr: This is the first study to provide direct kinetic and kinematic sprint acceleration data in Division I football players by position group. Pmax is the single strongest predictor of 0–10 m acceleration across all positions, making it a priority training target. Skill players are fastest, linemen slowest, with distinct mechanical profiles: linemen have longer contact times, smaller flight lengths, and less favorable limb angles. Practically, performance staff should prioritize increasing Pmax and optimizing FVP profile orientation for position-specific sprint distances, and use kinematic feedback (swing leg hip/knee flexion at toe-off, shank angles at touchdown) to identify and correct sprint mechanics that limit acceleration.
methods: Pilot study with 128 male NCAA Division I players (age 21±2 yr; mass 101.5±20.0 kg) performing 1–2 maximal sprints of 20–30 m from a three-point stance on indoor artificial turf. Kinetics from 1080 Sprint linear encoder (333 Hz) in isotonic mode; FVP profiles calculated per validated field method (Samozino et al.). Kinematics from 240 Hz slow-motion video analyzed in Kinovea with 18-point manual digitization; first 3 steps including initial push-off analyzed. ICC = 0.96 for sprint trial reliability; ICC = 0.96±0.03 for kinematic marker reliability. Groups: Skill (n=62), Big-Skill (n=37), Lineman (n=24). ANOVAs with Bonferroni post-hoc and Pearson correlations used.
findings: Pmax (W/kg) had large-to-nearly-perfect relationships with 0–10 m time for all groups (r = −0.60 to −0.90, p<0.001). Skill group: Pmax 18.6±1.4 W/kg, 10 m time 1.93±0.07 s. Big-Skill: 17.0±1.5 W/kg, 2.00±0.07 s. Lineman: 14.3±1.6 W/kg, 2.14±0.08 s. F0 had large-to-very-large relationships for Lineman (r=−0.80) and Big-Skill (r=−0.65) but not Skill (r=−0.27). RFmax only significantly related to Lineman 0–10 m (r=−0.72). Spatiotemporally, contact time (r=−0.69) and flight length (r=0.56) had large correlations with Pmax. At toe-off, swing leg knee angle (r=−0.67), swing leg hip angle (r=−0.59), and CM-toe distance (r=−0.55) had large correlations. At touchdown, stance and swing shank angles (r=−0.57 each) and stance knee angle (r=−0.51) had large correlations.
limitations: Kinematic-FVP relationships reported only across the whole population, not at inter-group level. Lineman group was relatively small (n=24). Data from Division I collegiate players may not generalize to NFL populations. Training interventions were not identical across two testing cohorts. Some recordings were captured at 120 Hz rather than 240 Hz due to IOS software issues. No analysis of intra-group or intra-positional fastest vs. slowest performers.
practicalImplications: Pmax is the primary kinetic KPI for initial acceleration monitoring in all football players. Training should prioritize increasing Pmax, with secondary attention to FVP profile orientation appropriate for position-specific sprint distances. For linemen (≥106 kg), F0 and mechanical effectiveness (RFmax) become increasingly important. Coaches and S&C staff should use sprint encoder profiling alongside video-based kinematic assessment to identify swing leg mechanics (hip/knee flexion at toe-off) and landing shank angles (at touchdown) as technique targets for acceleration improvement.
athleteDev: Position-specific normative kinetic and kinematic data from this study allow football performance staff to benchmark 0–10 m sprint acceleration for Skill, Big-Skill, and Lineman groups. Sprint FVP profiling with a linear encoder is now feasible as a standard off-season testing tool to monitor Pmax development. Linemen require force-oriented training while skill players benefit from velocity-oriented approaches. Kinematic cues — greater swing leg hip/knee flexion at toe-off, toe further behind CM, and reduced shank angles at touchdown — are measurable technical targets for acceleration phase coaching.
rtp: This study's FVP profile and kinematic normative data can serve as return-to-sprint benchmarks for football players recovering from lower extremity injuries. Pmax deficits relative to pre-injury or position-group norms provide an objective threshold before clearing for full-speed sprint work. Contact time prolongation and flight length reduction (common compensations post-injury) can be monitored via linear encoder testing. Kinematic asymmetries at toe-off and touchdown can identify limb-loading hesitancy before return to practice.

citation: Andriolo L, Marín Fermín T, Gaggia GMMC, Serner A, Kon E, Papakostas E, Massey A, Verdonk P, Filardo G. Knee Cartilage Injuries in Football Players: Clinical Outcomes and Return to Sport After Surgical Treatment: A Systematic Review of the Literature. Cartilage. 2024;1-15.
abstract: This systematic review analyzed clinical outcomes and return-to-sport after surgical management of knee cartilage lesions in football (soccer) players. Fifteen studies on 409 football players (86% men, 14% women, mean age 26.3 years) were included from PubMed, Web of Science, and Cochrane Library searches through August 2023. Surgical techniques studied included arthroscopic debridement, bone marrow stimulation (BMS), scaffold-augmented BMS, osteochondral autograft transplantation (OAT/mosaicplasty), autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI), and matrix-assisted autologous chondrocyte transplantation (MACT). Lesion size influenced treatment choice, with debridement used for smallest lesions (~1.1 cm²) and ACI for largest (~5.8 cm²). Fastest recovery was seen with debridement and scaffold-augmented BMS (~2.5–3 months); OAT averaged 6.1 months and MACT 10.3 months to return to sport.
tldr: This systematic review synthesizes the best available evidence on surgical treatment of knee cartilage injuries in competitive football players. The key message for sports medicine staff is that treatment choice is driven by lesion size, and microfractures (BMS) — while most commonly used and fastest to RTS at ~4.6 months — show clinical deterioration over time. Scaffold-augmented BMS and OAT offer better durability, while MACT provides more stable long-term outcomes but requires ~10 months recovery. Decision-making in elite athletes must balance early return-to-sport against long-term joint health. Staff should set realistic expectations: overall RTS to previous level ranges from 33% (ACI, large lesions) to 100% (debridement/scaffold-BMS, small lesions), and combined ACL/cartilage lesions carry a 5-fold increased OA risk.
methods: Systematic literature review per PRISMA guidelines using PubMed, Web of Science, and Cochrane Library (search August 7, 2023). Included studies with football player populations, knee cartilage injuries, and surgical management. Methodological quality assessed with modified Coleman Methodology Score (CMS); risk of bias assessed with RoB2 (RCT) and RoBANS2 (non-randomized studies). Fifteen studies included: 9 prospective case series, 2 retrospective case series, 1 RCT, 1 prospective comparative study, 1 case report, 1 multicenter survey (N=409 football players total).
findings: BMS (n=139): RTS to same level 81%, mean 4.6 months; clinical scores improve but deteriorate over time. Scaffold-augmented BMS (n=36): 100% RTS at same level, 2.5–3 months; short follow-up only. OAT/mosaicplasty (n=122): 67% RTS to same level, 6.1 months; RCT showed OAT superior to microfractures (93% vs 52% RTS). ACI first generation (n=49): 33% RTS, 18.1 months; used for large lesions (~5.8 cm²). MACT (n=48): 69% RTS to same level, 10.3 months; more durable long-term vs BMS. Mean modified CMS of included studies was 43.5/100, indicating low methodological quality with high risk of bias overall.
limitations: Low methodological quality (mean CMS 43.5/100) and high risk of bias across studies. Many studies mix football players with broader athlete populations. Region-based patterns in surgical technique use introduce confounding. No comparative studies exist for OAT vs. MACT in professional football players specifically. Most ACI data is from older first-generation techniques. Evidence is insufficient for definitive consensus guidelines.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine staff managing football players with knee cartilage injuries should expect: debridement suitable for small lesions with fastest RTS (~2.5 months) but risk of progression; BMS most commonly used with fastest RTS among repair techniques (~4.6 months) but potential for long-term deterioration; OAT/mosaicplasty offering better durability for lesions ~2.2 cm² with ~6 months RTS; MACT best long-term results but longest recovery (~10 months). Lesion size, subchondral involvement, and athlete age/level should guide surgical decision-making. Combined ACL and cartilage lesions dramatically increase OA risk and warrant careful long-term monitoring.
athleteDev: American football players with knee cartilage injuries represent a similar population to soccer players in terms of high-impact loading demands. This review's framework — lesion-size-guided treatment selection and RTS timelines — is directly applicable to football athlete management. BMS (microfractures) is the fastest path back but may compromise long-term joint health in young football players who face many more competitive seasons. For development-stage athletes (college and early professional), scaffold-augmented BMS or OAT may be preferable to prioritize durability over the earliest possible RTS.
rtp: Return-to-sport timelines from this review provide benchmarks for football RTP planning: debridement ~2.5 months; scaffold-augmented BMS ~2.5–3 months; BMS/microfractures ~4.6 months; OAT/mosaicplasty ~6.1 months; MACT ~10.3 months; ACI ~18 months. Key RTP predictors include shorter preoperative symptom duration, younger age, and smaller lesion size. Younger patients and those with trauma-related (vs. degenerative) lesions have better RTS outcomes. Previous surgery is the strongest negative predictor for returning to prior performance level. Football staff should monitor for long-term BMS deterioration and plan re-evaluation imaging at 1–2 years post-procedure.

citation: Hernandez RJ, Lamplot JD, Hammond KE, Joshi NB, Wong PK, Umpirrez M, Singer A, Gonzalez FM. Knee Injuries in the Elite American Football Player: A Descriptive Pictorial Imaging and Mechanism of Injury Review. J Comput Assist Tomogr. 2022;00:00-00.
abstract: This narrative review article, supported by illustrative MRI and radiograph cases from Emory University, describes the most common knee injuries in elite American football players and the imaging findings used to diagnose them. Injury types covered include MCL sprains (Grades 1–3 including distal Stener lesions and ramp lesions), medial and lateral meniscal tears (bucket handle, radial, root, Wrisberg rip), ACL tears and secondary signs, PCL tears, posterolateral corner (PLC) injuries, posteromedial corner (PMC) injuries, extensor mechanism ruptures, IT band syndrome, bone contusion patterns (pivot-shift, dashboard, hyperextension, clip, patellar dislocation), and patellar instability. The review synthesizes epidemiology, MRI characteristics, and treatment frameworks for each injury type.
tldr: This comprehensive pictorial review is an essential reference for sports medicine staff evaluating knee MRI in American football players. The knee is the most commonly injured joint in football (75% of injuries during games), and this paper provides a systematic imaging-based framework for each major injury type. Key highlights include: MCL injury in 78% of knee sprains; lateral meniscus injury twice as common as medial in NFL Combine athletes; 10% of NFL Combine players report prior ACL tear; articular cartilage defects in up to 60% of NFL prospective players; PCL injury more common on artificial turf; patellar instability ~3% of knee injuries. Understanding characteristic bone contusion patterns (pivot shift, clip, hyperextension, dashboard) allows radiologists and clinicians to predict ligamentous injury from MRI alone.
methods: Narrative/descriptive review article with pictorial case examples. Literature synthesis from published epidemiology, imaging studies, and clinical outcome data in NFL and collegiate football players. Imaging examples sourced from Emory University's musculoskeletal radiology department. Injuries reviewed: MCL (including ramp lesions), meniscal pathology, ACL, PCL, PLC, PMC, extensor mechanism, IT band, bone contusions, and patellar instability. MRI sequences and findings described for each injury type. Relevant radiograph findings and grading systems summarized.
findings: MCL injured in 78% of knee sprains at NFL training camp; distal sMCL tears (5% of MCL injuries) and Stener lesions have poorer healing. Lateral meniscus tears occurred twice as often as medial (15.6% vs 7.8%) in NFL Combine athletes; prior partial meniscectomy associated with up to 27% prevalence of radiographic knee OA. ACL tear reported in 10% of NFL Combine athletes; noncontact mechanism now considered more common in professional football. PCL injury in 3% of NCAA football players; 67% more common on artificial turf. PLC injury in 1% of NFL Combine athletes; 70% treated surgically. Articular cartilage defects in up to 60% of NFL Combine players; most asymptomatic. Patellar instability in ~3% of knee injuries; cartilage injury in up to 95% of first-time dislocations. QT ruptures return-to-play 50% vs 80% for patellar tendon ruptures.
limitations: Narrative review without systematic search or meta-analysis; no pooled outcome data. Imaging examples reflect a single institution's case archive. Generalizability limited to elite (NFL/college) football players. Epidemiological data cited from varying study designs and time periods. MRI sensitivity for ramp lesions remains less than 50%, emphasizing clinical suspicion over imaging alone.
practicalImplications: Football sports medicine staff should use the bone contusion 'footprints' described in this review to predict associated ligamentous injuries from MRI: pivot-shift pattern (posterior lateral tibial plateau + midlateral femoral condyle) should trigger PLC evaluation; clip injuries suggest MCL damage; hyperextension patterns may involve both ACL and PCL. Ramp lesion incidence of 40% with ACL injury warrants high clinical suspicion even with negative MRI. Meniscal preservation should be prioritized given the 27% OA prevalence with prior meniscectomy. Cartilage defects in NFL players are common but often asymptomatic, requiring careful decision-making about intervention timing.
athleteDev: American football athlete development staff should be aware that knee injuries increase proportionally with competition level — emphasizing the importance of neuromuscular training, landing mechanics, and lower extremity strengthening programs throughout development. The high prevalence of asymptomatic cartilage defects (up to 60% at NFL Combine) suggests that routine pre-participation MRI screening in elite football players may reveal subclinical pathology that requires monitoring over time. Extensor mechanism health (pre-season ultrasound screening showing 80% of NCAA players have prior pathology) correlates with future injury risk and should be incorporated into athlete monitoring programs.
rtp: This review provides essential imaging criteria for RTP decision-making in football players. Grade 1–2 MCL injuries are managed conservatively with progressive rehab; Grade 3 or Stener lesions may require surgical intervention before safe return. Meniscal repair RTP is slower than meniscectomy but associated with lower long-term OA risk. ACL RTP protocols should include evaluation for ramp lesions and concomitant PLC/PMC injuries that affect reconstruction stability. PCL injuries managed surgically (15%) have extended timelines. Cartilage defects requiring OAT are generally season-ending, while chondroplasty allows return within weeks. Patellar instability RTP requires restoration of full ROM and quadriceps strength before return.

citation: Ruh E, Kallman T, Lyden E, Greiner J. Knee injury mechanism varies by injury category: Video analysis of multi-ligament knee injuries in the National Football League. Knee Surg Sports Traumatol Arthrosc. 2025;1-8.
abstract: This retrospective video analysis examined 35 multi-ligament knee injuries (MLKIs) from official NFL game footage spanning 1997–2022, identified from publicly available injury surveillance data. Athletes were categorized by ligament injury pattern (Category I: ACL+MCL; IIa: ACL+LCL; IIb: ACL+PCL; IIIa/IIIb: three-ligament injuries). A standardized 9-question questionnaire assessed hip, knee, and ankle position at time of injury. The most common MLKI was ACL+MCL (65%) and occurred predominantly from direct contact (80%). Statistically significant differences in contact direction, knee valgus/varus, knee rotation, knee flexion/extension, and hip rotation were found between injury categories, confirming that injury mechanism varies meaningfully by MLKI type.
tldr: This is the first study to characterize multi-ligament knee injury (MLKI) mechanisms specifically in NFL athletes using video analysis. The key finding is that 80% of MLKIs result from direct contact — contrasting with isolated ACL injuries which are predominantly non-contact or indirect. ACL+MCL injuries (65% of MLKIs) occur from lateral direct contact causing valgus/external rotation on a flexed knee with hip internal rotation — closely resembling isolated ACL injury mechanics. ACL+LCL and ACL+PCL injuries occur from varus/internal rotation on an extended knee, and three-ligament injuries exclusively from direct anterolateral contact. Offensive skill players (QBs and WRs, 17.5% each) are disproportionately affected during ball-carrying plays, with 57% of MLKIs occurring while the player was the ball carrier. Football sports medicine staff must plan for position-specific MLKI risks and be prepared for the substantially worse RTP outcomes with non-MCL MLKIs.
methods: Retrospective video analysis of 40 identified MLKIs from NFL injury surveillance data (1997–2022), confirmed via team reports and ESPN articles. Videos obtained from NFL+ streaming service; 35 cases had analyzable footage (5 excluded — practice injuries). A 9-question questionnaire assessed contact type (direct/indirect/noncontact), direction of contact, and hip/knee/ankle position (flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, internal/external rotation) at time of injury. Two raters (orthopaedic surgeon with sports medicine fellowship and medical student) analyzed videos independently; consensus achieved on all disagreements. Fisher's exact test used for categorical comparisons between MLKI groups.
findings: 35 MLKIs analyzed: 65% Category I (ACL+MCL), 15% Category IIa (ACL+LCL), 5% Category IIb (ACL+PCL), 7.5% each Category IIIa/IIIb. Direct contact: 80%; indirect: 14.3%; noncontact: 5.7%. Lateral/posterolateral force most common (67.9%). Overall: valgus in 85%, external tibial rotation in 73.5%, knee flexion in 70.6%. Category I: 100% valgus, 91.3% external tibial rotation, 82.6% knee flexion, 95.7% hip internal rotation; lateral/posterolateral contact in 84.2%. Category IIa/IIb: 66.7% varus, 66.7% internal tibial rotation, 83.3% knee extension. Category III: 80% valgus, 60% internal tibial rotation, 80% knee flexion; 100% direct contact; all three-ligament injuries. Significant differences across groups: knee valgus/varus (p<0.001), knee rotation (p=0.002), knee flexion/extension (p=0.009), hip rotation (p=0.009).
limitations: No centralized verified injury database; reliance on publicly available surveillance data may under-capture lower-profile injuries and overrepresent catastrophic direct-contact events. Ligament injury grade and degree of laxity could not be confirmed without imaging. Video quality was variable, especially pre-2014. Exact timing of ligament rupture within the dynamic injury event cannot be determined. Cohort size is limited for rarer injury categories (ACL+PCL, three-ligament injuries). Study does not evaluate exposure rates by surface type.
practicalImplications: Football sports medicine staff should recognize that MLKIs are predominantly direct-contact injuries (80%), particularly during ball-carrying plays targeting offensive skill positions. Unlike isolated ACL injury, prevention strategies based on neuromuscular training for noncontact mechanisms are less applicable to MLKI. RTP prognosis varies markedly: ACL+MCL averages 305 days, 70.8% RTP rate, 43.5% returning to prior performance level; ACL+LCL or ACL+PCL averages 459 days, 55.6% RTP, only 18.5% at prior level; three-ligament MLKI averages 609 days with no players returning to prior level. These data should directly inform pre-injury counseling, contract decisions, and post-injury career planning conversations.
athleteDev: MLKI risk is highest for offensive skill positions (QB and WR each at 17.5%) during high-velocity direct-contact ball-carrying plays, suggesting that targeted protective rule enforcement and practice protocols may reduce exposure for these positions. Training staff should include MLKI education and mechanism awareness in pre-season educational programming. The finding that 97.1% of affected athletes were not wearing knee braces raises questions about prophylactic bracing for high-risk positions, though evidence on brace efficacy for MLKI prevention is limited.
rtp: MLKI RTP outcomes from this study's referenced data provide essential benchmarks: ACL+MCL injuries average 305 days to RTP with 70.8% return rate and 43.5% returning to original performance level — the best outcomes of the MLKI categories. ACL+LCL or ACL+PCL injuries average 459 days, 55.6% RTP, 18.5% at prior level. Three-ligament injuries average 609 days with 0% returning to prior performance. Staff should establish RTP milestones calibrated to injury category severity. The biomechanical differences between categories (valgus/flexion for ACL+MCL vs. varus/extension for ACL+PCL and ACL+LCL) may also inform rehabilitation emphasis — restoring valgus and rotational stability being paramount for ACL+MCL injuries.

citation: McCormick WF Jr, Lomis MJ, Cooper CB, Yeager M, Tsavaris NJ, Patch D. Knee Ligament Injuries in the National Football League: Impact of Field Surface Type During the 2020-2023 Seasons. Cureus. 2024;16(12):e76425.
abstract: This retrospective epidemiological study examined in-game knee ligament tear rates in the NFL across the 2020–2023 seasons, comparing natural turf versus artificial turf surfaces. Data from publicly available sources identified 149 knee ligament tears (ACL, PCL, MCL, LCL) over 1,213 games. Natural turf produced 58 injuries over 524 games (0.111/game) and artificial turf produced 91 injuries over 689 games (0.132/game). Two-sample t-tests found no statistically significant difference between surfaces (combined p = 0.379).
tldr: Over four NFL seasons, artificial turf was associated with slightly more knee ligament tears per game than natural grass (0.132 vs. 0.111), but this difference was not statistically significant. The proportion of injuries on each surface roughly mirrored the proportion of games played there. Applied sport science and medical staff should interpret this as context for field-surface policy debates: surface type alone does not appear to drive a statistically meaningful difference in serious knee ligament injury rates, though continued monitoring — and consideration of other injury types and player preference — remains warranted.
methods: Retrospective analysis of publicly available NFL injury data (fantasydata.com, profootballreference.com, spotrac.com, ESPN, NFL.com) across the 2020, 2021, 2022, and 2023 seasons. Only in-game knee ligament tears (ACL, PCL, MCL, LCL) were included; postseason injuries and non-ligament knee injuries were excluded. A surface containing any artificial component was classified as artificial turf. Injury rates per game were calculated by surface, and two-sample t-tests (p = 0.05 threshold) were used for significance testing.
findings: 149 total knee ligament tears were recorded: 58 on natural turf across 524 games (0.111/game) and 91 on artificial turf across 689 games (0.132/game). ACL was the most common injury type each year. Year-by-year artificial turf rates ranged from 0.088 to 0.152 per game; natural turf ranged from 0.081 to 0.136 per game. No individual year nor the combined four-year period showed a statistically significant difference (p-values: 0.382, 0.854, 0.116, 0.709, and 0.379 combined).
limitations: Reliance on publicly available data may introduce inconsistencies. Player position, weather conditions, and variation in turf type/maintenance were not controlled. Small sample size and focus solely on knee ligament tears limits generalizability. Postseason data were excluded.
practicalImplications: Teams and league administrators cannot conclude from this dataset that switching surface type would meaningfully reduce knee ligament injury rates. However, monitoring of surface characteristics (hardness, fiber density, maintenance) remains important. Other injury types (e.g., concussions) and player preference should also factor into surface decisions. The trend toward higher artificial turf injury rates in most seasons, while not significant, may become clearer with larger samples or when controlling for turf subtype.
athleteDev: For football programs at all levels, this study suggests that surface type should not be the primary focus of ACL/ligament injury prevention efforts; athlete conditioning, biomechanics, and workload management are likely more modifiable factors. Screening and neuromuscular training programs should be prioritized regardless of playing surface.
rtp: Return-to-play decision-making following knee ligament injuries should not be materially altered based on surface type alone. Clinicians can reassure athletes that no surface has been definitively proven safer for knee ligaments in the NFL context, though individual surface characteristics and athlete footwear compatibility remain relevant considerations.

citation: Lewis RA, Cook CR, Smith PA, Stannard JP, Sharp RL, Blecha KM, Cook JL. Knee Ultrasonography to Determine Risk for Noncontact Injuries in Collegiate American Football Players. J Knee Surg. 2019.
abstract: This prospective longitudinal study evaluated whether precareer comprehensive knee ultrasonography could predict noncontact lower extremity injuries over a 5-year collegiate career in 48 NCAA Division I American football players. Ultrasonography was performed on both knees at career entry; injuries were tracked through institutional databases and medical records. 79% of athletes had at least one ultrasonographic abnormality, and 79% sustained at least one noncontact lower extremity injury (mean 2.8/career). Patellar tendon ultrasonographic pathology significantly predicted patellar tendon injury (p = 0.024; OR = 11x), and quadriceps tendon pathology significantly predicted quadriceps muscle-tendon injury (p = 0.0012; OR = 140x).
tldr: A comprehensive preseason knee ultrasound in incoming college football freshmen can reliably flag athletes at elevated risk for patellar tendon and quadriceps muscle-tendon injuries during their careers — with odds ratios of 11x and 140x respectively. However, the scan does not predict overall knee injury burden or injuries to the ACL, MCL, or menisci. Staff should consider integrating ultrasound as one component of a multi-modal precareer screening battery to guide targeted prehabilitation, load management, and athlete education for tendon-vulnerable players.
methods: Prospective longitudinal cohort study at a Midwest NCAA Division I institution. 48 male football players (ages 17–21) underwent standardized comprehensive ultrasonography of both knees as entering freshmen using a portable LOGIQ i (GE Health Care) 10–13 MHz linear array transducer. Structures assessed included medial/lateral menisci, MCL, LCL, patellar tendon, quadriceps tendon, and presence of effusion. Severity scored 0–3. Injuries were tracked over 5-year careers via institutional injury databases and medical records. Statistical analyses included Spearman rank-order correlations, Fisher's exact tests, and odds ratio calculations (OR > 2 = clinically significant, p < 0.05).
findings: 79.2% of athletes had at least one ultrasonographic abnormality; quadriceps tendon (47.9%) and patellar tendon (39.6%) pathology were most common. 79.2% sustained at least one noncontact lower extremity injury (mean 2.8/career); most injuries occurred in years 2–3. Of 132 injuries, ankle (44) and hamstring (29) were most frequent; 31 were knee injuries. Patellar tendon pathology predicted patellar tendon injury (p = 0.024; OR = 11x). Quadriceps tendon pathology predicted quadriceps muscle-tendon injury (p = 0.0012; OR = 140x). Total ultrasonography score did not predict overall injury risk (p = 0.65). Preexisting effusion was present in all athletes with subsequent meniscal injuries but did not reach significance as a predictor.
limitations: Single ultrasonographer limits immediate widespread applicability. Subjective nature of ultrasonographic grading. Injury categorization depended on athlete self-reporting plus clinician documentation, raising risk of underreporting or misclassification. Small sample size (n = 48) at a single institution. Cruciate ligament imaging is limited with ultrasound.
practicalImplications: Preseason knee ultrasound screening is a cost-effective, portable, and radiation-free tool that identifies tendon pathology predictive of future injury. Programs should consider implementing precareer knee ultrasound for incoming athletes, particularly to identify patellar and quadriceps tendon abnormalities, which can then inform individualized prehabilitation, exercise selection, and monitoring protocols. Athletes with abnormal findings should receive targeted education, preventive training, and increased monitoring during years 2–3 when injury risk peaks.
athleteDev: Football programs can use precareer ultrasound screening as part of an athlete development entry assessment. Findings can guide sport science staff in designing individualized tendon loading progressions, identifying athletes who may benefit from extra eccentric strengthening, and flagging those needing closer monitoring during the high-risk second and third playing years.
rtp: For athletes returning from patellar or quadriceps tendon injuries, baseline precareer ultrasound data provides a reference standard for tissue health. Clinicians can use follow-up ultrasound as part of the RTP process to assess tissue healing relative to the athlete's own preinjury baseline, rather than relying solely on population norms.

citation: Battiste IV CI, Danoff CJ, Hanes C. Lending a Hand in Bringing Ballcarriers Down: An Extended Study of Individual Defensive Contributions in the National Football League (NFL). SN Comput Sci. 2025;6:622.
abstract: This study introduced a novel defensive metric called 'slowdowns' to quantify individual defender contributions to ballcarrier speed deceleration in the NFL, using Next Gen Stats player-tracking data from the 2022 NFL season weeks 1–9 (12+ million observations, 136 games). A distance matrix measuring defender-to-ballcarrier proximity was combined with ballcarrier speed curves to assign deceleration credits to the nearest defender at each frame post-peak-speed. Slowdowns correlated strongly with individual tackles (r = 0.92) and assisted tackles (r = 0.82), and defender distance was a statistically significant predictor of ballcarrier speed across all play types.
tldr: Traditional tackle statistics capture only who made the final stop, but this study shows that the nearest defender — even one who never records a tackle — significantly slows the ballcarrier. The 'slowdown' metric, derived from player-tracking data, correlates at 0.92 with individual tackles and reveals high-impact defenders (predominantly linebackers) whose contributions are invisible in box scores. For NFL teams and performance staff, this framework suggests that swarming behavior and hustle metrics are quantifiable and should factor into player evaluation, contract discussions, and film-session feedback, even for athletes who do not finish the play.
methods: Data from the 2024 NFL Big Data Bowl (Kaggle), comprising NFL Next Gen Stats and Pro Football Focus data from weeks 1–9 of the 2022 NFL season. Over 12 million observations across 136 games, 12,483 plays, 1,683 players were analyzed. A distance matrix using the Euclidean distance formula calculated each defender's distance from the ballcarrier each frame (10+ frames/second). Ballcarrier speed curves were tracked in mph and yds/sec from snap to tackle. Slowdown credits were assigned to the nearest defender in the frame preceding each post-peak speed decrease. Pearson correlations, OLS regression (univariate and multivariate), lag testing (1, 2, 3 frames), and Variance Inflation Factor testing were performed.
findings: Slowdowns correlated r = 0.92 with individual tackles and r = 0.82 with assisted tackles across the first 9 weeks of 2022. All top-10 slowdown leaders were linebackers; 9 of 10 also finished top-10 in total tackles. Defender distance under 1 yard predicted ballcarrier speed (coefficient = 0.75, p < 0.001); each additional defender within 5 yards reduced speed by 0.66 yds/sec (multivariate model R² = 0.401). One-frame lag best explained current ballcarrier speed over 2- and 3-frame lags. Missed tackles increased ballcarrier speed by +0.5 yds/sec. Scramble plays showed the largest effect of individual defender proximity (β = 0.94).
limitations: Data limited to weeks 1–9 of a single season; full-season and multi-season validation needed. Slowdown attribution assumes the nearest defender caused the deceleration, which may not always be accurate. The analysis is limited to plays captured in the competition dataset. Positional context and scheme were not fully controlled.
practicalImplications: Coaching staffs should supplement traditional tackle counts with slowdown data to identify unheralded defenders whose proximity and pursuit measurably contribute to stops. Players and agents can use slowdowns to demonstrate value beyond conventional statistics. Teams should reinforce 'swarm' defensive principles, as the data confirm that defenders within 4 yards — even if not making the tackle — significantly reduce ballcarrier speed and gain options.
athleteDev: Slowdown metrics provide football programs with a data-driven way to identify and reward effort and pursuit in practice and game contexts. Defensive linemen, linebackers, and safeties can be evaluated on their ability to close distance, informing position-specific training emphasis on pursuit angles, acceleration, and field recognition.
rtp: While this paper does not directly address injury or RTP, the proximity-speed relationship reinforces the importance of high-speed closing ability in football. For athletes returning from lower-extremity injuries, monitoring their ability to close distance to the ballcarrier — a functional demand of their role — could serve as an objective performance readiness benchmark alongside traditional speed and strength tests.

citation: Ishøi L, Meincke S, Lund AP, Stenholm A, DeLang M, Thornton K, Thorborg K. Less pain, same gain? A randomized controlled trial comparing assisted- versus non-assisted Nordic Hamstring exercise training in elite youth football players. Phys Ther Sport. 2025;73:39-47.
abstract: This single-assessor-blinded randomized controlled trial compared assisted Nordic Hamstring exercise (A-NHE, using elastic bands to reduce eccentric load) versus conventional non-assisted NHE in 31 elite youth football players (U14-U15) over an 8-week in-season program. Both groups achieved approximately 20% improvement in Nordic hamstring strength with no significant between-group difference (p = 0.61). The A-NHE group reported significantly lower hamstring muscle soreness (β = 1.77, p < 0.01) and lower perceived exertion (β = 1.54, p < 0.01) throughout the intervention.
tldr: The assisted version of the Nordic Hamstring exercise (using an elastic band around the chest to reduce eccentric load) produced the same ~20% strength gains as the standard NHE in elite youth footballers over 8 weeks, but with meaningfully less muscle soreness and perceived exertion. For football programs where soreness and athlete buy-in are barriers to NHE adoption — particularly with younger or less-conditioned players — the assisted variation offers a more tolerable entry point without sacrificing strength adaptation. This is practically significant because compliance is a known driver of NHE's injury-prevention efficacy.
methods: Single-assessor-blinded RCT at a Danish Superliga professional football club (in-season, September–November 2021). 31 elite male youth players (U14/U15) were randomized 1:1 to A-NHE (n=16) or NHE (n=15) using an 8-week van der Horst protocol (1–2 sessions/week, 2–3 sets, 5–10 reps/set). A-NHE used Theraband elastic bands progressing from heavy (black) to light (yellow) resistance. Nordic hamstring strength was measured pre/post using H-Station (Fysiometer) force device (ICC = 0.96). Soreness (VAS 0–10) and perceived exertion (Borg CR10) were collected each session. Linear mixed models and intention-to-treat analysis were performed in R.
findings: Both groups improved Nordic hamstring strength by approximately 20%: A-NHE +3.7 kg (95% CI [2.1; 5.3], p = 0.00015); NHE +3.2 kg (95% CI [1.2; 5.2], p = 0.0038). No significant group-by-time interaction for strength (p = 0.61; between-group difference: −0.57 kg). NHE group reported significantly higher soreness (β = 1.77, p = 0.002) and perceived exertion (β = 1.54, p < 0.001) across sessions. No players in the A-NHE group progressed to no assistance by program end. Compliance was similar: A-NHE 76%, NHE 82%.
limitations: Small sample (n = 31); single club limits generalizability. Exploratory, non-preregistered study with flat outcome structure. Elastic band individualization was limited (standardized by phase). Biceps femoris fascicle length — a key surrogate for injury prevention potential — was not measured. Recall bias possible in soreness reporting. Long-term injury prevention and performance outcomes not assessed.
practicalImplications: Sport science and medical staff should consider the assisted NHE as a viable first-line implementation strategy, particularly for youth athletes or those unaccustomed to heavy eccentric loading. Reducing perceived barriers (soreness, fatigue) may improve program adoption and long-term compliance — which is critical since NHE's injury risk reduction is dose-dependent. Progressive introduction using the assisted variation could facilitate positive attitudes toward eccentric training that persist into senior competition.
athleteDev: Football programs at the youth academy level (U14+) should begin implementing structured eccentric hamstring training, as hamstring injury proportion rises sharply around U14. The assisted NHE offers an appropriate entry point for young athletes developing their hamstring strength base, supporting long-term athletic development and reducing time-loss injuries that may hinder progression toward first-team selection.
rtp: The assisted NHE may be particularly useful in the early-to-mid stages of hamstring injury rehabilitation, when full eccentric loading is contraindicated or poorly tolerated. Progressively reducing elastic band assistance as the athlete regains strength mirrors natural RTP progression criteria and allows objective strength benchmarking (H-Station) against normative values and the athlete's own preinjury baseline.

citation: Hannay WM, Sliepka JM, Parker K, Sammons K, Gee AO, Kweon CY, Hagen MS. Limited Return to Preinjury Performance in NCAA Division I American Football Players With Hamstring Injuries. Orthop J Sports Med. 2024;12(5):23259671241243345.
abstract: This case series used GNSS/LPS wearable technology (Catapult) to objectively measure pre- and postinjury athletic performance in 20 NCAA Division I football players sustaining 25 acute hamstring strains over a 4-year period. Players returned to play at a mean of 9.2 days, but at mean follow-up of 425 days only 4 of 25 cases (16%) achieved preinjury levels in all four performance metrics (high-velocity maintenance, maximal velocity, triaxial acceleration, IMA). High-velocity maintenance was most consistently impaired; mean postinjury distance at speed was 442 m vs. 722 m preinjury (P = .016).
tldr: College football players return to practice quickly (mean 9.2 days) after hamstring strains, but objective wearable-technology data reveals that most do not recover preinjury speed and acceleration metrics even 13+ months later. The ability to sustain high-speed velocity (>12 mph) was the most persistently impaired metric, falling to roughly half the preinjury value for non-recoverers. This challenges the common reliance on symptom resolution and clinical clearance alone as return-to-play criteria and underscores the need for objective performance benchmarks — particularly high-speed running metrics — before declaring full recovery.
methods: Prospective case series at a single NCAA Division I football program (September 2016–August 2020). Certified athletic trainers recorded all hamstring injuries in the EMR; starting players wore Catapult S5 GNSS/LPS devices recording position at 10 Hz and triaxial acceleration at 100 Hz during all practices and games. Four metrics compared pre- vs. postinjury maximums: (1) distance maintaining high velocity (>12 mph), (2) maximal velocity, (3) total triaxial acceleration (player load), and (4) inertial movement analysis (IMA, >3.5 m/s event count). Paired 2-tailed t-tests assessed significance. Final sample: 25 injuries in 20 players (33 excluded for lack of device data).
findings: All 25 injuries resulted in return to play (mean 9.2 days). At mean follow-up of 425 days: only 4 cases (16%) met all 4 preinjury metrics; only 12 cases (48%) met 2 of 4 metrics; 4 players met no metric. High-velocity maintenance was most impaired (8 of 25 recovered; mean 722 vs. 442 m, P = .016). For cases achieving recovery, mean time to recovery was 204 days (triaxial acceleration), 189 days (maximal velocity), 185 days (IMA), and 85 days (high-velocity maintenance), averaging 166 days across metrics.
limitations: Small cohort (25 injuries, 20 players) from a single institution. Only starters wore devices, limiting generalizability. Retrospective EMR queries could not capture injury grade, exact location, or tendon involvement. Practice and game sessions not differentiated. Seven players (9 injuries) sustained subsequent lower extremity injuries potentially confounding recovery metrics. Catapult proprietary metrics lack validated RTP-specific thresholds.
practicalImplications: Clinicians and coaching staff should not rely solely on symptom resolution and time-loss criteria for hamstring RTP clearance. Objective high-speed running metrics should be incorporated into RTP protocols; athletes cleared by clinical criteria often still show significant performance deficits at the velocities required for game-speed demands. A mean of 163–204 days to recover performance metrics suggests that a single season may be insufficient for full functional recovery, and season-end prognosis discussions should reflect this reality.
athleteDev: This study supports the integration of GNSS/LPS wearable technology into routine practice and game monitoring for football athletes, enabling data-driven identification of performance deficits following hamstring injuries. Athlete development staff should track high-speed distance and maximal velocity trends longitudinally to detect sub-clinical impairments that might otherwise go unnoticed during standard clinical evaluations.
rtp: High-velocity maintenance (>12 mph) emerged as the most consistently impaired and arguably most sport-relevant metric, suggesting it should be a primary RTP threshold for football players post-hamstring strain. Programs should establish athlete-specific preinjury speed baselines and require athletes to demonstrate sustained return to those values — not just symptom freedom — before unrestricted return to full practice and game participation.

citation: Cain EL Jr, Mussell EA, Crawford AE, Ithurburn MP, Layton BO, Fleisig GS, Rothermich MA, Emblom BA, Ryan MK, Dugas JR, Andrews JR. Long-term outcomes of multiligament knee injuries in American football players. Am J Sports Med. 2024;1-9.
abstract: This case series examined longitudinal patient-reported and return-to-sport outcomes in 53 competitive American football athletes (mean age 18.1 years) who underwent primary, single-staged multiligament knee reconstruction between 2001 and 2020, with a mean follow-up of 7.7 years. The mean postoperative IKDC score was 84 ± 16, and 94% of athletes were satisfied with their surgical outcome. Of athletes who attempted return to their preinjury level, 82% successfully did so, though overall RTS was 60% when accounting for all reasons for non-return.
tldr: Despite the severity of multiligament knee injuries (MLKIs), the majority of competitive football athletes who undergo acute, single-staged reconstruction by a fellowship-trained surgeon can expect good knee function and high satisfaction at long-term follow-up. Approximately 82% of those who attempt return to their preinjury level succeed, though this rate lags behind isolated ACL reconstruction outcomes. Staff should counsel athletes that injury severity (2 vs. more than 2 ligaments, ACL-only vs. bicruciate) does not appear to predict outcomes, and that aggressive postoperative rehabilitation is a key contributor to success.
methods: Retrospective case series using a prospectively collected database at a high-volume sports medicine practice (Andrews Sports Medicine and Orthopaedic Center). Patients who underwent single-staged MLKI reconstruction for injuries sustained during competitive American football participation from January 2001 to August 2020 were identified. Patient-reported outcomes were collected at minimum 2-year follow-up via IKDC Subjective Knee Form, surgical satisfaction survey, and return-to-sport assessment. Subgroup comparisons (2-ligament vs. >2-ligament; ACL-MLKI vs. bicruciate MLKI) used independent t-tests and chi-square tests; predictors of outcomes were evaluated via linear and logistic regression.
findings: 53 of 73 eligible athletes (73%) completed follow-up (mean 7.7 ± 4.0 years). Mean IKDC score was 83.7 ± 15.5. 94% were satisfied or very satisfied; 92% felt their procedure met or exceeded expectations. 60% (32/53) returned to preinjury sport overall; 82% (32/39) of those who attempted return succeeded. Mean time to return to practice was 7.9 months and to competition 9.5 months. No significant differences in IKDC or RTS were found between 2-ligament and >2-ligament groups (P = .96 and P = .77, respectively) or between ACL-MLKI and bicruciate groups (P = .89 and P = .77). Age and BMI were not significant predictors of outcomes. 17% required subsequent ipsilateral knee surgery; ACL graft failure rate was 8%.
limitations: No baseline/presurgical patient-reported outcome data; recall bias in RTS assessment; no radiographic follow-up data; relatively small sample size; retrospective design; follow-up not achieved in 27% of eligible patients.
practicalImplications: MLKIs in football athletes treated with acute, single-stage reconstruction by experienced fellowship-trained surgeons yield good long-term outcomes. Sports medicine staff should prioritize early surgical intervention and aggressive postoperative rehabilitation. Athletes can be counseled that injury complexity (number of ligaments, PCL involvement) does not significantly worsen prognosis. A return-to-competition timeline of approximately 9–10 months should be anticipated.
athleteDev: High school and collegiate football athletes sustaining MLKIs can expect high rates of successful return to competitive play with appropriate surgical and rehabilitation management. The majority of athletes in this cohort were high school players, indicating that younger athletes are not at a disadvantage for recovery. Early identification of MLKI and expedited referral to a high-volume specialist is critical for optimal athlete development outcomes.
rtp: 82% of athletes who attempted return to their preinjury competitive level of football were successful after multiligament knee reconstruction. Mean return to competition was 9.5 months post-surgery. Criteria for return included satisfactory clinical exam, isokinetic quadriceps strength greater than 80% of the contralateral limb, and single-leg hop greater than 75% of the contralateral leg. Peroneal nerve involvement did not preclude return to play, with 67% of those affected successfully returning.

citation: Wichmann TK, Wolfson J, Roelofs EJ, Bosch TA, Bach CW, Oliver JM, Carbuhn A, Stanforth PR, Dengel DR. Longitudinal assessment of NCAA Division I football body composition by season and player age. J Strength Cond Res. 2022;36(6):1682-1690.
abstract: This longitudinal study used dual X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) to examine body composition changes by position, season, and player age in 719 NCAA Division I football players (aged 18–22 years) from 5 universities scanned between 2008 and 2018. Players were categorized as Linemen, Big Skill, Skill, or Special Team. Fat mass and percent body fat decreased from preseason/postseason to spring season in all positions, while lean mass decreased from preseason to postseason and then increased during the spring. Fat mass, lean mass, total mass, visceral adipose tissue, and bone mineral density all increased with player age across every position group.
tldr: Body composition in collegiate football players follows predictable seasonal and developmental patterns that staff should use to set realistic expectations. Every position group loses lean mass during the competitive season and regains it in the spring — likely reflecting a shift from performance-focused training to heavy resistance work in the off-season. Over a player's career, fat mass and visceral adipose tissue (VAT) accumulate alongside lean mass gains, and these trends are consistent regardless of position. Staff should monitor VAT in all positions but especially linemen, who carry the highest absolute values and face the greatest long-term cardiovascular risk.
methods: Retrospective analysis of DXA scan data collected from 719 male NCAA Division I football players at 5 universities (University of Minnesota, University of Texas, University of Kansas, University of Nebraska, Texas Christian University) from 2008 to 2018. Players were categorized into Linemen, Big Skill, Skill, and Special Team groups. Seasons were defined as preseason (June–September), postseason (December–February), and spring (March–May). One scan per player per season was used. Repeated ANOVA with linear mixed-effects models assessed body composition differences across age, position, and season. Tukey HSD post-hoc tests were used for multiple comparisons (p < 0.05).
findings: Linemen had significantly greater total mass (129 kg), %BF (27%), fat mass (35 kg), lean mass (89 kg), BMD, and VAT compared with all other position groups across all seasons and ages (p < 0.001). From preseason to postseason, lean mass and BMD decreased significantly in all position groups; from postseason to spring, lean mass increased and %BF, fat mass, and VAT decreased in all groups. Total mass did not change significantly across seasons. With increasing age (18 to 22 years), total mass (+4.2 kg), lean mass (+2.7 kg), fat mass (+1.3 kg), VAT (+0.17 kg), and BMD (+0.1 g/cm²) all increased significantly in all position groups, but %BF did not change significantly with age.
limitations: Different scanning locations and scan technicians across 5 universities; lack of documentation of seasonal activity, nutrition, and injury status; year of eligibility not defined. These biases are partially offset by the large sample size and standardized DXA analysis protocols.
practicalImplications: Performance staff should anticipate lean mass loss during the competitive season and plan spring training to recover it. Fat mass and VAT reductions in the spring indicate that off-season training effectively reduces abdominal obesity. Coaches should focus on building lean mass in players needing size gains while providing nutritional education to prevent unhealthy fat accumulation. Positional norms from this study can guide realistic body composition targets across a player's collegiate career.
athleteDev: Body composition changes are consistent across positions, meaning staff can apply similar monitoring and periodization strategies regardless of a player's role. Players see the most dramatic body composition changes early in their careers (ages 18–19), with changes slowing thereafter. This underscores the importance of high-quality nutrition and strength programming during the freshman year. Linemen's exponentially higher VAT at heavier body masses warrants proactive long-term health counseling as players develop.
rtp: While this study does not directly address return to play, the finding that lean mass is lost during the competitive season has direct implications for RTP decisions after injury. Tracking DXA-measured lean mass and VAT longitudinally can help quantify deconditioning during injury absence and guide clearance thresholds. Staff should account for seasonal variation in body composition when establishing RTP benchmarks — comparing postseason measurements to postseason norms rather than preseason values.

citation: Morrison M, Mann JB, Read DB, Thurlow F, Cowley N, Silverthorne K, McMahon E, Weakley J. Longitudinal changes in strength, power, speed, and body mass in American football players: diminishing returns as training age increases. Int J Sports Sci Coaching. 2025;1-13.
abstract: This retrospective longitudinal study analyzed 2628 testing observations from 512 NCAA Division I football players at a single university across a 15-year period to detail changes in strength, power, speed, and body mass by playing position over a 4-year collegiate career. The study found that large improvements in bench press (ES 1.63) and back squat (ES 1.62) occurred in the first year, but subsequent year-on-year improvements were progressively smaller, demonstrating the law of diminishing returns. All 11 positional groups showed this pattern, though absolute physical qualities differed substantially by position.
tldr: This is the most comprehensive positional normative data set for NCAA Division I football physical qualities to date, with 15 years of longitudinal data. The critical practical insight is that the largest performance gains occur in Year 1, making freshman development the highest-leverage period for strength and conditioning investment. By years 3 and 4, improvements are small in magnitude, meaning coaches should recalibrate athlete and staff expectations accordingly. Position-specific normative values (e.g., offensive linemen Year 1 back squat ~215 kg, wide receivers 40-yard dash ~4.62 s) provide concrete targets for athlete development and recruiting evaluation.
methods: Retrospective analysis of 15 years of standardized off-season testing data from 512 NCAA Division I male football athletes (ages 18–23) at one university.
