Athletes were grouped into 11 positional categories. Testing measures included 1RM bench press and back squat (with Epley equation used if 2–5 reps performed), vertical jump (Just Jump mat), standing broad jump, 40-yard sprint (electronic timing via SpeedTrap), and body mass. Linear mixed models with Athlete ID as a random intercept assessed year-over-year changes by position and for the whole cohort. Tukey-adjusted pairwise comparisons and Cohen's d effect sizes with 95% CIs were calculated.
findings: Across the cohort, Year 1 to Year 2 changes showed large effects in bench press (ES 1.63 ± 0.18) and back squat (ES 1.62 ± 0.18), moderate effects in vertical jump (ES 0.71) and broad jump (ES 0.61), and a small effect in 40-yard sprint (ES −0.44). Year 2 to Year 3 and Year 3 to Year 4 changes were substantially smaller in all measures. Offensive linemen were the heaviest (Year 1: ~129 kg, Year 4: ~137 kg) and strongest (back squat Year 4: ~272 kg), while corners and receivers had the fastest 40-yard times (~4.54–4.62 s) and highest vertical jumps (~88–94 cm). Body mass increased significantly across all 4 years for all positions combined (Year 1: 101.4 kg; Year 4: 106.3 kg; ES 0.78 in Year 1 to Year 2).
limitations: Single-university data from one strength and conditioning program limits generalizability. Small year-to-year modifications to training protocols occurred. Potential reverse survivorship bias exists if athletes with the highest physical qualities declared for the NFL Draft in years 3–4, slightly suppressing later-year group means.
practicalImplications: Coaches and athletes should expect the greatest measurable performance gains in the first year of structured collegiate training. After Year 2, improvements slow considerably, requiring recalibrated goals and potentially greater individualization to continue progress. The normative data by position provide concrete benchmarks for identifying underdeveloped athletes, setting realistic development targets, and supporting recruiting decisions. High school programs should be encouraged to invest in strength and conditioning to narrow the gap before college entry.
athleteDev: The law of diminishing returns in physical development has direct implications for long-term athlete development planning at the college level. Freshmen should receive the most intensive and broad physical development stimulus, as this is when adaptation rates are highest. Position-specific strength and speed targets (e.g., offensive linemen targeting >200 kg back squat and >125 kg body mass entering Year 1) provide actionable benchmarks for high school and collegiate programs. Individualized programming becomes increasingly important as training age accumulates beyond Year 2.
rtp: This study does not specifically address return to play, but its normative data can serve as physical benchmarks for RTP readiness after injury. An injured player returning from a multi-month absence may present with physical qualities resembling a younger training age, and expected recovery trajectories can be informed by Year 1 gains documented here. Strength and power measures such as bench press, back squat, and broad jump can be compared against positional Year 1 baseline values to quantify rehabilitation progress and guide return-to-full-activity decisions.

citation: Makovicka JL, Patel KA, Deckey DG, Hassebrock JD, Chung AS, Tummala SV, Hydrick TC, Gulbrandsen M, Hartigan DE, Chhabra A. Lower back injuries in National Collegiate Athletic Association football players: a 5-season epidemiological study. Orthop J Sports Med. 2019;7(6):2325967119852625.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study analyzed the NCAA Injury Surveillance Program database over 5 academic years (2009/2010 through 2013/2014) to characterize the epidemiology of low back injuries in collegiate football players. From 267 reported injuries, a national estimate of 7076 low back injuries over the 5-year period was generated, yielding an overall injury rate of 2.70 per 10,000 athlete-exposures. The majority of injuries (82%) were new injuries, 64% were classified as unspecified low back pain, only 1.6% required surgery, and approximately 60% of players returned to play within 24 hours.
tldr: Low back injuries are common in collegiate football, occurring at a rate of 2.70 per 10,000 athlete-exposures, with defensive linemen most frequently affected. The vast majority are mild — 60% of players return within 24 hours — but nearly 18% are reinjuries, underscoring the importance of thorough rehabilitation before return to play. The preseason carries the highest injury rate, implicating poor conditioning and core stability deficits as risk factors. Staff should prioritize preseason core strengthening and movement screening to prevent both new and recurrent lumbar injuries.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using the NCAA Injury Surveillance Program (ISP) database for the 2009/2010 through 2013/2014 academic years. A convenience sample of NCAA varsity football programs provided data electronically via electronic health records. Injuries involving the lower back or lumbar region were extracted and analyzed by injury type, event type, season, mechanism, chronicity, position, and time lost from sport. National injury estimates were calculated using poststratification sample weights adjusted for an 88.3% capture rate. Injury rates were expressed per 10,000 athlete-exposures; incidence rate ratios (IRRs) with 95% CIs were calculated.
findings: Estimated 7076 low back injuries nationally over 5 years; overall rate of 2.70 per 10,000 AEs. Competition injury rate (7.10/10,000 AEs) was 3.12 times higher than practice (2.30/10,000 AEs). Preseason rate (3.50/10,000 AEs) was 1.41 times higher than regular season and 4.67 times higher than postseason. Injury distribution by type: unspecified pain 64.2%, contusion 11.8%, sacroiliitis 8.2%, disc herniation 7.6%. Contact and noncontact mechanisms were nearly equal (37.8% vs. 38.3%). Defensive linemen had the most injuries (20.7%). 82.2% were new injuries; 17.8% were reinjuries. Only 1.6% required surgery (disc herniation 15.6% surgical rate; radiculopathy 25.9%). 59.6% returned to play within 24 hours; 53.7% of radiculopathy cases required >21 days absence. Division I and III rates were similar (3.30 and 3.10 per 10,000 AEs).
limitations: NCAA ISP data quality is dependent on athletic trainer documentation accuracy, creating potential for over- or under-reporting. Participation is voluntary, introducing selection bias. Diagnoses are not granular enough to capture spondylolysis/spondylolisthesis separately. Study relied on symptomatic presentation only and did not include imaging data.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and sports medicine staff should implement preseason core strengthening and lumbar stability programs to address the highest-risk period. Given the nearly equal rates of contact and noncontact mechanisms, both load management and movement quality interventions are warranted. The 18% reinjury rate argues strongly for complete rehabilitation and return-to-play clearance protocols rather than early or premature return. Disc herniation and radiculopathy warrant closer monitoring due to higher surgical rates and longer absence durations.
athleteDev: Defensive linemen sustain the most low back injuries among all positions, followed by defensive backs, wide receivers, and offensive linemen. This positional profile can guide position-specific prevention programming — for example, incorporating lumbar stability and hip mobility work for linemen during preseason. The high preseason rate suggests that conditioning deficits from the off-season are a primary risk factor, reinforcing the value of year-round training programs for athlete longevity.
rtp: Approximately 60% of low back-injured collegiate football players return within 24 hours, but injury type greatly influences RTP timeline. Disc herniation results in absence greater than 24 hours in over 72% of cases, and radiculopathy leads to greater than 21 days missed in over 53% of cases. While surgical discectomy data are from the NFL literature (over 80% RTP rate), the collegiate-level surgical rate of 1.6% is low. Staff should use symptom resolution, functional testing, and core stability criteria when making return-to-play decisions for lumbar injuries.

citation: Lamplot JD, Wang D, Weiss LJ, Baum M, Zeidler K, Mack C, Barnes RP, Warren RF, Taylor SA, Rodeo SA. Lower extremity compartment syndrome in National Football League athletes. Sports Health. 2021;XX(X):1-5.
abstract: This retrospective case series used the NFL Injury Surveillance System and electronic medical record system to identify and characterize all cases of lower extremity compartment syndrome in NFL athletes from 2000 to 2017. Over the 18-year period, 22 cases of leg compartment syndrome were identified in 21 athletes. The mechanism was direct impact in 73% of cases, surgery was performed in 68% of cases, and all athletes with acute non-fracture compartment syndrome who underwent surgery returned to full participation within the same season, missing an average of 24.2 days.
tldr: Lower extremity compartment syndrome is rare in NFL players but carries a high surgical rate and significant missed time. The key finding for team physicians and athletic trainers is that the majority of cases result from direct impact to the leg without associated fracture — a mechanism not previously well-described in athletic populations. Expedient diagnosis and prompt fasciotomy lead to excellent same-season return-to-play outcomes. Staff should maintain a high index of suspicion for this diagnosis in any player presenting with leg pain, tightness, and sensory/motor changes after a direct blow, even without fracture on imaging.
methods: Retrospective case series using the NFL Injury Surveillance System (IQVIA) and electronic medical record system. All cases of lower extremity compartment syndrome recorded from 2000 to 2017 were identified, as well as any decompression fasciotomy performed (CPT codes 27600-02, 27892-94). Two authors classified each case as acute compartment syndrome (ACS), acute exertional compartment syndrome (AECS), or chronic exertional compartment syndrome (CECS) based on mechanism, player activity, injury timing, and clinical impression codes. Injury timing, anatomic location, laterality, mechanism, roster position, surgery rates, days to surgery, and days missed were analyzed using Microsoft Excel.
findings: 22 cases of lower extremity compartment syndrome in 21 athletes over 18 years. All cases involved the lower leg only. Mechanism was direct impact in 16 cases (72.7%), acute exertional in 3 cases, chronic exertional in 1 case, and uncharacterized in 2 cases. 50% of cases occurred during regular season games; one-third occurred in linemen, one-third in defensive secondary players. Concomitant tibial fracture in only 2 cases (9.1%). Surgery in 15 of 22 cases (68.2%). For acute non-fracture ACS cases, mean days to surgery was 1 ± 0.6 days; mean days missed was 24.2 ± 15.5 days (range 5–54). All ACS non-fracture cases returned to full participation within the same season.
limitations: Retrospective database study; injury incidence data not directly comparable across the full 2000–2017 period due to documentation system changes (case report forms prior to 2011, IQVIA ISS 2012–2013, NFL EMR from 2014). Off-season injuries were not required to be documented until 2015. Specific diagnostic criteria (compartment pressure measurements) were not available in the database. Performance data after return to play were not available. Underreporting is a recognized limitation of the earlier data years.
practicalImplications: Team physicians and athletic trainers should maintain a high index of suspicion for acute compartment syndrome following any significant direct blow to the lower leg, regardless of whether fracture is present on imaging. Prompt compartment pressure measurement and expedient surgical fasciotomy lead to excellent outcomes — all treated ACS patients returned within the same season. The absence of thigh compartment syndrome cases in this series may be partially attributable to mandatory thigh pad use in the NFL, supporting continued enforcement of this equipment rule.
athleteDev: While compartment syndrome is rare, it is a potentially limb-threatening injury that can abruptly end an athlete's season or career. Its occurrence in linemen and defensive secondary players — positions involving frequent collision — highlights the importance of proper leg protection equipment and collision technique coaching for athlete safety. Awareness of this condition in younger developmental players (high school and college), where thigh pad use may be inconsistent, is critical for athletic trainers at all levels.
rtp: All NFL athletes with acute non-fracture leg compartment syndrome who underwent prompt surgical fasciotomy returned to full participation within the same season, with an average of 24.2 missed days (range 5–54 days). This represents a favorable RTP prognosis when the diagnosis is made and treated expeditiously. Cases associated with tibial fracture had significantly prolonged recovery. AECS cases carry a less predictable RTP timeline and require careful differential diagnosis to avoid delayed treatment and associated myonecrosis or nerve injury.

citation: Venishetty N, Xiao AX, Ghanta R, Reddy R, Pandya NK, Feeley BT. Lower Extremity Injury Rates on Artificial Turf Versus Natural Grass Surfaces in the National Football League During the 2021 and 2022 Seasons. Orthop J Sports Med. 2024;12(8):23259671241265378.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study analyzed 718 lower extremity injuries across the 2021 and 2022 NFL seasons using publicly available data from ESPN and NFL.com. Injuries were classified by type, player position, age, surface, weeks missed, and surgical intervention. The incidence rate was 1.22 injuries/game on natural grass versus 1.42 injuries/game on artificial turf, and multivariable logistic regression found the odds of season-ending surgery were significantly higher on artificial turf (OR = 1.60; 95% CI, 1.28–1.99; P < .05).
tldr: NFL players sustained lower extremity injuries at a meaningfully higher rate on artificial turf compared to natural grass over two recent seasons, and those injuries were 60% more likely to require season-ending surgery. ACL reconstructions and Achilles repairs were the most common surgeries, and they occurred at higher rates on turf. For applied sport science staff, this reinforces existing evidence that surface selection is a genuine player-safety variable — teams and facilities that have a choice should weigh the surgical risk premium associated with artificial turf, and injury surveillance systems should track surface type as a covariate when evaluating lower extremity injury trends.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using publicly available injury records (ESPN, NFL.com) for the 2021 and 2022 NFL regular and playoff seasons. Variables collected: injury type, player position, age, playing surface (16 natural grass stadiums, 13 artificial turf stadiums), weeks missed, and surgical status (minor vs. season-ending). Injury incidence calculated as injuries per game played on each surface. Multivariable logistic regression assessed the influence of surface, weather, age, position, week of season, and prior injury history on odds of season-ending surgery. Statistical analysis performed in R; P < .05 significance threshold.
findings: 718 total lower extremity injuries (327 in 2021; 391 in 2022). Incidence: 1.22/game on natural grass vs. 1.42/game on artificial turf. Season-ending surgeries: 50 on grass, 83 on turf. OR for season-ending surgery on turf vs. grass = 1.60 (95% CI, 1.28–1.99; P < .05). Most common surgeries on turf: ACL reconstruction (n=30), Achilles repair (n=12), patellar ORIF/tendon repair (n=8). Most common on grass: ACL reconstruction (n=17), Achilles repair (n=9), ankle/syndesmosis ORIF (n=7). Weather, age, position, season week, and prior injury history were not significant predictors of season-ending surgery.
limitations: Data sourced from public records (ESPN, NFL.com) may undercount injuries not prominently reported; practice injuries were excluded entirely. Personal factors such as player preference and league-specific surgical decision factors could not be controlled. Preseason data were excluded due to incomplete reporting. The study covered only two seasons, limiting generalizability.
practicalImplications: Artificial turf fields carry a statistically significant 60% increase in odds of season-ending surgery for lower extremity injuries compared to natural grass. Sports medicine and performance staff should document playing surface in injury surveillance databases, advocate for natural grass where feasible, and consider turf-specific cleat selection protocols. High-risk injury categories on turf (ACL, Achilles, extensor mechanism) should inform pre-game warm-up and load management decisions when teams play on artificial surfaces.
athleteDev: For NFL programs and collegiate football using artificial turf, this study supports prioritizing cleat-surface compatibility education during athlete development. Young players developing on artificial turf fields may benefit from targeted lower extremity strength and neuromuscular control training (single-leg stability, landing mechanics) to mitigate the elevated surgical risk. Facilities managing field selection for practice vs. game environments should weigh cumulative turf exposure over a career.
rtp: Return-to-play planning following lower extremity surgery in NFL players should account for the playing surface of upcoming games. Players returning from ACL reconstruction or Achilles repair — the two most common season-ending surgeries on turf — may warrant additional functional testing benchmarks or modified return timelines when the RTP game is played on artificial turf, given the surface's demonstrated association with higher re-injury risk due to reduced cleat-release mechanics.

citation: Herzog MM, Weiss L, Lee RY, Williams T, Ramsden S, Sills AK, Mack CD. Lower Extremity Strains in the US National Football League, 2015-2019. Am J Sports Med. 2023;51:1-10.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study used the NFL's leaguewide electronic health record (EHR) across all 32 teams to characterize lower extremity (LEX) muscle strains — hamstring, adductor, quadriceps, and calf — over five seasons (2015–2019). A total of 5,780 LEX strains were documented among 2,769 players, with a 1-year risk of 26.7%. Hamstring strains comprised 54.7% of all LEX strains, and 69% of all LEX strains resulted in time loss, yielding approximately 16,748 player-days missed annually.
tldr: Roughly 1 in 4 NFL players sustains a lower extremity muscle strain every year, making this injury category a greater source of missed participation days than ACL tears when viewed in aggregate. Hamstring strains dominate, but adductor, calf, and quadriceps strains are also substantial. The highest-risk windows are preseason training camp (especially the first full-contact days after the contact acclimation period ends) and early regular season games. Punt plays carry nearly twice the LEX strain rate of kickoff plays and more than three times the rate of pass plays — a finding with direct implications for special teams design, load management, and prevention targeting. For staff, this data argues for structured ramp-up protocols entering training camp, in-game warm-up considerations for special teams players, and focused secondary prevention given LEX strains' high recurrence rate.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using the standardized NFL leaguewide EHR (all 32 teams, 2015–2019), including offseason, preseason, regular season, and postseason. Included all players who played in at least one NFL game or sustained a LEX strain during NFL participation. LEX strains categorized by muscle group (hamstring, adductor, quadriceps, calf, multiple), time-loss status, season timing, team activity, roster position, and years of service. Game incidence rates calculated via Poisson regression using player-plays and play-type data linked from the Game Statistics and Information System. IRB approval obtained (Mt. Sinai Pro00060188).
findings: 5,780 LEX strains over 5 years among 2,769 players; 1-year risk 26.7% (95% CI, 26.0%–27.3%). 69% (n=4,015) caused time loss; median 12 days missed (IQR 5–25); mean 22.0 days. Annual injury burden: ~16,748 player-days missed, surpassing ACL burden (~12,369 days/season). Strain distribution: hamstring 54.7%, adductor 24.1%, calf 12.6%, quadriceps 8.3%. Most time-loss strains occurred in preseason practices (27%) and regular season games (26%). Training camp peak: 19% of all time-loss strains in <10% of active season; highest day = Day 5 post-veteran report (first/second day of full-contact practice). In games, punt plays had injury rate of 14.9/1,000 plays vs. 7.5 for kickoffs, 4.3 for pass, 2.6 for run plays. Defensive secondary, wide receivers, and linebackers accounted for ~64% of game injuries. Punters/kickers had highest per-player-play rate (3.8/10,000).
limitations: Practice participation data (minutes/plays) were unavailable, preventing practice incidence rate calculations. LEX strain grade was not consistently documented during the study period (82% unknown in 2015, improved to 16% unknown by 2020). Injury burden calculations could not fully account for players who left their team before return or had incomplete records. Days-missed metric does not capture pain burden or reduced performance in athletes who played through injury.
practicalImplications: The spike in LEX strains on Day 5 of training camp — the first or second full-contact day — argues for deliberate progressive exposure protocols entering preseason. Gradual workload ramp-up that mirrors the contact acclimation period philosophy for head injuries may be warranted for muscle strain prevention. Special teams practitioners should consider in-game preparatory protocols (dynamic warm-up between plays, extended activation for punt unit players) and targeted strength training for punt-unit athletes, who face LEX strain rates nearly 3x higher than pass-play rates. Secondary prevention programs focused on recurrence risk are essential given the high rate of repeat LEX strains.
athleteDev: The 1-year strain risk for NFL players is 26.7%, with the highest vulnerability among defensive secondary players, wide receivers, and linebackers — all positions requiring repeated high-speed running and change of direction. Developmental programs should emphasize hamstring and adductor strength across all positions from college through NFL, with particular focus on progressive loading through training camp. Monitoring acute:chronic workload ratios during preseason camp is especially important given the documented Day 5 spike in time-loss LEX strains coinciding with introduction of full-contact practices.
rtp: With a median of 12 days missed per LEX strain, NFL medical staff should anticipate a return-to-play window of 1.5–2 weeks for most hamstring and calf strains, though the mean (22 days) and wide IQR indicate considerable individual variation. High recurrence rates for LEX strains in the NFL argue for objective functional criteria (e.g., isokinetic strength symmetry, sprint velocity benchmarks) before clearance rather than relying on calendar-based timelines alone. Players returning from hamstring strains should be progressively exposed to high-speed running and special teams-specific sprint demands before full clearance.

citation: Taberner M, Haddad FS, Dunn A, Newall A, Parker L, Betancur E, Cohen DD. Managing the return to sport of the elite footballer following semimembranosus reconstruction. BMJ Open Sport Exerc Med. 2020;0:e000898.
abstract: This case report describes the 18-week return to sport (RTS) process for an English Premier League (EPL) footballer following surgical reconstruction of a high-grade semimembranosus (SM) proximal free tendon tear sustained during training. The paper details the surgical approach, post-operative physiotherapy, progressive gym-based and on-pitch rehabilitation using the control-chaos continuum framework, GPS running load monitoring, isometric posterior chain (IPC) and eccentric knee flexor (ENF) strength diagnostics, nutritional support, and MRI-guided clinical decision-making. The player returned to team training at 18 weeks post-surgery and remained re-injury free at 2.5-year follow-up.
tldr: Semimembranosus (SM) injuries are less common than biceps femoris strains but can involve the proximal free tendon and require surgical reconstruction when significant retraction occurs — conservative management in such cases risks permanent loss of force capacity and compensatory injury to other hamstring muscles. This case illustrates a comprehensive, data-informed RTS framework integrating weekly IPC force platform testing (tracking peak force and rate of force development), ENF strength, GPS-based high-speed running progression, serial MRI, and player-reported pain/readiness. The rehabilitation progression — from isometrics through eccentric loading to the control-chaos continuum on pitch — provides a practical template applicable to any hamstring surgical RTS. Key takeaway for staff: objective bilateral asymmetry thresholds (<10% IPC peak force ILA, <15% ENF ILA) should gate on-pitch high-speed running progression, and pre-injury GPS gameload data should guide running load targets throughout rehabilitation.
methods: Single case report of a male EPL footballer with a surgically reconstructed SM proximal free tendon tear (grade 3C on post-injury MRI). Outcome measures included serial MRI (at 7, 11, and 17 weeks post-surgery), unilateral isometric posterior chain (IPC) force platform testing (peak force and force at 100 ms / rate of force development) assessed weekly, bilateral eccentric knee flexor (ENF) strength via NordBord, countermovement jump (CMJ) bilateral kinetics, and GPS-based running load metrics (total distance, high-speed running >5.5 m/s, explosive distance). Nutritional support included ~2 g/kg/BM protein, creatine (5 g/day), omega-3 (3 g/day), and collagen + vitamin C (20 g/80 mg pre-activity from week 8). Rehabilitation followed the control-chaos continuum framework for on-pitch reconditioning.
findings: 18 weeks post-surgery, the player returned to team training with IPC peak force ILA <10%, ENF ILA <10%, and 97% of pre-injury maximal speed achieved. At Week 8 (initial IPC test), ILA in peak force was 13% and in force at 100 ms was 7%. Progressive improvements in both limbs were documented weekly through the RTS process. At Week 17, CMJ showed 5% deficit in jump height and 7% deficit in peak power vs. pre-injury, but substantial improvements in eccentric deceleration RFD (+20%), concentric impulse-100 (+8%), and lower limb stiffness (+13%). The player passed a medical for an EPL transfer at 20 weeks post-surgery. At 2.5-year follow-up, he remained re-injury free and playing regularly in the EPL.
limitations: Single case report; findings cannot be generalized without controlled studies. Pre-injury and RTS data were collected under different coaching regimes, complicating direct running load comparisons. The surgical procedure was performed by a single specialist and the rehabilitation by a specific multidisciplinary team, limiting reproducibility in settings with fewer resources. No control condition for the nutritional supplementation protocol.
practicalImplications: When SM free tendon retraction is confirmed on MRI, early surgical referral is warranted — conservative management of significant retraction risks scar tissue formation, loss of tension, decreased force production, and compensatory injuries to adjacent hamstring muscles. Rehabilitation should be staged: early isometrics (short to long lever, >80% MVC) to restore tendon stiffness and force capacity, followed by eccentric loading with bi-articular bias, then the control-chaos continuum for on-pitch progression. IPC force platform testing provides a low-cost, repeatable method to track neuromuscular recovery and gate phase progression using bilateral asymmetry thresholds.
athleteDev: For football programs at all levels, this case highlights the importance of having pre-injury strength and GPS baseline data that can serve as objective RTS targets — without pre-injury benchmarks, clinicians must rely on normative data or contralateral symmetry alone, which may underestimate the individual's true recovery status. IPC testing protocols (supine heel drive at 90° hip/knee flexion) are practical with portable force platforms (ForceDecks, Pasco) and should be integrated into pre-season athlete profiling. Collagen + vitamin C supplementation before loading sessions warrants consideration in tendon injury cases.
rtp: RTS following SM reconstruction requires ~18 weeks of structured rehabilitation before return to team training, with the following objective criteria providing a useful framework: IPC peak force ILA <10%, IPC force at 100 ms ILA <10–15%, ENF ILA <10–15% (with trending toward pre-injury norms), pain-free status (<2/10 NRS), positive MRI healing, surgeon discharge, and GPS running loads matching pre-injury chronic training volume. The control-chaos continuum (high control → moderate control → control-chaos → moderate chaos → high chaos → team training) provides a structured on-pitch progression framework that can be adapted to American football position-specific demands and speed exposure requirements.

citation: Whiteley R, Massey A, Gabbett T, Blanch P, Cameron M, Conlan G, Ford M, Williams M. Match High-Speed Running Distances Are Often Suppressed After Return From Hamstring Strain Injury in Professional Footballers. Sports Health. 2020;XX(X):1-6.
abstract: This non-randomized cohort study examined match high-speed running (HSR) distances in 15 professional footballers (soccer, Rugby League, Rugby Union, Australian Rules) before and after 22 hamstring strain injuries across multiple sports. Individual linear regression models were used to establish pre-injury HSR baselines (r² = 0.92–1.0) and detect post-return deviations. Post-injury HSR variance was significantly greater than pre-injury (P = 0.0005), with 7 of 15 players showing sustained absolute reductions, 7 showing no change, and only 1 showing an increase in HSR distance after returning to play.
tldr: Clearing a player for return to play after hamstring strain injury does not reliably mean they are returning to their pre-injury high-speed running performance — nearly half of the professional athletes in this study permanently suppressed their match HSR output for the remainder of the season. This has direct implications for how staff define 'successful' RTP: time-loss and reinjury rate alone are insufficient outcome metrics. HSR distance should be tracked as a performance outcome in post-hamstring RTP monitoring, and any individual showing persistent post-RTP HSR suppression should prompt a re-examination of rehabilitation completeness, residual tissue concerns, or subconscious avoidance behavior. Coaches should be informed that players' sprint contributions may be meaningfully lower than pre-injury baselines even if they appear fully recovered and reinjury-free.
methods: Retrospective non-randomized cohort (Level 3 evidence). Ten professional football teams (soccer, Rugby League, Rugby Union, Australian Rules) were invited; 5 provided anonymized match HSR data for 22 hamstring strain injuries in 15 players. Inclusion required MRI-confirmed hamstring strain and a minimum of 5 complete matches of HSR data both pre- and post-injury within the same season. HSR thresholds were organization-specific but applied consistently within each organization (GPS or validated video tracking). Individual accumulated HSR vs. game sequence was modeled with linear regression pre-injury (r² 0.92–1.0); post-injury residuals (actual minus predicted) were compared pre- vs. post-injury using paired t-test on median residuals. Ethics: Anti Doping Lab Qatar Research Ethics Committee, No. E2017000264.
findings: Pre-injury HSR was highly consistent within individuals (r² = 0.92–1.0; P < 0.0001). Post-injury variance was significantly greater than pre-injury (P = 0.0005), confirming HSR suppression as a group trend. Individual outcomes: 7/15 players showed sustained absolute HSR reduction; 7/15 showed no change; 1/15 showed an increase. The group mean suppression was approximately 1,000 m in HSR per game post-injury. Reinjuries (n=7) did not produce a further decline in HSR beyond the index injury. Between-player variability in baseline HSR was large (~75 to ~1,000 m/game).
limitations: Small sample (15 players, 22 injuries) due to strict inclusion criteria and organizational reluctance to share performance data; prevents meaningful subgroup analysis by sport, position, or pre-injury HSR volume. Single-season analysis only — it is unknown whether suppressed HSR normalized over a longer period. Rehabilitation approach was not standardized across teams and was not analyzed as a variable. HSR does not capture all dimensions of football performance; some players may maintain effectiveness through tactical and technical means despite lower sprint output.
practicalImplications: RTP decisions after hamstring strain should incorporate HSR performance benchmarks alongside traditional time-loss and reinjury metrics. Applied staff should document the player's pre-injury match HSR profile (using GPS or video tracking) and use this as a post-RTP target, not merely a secondary check. Players demonstrating persistent post-RTP HSR suppression should be flagged for further evaluation (residual strength deficits, psychological readiness, inadequate high-speed running exposure during rehabilitation). Prolonging rehabilitation to accumulate sufficient high-speed running volume prior to clearance may improve post-RTP HSR restoration.
athleteDev: NFL players and footballers across all codes who sustain hamstring strains face a meaningful risk of permanently reduced sprint output for the rest of that season. Building pre-injury GPS baselines for all athletes — particularly for high-speed running distance per game — is essential infrastructure for objective RTP decision-making. High-speed running volume during rehabilitation should be tracked and targeted to meet or approach pre-injury per-session HSR loads before full RTP clearance, consistent with emerging evidence that higher rehab HSR workloads may protect against reinjury.
rtp: Traditional RTP metrics (time to return, reinjury rate) are insufficient after hamstring strain in professional football. A meaningful proportion of players (approximately 47%) show lasting HSR suppression for the remainder of the season following RTP clearance. Staff should: (1) document pre-injury match HSR profiles before each season, (2) set explicit post-RTP HSR targets as an exit criterion, (3) monitor weekly match HSR in the first 5–10 games post-RTP for signs of suppression, and (4) consider individualized high-speed running-focused rehabilitation extensions for players who reach clearance without meeting HSR performance benchmarks.

citation: Hopwood HJ, Bellinger PM, Compton HR, Bourne MN, Derave W, Lievens E, Kennedy B, Minahan CL. Match Running Performance in Australian Football Is Related to Muscle Fiber Typology. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2023; Ahead of Print.
abstract: This longitudinal observational study examined the association between non-invasive muscle fiber typology (MFT), estimated via proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H-MRS) carnosine content (expressed as a CAZ score), and match running performance in 23 professional Australian Football (AF) athletes across 224 games in the 2020 AFL season. Using GPS-derived metrics and a moving mean technique, the study found that a greater estimated proportion of type I muscle fibers (more negative CAZ score) was significantly associated with greater high-speed running (HSR) distance (P < .001) and a superior ability to maintain HSR performance during peak intensity periods of increasing duration (HSR slope; P = .01).
tldr: Muscle fiber typology — the proportion of slow-twitch (type I) vs. fast-twitch (type II) fibers — meaningfully influences how much high-speed running a professional Australian footballer can sustain across a match. Athletes skewed toward type I fibers covered more total HSR distance and maintained HSR intensity better during peak play periods, likely due to their superior fatigue resistance. Importantly, this study used a non-invasive carnosine-based MRS method to estimate fiber type, making it feasible in applied settings. For football programs, fiber typology assessments could inform position fit, conditioning program design, substitution strategy, and high-speed running expectations. The finding also connects to return-to-play considerations: athletes with more type II fiber profiles may need longer recovery windows and more conservative re-introduction to repeated sprint loads after injury.
methods: Longitudinal observational study. 23 male professional AF athletes from one AFL club (mean age 20.1 years) participated. Muscle fiber typology estimated via proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H-MRS) of the gastrocnemius medialis and soleus on a 3T MRI scanner; carnosine concentration expressed as a CAZ score relative to 40 age-matched male controls. GPS running performance collected via Catapult Vector S7 devices (10 Hz) across 224 match files (109 AFL, 115 reserve grade) during the COVID-19-modified 2020 season. Match metrics: total distance, HSR (>4.17 m/s), VHSR (>6.67 m/s), maximum velocity, and mean acceleration. A moving mean technique (1–10 minute durations) with power law modeling quantified peak intensity, slope, and intercept values. Mixed linear models with player position as a random effect assessed associations between CAZ score and running outputs; significance set at P < .05.
findings: Mean CAZ score: −0.60 (range −1.89 to 1.25); 83.3% of athletes had negative CAZ scores indicating greater estimated type I fiber proportion. Position differences in CAZ score were trivial to moderate (tall > hybrid ES 0.67; tall > small ES 0.44; all P > .20). Significant negative association between CAZ score and total HSR distance (c = −207.71; P < .001): greater type I proportion = more HSR distance. Significant negative association between CAZ score and HSR slope (c = −0.018; P = .01): greater type I proportion = better sustained HSR across 1–10 minute peak periods. No significant associations with total distance, acceleration, VHSR, or speed-derived intercept/slope metrics. Between-player CAZ score range was large despite absence of a dominant positional MFT profile.
limitations: Data collected during a COVID-19-modified 2020 AFL season with reduced quarter duration (16 min) and compressed scheduling (4-day breaks between some matches), limiting direct comparison with standard seasons. Combined AFL and reserve-grade match data were used to increase sample size, despite potential differences in competitive intensity. Sample size was small (n=23), reducing statistical power for positional subgroup analyses. CAZ score is an estimated — not directly measured — fiber typology proxy. Dietary exclusion criteria (no vegans/vegetarians, no beta-alanine use) may limit generalizability.
practicalImplications: Non-invasive muscle fiber typology assessment via 1H-MRS carnosine measurement (CAZ score) provides actionable information about an athlete's potential high-speed running capacity in field sports. Applied sport science staff can use this information to contextualize GPS workload data: athletes with fast-twitch profiles (positive CAZ scores) may show steeper HSR decline curves during peak match periods and require substitution strategies or modified conditioning programs. Athletes with predominantly type I fiber profiles can be expected to sustain HSR output more consistently throughout a match. These principles translate directly to NFL contexts where repeated sprint demands across 60+ plays characterize game performance.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, MFT assessment could become a useful tool in talent identification and position allocation — not as a fixed determinant, but as one physiological input alongside speed testing, strength profiling, and GPS data. Type II-dominant athletes may be better suited to roles requiring explosive bursts with recovery time (e.g., defensive line, running back in American football), while type I-dominant athletes may sustain high-speed contribution more reliably across longer playing time. Conditioning programs for type II-dominant athletes should emphasize repeated sprint tolerance and recovery capacity, particularly entering heavy game blocks.
rtp: The relationship between muscle fiber typology and fatigue resistance during high-speed running has direct implications for return-to-play from sprint-related injuries (hamstring strains, hip flexor strains, calf injuries). Type II-dominant athletes with positive CAZ scores may require longer recovery windows between high-intensity training sessions and more conservative progressive overload of repeated-sprint workloads during rehabilitation, given their greater fatigue vulnerability and longer time course of recovery. Clinicians should consider fiber typology context when setting HSR volume targets and return timelines for sprint-position athletes.

citation: Dillon P, Lovell R, Joyce D, Norris D. Maximum Speed Exposures in Australian Rules Football: Do Methods Matter? Science and Medicine in Football. 2023;DOI:10.1080/24733938.2023.2211048.
abstract: This methodological study compared weekly near-to-maximum speed (MS) exposure counts derived from two approaches in 47 professional Australian Rules Football players over two consecutive seasons: pre-season sprint assessments (PSSA) using 3x50m maximal efforts via 10 Hz GPS, and in-season monitoring of the highest speed attained during routine training and matches. Exposure counts were aggregated at ≥80%, ≥85%, ≥90%, and ≥95% of individual maximum speed. Weekly near-to-MS exposures were significantly lower when derived from in-season monitoring versus PSSA across all thresholds (p<0.0001), though the effect was trivial at the ≥90% and ≥95% thresholds. Playing position had no meaningful effect on exposure counts.
tldr: When tracking near-maximum speed exposures for injury prevention and performance, the method used to determine an athlete's maximum speed (pre-season sprint test vs. in-season GPS monitoring) produces meaningfully different counts at lower thresholds (≥80%, ≥85%) but largely interchangeable results at higher thresholds (≥90%, ≥95%) that are most relevant for hamstring injury protection. For applied staff, this means discrete sprint assessments may not be necessary — routine GPS monitoring is sufficient for monitoring the high-speed exposures that matter most for athlete health and performance.
methods: Observational longitudinal study. N=47 professional male Australian Rules Football players from one club, two consecutive seasons. Pre-season sprint assessments (PSSA): 3x50m maximal sprints on grass, 10 Hz GPS (Catapult Optimeye S5). In-season monitoring: routine 10 Hz GPS tracking across 585 match observations and 6,099 training sessions. Weekly (match-to-match microcycle) near-to-MS exposures aggregated at ≥80%, ≥85%, ≥90%, ≥95% of individual maximum. Zero-inflated negative binomial mixed model (glmmTMB in R) with interaction terms for threshold x method and position x threshold; random intercept for player.
findings: Maximal speeds were achieved in training (55%; 32.4±1.4 km/h) and matches (45%; 33.3±1.3 km/h) but never during PSSA (31.0±1.0 km/h). Weekly near-to-MS exposures were significantly lower via in-season monitoring vs PSSA: ≥80% (-1.26; CI: -1.58 to -0.93), ≥85% (-0.78; CI: -0.97 to -0.59), ≥90% (-0.42; CI: -0.53 to -0.32), ≥95% (-0.09; CI: -0.12 to -0.06), all p<0.0001. No effect of playing position (nomadic vs non-nomadic) on exposure counts at any threshold (P≥0.161).
limitations: Only 10 Hz GPS was used — higher-resolution radar or laser guns may yield different results. Dwell-time settings (minimum effort duration of 0.3 s) were not varied. Single-club case study limits generalizability. The PSSA maximum speed was never exceeded in testing, meaning PSSA-based benchmarks consistently underestimate true maximum speed attained in competition.
practicalImplications: Sport science staff can use routine GPS monitoring (in-season data) as the reference for maximum speed rather than conducting discrete pre-season sprint assessments, as the two methods are functionally interchangeable at the ≥90% and ≥95% thresholds most associated with injury protection. This simplifies workflows and reduces the burden of pre-season testing. At lower thresholds (≥80–85%), the method chosen does produce meaningfully different counts, which should be considered when setting training targets or reporting sprint exposure metrics.
athleteDev: For American football athlete development, these findings support using GPS-derived in-season maximum velocity rather than isolated testing days to set near-maximum speed training benchmarks. Skill position players accumulating high-speed running volume during practice can have their sprint exposure thresholds anchored to their best in-game speed, removing the need for separate speed testing and streamlining load management. Coaches should be aware that in-game maximum speeds typically exceed those seen in isolated speed tests, so training targets set from game data may be more representative of true maximum velocity.
rtp: When determining return-to-play sprint thresholds after soft-tissue injury (e.g., hamstring strains), NFL practitioners can confidently use a player's historical in-season GPS-derived maximum velocity as the benchmark for near-maximum speed exposures during rehabilitation. The finding that ≥95% threshold counts differ by only ~0.1 per week between methods means RTP progression criteria based on in-game speed data are not meaningfully compromised compared to using formal sprint testing. This reduces logistical barriers and allows more ecologically valid RTP benchmarking.

citation: Ryan S, Pacecca E, Tebble J, Hocking J, Kempton T, Coutts AJ. Measurement Characteristics of Athlete Monitoring Tools in Professional Australian Football. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. 2020;15(4):457-463.
abstract: This study examined the reliability and sensitivity of four common athlete monitoring tools — perceptual wellness questionnaires, eccentric hamstring force (Nordbord), countermovement jumps (ForceDecks), and a submaximal heart rate recovery (HRR) test — in 42–45 professional Australian Football players during a full competition season. Test–retest reliability (noise) and weekly variation (signal) were quantified, and signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) calculated. All measures displayed acceptable to good SNRs (range 1.3–11.1). Perceived stress and soreness were the most sensitive wellness indicators; concentric mean force and relative peak force were the most sensitive CMJ variables; and exercise heart rate showed greater sensitivity than heart rate recovery.
tldr: All four commonly used monitoring tools in professional Australian football — wellness questionnaires, eccentric hamstring strength (Nordbord), countermovement jumps (ForceDecks), and a submaximal heart rate run test — can detect meaningful changes in player readiness beyond their measurement noise. Applied staff should prioritize perceived stress and soreness as the most sensitive wellness indicators, use CMJ concentric mean force and relative peak force for neuromuscular monitoring, and prefer exercise heart rate over recovery heart rate in submaximal running tests. SNR analysis is a practical way to evaluate whether monitoring data is worth collecting.
methods: Observational study during the 2018 AFL competition season (rounds 1–23). N=42–45 professional Australian footballers from Carlton FC. Perceptual wellness (5-item Likert questionnaire, z-scores) at 48, 72, and 96 h postmatch. Eccentric hamstring force (Nordbord, Vald Performance) once per week at 72 h postmatch. CMJ (ForceDecks, Vald Performance; 5 variables) at 72 h postmatch. Submaximal HRR test (80 m intervals at 12 km/h for 5 min, 60 s recovery) at 48 h postmatch, 8 occasions. Test–retest reliability: CV% and ICC via Hopkins spreadsheets. SNR = weekly CV% / test CV%; rated poor (<1.0), acceptable (1.0–1.5), good (>1.5).
findings: All measures showed acceptable–good SNR. SNR range: 1.3–11.1. Perceived stress had highest SNR at 48 h (11.1) and 72 h (10.2) postmatch. CMJ relative peak force (SNR 2.5) and concentric mean force (SNR 2.3) were most sensitive CMJ variables. Average eccentric hamstring force SNR was 2.4 (right limb) and 2.0 (left limb). Exercise heart rate SNR was 5.3 vs HRR SNR of 1.4. ICCs ranged from .30 (perceived fatigue) to .97 (CMJ concentric mean force). All measures SNR declined at 96 h postmatch compared to 48–72 h, consistent with return to baseline.
limitations: No outcome measures (injury or performance) were used to validate the monitoring data. Wellness questionnaires were not developed using formal psychometric validation. CMJ and hamstring test–retest data were collected after morning training, not in a fully rested state, which may have inflated test error. Sample from a single club limits generalizability. HRR test conducted outdoors in variable weather conditions may have increased TE relative to indoor protocols.
practicalImplications: Performance and medical staff can confidently use wellness questionnaires (particularly stress and soreness), eccentric hamstring force testing, and CMJ (emphasizing concentric mean force and relative peak force) as sensitive monitoring tools. SNR >1.5 indicates a test can reliably detect real changes — practitioners should prioritize measures above this threshold and interpret those at 1.0–1.5 with caution. The submaximal heart rate test is best interpreted using exercise heart rate rather than recovery heart rate due to superior sensitivity.
athleteDev: For American football athlete development programs, this study validates the use of a Nordbord-type hamstring strength test, force plate CMJ assessments, and simple wellness questionnaires as meaningful tracking tools across a season. The SNR framework helps strength and conditioning staff justify which tests add value versus measurement noise. Monitoring perceived stress and soreness weekly can flag early signs of overtraining or underrecovery in developing players before performance or injury outcomes appear.
rtp: In NFL return-to-play contexts, eccentric hamstring force (SNR 2.0–2.4) and CMJ concentric mean force (SNR 2.3) provide sufficient sensitivity to detect meaningful recovery progress in players returning from lower-extremity injuries. Changes exceeding test CV% thresholds in these measures can be interpreted as real and used to guide RTP progression. Perceived soreness (SNR 2.9–3.2 at 48–72 h postmatch) also provides a sensitive, low-cost indicator of recovery status alongside objective testing.

citation: Ryan S, Kempton T, Pacecca E, Coutts AJ. Measurement Properties of an Adductor Strength-Assessment System in Professional Australian Footballers. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. 2019;14(2):256-259.
abstract: This brief report examined the measurement reliability and sensitivity of the GroinBar hip-strength testing system (Vald Performance) for monitoring adductor strength in professional Australian footballers during the 2017 AFL season. Reliability testing was conducted on 18 players; weekly variation data were collected from 45 players at 48, 72, and 120 hours postmatch. The system demonstrated a test–retest CV of 6.3% and ICC of .94, with SNRs of 1.6–2.6 across postmatch time points. Groin pain had a very likely moderate negative effect on adductor force output (ES: 0.41), indicating appropriate construct validity.
tldr: The GroinBar standardized adductor squeeze testing system is a reliable and sensitive tool for tracking groin strength in professional footballers. Changes exceeding ~6.3% in adductor force can be interpreted as real, not measurement noise. Groin pain meaningfully reduces squeeze force, validating the system as a construct-valid measure of adductor health. For applied staff, this supports routine adductor strength monitoring as a practical, objective indicator of groin load tolerance and recovery status across the competition season.
methods: Observational, longitudinal design. Reliability cohort: 18 professional Australian footballers (age 23.1±2.1 y), same-day test–retest prior to 2017 AFL season. Weekly variation cohort: 45 professional Australian footballers (age 24.6±4.0 y), 2017 AFL season rounds 1–23. GroinBar system (Vald Performance): supine position, knees at 60°, femoral medial condyles on load cells at 50 Hz. 1 warm-up rep (80%) + 1 maximal 5-second isometric squeeze. Force extracted for left, right, and average limb (N). Pain rated 0–10 (numeric rating scale). CV, SWC (0.2×SD), ICC, and SNR calculated via Hopkins reliability spreadsheets.
findings: Test–retest CV: 6.3% (CI: 4.9–9.0%); ICC: .94 (.87–.96). CV exceeded the SWC (5.0%) on both limbs, indicating the test detects more than the smallest worthwhile change. SNRs ranged from 1.6 to 2.6 across 48, 72, and 120 h postmatch time points; greatest sensitivity at 72 h. Reported groin pain had a very likely moderate negative effect on adductor strength (ES: 0.41; CI: 0.26–0.55). Weekly CV ranged from ~9.9–16.2% across time points and limbs.
limitations: Study was conducted at a single professional club, limiting generalizability. Individual recovery strategies and match loads were not controlled and may have contributed to week-to-week variation. Groin pain was treated as a binary variable (present/absent); the relationship between pain severity and force output was not fully explored. No comparison was made between morning and evening testing times. The reliability data was collected prior to the season, not during it.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine staff can use the GroinBar system to reliably monitor adductor strength throughout the season. A change exceeding 6.3% in adductor squeeze force should be interpreted as a real change in strength rather than measurement noise. The 72 h postmatch time point shows the greatest sensitivity and may be the optimal monitoring window. The presence of groin pain reliably reduces measured force, so pain status should always be recorded alongside adductor strength scores to aid clinical interpretation.
athleteDev: For American football programs, a standardized bilateral adductor strength assessment offers a practical tool to screen for adductor/groin deficits in skill position players at risk for hip adductor strains. The GroinBar-style approach offers superior reliability (ICC .94) over handheld dynamometry or sphygmomanometer methods used in other settings. Serial monitoring across training blocks can identify players with progressive force reductions that may precede groin injury, enabling early intervention.
rtp: In NFL return-to-play protocols following groin or adductor injuries, the 6.3% CV threshold provides a quantitative benchmark to confirm meaningful strength recovery before full clearance. Serial testing at 48–72 h intervals during RTP progression allows staff to track adductor strength recovery relative to baseline. The finding that pain significantly reduces force output supports using a pain-free adductor squeeze force target alongside clinical assessment as an objective RTP criterion for groin-related injuries.

citation: Littleford K, Arbon JJ, DeFoor MT, Cognetti DJ. Medical Malpractice Lawsuits Against Team Physicians in the National Football League (NFL) Increased Between 1971 and 2020 But Remain Low Overall. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation. 2024;doi:10.1016/j.asmr.2024.101013.
abstract: A comprehensive review of publicly available legal databases, court records, and news archives identified 28 medical malpractice lawsuits filed by NFL players against team physicians between 1971 and 2020, among 273 total team-associated physicians (190 of whom were orthopaedic surgeons). The lawsuit rate significantly increased over time (p=0.0086, R²=0.60), rising from 0.28 lawsuits/year (1971–1995) to 0.84 lawsuits/year (1996–2020). The most common body areas involved were the knee (n=11), spine (n=4), and foot/ankle (n=4). The highest lawsuit amount filed was $180 million, with at least six suits exceeding $1 million. Despite the statistically significant increase, the absolute number of lawsuits remains low.
tldr: NFL team physicians face a statistically significant but still numerically rare risk of medical malpractice litigation — roughly one lawsuit filed per year across all 32 teams. Knee injuries, spinal conditions, and foot/ankle problems are the most frequent triggers. Emerging liability areas include improper medication prescribing (NSAIDs, opioids, Toradol) and concussion return-to-play decisions. Applied sport science and sports medicine staff in professional football should understand that clear documentation, transparent communication with players, informed consent, and prioritizing athlete long-term health over performance pressure are the key risk-mitigation strategies.
methods: Retrospective legal database review. Two independent reviewers searched publicly available search engines, legal databases, court records, and news archives in August 2023. Search terms combined each of the 32 NFL teams and their associated orthopaedic surgeons with terms including 'malpractice' and 'lawsuit.' Inclusion: lawsuits filed by NFL players against team physicians; exclusion: lawsuits by non-players or non-medical claims. Data collected: body area, complaint type, medical condition, and lawsuit outcome. Linear regression assessed temporal trends in lawsuit frequency from 1971 to 2020 (p<0.05 threshold).
findings: 273 physicians identified across all 32 NFL teams; 190 were orthopaedic surgeons. 28 total lawsuits identified (1971–2020): 2 (1971–75), 0 (1976–80), 1 (1981–85), 3 (1986–90), 1 (1991–95), 4 (1996–2000), 4 (2001–05), 4 (2006–10), 3 (2011–15), 6 (2016–20). Significant increase over time: p=0.0086, R²=0.60. Rate nearly tripled from 0.28/year to 0.84/year across 25-year epochs. Most common anatomic sites: knee (n=11), spine (n=4), foot/ankle (n=4), shoulder (n=2). Highest suit: $180M. At least 6 suits >$1M. Notable cases include wrongful death from heat stroke, class action over painkiller prescribing practices, and multiple knee cases involving missed diagnoses, career-ending injuries, and unauthorized surgical procedures.
limitations: Relied primarily on publicly available internet searches, which may miss sealed, pre-internet, or unreported cases. Possible recency bias — older suits may be underrepresented due to limited online records. Many final verdicts and settlements were sealed or unavailable. Study design could not account for all 273 physicians' individual case histories comprehensively. Non-orthopaedic physician malpractice may have been underrepresented given the search approach focused on orthopaedic surgeons.
practicalImplications: NFL team physicians and sports medicine staff should be aware of the increasing (though still rare) legal risk environment, particularly involving knee surgeries, spinal injuries, pain medication prescribing, and return-to-play decisions post-concussion. Key risk mitigation strategies include thorough documentation of medical decisions, robust informed consent processes, clear communication with players about the extent of injuries, and ensuring that return-to-play decisions are made on medical grounds rather than performance or financial pressures. The NFL's 2004 prohibition on team-physician marketing contracts underscores the league's recognition of conflict-of-interest risk.
athleteDev: For American football athlete development programs at all levels, this paper reinforces the importance of establishing trust-based physician-athlete relationships and implementing clear medical protocols for injury management. High-profile lawsuits over missed ACL tears, unauthorized surgical procedures, and heat-stroke deaths highlight the need for systematic and transparent medical practices. Development programs should ensure that medical clearance processes are rigorous, documented, and athlete-centered rather than team-performance-focused.
rtp: Several of the 28 lawsuits involved premature return-to-play decisions — including a concussion case resulting in a $1.55 million verdict and multiple knee cases involving clearance before full recovery. These cases underscore that NFL RTP protocols must be medically justified, well-documented, and communicated clearly to players. Return-to-play decisions involving the knee, spine, or head carry the greatest legal exposure, and independent medical consultation may provide additional protection for both the physician and the athlete.

citation: Almendros-Ruiz A, Conde-Pipó J, Aranda-Martínez P, Olivares-Jabalera J, Acuña-Castroviejo D, Requena B, Fernández-Martínez J, Mariscal-Arcas M. Melatonin Secretion and Impacts of Training and Match Schedules on Sleep Quality, Recovery, and Circadian Rhythms in Young Professional Football Players. Biomolecules. 2025;15(5):700.
abstract: This cross-sectional study compared circadian rhythm markers, melatonin secretion, and sleep quality in 40 elite male youth football players (ages 16–18) from two youth-category teams of the same Spanish club. Seventeen players trained in the morning (9:00–10:30 AM; 53,626 lux natural sunlight) and 23 trained in the evening (20:00–21:30 PM; 4,154 lux artificial light). Salivary melatonin was measured at six time points, and chronotype, sleep quality (PSQI), and daytime sleepiness (ESS) were assessed via validated questionnaires. Morning trainers had significantly higher mean melatonin levels (52.49 vs. 29.93 pg/mL; p=0.026), earlier DLMO (21.20 vs. 21.93 h; p=0.023), and better sleep quality (p<0.001) despite shorter sleep duration (p=0.014).
tldr: Evening training sessions — typical of elite European football schedules — are associated with significantly lower melatonin levels, delayed circadian phase (later dim light melatonin onset), and poorer sleep quality compared to morning training, even though evening trainers sleep longer. Exposure to artificial stadium lighting during night-time sessions likely suppresses melatonin production and disrupts circadian alignment. For applied football staff, this evidence supports considering training timing as a modifiable recovery variable, exploring morning scheduling when possible, and implementing blue-light blocking strategies (glasses, screen restrictions) for players who must train or compete in the evening.
methods: Descriptive, comparative, cross-sectional study. N=40 elite male youth football players (under-19) from one Spanish club, two youth teams differing only in training schedule. Morning training (MT): n=17, mean age 17.59±0.62 y; Evening training (ET): n=23, mean age 16.61±0.58 y. Salivary melatonin at 6 time points (4h before bedtime, 2h before bedtime, at bedtime, 2h after bedtime, 4h after bedtime, waking) via Salivettes; UHPLC-mass spectrometry analysis. Circadian parameters: mean melatonin, amplitude, acrophase (decimal hours), DLMO. Chronotype: Horne-Östberg Morningness-Eveningness Questionnaire. Sleep quality: PSQI. Daytime sleepiness: ESS. Anthropometrics via ISAK standards. Dietary intake via 3x 24h recalls. Light intensity measured via lux meter at each site. Statistics: Mann-Whitney U, Student's t-test, Chi-square/Fisher's exact; Cohen's d and Cramér's V effect sizes.
findings: MT group had significantly higher mean melatonin (52.49±41.81 vs. 29.93±21.19 pg/mL; p=0.026; Cohen's d=0.71). DLMO was earlier in MT (21.20±1.36 vs. 21.93±1.48 h; p=0.023); 41.1% of MT vs. 8.7% of ET had early DLMO (<20.5 h; p=0.040). No significant difference in amplitude or acrophase. MT woke earlier (7.22 vs. 8.58 h; p<0.001), slept less (6.95 vs. 7.95 h; p=0.014), but reported better sleep quality (70.6% good vs. 47.8%; p<0.001). ET group showed complete loss of circadian melatonin rhythm across all chronotypes. MT group showed defined melatonin acrophase (~2:15 AM); ET group showed suppressed/phase-disrupted rhythm. Chronotype distribution differed: ET had more moderately-evening types (75.1% vs. 47.1%; p<0.001). Anthropometric and dietary variables were similar between groups.
limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. Imbalanced group sizes (n=17 vs. n=23) may reduce statistical power. Sample from a single club limits generalizability. Sleep assessed by self-report only (no actigraphy or polysomnography). No objective performance measures included. Individual light exposure outside training sessions not controlled. Caffeine intake and psychological stress not formally assessed. Core body temperature was not measured. The ET group had a significantly different chronotype distribution from the MT group, which may confound training-time effects.
practicalImplications: Sport science and sports medicine staff should recognize that training schedules are a modifiable factor in circadian health and recovery. Evening training — particularly under artificial lighting — disrupts melatonin rhythms and degrades sleep quality even when total sleep duration is maintained. Practical interventions include scheduling critical training blocks in the morning when possible, providing blue-light blocking glasses for players in the hours before night training or matches, limiting screen use within one hour of bedtime, and considering individual chronotypes when designing weekly training schedules.
athleteDev: For American football programs, where evening practices and night games are common, this study highlights the chronobiological toll of artificial light exposure on young players' hormonal recovery systems. Strength and conditioning staff should consider sleep hygiene education as a core component of athlete development programs, particularly for younger players (high school, college) whose circadian systems are still maturing. Monitoring sleep quality periodically using PSQI or similar tools can identify players experiencing night-training-related circadian disruption before it impacts performance or recovery.
rtp: In NFL return-to-play contexts, players recovering from injury who are also enduring night-game schedules or late practice times may have compromised melatonin-mediated recovery. Since melatonin plays a role in muscle repair, immune function, and oxidative stress management, circadian disruption from evening schedules may delay tissue healing. Medical staff should incorporate sleep quality assessment (PSQI, sleep duration) into RTP monitoring, and consider optimizing sleep environment and timing as adjunct recovery interventions for injured players navigating evening game/practice schedules.
